Representative_Docs: ['Eriks Adamsons During the reign of Philip IV jhc small donkey of Don Garcia Fernandez Manrico conde de Osorio stopped one fine day on the road that runs between the gardens of Aranchuez and Madrid. This was the donkey Pantofilado, who was carrying on his back two servants of his master — Riiiz and Diego — and two bags of wine. The two servants who knew the character of Castilian donkeys in general and especially that of their master\'s donkey waited patiently for the donkey to change his mind and to continue on his way. Moreover, as their destination — the home of Don Garcia — was.so near that it was visible to the eyes of an old man and not only to those of a donkey who, as a beast of burden, is endowed with sharper senses than a human being. Several moments passed, during which a bullet shot by an arcabuz could have flown four or five times from the road to the home of Don Garcia and back; more time passed, a crawfish would have been able to crawl there but the donkey Pantofilando had not yet moved. Although the donkey with his burden did not take up much room, still, by stopping in the middle of the roati he blocked the traffic and got into the way of the drivers and especially the riders. Urged by their heartless words Ruiz then kicked the animal in the thigh with his heel and Diego in the abdomen and when one kick did not help the kicking was repeated with increased vigour. However, Pantofilado did not move Both servants got off the donkey uttering groans. "What a disgrace to Don Garcia Fernandez Manrico conde de Osorio!" exclaimed Ruiz. "His servants will have to walk." "What a disgrace, what a disgrace to us, the only sons of honest parents/ exclaimed Diego, too, "we shall have to walk." Both Ruiz and Diego tried to push the donkey and to drag him; they pulled his long ears which were like those of a hare, they tore him by his nostrils, they kicked him repeatedly in the most sensitive parts of his abdomen; all this was in vain. Pantofilado resembled a Babylonian tomb, the base o£ which is deep in the ground. "Diego," said Ruiz then, "go and inform our master, tell him what a piece of bad luck has befallen us. We cannot be expected to carry the donkey home, neither can we kill him and leave him a prey to wolves and ravens. "You are right," agreed Diego, "I will go to Don Garcia, our gracious master." While Diego was away carrying out this decision in a leisurly way Pantofilado blocked the way of several carriages belonging to noble cavaliers and to rich merchants who were on their way to Madrid and of a gipsy caravan who were THE OBSTINATE DONKEY A Short Story marching with their tame wild beasts in the direction exactly opposite. Unescorted riders got in among them: — tradesmen, arcabuceros, Dominicans, — and pedestrians: — herdsmen, students, highwaymen, beggars. The confusion created by , them was something like the stir at the entrance to hell before the witches\' sabbath. The noise was intensified by quadrilliers of the Holy Hermandada who had appeared from somewhere and were now calling repeatedly: "In the name of God and of justice ,what is happening here?" The English and Dutch merchants were the first to lose their patience as nothing else in the world was of any interest to them, but business transactions, then the highwaymen began to grow impatient, for they had arranged to surprise rich travellers at certain places. The noble cavaliers were annoyed, too; they were afraid that they would miss their gorgeous merry-making, so that at last a string of insults, well-chosen, surprising, bewildering, spiced and colourful, witty and long in Castilian, English, Dutch, Italian rose from the road and resounded over the country burnt by the tropical sun, barren, covered by a few olive groves and numerous brown hillocks. These insults, directed towards Pantofilado developed into a real contest, for everyone of those present tried to outdo the * others by his imagination, so as to calm his impatience and appease his anger. Only the beautiful Zoraide whose mother was a Moor and whose father was a gipsy beast-tamer laughed melodiously and winked playfully at Don Alonzo Alvarez de Torres y Villaroella who was bored in his comfortable carriage and who had covered his face with a light black silk mask, so as to prevent it from exposure to the dust and the sun. Teasing him mischievously Zoraide took a few graceful bolero steps and so showed her slim figure wrapped in materials of Egyptian brown, green, and red. Loveliest of all was her black hair adorned with red camellias, this was in sharp contrast to her light-grey eyes iri her brown face. Suddenly, however, just when the flow of insults, the prickings of halberds by the quadrilliers of the Holy Hermandada and the hail of stones thrown by the gipsy boys at the still unmoving and indifferent donkey had attained its highest Don Garcia himself, the broad white ostrich feathers of his hat swaying and the lace on the cuffs of his green wrap and the tops of his riding-boots fluttering, appeared on his steed Boldovinosa, his servant Diego, holding to the stirrup of his master running ligthly beside him. Stern ancf proud Don Garcia rode into the midst of the undisciplined crowd and so effectively raised his hand trained in tauromachies that for a moment both the richly and the poorly clad were silent. Foreigners, Castilians, come to your senses! Do you not see that this is a donkey that can neither defend himself, nor answer for himself. I will defend him and answer for him, for he belongs to me — Don Garcia Fernandez Manrico conde de Osorio." "My noble sir," answered a French traveller, "your donkey, excuse my bold words, somewhat obstructs the traffic on the road and robs us of our precious time." "Yes, yes, we are in a hurry," began to shout not only the English and Dutch merchants, but even the Gastilians — the riders as well as the pedestrians, \'we must hurry." A wave of proud anger surged up in Don Garcia. "Beware of blasphemy, he began in an earnest voice, that was trembling slightly, and then after a short break, filled with impressive silence he repeated again, "beware of blasphemy, you foreign gentlemen, speaking of haste, for death overtakes a falcon as easily as a crawfish.\' But his proud anger grew keener as he addressed his own Cast\' ilians: "Shame to you, Castilians," he said, "you should know by now that if there was time enough for us to have been born there will be sufficient time to die. Our onl> care should be how to spend our. time as pleas antly as possible." And then added: "To do honour to ou name, this is our only duty." And Don Garcia Fer nandez Marinco conde de Osorio • himself who poss essed neither more nor less than a coat-of-arms a horse JSoldovinosa, a roof to sleep under, a donkey Pantofilando, and This was tried, but without results. Pantofilado did not move. his servants Ruiz and Diego had always known how to do honour to his old, beautiful and long name. "Senor is right" — whispered the Castilian monks, quadrilliers, herdsmen, robbers and beggars reverently, ashamed of their undignified haste. "The Castilian nobleman is right," agreed even the English and Dutch merchants. However, several voices were heard to shout: "Get the donkey off the road." \'\'Certainly," agreed Don Garcia, "but not by force." "Senor, 1 know in what way," suggested a merchant from Tuscany, who had noticed the motheaten ostrich feathers of the proud hidalgo and his faded velvet clothes and he quickly strewed a row of beautiful golden oats at a distance of about three feet from the mouth of the donkey Pantofilando. The dark shining eyes of the animal rested for a while on the oats, but he did not move. Don Garcia looked at the Tuscan merchant with disguised disdain and then addressed his donkey with conviction: "Pantofilando, you are neither bred by a zebra and a hippopotamus, nor by a tortoise and a guinea-pig as people have been saying in their barren impatience and rash anger; you, whose ancestor once carried the King of Glory himself as he rode into the Eternal City cheered by the crowds and greeted with waving palm-branches. Neither are you the donkey of Buridan, the donkey who died of hunger between two haystacks because he could not decide from which of the stacks to eat. You are Pantofilado, the donkey of Don Garcia Fernandez Manrico conde de Osorio, clever, meditative and obstinate; obstinate to the very last. But enough! I will give you a rose, suck its juice and perhaps you will turn into some honourable being like the golden donkey of Apuleius. And then you will no longer be obstinate without reason." While Don Garcia was persuading his donkey in this way, Don Alonzo Alvarez de Torres y Villaroella was glad of the unexpected obstacle which had come into his way and which, although putting off his meeting with Don Leonella, had given him the chance of seeing the lovely Zoraide. El caballero, supported by a few servants (the number of his servants was 500), got out of his carriage and bowed his knees in a French fashion to Zoraide who threw confused looks at the gorgeously clad nobleman. He was dressed in dark-brown brocade, covered with six rows of silver and gold lace. The gown was finished off with pale blue taffeta and in place of buttons precious stones were glittering. Blue and red silk ribbons fastened with heavy Malaga rubbies fluttered from his high hat and the lace of his collar. Round his neck glistened a gold chain with a miniature of Philip II in a locket set in by diamonds. On his legs he had long red silk stockings and low shoes adorned with silver rosettes on his feet. Smilingly el caballcro pointed with a finger clad in a perfumed glove and adorned with a stoned ring from the Indies to a red ribbon that was fastened to his hat and said: "The meaning of this ribbon is — in love." "But what is the meaning of this ribbon, senor?" Zoraide had the courage to ask blushingly and pointed to a blue ribbon .which adorned the neck of the cavallier. "A murderer," explained the cavallier and made Zoraide a present of a Venetian handkerchief resembling an orange blossom, which had been sold to him by the representative of the City of Doge, Paolo di Sera, for 200 ducates. Supported by his servants he then got back into the carriage, no longer looked at Zoraide, but had turned his thoughts to Donna Leonella, the golden Rose of Andalusia, to whom he, the Lion of Castile, would soon be joined by the pleasant bonds of Hymen. All this time Don Garcia was still eloquently persuading his donkey Pantofilado who, however, did not move an inch from his place. The spectators looked at each other at a loss what to do. "Your gracious highness," a student of Chaldean and Aramaic from the University of Salamanca addressed Don Garcia timidly at last, and expressed the thoughts of all the other travellers by saying, "it seems to me that the wisdom of all the Persian magicians, the Brahmins from India and the Ethiopian fortune-tellers would not suffice to make your marvellous donkey continue on his way or step to the side of the road " But now from the direction of Aranchuez a cloud of dust gilded by the sun rose from the road and approached quickly. It was preceded by goatherds who, swifter still, ran before it and shouted terrified. "The King is coming! The King is coming! The King is coming!" The bewildered crowd round the obstinate donkey had not even time to draw up or to make way when la elegancia itself came galloping on thoroughbreds which resembled wingless birds and stopped abruptly spreading the fragance of strongly perfumed gowns and gloves. There could be no doubt, this was King Philip IV himself with Queen Isabella who, accompanied by their courtiers, were returning from hunting in the gardens of Aranchuez. Philip IV, the Prince of Asturia, under the influence of Francisco Gomez de Sandoval, the Duke of Lerma, had taken part at incessant sumptuous amusements. The King had devoted most of his life to merry-making und had taken little interest in state affairs, for he did not lack the gold of the New World. England and Lombardy provided him with gowns, the Netherlands with linen, Florence with silk, Germany — shoes, Milan — iron, France —: wax, Venice, Niirnberg, Cologne, Mainz — books; he lacked none of the material benefits of this world. He was an excellent connoisseur of art — especially of painting and the theatre; soft-hearted, inert and somewhat sad like all the Spanish klings, with a sparse, pointed fair beard and a thin moustache with its endsturned up. He was dressed in a green velvet hunting jacket and red silk shirt adorned with gold lace. Round his neck he had a broad starched collar, somewhite like a dazzlingly white millstone. The cuffs of his thickly stuffed sleeves, too, were adorned with golden lace. Large diamonds glittered in place of buttons. The ostrich feathers on his very high hat which looked like rich bunches of white and violet lilac and the price of which would have bought several well-kept vineyards swayed in the . light breeze. The broad tops of his riding-boots made of soft Cordova leather were adorned with ribbons and rosettes of red atlas. His boots had high red heels and the spurs were of pure silver. The lovely and charming Queen, Isabella of Bourbon, was dressed in ten starched, basket-shaped velvet and brocade skirts of various colours, interwoven with gold which had been put on one over the other, but cut out in such a way as to show a bit of each skirt. 32000 pearls and 3000 diamonds were stitched on to her gowns and with the Venice and Bruxelles lace that adorned them it would have easily been possible to cover the road from the beautiful gardens of Aranchuez to the royal town of Madrid. Her stiffly starched white and round collar was so broad that the handles of the Queen\'s spoons had to be specially lengthened so as to enable her to use .them for eating. Carrying her excessively heavy gowns, made according to the complicated fashions of those days, in a dignified way this royal amazon sat lithely on her fragile and restless horse, which like a celestial antilope carried her easily. Beside the Queen rode the counsellor of Philip IV, Gaspar de Gusman, the Duke and Count de Olivares, hard, vigilant and clever, of whom it was said at foreign courts that he had lost his master more kingdoms than all the royal predecessors had been able to gain. Like Philip II he liked to wear black silk that was moderately adorned with white lace and at the hat, neck, shoulders, and feet with red atlas ribbons. Among the followers, dressed in heavy black, violet and green you could see the fat court dwarf, Don Antonio el Ingles, the historiographer of the King, the author of a description of the conquest of Mexico, Antonio de Solis, and the young, handsome, rich and mysterious Count de Villamediana who for a tournament had ordered the goldsmith cover the whole of his jacket with reals and who had added the motto: "Mos amores son reales" to his coat-of-arms. He was fond of fine Dutch linen and Arabian fabrics interwoven with gold. The Count possessed silver plates, 600 of them very large and 900 small. In the gardens of Aranchuez, unnoticed by the evil court dwarfs even, the eyes of Queen Isabella and Count de Villamediana used to meet for longer than was usual, for in spite of the strict etiquette of the Habsburg dynasty at the Spanish court the Queen had not lost her youth, beauty and love of life. Long-bodied and lithe hounds barked and jumped excitedly round the elegant riders, their silver and gold collars adorned with precious stones and initials. The royal hunters and their attendants now took off their light dust and sun masks and their faces, usually gloomy, proud, and pale, but smiling now, became visible. Pallor in those days was cultivated in every way possible and was much admired by the nobility. Gracefully Don Garcia jumped off his Boldovinosa, threw the reigns to the servants, Ruiz and Diego, then with a dignified gesture he took off his enormous hat and went up to the King, begging his pardon for the road being obstructed in a language that was both colourful and elaborate, then in the same extensive and decorative way he explained to him the case with the stubborn donkey and finished his beautiful speech with the following words: "Obstinate to the very last. Such is my donkey Pantofilando, aged six. Such all of us are at times " At these words the King looked at the Duke and the Count Olivares Then Don Garcia asked the King to give his gracious advice on the matter. Philip IV, as well as the Queen and the courtiers were in especially high spirits after successful and merry hunting. They listened with great attention, expressed their laughing surprise at the obstinacy of a donkey, unheard of before, and demanded whether there was no one who would know of some means to make the animal move. The first to come out before the King, bowing low according to the custom of Venice was a highwayman — a bandelero. "Your Royal Highness, I am the well-known highwayman Brocaburno from the caves of the Sierra Morena," he said humbly, but with a certain self importance, "let me work upon this animal with my whip made of the sinews of oxen, and if this will not make him sing like a canary, then I am no real Castilian and not the famous robber of the King\'s highways." The King permitted him to use his whip, but as soon as the highwayman had started his ugly execution, the King gave ear to Don Garcia\'s appeal and told the shamefaced highwayman to disappear from his eyes for spoiling the donkey\'s skin in a rough and unnecessary way. The tradesmen, who were present were anxious to gain the King\'s applause and be given presents and so came forth with the most fantastic of suggestions. "Your Royal Highness, the smoke of burning human bones must be blown into the donkey\'s nostrils," said a wool spinner from Segova. "You Royal Highness, we must trumpet with a hunting-horn into one ear of the donkey and with a bassoon into the other," said a needle-smith from Cordova. The strangest advice that raised a storm of merriment was expressed by a pork-salter from Sevilla: "Your Royal Highness, a smell, a frightfully pungent smell is the only means of making the donkey move." All this ridiculous advice was of course not followed. Then a gipsy, the father of Zoraide, said: "Gipsies make donkeys and mules move by pouring quicksilver into their ears." This was tried, but without results. Pantofilado did not move. Then a quadrillier of the Holy Hermandada exclaimed: "Your Royal Highness, a low fire must be kindled under the donkey." "I would advise you to prick the donkey in his most sensitive part with a needle," added a Dominican who had taken part in many auto-da-fes. A small fire was kindled and the nap of the donkey\'s skin was singed, but the donkey did not even move his ears, then a needle was pricked into his most sensitive place, but without results, so that the Queen who was pretending she was interested in breaking the obstinacy of the donkey, began to doubt whether the animal, who seemed possessed of the evil spirit, were still alive. But there could be no doubt as to the donkey being alive and this was made clear by the hist oriographer of the King, the learned man Antonio de Solis, who added in Latin: "It seems to me that fear is stronger than pain,*\' and then in Castilian: "The obstinate donkey must be made to feel fear, then he will move " This suggestion was gladly accepted by the owner of Pantofilado, who was not pleased with the way his only donkey was coming to harm In order to frighten the donkey the gipsy kept parading huge. bears from Asia Minor past his snout; the bears rose to their hind legs and stretched out their paws in the direction of the donkey. Dreadful African lions were lead out, their roar was so loud that the stones of the road resounded and the dainty horses of the riders snorted with fright. But even then Pantofilado did not move. "Solitude is stronger than fright," said Antonio de Solis and so the donkey was for a time left alone in the middle of the road. But the donkey\'s obstinacy was not broken by this act of cunning. \' When all the travellers had again gathered round the donkey, Brocaburno, the famous highwayman of the Sierra Morena, said again: "Your Royal Highness, in our country criminals are hung up by one arm and one foot in a loop, the so-called garrucha and a garrucha is the only way to make this donkey move. We could hang him up by the right front leg and the back left leg, or by the ears and the tail.\'" "I do not wish to kill the donkey," protested Philip IV and threatened that he would apply the garrucha to Brocaburno himself. The peasants and the herdsmen from the villages round-about used to relate that a bronze cannon was then fired before the very nose of the donkey and that the smoke of the powder covered his grey coat with soot, but that otherwise he only slightly swayedThe peasants record, too, that together with the Dominicans and the students from Salamanca Antonio de Solis used magic words and called down a sand-storm and rain-showers also the cold and the snow-storms of the North Pole over the head of the donkey, but that still the animal did not move. In reality, however, the unsuccessful breaking of the donkey\'s obstinacy finished thus: After King Philip IV had said to the satisfaction of Don Garcia: I do not wish to kill the donkey, the historiographer Antonio de Solis added in ! atiii. "Your Royal Higness," there is nothing that can move this donkey, neither the cunning of Ulysses, nor the piousness of Aeneas; the manliness of Caesar, the generosity of Trajan, the prudence of Cato, the wisdom of Plato, nor the cruelty of Nero.\'\' "True," said Philip IV, "a donkey has gained victory over us.\'. The Duke and Count of Olivares laughed a dry and malignant laugh, for his secret thoughts were: "A donkey has gained victory over you, Philip IV, as everybody else does." "A donkey has gained victory over us," repeated Philip IV once more and gave the order: "He refuses to walk, we must, therefore, carry him." This was a triumph. A donkey of Castile had never been honoured in such a way. Two bars of wood were pushed under the donkey, one between his front legs, the other between his hind legs. The first bar was taken up, from the right and the left, by Ruiz and Diego; the second bar was carried by the highwayman Brocaburno and a quadrillier of the Holy Hermandada, both of them walking docilely side by side, antagonists though they were. The number of people on the road had now increased tenfold and the news had spread that a donkey had gained victory over the King. It is, therefore, not surprising that there were more people present than at the coronation of Charles V. A procession now started, the kind of which had never before been seen in proud Castile. The procession was headed by jumping and furiously barking dogs of various breed. They were followed by a crowd of gipsy boys whose shouts were louder than the barking of the dogs. Then rode the quadrilliers of the Citizen and the Peasant Brotherhood, the Holy Hermandada, who were equipped with shining arms from Toledo. Behind them rode King Philip with the Queen and the courtiers surrounded by long-limbed hounds. The next was the owner of the triumphing donkey, Don Garcia Fernandez Manrico conde de Ozorio, who rode proudly on his horse Boldovinosa and silently enjoyed the victory and the triumph of his donkey. Don Garcia possessed only one gown, but he was kept changing it so skilfully that even his servants Ruiz and Diego thought that their master had several. Next Pantofilado himself was being carried in triumph. . The peasants from the villages of the neighbourhood spoke for a long time after the event about how heartily the donkey had laughed while he was being carried, but the truth is that he let himself be carried with the greatest indifference in the world. Both wine bags were dragged along by the students from Salamanca. Behind the students Dominicans in their wide gowns rode on mules They were saying their prayers. The students were followed by the carriage of Pon Alonzo Alvarez de Torres y yillaroella together with a long row of various foreign merchants. Then came the gipsy in his black cloak, his blue sleeveless jacket and red trousers, a moor sword sparkling from a strap. Beside him on a mule rode the lovely Zoraide. Tranquilly happy she was playing with her costly Venetian handkerchief and singing an old Castilian romance in a low voice: Don Enrico is now buried; God has called him to his fathers. To the Indies to get-gold, Goes Don Manuel, the young count Don Manuel, he is my sweetheart, My thoughts they fly to him. Tamed wild beasts whose roar resounded fright Fully from time to time followed the gipsies. A huge crowd of tradespeople, peasants, herdsmen, pilgrims, highwaymen, and beggars finished off the procession. The crowd was chanting incessantly: A donkey has gained victory over us! We have been overpowered by a donkey! A donkey has gained victory over us! The donkey is possessed by the archangel of darkness! A donkey has overpo wered man! Even the King! The donkey has won, the donkey has won, the donkey has won! So Pantofilado was carried in a triumphal procession to the home of Don Garcia, was taken into his stable and placed before an empty crib into which the merchant of Tuscany strewed some oats and all of those who were present hastened to make the sign of a cross. "This is no donkey, it might be the enchanted emperor Alifanfaron de A red moon cast its light on far away hills... IV finally with a laugh and then called to his attendants: "And now as quickly as possible to Buen Retiro!" Buen Retiro was the name of the King s pleasureseat in the suburbs of Madrid, where theatricals used to be staged under the direction of the famous poet Calderdn de la Barca himself. Queen Isabella who was followed by Count de Villamediana took care that the latter should not see her feet, for the man who had seen the feet of the Queen of Spain had to die and did not dare look at the sun any longer. Her skirts were, therefore ,very long and as a small girl at the court of the Bourbons she had been taught by her maids-in-waiting how to tread on the hems of her skirts without falling. Riding beside her royal husband she felt the looks of Count de Villa mediana on her shoulders which were covered with shining curls held in place by pearls and warmed by the rays of the setting sun. Both she and Count Villamediana were remembering the shady walks bordered by fruit-trees of Aranchuez, the trees and bushes trimmed in the shape of battlements and towers and they were both wondering what slight possibility of approaching each other the \' evening performance would offer them. King Philip IV with the Queen and the courtiers rode to the castle of Buen Retiro; the noble cavalliers, merchants, quadrilliers, tradespeople, monks, students, gipsies, highwaymen, all drove, rode or walked either to Madrid or to Aranchuez; the peasants and the herdsmen went various ways to their homes; Don Garcia with his servants went to bed after a day of hard trials and a warm night fell on Castile. A red moon cast its light on far away hills, green waters and shining laurel, lemon and orange leaves; oleander and myrtle blossoms smelt fragrantly and all around reigned the deep silence of lent. And suddenly the peace of this warm night, this noble silence was broken by several shrill cries, which sounded like the rusty alarm trumpet of a musketeer. This was the braying of the donkey Pantofilado who had left his stable and come out into the yard and finally into the road and who had suddenly felt a desire to go for a walk in the moonlight; now no one wanted him to do anything of the kind. .In a second peasants, herdsmen, pilgrims, beggars, Ruiz and Diego and Don Garcia Fernandez Manrico conde de Osorio himself who was morally hurt at the behaviour of his donkey, rushed out of their huts and their houses, caught hold of anything they could lay hands on, overcome by frightful anger they threw themselves on the donkey Pantofilado, with inconceivable rapidity they tore him to pieces and carried the pieces away with them as relics. Thus the obstinate donkey came to an untimely end, but alas obstinacy itself still continues to exist in this world. Translated by Lacy Berlins.', 'JUSTE By Oskars Kalējs Holding a clay bowl in both hands Juste came out of the kitchen. In the courtyard one could still see the paths but darkness was thickening over the horizon, creeping over the bends of the river Palade. In the west a pink cloud- slowly merged with the forest top. There was still frost at night in this unpredictable month of spring. Tonight the warmth was a little more friendly. Swollen round buds were tearing their shells. The month of leaves was advancing and tomorrow, perhaps, would be the first leaf-bringing day. Juste stood a while, hesitating, then walked towards the byre, where in front of the kennel sounding its chain was the house-dog Tecis. Putting the bowl before the dog, she said: "Here, Tecit, supper time! Munch your bone and watch the house!" Tecis\' warm big tongue licked her cheek and then began to lap up the porridge. Juste turned back towards her room. Darkness was now wavering all around like a blanket. Spring sounds penetrated the darkness: a late bird bending its voice, frogs and crickets sounding theirs and across the river in the wood heath-cocks could be heard. Sounds of life passed over her head. She had reached a stage in her life when blood no longer flowed in fast rhythms. Her mind was tied to work, work which waited to be done each spring. Juste stopped at the door of her house and her eyes looked back across the yard. Around the buildings was a more solid, a thicker night than along the horizon. The pink cloud had merged more fully with the blackness of the forest, and above pale stars perched in the dome. The buildings slumbered, as they did each night when the day was done. Juste stepped across the high threshold of her house and bolted the door. Everyone else was already asleep, their healthy breathing echoed strongly in the beams. Juste lit a lamp. There were three beds in her room. All three were covered with similarly patterned blankets. Spread out in the corner was a brown wardrobe. By the window, covered with a dark cloth sprawled a table. On the opposite side were a pair of chairs. Juste dropped down on her bed. The night was for regaining breath and peace. Days were long and claimed all her strength. At nights, having put her head on the pillow, she slept like a log and woke in the morning refreshed and full of strength. While the summer sky stood clear above her head for weeks on end, not one of her clays was bright. Concern and worry about the farm and the household continually grew in size, like mountains in a stormy sky. Concern and worry had claimed her youth. Not fully nineteen years old had she been when Karlis Skalde had brought her across two boroughs to the Grivas farm as a wife. Karlis had had a clever head, but his hands had been too fine for rough work. Those had been restless times. The majority of people had revalued their lives and had found that somewhere it was better and that it had to be fought for, if not with wisdom, then with strength. Karlis had then found a good use for his wise head. He had travelled around attending meetings and had left the farm to her management. It had been fortunate that she had known how to work and how to organize her people. The fields of Grivas had been then as if sown with stones and the meadows were marshy for lack of drainage. She had spent every spare moment gathering stones off the fields and filling with them the ruts in the road. In the spring there had not been enough hay tor the cows, so she had run to the meadows by the river creek to pick butter-cups and marsh marigolds, and so had pulled the cows through until the first green shoots of grass appeared. In the summer there had not been enough flour to fatten the pigs, again she had been saved by the river Palade where among the alders on the bank grew lush dandelions. She had plucked those and steamed them together with bran and had poured this in the troughs before the piglets. Karlis\' fine hands had been of little help with such jobs. Living, as he had been in the world, he had little knowledge whether at times . she had any food to put before the workers. Through those many summers her legs had been raw, as if scalded, her palms cracked, her fingers stiff, her cheeks darkened and her body bent before time, like the old wild apple tree behind the house in the hop field. Thirty years had gone by treading the Grivas paths, but there had been no gain. Juste hid her gnarled fingers in the folds of her skirt, as if protecting them from the gaze of an invisible crowd. Her thoughts continued to pry into separate parts of her past life. Her husband had spent those restless years without as much as a beating, but during the war he had begun to sicken, had grown weaker and weaker for a few years and then died. He had not seen his daughters grow up and he had not seen the realisation of his work, the founding of the state or the independence. Neither had he seen his eldest daughter wander off to a foreign country, where honest hard work was laughed at. However, the younger and the youngest daughters had remained at Grivas. These had been Juste\'s happiest times but no sooner had they recuperated and started to live than her younger daughter took ill and withered like a broken branch. In the first year of the free state she ceased her short life in just such a spring as her father. Juste had then been left with only her youngest daughter Narda, who grew and flourished like a lush plant. But lush plants quickly overgrow and fall, overfull with juices. Narda had turned her back on Grivas and gone to live in Riga when still a very young girl. Juste hadn\'t seen her home to this day. Juste clenched her hands and her deeply sunken eves directed their attention to the log walls of the room. A life had been spent without ease. The fields of Grivas were now well-kept and even, as if they had been swept clean with a brush. A cowherd could not find a stone there to put in his catapult. The marsh had been drained into the river and instead of the grass of parnassus, white clover now grew there. There was plenty of everything at Grivas, but the plenty could not dispel the coldness in her heart. One could not take earth on one\'s lap and hug it like a small child. Everyone who walked the Grivas land was a stranger to her heart. She sighed deeply and stood up. She looked at the time-piece: midnight was near, there were only a few short hours left for sleep. Juste uncovered her bed; it was also the bed of her youth and used to her bones. By the opposite wall stood the two empty beds of her daughters. The blankets were fading and the straw in the mattresses had flattened, but Juste had not the heart to do away with them. Finally she blew out the light and crept under her blankets. Her eyes glued shut as soon as her head touched the pillow. She saw a strange dream. Narda was wading across the river towards this side. The water was bubbling and turning around her. She had nearly readied the bank when a whirlpool seized her and pulled her under. She emerged momentarily, shouting: "Mother, mother!" and then disappeared again. Juste woke up. There was no sign of the life-like vision, but the dog was barking outside. After a while its barking changed to joyful whining and then there was silence. Juste listened. That could not be a stranger out there. Perhaps Jekis was feeding the horses. She calmed down and relaxed. Juste was very seldom troubled by dreams. Sleep, like a cool drink in the heat of midday, was always refreshing. Yet, a vision like that in sleep could not be a sign of anything good. But one could not start interpreting dreams in the middle of the night, one had to sleep. She turned towards the wall and tried to doze off. Her eyes were falling shut again, but didn\'t quite manage, when someone was knocking on the window. Juste jumped up and holding her breath stared at the darkness. The distance was too much, she couldn\'t even see a shadow outside. When she heard the second rattling at the panshe got up and crept towards the window. In the darkness she could make out the shapes of three apple trees, but no human likeness was visible. "Who is knocking there?" she asked, holding her petticoat tightly around her chest. "It\'s me, mother! Let me in!" "You!?" Juste exclaimed and pulled back from the window. It was Narda, noone else. Breathing laboriously Juste whispered: "Go to the door, I will meet you and let you in." "Let me in through the window." "Honest people use the door," Juste said and turned her back to the window. "If I go through the door I\'ll wake everybody in the house," Narda tried to convince her mother. "The window has no hinges. The people are in their first sleep—they won\'t hear," Juste answered rather abruptly. She groped her way as far as the door and drew back the bolt. Hiding in the darkness of the walls came Narda. She quickly stepped over the threshold and hid behind her mother\'s back. Juste closed the door, took her daughter by the hand and pulled her towards her room. "Mother, bolt the door, someone might come in!" "it\'ll be morning soon—the bolt is there only after a fashion." Narda didn\'t reply, only her steps became more wary. In her mother\'s room she was overcome by a strange sensation. The breath of childhood lingered there, among all the old and familiar things. The beds, the wardrobe, the table, the bookshelf and both chairs stood in the same places as they had stood five years ago. "Sit on the bed while I light the lamp," Juste said softly, searching for matches. "No, mother, let\'s sit in the darkness for a bit." Narda made her mother sit on the bed beside her. Juste was looking at her daughter askance. "Child, how did you get here so late? The train from Riga arrives while it\'s still daylight. You could have let me know you were coming, I would have gone to meet you. To cross the river at this time isn\'t safe. The planks are half submerged." "I got across quite easily and I\'ll get back all right too." "Back? Surely not now—this night?" "I only came in for half-an-hour. Before it is light I have to get back to the station." "Just as if you\'d brought the fire!" Juste exclaimed, panting. "It\'s true, mother, I brought fire, that\'s why I have to get back quickly." Narda replied with a strange laugh. Darkness hid her face and her agitated expression but Juste was troubled by her daughter\'s dark laughter and strange words. She moved closer to her and touched her clothes—the edge of Narda\'s skirt was wet and her feet were soaking. She withdrew, exclaiming: Daughter, daughter! What roads have you been travelling? Take off your coat and let me dry your stockings and shoes!" Juste got up and began to grope for her own clothes. "My shoes and stockings will dry in the train. Better give me an old blanket to cover myself." Narda rejected her mother\'s concern. She didn\'t dare to weaken and to accept even the smallest thing. Strong webs of memories had pulled towards the old house, now she sat there as if dazed and stared in the darkness, when she ought to have gone on. Her mother\'s breathing was troubled. It wasn\'t the breathing of a happy person. Was there suspicion in her heart? No, no! Mother couldn\'t possibly know. Narda sat with her head bent. Mother was showing the same concern as before, but that wasn\'t what she had expected. Narda would have felt better for one good word. Juste pulled on her dress and sat down at the table. The joy of seeing her daughter again had shrunk and hidden itself in the corners of the room. Narda had arrived empty-handed and only bad people wandered around in the middle of the night. "Nobody travels a righteous road in the night. You have arrived empty-handed. Tell me where you have been and what you have done?" she asked. Narda was silent for a while. But for the darkness Juste might have seen that her daughter\'s mouth was trembling and that there was a salty haze in her eyes. "On my way here my hands were not empty. I emptied them along the way." Xarda lifted her empty palms towards her mother. "If you hide your deeds, then I shall ask no more. The train will soon be coming, if you want to get to the station in time, then you will have to go. Which blanket do you want. Yours?" "Give me an old one! I only need it for the little way to the station." Juste opened the wardrobe and raked about amongst the clothes. At last she found a soft woolen plaid and placed it around Narda\'s shoulders. "If you think it unworthy, throw it away! It is yours. I shall go with you as far as the river—but I can\'t go any farther." Her mother\'s determination surprised Narda. She wanted to refuse her, but her lips, to spite her, said something quite different: "All right, mother! But do put on something warm. The night is quite cold." "I\'ll just wrap this old plaid around me. Then I\'ll be warm enough," said Juste. They huddled close together and went out into the yard. The night was turning towards morning, but the darkness was still intense, only when their eyes got used to it they could see the path. "Are you going straight across the river?" asked Juste. "Yes." They turned the corner of the byre towards the river. The road, which was green in the summer, was still black and damp. It was rugged and uneven and the occasional holes divided Narda from her mother. Their two shapes were silhouetted against the darkness of the forest. Narda grasped her mother\'s hand. Through all her experiences and adventures her childhood glittered as if it had been lived only yesterday. Beyond the fields bubbled the river. In the spring, as soon as the ice had gone, she had run to the river many times • during the day. And so—all through the summer. Her playmates had been the cowherd or Mirte from Lieknu farm. On the river bank she had found her first primroses and violets, later alders bloomed there and nightingales sang through the nights. On the banks of the Palade they had run wild like foals in spring when the school broke up. What racing there had been, what shouting! Then the whole world had been confined to Grivas and its boundary had been the horizon. At first, when in Riga, she had felt that all that was lovely had been left behind in Grivas. This feeling had lasted for years. Her thoughts were travelling the paths of her early youth and Narda had not noticed that the road had ended and that they were now crossing the meadow. Her foot caught on a mound and the rhythm of her steps and thoughts dispersed. She held on tighter to her mother\'s hand and let her lead the way. Only now she became aware of the roughness of her mother\'s hand. Turning her face towards her, she asked: "When are you going to stop working so hard?" "When you return home." "I shall not return. Grivas doesn\'t mean anything to me, don\'t save them for me!" "You are full of alien knowledge. Bread does not grow without sowing. I don\'t belong anywhere else except here!" Juste\'s reply was bitter. She did not like her daughter\'s words. It became difficult walking hand in hand and their conversation was strained and uneas\\. Narda let go of her mother\'s hand and walked behind her. She glanced around anxiously at the slightest sound and wrapped herself more tightly in her plaid. Noone must know of her visit this night. "Mother, don\'t tell anyone I came to see you tonight," Narda whispered. "1 shall not go around Grivas shouting, if your movements have to be so secret. Jekis is a light sleeper, he will probably have heard," replied Juste, carefully treading the path before her. "If only Jekis knows, then it does not matter so much. I haven\'t taken anybody\'s life." Juste\'s legs became unsteady. She stopped in the middle of the path and stared at her daughter\'s face. "You could have stayed away," she said, sharply. Narda stepped back a pace. No, she should not have come and shown herself to mother, but as she was opposite the Grivas road, it was as if strong hands had pulled her towards the house. The dog had recognized her immediately and brushed against her whining. She couldn\'t have borne leaving without seeing her mother. "We\'ll have to go, the train will soon be in," said Narda. Juste began to walk. Damp coldness was blowing from the tassels of her plaid. In silence Narda walked behind her. In the darkness ahead the river gurgled. The sound quieted her. At the river they would part and go each in their own direction without having found a mutual understanding. The path swiftly inclined downwards. Juste first, then Narda stopped at the water\'s edge. Above the dark water they could see the planks of a foot-bridge swaying. Narda took off her plaid and gave it back to her mother. "Give me the old one, mother. It would be a pity to discard this new one at the road-side." "Keep it, it\'s made of wool from our own sheep, it should be warmer than a bought one," said Juste, pretending not to have heard the bit about throwing it away. Narda wrapped the plaid tightly around her body. The old one would have made her look older and more unrecognizable, but she could not oppose her mother\'s will. "Farewell, mother, and don\'t think of me with bitterness." Narda bent forward and kissed her on the cheek. Juste shivered. Her daughter\'s lips were as cold as ice. But she lifted her roughened hands and caressed N\'arda\'s cheeks. "Go, if you have to go. You are my one and only, how could I forbid you?" Narda tore herself away and stepped on the planks. Having reached the other side she waved and then disappeared behind the alder bushes. Juste stood at the bridgehead like a dead tree stump. Cold shivers ran over her back, like insects. She had seen off all her loved ones. Her husband and her second daughter she had seen off to the cemtery, her oldest daughter to unknown foreign lands, her youngest to the capital. All that was left was Grivas. Grivas had taken away all her strength, but they took the place of a husband and three daughters. Narda had talked nonsense by asking her to stop working. It was like asking her to stop living. Juste was still staring towards the bushes across the river, but only darkness lay there. When she finally heard the train, she wrapped the plaid tighter around her shoulders and turned her back towards the planks. In vain she waited and deluded herself. The girl had other roads to travel. (iroping along the path with her feet she went back. The darkness was becoming less dense. A faint redness in the east proclaimed the morning. As if by magic the birds came to life. They twittered and sang each its own song. Dew hung over the grass and behind her Juste left a long dark trail. II From the region of the forest the sun stepped out and covered the world with a rosy glitter. Juste came into the yard. In the garden and in the trees the birds sang just as vigorously as they had done by the river. Juste sat down in front of the porch. The night was away and the day brought work and release. It was good to know that it was Sunday. The women would help her milk the cows and then they would return to their beds, she would be left alone, she would not need to share her thoughts with anyone. She sat there until the sun rose above the appletrees, then she went to call the maid and the cowherd. While milking, the calmness of the animals dispersed the last of the ill-feeling. Slowly the night visitor departed from her mind. Juste was straining the milk and giving orders to the cowherd and the dog in a loud voice. Jekis, having harnessed the horse, lifted milk cans into the cart and drove off to the dairy. The maid and the cowherd went to have their second sleep and Juste returned to the warm byre alone. The quiet chewing of the animals came from all corners and rose towards the beams and the empty swallow nests. Soon these agile birds would return and occupy their empty nests. The cattle would go out to their green pastures and the door of the byre would remain open until autumn. Juste was stroking the back of the nearest cow, then she turned and went out. The sun was up above the elms, yet she hadn\'t even thought about breakfast. At a trot, she hastened towards the house. Soon the chimney was smoking and the pot bubbling over the fire. When she heard the wheels of the returning cart, she began to lay the table. Having lifted out the cans and unharnessed the horse, Jekis came in and was the first to sit down at the table, waiting for the others. Juste called the others, brought in a big-bellied coffee-jug and sat down at the table at last. Jekis took two hasty sips of the hot drink, then put his hand in his pocket. "Look!" he said, producing a piece of paper from his pocket, "the roads are strewn with these leaflets this morning." He handed the leaflet to Kokis. The lad read it, then folded it up small. "Let me see it!" said Tince, stretching out her hand. "It\'s not for the likes of you!" said Kokis teasing, and hid it under the table. Juste was listening to all this with her eyes downcast, quietly eating her breakfast. A redness started to mount in her cheeks and mist clouded her eyes. How had Narda happened to be here in the night? She had said something mystically about having come with her hands full. Was not she. .. ? Suspicions about her daughter multiplied in her mind. She lifted up her head and quietly demanded: "What sort of leaflet is it? Let me see it!" Kokis gave it to her and betian to eat Juste was turning the leaflet over in her fingers, unable to sit still. She took an empty dish from the table and went to the kitchen. She put down the dish and hastened to her own room, wherein, after putting on her glasses she began to decipher the small print. She couldn\'t understand the meaning of all the words. Repeatedly there was mention of the workers, work and the exploiters, the overlords, the landowners. Juste read all of the printed matter and then threw the leaflet down on the table, exclaiming: "Villains!" Her daughter\'s night-roads stretched before her eyes, black as pitch. Narda, noone else had scattered these slanderous leaflets. What did she know about work, having grown up in great freedom ? She had never held the cowherd\'s switch in her hands, she did not know how sweet is the early morning sleep for a child who has to get up. She had never lifted a strand of meadow grass in the fields of Grivas, she did not know how to work for bread! But she knew how to write and how to talk! Juste squared her shoulders and lifted her head. Such words could only have the value or a mosquito bite. Grabbing the leaflet she crumpled it up and threw it in the flames of the kitchen stove. With her head held high she went to clear the table. Only Jekis had remained sitting, smoking his pipe. "Same sort of joke happened five years ago," said Jekis, "now again we have some mischief-makers tramping in the vicinity." "Are you still blabbering about the same thing? Makes me sick to listen to it!" Juste gave her brother-in-law an angry look. Jekis had read the leaflet on his way home from the dairy, but was that any reason why Juste had to be cross with him ? At the dairy nearly everyone had had a leaflet in his hands. Then he remembered the noise he had heard in the night and asked: "Who disturbed you last night?" Juste\'s hands stumbled against the edge of the table, a cup fell to the floor and broke. While picking up the broken pieces she was trying to think of a reply. She couldn\'t tell the truth — Jekis\' mouth wasn\'t locked with two keys. She threw the pieces away and said: "The dog was barking furiously, so I went out to see what\'s up. Saw a man standing in the yard. When he saw me he came up and asked the way to the station. I showed him and he went." Juste lied. Her eyes were averted and her voice did not carry the necessary conviction. Jekis was looking at her for a long while, shaking his head in disbelief. The dog had barked all right, but it hadn\'t been a furious bark and it had stopped soon. He had wanted to get up and investigate, but Juste had beaten him to it. He had heard no stranger\'s voice in the yard, yet his ears were good. Later he seemed to have heard voices in Juste\'s room, but sleep had soon overcome him. Juste was hiding something, her manner and gait revealed that. "Ach, we should have detained him! How could anyone not knowing the way wander into our house on such a night?" Jekis grumbled and hastily rose to discuss this news with Kokis. Juste\'s limbs relaxed, as if they had become untied. Jekis hadn\'t recognized the night visitor. She would be able to walk among her people with her head held high. No one would be able to point his finger at her. 11l People talked this way and that, but not being able to shed any light on the matter they soon discarded it. No one had seen any strangers walking about on that night. Juste went about with her head held high. Jekis hadn\'t revealed to anyone that she might have been lying. Narda had managed to sneak away unrecognized and unseen. After all, she had a right to visit Grivas in the middle of the night or noon if she pleased, it wasn\'t anybody else\'s business. For days and weeks Juste hadn\'t had much time to devote to thoughts of her daughter. Spring was advancing with great urgency and one job chased another. Having finished all the work in the house, Juste hastened to help in the fields. In the evenings she felt exhausted. She neither felt nor saw how the apple trees came to bloom, she did not hear how the nightingales began to sing on the banks of the river. The sun rose earlier every morning and set later every evening. Sitting at the table with her people she listened to their talk, but their conversation concerned only work and what went on in the near neighborhood. The night\'s events had submerged into forgetfulness. At noon Juste looked up at the sky and noticed the liveliness of spring all around her, but her heart did not warm with joy. During the afternoon her hands hurried over work and hard lines had set in her face. Days and weeks flowed on ahead, like the current of the river. The summer swelled, havings drunk its fill of the sun. One morning, having just returned from the dairy, Jekis stopped at the head of the cabbage furrow, where Juste was pulling up the fat weeds, and asked: "Aren\'t you coming to the house?" Juste\'s eyebrows shot up, but she did not lift her head as she replied: "If you have something to say, you can say it here." Jekis went up to her and placed his hand on her shoulder, but said no more. His strong calm won her over. She threw down the weeds, shook her apron and turned to go to the house. In the big room she sat down on Jekis\' bed and stared in front of her. "Here, read this!" Jekis placed a newspaper on her lap. "Can\'t read it without my glasses." Jekis hurriedly went to the other room and brought her glasses. "Read here!" He put his finger to a long column. Juste read, then stopped. It was an account of how the city people spent their weekends and holidays at the seaside, Forest Park and other places. "What\'s that to do with me? They can do as they like in Riga. I don\'t have holidays or Sundays," she said sulkily and let the paper drop on her lap. "If you don\'t want to read it, I\'m not going to force you. They live in all manners of ways in Riga, but among them is also your daughter, and it is about her r they w rite in this paper," Jekis said, not retreating a step. Juste bit her lips, then began to read again. With a great deal of determination she began to understand what she was reading. The article said that several men and women had been charged with antistate activities. Among them was also ... her daughter. Darkness drew over her eyes, her hands slid down by her sides and the paper fell under the bed. Jekis picked up the paper and left the room on tiptoes. Juste neither saw him nor heard him. Her head felt hot and painful. Narda, her sweet little girl, in prison! At home she had loved and spared every animal, every ladybird. At school she had been more advanced than the rest. But now? No sigh, no groan left Juste\'s lips. She remembered the spring night and hard spite circled her head. Her husband had fought for the freedom and honor of his country, and now his own daughter was opposing him with different ideals. Juste\'s eyes were burning, but no tears appeared. An empty heart was more painful than anything else. The sound of a banged door pulled her up on her feet. There was no one in the room. Sunrays played on the floor and flies buzzed around the white-scrubbed table. The sounds of day pushed her sharp thoughts to one side. Cares of the house sounded in her ears and forbade her to think. Only Grivas was left to her now and because of Grivas all else had to be let go over her head. She went to the kitchen, washed her eyes in cold water, then went out in the yard. In front of the granary Jekis and Kokis were lifting sacks of grain on to the cart. They were getting ready to go to the mill. The last of the flour had already been used up. "Cover up the load, it might rain!" Juste called out loudly. Jekis looked at her and shook his head slowly. How little strength remained in her, only her great will kept her going. The men covered the loads and drove away. Juste walked back and forth across the yard, not knowing what she was looking for. She couldn\'t think of a single job that might occupy her hands and her mind. At noon, when she was milking the cows, tears streamed into the bucket together with milk. She wasn\'t crying because she pitied her daughter, for those that had earned their sentence of judgement were not to be pitied. She was sorry for those who lay in the cemetery. The ways of her daughters might have turned out differently if their father had been there to stand by them with his advice. She had been too busy with work to watch Narda closely, when she should have shown more determination and used stronger words in her upbringing. Now she was empty-handed and would remain empty-handed. Juste finished milking and came out of the byre with reddened, but clearer eyes. She had reached an understanding with herself and would be readier to withstand any attacks made on her by others. Within a couple of days the whole parish was talking and the words buzzed and stung her like wasps. Juste did not feel the pain of these stings. She had never encouraged or supported her daughter in doing wrong. She also couldn\'t set up herself as her daughter\'s first judge. Meeting with Juste\'s calmness the rumors flattened like smoke in heavy weather. The gossipers\' tongues soon found other sources for discourse. The whole summer was spent in endless haste and hurry. Nights were spent in deathlike sleep, which neither dreams nor nightmares could disturb. With the first frost Juste received a letter from Narda. A neighbor, coming from the dairy one morning, brought it and gave it to Juste. "Here\'s a letter for you. Perhaps it\'s from your daughter. When you\'ve read it, tell us how she\'s getting on." He handed over the letter, then quickly turned his horse and drove away. From the granary came Jekis. "A letter, is it?" he asked. Juste\'s fingers were not used to handling paper, they trembled and slithered across the white envelope. At last she managed to get it open. "What\'s she writing?" Jekis couldn\'t contain his curiosity. "What could she write from a place like that?" Juste replied sharply and went to her room. Jekis stamped his feet for a while, then went. It couldn\'t be a good letter if his sister-in-law gave him such an icy answer. Reading the letter, Juste\'s lips narrowed even more. Her daughter wasn\'t troubled by shame or regret. Each word in her letter was as if hewn out of granite. Narda asked for woollen stockings, towels and sheets. Juste hid the letter in her pocket and on meeting Jekis again, she never mentioned the letter or her daughter. In her spare time she looked over the clothes in the wardrobe and tied up a few of them in a bundle. At the end of the week she ordered Jekis to harness the horse, then took the bundle and drove off to the post office. On her return she took off her best clothes and went about her work as usual —unfeeling, like a dead tree. She had responded to her daughter\'s request. The linen had been made of flax grown in their own fields, the woolens made of wool from the backs of their own sheep. And all the clothes had been her own handwork. These things weighed more in value than printed matter or words. She had not said a good word to her daughter when they had met, and she had said none now. Good words could not be picked up like pebbles on the river-bank. Narda herself had helped to demolish her feelings. She had built up Grivas with the help of strangers, she would take her last refreshment from the hands of strangers when her strength had failed. Amidst people Juste walked with her head held high. If anyone asked her about her daughter, she always had an answer ready. There were no round-about answers or lies. Because of her daughter she did not hide behind other people\'s backs. She bent her knees only on entering the church. (Translated by Monika Zariņa) Ēriks Dzenis — "The Pause" — Pastel P. Polna — "The Old Troll" — Bronze K. Langenfelds — "Still Life" — Oil A.
