Lieven A MAP OF LIVONIA', 'THE DAY WAS HOT, THE NIGHT WAS CLEAR (Translated from the Latvian by Ruth Speirs) He could not tear his eyes away from the ladybird which kept running up and down a blade of grass. Now and then it tried to take wing, but perhaps it was not really serious about it. A bird flew out of a crevice in the wall, described a few circles, and disappeared. A couple of thistles stood with bowed heads; dryness and heat—there was not a trace of moisture in the air—and a merciless blue sky had powdered the highroad with dust. Max was asleep. He had bent one leg at a sharp angle, the other was stretched across it, his folded arms protected his eyes. His shirt was open at the chest; it had evidently once been white—with tiny violet-colored flowers. Now those botanical adornments had faded, and its whiteness was that of the dust on the highroad. He had put his jacket under his head as a pillow. "Has it ever struck him what a curious thing a pillow is?" flashed through Klas\'s mind when he got bored with watching the ladybird and his thoughts turned to Max and his pillow. ANDREJS IRBE The last few days, Max had been rather odd. He did not show it in any particular way, except that his reticence had become even more marked. He was very quiet. "Yes." "No." "Perhaps." "I see." That was all, or practically all, Kias had heard from him in the last forty – eight hours. Yet they had been walking together for almost a hundred miles and. all things considered, that was no mean distance—especially in this heat which lay over the land like a curse. The vegetation by the roadside had also begun to suffer. The grass looked like hay though its roots were still in the ground, the thistles hung their purple heads and no longer appeared threatening; rather, they were a pitiful sight—such proud plants! As to the hedge parsley growing there—it wasn\'t anything much to look at, the tiny blossoms had shed their petals. Klas had always felt that the flower of the hedge parsley was the old symbol of Virtue—it was so dainty. clear, transparent—just like virtue. Yes, that\'s how it was: transparent. The ladybird finally spread its wings and flew away. "If I\'d put it on the palm of my hand," Klas thought, "I\'d have known which direction to take. Now I might go anywhere, for all the difference it makes." And there was Max, still asleep and perhaps it was just as well because it was easier to put up with a silent sleeper than with a silent companion when walking the highroad. But what was the matter with Max? Klas was not quite sure where he was going. He had struck up an acquaintance with Max when they both happened to drink milk on some farmstead. Later it emerged that Max had spent two, three days there. He had even helped with the farm work — hay-making came at the height of the heat. But then he had liad to leave. Gradually, Klas began to understand the reason for it. Max was a strong, vigorous man, and there had been the farmer\'s daughter and the servant-girl. The Devil is said to feed on flies when there is a famine, but those two had apparently been anything but flies, they\'d have been just as appetizing at feast times. At least, that was the impression Max gave when he talked about them. He would not commit himself into which apple he had bitten, but Klas had the uneasy suspicion that it had been both. They had drunk the jug of milk cooled in the water of the spring. Then Max had looked about him and, not seeing anyone near, had said: "I\'ll go, too." And that settled it. On the road, they battled with heat, dust, and sweat; they trudged over the dusty earth, took off their shoes and walked with bare feet, frequently dropping down to rest in the shade. Klas was not particularly interested what place Max was making for—he would much rather have been sure where he himself wanted to go. But since that day when he had luckily escaped a very disagreeable death his sole idea was to walk and walk and see the whole world while it lasted. Imagine what a nasty situation to get into: four machine-gun barrels staring at you full of solemn purpose! Klas burst into a sweat, merely thinking of it. One machinegun was bad enough—but four of them! There was no law and order in the country and, although there was no reason why anyone should want to shoot Klas, those four gave him such a hard stare that he instantly rolled across the verge of the road and fell crashing down to the river bank where brambles and willows grew— and there he put in a great spurt and very soon was somewhere far away. Thinking of it, Klas asked himself whether they had fired at him. He could not remember. He had got such a terrible fright that it never entered his head to listen whether they were firing or not. But he could still feel how cruelly the branches of those riverside bushes had torn at his clothes and skin—even now, he still had a scar on his cheek. The next misfortune had been his encounter with the girl. She had walked up to him and asked whether he was her father. What a ridiculous notion! Father, indeed! She seemed to be in her early twenties, and Klas himself was not yet thirty. But she obviously was not in her right mind. She smiled and said: "If you are my father, put out the light! Then I\'ll be your sweetheart. Ah, what a sweetheart ..." She came with him, regarding him with hungry eyes. Klas remembered some village philosopher who once said to him—they had been drinking cider brought up cool from the cellar—: "We\'re getting intoxicated, but this intoxication has been fermenting for millions of years. Ever since that Serpent. It\'s the apple that does it!" Klas glanced back at the crazy girl. But in her case the cider had gone bad. It was different with that other one—in the Garden of Eden. The cider there was sure to have been clear and sparkling. And Klas continued on his way, dreaming. Finally he heard the crazy girl murmuring behind him: "I see that you don\'t want me. I\'m too light for you, and you don\'t want to press me against the ground. Very well, I\'ll go up into the clouds!" After a while, when Klas looked back, she was gone. He began to feel sorry for her—and for himself, too. She\'d have become his sweetheart if he had said he was her father. Klas stopped for an instant, wondering whether to turn back and look for her. The whole place was deserted, devastated: there was no chance of finding any better cider. He suddenly felt very lonely. But then he clenched his teeth, and walked on. Afterwards, he sometimes regretted having walked away like that— especially in the evening dusk when he crawled into some shelter and tried to sleep. Most of the farmhouses stood empty, nobody wanted to live in that part of the country. Once he had even come across a freshly made bed in a farmhouse, – had crept into it and gone to sleep—and on that occasion, yes, it was particularly then, he had been overcome by a great longing for another human being. And in his mind he had seen the crazy girl standing before him, almost as though she were real, and he had longed for her to enter the room —even if she was as mad as she had been on the road. But now there was no longer anyone at all. About a week later, he met Max, and they had now been walking the highroads for ten days or so. At the beginning, Max had been talkative. He had told him many things, but Klas could not be bothered to listen. His old restlessness had set in again —and it did not improve matters to imagine what Max had been up to on that farmstead where people were still busying themselves with hay-making. But then Max had suddenly fallen silent. It happened when they were passing a signpost marking a district boundary. It was undamaged and clean—that is, if one discounted the birds which had used it as their perch. Max kept looking at it, walked up to it, stroked it—and fell silent. The only words he spoke were *yes\' an(l no-\' Again, there were no people anywhere. The farmhouses stood deserted. Here and there, a stray cow grazed in a field. They tried to catch one, Max himself had run in pursuit like the wind, but nothing came of it—the cow soon outstripped them both. They gathered spinach, but the plants did not appease their hunger. Still, Klas did not think that Max was silent because he was starving. They came to a farmhouse, Klas immediately started rummaging in closets and cupboards, but Max sat on a bench in the yard and did not lift a finger. When Klas put freshly made pancakes beside him—he had found some flour and lard, and there was plenty of water—Max devoured them with a healthy appetite, thanked Klas, and then relapsed into his former silence. Today the heat was quite unbearable, and they had flung themselves down in this spot here, not far from the road, in the shadow of a tree. About twenty paces from where they lay there was a wrecked building, birds had taken possession of it and were probably nesting in there. Max had been sitting a long time, motionless, staring into the valley which spread out in a westerly direction on the opposite side of the road. Then he suddenly tore off his jacket, folded it, and let himself drop down on it. Very soon, he was asleep. Klas did not feel drowsy even though the heat and the mid-day peace and quiet prompted him to sleep. He sat there and tried to remember something, but in the end he did not succeed in that either. For a moment he thought of the crazy girl, but her image vanished as quickly as a drop of water on dry sand. And now Max had already been asleep for a whole hour. Klas quietly pulled a cheap silver watch out of his pocket, which he had picked up in some farmhouse. He looked at it. It was ticking. The only trouble was that Klas did not know if it showed the right time. Still, it did not matter. He was not tied down to any particular schedule, any particular time, and it was immaterial what the watch registered. The ticking of the silver toy merely showed that time had not stopped. But this fact could be told also from sunrise and sunset—they followed each other the same as before. The shadows were already beginning to lengthen a little, and Klas glanced somewhat impatiently in Max\'s direction. "Sleeping like a log," he thought, "just as if he\'d come back to his own home." But he felt it would be unpardonable to try and wake his companion—after all, they were in no hurry to get anywhere. The sack Klas had thrown down near by contained provisions for at least two days. They could easily stay where they were now. Suddenly—at the same moment as Max gave a snort in his sleep and stretched out the leg he had folded under him—-shoes sounded somewhere in the distance, and Klas plainly heard the whine of a whole swarm of bullets passing over their heads. He flattened himself against the ground and tugged at Max\'s sleeve: "Wake up! There\'s firing!" But Max was already awake and replied with surprising calm: "Yes, they usually do that here. They must have spotted something. Be quiet!" "What?" Klas was astounded. "You know about it?" "Of course I do, Klas." Max spoke in a half-whisper. "Don\'t you know what place we\'ve been making for?" Klas had given it no thought. He tried to raise his head, but at the same moment another volley swished over them. "Damn it all," Klas thought, for the first time in a temper, "that\'s what comes of it when one doesn\'t stop to think where one\'s going! Max probably knows, though." "Max," he said, endeavouring to sound calm, even cheerful. "Max, tell me where we\'ve got to!" "Close to the frontier," his companion said briefly. "As a matter of fact, we\'re in no man\'s land." "And you were making for this place?" "Yes—weren\'t you, too?" Max looked at him in evident surprise. "I never bothered to think where I was going, Max. I just kept on walking. Did you have some definite objective?" "Objective—yes," Max sighed. "But I\'ll never reach it, it\'s where the firing comes from." "What the hell are you doing here, then?" Klas flew into a rage. "I wanted to have a look," Max replied in a hushed voice. He unfolded his jacket and extracted an old pair of binoculars from an inner pocket. "You\'re the perfect tourist!" Klas sneered, and his anger mounted with every minute. All was quiet around them; he congratulated himself on the fact that they had dropped down in a kind of hollow when looking for shade, but then he remembered that it had been Max who found this place and that he himself had only followed his lead. Evidently, Max had had a purpose in coming here. "What do you want to do with that thing?" Klas asked, prodding the binoculars with a grubby finger which terminated in a long and dirty nail. "I\'ve already told you—l want to have a look." "I think you\'re crazy," Klas observed and was instantly sickened by his own forceful pronouncement. Everything was silent again, but now—yes, only now— Klas realized that he could not afford to peep across the edge of the hollow in which he was lying. In his imagination, the barrels of the four machine-guns suddenly blossomed before him like black flowers. "Klas," Max whispered, leaning towards him and staring him straight in the face. "Klas—aren\'t you, too? Aren\'t you mad?" Klas burst into a sweat. Max\'s eyes were strangely dark. "Well, yes—a little," he changed his tone, trying to sound amiable. He thought of Max\'s peculiar silence, and it even struck him as odd that Max had so readily come away with him after they had drunk milk on the farm. Max leaned yet more closely towards him. "Klas, you\'re lying. You aren\'t mad. You\'re only pretending!" What a situation! "Don\'t worry, Max, calm yourself," Klas said and felt the sweat trickling into his eyes. "Yes," Max sighed, "you aren\'t mad. What a pity. I thought you were—l thought so all the time. But then I began to have my doubts. Now it\'s clear that you can\'t enter my kingdom. Except," he plucked a blade of grass and started to suck it, pensively gazing at Klas, "except if 1 were to make you mad. Don\'t be scared," he added when he saw Klas shrinking back, "it isn\'t so terrible as you think! One skilful blow over the head, in the right place—and you\'ll be wearing royal robes. Just like myself. How stupid of me not to have noticed before that you have none of the royal purple in which I am clad!" Klas surreptitiously tried to crawl to the edge of the hollow. He recalled his leap dowm to the river-bank and hoped he would be no less agile now. "Don\'t sneak away," Max growled. "Remember—they\'ll shoot you! Didn\'t 1 tell you we are in no man\'s land? Anything may happen here." Klas stopped short. True enough. But what if he were very quick and careful? Max reached out a hand and took hold of Klas by the shoulder, jerking him back. Klas strained every muscle in his body, trying to break free, but Max held him as though in a vice. He expected a blow, and his eyes filled with tears. "Max, don\'t hit me," he begged. "I really am mad!" "You aren\'t mad at all—if you were you\'d have known where you were going," his companion said quietly and slightly relaxed his grip on him. His eyes still had that dark glitter. All of a sudden he sternly demanded, giving Klas a hard stare: "What have you done to my sister?" "Your sister? I\'ve never seen her .. . I didn\'t even know you had a sister!" "And those fingernails, look, those marks," Max touched Klas\'s scarred face. "Who did that? Angels?" "Max, Max," Klas pleaded, "I got scratched, running away from people who were after me." "Now, you see," Max said, releasing him, "you see—and you tried to tell me lies. You tried to persuade me that you\'re mad—but you\'re on the run! A madman doesn\'t run away. He walks erect, his head held high; he doesn\'t stoop even if the golden bar on the Gates of Glory brushes against his hair." "And what about yourself?" Klas suddenly asked. "I think you\'re talking nonsense. Where are you going with your head held high? What\'s more, I think that you, too, are running away—from that farmstead. And you said something about the farmer\'s daughter and the servant-girl there. Isn\'t it from them you are running away?" "I\'m going where I please, and you aren\'t likely to come with me. After all. you\'re a tramp, and that\'s worse than being mad. As to myself, I am mad, I\'ll have you know! It\'s of no importance whether I really am mad or not—nobody bothers to differentiate these days. It\'s all settled by those who do the locking up. Nowadays everything has to be worked out in reversed equations." "Eg ... quations?" "Oh well, of course you wouldn\'t know what that is, would you. Silence! Quiet! If one is mad one\'s mad—very simple, isn\'t it? It\'s a very different matter if one asks who is not mad. That\'s something nobody can know for sure. Such is the way things are with us these days." Klas did not understand anything any more. Neither did he attempt to run away. Pressed close against the edge of the hollow, he tried to make himself as inconspicuous as possible. He carefully watched every one of Max\'s movements so that he could take flight at a moment\'s notice. Now that he had been freed from his companion\'s merciless grip he began to hope again that he might escape after all. But he could not afford to take any chances. Klas saw that the evening was drawing near; they had spent a long time talking, and the shadows had grown considerably longer. "I came here just to have a look," Max suddenly broke the silence, and his tone had become peaceful, almost gentle—he seemed to be talking in a happy dream. "You wouldn\'t understand," he went on, "you seem to walk the highroads with no aim in view—l don\'t really know why you do it. But perhaps it\'s of no consequence. There are all kinds of people in the world. I myself have a definite destination, but I won\'t be able to reach it. All I want is to have a look, and this in itself would be sufficient proof that I\'m mad. Besides, on the farm where we met, I could not rid myself of the urge to level everything to the ground. I only spent four days there, and every evening I looked at those people who stored up their clover as winter fodder for the cows, I looked at them and had the urge to round them all up and set fire to them. But, first of all—this—" with an eloquent gesture, he drew his hand across his throat. Klas again felt the perspiration breaking out all over his body. Now he was quite sure that his companion was not in his right mind. But he tried to appear perfectly calm, and asked casually: "Why did you want to do it?" "As if I knew!" Max replied. This did not make things any clearer, but Klas persevered, and asked: "Max, what exactly is it you want to do? Try to put everything into one straightforward answer! After all, we\'ve been walking the roads together for quite a while. It really was all the same to me where I was going, and that is why it never struck me that you might have some, definite destination in view. Now, as far as I can make out, you seem to be almost within reach of it. True enough, I\'m no good at finding my way about this place. But I do know that we\'ve come to the area which was laid waste in the war, which is not going to be built up again, and which has a new frontier running across it. I know that we\'ve been here for quite a time—it was obvious from those deserted farmhouses. But I thought that you, too, were wandering about in this place in order to keep it alive and free: you know as well as I do that life and freedom are neither on the one side nor the other—they are only in the devastated areas to which nobody wants to return. If one manages to elude the armed patrols— and that\'s what I\'ve been doing all along —one can be as free as a bird here. But now I realize that for the last few days you\'ve been leading me straight towards the frontier. True, there is even greater freedom here because there isn\'t a single human being anywhere, and there are still a few things to be found in the deserted farm buildings. But now we are squatting in this hole in the ground and daren\'t raise our heads for fear that the shooting might start again. And, who knows, perhaps somebody will come to look for us. —I, for one, can\'t see any sense in this at all!" "There\'s sense in it if one\'s mad," Max replied. Suddenly he smiled: "Come on, don\'t be frightened! I won\'t hit you over the head—it\'s empty! But I won\'t let you go either. You\'ll have to come with me, to look . . ." "Look at what?" Max took Klas by the hand and drew him to the edge of the hollow topped by the wilted, drooping thistles. "Have a look," he urged him. "There\'s quite a large building over there. It used to be a school. From there, one can see the farmstead which once was my home. It\'s about three hundred yards across the frontier. I want to have a look at it. Who knows —some of my people might still be there. We have to get to the school building in the night, then we can sit in the attic all day and have a good look. Tomorrow night we\'ll come back. If we are still alive." "But there\'s no reason for me to go there. Why should I ?" Klas protested. "Why? I might begin to feel bored, sitting there all day under that hot tin roof. We\'d be able to talk about things, about life ... It will be quite pleasant. The frontier is only about fifty yards from the corner of the building." Klas feverishly cudgelled his brain, trying to find a way out of his predicament. "Don\'t think of running away," Max said quietly. "If you do you\'ll certainly remain here. But if you come with me there\'s a chance that you\'ll be able to get back again." "What about you?" "Yes—perhaps I\'ll come back, too. I\'d like to cross over, though." "Cross the frontier?" "Why not? I\'m mad. I know no frontiers!" \' Klas had to resign himself to his fate. They went on sitting in the hollow, and the dusk gradually enveloped the tree above them. Cool air seemed to stream from the darkening sky itself—it could not rise from the ground, the earth was overheated. A light breeze stirred in the tree, and when the sun went down Klas began to feel sleepy. Max had had all the sleep he needed, but Klas had been sitting up in the heat, watching a ladybird. About an hour later, a big yellow moon appeared through a gap in the derelict wall. The twilight of the summer night was suffused with its pale glow, there was no real darkness. At last Max took Klas by the hand and said: "Let\'s go now. Or, rather, let\'s crawl through the grass. The night is too clear for walking upright." There was nothing Klas could do but obey. His eye momentarily caught sight of a large knife in Max\'s hand. It had a bright, sharp glitter in the moonlight. They crossed the overgrown grassy slope and the road without mishap. On the other side, trees and bushes afforded them some shelter as they made their way towards the school building half a mile farther down the slope. Finally they reached the gravelled courtyard of the school. The old, empty basin of a fountain, and its statue, cast a long shadow; the main door reflected the moonlight in its ornamental glass panels, it stood slightly ajar, and dense darkness seemed to stream out into the open from the interior of the building. "Come!" Max whispered and dragged Klas after him across the courtyard. The last vestige of courage deserted Klas, but there was nothing for it—he had to follow. The gravel seemed to crunch inordinately loudly under his feet, and he thought he saw the barrel of a rifle pointing at him from every one of the bushes surronding the courtyard. At last they reached the main entrance. Max gave the door a push with his foot, and it opened, creaking noisily. Klas\'s back was damp with cold sweat. They entered the semi-dark building; it smelt of emptiness and neglect. A shaft of moonlight from the window lit up a black notice board on the opposite wall, with a few bits of paper still pinned to it. Two overturned chairs lay in a corner, and the floor was encrusted with mud which had dried in the heat. "Let\'s go upstairs!" Max said. Though he did not speak particularly loudly he could hardly be said to be whispering. "I\'ll show you where I used to sit and learn the multiplication tables." Klas followed. He did everything he was told, he had no strength left to protest. They walked along a passage on the second floor, and the worn-out wooden boards creaked noisily underfoot. Klas inwardly said goodbye to life. They were sure to be discovered, and then everything would very quickly be over.—But nothing happened. "Come," Max said, and there was a velvety gentleness in his voice. "I\'ll show you something — something that is the rason for my coming here." They were at the end of the passage, at the point where it made a sharp turn and led to a small balcony. Klas could not control himself any longer: "Let\'s not go out on the balcony! If we haven\'t yet been sighted let\'s at least keep out of the line of firing!" "Do you really imagine you\'ll get away from here alive?" Max asked sarcastically. "But you promised ..." "Don\'t be a fool! We\'re not leaving this place. We\'ll stay here, near my former home, until we die. You\'ll help me pass the time. Listen," Max suddenly stopped and gazed at Klas in the semi-darkness, "don\'t close my eyes for me if I should die before you. I want to go on looking at my people\'s farm. But what am 1 to do about you?" "Let me go," Klas begged him. "You don\'t know how to get back. I was able to bring you here because I\'ve been here before. I know the way. The whole garden behind the school is mined." "Oh Lord ..." Klas breathed. "He won\'t help you!" "Max," Klas pleaded once more, "take me back! After that, you can stay here if you like. I understand you ..." "How can you understand if you don\'t know anything! Come, I\'ll show you something. I\'ll tell you the whole story. There\'s tomorrow, then there\'s the day after tomorrow ... If I die first you can make your way back. But will you still want to?" "Max, please ..." "Don\'t be a fool, come!" Max dragged Klas towards the balcony and slowly opened its white door. There was a sickening smell, and Klas caught a glimpse of shadowy forms in a corner of the balcony, like shapeless sacks thrown down there. "What\'s that?" he whispered despairingly. "My people. Those who were on this side of the frontier. Nobody must ever desert his home—the least he can do is stay near it! I brought them here. When they didn\'t want to come I used this on them," Max pressed his knife against Klas\'s chest, "like that—and then I carried them here. Now I\'ve come here myself, and I\'ll stay. My friend," he added dreamily, "you\'ll stay, too. It won\'t hurt ... not really ..." "But ..." Klas did his best to appear calm although he could barely control his panic. "Max, I was to keep you company here! You said yourself that you\'d get bored." "Yes, that\'s true. It must have slipped my memory." Max brushed his hand across his forehead. "But promise me one thing—stay on, afterwards! If you go running back you\'ll get in among those mines, and that will be the end of you. But here you\'ll live for ever.—Did you hear that?" "Hear what?" "Marta\'s calling ..." "Marta?" "Yes—little Marta. Oh, you don\'t know, of course! Cornel" He drew Klas after him along the passage with its creaking floorboards. Max opened the door to a room in which there were two rows of school desks squatting like strange birds in the semi-darkness of the summer night. A carafe, though now empty, was still standing on the teacher\'s table. The teacher\'s chair was gone. "There!" Max pointed to the last desk but one. "I was sixteen years old and she was fourteen. At first we didn\'t even know how enjoyable it could be ..." "But who\'s been calling you?" Klas talked for the sake of talking, he made no attempt to listen to what Max was saying. His sole idea was to play for time. "Marta. I\'ve already told you." "But she isn\'t here!" "How dare you talk such nonsense?" Max suddenly roared and flung himself at Klas. With the agility of desperation, Klas drew back and rushed out of the classroom, slamming the door shut. He heard Max thrusting the whole weight of his body against it, but at the same moment Klas\'s hand, clutching the door handle, came into contact with the cool, oval shape of a key in the lock beneath. Instinctively his hand turned the key. Once, twice. The door was securely fastened. For an instant, Klas stood motionless, as if paralysed. Max\'s shouts behind the door subsided, and Klas heard his half – frightened whisper: "Dear little Marta, I won\'t do it again!" Klas turned, and took to his heels. He almost ran past the long stairway, doubled back, and rushed down the stone steps in the darkness, three at a time. Outside, he stopped short. Everything was quiet in the house. Suddenly he was overcome by a strange, uncontrollable curiosity. With a stealthy, cat-like tread, he approached the bushes bordering the courtyard, and parted the wilting branches of an acacia, cautiously moved half a step forward — and saw a stretch of open ground, with a white farmhouse, brightly lit up by the moon, at its farther end. Everything was silent there, and even at that distance he could see that the doors were hanging from their hinges and that the window-panes had either tumbled out of their own accord or had been shattered by intruders. There was nothing to look for there, the house was obviously empty and deserted, robbed of even the smallest trifles. Klas let the branches slowly drop back into their former position, and listened. Silence. It flashed through his mind that it was cruel to abandon Max to his fate, but then he thought of the knife in the man\'s hands and the merciless grip in which Max had held him that afternoon, up there, on top of the slope. He hurried across the courtyard and, trying to remember the way by which he had come, cautiously retreated in the direction of the highroad, keeping in the shadow of the trees. He bounded across the stretch of dusty open ground, and himself into the hollow beneath the tree. The place was in deep shadow, and he groped despairingly, with trembling hands, for the sack of provisions. When his fingers contacted its rougii surface he picked it up and rose to his feet. In the shadow of the tree-trunk, he hurriedly made his way towards the derelict wall. Suddenly, in the bright moonlight, he saw a human form standing there, one hand resting against the wall and the other reaching out towards him. Klas instantly recognized the crazy girl of whom he had been thinking when he walked the empty roads of this deserted region, alone, and wishing that at least she were near him. "Max," whispered the crazy girl, "now you may do it! Now I won\'t cry any more ..." Never in his life had Klas run so fast as he was running now. When Klas woke up it was hot again, and the sun beating down on him had dulled his senses. He had dropped down in the corner of a meadow which was full of mounds and hollows, and had slept the sleep of the dead. Unable to rouse himself, he drowsily gazed at the long grass and the colored insects scurrying among it. A gaily-dotted little ladybird crawled up to the top of a blade of grass, rose into the air, and flew away. Klas sat up, feeling hungry. He untied the sack, extracted from it a hunk of bread wrapped in a newspaper, and started to eat. While he was chewing the bread his glance idly traversed the large black headlines on the crumpled paper. They spoke of peace, and of friendship among men. What does the author say about his short story: "The Day was Hot, The Night was Clear?" "In this short story you might see people, who in varying degrees are abnormal. You are mistaken. All of them are quite normal, but cast in abnormal times. What can be more normal than Max longing for his home, return to it? But he is the craziest in this story\'s plot of few characters. Why?— Because his home lies beyond the frontier—forbidden to rross. No, with this dividing line I have not meant anything factual, it can lie in any old place: one day in one area, the other—somewhere else; such restraining zones are fund in our everyday life, in our relations with other people."']

Representative_Docs: ['Two distant streams of musical activity are to be traced in the development of Latvian music. The first is the cultivation of the inherited and immensely rich and varied folk music; the second is tfye influence of the Modern Western music, especially its latest schools of the 19th century: romanticism and the main national schools in the other European countries. The close affinities of both these factors is the principal characteristic of modern Latvian music. Therefore we do not notice the conflicts between the sacred and secular music in Latvian music at its historic beginning which were so peculiar in the medieval period in Western Europe; for the same reason Latvian music could develop and grow up in the shortest possible time without much wasted effort or time. Different schools and styles of music in Western Europe gained popularity and became outdated while the Latvian nation, being isolated from the outside world, could do nothing but cultivate the cultural treasure of its own—the admirable folksongs, which were a direct expression of the people\'s common emotions. There is also one reason why the treasure box of the folk songs of the Latvians is so particularly rich both in quality and quantity. There are about 900,000 folksongs, including* numerous variants, collected, compared and systematized by the Institute of Folklore. These folksongs are known as tautas dziesmas or dainas. More than 18,000 melodies have been preserved. Qualitatively they express themselves by a rhythmical richness, delicate style and old tonality, which can be traced ANCIENT LATVIAN FOLK MUSIC by VALENTĪNS BĒRZKALNS The ancient Latvian musical instrument—Kokle. back to the ancient Greek tone system. Moreover, the Latvian folksongs are of great importance as an abundant source of the ancient Latvian religion, traditions and customs. Even the history of the nation is reflected in those little songs. A great many songs perished during the centuries because they were handed down by word of mouth, this being the only wavy to save them and deliver them to the next generations. However, a quantity of them is still alive, and are sung at home and in school, in different festivities as well as in social gatherings, in spite of the strong influence of the modern popular song "hits". Less important and less preserved is the ancient instrumental music. The principal instrument of music of the ancient Latvian was kokle, related somewhat to the medieval dulcimer or hackbrett, essentially being an entirely original and different form known only in Northern Europe. It was used for accompanying the singers and dancers. The origin of it dates back to the centuries even before the Middle Ages. We can find some samples in the museums—instruments one and a half to seven feet long, which consist of broad and long excavated boxes of fir or linden tree, with the one end narrower than the other, and a couple of holes in the top for better acoustics. The kokle was furnished with five to twelve strings, tuned diatonically, and stretched from an iron bar on the one end to wooden plugs on the other end. The player produced the tones by plucking the strings with the forefinger of his right hand,- and muffled the discordant sounds with his left hand. Another ancient instrument was the so-called eglite, about one and a half feet long made of a barked fir tree with little sounding pendants on its branches. The more perfected form was called the trideksnis, furnished with special copper pendants. Both instruments were used for rhythm and can be regarded as the predecessors of the conductor\'s baton. Bag-pipes, drums, horns, wooden trumpets, whistles and fiddles were also Latvian Minstrels in National Costumes. used by the ancient Latvians, but they are not as characteristic of the country as the kokle or eglite mentioned above It is evident that vocal music was most characteristic among the Latvians. One can divide the folksongs into two different groups—the so-called recitative songs and the solo songs. Melodies belong to one or the other group, according to their formal structure, text, and performance. The recitative songs are the oldest ones. Some of them belong to the pre-historic period, thus being in existence long before* the establishment of Christianity in Latvia. A great many of them disappeared after they lost their primary meaning. The recitative melodies were sung in a choir. One voice (teicēja) led the tune, reciting the first two lines of the verse, then the other voices (locitajas) took over the melody and "drew" it, while a third group (vilcējas) chose one of the vowels (usually "c") keeping it in the same pitch until the end of the melody thus giving an impression of organ point of modern harmony. Sometimes another singer, a so-called sijātajā or sikalina sang another melody higher than the main tune. Such singing reminds us of an improvised polyphony and proves that the polyphony is not an invention of the sacred music. The same kind of singing is also evidenced in some other North European countries. The recitative songs did not have a strict unchangeable text, but depended upon the occasion and place where it was sung. The "Ligo Songs", the favorite of the age-old folk songs, were sung on the eve of Midsummer Day, and have survived to the present time. So also did other songs sung on special occasions at different seasons of the year. Many of them are connected with various rites and customs at wedding, child birth, folk festivals and funerals. A great number of these songs praise the work and describe different activities at home and in the field. The rhythmic structure of this first group is simple. Two-four time is predominant. The text of these songs, as mentioned above, is changeable, depending on what the singer is singing about, but there are some strictly unchangeable refrains, like "ligo" for Ligo Songs, "rota" for Spring Songs, etc. These refrains usually consist of one or two bars. A basic form of the melody is made of one musical period expressed in eight bars, sometimes also abbreviated, but more frequently extended to twelve or more bars. The songs are pretty avaricious by nature as concerns the melodies. We can find some tunes consisting of two tones only, but dominating tunes consist of five to six tones, seldom extending to an octave. However, with this scanty material the old singers could produce beautiful and impressive songs. The dominating part of the songs of this group are conceived in the Dorian and Aeolian modes, more seldom in the lonian, Lydian and Mixolydian ones. It is also characteristic that they are entirely devoid of the artificial introductory note, which is evidence that they are older than those of the 17th century. The other group of folksongs, the so-called solo dainas, were usually sung by soloists only. If they appeared in a choir, they were sung in unison. Rhythmically they are more rich than the recitative songs. The same applies to their melodies. Most of them were developed in the last centuries. The text of these songs, in opposition to the other group, is strictly unchangeable. It consists of many four-line verses about some particular events, expressing the emotions of love, joy, about the beauty of nature, satisfaction at work, etc. Many of these songs are exclusively lyric by nature. A careful student can also find fragments of the balladic or epic elements among the songs belonging to this group. The majority of the songs of this group is conceived in the lonian, Aeolian and Mixolydian modes. The Phrygian and Dorian modes were used less frequently. Lydian modes are almost untouched. The range of the melodies of this group is more extended than that of the other group. Predominating tunes consist of an octave, but we can find melodies consisting of ten to fourteen tones. Many of these songs are composed so skillfully and beautifully, that they can compare with the themes of the art songs of any time; therefore they play an important role in the works of the modern Latvian composers. The same can be said about the rhythm of these songs. We find many kinds of measures, most common are those with the regular five-four and seven-four time, and irregular mixed time. The characteristic of all the Latvian folksongs is that they start on the strong beat only. The melodies of the second group of songs are also richer in their forms. Basically made up of eight bars, they vary in their structure, sometimes abbreviated, sometimes extended. The extensions are made by repeating a part of some bars or including interjectory words without any meaning, as "aijāja", "trallalla", "raidairidira" and the like. The first account of the Latvian folksongs dates back to 1632, being found in the printed work of Menius, professor of the University of Tartu (Sintagma de Origine Livonorum). The real renaissance of the folksongs began about 200 years later, when the publications of Johann G. Herder, concerning the influence of folksongs in the culture of the nations, gained wide popularity in different countries. The first attempt to make a systematic collection and studies of the Latvian folk music was shown by Janis Cimze (1814-1881), director of the Latvian teachers\' college at Valmiera. His collection "Dziesmu Rota", published in 1873, gave stimulus to further studies of Latvian folk music on a broader scale. The songs of his collections were harmonized and thus formed the first national repertoire of the Latvian choirs. The most important authority of Latvian folk music was Andrejs Jurjans (1856-1922), the first Latvian composer academically educated. Being a graduate of three different departments of the Conservatory of St. Petersburg, he was a distinguished scientist of the theory of music and an active musician himself. His principal work, "Materiaux pour la musique populaire Latavienne", 1894, consists of 1200 folksongs scientifically collected and interpreted. He was the first scientist who formulated the theoretical principles of Latvian vocal music in analyzing the tonality and harmony of the folksongs, and emphasizing the principles of a diatonic characteristic of these songs. Further studies of the Latvian folksongs were contributed by the composer Emilis Melngailis (b. 1874) and Professor Jēkabs Graubins (b. 1886). However, a complete systematization and publication of the Latvian folk music still must be done. Especially needed are publications and interpretations in foreign languages. The general events of the musical life of Europe were reflected in Latvia since the 13th century. With the introduction of Christianity the church immediately made every effort to strengthen its position among the indigenous people and thus introduced the sacred music to the Latvian audience. However, the sacred music evidently did not affect the Latvian folk music and both forms existed separately until the modern time. From 1682 to 1698 the Duke of Kurzeme, Friedrich Casimir, supported the first Italian opera and court ballet in his capital Jelgava. In 1772, the German opera theater in Riga opened its doors to the audience. From 1837 to 1839 the famous German composer, Richard Wagner, was a conductor of this opera theater and composed his opera Rienzi. Some other German, Russian, French, Finnish musicians spent a shorter or longer time in Latvia. Tied up by their political and economical situation the majority of the Latvians could not play an active role in the art music. Therefore we can assume that the first half of the 19th century was the real beginning of the purely Latvian art music, when the first Latvian choirs made their public appearances under the leadership of the native conductors. The folksong regarded as musical nonsense by their former dictators of musical life in Latvia proved their vitality throughout centuries and did much to promote cultural renovation and new trends in Latvian music. The first General Song Festival held in the summer of 1873 is to be considered the most important event in the natural incline of Latvian music to the musical life of Western Europe. It was the beginning of a series of song festivals which became a fertile ground for the future development of Latvian music and the wide popularization of the musical treasures among the population. In order to illustrate the importance of the song festivals as a growing and attracting musical event among the people we shall mention a few data. The first song festival in 1873 took place with 1,003 singers participating. The number of singers of the last song festival in independent Latvia had increased to 16,000. The audience also increased proportionately, reaching the number of 100,000 in 1938, which means that every twentieth inhabitant of the country took part in this unusual celer bration of Latvian song. Simultaneous with the growth of the festivals, native composition suddenly began to display increased vitality. However, regular and active developments in all branches of music could take place only after 1918, when the independence of Latvia was reestablished. The leading factors of the musical life became the National Opera and the State Conservatory founded in 1919, and the State Broadcasting Corporation, founded in 1925. The first Latvian Opera .was founded as a private enterprise in 1912 ,', 'SVEŠVALODU NODAĻA OTHER LANGUAGES SECTION LATVIAN MUSIC CAMP IN OGRE: "INTERACTION OF MUSIC STYLES OF DIFFERENT PERIODS – A NEW SIT FOR THE CONTEMORARY MUSIC IN EASTERN EUROPE On July 8 – 18, 1998 the 3rd Summer Music Camp took place, in Ogre, Latvia on the site of the Ogre Music School. The camp was entitled "111 Starptautiskā latviešu jauno mūziķu radoša nometne" (\'Third International Music Camp for Young Latvian Musicians") and had the supplementary title "Dažādu laikmetu mūzikas stilu mijiedarbība" ("Interaction of Music Styles of Different Periods). The camp was directed by Ingrida Gūtberga and Dace Aperāns (or Aperāne, to use the Latvian feminine ending for proper names). Dace Aperāns is a composer of Latvian descent, born in Canada (where her parents emigrated after the Soviet takeover of the three Baltic countries). She studied composition at McGill University in Montreal with Brian Cherney and Bruce Mather and then moved to New York, where she studied at Mannes College and Hunter College in New York. Mrs. Aperāns has had numerous performances of her compositions in the USA and Canada. Since 1989 she has been frequently visiting Latvia and contacting numeroues Latvian composers. Since that time, her compositions have been frequently performed in Latvia, most recently a ballet based on the life of Edith Piaf, entitled "Edīte"("Edith"), commisioned by the Latvian Opera, and she has been actively promoting the music of Latvian composers, such as Pēteris Vasks, Pēteris Plakidis and others, inactivating performances of their works by American soloists or ensembles. In July 1994 she arranged the first summer music camp in Jaungulbene, I.atvia, which brought many musicians from all over Latvia as well as a number of Latvian Americans and Canadians, which featured courses, lessons, lectures and seminars by prominent instrumentalists, composers and performers. Since then the camp has been held in Ogre, small town in  about 50 minutes away on the train from Riga, every two years, the second camp took place in July, 1996 and the third happened in July, 1998. Dace Aperāne has been the artistic director for these three camps, and has been actively bringing together all of the best Latvian musicians to take a creative part in the camp. The 104 music students and 40 musicians came not only from Riga but from the whole country, many of them teachers in the Latvian Music Academy (i. e. Conservatory) named after Jāzeps Vītols in Riga, as well as in many music colleges throughout Latvia. The Camp was sponsored by the Soros Foundation, the British Council, the Riga Early Music Center, the Latvian Cultural Ministry and the Latvian Cultural Fund as well as by the Ogre Dome or City Hall and the Ogre Music School. In addition, a private donor, Brigitta Greenwood, a Latvian-American donated a substantial sum to the music camp. Also, all of the main Latvian orgaqnisations in Canada and the USA donated funds to the music camp; about 15 in all. Latvia, one or the three Baltic States which have obtained their independence from the Soviet Union in August 1991, is appearing on the scene of contemporay music with a number of interesting composers, who have started out their compositional career in the last few decades of the Soviet Union (after the cultural "thaw" of the 1960\'s when more unofficial writers, artists and composers were given a little more breathing space for their art in the Soviet Union) and who are now, after the demise of the Soviet Union, making their way into the international contemporary music scene, their music becoming more available in concert halls and CD stores in Europe and America. One of the most prominent composers in Latvia is Pēteris Vasks, who has had a rapidly increasing recognition as a composer, first among the unofficial composers in the Soviet Union (especially by such c<#nposers as Edison Denisov and Alfred Schnittke in Moscow) and then in the Western countries. He has made his\' USA debut in 1989 when Thomas Stacy performed his Oboe Concerto. In Europe where his music ir frequently performed and recorded on CD s, most of his compositions are published with Schott Publications. Other composers are also gradually coming out onto the international scene, including Pēteris Plakidis, Imants Zemzaris, Selga Mence, Imants Kalniņš and Maija Einfelde. Many of their works are performed in Germany as well as in the Scandinavian countries – Norway, Denmark, Sweden and Finland, with which they are developing a steadily increasing connection. Many Latvian composers have a very unique style of music, which can loosely be termed as Neo-Romantic, a lot of which features long, static tonal harmonies and a unique approach to texture and instrumentation, often connected to the organ tradition and religious musical genres. Many of the latvian composer\'s works, though tonal in harmony, can sound rather avant-garde in texture and instrumental usage and thus establish a connection to some avant-garde trends in European music as well as some of the avant-garde Russian composers, with whom the I.atvian composers were in close connection with when both countries were part of the Soviet Union. Some of this music remotely contains elements of minimalist technique, due to the static tonal harmonies, nevertheless their usage of the latter is totally different and distinct from the American minimalists Glass, Reich and Riley, due to the more emotional quality of the  composers\' music and in their case the minimalist technique remains a side feature and never becomes the central element of their work. Latvian music is also distinctive for its unique folk choral tradition, which many contemporary Latvian composers keep up, contributing to it with imaginative pieces and modifying it to suit their individual styles. A few Latvian composers also take up techniques from the European avant-gard tradition, sometimes concentrating on them fully and somtimes blending them with the Neo-Romantic Latvian tradition. During the last couple of years there has emerged a new "Society of Contemporary Music" the members of which consist of young composers, mostly in their 20\'s, who arrange a concert series of conemporary music and actively perform each others\' music as well as the music of their older colleagues and also develop connections with Western Eureopean musicians. Two of the cocmposcrs from this group, Rolands Kronlaks and Jānis Petraskevics, took part in the music festival, the former a lecturer and the latter as a student. The faculty members in the camp included Such prominet musicians as composer Selga Mence, who was the head of the program for composers, composer Pēteris Plakidis, who taught chamber music, harpsichordist Aina Kalnciema, choral conductor Juris Vaivods, pianists Teofils Biķis, a professor of the Latvian Musical Academy, pianist Ingrida Gūtberga, a Latvian living in the USA, pianists Uga Grants and Arthur Ozoliņš, viobnist Rasma Lielmane, A Latvian living in South Dakota and teaching in Mexico City, Latvian-American composer Imants Mc/a raups, born ir Philadelphia, a graduate of University of Philadelphia, a former student of George Rochberg and George Crumb, teaching in Latvia for the last two years, composer Rolands Kronlaks, a prominet 25-year old composer, who has been actively participating in the Western European contemporary music scene, musicologist Boriss Avramecs, director of the Riga Early Music Center who has been actively arranging the International Early Music Festival in Bauska-Rundāle, featuring Medieval music, as well as other festivals including those of Indian and Chinese music, musicologist Juris Grinevičs and host of others. Some of the musicians who came over from the United States and Canada included latvian-American baritone Paul Berkolds, a doctoral student ar UCSD, who has had a wide acclaim throughout the USA, clarinetist Eric Mandat, a faculty member at the University of Illinois and a member of the Transatlantic Trio (an ensemble consisting of piano, clarinet and cello, two member of which, Pēteris Plakidis and cellist Ivars Bezprozvanovs live in latvia, and the third member, Eric Mandat lives in Illinois; the ensemble meets together to perform as a trio regularly once a year when Eric Mandat comes to Latvia every summer), American composer Robert I>evin, composer Anton Rovner, Canadian composer Nic Gotham and others. In addition a number of composers, though not being active participants of the festival, came to Ogre to give presentations of their music as well as to attend the performances of their compositions – those included the famous composer Pēteris Vasks, who has been achieving immense success with his music throughout the world as well as composers Maija Einfelde, Imants Kalniņš, Imants Zemzaris, Ilona Brege, Vineta Lice and others. In additon the camp featured the Hilliard Ensemble from England, which "presented" a program of Medieval, Renaissance and Contemporary music, as well as an ensemble from Riga which performed Indian music on traditional Indian instruments. Each morning in the camp started out with a lecture at 9 AM in the main auditorium of the Ogre Music School, usually by prominet musicologists and composers and each evening ended with a concert at 8 PM in the same auditorium, where, for the most part, faculty members of the camp presented both traditional Classical repertoire and works by contemporary latvian composers. The rest of the day, between the morning lecture and the evening concert, was very tightly scheduled and featured more indidividual lessons, lectures amd masterclasses divided between each of the respaective groups of musicians. For the most part the faculty members who were instrumentalists and vocalists (most of whom were faculty members either in the Latvian Musical Academy in Riga or in the music colleges throughout Latvia) gave private lessons to students (most of whom were students either in the latvian Musical Academy in Riga or in the music colleges, of which there is a total of ten in laivia – but there were also some master-classes as well as cham ber-music coaching. The composition department of the camp, directed by composer Selga Mence, had a special individual program of its own composers. There were numerous lectures and presentations by composers, both those attending the camp in its entierety and those visiting the camp for one day. There were both individual presentations by composers of their own oeuvres and lectures read about wider topics in contemporary music. In addition the composers who were lecturers gave private consultations to the student composers upon the latters1 request. The composers who gave presentations of their works included Maija Einfelde, Ilona Brege, Rolands Kronlaks, Nic Gotham, Anton Rovner and others. Maija Einfelde, a composer of mostly chamber music and orchestral music who has recently received an award from the Barlow competition in the USA for her choral work "Pie zemes tālas"("Near the Far-Away Land"), presented a number of her choral works, including the composition which won the competition. Ilona Brege, composer and director of the Latvian National Symphony Orchestra presented her one-act opera "Dzīvais ūdens ("The Water of life"), based on religious, ritualistic story in an abstract mythical time and place, which has recently been premiered in Riga, Rolands Kronlaks, a graduate of the Latvian Music Academy, a composer following Western European avant-garde contemporary musical trends, who has participated in Franco Donatoni\'s master-classes in Mexico City and a number of contemporay musical festivals in Europe and who has been invited for one year to IRCAM in Paris, presented his orhestral piece "Under Pressure", his work for two pianos "Knots\'1 and his "Conversion" for oboe and string quartet. Canadian composer Nic Gotham, currently staying in  for one year, presented a number of different compositions of his including one chamber composition "Spirit of St. Ix)is" for violin, trumpet, piano and percussion and a fragment from his opera "Nigredo Hotel". Gotham sludied with the famous American composer James Tenney and his own compositional style features an organized blend of innovative "Dontown school" experiments folowing the tradition of his teacher Tenney with a traditional Romatic semi-popular operatic style. Anton Rovner shoved a diverse variety of his chamber compositions written during various years. One of the most talented students of the Latvian Music Academy, Jānis Petraskevics, presented a few of his works, including a solo cello work and a work for clarinet, viola and piano. Petraskevics, a follover of European avant-garde trends, has participated in a number of composers\' workshops in Europe including summer courses with Boguslaw Schaeffer in Vienna. He has received a grant to study at the Stockholm Conservatory with Magnus Linborg during the upcoming year. Some of the lectures about contemporary music of various kinds featured a wide variety of interesting music.  composer Imants Mežaraups read a lecture about American electronic music from a historical perspective, demonstrating the works of Vladimir Ussachevsky, Otto  Milton Babbitt and a host of others. He also demonstrated a variety of American new compositional and theoretical systems, such as Schenkerian analysis, set theory and others. Dace Aperāne presented a selection of contemporary American compositions, including a work by Christopher Rouse. Rolands Kronlaks read a lecture about Franco Donatoni and demonstrated his system to the interested audience. Anton Rovner presented a selection of works by contemporary Russian composers following European trends, including Edison Denisov, Dmitri Kapyrin and Victor Ekimovsky. These Russian composers were in very close and friendly relations with the Utvian composers (as well as composers from other Baltic countries) during the time of the Soviet Union, since both of these groups of composers had the common goal of finding new forms of expression, alternate to the prevailing "socialist realism". Since the breakup of the Soviet Union and the cooling down of relations between Russia and the Baltic countries, contacts between Russian and Latvian composers became fewer and more rare. Nevertheless some composers maintained contact with each other, certain Latvian composers visited contemporary musical seminars in Russia and vice versa. Anton Rovner also read a second lecture about the system of music education in the conservatories and universities in the United States, closely tying it with the various trends of contemporary American music, such as the Uptown school, the Downtown school the tonal composers, some of the systems of music theory and demonstrating the music of Andrew Thomas, Milton Babbitt, Roger Sessions, Charles Wuorinen and Daniel Goode as musical examples. Clametist Bric Mandat gave a lecture about aleatory music and improvisation, in the course of which the students had a chance to improvise with their instruments, following certain charts which Mr. Mandat placed on the board. Composer Robert Levin was teaching a course in African rhythm which was open for participation not only for composers but for all the instrumentalists. The composers\' lectures were held in in English and in few instances in Russian. The student composers, who attended the composers\' lectures, contained many talented young composers, who were primarily students or the Latvian Music Academy, as well as the various music colleges throughout via, most of which had their compostions performed in the composers\' concert-marathon. Among the young composers, some of the most prominet names winch could be mentioned are those of Jānis Fetraskevics, Solveiga Selga, leva Pane, Agita Zeļča, Agneta Krilova, Maija Homjakova, Mārīte Dombrovska and Sintija Deruma. They were very receptive to the new music demonstrated in the lectures and performed in the concerts and attended all of the events with great interest. The big lectures in the morning had a wide variety of topics which were presented to a larger audience than the composers\' lectures and seminars and, in general, often had a more scholarly and formal approach. They were read exclusively in Latvian. The first lecture was read by Dace Aperāne about the music of Pēteris Vasks. Other lectures included those about Baroque music, Medieval music, traditional and contemporary Latvian music, lectures by Boriss Avramecs about Asian music, a lecture by Latvian composer Imants Kalniņš about his 4th symphony and its performance in the USA and in the Utvian cities of Liepāja, Rīga and Cēsis, a lecture about the influences upon  music from Eastern Europe and Russia by-Arnolds Klotiņš and a lecture by Imants Mežaraups about contemporary American electronic music. They were well attended by the students and by the faculty members. There were two cases when the evening concerts contained lectures or presentations as part of them: the first time when Pēteris Vasks demonstrated a video recording of his new ballet "We Set Out Early...Visibility was Poor", choreapgraphed by choreographer Bill T. Jones to the music of his Symphony for string Orchestra "Voices", performed at the Kennedy Center in Washington DC in October, 1997, and the second, when Dace Aperāne presented a variety of contemporary American compositions (as well as compositions from other countries) of the most varied stylistic trends, including Christopher Rouse, John Adams, Ukrainian composer Valenti Silvestrov and a a host of others. The two  composers in the camp, namely Dace Aperāne and Imants Mežaraups, could be especially commended for their enthusiasm in bringing American compositions to Latvia and presenting them with American music and all the up-to-date information about the American musical scene in all of its diversity. The concerts in the evening were an important element of the music camp, their generous, saturating quality gave the camp a quality of a music festival. Many of the faculty members performed in them both as soloists and in ensembles, sometimes presenting recitals taking up half of the program of a concert and often performing together, one after the other in various combinations with each other and with the most varied program. Among the performers of the concerts one can name harpsichordist Aina Kalnciema who did a solo recital, violonist Rasma Lielamane, pianists TeoGls Biķis and Arthur Ozoliņš, the Transatlantic Trio (featuring Pēteris Plakidis, Eric Mandat and Ivars Bezprozvanovs), baritone Paul Berkolds and many other musicians. In addition to compositions from the Classical, traditional repertoire (among which especially impressive was Teofils Biķis1 performance of Scriabin\'s sth Sonata), the concerts featured many performances of  composers, many of which have been present at the concerts. Pēteris Plakidis has had his trio for clarinet, cello and piano entitled "Bezmiegs"("lnsomnia") performed by the Transatlantic Trio, Dace Aperāne has had her new composition, "Pastorale" for violin and piano performed by Rasma Lielmane and Arthur Ozoliņš, and also had her songs for baritone and piano and a cycle of pieces for two pianos entitled "Songs" performed. Imants Mežaraups performed a few of his short piano pieces "Miniatures" and also has had his composition for two pianos, celesta and percussion "Dalijumi un saites: zvani un sitieniI\'("Divisions and common bonds: bells and strikes") performed by a group of instrumentalists. composer Tālivaldis Ķēniņš "Partita for strings" was performed by the music camp\'s chamber orchestra with Mārtiņš Bergs conducting. I have already mentioned the group of instrumentalists who performed Indian music, accompanied by a 75 year old Lavian dancer Vija Vētra a reknowed specialist in Indian dance, who danced as if she was a young lady, as well as the Hilliard Ensemble from London, which performed a selection of Medieval and Renaissance music, as well as choral works by contempo rary composers Pēteris Vasks and Veljo Tormis. Two of the concerts at the end of the camp were marathons, featuring students – one of the concerts presented compositions by the student composers, performed by the student performers, which was a sort of contest, which lasted until 1 AM and at the end of which the composers who were on the faculty, i. e. the lecturers met and chose one of the compositions to be performed at the last concert, namely a piece for soprano and chamber ensemble by composer Agneta Krilova. The other concert was a similar contest for students who were performers, who performed traditional repertoire, several of which were chosen for the last concert. The final feature of the camp was an opera, staged during the last day of the camp, on July 18th, which was Albert Ixntzing\'s opera "Rehearsal of an Opera", staged by Guntis Gailītis and conducted by Viesturs Gailis and performed by the students of the camp – this performance brought the camp to a grandiose and festive conlusion. In general the music camp has had a very invigorating and creative atmosphere, its participants had a lot of enthusiasm in bringing all of this music out to the audiences as well as a lot of professional skills, and the broad variety of latvian music in the context of music from other countries, presented at this camp, has made this camp a major event in the scene of contemporay music in Eastern Europe and an important step in bringing Latvian musicians into a more distinctive position in the internatinal musical scene. □ member of faculty at the camp Anton Rovner,', '10th LATVIAN SONG FESTIVAL in CANADA July 3 – 7, 1996 Toronto/North York Ontario PROGRAMME WEDNESDAY – 3 July 20:00 A. Inn on the Park 1100 Eglinton Avenue East (Eglinton and Leslie) North York, Ontario Canada M3C IHB Tel: (416) 444-2561 Fax: (416) 446-3308 Since the First Latvian Song Festival in 1873 in Latvia, the song festival has grown to encompass not only a few choir concerts, but also concerts for chamber music, sacred music, symphonies, operas, ballets, theatre, art exhibits, writers\' forums, etc. Song festivals are cultural "events", equal to none. They have acted as a catalyst for the preservation as well as creation of Latvian music, dance, art, theatre and literature. After World War 11, overcoming various hardships, Latvians in Latvia under the occupation of the U.S.S.R. and Latvians in exile each continued to build on this tradition and continued organizing song and dance festivals throughout the world. The First Latvian Song Festival in Canada took place in Toronto in 1953. Since the majority of Latvian-Canadians reside in and around Toronto, all subsequent song festivals (1953, 1957, 1961. 1965, 1970. 1976, 1981, 1986, 1991) also took place in Toronto, drawing performers and spectators from Canada, the U.S.A., Europe and Australia, It is interesting to note that folk dancing only became part of the repertoire for the 3rd Latvian Song festival in 1961, and, owing to its success, all future song festivals included folk dancing as a vital part of the festival, not only here in Canada but also in the song festivals in Australia, Europe, the USA and Latvia. After the lifting of the Iron Curtain, Latvian-Canadians were invited to perform in Latvian Song and Dance Festivals in Riga, Latvia in 1990 and 1993 respectively, thus becoming participants of the "Singing Revolution". A limited number of singers and dancers from Latvia performed in the 9th Latvian Song Festival in Canada in 1991. This year we expect an even greater number of singers and dance groups from Latvia, thus helping bridge the gap of 50 years of separation between the developing Latvian culture in Latvia and Latvian communities in exile. We hope you will enjoy the 10th Latvian Song Festival in Canada. The concerts and performances should please everyone, young and old. The organizing committee has endeavoured to engage the best Latvian talent available, be it for the concerts, dance, theatre, art and crafts exhibits, films, rock and jazz concerts and writers\' forum.
