Katru, kas par viņu ko zinātu , lūdzu rakstīt tēvam – 1. VTkis, Scott 5-16. Miss. U.S.A.', 'The Why and How of Post-War Immigration By ARTHUR A. CALWELL, M.H.R., Deputy Leader of the Federal Opposition. Excerpts from a paper read at the 1953 Summer School of the Australian Institute of Political Science. It is literally true to say that, g no other immigrant receiving I tl country had such grave prob- P lems to solve as those which A faced Australia when the mil- t( lions of war-weary British n people, and the dis-possessed c and displaced millions wander- c ing in Europe on this side of tl the Iron Curtain, sought to es- a cape to the new world. It is si also true that no other country c faced up to its task, nor did r more for those who came to n seek its citizenship, than did ti Australia. Second only to the t< United States in the total num- g ber of persons admitted, Austra- 1 lia was more humane in its p consideration of each individual\'s s needs and gave more attention t to individual difficulties than r any other country. In addition, c people were admitted on com- p passionate grounds, whether they v were assisted or non assisted by r Government agencies, much f earlier than in any other conn- \\ try Australia also took more f dependent elderly and juvenile r close relatives than did any > other country. In short, Aus- r tralia\'s post-war immigration 1 policy has succeeded beyond the \\ most optimistic hopes of those s who were charged with the re- 1 sponsibiliiy or framing it and r putting it into operation, just £ seven short years ago. c The main purpose of this £ paper is not to deal with the r success and failures of the i scheme. There were some fail- c ures, but they were mostly un- £ avoidable. Nothing was ac- j complished according to a com- t plete detailed plan. There was 1 no time for its preparation. It s was, however, a time for imagi- t i nation, initiative and, above all, c for hard work. If, in the light s of the experience gained, this t country were again undertak- i ing the transfer of large num- < bers of people here, things 1 would, of course, be done some- ( what differently. Experience i has taught its lessons, but as \' this generation is never again < likely to be confronted with a i similar problem, it is somewhat i idle to talk of variations and ] improvements in methods for I the selection and transport of ; a vast army of new settlers un- » less, of course, another World I War leaves the same sort of ) aftermath as did Worldd War 11. ; The effects of the new environment on the migrants and : the problems they faced fitting into their new homes and (he ! Australian community is the principal contribution thia paper is intended to make. Tailing the aspect of environmental \' effects first, it is generally accepted that this is of less con- 1 sequence in the case of children, adolescents and young people than it is with those passed 30 years of age. Not that children have not had to meet certain difficulties, due principally to peculiarity of speech and dress. There have been cases in which they have suffered humiliations and distress at the hands of other children, but generally, after a little while they have all been accepted and have won positions in school and sport life on their own merits and, in some instances, have surpassed their Australian born classmates. Being bi-lingual and even tri-lingual, the new students have a distinct advantage. Few, if any, nave failed to learn and speak English quickly. With those under 30. and passed school age, the problems of adaption and absorption, are in most instances quickly overcome. But as people advance in\' age, so the difficulties of assimilation correspondingly increase. Success will vary from person to person, and in some cases will be small. However, where there are children in the family there need be no fear of any great unhappiriess for the parents, or even for the grandparents. The children will be the interpretive and the liaison force as they have been and are in the United States and Canada. The settled policy of the Department of Immigration is to discourage the segreition of racial groups and in tat respect they have well, owever, the attitude of the ustralian people themselves wards the newcomers is of ajor importance. Segregation in often be the result of ostrasm by thoughtfless sections of ie Australian people. Fortun,ely xenophobia has died down imewhat in our midst and the "ivironmental difficulties cor■spondingly diminished for the 2w people. In any case, Ausalia has never had to conmd with such large national roupings as America has had. i spite of such groupings, the roblems that have arisen from ich separate racial communies in the United States have ot prevented the first Amerim born generation of such eople from becoming loyal, deoted, Americans. The same ?sults will happen with the rst generation born of those •ho have decided to join their ite with ours and share a comīon destiny with us in the ears that lie ahead. This is ot to say that in the particuir tragic circumstances in rhich nearly 200,000 people aught asylum and a new homeand here, there will not be iany who will never be fully ssimilated. The poignant memries of depression and tyranny | t the hands of Nazi and Com- | īunist dictatorships, the hauntlg fears for the safety of loved I nes whom they may never see gain, the thoughts of happier I esteryears and the doubts as 3 the extent to which they will c permitted to play and fill a atisfying part in the profes- j ional, industrial and social life, f the community, and the deire to preserve their own culural values, all play a part in etarding absorption. These ften sad eyed people want to ie accepted and want to give f their best, but many of them emain uncertain and nostalgic. This is not unnatural in view f their tribulations and the nental tortures they have sufered as well as their natural •ride in the particular race to yhich they belong. With the oung, the problem is less than vilh adults. Considered broady, and with certain reservations, he environmental difficulties re not of economic origin. They belong to the spirit. At i recent All-Australian Latvian festival held in Melbourne, Proessor P. Jurevics spoke what s probbaly the general opinion >f the leaders of all the Euro>ean national groups that have :ome to Australia in the postvar years. Concluding his adiress he said:— "It takes many years, many generations to assimilate a different culture. If one has lost one\'s own culture, it does not mean that one has already adopted the culture of others—in most cases it simply means that one has no culture at all any more, that one has become a barbarian. For adult people it is almost impossible to adopt another culture—they are separated from it by all things—by) language, by entirely different customs, by a strange approach to things, by the lack of answers to their own problems. The situation is rendered yet more hopeless when, as is actually the case, we, as predestined "labourers" and "domestics," as a shipwrecked people, have not even access to the cultured circles . . . "Insofar as we save our cultural inheritance and possibly develop it further can we hope to preserve our full human dignity and not to slide into barbarism. That this should not happen is not only in our own interest, but also in the interest of the society in which we now live. Let us not forget that among the cultural values safeguarded by the national community are also the moral values. If a person has torn himself away from his old community, and at the same time has not been able to become a member of another, then that type of man is created who initiates all catastrophies—the completely isolated human being." Expressed in a sentence, the newcomers believe that man does not live by bread alone. Tolerance and a sympathetic understanding as well as a readiness to help and a determination to beat down prejudice will pay rich dividends for ourselves and for our new found friends in both the short-term and long-term view. The criticism of some Australians of foreign-born people speaking a language other than English in trams and trains and shops tends to create barriers. When Australians travel abroad very few of them can speak the language of each or, indeed, any country they visit in Europe and behave exactly as do the new arrivals here in conversation with their own nationals. Very often, the criticism heard comes from Australians whose knowledge of their own language or of any other language is by no means perfect. Migrants needs are capable of satisfaction and must be satisfied if they are to be happy and content. They want homes. If they are agricultural workers they want an opportunity to buy land at reasonable prices. If they are professional people, they want reasonable treatment in the recognition of their academic degrees and professional status. If they are tradesmen, or are possessed of any special skills or aptitudes, they want a chance to utilise their talents and their knowdledge to the best advantage of themselves and the community. By self help and hard work, quite a percentage of the migrants have acquired land and have built, or are building or are contemplating building, homes of their own. Their example is worthy of emulation by many Australians and by many British mi-I grants. I When it comes to professional people, frustration and unfriendliness have been their common experience at the hands of the Australian professions. Medical boards, dental boards, veterinary boards, engineers\' and lawyers\' institutes and all similar groupings of our Universitytrained people have been about as non-co-operative as they could be in according professional status to those among the new Australians who have sought it. Again, State legislation bars the way to a fair deal for the newcomers and the ] rights conferred by it are jeal-1 ously guarded by the practitioners concerned. Fortunately under the Australian Constitution, the Commonwealth Parliament has complete legislative authority over Commonwealth Territories, such as the Northern Territory, the Australian Capital Territory (Canberra) and the Territory of Papua and New Guinea. By virtue of this authority, the Commonwealth Department of Health four years ago appointed a number of Aus- t tralian doctors to examine more than forty European doctors. They selected over thirty, both J men and women, for service as medical officers in Papua and New Guinea. These European doctors have acquitted themselves very well, and their professional skill has been availed of by residents as well as by I natives. No Australian doctors ļ wanted to go there, so New Australian doctors had to be sent or the Communities left largely without doctors. But the interesting point is that! when the New Australians re-1 turn to the mainland they will, under existing conditions, find I themselves, to all intents and purposes, unable to secure registration in any of our States. America does not act this way and unless our professional people will make further advances on the slight progress registered so far in the recognition of the degrees of the new people concerned, many hundreds, and with their families some thousands, of our new citizens will feel great difficulty in fitting into their new Lomeland. As a contrast to the refusal of professional people to help carry out our immigration programme, there is the ready assistance given by servicemen\'s organisations in granting membership to those of the new arrivals who served in the World War 11. in armies under allied commands. But while this is pleasing, the attitude of the Navy and Air Force Chiefs to accepting into their services young people who are still tech-1 nically aliens is hard to under-1 stand. Even the Army which is not so rigid in its refusal to | accept enlistments from alien ranks, also places difficulties in the way of those wishing to serve Australia. Briefly put, there are still many discriminations against, and much passive resistance towards, our new citizens in the highest professional and defence quarters. There is in most other places a willingness to assist and co-operate which has made our bi-partisan Immigration scheme the most successful of any of those operating anywhere since World War 11. finished. This survey does not cover arrivals under the Dutch. Italian and German Migration Agreements negotiated since the Menzies Government took office at the end of 1949. It deals with those who came before that date, and those who arrived during 1950 and early in 1951 under arrangements which had been made by the Chifley Government, and the alterations made to the decisions by the Menzies Government. The migrants from Britain, and the relatively few from the other Dominions, and from America, and from Ireland, have not experienced the same difficulties as the migrants from European countries. The fact that there is a common bond of language makes it easier for such people to fit in more easily into our national framework. More important as a bond that binds than language, or a common cultural heritage, or degrees of consanguinity is the possession of common religious beliefs and so the kindly understanding and enduring work of the different religious groups in welcoming new arrivals is to be commended. The importance of it cannot be too strongly emphasised. To the extent that this work has been performed the benefit has been widespread, to the extent that it has been neglected, it is unfortunate. Happily, few new arrivals have not received the welcoming hand and the kindly gesture they would like to receive from their co-religionists, whatever the denomination. If it were not for the Churches the work of reception and settlement and assimilation would have been largely in vain. To all who have taken a part in the great adventure and given their services generously and often anonymously, the thanks of this generation, and of posterity, are due in full and handsome measure. The nation is the beneficiary of the Immigration scheme carried out by both the Chifley and the Menzies Governments and that, to all who participated in the preparation of the plans and their execution, is both enough reward and enough satisfaction.']

Representative_Docs: ['ANSLAVS EGLITIS Translated by Ruth Speirs The Best Seller Even outside his immediate circle of followers and disciples, there were many who regarded Milford Percy Donegall not only as a writer of genius but also as one of the most refined and cultured personalities of pur century. He was the last representative of a famous, very learned family which could boast of having produced a great number of professors, physicians, and eminent churchmen. Tall and stately, with an attractive silvery sheen in his plentiful hair, and his sensitive but well-defined features emanating a calm, assured dignity, Donegall presented an impressive appearance. Celebrities often avoid and despise society, but Donegall was a pleasant exception. In fact, it surprised his admirers how readily this brilliant personage accepted their invitations. Socially, Donegall was most agreeable; his hostess was always delighted with his excellent appetite, and he never entered into heated arguments about art. If some ill-bred guest importuned him with tactless questions, Donegall soon put him in his place with a tolerant smile or a good-natured little joke. Among close friends and followers Donegall spoke with great readiness, but he did not like to be interrupted or to have the flow of his thought obstructed by questions. His discourse included and anticipated everything a disciple, a layman, or the uninitiated could possibly ask him; they only had to understand, feel, and interpret his words correctly. It was hardly his fault if the common crowd could not follow the soaring flight of his ideas. Unfortunately, the crowd very rarely rose to those heights; to put it plainly, it never kept pace with him at all. A curious thing happened when Donegall spoke. He had a beautiful resonant baritone voice, vibrant with feeling. This captivating voice, the play of emotions on his noble features, and the melodious cadences of his flamboyant oratory so fascinated his listeners that they soon lost the thread of his argument. But they never failed to be profoundly impressed, even stunned, by his inspired fervor and deep solemnity. People left such gatherings feeling cleansed of all earthly impurities and elevated to a higher level of intellectual perception. They quickly forgot the slight uneasiness that had beset them when they lost their way in the maze of Donegall\'s glorious phraseology; after all, there was no reason why they should not gain a firmer grasp of the whole matter on some future occasionespecially if they were fortunate enough in the meantime to develop spiritually and mature emotionally. Besides, if any newcomer were immediately to understand everything he said, Donegall could obviously not have been a genius or even a man of striking originality. Donegall wrote in the same manner as he spoke. His fat volumes made an even greater impression than his oratory. There was a tumult of dark passions in his books, unbridled, unfathomable, rooted in the unknown and reaching out into the subconscious. Love was so powerful that it deflected the sun from its course and retarded the lunar phases. Sufferings were so profound that the earth groaned in sympathy and shuddered to its very core. Hatred was so fierce that it inflamed the firmament to a red-hot glow like a furnace. By the time the hapless reader had reached the end of one of Donegall\'s novels he was bathed in cold perspiration and his eyes protruded like a lobster\'s. Sometimes he literally collapsed across the last pages of the book. The only thing that saved him from irremediable despair was the fact that he was never quite sure which of the superhuman characters in the novel had loved and suffered, and which had hated and undergone great agonies of mind, and why. Even Donegall\'s most fervent admirers recoiled at the thought of reading the book a second time and again exposing their nerves to its shattering impact. Donegall certainly was famous, though only within the narrow circle of his admirers. A well-known critic referred to him as the profoundest and noblest spirit of the age; a few others called him the Abstruse Heraclitus of our times; but all the rest cautiously gave his books a wide berth. As a result, Donegall\'s works were published only in limited editions, to be bought by aesthetes and literary-minded society ladies. Donegall made no secret of it that he wrote for future generations of discerning readers with lofty minds and delicate sensibilities. He could afford to ignore the contemporary public: he had inherited some stocks and shares which provided him with a comfortable income. In other words, he could afford those limited editions for quite a long time. Unluckily, for some inexplicable reason, money began to lose its value. At first this happened in impoverished, war-devastated countries, then the wealthier countries were also affected, and finally even the evergreen American dollar, that currency of currencies, perceptibly depreciated in value. Donegall\'s income dwindled from day to day, and the moment came when he was faced with a difficult decision: either to stop having only limited editions of his works, or to increase his income by some other means. He tried the first alternative. With desperate inspiration he wrote a novel which loyal critics acclaimed with greater enthusiasm than ever. But even most of the copies numbered by the author himself and embellished with his graceful signature remained on the shelves of the bookshops. There was nothing left to Donegall but to try and supplement his income in some other way. People who did not know all the circumstances could never understand how the noblest and profoundest spirit of the century could have married such a deplorable creature as Pixy. Nobody dared even hint at her origins. She came from an orphanage and had, supposedly by extremely dubious means, worked her way up to the ownership of a small, nondescript hotel. Her lack of education was disastrous. She was apt to use the coarsest slang of the streets, and everybody winced when, she was about to open her mouth at some social gathering. Pixy may not have been exactly a fool, but her expressions were so crude and her remarks so embarrassingly uninhibited that Donegall\'s lady friends and admirers wrung their hands in despair. Could there be a greater irony of fate than that the most illustrious spirit of the age should be married to that primitive wretch? His friends and followers vowed they would never set foot in his wife\'s hotel, and they only changed their minds when they discovered that Mrs. Donegall provided her guests with simple but substantial meals instead of those tiny titbits, barely enough to feed a bird, and those watery iced beverages customarily proffered in intellectual and refined circles. Pixy certainly was not clever, but in her plain, homely manner she was pleasant to everybody and, on the whole, knew how to keep inconspicuously in the background. But, even so, she remained an ugly blot on Donegall\'s life history. Sadly enough, to quote the great Donegall\'s words in one of his most famous novels, "the piercing arrows of unreasoning fate, in their wanton, allengulfing lust for destruction, spare neither the humble nor the great, nor even the greatest and most illumined spirits among us." The distinguished novelist was carried off by a vulgar disease of the bladder. He passed away in the full flowering of his genius, leaving his widow destitute and in despair. Donegall had himself financed the publication of several of his latest books; during his prolonged illness he had been attended by famous doctors who had performed expensive but quite unnecessary operations on him; and, finally, he had had a magnificent funeral. The expense cost Pixy her hotel. Pixy was no longer young, and she did not feel strong enough to plunge once again into the uncertainties of hotel business. Grateful to fate for having granted her a few years in the best and most enlightened circles, she settled down to a very modest, frugal existence. The only adornment of her narrow little room in the suburbs was a shelf filled with her late husband\'s luxuriously bound volumes. She never read them because she had long ago realized that the flutterings of her limited intelligence could not keep pace with the assured wingbeat of her husband\'s spirit. She had never got beyond the first page of any of his books. Even then she had sat for long stretches of time, the book lowered to her lap, desperately but vainly trying to understand what it was all about. Apart from his novels, Pixy had inherited several enormous, richly ornamented, and very heavy scrapbooks. They contained literally every article, review, notice, and announcement that had appeared in any newspaper or magazine mentioning Milford Percy Donegall. Her husband had industriously collected all those cuttings and pasted them in, always carefully underlining his name in red. In addition, a whole corner of Pixy\'s room was taken up by a large wooden chest filled with her late husband\'s personal records—a variety of notes and memoranda, diaries, letters from admirers, and copies of letters by the great man himself. Donegall had providently preserved every word he had ever put on paper. He had composed all his private letters with an eye to the future, certain that eminent scholars and literary critics would in due course diligently study and analyze them for the benefit of posterity and that large volumes of his published letters would form a substantial addition to his Collected Works. One day, out of sheer boredom, Pixy rummaged in that wooden chest and found an almost completed manuscript of an unpublished novel. There was nothing to show whether it was one of Donegall\'s earlier novels or his last one, and whether it was merely unfinished or had been discarded as unsatisfactory. Donegall had never spoken to his wife about literary matters, and it would certainly not have entered his mind to discuss any unfinished work with her. Pixy knew that a deceased author\'s relatives usually published everything they could lay hands on, even if it did not make much sense. Besides, she was sure that nothing which had come from the pen of a genius could be entirely worthless. She decided to try to publish the manuscript. In publishing it, she would accomplish three things: she would add luster to her beloved husband\'s memory, she would transmit a priceless literary heritage to future generations, and she might perhaps even receive some small payment in return. She was badly in need of money, and there surely was a reasonable hope that people would buy a well-known writer\'s last work more eagerly than they had bought his previous ones. Pixy approached the publisher who had brought out most of Donegall\'s books, and she met with a ready response. Unfortunately, the manuscript was extremely difficult to read. Things had been crossed out, altered, and altered again. Corrections were superimposed on corrections. Long passages had to be inserted at various points in the text, each marked in accordance with a complicated system of numbers and letters. Donegall had spared no pains, shaping and reshaping his sentences and elaborating his style until it met with his approval. Besides, he had always refused to type his manuscripts. He despised typewriters, convinced that they mechanized all thought and that their odious dry clatter imparted a prosaic dullness and triteness even to the most inspired lines. In his endeavor to keep up with the rapid flight of his changing moods and emotions, Donegall had written at such speed that his handwriting was practically illegible. No printer would ever have been able to decipher it. The manuscript had to be put in order and neatly typed before it could be handed to the publisher. In the good old days of hotel management Pixy used to type all the bills. Being anything but lazy, she now produced her ancient dusty typewriter from its hiding place and settled down to work. The task ahead of her did not strike her as wholly disagreeable. In the past, the writing of business letters had been a source of enjoyment to her rather than an irksome duty, and she had sometimes even taken the liberty of enlivening her correspondence by adding some quiet little joke. But to prepare a valuable and important manuscript for publication was a very different matter. Her hands trembled when she opened the folder and began her work. It took her over an hour to restore order among the corrections, alterations, and insertions on the first page. At last the following text began roughly to take shape: The night slowly fades, and a mysterious mother-of-pearl dawn glimmers tremulously through curtains of costly Brussels lace, casting a miraculous tracery of delicate shadows over a luxurious bed on which there reposes a strange, singularly gifted, rich, and eccentric youth, Dr. Michel de St. Bayeaux, the celebrated astrophysicist who has devoted his life and the superb powers of his intellect to the unravelling of the enigma of the universe. Fitful shadows, fraught with destiny, flicker across the manly beauty and classic purity of his pallid features as, waxing in splendor, the triumph of morning unfolds and the golden trumpets of the rising sun herald the mystic birth of a new day, jubilantly proclaiming the eternal cycle of rejuvenation. The world rejoices at the promiseful tidings, and the learned, beautiful youth is roused from his slumbers, waking, alas, not to unclouded joy but to the torment of a strange, indefinable inner discontent. He feels weary in spirit and worn in body, and his noble brow, white as alabaster and delicately veined at the temples, is hot and heavy to his gentle touch. His slender fingers, sensitive as a violinist\'s, tremble as a strange emptiness stirs within him, pulsing against his frame. A dull weight of pain descends upon him, crushing his body and burdening his mind, and although the agonies of his spirit are as yet inarticulate, as yet confined to the subconscious, the youth is seized by feverish disquietude, premonitions of evil, the dark fear of the unknown, and its consequences. Pixy read the page once, twice, and a third time. Never before had she so wholeheartedly immersed herself in her husband\'s writings. She had a vague feeling that something quite simple and human might eventually emerge from underneath those lavish embellishments and puzzling emotions. The hapless widow\'s head grew hot with her exertions and threatened to burst, but she still could not understand anything she had read. She would long have abandoned her whole project out of sheer exhaustion and hopeless bafflement if there had been no money at stake. Pixy was a simple soul, and it was not surprising that her husband\'s intellectual friends despised her. True enough, she was dull-witted, and art for art\'s sake did not inspire her with any enthusiasm. But she had known hard times, and now that art held out a promise of some small pecuniary gain she became possessed of almost superhuman powers of perseverance. She turned to the second page of the manuscript, hoping it might furnish her with some clue to the mystery of the first page. The astrophysicist, racked by dread and dark forebodings, is suddenly struck by a lightning flash of insight, and he shudders, overwhelmed by a convulsion of violent feeling. Searing shame envelops him like a heavy purple cloak as he sinks down into his pillows and with merciless self-torture recalls what had passed in the night that was gone. Having in vain endeavoured to drown his Weltschmerz, his sorrow at life\'s futility, in champagne, he had returned from the city\'s most expensive nightclub to his mansion in which exquisite taste and luxury reigned, and there, beneath the Venetian chandelier, in a corner of the marble hall which enshrined a priceless treasure, a magnificent fourteenth-century Mantovan suit of armour, he had succumbed to a fateful, inexorable upsurge of passion. Indeed, Michel de St. Bayeaux had clasped unto himself a chaste, fragile, unresisting female form, the somber black of whose attire was relieved but by a small white apron, and his hands, strong as the paws of a young leopard, had almost strangled her as he imperiously kissed her cool, full lips. Not dissimilar from the astrophysicist\'s lightning flash of insight, a spark of inspiration suddenly kindled in Pixy\'s tormented mind. She could scarcely believe it yet, but it seemed to her that she at last understood everything. The key to the mystery lay in the reference to the city\'s most expensive nightclub. Pixy knew life, especially hotel life. Anxious not to lose the thread, she hurriedly typed the following short version of what she had gleaned: Mike, the physicist, awoke in a bad temper. His head ached, and he felt a pressure in his chest. He suffered from a hang-over. Ashamed, he lay back in his bed, remembering that he had kissed the maidservant when he came home from a bar the night before. Pride and joy brought out beads of perspiration on Pixy\'s brow. She was not so stupid after all! Indeed, she was perfectly able to understand her famous huband\'s novel. With growing excitement she continued her task, deciphering his handwriting, putting everything she understood into short, concentrated sentences, and quickly typing it out. Suddenly, with a bittersweet pang, Pixy realized that the novel was about herself and her late husband. With a fine disregard for fact, the great artist had deliberately re-created much of his material. The learned, noble, and celebrated astrophysicist was depicted as fabulously rich, whereas the slowwitted but kindly maidservant was as poor as a church mouse. Kissing, lovemaking, and other noteworthy incidents had been transferred from Pixy\'s modest hotel to the astrophysicist\'s incredibly luxurious mansion. Nevertheless, the novel was certainly the love story of the great Donegall and humble, foolish Pixy. Quite possibly, this was the reason why it had never been published. The widow drew a deep, proud breath, wiped away a tear, and diligently applied herself to the manuscript. Now she had not the slightest doubt that she would understand everything and follow the story right through to the end. —After the inadvertent but fateful kiss, the fantastically wealthy and fantastically noble-minded astrophysicist feels that he cannot abandon the poor maiden to grief and sorrow. Out of compassion and humane kindness he commits himself to further, more serious intimacies with her. This gives rise to frenzied, almost murderous hatred and jealousy on the part of his aged parents, his betrothed and her parents, and numerous friends. Tumult and commotion engulf everybody concerned. —It was not easy for Pixy to unravel the plot from the thick tangle of passion, love, hatred, madness, interventions of fate, and cosmic repercussions. But by means of hard work, perseverance, feminine intuition, and some imagination, she did not do too badly. Pixy liked the novel very much indeed. But she could not understand why the astrophysicist continually referred to himself as a profound and tragic philosopher. There was nothing to substantiate such a claim. All he did was sigh now and again, saying, "Why does everything in the world happen in this way, and not another? Where is the end and the beginning of it all?" Pixy thought that these were perfectly ordinary questions which inevitably occurred to everybody at some time or other. She herself had also frequently pondered over such things, especially after her husband\'s death, but it had never entered anybody\'s mind to call her a philosopher. The descriptions of the infinitely loving, kindhearted, and clinging maidservant drew many a sigh from Pixy, but she was annoyed with the girl\'s excessive humility. She would have liked to see her pluck up her courage and do something of her own accord just for once. The final episodes caused the tenderhearted widow acute distress. Persecuted, scorned, ridiculed, and tormented by infamous relatives and friends, the astrophysicist and the maidservant realize that life on this earth holds out no hope for them. They decide to lie down together on a powerfully radioactive bed. Instantly consumed by blue flames, they are united forever, in death and in eternity. Pixy could not bear to have such a good and decent girl as the maidservant die that terrible, undeserved death. It was bad enough that Donegall had made her out to be as poor as a beggar, but to kill her in such a barbarous manner was really too much. Pixy felt she had the right to speak up for herself. She took her courage in both hands, discarded Donegall\'s final chapters, and substituted her own version: the maidservant tells the physicist\'s friends, his betrothed, his parents, and his former ladyloves to mind their own business; she marries the hopelessly confused and irresolute Mike, and they live happily ever after. Now that she understood the whole novel and had neatly typed it out in short, clear sentences, Pixy had to retype the manuscript in the heavily ornate style of the original, inserting all the aesthetical and philosophical embellishments she had omitted. Her heart sank when she thought of that enormous labor. Yet, having a strong sense of duty, she might have sat at her typewriter until she collapsed if she had not suddenly received a letter from the publisher. He wrote that he had to have Donegall\'s manuscript within a week, or not at all. Sales were very slow, and business was steadily deteriorating. If conditions did not improve, publishing firms would have to close down and books would disappear forever from the cultural scene. It was only on the first anniversary of Donegall\'s death that there was a chance of his novel being published without too heavy financial losses. "After a year, or a year and a half, the thoughtless crowd forgets even the greatest genius and most illustrious protagonist of eternal beauty," wrote the publisher, who had acquired rather a flowery style, having spent all his life among books. "Only an extremely costly large-scale advertising campaign can then lure the public back into buying a deceased author\'s works. But, owing to a lack of means, I find myself unable to embark upon such a project." Pixy was profoundly distressed. It was quite impossible to reintroduce Donegall\'s lavish style into her typescript within a mere week, even if she were to pound her typewriter for twenty-four hours a day. But when she glanced at her own version of the novel it seemed to her that it read rather well and was very easy to understand. Abbreviated novels were nothing unusual. Besides, Pixy was lamentably short of money. At last she decided that even to add only as much luster to her husband\'s memory as present circumstances permitted was better than to do nothing at all. She made some final corrections and alterations and sent her typescript to the publisher. The instantaneous success of the novel surprised everybody. The critics unanimously declared that Donegall, formerly complex and obscure, had towards the end of his life cast aside his polychromatic mode of expression and had adopted a fluent melodiousness. Indeed, in his last novel he had discarded his elaborate style and had returned to the simple, natural speech of the people. Significantly, the master had in his final act of self-realization achieved complete fusion between the essence and appearance, between the individual and the whole, between oneness and plurality. Victoriously emerging from the nihilistic hell of sterile pessimism, he had attained the goal of constructive happiness. This was conclusively evidenced by the triumphantly happy ending of his last novel. There was only one critic who disapproved of the novel. He had all his life extolled Donegall\'s abstruseness, and now he bitterly complained that this great genius had in his old age betrayed the ideals of his youth in order to curry favor with the crowd. But his lament was drowned in the jubilant choir of general acclaim. Even more important than the favorable reviews was the fact that Donegall\'s novel sold extraordinarily well. After three months it was already in its second edition. Now Donegall\'s previous works also began to reappear in the windows of the bookshops. They remained there, however, because the public obstinately refused to buy anytihing but his last novel. The publisher, seeing how matters stood, asked Pixy to re-examine Donegall\'s private papers most carefully in case there were some fragments or notes among them which had previously escaped her attention and might now be published. Any piece of writing in Donegall\'s latest style was worth a great deal of money. Very obligingly he offered to put a whole staff of experts at her disposal, to help her sift Donegall\'s papers. He was most indignant when she resolutely refused to grant anybody, even the most welldisposed critics, any access to her late husband\'s private records. While the newspapers angrily accused Donegall\'s widow of thoughtlessness, unnatural jealousy, or plain stupidity because she kept the great author\'s possessions to herself, she diligently studied every scrap of paper she could find in that big wooden chest in her room. Donegall\'s diaries only contained descriptions of celebrations and parties given in his honor, the names of prominent guests, and exact quotations of their gratifying references to his works. His large, bulky notebooks were filled with detailed analyses of the flattering reviews his books had received, and vigorous refutations of reviews that had been unfavorable or impartial. Donegall\'s letters were also rather a disappointment to her. True enough, they were in his usual rich style, but they struck Pixy as ponderously didactic and full of tedious generalizations. Fortunately, Donegall had preserved all the letters he had received from female admirers. Lightheaded girls had written him all lands of absurd nonsense; women in maturer years had effusively indulged in reminiscences and fantasies; jealous ladies had scolded, reproached, and pleaded; the pious ones had implored him to return to God and join the religious sect to which they happened to belong themselves. Pixy thought she might use those reminiscences, fantasies, and absurdities in a description of a whole crowd of admirers who had surrounded the great novelist in his bachelor days. But all those girls, middle-aged women, and old ladies soon began to lose their separate identities and merge into a single crazy female of indeterminable age. This composite creature, an incredible mixture of opposites, was alternately possessed by carnal desire and by extreme piety. Desperately, with all the means at her disposal, she fought for the love of the serene sage. Pixy\'s work was far more difficult than it had been at the time when she simplified Donegall\'s own manuscript. But she had already gained a little experience and, even more important, she had faith in her ability. Barely six months had passed when Pixy called on the publisher and handed him a newly found manuscript of a novel by Donegall. The publisher instantly forgot their differences and quarrels about her late husband\'s private papers. The success of Donegall\'s second posthumous novel surpassed even that of the first one. It was immediately sold out, a second and third edition quickly followed, and the novel was translated into Italian, Dutch, Swedish, and Estonian. The critics recognized with increasing clarity the author\'s new artistic ideals. They drew particular attention to the fact that his latest style was even more limpid, supple, and tastefully popular than it had been in his previous novel, and that it at times enchanted the reader with its lucid simplicity. Above everything else, forgetting synthesis, essence, and totality, the critics were enthusiastic about the unfathomable depths of Donegall\'s understanding of a loving woman\'s heart with all its illogicalities, contradictions, and absurdities. Again, a storm of abuse burst over the unreasonable widow\'s head. She was violently reproached for her criminal refusal to transfer the famous novelist\'s private records to the National Museum where they might be sifted and analyzed by the country\'s leading literary experts and scholars. Loud voices demanded that the old fool should be declared insane and that her late husband\'s property should be placed under a trusteeship. In the meantime, hardly ever leaving her room, Pixy unremittingly worked on yet another novel. All the material was exhausted, and she had to rely solely on her own limited abilities and on the memories of her melancholy childhood at the orphanage and her checkered career as a maidservant in hotels, restaurants, and bars. Pixy soon realized that a writer\'s art could at times be extremely tricky. She often reached a complete deadlock, incapable of producing a single sensible thought. In that frame of mind, any solution or development in her narrative struck her as insufferably boring, commonplace, or quite inane. Poor Pixy felt like knocking her head against the wall to make some sudden idea tumble out of her shaken brain, the way a silent radio can sometimes be brought back to life by violent jolting. The only thing that saved her from utter collapse and despair was the knowledge that she was working for the sake of her husband\'s memory and fame. After six months of gmelling labor, a worse time than she had experienced in her youth when she opened her small suburban hotel entirely on credit, Pixy again visited the publisher. Rather nervous, she timidly placed a newly discovered manuscript of an unpublished novel by Milford Percy Donegall on his table. The publisher stared at her, speechless with amazement. But he quickly pulled himself together and called for the managers of the printing and bookbinding departments and for the chief illustrator. Everything possible was to be done to get the book into print in time for Christmas. In due course, the book appeared. In this novel, the authors astonishing versatility was at last fully revealed as it showed his intimate knowledge not only of intellectual circles and high society but also of the gray, dreary lives of the poor and humble. The largest book club chose the novel as its Book of the Month, and it became one of the most successful best sellers ever published. It went into many editions and was translated into a variety of languages. The biggest Hollywood studios fought for the film rights; it was made into a stage drama and later into a musical, and was performed all over the country. Donegall\'s fame was securely established: he had become a popular writer in every sense of the word. On the third anniversary of the novelist\'s death, a solemn commemoration ceremony, organized by Donegall\'s publisher, took place in the main hall of the National Museum. The specially invited audience represented the world of art and letters. Donegall\'s gleaming bust of gilded bronze stood in a prominent position, flanked by evergreen laurel bushes. Its tall pedestal of pink marble, festooned with laurel wreaths and silk ribbons imprinted with gold lettering, was almost hidden from view by a profusion of roses, lilies, and chrysanthemums. Donegall\'s friends and lady friends, his disciples and admirers, listened with bowed heads to the mournful music and the laudatory speeches. Eminent literary personages discoursed upon the master\'s immortal art and his fleeting sojourn upon this earth. What a perverse fate that beloved writer had suffered! Singular modesty and exaggerated self-criticism had prevented him from pubhshing his last works whose profundity and stark realism were to bring him the greatest fame. If these novels, discovered only after his death, had appeared during his lifetime, they would have saved him endless worry and vexation. In fact, the great novelist would never have suffered financial hardship and (the speakers only implied this, but everybody understood what they meant) he would not have had to demean liimself by marrying Pixy. The speakers did say quite bluntly, though, that Mrs. Donegall had never been able to appreciate her husband\'s greatness, and they reproached her for still hoarding his private papers and debarring all literary experts from access to them. Pixy knew perfectly well what Donegall\'s friends thought of her. Nevertheless, she attended the commemoration ceremony. She seated herself close to the entrance, at some distance from all the others. Folding her hands in her lap she listened respectfully to the speeches. Pixy\'s fur coat was ostentatiously expensive, she wore too much jewelry, and her scent was too obtrusive. A whisper went round the audience: Donegall\'s shameless widow, battening on his success, had come to show herself off! Poor Donegall had not in the least profited by marrying a woman of some means; on the contrary, it was that vile, illiterate wretch who had made her fortune by marrying him! For a moment, the crowd was so overcome with indignation that Pixy might have been told to leave the hall if the first solemn chords of a hymn had not been sounded on the organ. Pixy listened to the sad, beautiful music. Being a sensitive creature she v/as easily moved, and tears began to trickle down her cheeks, which had become pale and hollow in those long nights she spent at her typewriter. But when she heard the speakers extolling Donegall\'s last three novels and hurling reproaches at his refractory widow, a strange little smile appeared on her thin lips. The seeming complacency and arrogance of that smile so infuriated the company that everybody pointedly turned his back on her. But there was one man who could not tear his eyes away from Pixy. He was the publisher. Her queer smile fascinated him, though he did not know himself why it should affect him so strangely. But he had for a great number of years guided the affairs of his publishing house, and he had learned not only to obey the dictates of reason but also to heed his intuition; if he had not done so he might have gone bankrupt long ago. A vague suspicion formed in his mind as he looked at Pixy, and it crystallized into a thought so strange and yet so simple that he could not understand why it had not struck him before. A discreet little smile dimpled his smooth round cheeks. Quietly rising to his feet he tiptoed across to Pixy and sat down beside her. For a while he observed a solemn silence; on ceremonial occasions it was not proper to broach business matters straight away. Finally he whispered: "We have never talked about the future, Mrs. Donegall. Every time you brought me a newly discovered manuscript of a novel I was convinced it was Donegall\'s last one. But you probably know that it is essential for a publisher to plan a long time ahead. Therefore it would help me a great deal to know whether there is a chance of another manuscript, or perhaps several manuscripts, turning up among your husband\'s private papers." Pixy\'s limpid brown eyes flashed a quick glance at the publisher. Then she said, demurely looking at the ground: "It isn\'t impossible. There\'s quite a lot of papers. As a matter of fact, I\'ve already found some pages of an unpublished manuscript, almost a whole chapter, but"—she sighed—"it\'s an awful lot of work to put the whole novel together!" "When might I hope to receive the manuscript?" "Not before the usual interval." "In six months\' timer** "Yes, approximately "Would you like some money in advance?" "Well talk about it later. Just now I want to listen to Professor Rankin. He is discussing the development in my husband\'s prose style."', 'Old Jelgava – Etching by A. Apinis Janis Rudzitis One of the things which European literature has inherited from the 19th century is the modern artistic fairy-tale. In Germany, favoured and warmed by the sunshine of Romanticism, this genre flourished during the first half of the last century. First of all the merits of the Brothers Grimm must be mentioned in this respect. Their collections of fairy-tales Kinder- und Hausm&rchen (3 vols, 1812—1822) and Deutsche Sagen (2 vols, 1816—1818) created a powerful impression. But the fairy-tale soon rises ,to an equally eminent place in literary creation outside Germany. In 1835 the Danish shoe-makers son Hans Christian Andersen publishes the first collection of his fairy-tales gradually becoming the most famous fairy-tale writer in the world. The modern fairy-tale crystallizes into its purest shape, however, at the end of the 19th century. The British writer Oscar Wilde, that sensitive adorer of beauty, is the man who, as soon as he takes up writing fairy-tales, feels bound neither by any didactic aims nor by a craving shared by vast circles of readers — the craving for characters of an unreally fantastic world and for an interesting plot. The brothers Grimm and Andersen have not been able to get quite free especially from didacticism; Wilde is interested only in the aesthetic possibilities offered by the genre of fairy-tales. He has not created much in this branch of literature, only two books: The Happy Prince (1888) and A House of Pomegranates (1889), but by their quality he becomes the master and the fulfiller of the aesthetic fairy-tale. The author adds a characteristic aknowledgement to his second book, namely that it was "intended neither for the British child nor the British public". Wilde died in 1900, and was followed in the literature of the large nations of Europe by no authors who could have taken over and continued his manner of writing fairy-tales. But incidentally it has come to pass that the year of Wilde\'s de*ath at the same time marks the birth of the modern fairy-tale of a small European nation. It is the Latvian writer Anna Brigadere who publishes her modern fairy-tale Mare (Mary) in 1900. In 1902 the writer Andrievs Niedra follows with his Zemnieka dels (A Peasant\'s Son), in the sub-title directly called by the author "a modern fairy-tale". The talent of Anna Brigadere has all the necessary qualities to make her a writer of fairy-tales. In her conception of the world, however, the ethical ideals hold too important a place, so the fairy-tale is not her final aesthetic aim, but only a means to culminate the idea1 of woman\'s unlimited ability to sacrifice herself for the sake of the man THE MODERN FAIRY-TALE IN LATVIAN LITERATURE she loves. The genre of the fairy-tale is later valued by the writer only in her dramas which, like Mare, fascinates most with its high ethical ideals. Andrievs Niedra was a pastor and an eminent orator who dealt in his sermons not only with biblical themes but also with social and national problems of modern life. Also his Peasant\'s Son in a certain sense resembles a widely-conceived sermon shaped after the models of Christ\'s parables, yet intertwining into the plot genuine national motives taken both from the folk-tales and from the features and events of modem life. The Peasant\'s Son of Anrievs Niedra as a new and peculiar feature in Latvian literature, increased his fame. Yet after this Andrievs Niedra wrote no more fairy-tales- . In 1904 there was written the first fairy-tale by Karlis Skalbe — Ka es braucu Ziemelmeitas lukotips (My Travels to the Daughter of Northland). Skalbe was only 25 years old then The heroes of the first fairy-tales of both Skalbe and Niedra are young men who are driven away by an inner unrest from their idyllic parental homesteads into the wide world, this wonderland of the unknown. But Niedra and Skalbe were entirely opposite characters. The first of them preached conservatism; he hoped to settle all problems by means of a calm and cautious evolution, so during the revolution of 1905 in Russia he opposed it. The Peasant\'s Son of Niedra does not find in the world what he has been longing for and returns home dejected and disappointed, whereas the fairy-tale of Skalbe is a direct answer to the central thought of Niedra It contains c prophetic picture of the approaching revolution: the seeker of the Daughter of Northland, after having left his parental farm, as if winged and elated comes to the conclusion that "the real life is wide as the world and high as the heavens". If, consequently, we have reason to consider this first fairy-tale by Skalbe tt> be a reply to Andrievs Niedra, we on the other hand, may conclude that this time also the fairy-tale from itself has been chosen by the young writer only to use the same form as his opponent has used. In the person of Skalbe we meet an author who is a born fairy-story teller and to whom the fairy-tale may become the final aim of an aesthetic form. It is sometimes said that the fairy-tale sets a man free from the chains of the laws of nature. If it is so, then Skalbe performs this not only by phantastic plot of his tales, but still more by his stylistic portrayal, which resembles a painting, so that nearly every sentence wipes out the limit between the real and the unreal world. With the passing years Karlis Skalbe becomes the Latvian "King of Fairy-tales". More than fifty of his fairy-tales are among the most valuable treasures of Latvian literature. It has been said that Andersen by his fairy-tales has made the literature of the small Danish nation known throughout the whole of Europe. If the Latvian nation and the Latvian language were somewhat better known, if two World Wars had not devastated Latvia, the name of Skalbe would undoubtedly be celebrated in Europe together with that of Oscar Wilde. It is remarkable that there is not only a causal contact between the fairy-tales of Skalbe and Wilde, but also a few parallel features in the biography and the character of them Wilde was 35 when he published his second collection of fairy-tales. After a few years he was in prison and he thanked the world for this period of his life with the "Ballad of Reading Gaol". Skalbe Illustration from K-Skalbe\'s Fairy-Tales, by N. Strunke also was in prison, for the court of the Tsaric Russia did not forgive him his taking part in the events of 1905. As Skalbe was acquainted with and fascinated by the fairy-tales of Andersen already, during his imprisonment .he got more closely acquainted with the fairy-tales of Wilde, translated them and also wrote his own fairy-tales. He, too, was 35 when having left the prison he published (in 1914) his ZLemas pasakas (Winter Fairy-tales) which comprise the best of his fairy-tales. By a careful comparison it is possible to find several motives whicrf Skalbe and Wilde have in common, .not only in this one, but also in other collections. That Skalbe has looked at Wilde\'s works as models in a certain sense, may be seen also from his manner in choosing the titles of his books. Wilde has "The Happy Prince and other Tales" or "A House of Pomegranates and other Tales"; Skalbe has the same: "Mulka laime un citas pasakas\'(lA Simpleton\'s Luck and other Tales"); "Mans bernibu dienu menesis" ("The Moon of My Childhood . . .") "Sarkangalvite" ("The Girl Red-Cap") "un citas pasakas", etc The chief thing, however, is that both Wilde and Skalbe use the same method of artistic creation, that they are akin to one another in their visualization of the world, in which life and nature are visualized in soft half-tones ranging from a quite naturalistic reality up to the glorious mysteries of deepest essential meaning of everything, at the same time widely opening the gates towards the inside of the human soul. Together with this we must remember their difference: Wilde is British with Irish blood in his veins, a hedonist man of the aristocratic drawing-rooms; but Skalbe is the son of a Latvian blacksmith to whom even the drawing-rooms in the capital of his own rustic nation have never seemed enticing. This means that each of them has his own world, his own favourite characters, his own mentality which has been influenced by their nationality and the surrounding society. Finally, one more resemblance between their lives should be remembered: Wilde, expelled from his society, left England and in great poverty died in Paris. Also Skalbe had to leave his native country in the autumn of 1944, and he died in Stockholm, on April 16th 1945. In the fairy-tales of Skalbe, various elements have been blended together and his characters make quite a mixed society. In comparison with the countries of Western Europe, in Latvia the ancient folk-lore traditions are much more alive still in the 20th century. To the Latvians the folk-songs, fairy-tales and myths have not yet become just ethnographic materials collected in books in which only a few specialists might have an interest. All of the educated people, all scientists and artists have grown up in those traditions. The fairy-tales of Skalbe, too, contain much of the Latvian folk-lore. We see there, for instance, the same Latvian orphan-girl whose virtues and handsomeness is praised in folk-songs and fairytales; we see the witty cowherd boy and the clumsily powerful giant as well as the Latvian mythological characters who, perceiving im – perceptible, enter the modern life and settle the destinies of people. Of course, in the fairytales of Skalbe there are also the traditional kings, princes, princesses and court-people with whose splendour the fantasy of this nation, the bearer of a really severe destiny, has been so much occupied since ancient times. But they are rather strange, these royal noblemen, and rather strange is also the etiquette of their court; many a citizen of a monarchy might get puzzled by it. These oddities are caused by the fact that a king with Skalbe usually is a herdsman or generally a rustic man by his origin who has come to his high office either owing to his special merits, by the nobleness of his character, or by his wisdom. This is a usual motive of the folk-tales which is extended and developed on to its consequences in the works of Skalbe. The life at this court generally has the same features, the same conventions, the same style of language and even the same troubles which art to be mit at a well-to-do Latvian farm; everything is only depicted in brighter colours here; the court life culminates by mentioning the glistening of gold and silver there. Skalbe is not fond of animal personifications and he has therefore used but few of the folk-tales about animals. Personified domestic animals, wild beasts and birds appear in his fairy-tales only now and then and just episodically; they have no importance in the story as a whole. Yet among the most brilliant works of Skalbe there is one fairy-tale where the personification of animals is made into a wide allegory. Its title is "Kakita dzirnavas" ("The Pussy\'s Water-Mill"). Transferred into the human reality, this allegory is a carefully compossed short story on social and ethical motives with a pure-hearted Herrnhuter as the central character. The fairy-tales of different nations have common characters and common motives. In a similar way also Skalbe has not absolutely eliminated foreign elements from his tales. Amid purely Latvian surroundings there appear also characters closely akin to the world of Andersen and the Grimms, and next to ancient Latvian deities we find also mythological beings of other nations. But those have already become quite assimilated with their Latvian surroundings. And if on one single occasion Skalbe *s fantasy has reached distant India (in the fairy-tale "Nabaga bramins" – "The Poor Brahmin"), the same has happened. The hero of the tale is a Hindu only outwardly. He expresses only Skalbe\'s own "religion of silence" his creed, with his testimony, about the beauty of the word untold, about the value of the heart and the sun. Skalbe, now and then, introduces his fairy-tale by an enlargened and stylistically adorned variation of the usual "Once upon a time" formula, endeavouring to transfer the events of his tale into those times "when people had still no idea of mineral coal and of the teaching about the roundness of earth". The background of such far-off times adds to the effectiveness of the depiction, but it .cannot make this addition consequent and constant. As a rule, the events and characters in the fairy-tales of Skalbe are nearer to the present-day life than to the ancient past A modern millionaire who builds a modern town; a poor Incessant Student, for ever thirsting for knowledge; a farmer who makes preparations for a wealthy celebration of New Year\'s Eve; a mother who has lost her son in the Great War; soldiers, partakers of the War of Independence; a stingy bookworm; a hangman who wants to drown his conscience in alcohol; even a wanton woman — a whole collection of such 20th century characters is to be met in Skalbe\'s fairy-tales, together with fantastic kings, pagan deities and orphan-girls. Not always a "fairy-tale" of Skalbe is a true fairy – tale from beginning till end. As F regards the construction, Skalbe is fond of the so-called "frame-technique", where the fairy-tale itself is nearly lost in the magnificent arabesque of : the literary framework around it; and this frame• work is nothing but a vision of life and nature, seen with the eyes of a romanticist, or a characterization of the author s present state of mind or soul in statu nascendi of his fairy-tale At other times the short fairy-tale is added as a supplement at the end of a considerably longer depiction. It happens, too, that the fairy-tale at the end proves to be a dream, beautified memories, sudden eruptions out of the depths of the subconscious etc. On the other hand, Skalbe\'s fairy-tales never begin and never end with a fantastic event. In one of his fairy-tales Karala krasnkuris — The King\'s Stoker) Skalbe introduces his story by characterizing a pre-historic time, undatable and very ancient, and this characterization may be a sample of his style: "The human beings and the animals lived in a close friendship ... And the people had broad ruddy faces as if they had looked too long at the moon in the evening. They still did not distinguish between living and lifeless beings. The darkness was full of spirits; every corner was full of hidden life as a gently covered mother\'s womb ... And the people understood all voices. They understood also things that by their nature were dumb or suffered from a severe hoarseness just as the late autumn winds do. They talked to their cattle and to the stars in the sky, to the clay water-jug and to the tiny knitting-needle; when a child awoke he talked to his arms and legs. And on the thrashing-floor there was plenty of hay and straw, and plenty of beautiful legends were born incensed with the fragrance of hay ..." A glorification of the primitive man? No, it is a romanticist\'s vision of the paradise of all souls lost by civilized man. But if a person is able to return by his visualization of ;rhe world, in other words by his style, to such a panpsychism of the ancient man, he is indeed opening the gates to a fairy wonderland. Illustrations, metaphors, and personifications in his fairy-tales are not only means of style, but a brotherhood with plants, animals and things, professed in a peculiar visualization of the world. In his lyrics as well as in his fairy-tales Skalbe has depicted a strange feeling —- as if a man were growing into a tree. Even a whole Forest of Souls has grown up and the little boy, not being able to distinguish any more which tree is his mother now, kneels and says his prayers to the whole forest. The human beings in the fairy-tales of Skalbe, by being transformed into trees, are growing The Forest of Souls; in the same manner things and phenomena whenever* they come under Skalbe\'s poetic visualization obtain a soul and begin to live in the same society with human beings. Only a few of Skalbe\'s fairy-tales will interest children because usually his plot is too simple, without any outward dramatism; but the world of souls, of things awakened to life, shown by the< style of Skalbe, will be perceived in its full beauty only by a literarily educated aesthete. The same may be said about the contents, the ideas of these fairy-tales: they will captivate only such readers as know life already and who like to seek its meaning. On speaking about the ideas of Skalbe\'s fairytales it is useful to remember the definition of Prof. A. Svabe, as to where the fairy-tales of Skalbe differ from those of Wilde: "The hero of the legends of Skalbe is Virtue. By this fact Skalbe differs from the great British aesthete Wilde whose hero is Beauty." The ideas of Skalbe\'s fairy-tales are always determined by a virtue of the moral, social or national life. In his youth Skalbe turned against the peace and contentedness of the civic middleclass society. It was the virtue of struggle and of alertness that brought the hero of Skalbe to the cognition of the immeasurably wide range of the real life. Later on the heroes of Skalbe begin to return from the wide to a narrower range, even to contentment because they are seeking deepness of soul instead of the wide range now. This seeking of deepness is combined with the Latvian, this rustic nation\'s mentality of adherence to that narrow strip of earth wher* one has been born and has grown up. Thus in Skalbe\'s fairy-tales love towards the home-country is made the highest virtue. The crowned peasants of Skalbe have everything their heart may desire, but they feel unhappy as they yearn for the water which they drank in their youth from a spring at their homeplace- At another time the unrest is caused by the wish to pay a visit to one\'s first love who has been a simple rustic girl. There are several fairytales in which Skalbe has depicted the longing of a peasant youth to go into the wide world and look for a well-to-do and easy life. But at its most sublime we find this theme in the symbolic fairy-tale about the giant, who can be cheated by every cowherd-boy in the wide world and who grows worn-out and loses his strength while roaming in the world, aimlessly and without work, because he cannot find his proper place any more. Only when he digs himself deep into the earth again and starts carrying a mountain on his back as before, the giant lives a full life and is happy again. Those who are familiar with the history of the Latvian people, will understand that love towards his native country to a Latvian has always been connected with the virtues of simplicity and contentment. In Skalbe\'s fairy-tales these virtues have culminated in asceticism. Thus Karlis Skalbe, this writer of a rustic people, using an imprisoned prince as his spokesman, declares the sunbeam, water and rye-bread to be the highest treasures of the world; but the fisherman Jurgis throws into the water his only wealth, a silver beaker, only in orde. to get rid of the devils temptations and to be able to go on loving his simple life. And let us not forget to mention with honour "the most delightful virtues" of the granny: "the Virtue of Knitting, the Virtue of Darning, and the Virtue of Giving Gifts". "And then there Was one more virtue; it looked as a squirrel with a bent yellow little tooth: it was the Virtue of Gnawing Bred-Crusts." Skalbe has not written any legends about the saints of Christianity although several fairy-tales have been written influenced by the church festivals, especially by Christmas and Easter, and are intended for shaping the feelings of those festivals into poetic characters. But the virtue of Christian charity has dictated the gospel-like fairytales about the little daughter of an executioner and a Pussy\'s water-mill. In the hoarse throat of the executioner a peculiar sound is vainly seeking expression; but the executioner\'s little daughter, who sacrifices her life for a hero sentenced to death, finds out this holy word of life. Ascending the gallows she will tell this word to the witnesses of the public execution. This word is — charity. In the other fairy-tale Pussy meets one of the peculiar kings of Skalbe. Pussy might be the-very model of those "blessed" ones mentioned in Christ\'s Sermon on the Mount, but the king might have the title "rex dolorosus", as he mourns for all the sufferings inflicted upon the human beings as well as the animals and the beasts in the world. Neither Pussy nor the king are able to fight against the evil of the world. But both of these characters, idealized to the highest degree, join in the convention that evil must not be remembered and that one must not increase sufferings in the world. It would be a misunderstanding to think that virtue being such a dominant power in determining the ideas of Skalbe\'s fairy-tales, the result might be didacticism. It is not so. Not moralization, but an aesthetic experience is the aim of Skalbe-If the reader, influenced by the qualitative contents of the aesthetic experience, makes up his mind to become better, this is only a testimony of the catharsis which since the times of Aristotle has been mentioned as the mark of every eminent work of art on the public. It does not matter whether this catharsis displayed by the fairy-tales of Skalbe is of a religious, a national, or of a social character: after one has been purified by it, one obtains the visa to that world of a soulful beauty about which the writer has told in his very last fairy-tale: \'There beside blue waters golden trees stand in blossom every wish is fulfilled there, and the days pass in smiles and in playing." This takes place even if one like the hero of this last fairy-tale — the sailor Gaigals, who has lost his native country, is persecuted everywhere, having no rights whatever any more ... As an author of fairy-tales Skalbe holds quite a peculiar and distinguished place in Latvian literature. Nowadays the fairy-tale as a branch of literature is confronted with no bright prospects. But it is able to show many a characteristic feature in the face of the writer\'s nation, perhaps in a certain sense better than the other branches of literature might do it. He who will have the opportunity of spending some time in the world of Skalbe\'s fairytales will make friends with the Latvian. There he will make the acquaintance with a nation which has a quite unusual sense of life and a consciousness of cosmic deity. Translated by Janis Baltaks.', 'On the Bank of the Amata Arveds Svabe The Latvian folk-tales i. c. fairy-tales and legends were collected considerably later and written down in comparatively lesser number than the folk-songs. The elementary school teacher and writer Ansis Lerchis-Puskaitis (1859—1903) commenced the systematic collecting and publishing of folk-tales and collected 6000 legends and fairy-tales. His work (7 volumes, 1891—1903), however, remained unfinished. At the same time, the Latvian Learned Societies published some small collections of fairytales Unfortunately all these collections lacked system or the system adopted was based on theories (c. g. B. B. Tylor\'s doctrine of animism), already given up by scientists. In Northern Europe and in the Baltic, the scientific investigation of fairytales more and more followed the so-called Finnish School established by the Finnish folklorists X a a r 1 c X r oh n and Antti Aarne with their internationally known series of publications "Folklore Fellow Communications." A. Aarne\'s merit was that of succeeding in setting up, after a long investigation, the first general schedule for the types of fairytales (Verzeichnis der M&rchentypen, Helsinki 1910) which contained 485 types of fairy-tales and their variations widely known in Europe. According to this Aarne\'s schedule, I already in 1923 commenced to compile anew and to publish the Latvian fairy-tales (8 booklets), but I was prevented from finishing this work. The Latvian synologist Peteris Smits (1869—1938) succeeded in doing this. He compiled anew and published, from-1925 till 1938, the most complete collection of the Latvian fairy-tales and legends in 15 volumes (7636 pages) containing 7895 items including variations. This collection closely respects the principles set up by Aarne. It is advisable to find out the difference theoretically between legends and fairy-tales though practically it is difficult to draw the boundary line, for a legend can turn into a fairy-tale as easy as a fairy-tale can turn into a legend. The fairy-tale is a story about wonderful events occurring outside historical time and a fixed geographical spot. A formula like "once upon a time, once, long ago, in ancient times, in early times" etc is usually used to point out the time, or — the time of the events is not mentioned at all and merely the verbs in the past give an indication to the hearers that the activity is not of today. The spot of the events is pointed out by such formulas: "far, very far away," or "in the world, in a land, in a town, in a parish, on a hill, on an estate, in apoor cottage" etc In the most ancient fairy-tales this favourite formula is used: "Beyond three-timesnine (3X9) forests, three-times-nine seas, threetimes-nine hills in a land far away." As is ascertained now this formula used in the Latvian fairy-tales, folk-songs, and magic has originated in Asia Minor and is connected with the lunar caLATVIAN FOLK-TALES lendar based upon the phases of the waxing and the waning moon. Astronomy, cultivated by priests, imputed especial magical meaning to the figures 3, 9 and 3X9. Therefore in the oriental fairy-tales composed by the educated classes very often occur: 3 king\'s sons, 3 devils, 3 dragons, 3 snakes having 3, 6 and 9 heads which the hero has to hew off with a sword weighing 3, 6 or 9 "birkavs" — an ancient Latvian weight of about 3 cwt), before this having strengthened himself with a restorative from 3 vessels. The formula 9 or 3X9 was profaned later and what the ancient Latvians meant by it was that the numbers of things was so large that it was not possible to count them by the fingers of both arms c. g- the vessel cracked into three-times-nine pieces. The description of the heroes and other persons mentioned in the fairy-tales is in the same measure indefinite as the time and the space. These persons usually are not called by names, only their professions, social positions and relationships are mentioned: a king, the king\'s son or daughter, a man and his wife, a sister and her brother, three brothers, an old soldier, a beggar, an apprentice seeking his master, a traveller, an inn-keeper, a peasant, an owner of an estate, a step-mother and her step-children, God and the devil etc The heroes are not characterised in detail — only one or two physical or spiritual qualities are given (foolish — clever, bad — good, beautiful — ugly, strong — feeble) which then determine their activity and even their fate of them. When yet sometimes the heroes are called by names (c. g. Bearkiller, Pinedrawer, Hillroller etc) then they cannot be regarded as real proper names but only as those denoting the inherited qualities of the heroes: a man who is capable to kill bears, to pull out pinetrees, to roll away hills etc On the other hand, a legend is always connected with a fixed place or person. The fairy-tale tells about "a king,\'\' the legend tells about King Priam, Romulus or fCurbads, the hero of Kurzeme *(Couronia). The fairy-tale mentions "a castle", but in the legend the events occur at a fixed geographical spot c. g. Riga, the Daugava etc. Contrary to the fairy-tales where only little attention is paid to the previous life of the heroes, the legends try to explain where, when and why the event happened in such a way and not otherwise. The legend is also history, but history of a legendary kind. Therefore, contrary to the fairy-tales which follow international themes, legends handle national motives or try at least to localize travel motives. The authors of fairy-tales let their heroes act in the irrational world of magic together with or in opposition to miraculous forces and prove their action by rules of voluntary and emotional think ing, while the authors of legends try to solve their problems by means of rational, discursive thinking. Of the Latvian legends, we must mention the cosmogonial legends in the first place- They have originated partly under the influence of the Bible stories dealing with the creation of the earth, but in distinction from the Bible, God does not act single-handed but together with the devil in the Latvian cosmogonial legends. There are comparatively few folk-traditions of such a kind in Western Europe, but many in the Eastern regions of ancient Byzantium, Armenia, Mesopothamia, Syria. In the time from the Bth till the 10th century the Christian gnostics and other sectants brought them to the Balkan Peninsula especially to Bulgaria and Roumania. It is to be supposed that these Balkan cosmogonial traditions with the mediation of the Western Slavs (Poles) have travelled into the Baltic where they spread widely. The doctrine about God and the devil as two creators of the world, however, has not originated in Byzantium but corresponds to the Persian cosmogonial dualism according to which Ahuramazda, assisted by Ariman, created the world. There is. however, a difference namely that the devil in the Latvian myth does not help God, but aims to disturb and destroy his work, or to create evil beings which then try to exterminate God\'s beings. For example, the legend that the wolf is created by the devil is known to all Baltic nations md to the Russians, Poles, Roumanians and Albanians.
