Altough the cosmogonial dualism is not a Latvian doctrine the figures of God and the devil have gained real national features to such a degree that they cannot be deparated from history of Latvia. The sociological aspect of the Latvian devil is formed according to the prototype of the Baltic "baron," but the Latvian God is only an idealized old farmer and their mutual antagonism reflects the centuries long struggle between the Latvian peasant and the German estate owner. By way of contamination from some other cycles of fairy-tales in these cosmogonial legends God can be replaced by a clever or strong man (c. g. the clever brother, strong Bearkiller etc) and the devil — by an evil giant or monster (c- g. a dragon, a bear etc). The motif itself — about the fatal struggle between the just and the unjust, the good and the evil — remains unchanged- To illustrate this it suffices to remind how th Latvian fairy-tales and legends describe the devil- People call him ..Black Master" because, contrary to the Latvian peasant wearing gray homemade clothes, the German knights and feudals wore black tailored suits. The devil goes about in a luxurious carriage driven by a coachman or rides on a magnificent riding-horse to inspect his district- The devil has a large countryestate and a castle with many rooms, in the cabinet there is a bookcase containing many books in foreign languages, mainly German, there are old chronicles, luxurious works on arts and necromancy- The devils speak German among themselves though they speak Russian too, but with their servants the devils speak only Latvian. The devils engage the Latvians as their coachmen, servants or common workmen and they have to be skilled in all peasats\' works such as ploughing, sowing, mowing, thrashing, grinding and baking bread for his master s needs- When engaging a servant the devil demands that the servant should not know neither to read nor to write, nor to speak any other language but Latvian- It is dear that this devil of the fairy-tales is composed by the fancy of the people from elements which were to be found on the German knights1 estates. On the other hand — the devil is usually represented in a legend as an envious neighbour who tends to disturb the seasonable works of the busy and honest farmer — namely God — sowing weeds into his fields, strewing stones onto his meadows, clamming up rivers in order to destroy mills etc The so-called aethiological legends are closely related to the just named cosmogonial ones. These aethiological legends try to explain in a naive manner, often according to the pattern of the Bible, why some animals or plants have this or another shape, colour or function. For example the Latvian, Polish and Roumanian legends explain the well-known fact that the mole runs across the road with God\'s prohibition for it to run along the road because the mole did not obey the invitation to join the workmen once building the road. Likewise people tend to explain why the ash is called the blind tree, why the raven is black, why the fox has a white tail-top, why the cat catches mice, why the wolf has a black snout, why the hare has a split lip etc. Both these groups of legends gradually change into geographical and historical legends. Their international subjets, having magical, balladical or romantical arrangement are often localized. There are legends about hills made by devils; about lakes fetched by God from one place to another in the shape of a cloud in order to drown a castle or a farm to punish it; about mysterious churches on the shore of the sea, where a ship arrives in the night once a year and the priest has to marry a strange couple; about the church-bells sunk down in a swamp; about a cave in the rocks where a beautiful young woman is held imprisoned. Being prose compositions, legends generally are shorter and simpler in composition than fairy-tales. Legends whose kernel of the fable has grown up from a magical or religious belief can be considered as the elder, those where an anecdote or an adventure is arranged in the form of a short story of the Renaissance period can be taken for younger legends. On the other hand, fairy-tales as artistic prose compositions bear evidence of a certain literary technique, elaborated by unknown masters, the stylistic and compositional means of which are to be found in folk-epics too. The. fault of the earlier compilers and publishers of the fairy-tales was nut of considering each fairy-tale as an independent original with its homogeneous composition They separated only those compositions which differred from a type or cycle of similar fairy-tales as to their language (c. g. written in a dialect) or Couple of Latvian folk-dancers at D. P. Camp Meerbeck Display of national costumes at D. P. Camp Meerbeck style. But careful literary analysis proved that, in spite of the difference in language and style, many nations had common fairy-tales, and that the likeness existed not only as to their contents but also as to such trifles of their form as the so-called polygenesis theory, teaching that similar legends and fairy-tales were composed by various ancient nations without any mutual influence, could no longer be recognized. The anthropologists supporting this theory devoted their attention merely to the mythological contents of the fairy-tales, but kept their literary form in the background. On the contrary, the Aryan school, established by philologists, regarded fairy-tales as heritage common to all Aryans in the same way as the Indo-European languages. They thought that the fairy-tales had originated by way of the degeneration of myths, but myths according to them had grown up from the cult of nature. This they tried to prove with the writings of the ancient Hindoos. So this movement, contrary to the school of the anthropologists, ackonowledges monogenesis instead of polygenesis but only as far as it concerns the spiritual heritage of the ancient Indo-Europeans. The interest about the ancient Hindoo literature increased by the efforts of .linguists, urged the sanskritist Th. Benfey to establish a new theory about the origination of fairy-tales. Th. Benfey seeks the birthplace of fairy-tales in India, where they have been composed by the buddhists. Following the expansion of Buddhism, fairy-tales were brought first to China, later to Tibet and Mongolia, but during the time of the Crusade, together with the Arabian fairy-tales, also to the Balkan, Italy, Spain, where they were translated into Latin already in the 13th century and began their triumph over the whole continent. Thus contrary to the theories mentioned above, which sought the origin of fairy-tales in the time of the prehistorical myth, Th. Benfey considers them as comparatively young artistic prose works expanded over various countries by means of writings. No doubt, this theory exaggerates the part of India played in the origination and that of literature played in the expansion of the fairy-tales. The author himself had to recognize that India could not be the birthplace of legends but this was more probably to be sought in Greece, where Aesop composed the oldest samples in this branch. It is impossible, too, to prove that most of the fairytales have spread among the people by means of books, for during the Middle Ages the peasants and artisans did not know how to read. Thus, for example, the translation of „The Arabian Nights" appeared in Latvian only in 1866, but the Latvian arrangement of Aesop\'s fables in 1766 while the verbal traditions of the Latvians and other Baltic peoples knew fairy-tales and legends with contents resembling these much earlier. Th. Benfey*s theory had an important influence on the development of the science of comparative folklore. His theory taught the scientists to regard fairy-tales as artistic prose works and to look for their patterns not only in the area of the Indo-European culture, but also in countries of Buddhism and Mohammedanism, pointing out that the origination of fairy-tales under the influence of some other confessional system did not prevent the import of them into the world of the Christian civilisation. In possession of the chief idea of Th. Benfey s doctrine — about the spreading of fairy-tales — the Finnish folklorists\' school utilized it in its further research work proving that fairy-tales and legends as to their composition were easily to be separated into the original sujets and the literary motives which though typically similar with certain cycles of fairy-tales, but like a mosaic could be removed from them and used for another composition. Such a deforming of fairy-tales and legends can be explained by the fact that the tellers, knowing by heart 20—70 long tales, often forgot the beginning or the end of a tale or mixed the persons and contents of one tale with those of another thus causing defective text or contamination of it. Therefore, the Finnish school pays much attention to the critic of the text of the fairy-tales and legends and treats them in separate monographies with the usual philological methods of the history of literature. There are many instances among the Latvian fairy-tales showing how by forgetfulness the original text of a fairy-tale is altered. For example, the fairy-tale about the sorcerer\'s ring in its oldest version is to be found in India, and the kernel of it is what Buddhism teaches that beasts often can be more good-hearted than human beings: a Brahmin rescues from the gap a tiger, a monkey, a snake and a man; the beasts present him several worthy articles but the rescued man charges the Brahmin of theft so that the latter is sentenced to death. Then the snake rescues him. In this fairy-tale spreading from India to Finnland by verbal traditions the tropical beasts are replaced by arctical beasts, it loses Its dogmatical character too and the grateful beasts begin to play the main part until the ungrateful man disappears in the background and, in the end even vanishes. In some Latvian variations the beasts are forgotten too and the snake\'s present — the magical ring — becomes a remedy which enables the hero to gain the king\'s daughter. The opposite to forgetting is the amplification of fairy-tales by extending the beginning and the end of the tales with superfluous adornments or even with an episode from another analoguous fairy-tale. In the same manner as the short Latvian folk-songs often are strung into long chains, the teller of fairy-tales too joins many legends on the same theme (c. g. about God and the devil, about the fox and the wolf) in order to create a whole cycle of legends or even an epos. To escape some ocular dissonances which could be created by the alteration of something of little value, sometimes the whole contents of the tale has to be varied. These alterations are to be explained by the tendency of a talented teller to take an active part in altering the tale, to feel an independent author and to prevent the tale from becoming tedious. Even today actors are doing the same varying by altering and completing their rolls which they have to play many times. As mentioned before the poetics of fairy-tales and legends makes use of stylistic and compositional means used in the epic folk-poesy. In order to attract the listeners attention, the teller repeats three times a certain hero\'s action letting him overcome increasing difficulties gradually. Sometimes instead of such a gradation retardation is used in heroic tales, adding some unnecessary episodes to adjourn the expected solution. In some other cases when localization of the text takes place, a special or proper name is generalized (c. g. the Bulbul-Bird mentioned in the Arabian Nights, is called by the Latvians simply "magic bird" though its original meaning is that 6f a nightingale) or otherwise: an impersonal underground combatant receives the Latvian names — Kurbads, Lacausis, Dzelzs Martins (Martin Iron), Stiprais An*\'* (Jack the Strong) or simply Martins and Ansis. The real fairy-tales, however, respect the rule that the story must end happily so that the listener should remains thoroughly convinced that the good overcomes the evil, the just beats the unjust. As in the conscience of the Latvian nation, feudalism which made the German dominators of all legal and economic power, was the cause of nearly all evil and unjust, it is clear to every one of what socialpsychological importance the compositional manner of the folk-tales was to bring the dramatic struggle between the bright forces and the dark ones to such and end where the devil or his substitute was always defeated. The optimism of the folk-traditions strengthened morally the Latvian nation and maintained its belief that the divine truth, in fairy-tales represented by an old man in peasant\'s clothes (God) or by a national hero sent by God, sooner or later would help Latvia to regain all what was lost during her wars of liberation against the German invaders in the 13th century. This belief was connected with the conviction that in the world, besides power and cunning, reigns a good spirit, too, which, at the proper time and place, interferes personally or with his messenger (helpful beasts) into the struggle and; brings forth a favourable solution even in an apparently hopeless situation. Thus the divine stage-manager, by his intervention, prevents drama of the fairy-tale from turning into a tragedy ! It is a miracle from the view of discursive thinking, but we must keep in mind that the civilized! nations even now have belief in miracles and that the mentality supporting this belief even in times of our expressive rationalism has its firm footing; that some peculiar rules of thinking and some\' peculiar conceptions of causality are reigning there and it is the task of anthropology to clear them all up. It is true that nearly all primitive peoples! have their common beliefs in the human body and! soul, good and evil spirits, beasts and plants, their methamorphosis and the travel of souls. But the scientists are mistaken admitting that a myth, cult or belief can become a literary motif, to say nothing of their arrangement in the shape of a fairytale. Every fairy-tale is invented by an author. We do not know what inspired him to choose just such a compositional form and not another-this could be some visions seen in a dream, the nightmare of a sick man, visions caused by narcotics or the tendency of a talented artist to create his own artistic world. Be that as it may — when such fairy-tales composed by some individual authors pleased the listeners for the sake of their theme, arrangement or idea, they grew popular, spread from mouth to mouth, were remodeled and even vulgarized to suit the taste of uneducated classes but even then it is possible for the criticism of sources to trace the routes of the literary tradition and to discover their birth-places. This principle generally acknowledged in aesthetic folklore can be applied to the Latvian fairy-tales too: they usually have strange borrowed motifs and patterns, but as a literary commodity they circulate among people bearing their own national trademark. Translated by Adotfs Ermanovskis.']

Representative_Docs: ["Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš gratified with Council's conclusions on European conscience and totalitarianism On 15 June, Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš attended the meeting of the EU General Affairs and External Relations Council (GAERC) held in Luxembourg. At the GAERC meeting, the EU foreign ministers prepared for the European Council of 18-19 June as well as discussed topical issues of bilateral relations. The EU foreign ministers also approved the Council conclusions on European conscience and totalitarianism as well as a draft EU-U.S. joint statement on Guantanamo. While preparing for the European Council meeting of 18-19 June, the EU foreign ministers debated the issues on the European Council's agenda – the Lisbon Treaty and the EU's institutional issues; the economic, financial and social situation in the EU; climate change and sustainable development; illegal migration, as well as external relations. It is expected that the forthcoming European Council will achieve an agreement on the solution of legal guarantees for Ireland with regard to the Lisbon Treaty and on the appointment of the European Commission president. The European Council is to evaluate the measures taken to date for the stabilisation of financial markets and for economic recovery. The Member States will debate a new EU level framework for the monitoring of financial markets. In preparation for the United Nations Climate Change Conference in Copenhagen in December this year, the European Council will discuss the principles for financing climate change and the sharing among Member States of the relevant EU contribution. Minister Riekstiņš expressed satisfaction with the fact that the European Council is to address the issues of vital importance for Latvia – the EU Strategy for the Baltic Sea Region and the Baltic Region Energy Interconnection Plan. Minister Riekstiņš is gratified that the GAERC has approved the Council conclusions on European conscience and totalitarianism and that the Council supports the opinion by the European Parliament regarding the need to raise awareness in Europe on crimes perpetrated by totalitarian regimes. The GAERC approved the European Council's conclusions regarding Cuba. Last year the EU came forth with a proposal to resume a political dialogue with Cuba. On 17 September 2008, the Cuban government expressed its consent to renew a political dialogue with the EU and recognised that such a step was taken after the EU suspended diplomatic sanctions against Cuba in June. The Council exchanged views on the situation in Bosnia and Herzegovina in the light of the upcoming Peace Implementation Council's Steering Board meeting on 29 and 30 June 2009.", 'Foreign Minister takes part in annual meeting of Baltic and Nordic Foreign Ministers On 9 – 10 September Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš attended the annual meeting of Foreign Ministers of the Baltic and Nordic states (NB8) in Parnu, Estonia. During the meeting, the current situation in Georgia, relations with Russia, perspectives on Ukraine and Moldova, of Euro-Atlantic integration, the situation of security policy in Afghanistan, co-operation with countries of the Central Asian region, the peace process of the Near East and the European Union strategy for the Baltic Sea Region were all discussed. At the centre of discussions was the involvement of the international community in settling the Georgia and Russia conflict, especially taking into account the visits of France\'s President Sarkozy representing the presiding country of the European Union, to Moscow and Tbilisi on 8 September, at which agreement was reached on the decision of the Euro Council to send EU observers to Georgia. It is expected that a formal decision on sending a joint EU mission to Georgia, which, according to the agreement, should take effect on 1 October, will be passed at the regular meeting of Foreign Ministers in Brussels on 15 September. "At present, the offer by member states of the European Union to become involved in the EU mission in Georgia is only taking shape, and Latvia is also considering the possibility of participating, with consultations taking place with the relevant ministries", stated Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš. The ministers also exchanged views on the agenda of the EU, OSCE and UN concerning the Russian Federation, and were agreed on the need for an international donor\'s conference on assistance to Georgia. The Baltic and Nordic Foreign Ministers also discussed co-operation with Ukraine and Moldova, and the importance of their Euro-Atlantic perspectives in the democratic development of these countries. Foreign Minister Riekstiņš also drew the attention of his colleagues to co-operation with countries of the Central Asian region, reviewing the importance of these countries in the context of energy policy. During their meeting the ministers also reviewed the security policy situation in Afghanistan and the Middle East peace process. The issue of an EU strategy for the Baltic Sea Region and its future course, especially in the context of the coming Swedish presidency of the European Union, was also on the agenda. The meeting of foreign ministers in Pārnu at the invitation of the Estonian Foreign Minister Urmas Pact, was attended by the Foreign Minister of Iceland, Ingibjorg Solrūn Glslad6ttir, the Latvian Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš, Lithuanian Foreign Minister Petras vaitekunas, the Foreign Minister of the Kingdom of Norway, Jonas Gahr Store, the Foreign Minister of Finland Alexander Stubb, the Foreign Minister of the Kingdom of Sweden, Carl Bildt and the Secretary of State of the Foreign Ministry of the Kingdom of Denmark, Michael Zelmer-Johns. The next meeting of the Baltic and Nordic Foreign Ministers will take place next year in Iceland, which will also be the co-ordinating country for NB-8 countries in 2009.', 'Baltic Review PUBLISHED BY THE SYDNEY LATVIAN SOCIETY Foreign Minister discusses enlargement strategy of the Euro-Atlantic community at Brussels Forum From 14 to 16 March Minister of Foreign Affairs Maris Riekstiņš took part in the Brussels Forum, at which politicians and experts debate and exchanged views on current issues of transatlantic cooperation. On 15 March Minister Riekstiņš, Swedish Minister of Foreign Affairs Carl Bildt, the President of the Foreign Committee of the Rumanian Senate Mircea Dan Geoana and U.S. Senator George Voinovich participated in a panel discussion headed by Ronald d. Asmus, the Executive Director of the Transatlantic Centre of the German Marshall Fund of the United States. Topics of the debate included NATO and European Union enlargement, both in the context of the coming meeting of NATO heads of state and government in Bucharest, and also when evaluating future development perspectives of NATO and the European Union. The Foreign Minister noted that Latvia is able to share with present candidate states its unique experience which has been gained both on the way to NATO and the European Union, and also in its status as a full-fledged Member State of the Euro-Atlantic community. "It is important to be aware that the perspective of being a part of the Euro-Atlantic community is an excellent stimulus for candidate states to implement reforms, however, at the same time, when making decisions to admit new member states, a stable system for evaluating May, 2008 conformity with criteria and meeting of requirements must be followed," added Minister Riekstiņš. The Brussels Forum is an annual meeting of European and U.S. high ranking politicians, businessmen, academics, representatives of non-governmental organisations and of the media who discuss the challenges being faced in world politics. The agenda of this year\'s forum includes issues relating to the situation in the Western Balkan region, the enlargement of the Euro-Atlantic community, Russia\'s policies after the change of leadership, Afghanistan and China, as well as current questions of international trade policy and world economics, including energy security and climate change. The Brussels Forum is organised by the German Marshall Fund of the United States, in co-operation with partners the Egmont Institute, Daimler, Bertelsmann Stiftung, and also the Ministry of Defence of the Republic of Latvia. This year, with the support of the Latvian Ministry of Defence, participants and guests at the Brussels Forum will have the opportunity to sample Latvian national cuisine. UN Special Rapporteur encourages continuation of Latvia\'s policy in promotion of tolerance On 19 March, Doudou Diene, the United Nations Special Rapporteur on contemporary forms of racism, racial discrimination, xenophobia and related intolerance, presented a report on Latvia at the 7th session of the UN Human Rights Council. In his report the Special Rapporteur welcomes the fact that Latvia has adopted the necessary legislation and established a number of institutions with the purpose of tackling racial discrimination. Mr Doudou Diene also positively refers to how the Constitutional Court and the Ombudsman have contributed to fighting racial discrimination. The Special Rapporteur notes the Government\'s achievement in solving the problems that the Roma community faces in Latvia through implementation of the national action plan "Roma in Latvia 2007---2009". Special Rapporteur Diene calls on Latvia to continue its work on tackling intolerance and on promoting integration. The Foreign Ministry values the fact that the Special Rapporteur has become informed with Latvia\'s history and has analysed inter-ethnic contacts in relation to this history, mentioning also Latvia\'s centurylong traditions in the area of tolerance and the need to overcome the consequences of long occupation. Mr Doudou Diene previously visited Latvia from 20 to 24 September 2007 at the invitation of the Government of Latvia. During his stay, the Special Rapporteur met with state officials, representatives of NGOs and civil society, as well as visited the Olaine Prison and the Museum of the Occupation of Latvia. Foreign Minister and French Secretary of State for European Affairs sign memorandum on French language teaching On 31 March, Jean-Pierre Jouyet, the French Secretary of State for European Affairs, made a visit to Latvia. During the visit, Mr Jouyet met with representatives of the Foreign Ministry and discussed current events within the EU, including France\'s priorities for its presidency of the EU in the second half of 2008. At the meeting with Foreign Ministry State Secretary Normans Penke, the parties considered Latvia-France bilateral collaboration, especially noting the active ties in culture. Mr Penke and Mr Jouyet voiced satisfaction with the festivals Pārsteidzoša Latvija (Surprising Latvia) in 2005 in France and Francijas pavasaris (French Spring) in 2007 in Latvia, which resonated on an international level. The French Secretary of State for European Affairs provided information on France\'s priorities for its EU presidency. The parties shared opinions both on the newly established Reflection Group, with Vaira vlke-Freiberga as its Deputy Chairperson, and on the future prospects for Europe. The government representatives of Latvia and France exchanged experience on formats of regional co-operation, and their role in Europe. The Latvian side provided a briefing on its presidency at the Council of the Baltic Sea States (CBSS) and the upcoming summit of CBSS member states, which is due to take place on 3 and 4 June in Riga. Mr Jouyet, in turn, mentioned France\'s initiative of the Union for the Mediterranean and its key priorities in the areas of environment, civil security, and energy. Within the framework of the visit, Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš and the French Secretary of State for European Affairs signed the Agreement between the Government of the Republic of France and the Government of the Republic of Latvia on Mutual Protection of Classified Information and the Memorandum between the Republic of Latvia, the Republic of France, the International Organisation of the Francophonie, the Kingdom of Belgium, and the Grand Duchy of Luxembourg on the Multi-annual Programme of French Language Teaching in Public Administration Institutions of Latvia. "For the Latvian representatives, to be able to successfully work in EU institutions, as well in preparation for Latvia\'s presidency of the EU in 2015, the teaching of French takes an increasingly vital role in the education of civil servants," commented Minister Riekstiņš. Anders Fogh Rasmussen visits Latvia At the Riga Castle, President Valdis Zatlers welcomed the Prime Minister of the Kingdom of Denmark, Anders Fogh Rasmussen. During their meeting, the officials discussed the final documents of the NATO Summit in Bucharest and the compromises reached there during the debates, noting in particular the essential commitment alongside the military mission to strengthen the work of civil reconstruction in Afghanistan. The ratification process of the Lisbon Treaty in both countries was also a topic of discussion at the meeting, and the consequences of the ratification in areas such as the EU common foreign policy were considered, emphasizing the European Union\'s ability to address third countries "in one voice". The discussions also included the principles of the EU common energy policy relating both to energy efficiency as well as to source diversification. The officials also assessed the positive history of Latvian-Danish bilateral relations and considered further opportunities for cooperation in areas such as energy and within the Council of the Baltic Sea States. During his visit, the Danish Prime Minister also met with his Latvian counterpart, Prime Minister Ivars Godmanis. The officials discussed current issues of the European Union, including the Lisbon Treaty and its implementation, energy issues and climate change, regional energy-related projects and co-operation perspectives in the Baltic Sea Region. Prime Minister Ivars Godmanis meets with Finnish Prime Minister During his working visit to Finland, on 6 April 2008, the Latvian Prime Minister Ivars Godmanis met in Helsinki with Finnish Prime Minister Matti Vanhanen. During the meeting, the bilateral cooperation between Latvia and Finland regionally and within the EU was discussed, focusing on the energy issues and asking for Finland\'s support for Latvia in specific EU energy policy sectors. Prime Minister Godmanis confirmed that Latvia wished to promote bilateral economic co-operation in Latvia\'s priority industries. With respect to the relations with Russia, common issues within the EU were mainly focused on, such as vehicle queuing at the Russian border, Russia\'s discriminatory railway transit tariffs, and the increased Russian round timber export tariffs. The regional issues included work on CBSS reforms and on the new strategic priorities of the Council of the Baltic Sea States, and on the EU Baltic Sea Region strategy currently under preparation. Of the EU issues, the Prime Ministers exchanged views on the EU energy and climate policy. Prime Minister Godmanis\' working visit took place before the informal meeting in Lapland, Finland, of the prime ministers of the European liberal democratic parties, from 6 to 7 April 2008. Foreign Minister and German Federal Minister for Foreign Affairs discuss regional co-operation At their meeting on 9 April, Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš and German Federal Minister for Foreign Affairs Frank-Walter Steinmeier expressed satisfaction with the dynamic nature of bilateral relations between the two countries, and shared the opinion that regional co-operation should be reinforced. During the visit, Minister Riekstiņš briefed his German counterpart on the current news of Latvia\'s presidency of the Council of the Baltic Sea States (CBSS), including the summit of CBSS member state heads of government, which is due to take place in Riga on 3-4 June. As to the Treaty of Lisbon, both sides expressed the hope that it will be successfully ratified in all EU member states and will enter into force as expected – on 1 January 2009. The officials also acknowledged that the debate on the operation of EU institutions should be continued after the adoption of the Treaty. Similarly, when the implementation of the Treaty of Lisbon takes place, increased attention should be paid to issues that concern the establishment of the European External Action Service. The ministers also discussed current issues of international politics, including the NATO summit in Bucharest, which took place from 2 to 4 April. Mr Riekstiņš informed Mr Steinmeier about Latvia\'s contribution to the European Neighbourhood Policy, emphasizing in particular the reinforcement of relations with Ukraine and Georgia. At the meeting with Christoph Heusgen, the Advisor for Foreign and Security Policy to the Federal Chancellor, the officials discussed current developments in international politics. The conversation focused on Latvia\'s presidency of the CBSS and on the reform process at the organisation. Minister Riekstiņš noted that the present priorities of major significance for the future operation of the CBSS have been highlighted: energy, environment, economy, and civil security. Mr Riekstiņš also expressed the hope that Germany will actively engage in the reform process of the organisation. At the close of the visit, the Latvian Foreign Minister took part in the opening of the exhibition Geld in Lettland. Geschichte und Gegenwart {Money in Latvia. History and Today). The exhibition is being held as part of Essentia Baltica 2008, the German-Baltic year of culture. The Prime Minister meets representatives of the Swedish government and companies On 31 March, Prime Minister Ivars Godmanis of the Republic of Latvia, while on a working visit to Sweden, met Frederik Reinfeldt, Prime Minister of Sweden, Maud Oloffsson, Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Enterprise and Energy, and Anders Borg, Minister for Finance of Sweden, as well as with the management of the TeliaSonera company and the management of Svenska Kraftnat. The focus of the Prime Ministers\' meeting was on Europe\'s common policy and issues to be addressed during the Summit of the CBSS Heads of Government in June this year. The Latvian Prime Minister noted that the markets of Latvia and Sweden are closely linked with each other. Sweden, in both a direct and indirect way, is one of the key investors in Latvia in various sectors – banking services, telecommunications and other areas. The Prime Minister and the Minister for Finance discussed operations of the Swedish bank subsidiaries in Latvia in the area of housing and mortgage credits. They also analysed the structure of these credits and concluded that the share of doubtful loans is very low – up to 0.6%. The focus of the meeting between the Latvian Prime Minister, the Swedish Deputy Prime Minister and representatives of the Swedish energy company was mainly on the issues of constructing a link between Latvia and Sweden. During the meeting with the management of TeliaSonera, the sides repeatedly stated their position regarding the sale of Lattelecom and LMT shares.']

Representative_Docs: ['Baltic state officials meet with NATO Secretary-General On 12 September President Valdis Zatlers met with NATO Secretary-General Jaap de Hoop Scheffer in Riga. Their main attention was focused on such topics of security policy, the effect of the Georgian conflict on the security of Europe, NATO international peace-keeping missions and energy security and independence. "The situation in the world is changing and we, both as an alliance and each country individually, must be ready to face these changes and must be able to react to them, taking a strong stand on both the basic principles of collective security and on our common values", said the President, emphasising that this is ensured by both the joint military training and development of international training centres in the region, and regular patrolling of the Baltic air space. "NATO presence must be visible, so that every member of the population of Latvia may feel safe and sure of his/her future, living in a democratic country", added the President. During the meeting questions on energy independence were also discussed. Both parties agreed that in future the North Atlantic alliance must be more active in addressing the political dialogue with countries of both the Central Asia and Caucasus regions, thus emphasising their important place and role in the energy market of the future. It is also necessary to provide Georgia with economic assistance in its reconstruction and in future to back it on the reforms road to NATO, emphasised the President. During their meeting the future of NATO international peace-keeping missions were also discussed, focusing on the ISAF operation in Afghanistan, which is Latvia\'s priority mission. The officials also discussed the patrolling of the Baltic air space and other current issues of international security policy. On 12 September, as part of the visit by NATO Secretary-General Jaap de Hoop Scheffer to Latvia, a meeting took place with the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Latvia, Maris Riekstiņš, the Foreign Minister of Estonia, Urmas Pact, and the Foreign Minister of Lithuania, Petras Vaitekunas. During the meeting, the parties reviewed topical issues regarding NATO operations and the participation in these operations by the Baltic states, in particular in Afghanistan, which is an established priority in Latvia\'s security policy. The Baltic Foreign Ministers also debated with the NATO Secretary-General the transformation process of the Alliance, and the national contributions by the Baltic states within the framework of these processes. The Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš also pointed out that the joint NATO member state training, including the international mine-clearing operation Open Spirit 2008 which took place in Latvia today, is a good way of strengthening co-operation of the Baltic states with the framework of NATO, and this practice should be continued in the future. During the talks there was an exchange of opinions regarding security issues in relation to the crisis in Georgia and the active involvement of the international community in the settlement of the conflict. The role of NATO was examined, both in the context of practical, as well as political, support. The General-Secretary of NATO informed about his visit to Georgia together with Member State ambassadors scheduled for next week. The visit to Latvia by the General-Secretary had been planned in advance, and was organised as a part of the international mine-clearing operation Open Spirit 2008. During the visit the NATO General-Secretary also met with President Valdis Zatlers.', 'POSITION ON NATO ENLARGEMENT AND THE SECURITY OF CENTRAL AND EASTERN EUROPE Issue: With the end of the Cold War and the demise of the Warsaw Pact, the countries of Central and Eastern Europe have found themselves in a security vacuum, in a grey zone between NATO and the European Union on the one side and the Russian Federation on the other. As long as Russia remains weak, pursues democratic reforms at home, and foresakes expansionist policies abroad this vacuum could, perhaps, be tolerated, but. it cannot become a permanent condition. Europe\'s long term stability depends on a resolution of this state of affairs. The countries of Central and Eastern Europe desire to join the Euro-Atlantic alliance system and integrate into western European institutions, seeing in those institutions guarantees for their security and stable political and economic evolution. For its part, Russia opposes NATO enlargement to the east and seeks to maintain this grey zone as a region open to Russian influence and domination. Although commited to enlargement, NATO is hesitant and cautious about the extent of that enlargement and is unclear what a European wide security architecture should luok like. Indecisiveness on the part of NATO will provide Russia with a strategic opportunity to press her claims against NATO enlargement, particularly in relation to those countries that were former republics of the Soviet Union. Russia should not have an explicit or implicit veto right as to which countries should become members of NATO, and NATO should not exclude any country a priori. A European wide security system should be created to avoid any country from remaining in a security grey zone. Coucil of Central and East European Communities in Canada Rationale: Russia Over the past several years, developments show that Russia is reverting to its historical political traditions – authoritarianism at home and expansionism abroad. The recent presidential elections brought to power advocates of a strong Russian state for whom democratic values only have a superficial meaning. While Russia is unquestionably undergoing profound changes, no one should hold any illusions that Russia will soon emerge as a liberal democratic country based on the rule of Law. Some have suggested that she has already settled into a semi-feudal state, where the rule of personalities will be far more important than the rule of Law. Russian history does not inspire confidence in her ability to readily adopt democratic values and a free enterprise system, nor under these conditions will she reject atavistic yearnings for empire. Preocupied by the outward manifestations of power, Russia has traditionally preferred to conquer or dominate her neighbours rather than improve conditions for her people. Henry Kissinger has succinctly stated: "A country of that size ought not to suffer from claustrophobia. Yet creeping expansionism has been a recurring theme of Russian history. For four centuries, Russia has subordinated the well-being of its own population to this relentless outward thrust and threatened all of its neighbours with it." Her actions in Chechnya reveal a Russia that has no hesitation in using raw military power to achieve her ends, and in the Caucuses and in Central Asia she is quite capable of taking advantage of that region\'s political instability to impose a Russian solution on these countries, despite her own seeming weakness. Central and Eastern Europe For Central and Eastern Europe the issue is not immediate military agression, though it cannot be ruled out entirely Rather as Col. J. Dalbins, Commander of the Latvian armed forces has charaterized the situation: "The main threat is perceived to be that of being brought back into political, economic and military sphere of influence of Latvia\'s largest neighbour." Being left in a grey zone of insecurity would provide an incentive for aggressive Russian diplomacy to draw those countries into the Russian sphere of influence, creating political instability and jeopardizing reforms, from which Europe as a whole would not be isolated. The most vulnarable group of countries arc those that were part of the former Soviet union, those that Russia calls its \'near abroad\'. In Europe these include: Ukraine, the Baltic states. Belarus and Moldova. The latter has been brought to submission through the actions of the Russian Army led by general Lebed, and Belarus has \'petitioned\' to join in closer federation with Russia and the CIS. To what extent these two countries can be \'rescued\' is open to question, though there is nothing to justify their isolation from wider European developments. It is critical that Ukraine maintain its independence and not be intimidated to follow the Belarus example. The Baltic states too are vulnarable. They have already been invited to join the CIS, but have refused. They know that if they are left in a grey zone, Russia will seek to ensure that they become compliant satellite states. Russia\'s pobcy toward the \'near abroad\' gives her the right to interfere in the internal affairs of these countries, a policy for which Russia has sought the West\'s endorsement. If Ukraine and the Baltic states were left out of any NATO security arrangement, the door would be left open for Russian actions to undermine these countries sovereignty and independence. While the Central European countries are less directly threatened than those countries bordering on Russia, they too are susceptible to potential political destabilization that an expansive Russia could initiate, particularly if Ukraine and the Baltic states were lost to Russian domination. An arms race could potentially ensue, distorting political and economic priorities and setting back reforms. There is indication that NATO will expand to include Poland, the Chech Republic, Hungary, and possibly Slovakia But, if enlargement stops there, the prospects for European security would not be substantially altered for the better. Leaving some countries in the grey zone would likely intensify Russian-Western rivalry, creating for advantage from both sides. All of Central and Eastern Europe needs to be included in a pan-European security architecture, based on NATO membership or an expanded Partnership for Peace program that makes the difference between membership or not minimal. The West The Euro-Atlantic alliance has understood the need for enlargement of NATO, not only to provide security guarantees for member states, but as a vehicle that the countries of Central and Eastern Europe continue to engage in democratic reforms and integrate into European-wide institutions. However, the extent of enlargement has been blurred by indecision and caution, promted by expectations over domestic Russian politics. If Russia goes nationalist and communist, expansion can be hurried, if Russia maintains a reformist course enlargement can be slowed. Seeking to find fundamental differences in outlook of foreign policy between reformers\' and \'nationalists/communists\' is largely an empty exercise. One only has to read the writings of Russian democrats to be disabused of the notion that Russian foreign policy would substantially change under their leadership. All political groupings in Russia continue to dream of great power status. For Russian politicians this is a defining issue. Russia can either achieve this great power status by pursuing realpolitik and the balance of power, or by folowing the common norms of an international civil society. There is no evidence to suggest that Russia will voluntarily pursue the second alternative. Russian behaviour, as was the case with the Soviet Union, will change only as a result of a clear and firm western policy of delineating the norms of acceptable international behaviour and requiring that Russia abide by those norms. To encourage Russia in that behaviour, there is undoubtedly a significant role to be played by a separate dialogue between her and NATO, one based on the transparency of military intentions. But such a dialogue needs to be fixed in a well-balanced security architecture that achieves genuine security for Central and Eastern Europe, while at the same time not isolating Russia from those developments. Options 1) Croaa-quaranteea There have been suggestions that one way to ensure security in Central and Eastern Europe would see both NATO and Russia enter into a strategic agreement guaranteeing this region\'s security. The Polish Prime Minister. W. Cimoazewicz. has summed up the dangers of this option: The concept of so-called guarantees for Central Europe by Russia and West European countries implies that the division of Europe will be preserved and various parts of Europe will continue to have an unequal security status. In particular, Central Europe will be kept as a \'grey zone\' which will remains an object and not an independent subject of European relations, and the states guaranteeing its security would retain the right to decide on the political future of this region". To all intents and purposes such an arrangement would constitute an updated version of the Yalta accord. 2) Limited Enlargement Under this scenario, NATO would expand to only a few countries, leaving the rest to fend for themselves as best they can. It should be clear that such an approach would create political instability in non-member countries, with each attempting to find alternative security arrangement*. Different alliances could be created, some oriented to the west, some to the east. New divisions in Europe would be the result, initiating rivalry and instability. Complete Enlargement Complete enlargement would hold out membership for all countries of Central and Eastern Europe, explicitly barring none, but recognizing that this process has to be managed in such a way that it does not create overt hostility in Russia, while satisfying the security needs of Central and Eastern Europe. This approach will demand sophisticated diplomatic initiatives and a well-conceived strategy. The fundamental assumptions of this strategy should include: – No country wishing to join NATO would be barred, all would be in a postion to join once they met NATO\'s criteria There would no deliation of which countries would be in the first, second or third rounds. – Those countries like Ukraine, which has not sought NATO membership, would be offered the opportunity of establishing a special NATO-Ukrainian security dialogue, while Belarus and Moldāva would be given ample opportunity to choose closer ties to NATO through Partnership for Peace program. – NATO would simultaneously offer Russia an enchanced security partnership, but would make clear the norms of behaviour that NATO expects it to follow in relation to its neighbours. Canada\'s role in NATO enlargement and its policy toward Central and Eastern Europe As part of NATO, Canada has a vital interest in furthering policies that create stability in Central and Eastern Europe. Canada along with her NATO allies has an historic opportunity to fundamentally alter the geopolitical landscape of Europe, ending the rivalries that have plagued Europe for the past centuries A Europe based on interstate cooperation and concensus building institutions is possible, one that consigns power politics, spheres of influence and geopolitical divisions to the historical dustbin. This vision of Euro-Atlantic cooperation, however, can only be sustained if NATO enlargement leaves no grey zones that allow great power politics to be played out again. The Council of Central and East European Communities in Canada believes that the third option outlined above provides the best approach to fulfilling Canada\'s own foreign policy objectives, as expressed in the Government Response to the Recommendations of the Special Joint Parliamentary Committee Reviewing Canadian Foreign Policy: "Canada\'s engagement with NATO must be seen within this evolving context. Canada has been one of the strongest proponents of the Partnership for Peace prpogram – a firet step toward the eventual expansion of the Alliance – as part of a foundation for a broader European collective security system that includes Russia and the other members of the FSU. Canada also strongly supports a special dialogue on future security structures with the Russian Federation to ensure that its security concerns are reflected in NATO\'s evolution." The Government of Canada has concurred with redefining security more broadly to encompass threats to stability, democracy and sustainable development In the context of Central and Eastern Europe, such a broad definition of security would require that Canada continue to maintain active programs of assistance to these countries, since, as the Canadian Government recognizes: "The establishment of open and prosperous market economies in the region will generate important trade and investment oppotunities for Canada, yielding direct benefits to the Canadian economy. Second, the integration of the regions\' economies into the global economic and trade systems will contribute to a more prosperous worl economy and economic gains for all. Third, the promotion and maintenance of stability in the region contributes directly to enhanced international, security and stability." It is with some apprehension that the Council notes the media reports that the Department of Foreign Affairs and International Trade is to be considerably downsized and many of its embassies and foreign offices closed. While some reorganization may be necessary, it is vital that Canada\'s presence in Central and Eastern Europe be maintained. Building sustained relationships in that region can only be achieved on a long term basis. Canada\'s engagement in Central and Eastern Europe will bear results over the long term, particularly once the security architecture is in place that allows economic issues to take priority over concerns for security. Canada has not fully realized its trade and commercial opportunities in the region. Diplomatic disengagement would have a chilling effect on the bilateral relations that Canada has with each of the countries in the region. Undoubedly, these countries will reflect on Canada\'s contribution to their collective security, whether viewed narrowly or broadly. □ • Council of Central and Eastern European Communities in Canada is formed from the following communities: Canadian Croation Congress. Polish Canadian Congress, Belarusan Canadian Alliance, Latvian National Federation, Estonian Central Council, Bulgarian Community, Lithuanian Community and Hungarian Canadian Federation who have representatives in it. Its president at this moment is Steven Walter from The Hungarian Canadian Federation.', 'Baltic state officials meet with NATO Secretary-General On 12 September President Valdis Zatlers met with NATO Secretary-General Jaap de Hoop Scheffer in Riga. Their main attention was focused on such topics of security policy, the effect of the Georgian conflict on the security of Europe, NATO international peace-keeping missions and energy security and independence. "The situation in the world is changing and we, both as an alliance and each country individually, must be ready to face these changes and must be able to react to them, taking a strong stand on both the basic principles of collective security and on our common values", said the President, emphasising that this is ensured by both the joint military training and development of international training centres in the region, and regular patrolling of the Baltic air space. "NATO presence must be visible, so that every member of the population of Latvia may feel safe and sure of his/her future, living in a democratic country", added the President. During the meeting questions on energy independence were also discussed. Both parties agreed that in future the North Atlantic alliance must be more active in addressing the political dialogue with countries of both the Central Asia and Caucasus regions, thus emphasising their important place and role in the energy market of the future. It is also necessary to provide Georgia with economic assistance in its reconstruction and in future to back it on the reforms road to NATO, emphasised the President. During their meeting the future of NATO international peace-keeping missions were also discussed, focusing on the ISAF operation in Afghanistan, which is Latvia\'s priority mission. The officials also discussed the patrolling of the Baltic air space and other current issues of international security policy. On 12 September, as part of the visit by NATO Secretary-General Jaap de Hoop Scheffer to Latvia, a meeting took place with the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Latvia, Maris Riekstiņš, the Foreign Minister of Estonia, Urmas Pact, and the Foreign Minister of Lithuania, Petras Vaitekunas. During the meeting, the parties reviewed topical issues regarding NATO operations and the participation in these operations by the Baltic states, in particular in Afghanistan, which is an established priority in Latvia\'s security policy. The Baltic Foreign Ministers also debated with the NATO Secretary-General the transformation process of the Alliance, and the national contributions by the Baltic states within the framework of these processes. The Foreign Minister Maris Riekstiņš also pointed out that the joint NATO member state training, including the international mineclearing operation Open Spirit 2008 which took place in Latvia today, is a good way of strengthening co-operation of the Baltic states with the framework of NATO, and this practice should be continued in the future. During the talks there was an exchange of opinions regarding security issues in relation to the crisis in Georgia and the active involvement of the international community in the settlement of the conflict. The role of NATO was examined, both in the context of practical, as well as political, support. The General-Secretary of NATO informed about his visit to Georgia together with Member State ambassadors scheduled for next week. The visit to Latvia by the General-Secretary had been planned in advance, and was organised as a part of the international mine-clearing operation Open Spirit 2008. During the visit the NATO General-Secretary also met with President Valdis Zatlers.']

Representative_Docs: ['Results of 10th Saeima Elections The voter turnout for Latvia\'s 10th Saeima (parliament) elections on October 2, was 934336 or 62.63% of the electorate, according to the Central Election Commission. Voter turnout saw a slight increase compared to previous general elections in Latvia. In 2006, turnout was 60.91%. Preliminary results received from 1010 polling stations out of 1013, indicated the following: "Unity" (party alliance of New Era, Society for Other Politics, and Civic Union), represented by Prime Minister Valdis Dombrovskis, received 30.76% of votes or 33 seats in the 100-seat parliament. Coalition partners "Union of Greens and Farmers" (party alliance of Latvian Farmers\' Union and Latvian Green Party) received 19.41% or 22 seats and the Party association "All for Latvia" – "For Fatherland and Freedom / LNNK" received 7.56%, 8 seats. Together, existing coalition received strong representation in the new parliament with 63 members. "Harmony Centre" (association of Daugavpils City Party, National Harmony Party, New Centre, Socialist Party of Latvia, and Social Democratic Party) was the second highest vote-getter in thte election with 25.67% or 29 seats. "For Good Latvia" (association of People\'s Party with alliance of Latvia\'s First Party and Latvia\'s Way), had the lowest vote count among those who qualified, with 7.54% approval and 8 seats. Arnis Cimdars, Head of the Central Election Commmission, said: "No serious violations have been reported during Latvia\'s parliamentary elections that might change their outcome". The Organisation for Security and Co-operation in Europe observation mission in Latvia reported that elections generally met OSCE and international standards for democratic elections. "The election process provided voters with the opportunity to make a free choice among candidates presenting different political alternatives," said Nikolai Vulchanov, head of the ODIHR limited election observation mission. The Saeima elections were held in five constituencies: Rīga, Vidzemē, Latgale, Zemgale and Kurzeme. Each constituency has different candidate lists and voters can only select from the candidate lists from the constituency they are voting in. Voters residing abroad can participate in elections by attending any of the 64 polling stations established abroad or by postal voting organised by 24 of those polling stations. Voters abroad voted for the Rīga candidate lists. Only those lists of candidates which have received at least 5% of the total number of votes cast in all five election constituencies are elected to the Saeima.', "CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER Saeima Elections Lead To Victory For Latvia's Way The elections to Latvia's first post-communist, post occupation parliament, the sth Saeima, marked a turning point in the renewal of Latvian sovereignty and self-determination. A process begun just more than three years ago, on May 4, 1990, came to a triumphant close as Latvian citizens, both in Latvia and abroad, cast their ballots for candidates representing 23 election slates. Chicago-area Latvians were actively involved in the election process, with nearly four hundred voting in person and many more by mail. The elections also marked the end of the Latvian voter registration drive in the United States, which had been underway since last November. According to figures supplied by the American Latvian Association, more than 9,000 Latvians reactivated their Latvian citizenship, enabling them to vote in the Saeima elections. In spite of difficulties in obtaining registration codes for many who registered in the U.S., 8,064 people actually voted in the United States, or 90.44% of the total number of 8,916 to whom the Embassy of Latvia in Washington was able to distribute election materials. The elections, which took place June 5-6 in Latvia, and from May 29-June 2 outside of Latvia, resulted in a resounding victory for the center-right coalition Latvijas ceļš (Latvia's Way), which is made up of prominent Latvian and emigre politicians. An additional seven candidate slates, including Latvijas Nacionālās Neatkarības Kustība (National Independence Movement of Latvia-LNNK), Saskaiļa Latvijai-Atdzimšana Tautsaimniecībai (Harmony for Latvia-Renewal for the Economy-SLAT), Demokrātiskā Centra Partija (Democratic Center Party-DCP), Latvijas Zemnieku Savienība (Farmer's Union of Latvia-LZS) and Latvijas Kristīgo Demokrātu Savienība (Christian Democratic Union of Latvia-LKDS) also won more than 4% of the total votes cast, entitling them to seats in the 100-member Saeima. The Saeima political spectrum ranges July/August 1993 – Vol. XIII, No. 6 Chicago Voters Place Their Trust In LNNK from the nationalist Tēvzemei un Brīvībai coalition, with six seats, to the pro-Russian Ravnopraviye list, which includes among its seven deputies-elect former Communist Party boss Alfreds Rubiks, who is currently standing trial for attempting to overthrow the Latvian government by force in January and August, 1991 (election results follow in this issue of CLN). One of the interesting conclusions that can be drawn from the election results is the enormous faith placed in the emigre Latvian community by the Latvian electorate. Despite the fact that voters abroad only made up 1.6% of the total electorate, nineteen gained seats in the Saeima, 12 from the United States. Latvians living in the Chicago area were actively involved in both the registration drive and the election itself. According to Illinois registration coordinator and election commission Chairman Ilmārs Bergmanis, 837 people registered their desire to renew their Latvian citizenship at registration points in Illinois. Of this number, 377 actually voted in person at one of three precinct stations set up in the Chicago area, or in Rockford. The remaining persons who voted did so by mail, and were included in the aggregate totals for the United States. These results, along with those from 16 other countries outside of Latvia, were counted as part of the Riga election district. Chicago's results differed from the U.S. and Latvian totals in the victory gained by the National Independence Movement of Latvia (LNNK) slate. Of the 375 valid ballots cast, 166 went to the LNNK. Latvijas ceļš placed second, with 112 votes, followed by the Farmer's Union of Latvia (53 votes), Christian Democratic Union of Latvia (15), Tēvzemei un Brīvībai (12), Popular Front of Latvia (8), Democratic Center Party (3), Anti-Communist Union (2), Green List (2) and Latvijas laime and Economic Activity League (1 each). Once this information was known, the difficult process of coalition building began. As this issue of CLN goes to press, it is becoming clear that Latvijas cels will form a ruling coalition with the LZS, with a total of 48 seats. Talks continue with the LKDS, DCP and LNNK in an attempt to secure for the coalition an outright majority of at least 51 seats. The sth Saeima is scheduled to meet in its first session on July 6, at 12:00 noon in the Saeima building. Among the first actions scheduled to be taken by the Saeima will be the election of Latvia's first post-occupation President. Candidates include two emigre deputies-elect (Gunārs Meierovics-LC and Aivars Jerumanis-LKDS) and the nephew of Latvia's last elected President, Kārlis Ulmanis (Guntis Ulmanis-LZS). Latvijas ceļš has also put forward candidates for parliamentary and government leadership positions. Current Supreme Council Chairman Anatolijs Gorbunovs will be nominated to continue this role, now with the title of Chairman of the Saeima. Current Deputy SC Chairman Valdis Birkavs will be nominated as Prime Minister, with Latvian Ambassador to Germany Egīls Levits to be tapped as Justice Minister, SC Permanent Economic Commission Chairman Ojārs Kehris as Economic Reform Minister, and Foreign Ministry State Secretary Māris Gailis as State Minister. Other minister candidates will probably become known as the coalition process continues. □", 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER Latvian Decision 1995: 6th Saeima Election Preview When: The elections to Latvia\'s parliament, the 6th Saeima, are scheduled for September 30October 1, 1995. Who can vote: All registered citizens of the Republic of Latvia who have reached the age of 18 by the first election day, to include those residing outside of Latvia, are eligible to vote. Eligible voters should have received a mailing from the Embassy of Latvia and the ALA informing them about the 6th Saeima elections and their eligibility to vote in the Riga district. If you are eligible and have not received this mailing, contact the Embassy (202-726-8213). Where/How can one vote: Eligible voters residing outside of Latvia may vote either in person at a designated Latvian Embassy or consular office, or by mail. In the U.S., one may vote in person at the Latvian Ev. Lutheran Church of Washington, DC (400 Hurley Avenue, Rockville, MD 20850), between the hours of 8:00 AM and 8:00PM on either election day. A Latvian passport or proof of personal code form must be brought to the polling station in order to vote. In order to vote by mail, eligible voters residing in the U.S. must complete and mail a written request to vote by mail and their Latvian passport or proof of personal code form to the Embassy of Latvia (4325 17th Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20011) by September 10. The passport or proof of personal code form will be returned by mail to the sender along with official ballots and election materials. All ballots must be mailed on one of the two election dates, and post-marked on one of those dates (09/30/95, 10/01/95) or on the Monday following the election (10/02/95), to be considered valid. Who is elected: 100 deputies will be elected by strict proportional representation from five electoral districts: Riga – 27 seats; Kurzeme – 14 seats; Zemgale – 15 seats; Vidzeme – 25 seats; Latgale – 19 seats. A barrier of five percent of the votes cast nationally has been set for parties to be represented in the 6th Saeima. Former KGB agents and persons lacking full Latvian language skills cannot be elected to the Saeima. Individuals found to have been active members of either the Latvian Communist Party or the All-Union Salvation Committee ("Communists") after January 13, 1991 are scratched from election slates; those found to have been agents and collaborators with the KGB or other security forces ("Agents") are exposed publicly, but allowed to stand for election.
