Every attempt to characterize a people meets with considerable difficulties from the methodological point of view. Indeed, how can one to put into a formula a definition of millions of human beings— each with his own peculiarities of character—that form a nation? It seems such an attempt would invariably lead to failure even if we were to proceed in opposite directions, for, on the one hand, a description of national character reduced to only one type is arbitrary and subjective, on the other hand, an attempt to take into consideration all variations of character found in a nation results in chaos, in the denial of any kind of national character whatsoever, as every nation has representatives of types opposed in character to the others. In spite of all this there can be no doubt that every nation has its own spirit which reveals itself in direct intercourse with representatives of this nation and finds its expression in productions of art, literature, and science. If'we do not wish to ignore the existence of such a national spirit, we must risk an attempt to grasp it with the means in our power. Indeed, the science of national psychology still lacks precise methods of investigation. Moreover, if we take into consideration the difficulties of individual characterology, we may even doubt whether such methods are possible at all, and so the chief means of characterising a nation remains intuition, which results from a close acquaintance with it and is continually supplemented and improved upon by confronting it with fact. As regards my own attempt to characterise the soul of the Latvian people, I find it necessary to state that I have taken into consideration not so much the nation as a whole as its "avantgarde," its leading and creative group, which has generally represented the whole nation and so given the word "Latvian" its peculiar flavor and created the idea of a typical Latvian. We can presume that this leading group which has been .mainly responsible for the fate of our nation is also the most perfect embodiment of its national genius, whereas the remaining members of the nation are more or less specific variations of the same type, endowed with the same inner structure, but different and even contrasting among themselves in their expressions. Before embarking, however, on a "static" analysis of Latvian consciousness, I should like to make clear the exact moment of historical development they have now arrived at. More than one description of this is possible. On the one hand, the Latvians are I one of the oldest of European nations — e.g., Latvian is closer related to the parent Indo-European language than the languages of many other nations — and in this respect the Latvian nation is older than eg., the French or the English. As has also been proved by recent archaeological excavations, the Latvians possessed a high and independent culture as early as LATVIAN PSYCHOLOGY By P. JUREVICS 1000 A.D., which was equal to the independent culture of other northern peoples. However, historical events of the following centuries checked the development of Latvian culture, and also prevented their taking an active part in the common development of culture. Only about a hundred years ago the Latvians awakened again to a cultural life of their own and because of this the modern urban culture of our nation is still cornpoint of view the Latvians belong point of view the Latvians belong paratively new, and from this to the youngest of European nations. This fact has left its impress on the psychology of the whole nation. The mental and spiritual forces of our people for centuries deprived of any possibility of development, are still unspent and in no way constricted by conventions and traditions. This, on the one hand, makes hfe more difficult and dangerous for us; we have not many definitively fixed rules laid down by the past and not many precedents to follow. On the other hand again, this leaves us more freedom and opportunities of success; we are not hampered by traditions and a stabilised mode of living from seeking new paths and solving problems which older nations are incapable of doing, even if they sometimes feel that their life has entered a blind alley, and that the finding and following of new paths is of vital importance to their existence. Indeed, not so much the finding but the follow ing of new paths presents th greatest difficulties to olde nations; the breaking up of old forms of life in order to cast them aside or to change them is fa too difficult to undertake, for th interests of so many human being are involved in these old form that their breaking up woulc nearly cause a complete change of society, the powers of inertia of which are tremendous. As has been said before, conditions here are entirely different. The forms of our life have not yet reached the stage of rigidity, and much of it is still fluid and in a stage of constant development- As is generally the case with youth, the past has little hold on us, and life seems broad and full of possi bilities; unlike the future of olde: nations ours has not yet been de cided by the past. All paths seen open to us, we have courag< enough to follow even rather steeļ paths; in this respect older nation: seem to be more tired or mor* "sensible," which is perhaps th< same thing. Anyhow, we an turned towards the future, an< enthusiasm, "fling," an da sensi of "rush" are characteristic o our lives. The dynamic side o our life — the one of desires volitions and emotions — whicl seeks for ways of expressing it self in vivid experience, creatioi and action outweighs the static which finds its expression not onlj in clearness and precision but als< in a certain rigidity of forms. In this description of the pre sent cultural situation we have t< a certain extent characterised alsc the mental and spiritual structure of the possessors of this culture— the Latvian people. We hav< characterised its spirit as dynamic in its inclination, impetuouslj tending towards the future, anc towards the creating of a new life but little looking back at the pasi and also comparatively little — and then only for a short timedirected towards the present. Thij gives rise to the following questions: does this characterisatior describe the spirit of the natior at the present moment only, 01 does it express some permaneni national quality? The last question seems to require an answei in the affirmative. Impetuosity the wish to create, to shape, tc advance, the striving for something still more perfect, seem tc be only expressions of the youth of our nation, but characters pc culiar to them, a fact which is revealed by comparing the Latvians with their neighbors or in analysing the various typical features of Latvian life. One of these characteristic features is a striving to advance. The existence of this quality — denied by neither tne friends nor the enemies of the Latvians — has been proved by history, for in a hundred years' time the Latvians have risen from the oppressed class of farm laborers to the heights of a nation possessing supreme power. That events took such a turn can be explained only by the unquenchable aspiration of our people — like a plant in a cellar — towards something higher and more perfect and for more light. "Strivers" have always been popular with the Latvians, and representatives of this type are often found in Latvian literature Intellectual and moral strivers are represented in works of literature by means of symbolic figures, the most common of which are the "mountain – climber," and "sun-seeker." These symbols have been used so often as to be. , come almost hackneyed, but jfe , spite of their aaivite and tlHf ; ness they are still liked by nriK of our readers- It has always been sufficient for a young man to be iooked upon as a "striver" i pecially if he strive for ; education) in order to get '< from the local savings-bank, tc ■ gain access to the drawing-w^M of the wealthiest citizens, and U [ the hearts of sensitive girls. A striver was the pride of trict or town only longed for "higher also examples taken from evJH I day life prove that a Latvian is, whatever his work or position, content with his condition, but always the wish to to improve upon something. He aims either at rationalising his work, or extending his farm, or simply at making his home cosier and more comfortable. Absorbed by ideas of progress and perfection, a Latvian never takes into consideration forms of work and time, and does not think either of sparing himself or others in order to attain the end he has set himself. Because of thi quality of theirs, Latvians were very popular in pre-war Russia as landowners' stewards, organisers, inspectors of works, etc. However, all that has just been] said does not give us reason! enough merely to rejoice in this] quality of ours, and to be proudj of it. On the other hand, there! can be no doubt that only thanks to our endeavor and our will we have broken away by force and irresistably from the unfavorable circumstances we were forced into, and to become what we are now. On the other hand, we must not forget that all external conditions of life change, and that ond and the same way of acting can! have favorable results in one casfl and unfavorable results in an-1 other. In the days when Latvia! was not yet an independent State" the only way of rising personally and raising the entire nation* was by breaking away from the! peasant class, lrom the so-called 'black-work' to desk-work, thus in] a certain way approaching the ruling class. This tendency which was once of such great advantage to us, nowadays does us more harm than good. Already because of the reasons mentioned above, i.e., because of the youth of our nation, life in a certain way lacks, stability, and many things in it; are still fluid, in a stage of stant change and development* (To be Continued)', 'Saeima committee reintroduces amendments to citizenship law

Andris Straumanis

-Nov. 08, 2011

Amendments to Latvia’s citizenship law that, among other changes, would again allow VVorld War 11-era exiles to obtain dual citizenship have been reintroduced in the Saeima.

The parliament’s Legal Affairs Committee, led by Ilma Čepāne of the Unity (Vienotība) party, on Nov. 3 brought back six bills that were left unresolved when the last Saeima was dissolved. One of those, the amendments to the citizenship law, was originally introduced Feb. 17. One amendment in the bill vvould single out exiles, allovving them to register as Latvian citizens. Under the proposal, the amendment vvould apply to persons vvho were Latvian citizens and vvho left their homeland betvveen the start of the first Soviet occupation on June 17, 1940, and May 4, 1990, when Latvia’s Supreme Council declared the country’s independence from the U.S.S.R. The descendants of exiles also vvould be allovved to register as Latvian citizens.

Also under bill Nr. 52/Lpll, dual citizenship vvould be allovved for: • Latvian citizens vvho have obtained citizenship in another European Union or European Free Trade Association member state. • Latvian citizens vvho have become citizens of another members state of the NATO defense alliance. • Latvian citizens vvho have become citizens of another country with vvhich Latvia has

a treaty recognizing dual citizenship. • Latvian citizens vvho have the permission of the Cabinet of Ministers to maintain their Latvian citizenship. • A child born outside of Latvia, if at least one of their parents is a Latvian Citizen and if under the law of the country in vvhich they are born a child automatically become a Citizen of that country. • A person vvho as a result of marriage becomes a Citizen of another country.

In the 10th Saeima, the amendments were referred to the Legal Affairs Committee, vvhich set up a subcommittee to study the proposed changes. The subcommittee set a deadline of Sept. 1 to take comment on the amendments, but by that datē the 10th Saeima vvas running out of time.

Likevvise, the 11 th Saeima’s Legal Affairs Committee on Nov. 1 decided to again form a subcommittee to look at the law.

One of the charges of the subcommittee, Čepāne said in a Saeima press release, will be to call on experts to provide input on the proposed amendments.

“The citizenship law has not been amended for many years,” she said, “and fundamental changes in it are needed in both content and technical points.”

The subcommittee will examine four separate areas, according to the press release: dual citizenship for exile Latvians; citizenship for children born abroad to Latvian citizens; situations in vvhich Latvian citizenship has been revoked because of current restrictions on dual citizenship; and the granting of Latvian citizenship to children born in Latvia to non-citizens.', 'LATVIAN VOTERS SUPPORT MODIFIED CITIZENSHIP LAW: ALLOWING MORE RUSSIANSPEAKERS TO BECOME CITIZENS On October 4, Latvian President Guntis Ulmanis praised his country for voting in favor of amendments to the citizenship law, making it easier for approximately 650,000 "stateless" Russian-speakers to become citizens. Over 53 % of the voters endorsed the amendments that automatically grant citizenship to children born after 1991, and ease other rules for the country's large Russian-speaking minority. Last summer, Prime Minister Guntars Krasts and his Fatherland and Freedom party took the lead to hold a referendum on the citizenship amendments. Krasts criticized the EU, and said its pressure on Latvia to adopt the amendments was counterproductive, and that not even all EU countries have such liberal citizenship laws. However, earlier in the spring, Russia threatened economic sanctions against Latvia and the argument over the Russian minority erupted into a full-blown dispute. Therefore, last summer, the Latvian parliament passed amendments to the citizenship law in order to naturalize more Russian-speakers and appease Russia. Both the West and Russia warned Latvia that a rejection of the amendments could hurt the Baltic nation's diplomatic relations both to the east and the west. The rejection of the amendments would be perceived as a lack of commitment to human rights. According to the international community, the promotion of human rights is a prerequisite for integration into Western structures, including NATO and EU. Thus, due to these pressures, the people of Latvia voted for the amendments. WESTERN REACTION According to State Department Spokesman James Foley, "The United States Government welcomes the vote of the Latvian people to implement amendments to their citizenship and naturalization law. This decision, democratically reached through a national referendum, indicates the Latvian people's strong commitment to the international human rights standards Latvia accepted by joining the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE)." NEWS FROM WASHINGTON, D.C. November 1998 Reported by Aija Straumane OSCE high commissioner on national minorities, Max Van der Stoel, said in a statement, "By deciding to approve the amendments to the law on citizenship, the people of Latvia have taken a very important step towards solving inter-ethnic problems and promoting the process of integration." According to Nils Muižnieks, a political analyst in Rīga, "The approval of this legislation means the fasttrack to the EU." The EU indirectly linked approval of the citizenship law amendments to joining the 15-nation bloc. Moreover, EU authorities welcomed Latvia's vote. According to an EU executive statement, 'The European Commission believes these amendments will facilitate an acceleration of the naturalization process, which will allow for the further development of a stable and prosperous integrated Latvian society." RUSSIAN RESPONSE Russia's initial response has been encouraging. According to a Russian Foreign Ministry statement, "the future {Latvian} government, has received a clear signal that the people of Latvia link the long-term interests of their country with achieving ethnic harmony, the integrity of society and the observation of human rights," Russian Energy Minister Sergei Generalov said he would ask the foreign ministry to lift sanctions against Latvia to allow Russia to boost oil exports throughout Latvia's Ventspils port. Analysts now believe it is up to Russia to establish good neighborly relations by signing a border treaty with Latvia. However, Russian accusations of human rights violations in Latvia will most likely resume since Latvia's goal is to join NATO, and Russia sees this as a threat. RUSSIAN-SPEAKING POPULATION IN ESTONIA Integrating the Russian-speaking population is also a challenge in Estonia. Latvia and Estonia both have a sizable Russian-speaking population. Upon gaining independence in 1991, Latvia and Estonia adopted strict citizenship requirements. Estonia, one of five east European countries on the fast track for EU membership, has been urged by the EU to act faster to integrate its 500,000 Russianspeakers. Legislation is currently before Estonia's parliament, but, it is likely to be opposed by nationalist parties, which could slow, or even halt, its approval. This situation reflects the recent events in Latvia. However, if Estonia is not able to enact amendments to its citizenship law, it might provoke Russia to antagonize Estonia and build good relations with Latvia. This could be viewed as an effort to splinter Baltic cooperation. NEW LATVIAN PARLIAMENT On the same day Latvian voters approved the amendments to the citizenship law, in a referendum, they also elected a new parliament. Ex-Prime Minister Andris Škēle's pro-business People's Party won the largest percentage of votes, while the centrist party, Latvia's Way, came in second and Prime Minister Guntars Krasts' Fatherland and Freedom party placed third. Due to Škēle's previous political experience, he is recognized as a politician that has not mastered the skills of consensus building within his own party. Politics is the art of the possible and the ability to compromise is essential. It seems logical that the most efficient coalition government would be one that encompasses the three largest parties. However, the People's Party has been excluded from coalition discussions due to personality conflicts. President Ulmanis said he hoped to name a candidate for the position of Prime Minister by mid-November. BALTIC FUNDING IN THE FOREIGN OPERATIONS BILL Latvia's support for less stringent citizenship requirements enables it to integrate into Western structures, especially NATO. However, in order to make a successful transition into a defense structure, foreign funding is essential. Therefore, the FY99 Foreign Operations Appropriations Bill is seen as a vehicle to NATO membership. The Baltics receive funding from the following programs: Foreign Military Financing (FMF), International Military and Education Training (IMET), Support for East European Democracy (SEED) and International Narcotics and Law Enforcement (INL). This program funding will assist the Baltic countries in establishing democratic institutions, free-market reforms and in upgrading their defense structures to NATO standards. On September 3, the Senate FY99 Foreign Operations Bill passed, containing $12.6 billion in budget authority, which is $1 billion less than the Administration's request. On September 17, the House of Representatives passed their version of the Foreign Operations Bill, containing $12.5 billion in budget authority. As of press time, the bill status was not available.']

Representative_Docs: ['The Red and the Black (Continued from previous issue) MASS MURDER AND DEPORTATION. By June 1941 the Soviets had destroyed all organizations representing Baltic culture, had overwhelmed the population with I atheistic and Socialistic propa| ganda, and had imposed a general I state of terror. A widespread I programme of mass deportation and murder followed. Before their forced retreat from Nazi armies in late June, the U.S.S.R. deported 65,000 Lithuanians, including 26 Roman Catholic j priests; an additional 15 were murdered. In Latvia, 12 Evan! gelical Lutheran pastors were deported, 2 murdered; 37 Baptist ministers were deported; 4 Roman Catholic priests were deported, 7 murdered. A total of 9,730 were deported from Estonia, and of these 15 Evangelical Lutheran I pastors were included; 2 more murdered, along with 24 Church Council members. Six Greek Orthodox ministers were deported, 6 murdered. The terror which cnaracterised the last month of Soviet rule continued under Nazi occupation between 1941 and 1944. Arrest, murder and deportation were the Nazis\' principal weapons against clergymen and the population as well, but no \'concerted campaign to eliminate religious life in regard to schools, associations, Churches and traditions was apparent during their tenure. POSTWAR Methodical attacks against the Church were resumed and intensified by the Soviets when they retook the Baltic countries in 1944. ļ Since then all Churches have come under State administrations; many are closed. The laity is discouraged from attending services by replacing Sundays and religious holidays (declared illegal) with compulsory Communist celebrations. Religious ceremonials are forbidden to Communist Party members and their families. Marxist theory has an established place in all school curricula, supplemented by an intensive antireligious propaganda campaign directed against the population as a whole. Mass deportations and arrests affecting all segments of the Baltic population have continued both for religious and political [purposes, but it ts impossible-tr>\' give exact figures on the number of people involved nor is it possible to distinguish clearly the purposes of each wave of persecution. Exile researchers, by comparing 1939 census figures with such available data as the number of ballots cast in recent elections, have corroborated a number of reliable refugee reports, and some estimates can be made. In the first big postwar deportation, approximately 50,000 Latvians — mostly males from 16 to 60 years of age —were included. A second mass deportation took place in Estonia and Latvia in March 1949. It is estimated that at that time 80,000 Estonians and 70,000 Latvians were removed. In May 1948, 100,00 OLithuanians suffered the same fate and are included in estimates numbering 550,000 for the entire period 1945-1949—a total of six deportation cycles. Only 30 of Estonia\'s 250 Lutheran clergymen are still on their native soil; less than 340 Catholic priests are left of Lithuania\'s former 1600. Almost half of Latvia\'s 279 Lutheran pastors have escaped to the West; of the remainder, it is know that 21 were deported and 4 killed during the two Soviet occupations, and it is estimated that only about 30 per cent, still reside in Latvia. POLAND: For centuries religion has been an essential part of Poland\'s history and culture and the conflict of the Roman Catholic Church and the Communist State in modern times can be understood only in those terms. The Church\'s interpenetration of the life of the Polish community created specific problems in the Stalinist treatment of it. Moreover, the conflict can be truly comprehended only if the nationalist nature of I Polish Catholicism is taken for granted. Because Poland is almost completely Catholic (some 96 per .cent.), the Communists j were unable to assail the Church openly. Communist attacks was made even more difficult because both clergy\' and ChdriS had been ! anti-Nazi, part of the resistance j movement, and therefore popular with the people. Nevertheless, the battle was j undertaken between the sickle and the crozier in Poland, and it falls into two stages. From 1945 to 1948 there was a period of relative calm and quiet, with the Communists proceeding cautiously while they consolidated their political gains. From 1948 to the present, a new era of State-Church struggle was launched which is as yet unresolved. The campaign began slowly and the first step was revocation of the Concordat on September 2. 1945. Press attacks against the clergy and the Catholic Church\'s welfare organization, "Caritas," were invoked but not yet as a concerted campaign. Only when the Communists felt secure in \' their control of the goevrnmental machinery did they attempt to disrupt the clerical machinery and that was in 1949. The following chronology gives an indication of how rapidly the attack grew: August 5. 1949—Government decree published safeguarding freedom of creed and conscience. August 11. 1949—Government rouisitioned all birth records from parish churches. August 18, 1949—Intensive anti-Papal campaign begun in the Communist press. September 21, 1949- Church hospitals nationalised under Ministry of Health. November 23, 1949—Bulletin of the Minister of Public Administration imposes serious limitations on public worship. January 23. 1950—Catholic charity organisation, "Caritas," taken over in sudden government confiscation. March 20, 1950—Confiscation of 375,000 acres of Church property. From August 1949 to April 1950, press vilification of the clergy continued to increase in intensity. Charges of collaboration with the Nazis, espionage, bribery, immorality were alternated with accusations of clerical alliance with reactionary elements internally and imperialist elements externally. T-i the interim the Communists v \\d not been idle on the Trojan Horse front. A "patriotic priest" organisation and a lay organisation of "progressive Catholics" i had been called into being, their purposes to split clergy from episcopate, flock from Church, and simultaneously to create cadres ļ for a new "people\'s clergy." By i using elements from the Polish Army\'s Chaplain Corps, the regime mobilised the so-called "Commission of Priests" attached to the Union of Defenders of Fighters for Freedom and Democracy. These "patriot priests" were accorded special privileges by the regime in the way of finances, tax exemptions, confiscations of property, etc. Also, as early as June, 1946, the Communists had sponsored and propagated a "National Catholic Church" as a diversionary effort. Neither attempt at creating schism within the Church succeeded, however. THE APRIL AGREEMENT After the abrogation of the Concordat in 1945, the government and the episcopate conducted prolonged negotiations for an agreement to regulate Church-State relations. These negotiations began and continued for four years before they finally resulted in an agreement signed on April 14, 1950 by the bishops and the State Department of Religious Affairs. On paper the Communists had made a number of concessions, and the agreement received the support of all the bishops. Under this new arrangement, the Church agreed: (The April 22 episcopal statement on this agreement issued at a conference in Gniezno contained the complete text.) a. To teach respect for the State\'s law and authority among the faithful. b. To reprove anti-government activities. c. Not to oppose the govern-I ment programme of collectivisa- i I tion. j , d. To ask the Vatican for re-! i cognition of Poland\'s claims to the ļ so-called "Recovered" or "Wesj tern" territories, which had been ceded to Poland by East Gerļ many. c. To imbue the faithful with sentiments of brotherly love and peace. For its part, the government: a. Assured religious instruction in the schools. b. Agreed to the maintenance of Catholic schools, religious ministration in the Armies, hospitals and prisons. c. Conceded the right of the Church to conduct charitable works. d. Agreed to recognise the right to publish Catholic periodicals. c. Conceded that the Pope is the decisive and highest authority in matters of canon, faith and morality. The innocuous final point of the episcopate\'s concessions provided the Communists with the pietext for breaking the agreement. The regime asked the Church to support the Stockholm Peace Appeal and the Church refused. The Communists then insisted that the episcopacy had abrogated the agreement by not working "for peace" and proceeded to intensify their anti-religious persecutions. Under regime pounding the Church capitulated and agreed to sign the Stockholm Appeal, but the Communists remained unappeased. Many Catholic schools were closed; a hostile press campaign was invoked against both the episcopate and the Vatican; anti-religious propaganda was stepped up; the Catholic press was hamstrung by censorship and discrimination in newsprint allocation was used against its wide circulation. All these aggressive activities, of course, were directed at the central problem which the Stalinist regime wanted to solve; the problem of the Western territories. THE WESTERN TERRITORIES. One of the thorniest problems facing the Polish Church was that of the territories acquired from Germany after the war. Here were dioceses to which the Pope refused to appoint bishops, since Vatican policy has been not to create permanent bishoprics pending peace treaties and final disposition of territorial rights and claims. It was the Vatician refusal (point four in the April State-Church agreement) which actually was the Communists\' chief concern. An apostolic administration for the Western territories had been created by the late Cardinal Hlond, but it was a provisional arrangement. The Communists wanted permanent authorities installed, particularly after the signing of the Zgorzeled Agreement between Poland and East Germany on July 6, 1950, as a sign of the Vatican\'s recognition of the revised territorial rearrangements. On October 23, 1950, an ultimatum was sent to the Polish hierarchy in which the regime stated that the provisional Church administration in the Western territories could no longer be tolerated. A campaign of oppressive action, mass meetings, and press invective followed and on January 26, 1951, the regime issued a decree liquidating the temporary Church adminst ration and removing the priests who acted as apostolic administrators. Immediately, to avoid the possibilities of schism, the Church itself, according to canon law, elected its own diocesan administrators, capitular vicars, to function in these territories, and Archbishop Stefan Wyszynski (now Cardinal) immediately confirmed the appointments. In May, 1952, Primate Wyszynski granted bishops\' privileges to the capitular vicars of two of the dioceses, and established a curiae in Wroclaw. This problem of the Church administration in the Western territories was >the main object of regime attacks on hierarchy and Apostolic See, and the stabilisation of Church administration there was the best possible reply to Communist attack. In this issue the Communists have cleverly exploited Polish nationalist feeling, which overwhelmingly wishes to retain the Western territories, by accusing the Vatican of pro-German sentiments, thereby attempting to create a schism between the Polish Catholics and the Apostolic See. The numerical strength and the constancy of Polish Catholics has prevented the Communist anti-religious programme from being fulfilled as quickly in Poland as it has been elsewhere. Although curtailment of Church activity has been significant, there is still religious teaching in the schools (but the Communists are developing a parallel completely secularised school system run by the Communist Association of Children\'s Friends [the TPD]; the Polish Church has not been dispossessed; there are still theological faculties and seminaries; clerical press, encumbered and censored though it is, still exists and is circulated. A Catholic University is open; pastoral letters can be read from the pulpit; bishops may visit their dioceses; and there is no loyalty oath for clerg.\' (there is one, however, For newly appointed bishops and vicars capitular) as there is in Hungary and Czechoslovakia. Moreover, the campaign against the Church in Poland has had a minimal amount of the largejcale arrests, murders and deportations which have characterised sther Communist persecutions. Thus: a) the Church is prohibited to intervene in State affairs, but b) the State dictates internal Church affairs. THESE RUINS ACCUSE. Only official religbus activities arc allowed, ceremonies held at State determined sites by State contolled congregations. The Communist Party actively encourages that recently introduced secular ceremonies replace traditional religious rites ( burials, christenings, confirmations, weddings ) and that these be performed at locations that have special significance for religious communities, for example, cemeteries. MINISTERS ARE DENIED FREEDOM. Official Vladimir Kuroedov from the Soviet Union Council defines the Communist Party\'s views on the rights of religious practices and ministers ( Izvestija 1/13/76 ): "No citizen is forced to: a) believe or be an atheist, b) practice or not to participate in religious ceremonies." But even though freedom to organize and lead religious ceremonies has not been denied, there exists in occupied Latvia restrictions for individuals who manage or fulfill religious ceremonial duties. The State intervenes and determines the contents and order of religious ceremonies and restricts, at times even represses, the work of believers. It is prohibited to ring church bells in many places throughout occupied Latvia and to purchase necessary ceremonial implements. The number of candles during the Christmas season is limited to prevent their use during Christmas Eve services, but candle burning is encouraged during the official New Year\'s Eve celebrations. For the same reason candlesmay not be included in gift packages from abroad. The leaders of congregations are subject to threats and repressions by local authorities at the registration of commencement and conclusion of activities. Parents arc denied their rights to carry out religious training in the home throug force home visits and threats by school administrators and teachers. The Soviet State law prohibits minors to actively participate in religious training and services. The number of minister demeans as a result of continual restrictions on a) congregations, b) the education on new ministers, retirement and death of pastors as well as deportations, arrests and suspension of clerical rights. All parsonages have IScon expropriated by the State and, unlike churches, are not made available to congregations. Only 10 congregrations retain rights to their parsonages. In Gaujiena ( Livland ) services arc held in the parsonage. As a result: – only about 40 or one half of all ministers dwell near the congregation; – a disproportionately large number ( 23 ) live in the capital city Riga; – 10 congregations are without a minister. Often ministers must travel great distances to hold church services as well as serve several congregations simultaneously: Neretas ( Kurland ) minister serves 8 congregations. Araisu ( Livland ) minister serves 7 congregations. Almost 30 or one half or all ministers arc over 70 years old. Church workers/ministers are subject tothc State administration. The Soviet State has established the Council for Religious Affairs with representatives in occupied Latvia. This Council receives directives directly from the KGB, supports Communist Party ideology hostile toward religion, and has taken over the right of the church: – to prohibit the minister\'s right to practice; – to scrutinize candidates for the ministry; – to decide the appointment and transfer of ministers without consulting the congregations in question. Ministers are forced to work under the threat of arrests, deportation, and involuntary placement in psychiatric hospitals even after their release. 90% of all minsters ordained before 1945, who have continued their work in the official Church have been subject to one of the above. MINISTERS ARE DENIED FREEDOM Official Vladimir ;KUROEOOV from the Soviet Union Council defines the Communist Party\'s views on the rights of religious practices and ministers (IZVESTIJA 1/13/76): "No citizen is forced to: a) believe or be an atheist, b) practice or not participate in religious ceremonies." But even though freedom to organize and lead religious ceremonies has not been denied, there exists in occupied Latvia restrictions for individuals who manage or fulfill religious ceremonial duties. The State intervenes and determines the contents and order of religious ceremonies and restricts, at times even represses, the work of believers. It is prohibited to ring church bells in many places throughout occupied Latvia and to purchase necessary ceremonial implements. The number of candles during the Christmas season is limited to prevent their use during Christmas Eve services, but candle burning is encouraged during the official New Year\'s Eve celebrations. For the same reason candles may not be included in gift packages from abroad. The leaders of congregations are subject to threats and repressions by local authorities at the registration of commencement and conclusion of activities. Parents are denied their rights to carry out religious training in the home through forced home visits and threats by school administrators and teachers. The Soviet State law prohibits minors to actively participate in religious training and services. The number of ministers demeans as a result of continual restrictions on a) congregations, b) the education of new ministers, retirement and death of pastors as well as deportations, arrests and suspension of clerical rights. Number of Ev. Lutheran ministers in occupied Latvia: 1945 1963 1969 1980 120 103 87 76* *49 of these where ordained before 1945. It has become very difficult for ministers to perform thei- pastoral duties. All parsonages have been expropriated by the State and, unlike churches, are not made available to congregations. Only 10 congregations retain rights to their parsonages. In Gaujiena (Livland) services are held in the parsonage. As a result: ~ . \' – only about 40 or one half of all ministers dwell near the congregation; ; , – a disproportionately large number (23) live in the capital city Rīga; . – 10 congregations are without a minister. Often ministers must travel great distances to hold church services as well as serve several congregations simultaneously: nc Neietas (Kurland) minister serves 8 congregations AraiSu (Livland) minister serves 7 congregations. S 30 or one half of all ministers are over Church workers/ministers are subject to >he  The Soviet State has established the Council for Religious Affairs with representatives in occupied Latvia. This Council receives directives directly from the KGB, supports Communist Party ideology hostile toward religion, and has taken over the right of the church: – to prohibit the minister\'s right to practice; – to scrutinize candidates for the ministry; – toMecide the appointment and transfer of ministers without consulting the congregations in question. All employees of the Church are responsible for transgressions of Soviet law pertaining to religious practices. According to this law, imprisonment awaits those who "resist anti-religious propaganda", or who "utilize religion as pretext for the distribution of anti-Soviet propaganda". The minister of Ļaudona congregation (Livland) received a 5 year sentence for breaking the former law, and H. Grīvāns, the minister of the congregation at Ventspils (Kurzeme) was sentenced to 10 years imprisionment for breaking the latter law. This sentence was extended for another 8 years after the original term. Ministers are forced to work under the threat of arrests, deportation, and involuntary placement in psychiatric hospitals even after their release. 90% of all ministers ordained before 1945, who have continued their work in the official Church have been subject to one of the above. THE CHURCH IS DENIED FREEDOM OF THE PRESS According to Soviet law, only the State i.e. Party possesses the right to print and distribute any literature whatsoever. This has greatly restricted the freedom of the Church\'s activity. Announcements concerning services and those of an official nature may not appear in newspapers nor may they be distributed by mail. Announcements may only be posted on church doors. The necessary religious books may be printed only with special permission. Each service song sheet requires a special permit. The only translation of the Bible in Latvian was published in 1960. It did, however, contain only the New Testiment and Psalms and was published in 5000 copies, a number that was too small already during the time of printing. These Bibles have not been available for a long time. In 1976 the Church of Sweden offered to provide the paper necessary for a new edition of the Bible, but as of yet has not received an official reply. Similarily, permission has not been granted to print the already completed Minister\'s Handbook (agenda). In 1954 a revised edition of the Latvian Ev. Lutheran Song Book was published. It included new special songs for the celebration of State holidays, while many well known Psalms were eliminated on the grounds that they were "suspicious". The Latvian Ev.Lutheran congregations in occupied Latvia are prohibited to publish a religious newspaper. The only periodical of the Church is the Ev.Lutheran calendar. It is prohibited to send or bring into the Soviet Union Bibles, Psalm books and scientific theological literature published abroad. At the same time atheistic literature printed in the Soviet Union may not be sent abroad, probably so as not to give evidence of the Soviet Union\'s hostile attitude toward religion. Aaugstrozes Evangelical Lutheran Church is now a granary']

Representative_Docs: ['The Soviet price cut of April 1, 1953, has been widely popularized by the Western press as evidence of the "liberalization" of the Soviet regime and increased consideration for the needs of the population. llt has been taken largely on its face value as the Kremlin intended it to be taken. Actually the improvements this cut introduces are more seeming than real. The list of goods the prices of which Were cut is a long one, and, at first glance, makes the impression as if all articles sold in the retail trade had become cheaper. In reality a number of relatively expensive articles are not to be found on the list. For instance, the price of ready-made woollen clothes, both lor men and women remains unchanged, and artificial silk as well as clothes made from it likewise. The average monthly wage of an ordinary worker being S(X)-600 -rubles, he is bound to find a woollen suit costing 1000-1800 rubles rather expensive. Scythes and sickles, still widely used on the kolkhozes because it is unprofitable to hire agricultural machinery from the state, have become cheaper, but all kinds of fei filizers, the largest item in the rpoductional expenditure of the kolkhozes, remain as expensive as ever. The price cut decree lists a number of building materials that have become cheaper, but bricks, lime and timber are not among them, although they are the most important articles for kolkhoz building. The cut in the price of a number of articles has no real significance, e.g.. the prices of potatoes, cabbage, turnips and carrots have been cut by a whole 50 per cent. Unfortunately, the people do not profit from this. The Soviet press itself has mentioned a large number of important towns having only" 1-2 shops selling\' such goods in the centre and none in the suburbs, e.g., "Trud" (27/7/52) writes: "The population of Stalingrad is not properly supplied with vegetables. In the outskirts of the city, where the population consists of workers, there are no stores for vegetables, and no milk in the srCres." Or to quote "Pravda" (14/12/52). "The Briansk oblast is particularly rich in vegetables, fruit and berries. Nevertheless, in the stores and restaurants of this oblast it is difficult to get fresh vegetables, fruit, berries and mushrooms." In I the smaller towns, vegetables and j truit can be bought only on the ļ kolkhoz market, where prices are the USSR the low income classes grow most of their own vegetables on small plots rented from the state, working on them before and after working hours. In 1952, 18 million workers and employees, or almost half of all wage-earners, cultivated such small kitchen gardens ("Trud," 26/2/53». The prices of nails and cement have been cut by 25 per cent. However, at the end of last year the Soviet papers often wrote that kolkhozes had not been bale to build cowsheds for their cattle, or left them roofless because of the lack of the above articles. What is the use of a price cut if an article is unavailable? Such shortI ages are a daily ocurrence in many i places and apply to many goods besides nails and cement. As usual in other connections, the Soviet sources mention only percentages in connection with the price cut and never a single price The reason is that although the prices of some goods are cut every year, they remain exceedingly high as compared to wages. Ar example is given by "Izvestia" (3/4/53). In a report about tht Moscow department store, thf salesman in the department ol readymade clothes says to a client: "Take this dress, it suits you very well. . . Now it is 1(X rubles cheaper." This dress canont be either o! artificial silk or of wool or it< price would not be cut. Cottor dresses have become 14 per cent and silk dresses 12 per cent cheaper. Hence, if the dress is i cotton one it costs 615 rubles nov after the price cut and 733 ruble! if it is a silk one. At the same tim< the latest Soviet official data giv< to hand that the average monthl: wage in the USSR is 500 rubles. Many people wonder how th* i Soviets can cut their prices yea i after year while in the Westeri i world they rather soar than fall, i Actually, this is no miracle, bu • rather a propaganda trick. Th j Soviet prices include very higi \' turnover taxes which, in certaii ; years, and on certain goods, con : stituted 70 per cent, of the retai : prices and still constitutes mor • than half of the total revenues o I the state. Cutting prices, the gc : vernment cuts this tax on farm • i ing produce. In view of the fac 5 that the number of workers an : employees buying this produc 5 grows with every year, the tots • sum made by the government b : i means of this tax remains cor ; stant. The prices of industrif ■ goods (textiles, etc.), are cut b • means of pressing down the cost E of prduction. The costs of pit ;l duction, in their turn, are lowere "1 by means of a continuing increas The Price Cut of the quantity of owrk a worker must execute in order to earn a certain Wmmt. In other words. Ihc price cut is parallelled by a wage cut. This is the main reason why the USSIL never publishes any ligu res concerning wages and salaries. In the West, such a procedure would be impossible as the trade unions would not agree to any wage cuts. In the USSR it is one of their primary tasks to introduce such cuts. Hence, in the West wages rise alongside of prices while in the USSR prices tall as a result of a lowering of the wages I\'llE NEW PRK ES AMI WA(iES IN THE BALTIC STATES The Soviet maintain that the decree of March 31, 1953, cutting the prices of a large number o: goods by 5-50 per cent, has substantially raised the living standard of the population. "This decree is a shining example of the untiring care of the Soviet goveronment for the material and cultural needs of oqi workers," wrote the Soviet-Estonian paper "Rahva Haal" ot April 1, 1953, and all over the thre Baltic countries meetings o thanksgiving "lor the current raise of our living standard" were organised. Whether hte price cut raises the living standard or not may be seen from a comparison of prices and wages. Moreover, it must be remembered that even a number oi staple foods, such as milk, eggs, potatoes, vegetables, fruit, are not available in the state-owned shops but must be bought on the kelkhoz mai\'ket where prices are free, i.e., regulated by supply and demand. Likewise, persons who have left the Baltic states in January of this year maintain that readymade clothes and underwear, wool len : textiles, stockings, better quality shoes, nad a large number of household goods, are to be had in the shops only sporadically often with several months intervals. A factory worker\'s monthly wages in the Baltic states today range from 300-700 rubles, an ">()() rubles. At the same time the cheapest kind of black bread now costs 1.44 rubles the kg. (formerly 1.60 rubles), the cheapest wheaten bread 4.05 rubles (formerly 4.50), groats 3.10 rubles (formerly 3.45), cod, the cheapest fish 2.70 rubles (formerly 3.00). Potatoes and vegetables have to be bought on the kolkhoz market now as before where they cost: potatoes 1-2 rubles, cabbage 2 rubles, carrots 3 rubles, etc. All other foods is so expensive that the worker and clerk cannot afford them very often. The new price of castor sugar is 8.19 rubles (formerly 9.10) and of butter 27.90 rubles (formerly 31.00). Both articles appear in the shops i about once a month and only a restricted quantity (e.g. 200 groms I of butter) is sold to a customer at a time. Smoked ham costs 36.00 rubles (formerly 40.00), porl 17.00-32.20 rubles (formerly 20.00 38.00». beef 12.75-18.70 ruble (formerly 15.00-22.001. coffee 62.40 rubles (formerly 78.00). Milk costs 3.00-4.00 rubles on the kolk hoz market and eggs 1.50-2.00 rubles apiece. In a word, butter, meat, etc., i accessible only to persons wit high salaries and a reduction i their price will hardly affect th budget of the vast majority wit their low wages. The director of factory may enrich his bill of fare thanks to the new prices for hi monthly .takings are 3000-4500 rubles (a basic salary of 150( rubles plus various premia anc bonuses). They will, howeve bring no particular improvemen into the lives of a physicia (monthly income 600-1000 rubles or a teacher (monthly income 500 rubles in the country and 600 i the towns). The cut in the prices of tex tile goods, shoes and haberdasher> will not raise the living standarc of the low wage earners eithe for these articles remain just a much out of their reach as the used ot be. What they used t and will continue to buy is th cheapest cutton for 12.41 ruble the meter (formerly 14.60). Bet ter cotton costs 18.70 ruble woollen matreial 62.90-119.00 rubles (formerly 74.00-140.00 women\'s shoes 180.00-324.00 ruble (formerly 200.00-360.00). It hardly likely that a typist wit her salary of 450 rubles per mont I or a female bricklayer earning th ■ same wage, or a hairdresser <3C [ rubles monthly) will be able t • afford silk or kapron (Sovie nylon) stockings at 40 rubles th I pair (formerly 50). A femal lieutenant in the militia with i salary of 1000 rubles may buy sue • stockings occasionally, but for th I vast majority there are the chea the pair and the cheapest underwear at 60.45 riibles (formerly «5.00». The wife of a major in the aviation (monthly salary 6000 rubles) or some other high functionary, may now save 28 rubles on a pair of good underwear which used to cost 200 rubles and now costs 172. wAorker — even if he is a Stakhanovito earning 800-900 rubles monthly, or a foreman making 1000-1200 rubles — cannot buy a suit for his whole monthly wage. The price of readymade woollen clothes has not been cut, and they are very rarely found in the shops. He would have to order the suit and the material costs 213.75-380 rubles per meter (formerly 225-400). the best quality suiting being inferior to that used tor cheap reach-medown clothes in the West. The cheapest cotton socks cost 9.60-11.20 rubles the pair and shoes 180-360 rubles the pair. For everyday wear the workers wrap their feet in rags and stick them into heelless rubber boots which are very hard on the feet. Al lthe beter goods mentioned above — if they should be available—as well as hats (100 rubiest, gloves (50-75 rubiest, handbags (140-200 rubles), are only for the well-paid Soviet elite. For the lew kopeks they save per kilogram of bread or fish the broad masses must give — and keep promises to incerase their productivity and produce things over and above the plan as a token of gratitude to Party and government. hTe reports ol Baltic papers and radio substantiate our contentions. On April 1 the Riga radio said that trade had been lively as a consequence of the price cut. An old man told hte radio reporter in a department store that he had now been able to buy a kerchief each for his wife and daughter. On April 2 it was reported from Tallinn that more cheese, sausage, fish and butter had been sold than before the price cut. On April 4 the Latvian papers said that the demnad for groats, sausage, wheaten bread and sugar had increased with the new prices. Our idea is that none of these articles is a luxury — the first thing people buy whenever they really have money to spend. The population of any country should be able to buy them — price cut or no price cut — before there can be any talk of a decent living standard.', 'THE SECOND PHASE OF THE COLLECTIVIZATION OF SOVIET AGRICULTURE . A serious political crisis is approaching in the Russian country. Much new evidence shows that the bolsheviks have far from defeated the farmers by their ruthless collectivization of agriculture during 1931-1932 when 5 million "kulaks" were deported to Siberia and the structure of the Russian countryside was entirely changed. It has become clear that agricultural achievements made till now are regarded as unsatisfactory by the polit-bureau and that the communist party adopted another policy some months ago, the final aim of this new policy being to get a firm grip on the farmer population, which still comprises more than 50% of the people in Soviet Russia. This new policy started rather innocently, in a way inconceivable to an average western observer. On the 28th February, "Pravda" published a letter in which the polit-bureau member Andreyev apologized and admitted that his agricultural policy with the principle of organizing the workers in so – called Zvieno-groups was a mistake and that the right thing would be to re-organize the so-called working brigade into one unit. All this sounds mysterious and inconceivable/out can nevertheless be explained. During the nearly 20-year existence of the kolchoses the Soviet government tried a dozen different methods of improving the kolchose system, increasing the working capacity and making the management more efficient. The collectivization was carried through under great tension and with tremendous sacrifices. When the action was completed it turned out that the communist party could not rest on its laurels. The kolchoses had to be kept under constant gun threat. The leaders of the communist party and the government were forced to issue innumerable decrees,resolutions, instructions etc. and to expend milliards of rubels on an unparallelled propaganda. But the farmers proved to be tough objects for By T. Norvid. "socialistic conversion". They worked unwillingly and showed no interest in the result of production. The working capacity per capita became remarkably low. The harvests and*the cattle yielded muchless than before collectivization. The farmers\' income was so low that there could be no question of a personal interest in the work. Most kolchoses were run a"! a loss and no propaganda, no decrees could lift the kolchose system out of its back water. As early as 1935 the Soviet government introduced a policy in agriculture similar to the industrial NEP -policy from 1923 – 19291 Without making much fuss about it Soviet Russia introduced a new system which aimed at rousing the personal interest in production among the kolchose farmers. They formed so-called zvienja, i.e. small working groups within the kolchose, specializing in agriculture, cattle-breeding, hortj.cuj.uuieetc. At the same time this tactical retreat implied that each farmer was again given the right to own a piece of land and a certain number of domestic animals for his own needs. The result was not wanting. Tne farmer regained personal interest in his work, production increased considerably land the state "made a material profit b*; this development. Then in 1941 war broke out. The impaired political situation in the country and the shortage of bread, which was threatening even at the front, forced the bolsheviks to new concessions to the farmers, whose efforts to re-establisn private agricultural methods had never ceased. When collecting the cereals the government employed the zvienja-groups only, thereby passing over the nominally existing organization which consisted of the brigade and the chairman of the kolchose. This meant that the state administration actually only dealt direct with the farms andvillages and not with the kolchose organization. When the breao.crjsis passed owing to last year\'s good harvest, decided to reestablish the kolchose organization and to put the kolchose farmers under effective control. One must realize that in such a small workingunit as the zvieno there is no room for political superintendence and bureaucratic regime. This is possible only in large units the brigades (zvieno can consist of 5 – 50 farmers, the brigade often of 500-1000 workers). The apology by the polit-bureau member Andrejev does not sound genuine. He says that for the last ten years he has almost consistently been wrong in his policy with regard to the organization of the farmers. This could not have been done without the* full consent of the polit-bureau. His apology an? his promise to base his organization on the brigade system in future, means nothing else than that the polit-bureau decided to revert strictly to the re -establishment of the kolchoses and to cut entirely the farming population\'s ties with its normal atmosphere its own villages and own houses. The idea of the brigade is that all kolchoses must be made larger and comprise some thousands of farmers. This intention is proved by the present integration of kolchoses in various parts of USSR, especially in White Russia and the Ukraine, where dozens of kolchoses Ъесп combined in order to form a larger unit. At such kolchoses each brigade specializes in one kind of work only. The Independent farmer who can simultaneously cultivate the land, manage a cattle farm, bees and a garden is thus disappearing. Instead the specialized farm worker appears, who knows only one. kind of job. The brigades must work not only within their own kolchoses but also at other, often distant,kclchoses. The brigadeer, or the kolchose chairman, (who are the tools of the communist party or the state)direct the brigades. The Soviet government evidently intends to turn the farmers into the same mass of homeless and rootless people as the industrial workers in the Soviet Russia at present. Against this background the constant rumours about disturbances, opposition and partisan activity in many parts of Russia, especially in the west, are easier to understand. It is simply so that following the liquidation of the "kulaks" the smallest independent farmer is now being he defends himself to the of his ability. But in the new wave of deportations further millions of oppositionists are filling the slave camps in the desolate parts of the country. Soviet Russia\'s fight against the farmer is by no means over.lt seems as though the second round has just started. The bolsheviks do not appear to be nearer victory in this struggle than they were in 1930 – 1932. The agricultural index will influence the intensity of thts fight against the simple man. As long as it goes on, the Soviet government has a difficult home problem to cope with, which should gradually affect Soviet Russia s foreign political and military power, especially with regard to its mobilization capacity.', 'THE SECOND PHASE OF THE COLLECTIVIZATION OF SOVIET AGRICULTURE (By T. Representative_Docs: ['(This article by a well-known Baltic educationalist deals with a high official of the Bolshevik occupation authorities. It is a relation of facts, based on shorthand notes, and conveys an idea of the objectives and methods of the Bolshevik occupation. An attempt has been made to retain in the English translation the illiterate language used by the chief person in this article.—The Editor.) A Soviet book on the post-war economic planning of the Soviet Union stated (in 1946) the following:— "As long as capitalism exists, the basic reasons for the rise of wars are also present. Under conditions of imperialism, any war, even if small, threatens to grow into a world war." Whatever evil things they are doing or planning to do, the Communists impute to others. This is also true of imperialism in 194 6. The Political Bureau realised in 1946 that the Red imperialism will eventually meet with resistance, and that any war, even if small, may grow into a third world war. In order that the "last battle" ends with a total victory of Communism in the world, "Russia was, and is today, the only country in the whole world with a heavy industry larger than the light industry." The violent expansion of the heavy industry is not meant to ensure national defence, since it is perfectly clear that no country in the world plans to make war on Soviet Russia, and since it is equally clear that the Soviet Union wishes to engage in the final battle which in the Communist belief will lead to the establishment of Communist dictatorship throughout the world. The results of the Soviet armament policy are now being felt by the United States in Korea. A local small-scale war is being waged there now, but may develop into a third world war if the Kremlin imperialists deem a universal war opportune. Leading statesmen in the Western countries long refused to admit the existence of a Red imperialism. The war in Korea has opened their eyes. A dictator is like a man on a bicycle. He must pedal and move forwards in order not to fall to the ground. It is, therefore, a delusion to assume that there will be no large-scale war. It is approaching, and cannot be avoided. Some idea of the detailed THE LONG-RANGE POLICY OF THE SOVIET POLITICAL BUREAU scheme and careful preparation ol the Soviet long-range plans maj be gained from the following story, which deals with Comrade Shubitis, a high Communist official in Soviet-occupied Latvia, and his "philosophy." It will be noted that the story dates from 1941, when Soviet Russia still was Nazi Germany\'s ally, and an instigator of World War 11. Should some people object, stating that the views of one Communist are not representative of the official Soviet policy. I need only refer to the French author, Andre Gide, who spent considerable time and effort on studying Communism, and who said: "Every morning,it is decided by PRAVDA what to think, know, and believe. And it is not good to deviate from it. So every time you speak to a Russian, you feel you are speaking to all of them." Moreover, Comrade Shubitis was not just one of the 180 million inhabitants of Soviet Russia. He was more than that. WHO WAS COMRADE SHUBITIS? Biographical data on Comrade Shubitis are scant. His general education was limited to two years of elementary schooling. Somewhat too early he had adopted as his guiding principle Lenin\'s slogan: "Rob the spoils!" In school, he had started confiscating the belongings of his comrades and teachers. This happened in 1910, and the teachers failed to understand that this represented communistic ideological activities, and qualified it as theft. Shubitis was expelled from the school and sent to a reformatory. Its rigid discipline did not appeal to him. He escaped, and made his way to Russia. During the Russian Revolution he proved that he had well grasped the meaning of Lenin\'s saying: "Many a villain may be of great use to the Party just because he is a villain." The Communist Revolution gave Shubitis his big chance. The Party justly appraised Shubitis\' talents, and he was made Political Commissar (politruk) in the Red Army. After the end of the Civil War in Russia, he served in :he Secret Political Police (NKVD), and won recognition for ?fficiency. He also graduated :rom the Red Propaganda University in Moscow. In 1940, when he Soviet troops occupied Latvia, Shubitis was sent to the normal school for teachers in Jelgava (Mitau) as a political instructor. I met him there, and heard from him the few bits of biographical information given above. Don\'t judge a man by his appearance! He came to Jelgava in a shabby and dirty suit, and looked like the ugly duckling. The new clothes he bought in Latvia did not fit him at all. His silly talks—at that time both students and teachers believed his talks to be idiotic —made him a stock of derision. His language was coarse, somewhat incoherent, but had a quality of stylistic originality—it was extremely direct and pithy. WHAT DID COMRADE SHUBITIS TEACH, AND HOW? Comrade Shubitis extolled work and the socialistic competition for the greater glory of the Soviet Fatherland. I have preserved a few of his speeches in shorthand notes. Excerpts are given below. They may give some food for thought. "See, everybody works in the Soviet Union—young people and old people, men, women, and children. We are Communists, and therefore we work in socialistic competition. We call this Stakhanowism, and you do not know it. You will also have to work. You must take pattern on Soviet Fatherland, not England or America. "Comrades, Stalin is our father, friend, and our teacher. He wants everybody to work. We must do what our father and teacher says. If not, I will report. I am also with the NKVD, and you know it does not like fooling. Raise hand who wants to work with enthusiasm, so that I know who is saboteur, who is renegade." Naturally, all students and teachers raised their hands, since none of them wished to become involved with the NKVD. This added new zest to Shubitis\' speechifying. He went on: — "Good, good that everybody wants to work voluntarily. You are good comrades. See, I tell you, if we all work voluntarily in the Soviet Union, everything will be fine. There (in the Soviet Union Transi.) even the cowherd looks after cattle 8 hours a day, makes money, and buys what he wants. Buys a car and drives it, or else, if he prefers, buys vobla and eats it/ I had not known before the meaning of the word "vobla." During the Soviet occupation I learned that a vobla is a fish. Large quantities of vobla are being caught in the Caspian Sea. Dried in the sun, it is used for food. A dry vobla has a repugnant smell. People eat it when they have nothing else to eat. It stands to reason that a cowherd makes enough money to buy a vobla every day, since it costs only a few kopeks. What we did not understand was how a cowherd could buy a car, since there was no private property in Russia, and nobody could own a car, even assuming that a cowherd could actually buy one for the money he earned. Comrade Shubitis explained the functioning of the people\'s courts of justice as follows:— "See, comrades, only in Soviet Union there is culture. There are Red Corners in every kolkhoze, and there are people\'s courts. See, you did not have them, did you? What you had, was it culture? You had to study five years—rotten bourgeois law. Who can afford it? Only the bourgeois. And why study so long? It just makes people stupid. "Isn\'t it idiotic to study old things, and not know what Lenin and Stalin teaches? Is that progressive? Certainly not. Only Soviet Union is progressive country. We have people\'s courts. People\'s judges must not pain for five years studying; they are being eleced. Real Communists are being elected. If there is no real Communist, Party does not nominate candidates, and does not permit elections. "And Communist people\'s courts are progressive courts. I see you do not know what is progressive court. I will give example from life. Father has two sons, John and Peter. John is bourgeois; has had two houses, farm. Peter is proletarian, factory hand has had no private property. He is a real Communist. Brothers happen to meet after long years, drink vodka, start arguing, start fighting, disturb order and peace. Militiamen arrest them, and people\'s court tries them. Try how? Try progressively. John, former bourgeois, is sentenced to death. Peter, the proletarian, is acquited. Gets warning for drunken brawl, since Communist must not do so. See, this is real and progressive court! Did you have them? Certainly not!" I grant that these examples do not show Comrade Shubitis as an intelligent man. However, what he told us was just part of his Communistic Pedagogical methods. L too, remember them as macabre jokes. However, I cannot make myself laugh at. Comrade Shubitis\' political speeches, although I did in 1941. His language was vulgar as usual – already *Noteancient Greeks taught that each speaker determines the style of his speech. "Comrades, Stalin, a genius, is our leader, he sure is clever. The Politbureau works hand in hand – with him, and nobody can outwit them. You should know that we communists are people of a special type. We can маке\' white black, if that is needed.The first communist, Lenin, was clever. He said:  it serves communism, pat the guy on his back and call him Alexandre _ the Sreat, if not, then shoot him". This is hoy/ real communists must act J We now pat Hitler on the back, so he lustily beats the English, French an(j Americans.They is the real plutocrats, sweaters of workers,and capitalists. You will see. Hitler will cross the Channel to England but he can t do it without us. We will give gasoline, grain, you will also have to give bacon and eggs. These we have not got so much, but 3et-theGermans have them and beat the English and Americans. You knows, around the English island there is sea and you can\'t get there. We has few ships that shoot with cannons. Hitler has warships, u-boats,divebombers and he likes to fight.Hitler wants war and we gives him his chance. This is clever, comrades Jf we refuse, we would not have friendship pact, the lunatic might even atack us. And we is not ready yet. Stalin and the Politbureau gave signal – pat Hitler\'s back so that he beats the English and Americans. And he can\'t do it withou-tus, we have gasoline, Hitler has none. You knows, American plutocrats will sail to help old British lion when his tail will be bitten off.Hitler will sink American ships. Those who will come to the island will fight and turn round and round on the islet. Hitler will do the work which we would have to do in the future. And we will give – Germans gasoline, will give grain, and you will have to give bacon and eggs, since we nave not got so much of that, and let the Germans beat the plutocrats. We will stand aside, applaud and bide our time. Only when they will be tired on the English island from sheer fighting we shall rise, move" and walkthrough Europe. This, comrades, is the way of being farsighted in politics. We does not like to pull out chestnuts from fire, might bum our fingers. We need not run into the fire when there are fools to do it in our place. Sure, I knows you does not like Hitler because he puts the screws on Jews, Poles and Czechs. Communists does not like Hitler either, but we needs him now, he works for иг. Let Hitler beat Polish and Czech bourgeois, so we won\'t have to beat them when we will go and liberate those countries .Polish and Czech bourgeois does not want communists and communists does not want them. A Jew is good if he is communist and many Jews is communists. You knows, Kaganovich"daughter is Stalin\'s wife. But all Jews is not communists.Jews are nationalists, they wants to run off to Palestine and build there their own bourgeois state. From Germany they flee to us only because Hitler is chasing them. Let Hitler beat the kikes, when the time comes we will defend the Jews. We shall shout all over the world – Hitler is a monster, he beats Jews. Has got industry superior to ours. \\ Comrade Stalin says: we are behind America 30-40 years.Therefore – we* wants catch up with and distance American industry. If we fai*k .America will crush us under her heel,but we wants to crush America.For this we needs time".
