The German is strong now and we is not yet resdy for war. But then, comrades, our allies will be – England and America. We will fight together againsttheGermans. England and America will need us. Then we shall pat Roosevelt and Churchill on the back, and -call them Alexandre the Great or Julius Ceasar, if they likeut, if only they fight with us. It would not change anything. From our viewpoint, Germany may fight against the western plutocrats.lf not, we can also fight together with England and America against Germany. Germany will be beaten anyhow. Also America and England will be weakened. We also will be weakened. But we is clever, we have ideas, but the Americans don\'t*We will pretend to be loyal allies the same we do now with Hitler. To please Americans we will say that we give freedom to the church. We will order people to go church and then we will take Americans to the church to show how nice is everytning. Americans believe in this old stuff. They will be glad that we is democratic that we allows religious freedom. We will play democrats and speak only about democracy. We shall beat Germany and Then occupyall of Eastern Europe and promise to introduce there democracy on American pattern. You knows, we can make white black,!*? necessary. The Americans will swallow the bait. We will win this war, not the English and Americans.lf you will be good comrades I will make you my assistants. And then we will build a new world. We willmake Red Corners and elect people\'s courts in Poland, Czechoslovakia, Germany and elsewhere. I will teach you what to do. Anyhow, we will get Germany. And you knows,.Stalin said: "To make communism in Europe, one must have communism nn Germany, the heart of Europe.How to destroy America in the final battle, has not yet beendearly planned by the Politbureau. This is a task of the future. But be sure, comrades, the Politbureau will make the right plans also for that thing. Therefore comrades be loyal to our leader, father, friend and teacher Stalin. We will win this war." In 1941 I considered these dire predictions incoherent outpourings of a stupid fanatic.Today,in 1950, reading their stenographic records lam terrified. I know that Comrade Shubitis did not tell us any original ideas of his own. In the Institute of Red Propaganda he had become imbued with all these ruses and machinations of the Political Bureau and disclosed them to us in his simplicity. Much in Shubitis\'predictions has come true. Let us pray to God that not all of them come true. Dr. A.', 'Even very recently there have been discussions in the Press about the real purpose of the Soviet Unions\'s policy. In the majority of cases they used to lead to the conclusion that the Bolsheviks at the Kremlin did not want war. These conclusions are in no way founded on fact, but on theoretical assumptionr&e Soviet State is not yet ready for war; in the last war it suffered terrible losses, and its main object now is to restore its national life, etc. There is also another aspect, namely; the power of the Soviet Union is considered an indisputable fact that must be accepted ipso facto and which makes it necessary te find a modus vivendi and of delimiting the respective spheres of influence (Walter Lippman). The latter view is in directcontradiction to all that the United States of America has beenpromising the world in the name of Christian morals and human rights, but the former is based on a lack of knowledge of the real nature of the Soviet State and of Soviet activities in the course of recent years. The activities of the Kremlin prove something very different. Here we no longer have to deal with assumptions, but with actual events, well known to the world,but unfortunately very often disregarded. Let us go over them again. 1. It was the Red Army of the Soviet State,then concentrated in White Russia and the Ukraine which on 18 September,l939 broke into Poland, already defeated by the Germans. The direct purpose of this inroad was to wage an imperialistic war with a view to making conquests. Very soon after there followed an agreement between Nazi-Germany and Bolshevist Russia as to thcestabldghemont of a new boundary line between both of these huge aggressive States, which resulted in the division of Poland, a State of 30 million inhabitants. DOES MOSCOW WANT WAR? 2. Two months later the very same Soviet State, breaking existing treaties, attacked Finland, at the same time compelling the three Baltic States to suffer the establishment of Soviet military bases in Baltic territory. The Russo-Finnish war in the winter of 1939 showed the whole world how humiliatingly unprepared was the Soviet State even for a war against a people of 5 million and what incredible sacrifices it demanded from the Soviet Union; it was forced to carry out an important economic reorganization all over its vast territories, including severe restriction in railway traffic. Turing this war the Soviet State attempted to conceal this humiliatingful situation from its people by issuing propaganda pamphlets representing the Finnish people as ten Times more numerous and talking of a clash with a people of thirty million backed by imperialistic forces of the capitalist world. Thus, beginning its military operations with an inroad into Poland,the Soviet State was completely unprepared,having only weak support from its poorly organized socialistic state economy.lt was as late as during the Finnish war that important reforms were carried out in the top ranks of the Rod Army. Of course, the 160 millions of Stalin\'s Empire were able to force little Finland to capitulation in spite of the resistance of the Finns, which had called forth the admiration of all the world. But the proportion was 1:55! During the war the speaker of the Finnish radio expressed his disgust in the following terms: "We are not soldiers in this war, we are butchers"; the proportion of casualitics was 1:30 in favour of the Finns. The Russians broke the Mannerheim line only after the inhuman order was given that no lives were to be spared, whereupon the well fortified Finnish positions were rammed by setting in a tremendous mass of Soviet troops. 3. Forcing Finland into capitulation, the Soviet State continued its aggression.ln the middle of June,l94o,it broke into the Baltic territories,openly occupying the Three Baltic States: Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania, giving as its reason that these three small peaceful and nations consisting in all of 6 million inhabitants had joined a military union in order to attack the huge Empire of 160 millions!! Poland, Finland, the Baltic States and Bessa.abia – it was a thoroughly prepared plan of aggression,the realization of which Soviet Russia intended to bring about in the same way as in 1917 in Russia, i. c., by taking advantage of the exhaustion of other nations during the second world war and their desire to make an end to it, this time in order to accomplish the bolshevization of the world. Then also, just as today, much was being said about capitalist intervention (later on it was called "capitalist encirclement"),about instigators of war against the newly established "socialist home-country", about hostile imperialistic blocks, etc. And the poor giant of 160 millions was said to have no alternative in saving itself from 6 million Baltic аМЗтДlлоп Finnish people but to defend itself desperately,"unfortunately" being compelled to occupy these States! Thus, military unpreparedness and poor economic condit – ions are no obstacles to the Soviet State for starting a wan On the other hand, there is always a reason for war when world conditions seem propitious and when they can be rendered still more propitious by furthering civil wars wherever possible and by provoking permanent disturbances within democratic countries. So, at a recent meeting of the Ccminform it was again decided to continue. At the present moment the Soviet hope of achieving a universal communist subversion is founded on the tremendous mistakes made in the course of World War 11. This subversion would extend Communist power beyond all limits. Once again this aggressiveness is being applied under the disguise of slogans of peace: "Much as the war-mongers try to discredit our socialist country, they will not succeed in banishing from the mind of the common people the conviction that the Soviet Union is the sincere fighter for peace, and consistently protects this peace for the whole world;that our country, as Comrade Stalin says, is able to maintain a policy of peace, not Pharisaically, but honestly, openly,firmly and consistently, for international co-operation, and for friendship among all nations. The peace policy of the Soviet State is in agreement with the fundamental interests of our people and of all mankind". (Pravda, no. 54, 1950: Leader "Armed Forces of  Soviet Homeland"). The above mentioned events from recent history give the clearest evidence as to how this "struggle for peace in the whole world" looks in the light of truth: in all these cases the aggressive ends of the Soviet State were pursued by means of an open military invasion. The political aims of this State arc often presented to the world under the title of "international co-operation" and of "friendship among nations". Stalin himself did so on 5 different occasions which shows how important and necessary for Soviet politics this tactical move seems to be. 1. In September 1946 Stalin said to Alexander Werth, the "Sunday Times" Correspondent: "I doubt if the possibility of peaceful co-operation could diminish, on the contrary it could increase". , 2. In December 1946 he gave the same reply to Roosevelt, the Presidents son, who asked for Stalin sopmion on a possible peaceful co-existence of the United States and the Soviet Union: "Yes, of course. This is not only possible, but quite a reasonable and attainable thing". 3. And furthermore: in April 1947 in a conversation with Senator Stassen, in May 1948 in the well-known reply to Wallos the same thing was said, and in January 1949 in answer to Mr. Smith, Director of the International News Service, Stalin even expressed his willingness to discuss with U.S.A. a common Declaration (the Russian Communists arc very fond of discussions!) by which both Parties agreed to abstain from waging war on each other, to conclude a Pact of Peace, followed by gradual disarmament. (It should bo noted that Stalin calls "armament" not what he is doing by means of slave labor, but the measures Europe takes with a view to defending itself). And, referring to these five statements of Stalin, Malenkov repeats the same. As Stalin has been reiterating such statements almost every year since September, 1946, it is only natural to ask why when the democratic world led by the U.S.A. was without any doubt prepared to do everything towards securing peace and to establishing national rights did there follow no definite action in this connection on the part of the Soviet Union? Why did not this Power after all take any steps at the termination of the Second World War to enforce "international co-operation on a reciprocal basis according to all engagements assumed" as mentioned by if the Soviet Union really wished to act according to Stalin s Declarations?" The answer is: as these**statements have been systematically repeated since 1946, and not the slightest thing has happened towards making them come true, it is more than obvious that they are meant to serve other purposes, that they arc only the expression of certain tactics to produce an effect necessary for the Kremlin. When in 1946 Stalin for the first time declared ihe Soviet Uninonls readiness to go on living side by side with the capitalistic system, it had an amazing response in the world at large! – London: "Almost the only reassuring statement in foreign polities since the end of the war!" – Paris:"The statement was received with joy, and is considered as the turning point in the development of international relations!" This was the very effect Stalin was aiming at: a deceived world transported with new hope, but blind in regard to actial conditions created by the Soviet State, and at the same time weakened and neutralized in its capacity to resist communis!  Alexander Werth: "I consider it impossible that the Soviet Union would use Germany against Western Europe and U.S.A. in any way whatever . But – there followed the blockade of Berlin, the complete bolshcvization of Eastern Germany and all those events which are weH кпояп  with Staling declarations there has always \'been great communist action in one part of the world or other, as f.ex., in 1946, when Stalin assured A.Werth of a possible peaceful co-existence and of the fulfilment of international engagements, the Soviet Union started military action against Persia, terrorizing Turkey at the same time, surely a premeditated attempt to force the Near East to submission. Just as now Malenkovls similar statement has not been followed by its realization, but by the realization of declaration, which gives a perfect example of how Moscow understands this "living side by side"! Namely: the Korean people are being urged to liberatethemselves from foreign imperialists – in a peaceful way, i. е., without an armed clash. In ether words, the Koreans are being incited to rise against the American occupation power and to drive the Americans out of the country. In the meantime Moscow has already made its preparations for turning Korea, like the Baltic States, like Czechoslovakia and Eastern Germany,into an ordinary Soviet Republic. At this moment similar events are taking place in Germany. The so-called National Council of the National Front Movement has been established with the appeal that "the Anglo-Americans wished to make a colony outofGermany and that therefore Western Germany should be liberated by all means from foreign dependency and from the clutchof the Atlantic Pact". This statement is accompanied by typical attempts to bring the chauvinism of nazi-Germans to the point of ebullition (see "Pravda" of Febr. 4, 1950, p. 4). To divert the world\'s attention from these far – sighted actions ofthe Kremlin and to envelop mankind in a mist of new hopes for a compromise "between the great Powers", Stalin s statements are being repeated over and over again. Buta parallel action in the sense of Molotov\'s speech is carried outsimultaneously. Unfortunately and to the detriment of humanity these subterfuges of Moscow nearly always succeed, since there are enough people in the democracies spreading theseßussian statements as honest endeavours at furthering peace. To accomplish all this is the only purpose ofMoscows tactics. Never is there to be expected on the part of the Kremlin any other "international co-operation". All that is said for foreign use is only "tactics and strategy", and along withthis tho many millions of the Soviet Union are being prepared for the occupation of the free world. By means of its tactics Moscow has succeeded up to now in gradually extending the sphere of influence of the Communist system and in constantly pushing back the rule of democratic freedom. This will last untilthere is no way of retreat, because there will be no space anymore to retreat to. No treaties and no negotiations will evercnange these tactics of the Kremlin. . . Docs Moscow wish war? It wages war all the time,gainingone territory after the other. It has been preparing this war for 30 years. Whoever does not yet understand it, must be ready to become a Soviet "citizen" for the rest of his life, dedicating the future of his children to the camps of compulsory labour in the Soviet Union. А. Кrauer.']

Representative_Docs: ["ANDREW PEACOCK, AUSTRALIAN MINISTER FOR FOREIGN AFFAIRS Mr Speaker, I seek leave to make a statement on the Government's response to the Report of the Joint Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence on Human Rights in the Soviet Union. Mr. Speaker, the Report of the Joint Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence on «Human Rights in the Soviet Union» was presented to the Parliament on 8 November 1979. The inquiry which led to this report arose from the presentation of a petition in the House of Representatives on 2 November 1976 by the Hon. W. C. Wentworth who was then the Member for Mackellar. The petition requested the Australian Government to take up with the Soviet Union the issue of alleged Soviet discrimination against Soviet Jewry. The Joint Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence appointed, on 6 September 1977, a Sub-Committee to enquire and report on the status of Soviet Jewry. It resolved on 18 November 1977 to enlarge the terms of reference of the Sub-Committee to the following: «Human rights in the Soviet Union bearing in mind Australia's support for the principles contained in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and the Final Act of the Helsinki Agreement.» Mr Speaker, I might say at the outset that the brutal Soviet invasion of Afghanistan which has occurred since , the Committee tabled its report has provided stark new evidence of the Soviet Union's disregard for individuals and their human rights. While the Helsinki accords cover relations within Europe, they nevertheless involve an universal commitment. Soviet intervention in Afghanistan has breached not only the most fundamental human rights provisions of the Helsinki accords but also all other principles of the accords such as refraining from the threat or use of force and non-intervention in the internal affairs of other states and self-determination of peoples – to name but a few. The Government has considered the Joint Committee's report and regards it as a very significant contribution to the knowledge of this Parliament and the Australian public at large, of the internal processes of the Soviet Union, a vast and complex country whose aggressive actions have been debated in this Parliament at great length. FIRST OFFICIAL ENQUIRY Though a number of other parliaments and governments have given substantial attention to this problem over the years, to the Government's knowledge this enquiry of the Joint Committee is the first official parliamentary enquiry into the general subject of human rights in the Soviet Union. The Government considers that the members of the Joint Committee, and in particular its Sub-Committee on Human Rights in the Soviet Union chaired by Senator Wheeldon, are to be commended for their application in following through this difficult task, and for the honesty of purpose which they brought to their inquiry. The report is impressive notwithstanding the limits within which the inquiry HUMAN RIGHTS IN THE SOVIET UNION STATEMENT BY THE MINISTER FOR FOREIGN AFFAIRS, MR ANDREW PEACOCK, TO THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES ON 18 SEPTEMBER 1980,  CONVEYING THE GOVERNMENT'S RESPONSE TO THE REPORT Mj OF THE JOINT FOREIGN AFFAIRS AND DEFENCE COMMITTEE ON HUMAN RIGHTS IN THE SOVIET UNION had to operate. One particular limitation to which the report draws attention, was that despite persistent efforts by the Sub-Committee, no evidence was recei. ved by it from any official representative of the Soviet Government. It is clear from the report, however, that the inquiry went to some lenghts to examine official Soviet material concerning human rights questions, and it would be difficult to maintain that the conclusions of the report would have been substantially different had evidence been submitted by representatives of the Soviet Government. There are very clear indications throughout the text of the report that there has been a very scrupulous concern for the balance and objectivity of its judgements. In his preface to the report, the Chairman of the Sub-Committee addresses the question why the Committee should study the situation of human rights in one country, namely the Soviet Union, when it appears that there are numerous countries throughout the world where human rights and civil liberties are denied. The Government considers the Chairman's justifications to be most convincing. In the first place, not only is the Soviet Union a super power with world-wide interests and ambitions, but it promotes its own social and political system as the road to be taken sooner or later by all mankind. It does this not just through propaganda, but by patronage of revolutionary movements seeking to impose communism and by the use of its military might wherever opportunities arise. Mr Speaker, Australia of course has for long been the object of such attention in the form of Soviet Union's continuing support for the communist movement in this country. Australian communists, whose declared purpose is to see our parliamentary democracy replaced by revolutionary meaans, are received with honours in Moscow and decorated for their efforts. But notwithstanding there considerations, the Soviet Union of course rejects all outside criticism of its own system as illegitimate interference in its internal affairs. The Government, needless to say, finds such a proposition spurious, and thus unacceptable. 65 POINTS ALL ACCEPTED Sixty five points are set out in the conclusions and recommendations of the report. Fifty three of these are conclusions derived from the evidence given the Committee and all are accepted by the Government. Obviously it would not be praticable to comment on these fifty three conclusions individually. Collectively, they constitute a grim picture of a massive abuse of human rights in the Soviet Union. I note that a number of members of the Joint Committee have entered some reservations concerning the report. In particular, three members from the other side of the House have expressed general reservations about the report. Even they, however, concede, that «based on the substantial evidence available, the civil rights of certain groups, especially minorities, in the Soviet Union are seriously infringed and fall below that which should be expected in any civilised society.» There would therefore seem to be scope for a bipartisan attitude towards the issues raised in this report. In brief terms the report notes that the best available estimate, which comes from Amnesty International, is that there are at least 10,000 political and religious prisoners in the Soviet Union, of whom at least 2,000 have been imprisoned for their religious beliefs. Western authorities have estimated that at any time some 300 Soviet «dissidents» are believed to be held in prison psychiatric hospitals and more in ordinary psychiatric hospitals.There is a good deal of evidence indicating that thoise identified as «refuseniks», Soviet Jews who have publicised the Soviet Government's refusal of their exit visas, have numbered in recent years between 2,000 – 2,500. Quotations from some of the more general of the report's conclusions sum up the situation. «In would seem that there are several thousand Soviet citizens who have had the courage to attempt to excercise their rights to freedom of belief or freedom of expression, nominally guaranteed them in the Soviet Constitution, and enshrined in the international conventions on human rights to which the USSR is a party... These men and women are singled out for especially severe treatment within the Soviet legal and penal systems... Soviet dissidents cannot hope for any positive intervention on their behalf from those Soviet institutions that are intended to provide prisoners with an avenue of appeal... Conditions in the Soviet prison system are harsh, britai and degrading of human dignity... The Soviet Union is systematicallly abusing psychiatry through the forced treatment of mentally healthy people in order to «cure» them of their political or religious beliefs...» The Government accepts these comments as a reflection of the terrible plight of some thousands of people in the Soviet Union. The Government notes with the deepest concern that Soviet citizens cannot depend on the guarantees of the Soviet Constitution, because the repressive state apparatus ensures that those guarantees are meaningless. I now turn to the thirteen recommendations put forward in the report. RECOMMENDATION IN PARA. 5 The recommendation in paragraph 5 of the report's ((Conclusions and Recommendations» is that whenever possible the Australian Embassy in Moscow send observers to Soviet political trials, and should keep the Australian Government informed on human rights violations. The Government accepts this recommendation, while noting that there will be limits on the Embassy's ability to carry out the recommended role. Some of these limits will be imposed by the Soviet Government which seeks to prevent outside observance of such trials for reasons which are apparent to all; others will be as a result of the Embassy's small resources. In recent years the Embassy has been directed to seek and observe a political trial in the Soviet Union, and it has reported extensively on human rights there. The recommendation un paragraph 26 is that the Government request the Soviet Government not to circulate in Australia (an on Soviet cruise ships in Australian waters) Soviet books and pamphlets which contain the type of anti-Semitic material quoted in the report. The Government is prepared to act on this recommendation and will approach the Soviet Embassy accordingly. Further approaches will be made should specific .items cause offence. Honourable members will be aware that Soviet cruise vessels ceased operations in Australian waters from the end of May. RECOMMENDATION IN PARA. 31 The recommendation in paragraph 31 is that the Government use judiciously applied pressure on the Soviet authorities, so that the USSR will allow those Soviet citizens to emigrate who wish to be reunited with relatives in Australia. This recommendation is accepted by the Government. It has been and will continue to be the practice of the Government to make government-togovernment representations in those cases where there is a specific Australian interes. The majority of these cases involve family reunions. RECOMMENDATION IN PARA. 35 The recommendation in paragraph 35 is that the Government request the UN Secretary-General to utilise the UN information service in Moscow to make available copies of UN concentions on human rights. The Government accepts that recommendation. It has already taken steps to promore the dissemination of international instruments on human rights in all countries, including the USSR. The Australian delegation to the recent session of the UN Commission on Human Rights has actively promoted Australian initiatives aimed at securing wider publicity, at all levels, of the work of the United Nationj in the field of human rights. These initiatives will set in motion a program for the wide dissemination of the international instruments in as many languages as possible. The relevant resolutions have all been adopted by concensus, involving, ironically enough, the support of the Soviet Union. The Commission in 1979 also adopted by concensus a resolution on the international covenants on human rights which urges all governments to publish the texts of the covenants and to disseminate them to make them known as widely as possible within their territories. In light of this activity I consider that further action by Australia would not be appropriate until the time comes, probably next year, to review the implementation of these resolutions. RECOMMENDATION IN PARA. 48 The recommendation in paragraph 48 is that the Government give consideration to seeking the establishment of a permament, independent, international organisation to inquire into the abuse of psychiatry for political purposes. The Government has considered this proposal but takes the view that existing forums, particulary the UN Commission on Human Rights and the World Health Organisation, would seem to provide adequate scope for inquiries of this kind. The Commission on Human Rights has in recent years been examining the quesstion of the abuse of psychiatry, and the General Assembly has now received from the World Health Organisation a draft code of medical ethics which is currently under study. A proposal to set up a new international organisation would cut across these studies and would be unlikely to attract the support from other governments necessary for success. RECOMMENDATION IN PARA. 50 The recommendation in paragraph 50 is that the Government make every use of appropriate opportunities to: (a) give full support for the principles contained in the Final Act of the Helsinki Agreement; (b) joint other democracies in protesting against the repression of Soviet citizens who are monitoring the extent of the USSR's compliance with the Helsinki Agreement; and (c) seek the release from Soviet prisons and labour colonies of those Soviet citizens who have been imprisoned merely because of their Helsinki «watch group» acitivities. Australia is not a participant in the Conference on Security and Cooperation in Europe (CSCE) which produced the Final Act in Helsinki. The Government has made it very clear, however, that it gives its full support to the principles contained in the Helsinki Final Act including those concerning human rights and civil liberties. We accept this recommendation. It was intended that compliance with the Final Act should be monitored not only by the CSCE governments, but also by the citizens of participating States. Such activity is encouraged by the Final Act and the Soviet Union s repression and imprisonment of those who seek to monitor its provisions is one of its more blatant and hypocritical abuses of that Final Act. The Government is on record as strongly condemning these shameless abuses. We most certainly wish to see the release of these unfortunate people and the Government stands willing to lend its weight to international initiatives aimed at their release. The Government has strongly supported recent rezolutions of the UN Commission on Human Rights on these issues and has added its voice to the many expressions of international concern at the fate of the prominent dissidents Sharansky and Sakharov. RECOMMENDATION IN PARA. 58 The recommendation in paragraph 58 is that in the course of bilateral discussions or negotiations with the USSR, the Government takes the opportunities that arise to state its disapproval of Soviet breaches of human rights. The Government accepts this recommendation. It has been the Government's practice to register from time to time with a number of governments, including th at of the USSR, public and official concern felt in Australia over abuses of human rights in those countries. The Government will continue to register these concerns. I might add that it is the Government's experience that such approaches are more likely to be productive, in terms of alleviating actual human suffering, if attention can be drawn on these occasions to particular cases with a specific Australian connection. RECOMMENDATION IN PARA. 60 The recommendation in paragraph 60 is that Australia's representatives at the United Nations and its agencies, particularly the United Nations Commission on Human Rights, be instructed to use these forums to raise human rights issues. This recommendation is also already being implemented. The Commission on Human Rights is one of a number of bodies un which Australia takes an active part in the broad humanitarian work of the UN. Indeed, Mr Speaker, I would remind honourable members that the Government's singular commitment to human rights was recognised by our election to the Commission on Human Rights in 1978 and by Australia's re-election to the Commission in May of this year for a further three year term. Australia last participated as a member of the Commission as long ago as 1956. This success was achieved in the face of strong competition and was a tribute i to the leading role played by Australia in the previous three years in the development of the Commission's procedures as well as in the evolution of its human rights philosophy. We will be playing a similarly active role during our forthcoming term. I have welcomed the decision of the Commission on Human Rights at its most recent session, in February, to adopt a strongly worded resolution condemning the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan and the resulting violations of human rights in that country. The Australian delegation also supported moves to have the Commission's concern at the exile of Sakharov brought to the attention of the Soviet authorities. Sakharov continues to be harassed and humiliated in isolated exile, deprived of his human rights and professional activity, simply for having the courage to speak his mind. RECOMMENDATION IN PARA. 62 The recommendation at paragraph 62 is that the Government try to persuade the Soviet Government that the USSR would be seen in a more favourable light if large numbers of its political and religious prisoners were released from prisons, labour colinies and psychiatric hospitals before the Olympic Games. Moscow's «preparations» for the Olympic Games, far from including the release of political and religious prisoners, involved a wave of new detentions and the enforced relocation of dissidents and even children away from the city. Whilst no one could deny the genuine concerns to prevent terrorist attacks, the presence in overwhelming numbers of the KGB and other security organs as well as at least 230,000 militia was clearly designed to insulate the Soviet people from human contacts with visitors and to prevent Western visitors from being exposed to the starker realities of Soviet life. Such practices were in direct contravention of the USSR's obligations under Basket Three of the Helsinki Accords. Mr Speaker, honourable members will be aware thet this recommendation of the report was overtaken by the efforts of many Governments, including Australia's to boycott the Moscow Olympic Games as a consequence of the Soviet invasion and occupation of Afghanistan. Thouali incomplete, the boycott of the Olympic Games in Moscow was undeniable in its effect. It denied the Soviet Union both the total propaganda victory and the comprehensive endorsement of its policies which it has hoped to achieve. It helped focus the world's attention on the Soviet Union, on its activities in Afghanistan, and on Soviet foreign policy in general. It demonstrated plainly to the Soviet leadership the reality and depth of international anger at Soviet behaviour in Afghanistan. RECOMMENDATION IN PARA. 63 The recommendation in paragraph 63 is that the Government not allow the Soviet Government to take academic, cultural, scientific and other exchanges for granted. It should be made clear to X the Soviet Union that these exchanges could be discontinued as a result of human rights violations. While the Government accepts this recommendation, I note that in the terms it was put forward it has been largely overtaken by the Government's decision to act to curtail relations with the Soviet Union in a number of fields as a response to the Soviet Union's invasion and occupation of Afghanistan. For all practical purposes Soviet-Australian bilateral cultural, academic and scientific exchanges have been suspended. There is nolongjr any question of the Soviet Union taking them for granted. The Government accepts, however, that such contacts can provide channels through which concern on human rights issues can be conveyed. RECOMMENDATIONS IN PARA. 64 There are two recommendations in paragraph 64; first that the Government suggest to the Soviet Union that it may Beigas 5. lappusē. HUMAN RIGHTS IN THE SOVIET UNION be possible to arrange a reciprocal exchange of views in Soviet and Australian newspapers, and in other publications; and secondly that Australia seek the right to distribute publications in the Soviet Union, in the same way as the USSR is allowed to do in Australia. While the Government understands the sentiments hehind these recommendations, in the light of the Soviet practices ouUined in this report, there are no grounds for believing that proposals on these lines would gain any useful response from the Soviet Union. The Government stands ready to participate in a reciprocal exchange of views in the newspapers of both countries, indeed I hereby give notice to the Soviet Union that we are willing. But I do not expect that this challenge will be answered. The report also makes clear that the prospects for reciprocal rights to distribute publications in the Soviet Union are not bright. Should we therefore place bans on Soviet publications in Australia? I think not. We should not seek to emulate in this country those fearing and restrictive policies of the Soviet Union which have contrived to take meaning from the printed word in that country. Our society is strong enough for us to remain indifferent to the availability of Soviet material here. RECOMMENDATION IN PARA. 65 The final recommendation, in paragraph 65, is that the Parliament establish a Standing Commitee on Human Rights to report on serious violations of human rights in any country, including Australia. This is a matter for the Parliament rather than the Government to decide. I note, however, that four members of the Joint Committee have entered reservations on this recommendation. Two have noted that inquiries by the parliament of one country into the affairs of another country have implications which can be detrimental to external relations with countries which have different political systems, traditions, cultures and attitudes. Another has expressed the view that Australia can best work towards strengthening of human rights throughout the world by lending its full support to the appropriate international organisations. The Government broadly shares these views and suggests that it would be adequate to maintain the present practice whereby » human rights matters may be referred to existing committees as may be considered appropriate. Broad issues of foreign policy can of course be considered by the two Parliamentary Foreign Affairs and Defence Committees under existing procedures which are adequate for the purpose. The report we are now considering is testimony to this. I could also note that effective and imprtant work is already being done informally by the Parliamentary Group of Amnesty International. I support its activities and believe that its informality and its broad base in the Parliament enables it to achieve more, and often more quickly, than a formal Committee could. EASTERN EUROPE NOT PREPARED TO ACCEPT SOVIET MODEL Before I conclude I would like to refer briefly to what is now occuring in Poland. Events there have drawn attention again to questions of European security. They also point to the continuing struggle by people who live under Soviet control to achieve the basic freedoms of life which we in the West take for granted. Poland's workers have acted with great courage and dignity in their efforts to establish free trade unions and it is significant that their demands include other fundamental principles of civil liberty and human rights. If Polish workers are successful – and one must hope that the authorities in that country will honour their undertakings to carry out those reforms in practice – this develpoment will be of immense importance. It will have farreaching implications not only for the people of Poland but also for those countries of Eastern Europe whose people have for so long been deprived of their basic rights and freedoms. Above all, it will demonstrate again that the people of Eastern Europe are not prepared to accept the oppressions and indignities of the Soviet model of human existence. From recent reports it is also abundantly clear that abuses of human rights continue unabated within the Soviet Union itself in flagrant disregard of the spirit of the Helsinki Final Act and the other international conventions to which that country remains a signatory. I refer particularly to the recent trials of Father Yakunin, a Russian Ortodox priest, Tatyana Valikanova, and the lengthening of the sentence of Yuri Orlov, one of the founders of the Helsinki Monitoring Group. Mr Speaker, these continued abuses of the rights of Soviet citizens underline the necessity of heightened public consciousness, and the condemnation of such policies. Otrkārt, ir jānožēlo, ka nespēja vienoties par kopēju tekstu valdības un opozīcijas starpā rada iespaidu, ka Baltijas valstu jautājumā Austrālijā pastāvētu kādas bāziskas domstarpības. "Kalnins has always been the more idontifiably Latvian of the two men, by virtue of his stronger allegiance to his traditional musical roots, including the spirit of folk song that expresses itself in the new concerto\'s slow movement. Kenins, with his neoclassical French training, has been much more cosmopolitan and in recent years especially, much more contemporary in his musical language. There is nothing contemporay at all in the language of the Kalnins Piano Concerto, a lighthearted virtuoso romp, honoring the tradition of decorative piano writing embodied in the concertos of Saint-Saens and Prokofiev. It nonetheless proved an ideal vehicle for Arthur Ozolins, the Latvian-parented Toronto pianist, notonly because of the ease with which Ozolins\' technique coped with the runs and ornamental flourishes of the opening movement but because of his sensitivity to the unabashed lyricism embodied in the lovely folk song melody of the succeeding "Lento espressivo". As for the rhythmically jaunty finale, I couldn\'t make my mind up whether it reminded me more of the spirit of Prokofiev\'s Peter and The Wolf or the background for an animated cartoon. Now in his 80s, Kalnins appears to be grinning his way toward the grave. On the evidence of the Eighth Symphony, Kenins is still frowning. This is a serious work, three movements and 25 minutes in length, with subtitle ( "Sinfonia Concertante" ) alluding to the way an organ is drawn into dialogue with the orchestra. In actuality, and despite the strong presence of as strong a player as Anita Rundans at the console, the organ supplied more spice than meat or carrots to the stew. The percussion battery generated – as it has in much recent Kenins – more of the score\'s personality..." Kenins has had numerous new works premiered in this city, but he is setting something of a record this year with premiers in four other centres, including Paris, Bucharest and New York. His Eighth Symphony is eminently listenablc. The organ, played by Rundans, seemed more an orchestral member than a virtuoso solo instrument – a part played more effectively by the timpani. The outer movements roused excitement with an urgency in the dominating rhythmic patterns. Lyricism got full play in the middle movement, memorable for the gradual unfolding of the long opening crescendo. But much of the work\'s success could be attributed to the controlled use of dynamic and tonal contrasts. Janis Kalnins\'s Concerto for Piano and Chamber Orchestra, with Ozolins as soloist, is a brilliant composition of a lighter  than the Kenins piece and also more romantic and less modern in its style. Ozolins played it brilliantly, setting the style and mood, as the piano was given long solo introductions and cadenzas. He did not manage to avoid the sentimental tendencies of the slow movement, but otherwise inspired orchestra and conductor to precise, often dramatic heights. Peterson, who took part in last Wednesday\'s concert at the Toronto Convention Centre, was featured in four of Jan Medins\' Latvian folk songs. The most expressive of these were Sister\'s Lament for the Homeland and the equally plaintive There Are Hills beyond the Lake. Representative_Docs: ['ONLY ESTONIA HAD INFLATION IN AUGUST Estonia was the oniy one of the three Baltic states where consumer price index (CPI) increased in August over July as the consumer prices there rose by 0.1%. Latvia and Lithuania had deflation in August over July as consumer prices in Latvia fell by 0.5% and Lithuania by 0.6%. Latvia saw the consumer prices declining in August over July for the third year in a row. In Latvia the prices were pulled down chiefly as a result of seasonal drop in prices of food stuffs. The price index also declined as footwear and garments prices were reduced by discounts. In Estonia the prices were pushed up by growing meat prices, sugar price as well as heating related expenses. Year-on-year consumer prices in Estonia grew 4.4%. In Lithuanian the consumer prices were pulled down by failing food, fuel, garments and footwear prices. Year-on-year consumer prices rose 0.9% in Lithuania in August 2000.', 'INFLATION IN JULY Latvia: According to the State Statistics Committee, in comparison to June, the rate of inflation in Latvia for Jury was 1.1 %. The greatest increase was in public transportation – 45.5% – which is attributable to the almost two-fold increase in rail transport prices that came into force July 1. The Director of the Macro-fiscal Analysis and Forecasting Department of the Ministry of Finance, Inna Steinbuka, considers the 1.1% inflation rate to be within the normal range, noting that last year the level of inflation for July was 0.8%. She added that the main reason for the increase in the rate of inflation was the rail tariff increase, which is a "one-time" rise. Ms. Steinbuka commented that, although the rate of inflation for July exceeds the norm suggested by the International Monetary Fund by a few tenths of a percent, with the implementation of an 18% tax on foods, the real rate of inflation in Latvia will be half that forecast by the IMF. Given the marked increase in the price of gasoline at the beginning of August, it is expected that the rate of inflation will also rise in August, despite the fact that food product prices are not expected to increase. Estonia: The Estonian Statistics Department announced that the consumer price index rose (CP!) by 2.8% in July, BNS reported on 5 August The largest increase was for housing expenditures (10.9%), followed by housekeeping (2.6%) and various goods and services (2.3%). Prices for fruits and vegetables, however, fell by 6.2%, while prices of dairy products and eggs dropped by 2.3%. In comparison with July 1993, the CPI increased by 50.1%. Lithuania: On 8 August the head of the Lithuanian Statistics Department, Kestutis Zaborskas, told Radio Lithuania that the CPI had increased by 2.1 % in July, the same rate as in June. The greatest increase was for fuel and energy, which rose by 12.5%. while food prices increased by only 0.1%. He expressed satisfaction that health care costs, which rose by 43% in the first half of the year, grew by only 2 8% in July.', 'LATVIA HAS LOWEST INFLATION RATE OF BALTIC STATES IN JUNE This June Latvia had the lowest inflation rate of the Baltic states as compared to May, the Latvian Central Statistics Office reported on 11 July. in June 2000 consumer prices in Latvia grew by 0.1% over May 2000 butyear-on-year the inflation rate was 2.4%. The consumer price index in Latvia this June was boosted mostiy by growing prices for food and alcoholic drinks but separate food products saw price reduction. In Estonia consumer prices grew 0.3% this June over May and increased by 3.2% over June 1999. According to the Estonian Statistics Department, in June 2000 prices were up 0.2% for food, 0.3% for manufactured goods and 0.4% for services over May 2000. The Estonian consumer price index was affected mostiy by rising prices for fuel and food, Lithuania posted inflation of 0.7% this June over May but year-on-year the consumer price growth was 1.3%.']

Representative_Docs: ["Latvian migration to Ireland slowed in 2010, government data suggest Latvians Online January 10, 2011 The count of persons from Latvia registering for new identification numbers in Ireland dropped nearly 25 percent from 2009 to 2010, suggesting that the flow of immigrants has slowed. Data compiled by Ireland's Department of Social Protection show that a total of 3,134 persons from Latvia last year obtained Personal Public Service Numbers (PPSN), substantially fewer than the 3,916 in 2009. However, the data are not a true measure of immigration to Ireland. The PPSN is an identifier used by individuals in their dealings with various Irish state institutions. Since 2000, a total of 45,836 PPSNs have been issued to persons from Latvia. During the four years from 2000-2003, the total PPSNs issued to persons from Latvia reached 6,837. After Latvia joined the European Union in 2004, easing travel restrictions for guest workers, the number of new PPSNs jumped significantly. In 2005 alone, residents of Latvia accounted for 9,328 of the new PPSNs issued that year. From 2005-2008, a steady decline in new PPSNs was observed, although the number jumped 5 percent from 2008-2009 as the economic crisis in Latvia began to unfold. In all, more than 154,000 persons obtained new PPSNs last year in Ireland, a 7 percent drop from 2009. Ireland, which for several years saw rapid economic growth that lured tens of thousands of new immigrants, also has suffered an economic decline. Last year just 207 persons from Estonia sought PPSNs, a more than 50 percent drop from 2009. Meanwhile, 4,353 persons from Lithuania last year obtained the identification numbers, a 15 percent increase over 2009.", 'Population Changes in Latvia in Consequence of World War 2 At various times in the course of history Latvia has suffered great deciminations in population as a result of devastating war action by neighboring great powers on Latvian soil. Subsequent periods of peace, however, enabled the Latvian nation to recover, to redeem the population losses and to grow in numbers. During the long period of peace, before the outbreak of World War I in 1914, the population of Latvia had reached its peak of 2,552,000. This figure, however, included also Russian officials of the czarist regime, Russian soldiers stationed on Latvian soil, visitors to Latvian health resorts and other temporary residents. The actual number of permanent inhabitants of Latvia was probably somewhat lower. When the German armed forces of World War I invaded Latvia in 1915, there occurred a great exodus ol refugees and expellees, particularly from south-eastern Kurzeme and Latvia\'s capital, Riga, which swept 7-800,000 people into the interior of Russia. After the establishment of the independent Republic of Latvia, a quarter of a million Latvian refugees returned to their native land. When repatriation was completed in 1925, the population of Latvia still amounted to not more than 1,850,000. According to Soviet statistics, in 1926 some 150,000 Latvians were counted in Russia. What happened to the remainder of refugees is unknown. In 1939 the population of Latvia had grown to a full 2 million. Of these 1,520,000 (76 per cent.) were Latvians; but the remaining 460,000 ( 24 per cent.) were composed of so-called national minorities—Jews (94,000), Germans (60,000), Russians ( 207,000), Poles (49,000) and others (70, 000). Both world wars inflicted great suffering and devastating losses on the Latvian nation. But there is a fundamental difference in the consequences: While Latvia regained its independence after the first war, as a result of the nation\'s ceaseless struggle for liberation, World War 11. brought. renewed bondage of Soviet occupation, which continues to the present day. Concerning the question of population losses through deportations, emigration, mass murder, war casualties, etc., we find accurate data only for the period ending with 1944-45. From there we must base our calculations on unverified reports from Latvian underground sources, prisoners of war repatriated from Russia, other occasional accounts given by eye witnesses who chanced to escape to the | West, and whatever facts we may derive from Soviet statistics and announcements, weighing and balancing them in the light of facts and reason. On occasion of the so-called "eletcions" in Soviet Latvia in December 1950, a total of 1,504,-650 voters was registered. This number must comprise practically the entire population over 18 years of age, since in Soviet countries everyone must "vote" for the single Kremlin-approved slate of the "people\'s representatives." According to Prof. P. Stares, the total number of minors under 18 years would amount to about 35 per cent, of the adult population. Thus we arrive at the conclusion that Latvia at p-esent is inhabited by slightly i. ore than 2 million people, which was Latvia\'s population before the war. Prof. A. Schwabe, editor of the Latvian Encyclopaedia (Stockhom, 1951) arrives by somewhat different calculations at a slightly higher figure. At the beginning of World War 11. the entire number By R. G. Shillers (60,000) of former Latvian citizens of German nationality emigrated voluntarily to Germany, on the basis of a special agreement between Germany and Latvia. During the year of the first Soviet occupation 34,000 Latvians were deported or murdered by the Soviet political police. Of these, 30,000 have been identified and are listed in the files of the International Red Cross in Geneva (compare "These Names Accuse," introduction, pp. 35-36, Stockholm, 1951). During the German occupation (1941-1945) the majority of Latvian Jews, having amounted to 94,000 in 1939, was brutally exterminated by German – Nazi executioners. The number of Jewish survivors who were deported or fled to Soviet Russia in 1941 is estimated at 20,000. Some 4-5 thousand may now be living in Latvia. We have no exact data on Soviet deportations of Latvian citizens of Russian, Polish and other nationalities. But, according to the Latvian Encyclopaedia (p. 479), at least some 12-16,000 were deported to forced labor in Germany in 1942. During the German occupation many thousands of Latvians were imprisoned, killed, placed in concentration camps, or deported to forced labor in German war production. Beginning with 1943, Latvian men were compulsorily inducted into the armed forces. All together 25,000 may be listed as execcuted, missing or killed in warfare. But when Red forces once again invaded Latvia in 1944-1945, streams of refugees poured westward, and others were forcibly evacuated by the German occupation authorities. Most were compelled to proceed in the direction of Germany. In 1947 not less than 110,000 Latvian displaced persons and refugees were counted in Western Europe. Prior to that date their number had been much larger; but many thousands of Latvian refugees were intercepted by the advancing Reds in Eastern Germany, Poland and Czechoslovakia in 1945, and most were deported to Soviet slave labor camps. A few thousands of Latvian displaced persons in the West were later "repatriated." Adding 5,000 Latvians who had manged to escape over the Baltic Sea to Sweden, we arrive at a total of 115,000 Latvian refugees and DP\'s, of whom most have already emigrated to overseas countries—45,000 of them to the United States. Though we have no exact data on deportations and population shifts in Latvia since the second Soviet occupation, it is wellknown to us that, immediately after the re-occupation of Latvia, the Soviet MGB proceeded with its "filtering" actions, arresting and scrutinising all males of military age, as well as all "suspects." It is assumed that no more than half of these were released and that the rest was shipped to Soviet concentration camps. Adding to their number thousands of slave laborers of both sexes, driven earlier to centres of Soviet war production, students and other nationalists arrested and deported, we arrive at the overall figure of some 60,000 deportees and other victims of Soviet atrocities during the period from 1944-1949. Apart from the above mentioned, no other mass deportations were reported prior to March 1949, when total collectivisation of Latvia\'s agriculture was suddenly inaugurated and carried out in one stroke. It POPULATION LOSSES AND SHIFTS IN LATVIA SINCE THE BEGINNING OF WORLD WAR 11. Latvian Germans, who emigrated to Germany in 1940-41 60,000 Latvian Jews, murdered by the Nazis, deported or evacuated to Russia, excepting returnees 90,000 Members of other minority groups deported .. . . 15,000 Total of national minorities, deported, removed, murdered or missing 165,000 Latvians deported or killed during first Soviet occupation 34,000 Latvians, who perished during German occupation 25,000 Latvian refugees and displaced persons .. .. 115,000 Latvians, deported, killed or missing during second Soviet occupation 136,000 Total of Latvians, deported, exiled, missing or killed since the start of World War II 310,000 Total loss of original population in Latvia to the present time 475,000 It has been pointed out above that the overall population of Latvia today is the same (or slightly above) as in 1939, at which time it amounted to 2 million. Since at that time 1,520,000, or *76 per cent, were of Latvian nationality, it appears that some 1,210,000 Latvians may still be living on Latvian soil, constituting 60.5 per cent, of the population. The percentage of non-Latvians has, accordingly, increased from 24 per cent to 39.5 per cent, consisting at present chiefly of Russians and various Soviet Asiatics. Apart from Latvian citizens of Russian extratcion, they are composed of functionaries of the Soviet regime and the political police, Red soldiers, for-, mer Russian prisoners of war who remained in Latvia, and new settlers from the East. It appears that there are today more than three times as many Russians in Latvia than before World War 11. There are whole districts where Latvians today are in the minority, particularly the border regions and along the ~ea coast. However, while the political power presently completely rests in the hands of Moscow directed Soviet Russian functionaries (including the MGB), Latvians are still predominant in all fields of cultural, social and economic activities. may easily be imagined what that meant to Latvian farmers. It meant new mass deportations of the rural population, hitting hardest the so-called "kulaks," i.e., farmers who were holding 75 acres of land, living on individual homesteads. (Since the inauguration of a sweeping agrarian reform in 1922, independent democratic Latvia had done away with large agricultural estates). According to the best information available (compare with the Latvian Encyclopaedia, p. 480), it is presumed that not less than 50,000 Latvian "kulaks," including members of their families, fell victims to mass deportations during the ominous period of compulsory Soviet collectivisation. Aiding to the above figures a reasonable margin of other victims of Soviet deportations, carried out individually, as well as other conceivable population losses during the period of the second Soviet occupation to the present time, figuring it tentatively 26,000, we arrive at the following overall statistical picture: The figures provided in tihs article can by no means claim to be accurate, because the Soviet occupation regime, which alone could reveal the extent and depth of its crimes of genocide, is not inclined to open the files of the NGB for free inspection. In appraising Latvia\'s population losses, we also must take into consideration various fluctuations, as well as possible changes in the birth rate of Latvia, in consequence of the upheavals of war and foreign domination. Many former inmates ot Nazi concentration camps, or persons pressed into German war production, have later joined the masses of displaced persons. On the other hand, thousands of Latvian refugees were trapped in Eastern European regions behind the Iron Curtain, as has been mentioned above. Latvian functionaries of the Soviet Russian regime, as well as a part of the Soviet sympathisers, who had joined the flight of the Red Army in June 1941, and also survivors of such evacuees as Latvian transportation workers, soldiers inducted into the Red Army, etc., were undoubtedly later repatriated with the return of the Soviet occupation regime. Our figures comprise men, women and children. Even though the death rate in Soviet slave labor camps is appalling, having amounted in some instances to 70 per cent, within one year (compare with "Human Freedom is Being Crushed," Washington, D.C.. 1951) it is, however, obvious that a great many Latvian deportees are still alive, particularly among the expellees who have been settled in the Soviet East, without having been convicted to hard labor, this group being composed primarily of women and children. Any survivors, who may, will undoubtedly return to Latvia after its liberation, just as was the case after World War I. Some authors on this subject have put the total of Latvian population losses considerably higher than is estimated here, without however substantiating their claims, as has been attempted in this survey. Our estimates correspond in general with the calculations of more prudent authors on this subject. The Polish writer, K.M. Smogorzevski, for instance, maintains in his pamphlet "The Russification of the Baltic States," that Latvia has today a population of 2,100,000, of which 1,222,000, or 58 per cent., are Latvians. This corresponds closely to our own figures. This article aspires to serve evidence that the actual decimation of the Latvian nation has, up to the present day, been less than the population lowes caused by World War I, regardless of any other effects of the criminal Soviet occupation regime, which cannot be appraised ir mere figures. Latvia is at present still subjected to the yoke of unprecedented barbarian Soviet bondage. But the spirit oi the nation has not and wil; not be broken. It has withstood thr test of history for several thousand years. When liberation comes, the Latvian peopk will be quite capable of recovering and managing their own affairs as free people, just & they did during the past 2* years of Latvia\'s independence.', 'CHANGES IN THE LATVIAN ECONOMY: Can Productivity Be Increased? A striking feature of change in post-World War II Latvia has been the reduction in the proportion of ethnic Latvians in the population, caused, it is officially stated, by th acute labor shortage, hence the need to transfer labor and encourage migration official view is that this trend will continue, since the Latvian birth rate is so low – and the annual growth rate correspondingly low – while the labor needs of expanding industry cannot be met otherwise than by importing labor. The purpose of this paper is to check the population situation and examine the validity of the stated need to continue importing labor, and to consider suggestions for increasing the productivity of existing labor. Of necessity, this brief survey concentrates on manufacturing and does not cover agriculture, fishing, services, or the new growth in tourism. A word about sources. Apart from the published sources listed I gained much information by personal contacts on a visit to Riga last year ( my first in 50 years ), where I not only met officials but was able to gather information and opinions from some 30 to 40 informants, almost all Latvians, of various ages and occupations – a small sample of ground-level attitudes. Population Changes. Professor Zvidrins (1) equates the demographic trend in Latvia, already significant in the last century, with the Western European pattern, showing falling birth and death rates and extended longevity. Estonia has shared the same trend. Latvia lost more than a third of its population in World War I, and lost heavily again ir World War 11. Table 1 shows the population of ethnic Latvians as lower in 1970 ( 2) than ir 1935 (3), down from 75.5% 5o 56.3%. In 1979, the total population was 2,521,000, with ethnic Latvians 1,356,000 ( 53.0% ). Today the percentage is probably down to 52%. Following the pattern of Western countries, the population of Latvia is aging. In Table 2 Zvidrins (4) shows the historical trend, with the 60 – group increasing each decade and the proportion of the 0-19 group falling. Table 3 ( derived from more detailed figures listed by Rein Taagcpcra )(5) shows the same groups for 1970, divided between ethnic Latvians and others. The percentage of older Latvians is double that of "others", but the percentage of the 0-19 group is only about 8% less than for "others". Taagcpera, using his dctalcd figures, calculates that the reproduction rate for "others" is actually less than for Latvians, 0.04 to 1.02 (6). This indicates that if immigration were to stop, the "other" population numbers would fall. Actually, in recent years immigration has been falling steadily. The sources of new labor are drying up. However, immigration is unlikely to stop completely because free migrants will choose Riga, where incomes are higher, and life is more lively and interesting. Also it seems to be easier to obtain a residence permit than in Leningrad or Moscow. Manufacturing Industry. Visitor guilebooks make heavy political propaganda of the massive help given by other Soviet republics in the reestablishment of industry in Latvia after the war. It is true that Riga got prcfascntial treatment, but the reason was severely practical to the Soviet government. Though Riga suffered considerable damage, compared with the utter devastation of almost all the towns retaken by the Soviet armies, it was one of the best places to start reconstruction, so that its regenerated industries could help other areas to recover(7). Estonia received similar treatment, with attention given to Lithuania later. Capital and labor were poured into Latvian industry, setting np a pattern of "extensive" development in which.results were obtained at the expense of productivity. Now the official Soviet "buzzword" is productivity, but planners find it hard to break the habit of throwing capital and labor unprofitably at big projects. Riga in 1983 had a population of 058,000 (8). Together with its satellite Jelgava, with 70,000 it forms a large industrial complex with a wide range of manufactures, nearly half of which arc sold outside Latvia, including electric trains, vans and minibuses, machinery, washing machines and refrigerators, radios and electronic equipment, pharmaceuticals and chemicals, perfumery and fashion goods (9). Riga has the advantage of a good harbor and excellent rail connctctions with the vast Soviet market, but there arc limits to its industrial expansion. Latvia has no minerals, and no fuels except peat, and is dependent for energy on the oil and gas pipelines from the East, and the existing and planned power stations on the Daugava. There are plans for nuclear power stations, but the outlook is so uncertain that, according to Henry Ratnieks, there could be an energy crisis before the end of the century (10). Labor Productivity in Manufacturing. The rate of Soviet economic growth, particularly in the manufacturing sector, Ras been sharply declining since 1975. Western economists have tried to analyze the reasons. Both Lcvinc (11) and Cohn (12) in their contributions to the report of the Joint Committee of the Congress of the U. S., have focused on the problem. Lcvinc sees as reasons the decreasing returns to capital, low elasticity of substitution of capital for labor, and a low rate of introduction of new technology. He refers to the aging of capital stock, and states that while Soviet industries retired their capital at an average rate of 2% a year, the average U. S. rate was 4.2% ( In Riga I saw a line of operating machines which, I am sure, were 50 years old ). Cohn deals particularly with the collapse of labor productivity, showing that average man-hour productivity fell from 4.1 in 1971-75 down to 1.6% in 1976-79. 11csees an apparent inability on the part of machine manufacturers to procucc technologically advanced equipment. He notes a steady decline in the rate of growth of employment, investment, and capital stock. The rate of investment fell from 13% in the late \'50s to 7 1/2% in the late \'60s. The current 11th Five Year Plan calls for an investment rate of only 2 1/2%. Much has been written about the lag in introducing new technology into Soviet factories (13). Sometimes managers arc reluctant to welcome new machines. Various reasons arc given: fear of new methods, shortage of operator labor, uncertainty of supply cf spares, and uncertainty as to the bonus award for the effort. My guess is that when a manager is under constant pressure to maintain his production quota, he cannot spare the time to shut down to change machines. I had exactly this experience during World Was 11, when I managed a factory for the British Ministry of Supply. I carried on tfiththe old machines while the new ones sat in the yard, until an air raid shut us down for a spell. Labor productivity falls when machines are old, and new working with old can be equally inefficient. The parts of a factory must all fit together. It is difficult to get any details of the technology employed in Riga factories, but from what I saw and was told and have read, I get a vague general picture. The large new factories, as, for instance, the motor vehicle works, probably have moving production lines and a lot of automatic machinery, but the majority of production operates with 30 to 50 – year – old plant ( and 20 years is old today ). One catches glimpses of very old-fashioned ways of doing what Cohen calls "Auxilliary supporting operations." Riga industry is far from the computer-controlled, robotized, fcxiblc production lines being introduced in the U. S. and Japan, which will be necessary if Latvian factories are to turn out, for consumer needs, articles of variety and quality somewhat comparable to those of Western Europe and the U. S. Though computers arc used by the Soviet government for military and other purposes, they apparently arc not common in industry. I saw none in Riga, and though there is a computer center and some studies being done, computer language has not yet entered common speech, and no courses arc being taught in schools. Perhaps the central government is moving cautiously in this field, for, though computers would facilitate central control, their general use might present a danger to central authority. Computers can link in many directions, and in the right hands can transmit a vast amount of data outside the control of the bureaucratic structure. The typical factory manager, who for years has striven to meet quantity output quotas of products of one standard design and low quality, now faces new problems (14). Not only is he being urged to turn out goods of better quality, which will involve new, complicated costing and pricing procedures, and new manufacturing methods, and uncertainty as to supplies, and interference with his established bonus sct-up-but, in addition, his labor difficulties have increased. Just when he needs workers with greater skill, dedication, and cooperation, he complains of their greater neglect, indiscipline, theft, drunkeness, and high turnover. The old answer to production problems, throwing in more labor, is no longer possible (15). Hence his dilemma. The remedy to production problems, well understood by the government, is greater productivity. But that is not simple. Suggested Remedies: I sec no hope for these: 1. Reduce military forces. This is not now politically possible. 2. Employ more women; mobilize housewives. They already have been mobilized. 95% of Latvian women arc working or in fulltime study, the highest proportion in the Soviet Union. 3. Squeeze some underemployed labor from other sectors. Since, for example, it takes two store clerks to sell a customer one article, it would appear that retail service could afford to give up labor. But this would upset long-established custom. These suggestions, however, could be put into effect: 1. Use labor-saving devices and methods. Although capital investment under the 11th 5 year plan is greatly reduced, it should still be possible for managmcnt, with the help and cooperation of the workers, to install cheap devices to save some labor, for example, fork lift trucks and portable conveyers. In 1972, 50% of industrial workers were still performing unmechanized labor ( 16), and I have seen no evidence of substantial improvement. So there be some scope for inexpensive labor saving. 2. Introduce individual bonus incentives. Workers share in bonuses permitted to the en<* terprisc, but now there is discussion of personal incentives, and the slogan "scientific managment" has been introduced ( Taylorism in Soviet Union! ). 3. Improve factory working conditions. Much can and should be done to increase safety and improve sanitary conditions. There have been a number of critical reports (17). Last summer I walked carefully past unguarded machines in a factory shown to tour groups. I saw, one among many examples of unsafe conditions, a female worker with loose sleeves bending across a conveyor belt. In révélant literature there are references to noise, bad ventilation, and poor sanitary facilities. 4. Give more attention to younger workers (18), by counseling, job training, and selection, and provide more opportunities for promotion. 5. Build more housing units. Improved housing has been one of the keys to morale right back to Robert Owen. In Riga the official statement is that there is no housing problem, even though my ground-level informants gave me evidence of gross overcrowding. Based on official figures, the average per-capita urban living space in 1970 was 15.3 square meters (19), but this, even if true, covered up much discrimination. My chief street-level informant visited a flat measuring about 88 square meters that housed 12 persons – three middle-level income families – including a teacher and an engineer. They felt lucky that they were all related. Hany unrelated families, according to my informants, must share similarly small quarters. 6. Provide more convenient services. Many living quarters, especially some of the new apartment blocks in the factory areas, are far from shops, schools, and services. Improvement here would decrease stress and exhaustion, thereby increasing morale and productivity. 7. Provide relief for women. The lot of a woman is hard (20). She not only holds a job, but has to run a household and deal with shortages and the absurd distribution system, for example so many long waiting lines. It takes her two or more hours to shop for a day\'s meals. Workers rarely own cars, so she has to carry purchases home on densely crowded public transport. Convenience foods are unknown, so she can\'t get dinner on the table until about 10 o\'clock. The difficult! of women trying to bring up children are compounded. They must hold jobs to help support their families. Their weekends are filled with shopping, laundry, and cleaning with primitive equipment. Exhausted as they are, their 3ob productivity cannot be high. 0. Reduce purported discrimination. My Latvian ground-level informants were all convinced that Latvians face discrimination on nationality grounds, that they are treated like "second-class citizens". They feel sure that the new labor recruits – "acrogant Russians" and "disgusting Asians" – are brought in to dc-Latvianizc their country, and that they get priority in housing. The belief that this is true causes widespread dissatifaction. It is a serious morale problem. 9. Reduce privilege. Privilege exists at several levels, and while some people, by merit and service, may deserve their privileged status, many, as David Shipley writes in his book (21), pbtain entry to a privileged class by bribery and other devious means. It is hard to say how much class privilege operates at factory level, but undoubtedly the knowledge that it exists, and that favors can be bought, badly affects morale, hence productivity. 10. Open up "the second economy". This area of economic activity, separate from regular employment, described at length by Gregory Grossman (22) and David Shipley (23), includes not only the legal production and sale of the produce of private plots and a few handcrafts and services, but a vast variety of transactions, semi-legal and illegal, through which many people gain extra income or obtain scarce commodities. But relatively few arc able to operate smartly in this field, and some, the elderly in particular, feel disgust at transacting shady deals. The second economy latches onto both factory and farm, and diverts attention, energy, creative thought, and ambition, if not always labor and materials, from regular production and productivity. The Outlook. From the above comments, it is clear that, rather than the personal incentives which can increase production in a labor-intensive economy, we have in Latvia a range of disincentives, and severe stresses on the family life which is so important in Latvian tradition. The high rate of divorce and the prevclencc of alcoholism arc merely symptoms. There is deep dissatisfaction with the system. Hie Soviet economy has reached a critical point and faces many obstacles to improvement. Yuri Andropov saw something of the gravity of the situation. In his speech to the CPSU Central Committee Plenary meeting last year, he urged several steps toward improvement, most of which I have mentioned (24). How the government policy is unclear. My view is that the system is clogged, and there will be little or no growth in the next few years. Reasonable steps toward restarting economic growth would involve first some slowing down and sacrifice, but many such steps would be politically unacceptable, Tor instance, investment to concentrate on labor-saving with less attention to huge prestigious projects, the evolution of a price and profit system, incentive pay and promotion on obvious merit, rationalization of distribution, and legalization of much of the second economy. Above all, the system needs a great deal of decentralization, leading in the direction of greater economic autonomy for Latvia. But what appears to be the present policy offers little hope. * This particular paper was given at the 9.AABS Conference, Social Sciences Session VIII. Representative_Docs: ['THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER POLITICAL EVENTS, PICKETS AND DEMONSTRATIONS In April Latvia has had its share of pickets, demonstrations and unstable relations with Russia. The mayor of the city of Moscow Yuri Luzhkow has recommended Russian businessmen not to buy any goods from Latvia. In spite of this fact, the ambassador of Russia in Latvia denied that any economic sanctions on Latvia will be imposed. The ambassador also admitted that the relations between Russia and Latvia have declined. The governor of Yaroslav had also asked the Russian businessmen and entrepreneurs to boycott the goods from Latvia as well as the service industry. Many pickets were held at the Embassy of Latvia in Russia and many well known Russian citizens had taken a part in those pickets. On April Bth, the President of Russia announced that the government of Russia has made a decision to impose economic sanctions on Latvia, however, a day later his Press Secretary announced that no sanctions will be imposed. The Prime Minister of Latvia Guntars Krasts had asked the Ministry of Economics to prepare a report about the economic damages Latvia will face if Russia decides to impose sanctions on Latvia.
