Latvijas Vēstnesis, April 17. 1998 (http://www.lv-laiks.lv/raksti/1998/106/d.htm)). What about fascism? There has not been a history ol broad public support for fascism in Latvia. In the late thirties, there was only a small group of followers of fascism. This group was outlawed, along with other parties, during the authoritarian regime of Kārlis Ulmanis. Some adherents to German fascism can be found during the German occupation, but Latvia was not fertile ground for any German ideology, given the fact that Germans historically were associated with Latvian serfdom and repression over several centuries. Those who were conscripted and fought in the Latvian Legion were largely patriotic and politically naive young men who thought they coul do something to save Latvia from Communism and the Soviet Union. Most had probably witnessed the deportation of family and friends during the Soviet Union\'s first occupation of Latvia in 1940, and vowed not to have that experience repeated. After the war, most spend a period in the gulag for their naivete. To suggest that today these men in their 70s and 80s wish to revive fascism seems farfetched (This is the view of Antoniņa Lebedeva writing in the Russkij Telegra/oi March 19, 1988, as quoted in Diena. March 21, 1998. Of course, as in many European countries and even North America, Naci propaganda can entice the uneducated and the unemployed into acts of terror and recklessness. Latvia is not likely to be any more immune from such individuals. An organization that takes its name from the pre-war fascist organization Pêrkonkrusts (Lightning Cross) has appeared, though its membership and objectives are unknown at this time.). This is not to deny that there were some Latvians who participated in the Holocaust, a fact that is not fully appreciated by Latvians. But then so did a variety of other nationals in other German occupied countries. Utvia is not the only country that must deal with the tragic legacy of Naci occupation, Russia has persisted to accuse Latvia, and Estonia, of gross human rights violations in its treatment of "Russian speakers". It is the only country to do so. The United Nations, Council of Europe, the Organization of Security and Cooperation in Europe, the U.S. State Department have all confirmed that no such violations take place (Hans-Dieter Lucas, "The Baltic States in Europe: Problems and Prospects". AussenpoliHk, No. 2. 1997). As in most countries in transition, there are weak laws and an inadequate understanding of civil and human rights principles and how to implement them. Yet, one only has to compare Russia\'s adherence to those standards to be disabused of the notion that Russia lias anything to teach Latvia about how to adhere to those standards. By comparison, Latvia\'s human rights record is admirable under existing Utvian conditions. Finally, there is question of terrosrism. While bombings have taken place in latvia, they have been usually associated with mafia structures seeking some sort of retribution for wrongs done by one group against the other. In most cases there have been even assassiations. However, these recent bombings reveal a different pattern. The damage to property has been relatively minor, particularly in the case of the bomb that went off near the Russian Embassy. The Embassy was not really damaged. More importantly, no seemingly politically motivated act of terrorism has resulted in a loss of life. If we compare the terrorist acts commited by the IRA or the Basques, we find that the pattern of violence of these ultra-nationalists is different. They seek to cause as much property damage as possible, and are quite prepared to accept and condone the death of innocent bystanders. The pattern of violence in Latvia is very different from that of what could be called typical ultra-nationalists. These appear to reflect a more conscientious and considertate view of property and human life, and appear to be atypical for ultra-nationalists It is interesting to note the comments of Emanuelis Zingeris, member of the Lithuanian Parliament, who stated that the Russian government\'s intelligence services are actively promoting ethnic conflict and social unrest in iMtvia and Lithuania in an atempt to destabilize them and make them look less attractive for integration in the West (RFE/RL News Release, Washington, D. C, April 2. 1998. "Lithuanian legislator bays Mocow Stokes Conflicts In Baltic-States". The editor of the Russian language newspaper in Riga Panorama Latvii surmised that the bombings were the work of Russian nationalist extremist elements such as the "Limonov faction or neo-boshviks or ultra-rightists." Ilelsingin Sonomat, May 10, 1998.). Ultimately, the question that needs to be asked is – whose interests do these incidents serve, Latvia\'s or Russia\'s? If Latvian citizens are purposely engaging in an attempt to create a bad public image for liitvia, then these individuals ar profoundly out of tune with Latvia\'s national interests. If they misguidedly believe they are patriots, then they are fooling themselves and are out of touch with reality. While it is difficult to be conclusive about these \'terrorist\' acts, they clearly are not sactioned by the liitvian government nor by the people of Latvia, Latvian or \'Russian speaking\'. Subsequent to these events, a flurry of activity in Latvia and abroad has occured to lessen the rupture in Latvian-Russian relations. President Clinton wrote a letter to President Ulmanis of latvia and President Yeltsin urging both sides to enter into a dialogue (Embassy of Utvia, Washington, D. C, Press Release. April 14, 1998), and Prime Minister Persson of Sweden went to Latvia to give it moral support. The ruling coalition in Latvia has agreed to the recommedations of the OSCE High Commissioner for National Minorities, Max van der Stoel to change the citizenship law that would allow a quicker naturalization of non-citizens and to give citizenship to children on nonecitizens born in Latvia since 1991 (Minitry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Latvia. Current Latvia, April 20-27, 1998 (http//www.mfa.gov.lv/mfa/pub/news/cur/CUR24s.html)). Even during his most recent visit in April, the Commissioner confirmed that he is not aware of any systematic discrimination (The interesting question in this context for the European community and the OSCE is why it is inappropriate for Latvia to deny citizenship to children born in I.atvia of non-citizens, but such a policy is seemingly permissible for Germany. It is interesting to note that Germany\'s ruling Christian Democrats have rejected a proposal to alter the country\'s citizenship law to allow children born in Germany of immigrant parents to gain citizenship. William Drozdiak, "Kohl Government Rejects Citizen Law Reform", Washington Pust, March 28, 1998.) Similar views were expressed by Ole Espersen, Commissioner for systematic discrimination. (Latvijas Vestnesis, April 17, 1998 (http://www. lv-laiks.lv/raksti/1998/106/d htm). The OSCE and NATO members have condemned the use of sanctions by Russia against Latvia and have tiffed diplomatic dialogue as the means to resolve the differences (Jan de Weydenthal, "Latvia: Russian Threats Promtßeactions,"RFE/RL,AprillO,l99B (http://www.rferl.org/ri ca/fealures/ 1998/04/F.RU. 980410131632.htm1). Yet, despite the best efforts of Latvia to bring the crisis to an end and to try to find a way to have a dialogue with Russia (Maris Riekstiņš, State Secretary in the Latvian Foreign Ministry went to Brussels to talk with Russia\'s Deputy Foreign Minister, Avdejev. The talks were inconclusive. Latvijas Vēstnesis, op.cit. It is interesting to note that Mr. Avdejev has taken a much harder position towards Latvia than Primakuv. Avdejev does not accept Latvia\'s conformity with \'international standards with respect to minorities as being enough to satisfy Russia\'. Edvins Iņkēns recounting his conversations with both, as reported in Latvijas Vēstnesis, April 17,1998 (http://www.lvlaiks.lv/raksti/1998/106/att.htm), evidence indicates that Russia is not prepared to enter into such a dialogue. Russia is clearly intent on bullying Latvia. Yevgeny Primakov. Russian Foreign Minister, told a meeting at the Moscow State Institute of International Relations that in regard to its relations with Latvia, Russia will "use its size and power to insist that Russia\'s smaller neighbours conform to its demands." He also invoked, what has now become an established Moscow doctrine in relation to its so-called near abroad, namely that Russia has a right to protect \'our people1 in foreign countries (Russia Today, "Bear is Back in Latvia Relations", April 23, 1998). Primakov\'s words were echoed by Yeltsin\'s spokesperson Sergei Yastrzembsky, who stated that liitvia\'s changes to its citizenship law in conformity with the cecommendations of the USCb> were not sufficent to alter Russia\'s plans to punish Latvia economically (Associated Press quoted in The Washington Post, April 23.1998). Thus, we return to the issue of whose interests do these incidents serve? An answer to this question leads to a consideration of Latvia-Russian relations since the restoration of Latvian independence in August of 1991. As one analyst has remarked: Since the restoration of Baltic intependence, even a Russia in transition to demoracy has given numeroues indications of a desire to impose her will on the democracy and ensure that it remains within her sphere of influence. (A. Thomas Lane, "The Baltic States, the Enlargement of NATO and Russia", Journal of Baltic Studies, Vol. XXVIII. no. 4 (Winter 1997)) At that time, some 40,000 Russian troops were still stationed there, and Russia controlled its early warning radar station at Skrunda, which it still wants to keep operational despite an international agreement that it should be disbanded in 1998, as well as a communications monitoring station near Ventspils. For Latvia to claim its independence and sovereignty, the troops had to leave as quickly as possible. Yet, despite the fact that this was the Kozyrev\'s watch at the Russian foreign ministry and the romantic\' period in Russia\'s relations with the West, a troop withdrawal treaty was not signed until August 1994. Only after the intervention of the U.S. and the Russian expectation that it would gain financially from the agreement persuaded Russia to sign. The ostensible reason for the delay was Russia\'s insistence that Russians and then expanded to include all Russian speakers\' be given automatic citizenship. Initially, Russia expected that the West would assist Russia in forcing such a policy on Latvia, but the West refused and allowed a sovereign state to decide its own laws, through various international and regional organizations sought to guide Latvia\'s citizenship policy. By this time, the Kozyrev honeymoon with the West was largely over and Russian policy toward I.arvia and the Baltics came to be viewed through Russia\'s policy toward the \'near abroad\', the newly independent states of the former Soviet Union (For an analysis of this period and the turn towards a hardline Russian foreign policy under Primakov see, Leszek Buszynski, "Russia and the West: Towards Renewed Geopolitical Rivalry?" Survival, vol. 37. mo. 3, 1995; James H. Brusstar, "Russian Vital Interests". Strategic Forum, Institute for National Strategic Studies, November 6, September 1994.). The outward expression of this policy was Russia\'s insistence at various international fora and in its press that Latvia, along with Estonia, was systematically abusing the human rights of Russian speaking inhabitants of Latvia. The number of different organizations with a human rights remit that have visited Latvia must have broken some sort of international record: the United Nations Human Rights Commission, The Council of Europe, the Organization of Security and Cooperation in Europe\'s National Minorities Commissioner, the Council of the Baltic Sea States" Human Rights Commissioner, not to mention the U.S Slate Department, as well as private organizations such as Helsinki Watch. All reported that there were M systematic or government supported forms of human rights violations (The deputy head of the OSCE mission in Latvia, Pirkka Tapiola was quoted in the Baltic Times, Nov. 28- Dec. 4,1996: "During the three years that the mission has been here, we have not seen any massive or systematic violation of human rights. Problems have been of an adninistrative nature characteristic of transition countries,"). In 1994, the OSCE had even accepted Utvia\'s citizenship law, though it was identified that some of the provisions could be too difficult to satisfy. Yet, despite such reports that the human rights of Russian speakers were not being violated, Russia has persisted in making such claims. Why? It cannot be because Russia has an exemplary record of observing human rights and would want others to emulate it.; its record is universally recognized as being abysmal. It cannot be because it fears that the some 700,000 Russian speakers in Latvia will all leave for Russia and create a burden for it. Surveys have shown that despite not having the rights of citizens, Russian speakers are quite satisfied to remain in  the prospect of returning to Russia under the present circumstances must indeed be sobering. Is it because Utvia wants to deny Russia its ports and other transport facilities in order to hinder Russian trade with western Europe. Latvian ports have consistently seen a growth in the volume of trade tliat passes through them (Lionel Barber in the Finacial Times, April 15, 1998 refers to an unpublished Finnish government study that makes reference to Russia\'s dependency on Baltic ports.). Latvia would hardly seek to voluntarily decrease its income from this lucrative service. The answer does not lie in any of these explanations. The answer lies elswhere. Over the course of 1997, Russia\'s Baltic policy has taken a more definite shape. It should be seen in the context of Russia\'s overall military and security policy which over the last several years has been increasingly articulated by nationalist and populist politicians and the military, and implemented by Yeltsin and Primakov. These policies are predicated on Russia reasserting its great power status, of seeking to reintegrate the space of the former Soviet Union. One can point to the Belarus-Russia agreement that stopped just short of merging the two countries, but which with the help of Lukashenko is clearly aimed at creating a security zone separating Russia lrom NATO. Though Russia has had less luck with Ukraine, there is every, however, indication that Russia will continue io put pressure on Ukraine to keep it from completely escaping Moscow\'s reach. Ukraine has sought for the moment to balance its relations between Russia and the West. It is both a member of the Commonwealth of Independent States and is signatory to the Ukraine-NATO Charter. In the Caucuses and Central Asia, Russia pursues reintegrationist policies that focuses on Russia\'s interests coming first (Ariel Cohen, "Russia Won\'t Be I,eft Out", The Washington Post, Sunday, March 8, 1998. Russian officials are explicit about their determination not to lose their sway over the region. Andranik Migranian, a leading Russian policy analyst and a member of the Russian Presidential Council, recently co-authered an article in a leading Moscow newspaper, explicitly calling for destabilization of the \'near abroad\', the Russian term for the countries on its border. The article called for the encouragement of ethnic conflict in Georgia and Azerbaijan."). In 1997, there were already clear signs that Russia had not given up on its objective of bringing the Baltics back into its fold, of creating a dividing line in Europe that separates the space of the former Soviet Union from the rest of Europe. While Moscow\'s efforts to reintegrate the space of the former Soviet Union have not been as succesful as it would like to be, it is the clear aim of Yeltsin and his foreign minister Primakov to wage a foreign policy that gives priority to such integration. This policy should be set against Russia\'s larger strategic goals, which have been described as folows: • Russia\'s main goal is to avert the establisment of a monopolar world, with America at its center; • Russia must facilitate the development of a multipolar world, one in which Russia is one of the major powers; • Russia will be able to maintain and promote its major power status only if it stregthens it\'s sphere of influence – the entire territory of the post-Soviet space (read – the former USSR) – and if it manages to limit the influence of other large countries, especially the United States, in this region; • A policy of integration in the former Soviet space is one way in which Russia can accomplish its mission; • The success of these integration efforts will play a major role in determining the extent to which Russia manages to maintain a more or less equal relationship with the United States (Aivars Stranga, "Baltic-Russian Relations: 1995 – Beginning of 1997", in Atis  and Zaneta Ozoliņa, eds., Small States in a Turbulent Environment The Baltic Perspective. Riga: Latvian Institute of International Affairs, 1997. For the role of Russian Foreign Minister Evgenii Primakov in advancing this strategy see, Ariel Cohen, "The "Pimakov Doctrine": Russia\'s Zero-Sum Game with the United States", FYI No. 167. Dec. 15, 1977, The Heritage Foundation.). How this policy would affect Russia\'s relations with the Baltic states was enunciated on February 12, 1997, when Russia\'s long-term Baltic policy was unveiled in Washington, D. C, by the Russian Embassy as Yeltsin\'s statement on the Subject (Kmbasy of the Russian Federation, Washington, D. C, Press Release, No. 6, February 12, 1997.). This statement, while proclainung that it was issued with the objective of creating a climate of \'goodneighbourliness\' with the Baltic states, in fact expresses Russia\'s determination not to allow the Baltics the freedom to decide their own security policy and to keep them within her sphere of influence. The statement opposes Baltic entry into NATO and wants that such action could be \'a serious barrier between these states and Russia\' and would have \'an extremely negative impact\' on long term cooperation in the region. But by staying outside any \'blocks (ie., remaining neutral) there could be \'very concrete\' steps taken that could dispel any fears that the Baltic s may have for their security. As we shall see, this would appear to an allusion to the security guarantees that Russia was to offer the Baltics later in the year. With respect to Russianspeaking residents of Estonia and Latvia, Russia expects that they all wil] be granted citizenship. In economic relations with the Baltics, Russia expresses frustration that the transit routes for shipment of Russian energy and non-ferrous metals have been used to the detriment of Russia. Though this accusation is not clarified, Russia expresses a veiled threat that Russia will diversify the channels of transit, presumably to the detriment of the Baltics. Moreover, it advocates strenthening Russian capital in the Baltics and creating favourable transport conditions for the Kaliningrad region. Russia rejects I Latvia\'s \'underground" territorial claims and Estonia\'s desire to withdraw the border issue from the development of bileteral relations and ties signing of border treaties with improvements in the situation of Estonia\'s Russian speaking population. Most astonishing, the document speaks of eliminating the criminal threats against Russia from the territory of the Baltic countries, forgetting the fact that by some estimates 50% of Russian businesses are tied to the Russian mafia. Finally, Russia promises to eliminate fears of Russian \'cultural imperialism\', by seeking to create closer (sic) cultural ties with the Baltic States. The document is unshamedly one-sided in its approach to Russian-Baltic relations. Russia provides a specific list of what the Baltics shoul do to improve relations, what concessions they should make, but Russia provides no concessions on its part. Russia expects the Baltics to compromise their sovereignty and independence, while Russia gains the ability to control Baltic security and even domestic policy. In short, it is a zero-sum game, where the Baltics loose and Russia gains. As Stephen Blank writes: ... Russian elites believe that for Russia to be Russia\' it must have exclusive and uncontested dominance over the CIS and accomanying sphere of influence over the Baltic States (Blank, op. cit., p. 7.) Yet, the Russian Ambassador to Latvia, Alexander Udalcov claimed that the document was the basis for the start of a new period in Russia\'s relations with the Baltics and that it should be interpreted \'positively\' and that it would not harm Baltic-Russian relations {Latvijas Vēstnesis, February 17.1997 (http://www.lv-laiks.lv/1997/049050/kr.htm) This hypocritical attitude underlies Russia\'s aim to "blackmail those states with permanent exclusion from the NATO and possibly the EU... unless they surrender vital aspects of their sovereignty regarding legislation and foreign policy (Blank, op. cit., p. 12.). □ /To be concluded in November issue of MLB Ziņotājs)']

Representative_Docs: ['CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER Populists Surge, Conservatives Purged In National Elections The 6th Saeima elections opened many people\'s eyes, not least in Latvia. Although opinion polls prior to the elections on September 30-October 1 predicted success for the soviet-era nomenklatura filled Democratic Party Saimnieks (DPS), no commentator predicted the rise of the populist Popular Movement Latvijai (Siegerist\'s Party) or the Unity Party of former Gorbachev protege Alberts Kauls. As significantly, no one foresaw the collapse of the coalitions formed by both the Farmers Union or the National Independence Movement of Latvia, which were so confident of victory that they agreed prior to the elections to form a governing coalition afterwards. The Saeima that will meet at high noon on November 7 will be much different than that which last gathered for a plenary session just two days before voting began. Voters Protest Pain, Choose Promises Despite gloating and post-election spin doctoring by the "victors" of the election – the DPS, TKL and LVP – the message sent by the Latvian voters is one of pain, protest, and naivete. To their credit, the Latvian voters took advantage of the available literature and ever-present radio, television November 1995 — Vol. XV, No. 9 Saeima Election Surprises and print ads to educate themselves on the platforms of the nineteen parties and coalitions standing for election. Unfortunately, Too many of the voters, in my honest opinion, took those campaign promises much too literally. Sickened by the pain of reform brought to them by the politicians of the sth Saeima, the voters who took part (neariy 73% of the electorate – a significant drop from the nearly 90% participation rate of the sth Saeima elections, but still enviable considering recent experience in the U.S.) generally rejected those parties they considered to be "to blame" for their agony – especially the LZS and LNNK, but also Latvia\'s Way (which saw its support at the polls drop by half and number of seats cut by more than 50%) and their coalition partners, the small Economists Political Association. Who did the Latvian voter support instead? A mixed bag of populists, each of which generally vied with each other in expansiveness of the promises they could make. The Democratic Party Saimnieks (translated as "Master" or "Steward", depending on the source) gathered to itself most of Latvia\'s late-model upwardly mobile Soviet nomenklatura, led by one-time Latvian Communist Youth leader, former Interior Minister Ziedonis Čevers. The 35year old Čevers parlayed his Interior Minister\'s portfolio, which during his tenure under Prime Minister Ivars Godmanis was rife with former Soviet security personnel, into a successful private personal and business security enterprise, the fund Drošiba. The DPS candidate list was filled with many "formers", to include Latvia\'s last Soviet-era PM, Alfreds Čepānis; Ilmārs BiŠers, who resigned in disgrace as a result of corruption charges in the Godmanis-era Finance Ministry; Ivars Ķezbers, a Soviet diplomat who was declared persona non grata in Sweden; and Dainis Turlais, who prior to becoming commander of the Latvian Army served as Soviet Army Gen. Pavel Grachev\'s second-in-command in Afghanistan. Prior to the elections, Čevers often claimed to have information about corruption within Latvia\'s Way, which he threatened to reveal if elected. He also claimed to have similar information about prominent Western Latvians, to include LNNK leader Aristids Lambergs and LC members Gunārs Meierovics, Olgerts Pavlovskis and Valdis Pavlovskis. To date, no evidence has been revealed, but the claims were used to back up Čevers\' assertion that only he and the DPS could "clean up government" and bring about instant prosperity. Čevers has also taken to claiming that he will rule in Latvia "for the next ten years". Balm to a voting populace which grew up during the stable and predictable times of Brezhnev and Khrushchev, but not a reflection of democratic reality or the power of the ballot. The Popular Movement Latvijai, led by German-Latvian Joachim Siegerist. Siegerist, is built on the anti-Communist, pro-Latvia posturing of its leader. Siegerist, a failed radical conservative politician in Germany, came to Latvia and built up a following among the poor and dispossessed, to whom he offered humanitarian aid in exchange for loyalty. During the sth Saeima elections Siegerist bankrolled the LNNK party\'s campaign, and was himself elected to serve as a deputy. His radical politics and lack of Latvian language were his undoing, as Siegerist was kicked out of the LNNK and later the Saeima. In forming the TKL, Siegerist gave his loyal followers ā means of protesting the pain of transition by supporting him and his lieutenants. Often playing loose with the truth, Siegerist was able to claim that he alone cared about the oppressed ethnic Latvians suffering from the burdens of "communists and their useful idiots". The success of TKL was, perhaps, the most surprising to political analysts in Latvia and abroad, for his rhetoric and tactics had made him seem nothing more than an itinerant carpetbagger. Since the elections, Siegerist has brought his party of anti-Communists into the fold of the DPS leftist alliance, which may well, after November 7, be elected (with the help of the largely unreformed former communists of the socialist Party) to govern in Latvia. He echoes Čevers\' conviction that he will rule for the next decade. Prior to Alberts Kauls\' involvement, the Unity Party of Latvia stood no chance of gaining seats in the Saeima. With Kauls\' support, and that of his wide universe of political and financial contacts, the Unity Party became a major player in Latvian politics. As Siegerist targeted the LNNK as insufficiently nationalist, Kauls, a former chairman of the famed Ādaži collective farm, targeted the Farmers Union as uncaring of the plight of the farmer. Promising prosperity "like we had at Ādaži, Tērvete, Druva" (all Soviet-era collective farms), Kauls guaranteed to "feed and clothe the nation" if his party won and he became Prime Minister. What\'s Next? In the final analysis, no one party will be able to lead decisively based on the results of the 6th Saeima elections. Two evenly matched blocs of parties have developed, each of which will put forward a proposed cabinet of ministers and government program. President Guntis Ulmanis will then charge the leader of one of the blocs (either Čevers or For Fatherland and Freedom leader Māris Grinblats) to assemble their cabinet and secure Saeima confidence in their choices. The National Bloc, led by Grinblats and his surprisingly powerful party, also includes the LNNK and LZS coalitions. Latvia\'s Way has pledged to support a National Bloc government, and will sign a formal agreement with the bloc if it is given the opportunity to form a government. The bloc, together with Latvia\'s Way, can count on 47 votes out of 100; for the National Bloc to secure Saeima approval, they will have to find several defectors from the leftist coalition parties. This process has already begun, with TKL deputy Guntis Eninš declaring his intention to serve as an independent in the Saeima due to his disgust with Siegerist\'s choice to join with Čevers. The National Reconciliation Coalition, led by čevers and the DPS, also includes Kauls\' Unity Party, the Popular Harmony Party of former Foreign Minister Jānis Jurkāns, and Siegerist\'s TKL. This coalition has already signed a cooperation agreement long on promises and opportunities to spend money, but short on concrete plans and sources of national income. The NRC currently also has 47 votes, but may be in a better position to win approval for their government depending on the position taken by the Socialist Party (5 seats). On October 20, the NRC published the names of its its proposed cabinet of ministers: Prime Minister Ziedonis Čevers (DPS) Agriculture Roberts Dilba (LVP) Culture (Raimonds Pauls?) Defense Odisejs Kostanda (TKL) Economics Joahim Siegerist (TKL) Education and Science Modris Plāte (TKL) Environment and Regional Development Maris Ikaunieks Finance Aivars Kreituss (DPS) Foreign Affairs Jānis Jurkāns (TSP) Interior Jānis Ādamsons (LC) Justice Juris Kaksītis (DPS) Transportation Ēriks Kaža (DPS) Welfare Andris Saulītis (TKL) Ādamsons has already expressed to Latvia\'s Way leaders the his surprise at being included by čevers in this proposed government, although he has consistently maintained since the elections that he would abide by his party\'s decision on the matter. Lastly – it is worth noting that a "three strikes" rule does exist for forming a government in the Republic of Latvia. If President Ulmanis proposes three Prime Ministers in succession who fail to gain Saeima support for their governments, the President must dissolve the Saeima and call new elections. Stay tuned. #Martioš Zvaners Elected to the 6th Saeima (in order of preference, from the Riga, Vidzeme, Latgale, Kurzeme and Zemgale voting districts except where noted): From the Democratic party Saimnieks (18 seats): Ilga Kreituse, Valdis Krisbergs, Juris Zaķis, Ivars Ķezbers; Ilmārs Bišers, Aivars Kreituss, Dainis Turlais, Alfrēds čepānis, Ziedonis Čevers; Ernests Jurkāns, Ēriks Kaža, Leonards Teniss; Juris Kaksitis, Valdis Nagobads, Atis Sausnitis; Juris Celmiņš, Modris Goba, Jānis Rāzna. DPS received 144,758 votes (15,1% of votes cast). From Latvia\'s Way (17 seats): Indulis Bērziņi, Imants Daudiss, Aivars Endziņš, Andrejs Panteļējevs; Valdis Birkavs, Māris Gailis, Anatolijs Gorbunovs, Jānis Ādamsons; Pēteris Apinis, Vilis Krištopans, Antons Seiksts; Jānis Lagzdiņš, Kristiāna Libane, Dzintars Ābiķis; Romāns Apsitis, Jānis Bunkšs, Andris Tomasuns. LC received 139,929 votes (14,6%). From the Popular movement Latvijai (l6seats) Andris Rubins, Māris Rudzītis, Elmārs Zelgalvis; Oļģerts Dunkers, Ojārs Grinbergs, Odisejs Kostanda, Andris Saulitis; Jānis Strods, Kārlis Cerāns; Gunta Gannusa, Edmunds Grinbergs, Jānis Kazāks; Guntis Eniņs, Janina Kusnere, Jānis Mauliņs, Modris Plāte. Siegerist\'s party received 142,324 votes (14,9%). From For Fatherland and Freedom (14 seats) Māris Grinblats, Leopolds Ozoliņš, Normunds Pēterkops, Aleksandrs Petersons, Juris Sinka; Oskars Grigs, Guntars Grinblats, Jānis Straume, Juris VidiņS; Roberts Jurdžs; Aigars Jirgens, Roberts Zile; Vents Balodis, Andrejs Požarnovs. TB received 114,050 votes (11,9%). From the Farmers Union coalition (8 seats) Paulis Kļaviņš (KDS), Kārlis Jūlijs Druva (LZS); Aida Prēdele (KDS), Pauls Putniņš (LZS); Andrejs Naglis (KDS), Anta Rugāte (KDS>, Māris Vitols (KDS); Ervids Grinovskis (LZS). The coalition – made up of the LZS, Christian Democratic Union (KDS) and Democratic Party of Latgale (LDP) – received 60,498 votes (6,3%). From the Unity Party (8 seats) Gundars Valdmanis; Raitis Apalups, Roberts Dilba, Viesturs Gredzens; Viktors Kalnbērzs, Anatolijs Tučs; Edgars Bāns; Jānis Rubulis. The LVP received 68,305 vēlētāji (7,1%). From the Popular Harmony Party (6 seats in Riga, Vidzeme and Latgale) Vladilens Dozorcevs, Jānis Jurkāns, Ludmila Kuprijanova; Andris Ameriks; Leonards StaSs, Jānis Urbanovičs. TSP received 53,041 votes (5,55%). From the Socialist Party of Latvia (5 seats in Riga and Latgale) Oļegs Deņisovs, Modris Lujāns; Aleksandrs Bartaševičs, Martijans Bekasovs, Aleksandrs Golubovs. The socialists received 53,325 votes (5,58%)', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER Latvia Gets Best Christmas Gift Of All: A Stable Government The 100 deputies of Latvia\'s parliament gave their country the best Christmas present it could hope to receive on December 21, when they voted overwhelmingly (70 yea, 24 nay) to approve a "grand coalition" government led by successful Latvian businessman and political neophyte Andris Šķēle. A spectacularly unexpected breakthrough by most of the parties competing for influence in the Saeima allowed Latvia to end nearly three months of political paralysis and avoid the very real threat of parliamentary stalemate and new elections. The creation of a "grand coalition" of six moderate parties of both the right and left was made possible by the failure of both camps to secure approval for their proposed governments, as well as by President Ulmanis\' consistent and, to this observer, principled and proper rejection of radical extremist Joachims Zīgerists. Čevers Tries, Fails Following the failure of Tēvzemei un brīvībai leader Māris Grinblats to form a center-right cabinet consisting of ministers from the National Bloc (TB, the LNNK/LZP faction and the LZS/KDS/LDP faction) and Latvia\'s Way, President Ulmanis took six days to nominate Ziedonis Čevers, leader of the Democratic Party Saimnieks, as his next choice to be Prime Minister. Much of the delay, no doubt, can be attributed to Ulmanis\' wariness of offering the cabinet to any party bloc which could potentially include Zīgerists. Ulmanis stated in nominating Čevers on November 29 that the existence of two solid and equally strong blocs in the Saiema drove his selection-in interviews both before and after the fall of the Grinblats\' cabinet, more than ten potential candidates had rejected Ulmanis\' offer to become Prime Minister because they felt they could not gain support in the Saeima. One day earlier, both Andrejs Panteļējevs of Latvia\'s Way and Aleksandrs Kiršteins of LNNK said that they felt such a "third party" candidacy for the Prime Minister\'s seat could become a reality only after a failure by Čevers and his center-left bloc to set up a cabinet. In spite of statements that he would unveil a cabinet "within hours" of selection, Prime Minister-designate Čevers was forced by dissention within his bloc to revisit his proposed cabinet of ministers and government platform (see CLN 11/95). Both Popular Harmony Party (TSP) leader Jānis Jurkāns and members of the Latvian Unity Party (LVP) faction expressed their wish for an expanded cabinet which could be supported by more than the four parties of the Čevers bloc and the five members of the unreformed-communist Latvian Socialist Party. Jurkāns also reiterated his opposition to the inclusion of Zīgerists in any cabinet. To Čevers\' dismay, he was unable to secure outside support, and as a result also failed to become Prime Minister, In fact, Čevers was abandoned during the vote on his cabinet on December 7 by two members of the LVP. Although Čevers received a plurality of the votes cast (50 yea, 45 nay, 5 abstained), Saeima rules require a simple majority (50% + 1 of deputies voting) for approval of a Prime Minister. Because of the tie vote, a second ballot was held, with no change in the result. Predictably, Čevers was supported only by his party bloc and by the Socialists, and opposed by the opposing party bloc and Latvia\'s Way. However, the opposition was joined by LVP deputy Viesturs Gredzens, who voted no. and by LVP deputy Viktors Kalnbērzs, who abstained-thus guaranteeing Čevers\' fall. "Who Is Andris Šķēle?" After the collapse of the Čevers candidacy, discussions began in earnest about the creation of a "grand January 1996 — Vol. XVI, No. 1 coalition" government, which would cut across the two opposing party blocs and unite as many factions as possible in support of a "neutral" Prime Minister. Although the first new names to be mentioned as candidates included former Prime Minister Māris Gailis and former Saeima Chairman Anatolijs Gorbunovs (both LQ, the rival Tēvzemei un Brīvībai and Democratic Party Saimnieks factions soon reached agreement on an alternative candidate, whose best quality would be that he was not a member of Latvia\'s Way-prominent businessman and presidential advisor Andris Šķēle. As Šķēles name was heard more often as a potential Prime Minister, questions arose about his background and past activities. Bom in 1958 in Ape, near the Latvian-Estonian border in northeastern Latvia, Andris Šķēle graduated from the Latvian Agricultural Academy in Jelgava in 1981. After college, Šķēle worked for the "Stars" experimental agricultural facility until 1990, when he joined the Agricultural Ministry as a Deputy Minister in the government of Ivars Godmanis. After the fall of his superior Dainis Ģēģers in May 1993, Šķēle served as acting Agricultural Minister until September 1993. Šķēle was acting director of the Latvian Privatization Agency, and served as an economic advisor to President Ulmanis. Just weeks before his selection as a Prime Minister candidate, Šķēle had been elected chairman of the board of the huge state joint stock company Latvijas kuģniecība (Latvian Shipping). Šķēle is best known in Latvia as an extremely successful businessman, who has created a food services empire which has been estimated to encompass one half of all privatized food services and processing companies in Latvia. Although Šķēle personally claims to hold a controlling interest in only one company (46% of the bread company Baitmaiznieks), through his financial-production company Aye Lat, Šķēle made use of the privatization process to become a major investor in 15 different milk, egg, bread and meat processing facilities, as well as the champagne producer Rīgas vini, confectionery manufacturers Laima and Uzvara, and many others. In spite of intense scrutiny, none of Šķēles business activities has been found to be at all illegal. In fact, šķēle is widely considered to be an exceptionally competent and hard-working manager. These qualities must have swayed both TB and DPS, as these two parties both recommended Šķēle as a compromise candidate to President Ulmanis, who announced that Šķēle would be his candidate on December 14, during a stopover in Frankfurt, Germany while traveling to Germany and Spain for the EU summit. New Government Large, But Stable The new coalition government approved by the Saeima is the largest of any in the short history of modem Latvian parliamentary politics. Created to reflect the balance of power in the Saeima, each of the parties which joined the coalition (TB, DPS, LC, the IJWK/LZP faction, LVP and the LZS/KDS/LDP faction) was given cabinet portfolios commensurate to their strength in parliament. A party board will be created to help secure consensus on all issues before the government, and should any minister leave, their position will be filled by a candidate from the previous minister\'s party. Political responsibility for the actions of their ministers has also been taken by each of the parties represented. Latvia\'s previous governments were headed by persons representing the majority party, which then also took responsibility for the actions of the government. Šķēle is a political outsider, although during the 6th Saiema elections he and his business associates supported all parties except for the unreformed Communists of the Latvian Socialist Party and the Popular Movement for Latvia, headed by radical populist Joachims Zīgerists. The government platform agreed to by the coalition parties is an amalgamation of the plat-forms offered by both Grinblats and Čevers during their failed attempts to form a cabinet. It emphasizes economic liberalism while addressing social issues as well, and reflects Šķēles personal priority of developing a strong market economy and securing a positive investment climate for both foreign and domestic businessmen. As a reflection of the difficulty of forming a cabinet from so many parties, the current law regulating the structure of the government will be amended within the next few weeks to allow for more persons to be designated as Deputy Prime Ministers. In time, Māris Grinblats will step aside as Education and Science Minister and maintain only his Deputy PM portfolio. Grinblats will be replaced by current State Minister and former education and Science Minister Jānis Gaigals (LC). NATO-grade radar in Latvia put into active mode After several months of operation in the test mode, the NATO-grade 3-D radar TPS-117 installed in the Audriņi airstrip in eastern Latvia, was switched In active mode on 4 March 2004. Latvia has completed its homework to qualify for acquisition of EU funding With both framework documents, the Development Plan and the Program Amendment ?now drafted and harmonised with the EU structural fund management institutions in Latvia and the European Commission, Latvia has completed the prerequisite of the European Union for mastering the EU funds. Amendments to the Law on National Border of Latvia adopted With joining the northern Atlantic Treaty on 2 April 2004, Latvia not only acquires security guarantees provided by the NATO collective defence and security system, but also commits itself to provide assistance to its allies in case of military threat. The Paragraph 5 of the North Atlantic Treaty will be fully applicable to Latvia, stating that any attack on one or more allied countries is considered to be an attack on all allied countries. Due to this, the current allied countries committed themselves to provision of assistance and taking of steps they may consider necessary (including application of military force) to restore and maintain security in the Northern Atlantic region. Latvia has to provide the necessary conditions and amend its legislation to allow for reception of NATO assistance as well as to be able to support its partners. One of the fundamental elements of modern and effective defence system is control and defence of airspace. As Latvia joins the NATO, its airspace becomes a part of the common NATO airspace calling for patrol and surveillance measures. Latvia has yet to put in order its legal system and align its legislation to NATO requirements, so that the country is able to patrol Its own airspace as required by the NATO airspace defence policy beginning with 2 April 2004. The draft law provides for integration of NATO airspace defence guidelines with Latvian normative acts, so that Latvia can join the common NATO airspace defence policy. The draft law also provides for right of the North Atlantic Treaty Organisation to carry out control and defence of Latvian airspace to secure the sovereignty and inviolability of Latvian skies. One-third of Latvia\'s residents see national security guarantee in EU, NATO membership One-third of Latvia\'s population believes that integration in NATO and the EU is the most efficient way to counter threats to national security while one-fourth said improving Latvian-Russian relations was vital, according to the poll commissioned by the Ministry of Defence. The survey of the public opinion on national defence issues carried out by the Data Serviss Company last year showed strengthening of the public opinion about both NATO and the EU being an important and integral part of the national security. One-fourth (24.4 percent) is looking to the East for the sense of security, believing that improved relations with Russia will be good for Latvia\'s defence. Latvia honours victims of the Communist terror of 1949 On 25 March 2004, hundreds of people, Latvia\'s highest officials, including the President Vaira Vike-Freiberga, and diplomatic corps, were present at the procession from the Occupation Museum to the Freedom Monument in honour of the victims of Communist terror. Following the hearing of the national anthem, people laid flowers at the foot of the Monument. From March 25 to March 28,1949, rail coaches deported the accused "kulaks, bandits, nationalists and enemies of the state" from Latvia to forced labour camps in Amur, Omsk and Tomsk regions in Siberia. The total number of the deported was over 43,000, including 10,000 children and youth up to the age of 16. "This is a day of sorrow in Latvia as we remember the distant misty spring morning 55 years ago, when the order issued earlier in January by the Cabinet of Minister of the Soviet Union was finally carried out The order ruled to deport a certain number of civilians from Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia. It was justified as a means to assist collectivisation and establishment of collective farms, strengthening the revolution and extermination of individuāls hostile to the regime. [.] Basically, that was an act of terror to intimidate the three countries and their people occupied and annexed in 1940; to make them understand that their independence has vanished and their rights are lost," Vike-Freiberga said in her address to people gathered at the Monument Later on, with the President\'s speech and a report on the consequences of the Soviet repressive system in Latvia by Indulis Zalite, Director of the Centre of Documentation of Totalitarianism Consequences, the tenth volume of the Research papers of the Commission of the Historians of Latvia "Occupation regimes in Latvia in 1940-1959" was opened in the Riga Castle. The opening of the book was attended by foreign ambassadors to Latvia, MPs, researchers, museum and archive staff and the Association of History Teachers. The 15 articles published in the 10th volume introduce to the policy pursued by Soviet and Nazi occupation regimes and its consequences in Latvia. In this volume, researchers have addressed the repressive Soviet power in Latvia, change of occupation powers in 1941, .counteractivities of national guerrillas to the Soviet power, public opinion of those days, forming of police battalions during the Nazi occupation and many other issues. Some articles are dedicated to historiography analysis. "[..] I have started out by expressing my heartfelt thanks to the Historians\' Committee – this gathering of international specialists who for the last five years have laboured to present, to research the most painful pages of Latvia\'s history. Today we will be receiving the 10th volume of the very important series, which attests to the breadth and depth of the research that has been carried out during the last five years. Today is a particularly significant day on which to present this tenth volume, which presents research about the period of both occupations of Latvia – the Nazi and the Soviet one during the whole period of most severe repression, the period between 1940 and 1959. There is a variety of topics that are touched upon in this volume. It touches on the period of the Holocaust, that period is so important, there is so much to still do about It that the Commission will have a separate volume on it that will be presented later this year. The present volume you s! cc today, presents an overview and it is presented on this significant day ? March 25, which was the date of the last mass deportations from Latvia. [..] On that tragic day, over 43 thousand people were deported on that one date. Families were separated or taken all together to disappear later in the Siberian tundras. This was a crime against humanity, this was a genocide against the Latvian people, this was a tragedy that has touched so many Latvian families in whose memory and in whose lives the sequels of it remain alive to this day [..]," the President said at the opening of the book, "Latvia Returns to the Free World" – an exhibition opening at United Nations headquarters in New York The travelling exhibition of the Museum of the Occupation of Latvia, Latvia Returns to the Free World, was on view at the United Nations headquarters on 25 March 2004. The official opening was followed by a reception to which ambassadors of all the countries represented at the UN were invited. The exhibition shows Latvia\'s history in the 20th century. In 1918, Latvia declared its Independence and strengthened it in a short period. Latvia\'s freedom was interrupted by the secret Hitler-Stalin pact The exhibition shows the tragedy of occupation by two totalitarian regimes: first the Soviet Union (194071941), then Nazi Germany, (194171945) and then again the Soviet Union (1945? 1991). Latvia lost its independence; its economy, culture and society became subject to sovietisation and russification. For half a century Latvia had disappeared from the world map; however, the Latvian nation never accepted this circumstance and resisted the occupation in various ways until regaining its independence in 1991. After continuous falsification during occupation years, attempts are being made to research and understand this complicated historical period. Involved in the investigative process are both those who themselves experienced the occupationand those who viewed it from the outside. IJ 19 OAI ilk/li-tW! Representative_Docs: ["Latvia's leading freedom fighter Gunārs Astra, dies at the age of 56. Gunārs Astra, one of Latvia's most outspoken and respected nationalists, a man who spent 19 years of his life in Soviet prison camps and was looked upon by Soviet authorities as the Sakharov of Latvia, is dead at the age of 56. Astra died on the morning of April 6, several days after undergoing a 7-hour heart operation in a Leningrad hospital. While all the facts surrounding Astra's sudden illness and subsequent death are not clear, this iuch is known: Although he still had two years to go on his 7-year prison sentence, Astra was released from prison and returned to his home in RTga, Latvia in February of 1988. He was one of the last of Latvia's well known political prisoners to be released as part of Gorbachev's recent amnesty policy. In the beginning of March, Astra and his wife Līvija took a train from Riga to Leningrad, in order to visit friends there. Astra became sick en route, and was brought by his wife to a hospital in Leningrad. A great deal of confusion surrounded the diagnosis of his condition and treatment. He was variously diagnosed as suffering from Dneumonla. endocarditis and finally blood poisoning News of Astra's sudden illness reached the West immediately. The World Federation of Free Latvians made arrangements through Lufthansa and the West German government to supply Astra with broad spectrum antibiotics not Gunārs Astra igļi -1988 available in Leningrad. Sen. Paul Siaon (D-IL). Sen. Don Rlegle (D-MI). Cong. Steny Hoyer (D-MI). and Cong. Edward Pelghan (D-OH) all sent telegrams to Soviet authorities at the hospital, urging them to make every effort to bring Astra back to health. At his own request. Astra was moved from the Botkin hospital in Leningrad, to a military academy medical center, considered to be the best medical facility in the city. Physicians there apparently determined that center, considered to be the best medical facility in the city. Physicians there apparently determined that Astra's heart had been seriously damaged by the blood poisoning and recommended open heart surgery. On March 25. as thousands of Latvians were gathering in Riga to honor the victims of the 1949 deportations, Astra underwent a 7-hour heart operation, where two of his damaged valves were removed and replaced with artificial ones. His chance of full recovery was listed at 50-50. Astra's condition remained stable for the next few days His wife Līvija was at his side through much of the recovery period. At approximately 4AM. while sleeping soundly, he began to have trouble with his breathing. Although he received immediate medical assistance, he died at 6AN. An autopsy, performed by Soviet authorities a day later, attributed his death to pulmonary embolisms (blood clots in the lungs.) According to Livija Astra, one of her husband's last requests was that a foreign (non Soviet) pathologist be allowed to participate In his autopsy. Livija Astra conveyed this request to the Soviets and friends In the West. At the invitation of the World Federation of Free Latvians. Dr. Jdrgen Thomsen, from the Danish University Institute of Forensic Medicine agreed to make the trip to Leningrad on April 14. Although Soviet authorities never denied Dr. Thomsen permission to perform the autopsy, he was unable to get a visa to the USSR. While there is no hard evidence that foul play was involved in Astra's death, many questions have nevertheless been raised. Astra was not known to have heart problems while in prison, and had not been ill during the 2 months after he had been released from prison. According to former Latvian political prisoner and Astra family friend. Gunārs Rode, now living in Stockholm. Astra had been called in for questioning by the KGB several hours prior to his departure for Lenigrad. During the interrogation, Astra reportedly drank mineral water given to him by his interrogators. According to Rode's sources in Latvia, Astra first started feeling ill several hours later on the train Diplomatic representatives of the U.S. Consul General in Leningrad had contacted Mrs. Astra after her husband's death, and reportedly informed Soviet authorities of her desire to have a Western pathologist review the autopsy findings. The Latvian human rights group HELSINKI 86 has announced plans for a public funeral for Astra when his remains are returned to Riga. A tentative date of April 19 had been proposed.", '" I am a Latvian" by Ojārs Kalniņš Gunārs Astra was not a dissident. The dictionary indicates that a dissident is someone who disagrees with the opinions of a group to which he belongs. He was not a Soviet dissident, because he did not belong to the Soviets. He lived, and died, a Latvian. Nor was he a Latvian dissident, because his views were shared by the majority of the Latvian people. Gunārs Astra was many things a former political prisoner, a freedom fighter, a human rights activist, a student of language and philosophy, a father and husband – but above all, as he stated at his own trial in 1983, "I am a Latvian." He was highly respected, not only by Latvians, but by all those who knew of him in the human rights movement. He was a man fought for his beliefs, and stood up defiantly to his nation\'s oppressors. His wife once said that even if he were released from prison, he would not remain free very long, because he would continue to speak out about the same Issues that originally landed him in the Gulag. He would make no deals, sign no agreements, recant no statements.his wife said.  who fight for national and human rights. Gunārs Astra was schooled, trained and educated in the Soviet prison camps. He was born in Latvia on October 22, 1931 and as a youth worked at the VEF electronics factory in Riga. While working he enrolled in After completing his term, Astra returned to Riga in 1976 where he grew increasingly interested in Latvian history, culture, traditions and literature. While working at a factory, he spent his free time studying English and philosophy. He also began collecting Western books and magazines, as well as material published during Latvia\'s independence. Astra was incensed over what he saw as a deliberate Soviet Russian attempt to destroy the Latvian language and history. In 1983, during a major KGB crackdown on Latvian religious and national rights activists, Astra was again arrested and sentenced to 7 years imprisonment and 5 years exile. His "crimes" included possession of forbidden Western books (George Orwell\'s "1984" for example), photocopying historical monographs, and distribution of illegal Latvian books and materials. Astra was also accused of having translated the text of the secret protocol of the 1939 Stalin Hitler Pact. At his trial, Astra made a dramatic final statement to the Soviet court, setting forth his personal views on Soviet rule in Latvia, Russlfication and censorship. He condemned the "deliberate abuse of the Latvian language", stating that "It hurts and humiliates me to see my native language slowly pushed out of the way." In a passage that seemed to predict the nationalities unrest which presently besets the USSR, Astra denounced "Russian communist expansion and domination" in such non-Russian countries as Latvia. Moldavia, Bielorusia and Ukraine, and cited Napoleon\'s observation that "All imperialisms fall due to disorders in their digestive systems." "It is love and dignity for my people that has brought me to the defendant\'s dock, as I oppose the brutal acts against my people and the demoralization of my nation\'s soul, " Astra told the court. As fate would have it, Astra was imprisoned in 1983 for activities which have become tolerated, if not legal, in Gorbachev\'s USSR. In his final statement, he made an eloquent defense of freedom of speech and information: correspondence courses at the Latvian State University where he learned English and German. In time, desiring to practice his newly learned English, he befriended some Amerians he had met at a trade exhibition In Moscow. Because he continued contacts with these Americans, in 1961 he was arrested and tried for passing information about his job to foreigners. He was sentenced to 15 years in a strict regime labor camp. During his time In prison, he befriended many political prisoners of different nationalities, all of whom had been falsely charged just as he had. He began to develop contacts in the human rights movement, and participated in protests against the mistreatment of prisoners. "Nothing can Justify our being kept in the dark. People, being intelligent life forms, need to continually receive information about the real world – the more widespread and varied, the better. It is a well established fact that the opportunity to air opposing viewpoints facilitates choosing the best alternative in any given situation. A lack of information leads to the eventual inability to make rational judgements and promotes mental atrophy. A person who is incapable of rational thought becomes less than human He becomes a puppet and a slave, to be manipulated by the state." While such a statement was viewed as subversive in 1983, today it could have just as easily come from the mouth of Gorbachev and his fellow proponents of \'glasnost\'. At his trial, Astra was accused by the prosecutor as being Andrei  "comrade in thought." Back in 1983, Sakharov was still in exile and in disfavor with the regime. Today, Sakharov is free and speaking openly to the Western press about human rights, freedom of the press and democracy. Gunārs Astra, on the other hand, is dead. While Astra was deeply hurt by the suffering of his people and nation, he never gave up hope, and ended his 1983 court statement with a defiant optimism: "I fervently believe that these nightmarish times will end some day. This belief gives me the strength to stand before you now. Our people have suffered a great deal and have learned how to survive. They will outlive this dark period of their history." I join Gunārs Astra in this fervent belief. He was a living symbol of Latvian courage in the face of suffering, and although he did not survive his tormentors, his memory, his conviction, his fiery patriotism and love of Latvia will live on. Gunārs Astra was a hero. The Soviets turned him into a martyr. The Latvian people will not forget that. Ever.', "THE CASE OF JURĢIS SKULME (father: Uga), born in Riga, 13 November 1928, a Latvian of higher education, non-party member, divorced, previously unpunished, who worked in the laboratory of the Art History and Theory Section of the Latvian SSR State Art Academy as Senior methodologist, living in Riga, Ozolkalnu ielā Nr. 10, charged according to para. 183.-I of the Latvian SSR Criminal Code. During court proceedings the Criminal Case Court of the Latvian SSR Supreme Court found that Jurģis Skulme, working in the Laboratory of the Latvian SSR Mate Art Academy as Senior methodologist, in March 1977, at his residence in Riga, Ozolkalnu ielā No. 10, by his own hand prepared and wrote a manuscript for distribution abroad with wilfully false fabri cations which discredit the Soviet State and social order, and afterwards, during the same year 1977, in March, this manuscript was distributed abroad in written form through a person, whose identity could not be established by the investigatory proceedings, who delivered it to a (female) Latvian emigrant in Sweden, now a foreigner, Izabella Genmarker, in connection with her previously expressed desire to collect information about life in Soviet Latvia, which was to be sent to an address in Sweden given by her through an intermediary designated by her. J. Skulme went along with I. Genmarker's offer and wrote in his manuskript wilfully false fabrications discrediting the Soviet State and social order, which he had collected from persons unidentified by the investigatory proceedings and from anacļuaintance, Adams Osiņš. In his efforts to disinform the recipient of the manuscript about the true political conditions in Latvia, J. Skulme wrote wilfully false fabrications in his prepared manuscript which was distributed in written form in Sweden, falsely claiming as ii Latvia's old underground fighters-communists are expressing interest in knowing why the emigrant organisation «The Association of Baltic States» does not try to gain the same observer status in the United Nations as that of the Palestinian Liberation Organization. In the same manuscript what was distributed in written form abroad, J. Skulme deliberately and falsely claimed that worker unrest had occurred in the Daugavpils' Synthetic Fiber Factory and in the Daugavpils' Locomotive _Repair Works at the end of 1976 and that strikes had occurred in Liepāja where the strikers are to be summoned before a court. In his desire to portray life under Soviet power in dark colors, J. Skulme falsely claimed in the manuscript that the wheels of a railroad train in Lithuania had been welded to the rails, in result causing destruction of the railroad track, and that the Popov Radio Factory had been assigned to manufacture before the end of the year 56 «Symphony-Stereo» radiola sets with built-in listening devices. In his manuscript J. Skulme also wrote cynical, defamatory and scornful fabrications about the USSR Communist Party, Soviet State and Latvian CP leaders. The written manuscript prepared by J. Skulme, distributed abroad, and containing discrediting fabrications about the Soviet State and social order, was sent back to Latvia on April 11, 1977 from Sweden, addressed to the editorial office of the newspaper «Dzimtenes Balss» (Voice of the Homeland). The accused Skulme confesses his guilt of this incriminating crime and testifies that in March 1977, fulfilling the wishes of his childhood friend Izabella Genmarker, now residing in Sweden, to learn more about life in Soviet Latvia, wrote her a letter about various social questions in Latvia, which he, according to I. Genmarker's instructions, sent to Sweden though an envoy previously designated by her. Furthermore, J. Skulme testifies that in this letter he wrote down statements and utterances he had heard from various persons, that old Communistsunderground fighters in Latvia want to know why the «Association of Baltic States» does not try to gain observer status in the United Nations, similar to that of the Palestinian Liberation Organisation; that workers'_ unrest occurred in Daugavpils, and that there were strikers in Liepāja; that the Popov Radio Factory has to manufacture 56 «Symphony-Stereo» radiola sets with built-in listening devices; – because he found them interesting events though he personally did not know if this information was in fact true. He also wrote in the letter defamatory and scornful utterances about the USSR CP, the Soviet State and Latvian CP leaders, because he conseidered these anecdotes as folklore. The accused Skulme's testimony – that he has written a letter with there fabrications and has sent it to Sweden – is attested to with physical proofs: a letter of six pages, envelopes with Swedish postal stamps and information from trie editorial office of the newspaper «Dzimtenes Balss», from which can be seen that the hianuscript with the previously shown fabrications was received by the editorial office from Sweden on April 11, 1977 (Vol. I, page 5), in such a way proving the fact of the distribution of J. Skulme's written manuscript. By the conclusion of graphical expertise the six page letter enclosed with the case file proved the accused Skulme to be the author. (Vol. 2. pages 12 to 14k The case file also has information from Hotel Riga (Vol. 2, page 132) which confirms that the Swedish citizen Izabella Genmarker was actually in Riga, from December 2, 1976, to January 7, 1977, and by visiting J. Skulme could make her proposal to him to collect information for her, as testified by the accused Skulme. The witness Adams OsiņŠ testifies that he told J. Skulme about the alleged worker unrest in the Daugavpils Synthetic Fibre Factory and at the Daugavpils Locomotive Repair Factory, about strikes in Liepāja and about train car's wheels being welded to the railroad tracks in Lithuania. THE CASE OF JURĢIS SKULME He had overheard this information from unknown persons on a train, of which he had also told Skulme. That the news in the accused Skulme's written manuscript, which he distributed abroad, and which discredits the Soviet State and social order, is a fabrication, can be seen from documents attached to the case. Thus, information from the procurator on the City of Liepāja (Vol. 2, pahe 125), the Internal Section of the Executive Committee of Daugavgils City Workers' Soviet of Deputies (Vol. 2, page 126) and the Baltic .ailway Board, show that neither in Daugavpils or Liepāja workers' unrest or strikes took place, and no person has been called to account for such actions, no incident took place in Lithuania where train car wheels were welded to the railroad/ tracks. The Manufacuring Union «Radiotechnika's» information shows that the radiola sets manufactured in the above named union are built for normal social needs and no secret installations are built into them. (Vol. I, page 88). Hence, according to the above shown facts in the sentence, it is proved that the accused Skulme prepared and distributed a manuscript which contains the above named hearsay information not corresponding to reality, this wilfully false fabrications that discredit the Soviet State and social order. The accused Skulme has commited a crime with these actions according to para. 183-1 of the Criminal Code of the Latvian SSR, and for this he must be punished. In determining the sentence for the accused Skulme, the court has taken into consideration the character of the crime and the grade of social danger involved, personal data on Skulme's characted and extenuating circumstances of trie responsibility involved. the accused Skulme pleads guilty and regrets his crime, his character has been testified to as generally positive, which is why the court considers it possible to correct and rehabilitate him, and not to isolate him from society. However, he is to be under supervision, according to para. 24-1 of the Criminal Code of the Latvian SSR and is to be sentenced to conditional loss of freedom with compulsory labor to be done during the determined time of the sentence. Considering this and proceeding from paras. 299 and 300 of the Criminal Code of the Latvian SSR, the Criminal Case Court of the Latvian SSR found J. . Skulme (father Uga) guilty according to para 183-1 of the Criminal Code of the Latvian SSR and sentences him to loss of freedom to two (2) years and six (6) months; and in accordance with para. 24-1 of the Criminal Code of the Latvian SSR, J. Skulme's sentence of loss of freedom includes the committment to work under supervision of authorities for the execution of sentence. The incriminating evidence – the manuscript of six pages and two envelopes – to be deposited with the criminal case files. The sentence is final and cannot be appealed or protested against. (signed) Chairman U. Krastiņš, Member of the Latvian Supreme Court. Lay assesors I. Cepuks, I. Guzevs."]

Representative_Docs: ["GERMANY STILL LEADING FOREIGN TRADE PARTNER OF LATVIA Germany was still Latvia's most important foreign trade partner during the first four months of the yea-, the Central Statistical Bureau of Latvia reported. Latvia's exports to Germany accounted for 15.4% of the country's total export during the period, United Kingdom 14.9%, Sweden 10.9%, Lithuania 8.5%, Russia 5.9% of the total. Latvia's exports to the European Union member countries accounted for 60.2% of Latvia's total exports during the first four months of the year while exports to the CIS was 10.0%. All in all Latvia exported goods worth 430.508 million lats during the four months of the year, up by 5.2% year-on-year, including exports to Germany of 66.382 million lats, United Kingdom 64.203 million lats, Sweden 46.907 million lats, Lithuania 36.711 million lats, Russia 25.374 million tats. Germany was also the leading partner in imports, accounting for 17.0% of Latvia's total imports during the four months, imports from Lithuania 9.5%, Russia 8.6%, Finland 8.4%, Sweden 6.5%. Imports from the EU countries accounted for 54.1% of the total Imports during the four months, from the CIS 12.7%. According to the Central Statistical Bureau, Latvia in the first four months of 2002 imported goods worth 728.355 million lats, up by 10.2% year-on-year. including imports from Germany of 124.052 million lats, Lithuania 69.137 million lats, Russia 64.402 million lats, Finland 61.194 million lats, Sweden 47.376 million lats.", "LATVIAN FOREIGN TRADE VOLUME GOES UP According to the State Statistics Committee, the volume of Latvian, foreign trade has increased in the first five months of 1995, compared to the same time period in 1994. The export of goods has risen by 32.6% and reached 269 million lats. The total volume of imports has increased by 34.2% for a total of 347 million tats. The difference between the total volume of imports and exports has Increased; in the first five months of 1995, imports exceeded exports by 29%, while in the same time period in 1994 the difference was 27.5%. The relative importance of the European Union countries has increased in Latvian foreign trade figures, while the role of the CIS has decreased. In the first five months of 1994, Latvia exported 35.8% of its goods to the EU, but in 1995 this figure has already risen to 47,3%. Import from EU countries reached 50% of the total imports for Latvia to date this year, compared to 35.8% last year. Exports to the CIS added up to 44.5% of total exports fast year and 36.8% this year, while imports equalled 35.2% in 1994 and 29.1 % in 1995. In the first five months of 1995, timber and wood products occupied the most significant place in Latvia's exports – 28.6%. The share of total exports enjoyed by textile fabrics and goods was 13.9%, followed by foodstuffs at 8.9%, mechanical and electrical appliances at 8.3%, metal and metaiwork at 8.2%. In comparison to 1994, the export of timber and wood products has increased by 37.1 million lats, while the export of textile goods, as well as agricultural produce, has gone up by 7.3 and 5 million lats respectively. The export of transport vehicles, footwear and millinery goods has considerably decreased. Mineral products occupied the largest share of Latvian imports in the first five months of 1995 at 22.3%, mechanical and electrical appliances represented 17.9% of the total, chemical industry and related products 11.9%, textile fabrics and goods 8%, and transport vehicles 7.7%. When compared to the first five months of 1994, import of mechanical and electrical appliances and equipment has increased most rapidly – by 26 million lats. The import of chemical industry and related branch goods went up by 14.5 million lats, and the import of textile fabrics and goods increased by 13.4 million lats. The import of mineral products to Latvia decreased by 7.7 million lats and the import of meat and agricultural products by 2.5 million lats.", "GERMANY IS LATVIA'S MOST IMPORTANT TRADE PARTNER RIGA, 17 Oct, BNS – During the first eight months of 2001 Germany was Latvia's most important trade partner, the Statistics Office said. Exports to Germany accounted for 17.2% of the total exports in the first eight months of the year, Great Britain 15.9%, Sweden 9.5%, Lithuanian 8%, Estonia 5.8%. Latvia's exports to Germany surged by 12% in the eight months of 2001 yearon-year, exports to Great Britain dropped 0.3%, to Sweden 3.6%. Exports to Lithuania meanwhile grew 18% and to Estonia 22%. Latvia exported a total of 845.808 million lats ($U51.353 b) worth of goods in the eight months of the year, up 12% over the same period in 2000, from this exports to Germany were 145.04 million lats, Great Britain 134.197 million lats, Sweden 80.275 million lats, Lithuania 67.179 million lats, Estonia 49.275 million lats. Exports to the European Union accounted for 62.3% of Latvia's total exports in the eight months while the exports to the CIS were 9.5%. Germany was Latvia's most important import partner as well in the first eight months of the year with imports from Germany accounting for 16.8% of the total imports, from Russia 9.6%, Lithuania 8.1%. Finland 7.9%, Estonia 6.5%. Latvia's imports from Germany surged by 20.5% in the first eight months in 2001 year-onyear, imports from Russia dropped by 8.6%, from Lithuania fell 27.3%, Finland 2.2%, Estonia 22.6%. The Statistics Office said Latvia in the first eight months of the year imported goods worth 1409.269 million lats, up 14.3% from the same period last year. Latvia imported from Germany goods worth 237.253 million lats, from Russia 135.145 million lats, Lithuania 114.386 million lats, Finland 111.265 million lats, Estonia 74.985 million lats. Imports from the EU in the eight months accounted for 51.9% of the total imports, from CIS 15.2%. (SUSI = LVL 0.625)"]
