Representation: united nations_mac arthur_general mac_united states_korean war_north korean_north korea_chinese communists_soviet union_republic korea
Representative_Docs: ['Peace is more than a word (Concluded) The world knows that an era ended with the death of Josef Stalin. The extraordinary 30? year span of his rule saw the Soviet Empire expand to reach from the Baltic Sea to the Sea of Japan, finally to dominate 800 million souls. The Soviet policy shaped by Stalin and his predecessors was born of one world war. It survived with stubborn and often amazing courage a second world war. It has lived to threaten a third. Now a new leadership has assumed power in the Soviet Union. Its link to the past, however strong, cannot bind it completely. Its future is, in great part, its own make. v This new leadership confronts a free world aroused, as rarely in its history, by the will to stay free. This free world knows—of bitter wisdom of experience — that vigilance and sacrifice are the price of liberty. It knows that the defence of Western Europe imperatively demands the unity of purpose and action made possible by the North Atlantic Treaty Organisation, embracing a European Defence Community. It knows that Western Germany deserves to be a free and equal partner in this community; and that this, for Germany, is the only safe way to full, free unity. It knows that aggression in Korea and in South- east Asia are threats to the whole free community "to be met by united action. This is the kind of fre? world the new Soviet leadership confronts—it is a world that demands and expects the fullest respect of its rights and interests. It is a world that will always accord the same respect to all others. So the new Soviet leadership now has a precious opportunity to awaken, with the rest of the world, to the point of peril reached, and to help turn the tide of history. Will it do this? We do not yet know. Recent statements and gestures of Soviet leaders give some evidence that they may recognize this critical moment. We welcome every honest act of peace. We care nothing for mere rhetoric. We care only for sincerity of peaceful purpose — attested by deeds. The opportunities for such deeds are many. The performance of a great number of them waits upon no complex protocol hut upon the simple will to do ihem. Even a few such clear and specific acts — such as the Soviet Union\'s signature upon an Austrian Treaty or its release of thousands of prisoners still held from World War ll—would be impressive signs of sincere intent. They would carry a power of persuasion not to be matched by any amount of oratory. This we do know: a world that begins to witness the rebirth of trust among nations can find its way to a peace, we are ready — with renewed resolve —to strive to redeem the nearlost hopes of our day. The first great step along this way must be the conclusion of an honorable armistice in Korea. This means the immediate cessation of hostilities and the prompt initiation of political discussions leading to the holding of free elections in a united Korea. It should mean — no less importantly — an end to the dire,"! and indirect attacks upon the security of Indo-China and Malaya. For any armistice in Korea that merely released aggressive armies to attack elsewhere would be a fraud. We seek throughout Asia as throughout the world, a peace that is true and total. Out of this can grow a still wider task — the achieving of just political settlements for the other serious and specific issues ļ between the free world and the Soviet Union. None of these, great or small, la insoluble — given only the will to respect the rights of all nations. Again we say: the United States is ready to assume its just part. We have already done all within our power to speed conclusion of a treaty with Austria which will free that country from economic exploitation and from occupation by foreign troops. We are ready not only to press forward with the present plans for closer unity of the nations of Western Europe but also, upon that foundation, to strive to foster a broader European community, conducive to the free movement of persons, of trade, and of ideas. This community would include a free and united Germany, with a government based upon free and secret elections. This free community and the full independence of the east European nations could mean the end of the present unnatural division of Europe. As progress in all these areas strengthens world trust, we could proceed concurrently with the next great work — the reduction of the burden of armaments now weighing upon the world. To this end, we would welcome and enter into the most solemn agreements. These could propertly include: L The limitation, by absolute numbers or by an agreed international ratio, of the sizes of the military and security forces of all nations; 2. A commitment by all nations to set an agreed limit upon that proportion of total production of certain strategic materials to be devoted to military purposes; 3. International control of atomic energy to promote its use for peaceful purposes only; and to ensure the prohibition of atomic weapons; 4. A limitation or prohibition of other categories of weapons of great destructiveness; 5. The enforcement of all these agreed limitations and prohibitions by adequate safeguards, including a practical system of inspection under the United Nations. The details of such disarmament programmes are manifestly critical and complex. Neither the United States nor any other nation can properly claim to possess a perfect, immutable formula. But the formula matters less than the faith — the good faith without which no formula can work justly and effectively. The fruit of success in all these tasks would present the world with the greatest task — and the greatest opportunity — of all. It is this: the dedication of the energies, the resources, and the imaginations of all peaceful nations to a new world war; this would be a declared, total war, not upon any human enemy but upon the brute forces of poverty and need. The peace we seek founded on trust and effort among nations, can be fortified — not by weapons of war— but by wheat and cotton; by milk and by wool; by meat and by timber and by rice. These are words that translate into every language on earth. These are needs that, challenge the world in arms. The idea of a just and peaceful world is not new or strange to us. It inspired the people of the United States to initiate the European recovery programme in 1947. That programme was prepared to treat, with like and equal concern, the needs of Eastern and Western Europe. We are prepared to reaffirm, with the most concrete evidence, our readiness to help build a world in which all peoples can be productive and prosperous. This Government is ready to ask its people to join in with all nations in devoting a substantial percentage of the savings achieved by disarmament to a fund for world aid and reconstruction. The purposes of this great work would be": to help other peoples to develop the undeveloped areas of the world, to stimulate profitable and fair world trade, to assist all peoples to know the blessings of productive freedom. The monuments to this new kind of war would he these: roads and schools, hospitals and homes, food and health. We are ready in short, to dedicate our strength to serving the needs, rather than the fears, of the world. We are ready, by these and all such actions, to make of the United Nations an institution that can effectively guard the peace and security of all peoples. I know of nothing to add to make plainer the sincere purpose of the United States. I know of no course, other than ♦ hat marked by these and similar actions, than can be called the hhihway of peace. I know of only one Question upon which progress waits. It is this: What is the Soviet Union ready to do? Whatever the answer be, let it be plainly spoken. The hunger for peace is too great, the hour in history too late, for any government to mock men\'s hopes with mpr«» words and promises and gestures. The test of truth is simple. There can be no persuasion but by deeds. Is the new leadership of the Soviet Union prepared to use its decisive influence in the communist world — including control of the flow of arms — to bring not merely an expedient, truce in Korea but genuine peace in Asia? Is it prepared to allow other nations, including those of Eastern Europe, the free choice of their own forms of government and the right to associate freely with other nations, in a world-wide community of law? Is it prepared to act in concert with others upon serious disarmament proposals to be made firmly effective by stringent U.N. control and inspection? . If not — where then is the concrete evidence of the Soviet Union\'s concern for peace? The test is clear. There is, before all peoples, a precious chance to turn the black tide of events. If we fail to strive to seize this chance, the judgement of future ages would be harsh and just. If we strive but fail, and the world remains armed against ieself. it at least need be divided no longer in its clear knowledge of who has condemned humanity to this fate. The purpose of the United States, in stating these proposals, is simple and clear. These proposals spring—without ulterior purpose or political passion — from our calm conviction that the hunger for just peace is in the hearts of all peoples—those of Russia and of China no less than of our own country. They conform to our firm faith that God created men to enjoy, not destroy the fruits of the earth and of their own toil. They aspire to this: the lifting, from the backs and from the hearts of men, of their burden of arms and of fears, so that they may find before them a golden age of freedom and of peace.', '52 UNITED NATIONS CO-OPERATE TO STOP AGGRESSION IN KOREA Amid the confusion and frustration surrounding the Korean peace talks the world can recall on United Nations Day, October 24, one fact about the Korean aggression which history will remember: That this is the first time in history when 52 nations of the world have risen in united action to defend one small nation against aggression. It has been undeniably established with this first United Nations war that "collective security" is no longer a dream, a promise or a hope. With the Korean war, it became a reality. Viewed in this light, June 25, 1950, may well become the most important date of the twentieth century. For it was on this day that the North Korean army invaded the Republic of Korea. It was on this day that the United Nations Commission in Korea gravely wired Secretary-General Trygve Lie: "Commission wishes to draw attention to serious situation developing . . . assuming !| character of full scale war and may endanger the maintenance of international peace and security." A.nd, it was on this day that the Security Council of the United Nations by a 9/0 vote promptly placed the blame on North Korea and asked that no help be given North Korea but that all help be given to the United Nations in order to stop this aggression. First to respond to the UN request was the United States which two days later on June 27, 1950, | put United States troops at the disposal of the United Nations to help South Korea. By July 14, 1950, Tryve Lie announced that 52 : nations had endorsed a U.S.-spon- i sored resolution (June 27) recom- ; mending immediate military mea- \\ sures by the United Nations to ļ restore peace in Korea. By autumn 17 nations had contributed direct military aid in the form of planes, warships and troops. These nations were: Australia, Belgium, Canada, Colombia, Ethiopia, France, Greece, Luxembourg, Netherlands, New Zealand, Thailand, Philippines, Republic of Korea, Turkey, Union of South Africa, United Kingdom and the United States. While the rest of the 52 nations have contributer great quantities of money, blood plasma, military bases, food, clothing, rice, sugar, lumber and in a number of cases have offered troops which have not yet been used. These contributions to the United Nations\' cause in Korea have come from almost every land and every race; from such widely separated areas, for instance, as Sweden, Costa Rica, Iceland, Israel, Lebanon, Argentine, Liberia, Pakistan, India and Free China. Such a tremendous step, of course, was not taken ill-advisedly or hastily. Korea had long been the subject of dispute and disagreement. A short history of the Korean situation shows that as far back as the Cairo Conference (December, 1943) and the Potsdam Declaration (July 26, 1945) the great powers had agreed upon Korean independence from Japan. Yet, when Japan surrendered in 1945, temporary military expediency divided Korea at the 38th parallel, with the Soviets occupying the northern half (August 12, 1945) and the United States troops occupying the south (September 12, 1945). Real disagreement came when, despite an agreement (Moscow, December, 1945) between the USSR, the United States and Britain to set up a provisional Korean democratic government, the Soviets began to spurn or shackle all efforts to give Korea either unity or independence. In November, 1947, for instance, the United Nations called for a general election in Korea supervised by the United Nations Temporary Commission on Korea (UNCOK). Under the plan, the representatives\' elected would have constituted a National Assembly, which with UN help would be [charged with the drafting of a democratic constitution for a national government. The Soviet Union, however, frankly stated that Korea was none of the UN\'s business, and refused to admit UNCOK north of the 38th parallel. Thus, the May 10, 1948, elections were confined to South Korea, where the government of the United States assisted the United Nations programme. North of the 38th parallel, the Soviets set up their one-party communist government which they called the "Democratic People\'s Republic of Korea," and which claimed jurisdiction over all Korea. This regime lived, as it was created, in complete defiance of the United Nations. The United Nations had further trouble with the Soviet Union in 1948 over the matter of the withdrawal of troops from Korea. The United Nations had asked (December 12, 1948) that all foreign troops be withdrawn from Korea as early as practicable. The United States compiled by June 29, 1949, and threw its zone open to UN inspection. The Soviets, however, while claiming to have withdrawn its troops in December, 1948, refused to open its zone for USSR at the 38th parallel, and an iron curtain was drawn by the United Nations inspection. Thus, what had been a temporary line on a map, the Soviet Union now made a permanent barrier. In co-operation with the United Nations, the United States had meanwhile not only tried to unite Korea, but had appropriated more than 623 million dollars for Korea\'s welfare. This included 356 million dollars! in |funds |made available for aid in 1946-49 and 110 million dollars in Economic 110 million dollars in Economic Co-operation A d m i n i stration (Marshall Plan) funds in 1950. These funds were for public health, schools, communications, roads and for a constabulary force to aid the Koreans in controlling i border incidents caused by Communist guerillas in the North. This is how things stood until June 25, 1950, when the puppet. army of the satellite, North Korea, suddenly invaded the Republic of Korea. At the time, the North Koreans glibly placed the blame of the agression on the South, but the UN Commission on Korea, which only two days before the attack had made a complete tour of the South, was in a position to know the truth. The Commission, in fact, had reported that "the South Korean Army is organized entirely for defence, and is in no condition to carry out an attack." Later, documentary proof was discovered when the United Nations troops captured two North Korean ; Army documents: one document ; was a reconnaissance order which gave detailed instructions on the "enemy" before and during the attack, was dated June 18, 1950, seven days before the attack; and the other document captured was the battle plan for the invasion of South Korea written in Russian. Warren Austin, United States delegate to the United Nations, at a UN session on September 18, 1950, ; exhibited Soviet-made weapons ; captured from Communist troops. North Korean and Chinese Communist planes are also of Soviet design and manuufacture. The Korean crisis, of course, is , not yet over. And, the truce talks from time to time bog down in what seems hopeless frustration. Yet, for history, the real meaning of the Korean conflict is the fact that it was declared in the first place. The great meaning lies in the fact that men of many nations, races and religions are willing to unite for peace and fight aggression. No longer merely a pious hope among men, worldwide collective security is at long last a reality.', 'GENERAL MacARTHUR PUTS HIS CASE The controversy over General Mac Arthur\'s dismissal is far from dying down as U.S. watches the inquiry Congress is conducting on reasons which stood behind President Truman\'s drastic step. Both United States\' and overseas press is giving wide coverage to Gen. Mac Arthur\'s statements before the Congress Oral Committee, although some parts of their statements are not released for publication. The censors delayed for a long time the release of General Mac-Arthur\'s statement about General Marshall. General Mac Arthur said the Joint Chiefs-of-Staff had given their views on March 24 or 25. They had recommended that j the questions of turning Formosa over to Red China or of giving the Chinese Communists a seat in U.N.O. should not even be considered as a basis for peace discussions. General Mac Arthur said he understood that General Marshall had overruled them, and had said that he believed these two ques tions might well be considered a a Korean peace conference. General Mac Arthur added: " would ensure that Formosa doe not fall into Red hands. "I believe that, if it does, you will have lost everything we gained in the Pacific war. "You will have rolled back our strategic frontier from the little island groups that defend us now all the way to the west coast of the United States. "I believe that you must invite a third world war if you allow that great breach in our lines." General Mac Arthur said the U.S. Joint Chiefs-of-Staff had ap proved of his drive to the Yula River — Korea-China border — last November. Senators asked General Mac-Arthur to explain his famous "home by Christmas" message to his troops advancing towards the Yalu River. He agreed that he had since described his November drive as "a reconnaissance in force." , He said this might well have resulted in a full-scale assault which undoubtedly would have I destroyed the remnants of the North Korean army. Use Sanctions General Mac Arthur said that if the Chinese Communists refused to consider an armistice, the United States should use military and economic sanctions. This would mean the bombing 3f Manchurian bases. "In my opinion, there is no weapon of war that is quite as efficacious as a blockade," he added. "If you put on a blockade, and it is successful, you are apt to kill them by the million. It would be "tragic" if other United Nations\' members withdrew their troops from Korea, should his suggested policy be followed, General Mac Arthur added. But if one nation carried 90 per cent, of the burden, it was "inappropriate" for other countries to exercise "undue authority" to any decisions. At a later stage, General Mac-Arthur said: "All the troops that have been fighting on what was my line in Korea were of high calibre. "The Turks were fine, the British splendid, the French firstclass. "They were all good. The only criticism I would have of them is that there are not enough of them." Matter of Time Senator Willey (Republican): "The most speculative question is: \'If we were to follow your plan, would Russia come into the war?\'" . . General Mac Arthur: . "As long as you have a maverick running loose in the world it means that your necessity for preparedness increases unless you can find a formula to settle the whole matter." Criticises British I believe most sincerely that the British are cutting their own throats in following the plans they have of such complete support of Red China," General Mac Arthur said. They completely supported giving Formosa to the Red Army, yet he believed that to lose Formosa would be to lose the whole battle. The British had been inflexible on recognition of Red China and disposing of Formosa. That position ran counter not only to America\'s best interests— the very life of the nation—but also to Bitain\'s best interest. It would be one of the most tragic things in modern civilisation to see the\' Anglo-Saxon people split, but if the U.S. could not find a solution with her allies it practically means we are not cillics U.N. Could Win War in Korea General Mac Arthur said that the United Nations could win it they used their whole power in the Korea theatre. It was held back now. His plan would not involve an overall increase in U.S. military preparedness. Only a relatively small fraction of U.S. power would be needed to win in Korea. The air force could be unleashed at full strength without leaving the Allies unprepared against a possible Russian attack. Soviet submarines couldn\'t cut off the Eighth Army from Japan supply bases, nor could the Russians take Japan. "Ma nchuria Could be Signal for World War,"— Warns Gov\'t. Expert U.S. State Department expert on Russian problems, George Kennan, believes that*it is "almost inconceivable" that Russia would do nothing if Manchuria were bombed, and feels that adoption of General Mac Arthur\'s policies would be likely to lead to immediate world war. Stewart Alsop, noted news cornmentator. says that Kennan has been "dead right" all along. Representative_Docs: ['The Battle of Balloons On the night of July 13, and for four days following, thousands of balloons bearing printed messages of hope to the Czechoslovak people were launched on the German-Czechoslovak border in a project sponsored by the Crusade for Freedom. Borne by strong winds blowing from the west, the balloons reached as far as Central Slovakia, with Ostrava, Prague, Pilsen and Most as primary targets. Two types of balloons were p jsed: a rubber sphere which n bursts at high altitude, scatter,ng its cargo over a wide area, C md a plastic balloon which de- 1< scends on the target. They car- s! ried twelve million messages— p me for every person in the coun- b try—consisting of an illustrated leaflet describing the recent de- 1< monstration in East Germany and c the tall of former Soviet Police s Chief Lavrenti Beria; a likeness 1 of the one-crown ikoruna) note a issued during the recent currency t reform, with an inscription; and li an aluminium coin stamped with t the Freedom Bell (see cuts and texts on facing page). On the t final night of operations, 80 plas- t tic balloons with candle lanterns r attached were launched in pro- t cession. Since the night was i exceptionally clear, the "torch- v light parade" could easily be ob- t served from a great distance. As soon as the first balloons < had landed, Radio Free Europe \\ was on the air explaining to the 1 Czechoslovak people the purpose < of the campaign and the ways in \\ which they could assist it. The ( operation was explained politic- I ally as an overt appreciation by \\ the West of the Czechoslovak people\'s will for freedom as expressed in their recent demon- | siations against the Communist . iegime. The balloons were in- ! tended to prove the ineffectually \' of the Iron Curtain, to show that the West could and would ! always find nieans by which to \\ communicate with the enslaved , peoples. Radio broadcasting is , one well-established means; leaf- \' lets, showing photographs of events suppressed or distorted by the Communist press, provide a lurther means of contact which can parallel and supplement the efforts of radio. "Tne Iron Curtain Does Not Reach to the Skyr" a song composed especially for the occasion, was broadcast over RFE to dramatize the combined attack of radio and the printed word against the barrier set up by the Communists to isolate the | pip from the The technical progress of the balloon barrage was reported in regular bulletins over RFE. Figures were given on the number of leaflets, "crowns" and coins being sent and the estimated time and location of their . landing. RFE gave advice to the people on how to open the , plastic balloons, and how to circulate the contents. They were advised to protect themsleves by ; turning over a certain number I ot leaflets at the demand of the I wommunist police, but to keep ! enough lor tnemselves and their ļ immediate family and friends; in ļ this way, maximum distribution I could be assured. Ultimately, said Radio Free \' Europe, the balloon campaign j means that the West will not ; recognize as final any Communist appeasement measures which stop short of restoration of libi erty to the oppressed peoples of ļ Central and Eastern Europe. Communist counter – measures wfcre swift and direct. On July 15, Russian-built Mig-15 jet fighters attacked the balloons and are known to have hit two. Ground fire later downed three more. The Czechoslovak one-party press responded with clamorous indignation. On July 18, "Rude Pravo." official organ of the Czechoslovak Communist Party, published a lengthy article characterizing the operation as an audacious act "surpassing even Hitler\'s hate campaign against Czechoslovakia": Echoes reverberated throughout the entire Satellite area, and from the Soviet Union itself where, on July 20, "Pravda" (Moscow) published excerpts from the "Rude Pravo" editorial, with 1 this preface: "Having failed in their Berlin provocations, the : West European agents of the US imperialists are endeavoring to carry out still another provoca* tion aimed at hampering peacei ful construction in the countries > of People\'s Democracy. This new ■ sortie by the enemies of the i Czechoslovak people is exposed by "Rude Pravo" in its article ) entitled A Campaign Doomed to • l-\'ailure\'." , On the same day, Radio War– saw broadcast portions of the . article, and the Romanian radio denounced Radio Free Europe , and quoted "Rude Pravo." Radio – Budapest, July 21, described the ;, ballooon barrage as "directed against the peaceful co-existence – of the peoples" and an interfer. ence into the "domestic affairs" . of Czechoslovakia. Radia Sofit. ~ July 23, repeated the theme. "Rude Pravo\'s" article was reprinted in most Czechoslovak newspapers. An official protest by the Czechoslovak government followed. On July 20 the Czechoslovak Foreign Ministry dispatched this not to the U.S. Embassy in Prague: "Small balloons, contai ni n g leaflets of an inflammatory character, have recently been sent over Czechslovak territory. These leaflets are aimed at creating unrest among the population of the Czechoslovak Republic and at inciting it to activities against the state. "This new attempt to influence the Czechoslovak working people by such primitive propaganda methods was naturally met by them with feelings of contempt and revulsion and. \'m itself, would scarcely merit serious mention. "The crux of the matter, however, is that the whole action was prepared and carried out by U.S. citizens especially sent to Germany for this purpose and, what is more, to that part of Germany which is controlled by the U.S. Occupation Administration. "We are thus faced with another case of gross abuse by the U.S. Government of its position as an occupying power, incompatible with the four power agreements on Germany. Under these agreements, the United States assumed the obligation to carry out the occupation in accordance with the principle that Germany must never again become a threat to world peace and to its neighbors. "A further important consid- ; eration. which makes this latest infringement of the fundamental principles of international law particularly reprehensible, is that it is taking place at a time when the nations of the world are inten s i f ying their efforts to strengthen peace and secure international co-operation. "The objective of this provocation, organized under the egis of the U.S. occupation authorities, is to step up the so-called \'cold war\' policy and to hamper the relaxation of international tension. "For these reasons the government of the Czechoslovak Republic energetically pro tests against these acts and requests that it be informed by the U.S.\' Government of the steps taken to prevent the recurrence of similar provocations aimed against peaceful relations among the nations and constituting gross interference in the internal affairs of Czechoslovakia." The U.S. Department or State, through Ambassador Wadsworth in Prague, presented the following reply to the Czechoslovak government: "The American Embassy presents its compliments to the Czechoslovak Ministry of Foreign Affairs, and with reference to the Ministry\'s note of July 20, 1953, concerning the release of balloons from the U.S. Zone of Germany, has the honor, upon instructions of the U.S. Government, to make the following reply: "The balloons apparently in question were released by the Crusade for Freedom, an organization establshed by private American citizens. The U.S. Government has ascertained that the balloons were released for the purpose of communicatine with the people of Czechoslovakia. "Tt is a fundamental conviction of the American Government and people that free communication between people eonstitutes a principle upon which the very life of the international community should be founded. If any Government attempts to erect a wall sealing off its people from contact with the outside world, it is inevitable that ways will be found both by the people inside that country and by the people of other countries outside to penetrate that artificial barrier and maintain some line of contact with one another. The use by those outside of such media as balloons, confirm that I the Czechoslovak Government has created a barrier interfering with free communications. The U.S. 1 believes that the best assurance that this means of communication i will not be used is to obviate the need for it by permitting what is natural and necessary in the modern world: namely, free contact between nations and the free exchange of information and ideas. "As for the content of the messages carried to Czechoslovakia by this medium, it is understood that they contained information on current developments in Central and Eastern Europe and. in addition, various statements focusing on the idea of \'freedom.\' There would be no reason for a Government to be disturbed by the principle of freedom in a message to its people, if conditions of freedom actually existed in that country. One cannot readily believe that the Czechoslovak Government would even raise this issue with the U.S. Government il the fundamental freedoms were observed in Czechoslovakia, and if the Czechoslovak Government were not now especially concerned about the attitude of the people toward the absence of those freedoms. "The U.S. Government cannot agree that the transmission of these messages to the people of Czechoslovakia from the territory of the U.S. Zone of Germany by the"* Crusade for Freedom Committee involved any misuse of the position of the U.S. as an occupying power. Such communication is clearly not contrary to any quadripartite agreement affecting Germany, nor does such communication violate any other international agreement to which the U.S. Government is a signatory. "With reference to efforts to strengthen world peace, the U.S. as is generally known, has endeavored in every practical way to work step by step, in accordance with the deep interest of the American people in permanent peace, and in co-operation with other countries, toward building an enduring structure of" international peace and stability. The ■ U.S. seeks to help bring ■ about a relaxation of internai tional tension whenever there are ■ genuine possibilities of doing so. i It is recognized, at the same ■ time, that true international ■ tranquility presupposes conditions I which assure human rights and ■ fundamental freedoms for the ■ people concerned." Information on the reaction of the Czechoslovak people, to whom ■ the balloon messages were ad• dressed, began to come in slowly \' but steadily. Seven escapees from 1 Pardubice in Czechoslovakia, in– terviewed in Vienna, reported: ■ "All during the week people i rushed to catch the leaflets as 5 they drifted down. Many wore 5 the coins around their necks, f The Army posts sent out soldiers ■ to collect the leaflets, and even the postmen had orders from – the militia to ask everybody 1 along their postal routes if they – had found any leaflets." A man , in Bratislava related over the i Czechoslovak radio his own ex? perience after he had picked up i some of the replicas of the crown – notes. We\'ll take it."', 'A CHRONOLOGY OF EVENTS IN LATVIA -1988 The political and social upheaval that took place in Latvia in 1988 actually began in 1987, when the Latvian human rights group \'Helsinki 86\' staged the first unofficial public demonstration at the Latvian Freedom Monument in June. By August of last year mass rallies occured in all three Baltic capitals, also in defiance of official wishes. While these events were unprecedented, not much else had changed in Latvia when 1988 began. \'Helsinki 86\' was the only openly active political group in Latvia and was denounced by the authorities as a radical extremist group. The Latvian national flag and hymn were outlawed. The Latvian language had no official status. Latvian newspapers faithfully followed the Party line and the Latvian people had little say in their own affairs. In the beginning of 1988, the four decade-long Soviet stranglehold on Latvia began to lose its grip. Here\'s a brief summary of what happened: January The Latvian Communist Party was shocked to learn that one of their own, Dr. Juris Vidiņs\', chief physician of the city of Rēzekne, had joined \'Helsinki 86. VidiņŠ was subsequently fired from his job and thrown out of the Party. Shortly thereafter, \'Helsinki 86\' submitted a comprehensive 10-point proposal to the Latvian Communist Party, calling for radical measures to restructure the Latvian economy, preserve the Latvian language, establish an independent press and protect religious and minority rights. February Gunārs Astra, Latvia\'s foremost human rights activist was released from prison after spending 19 years in Soviet prison camps. Astra had once been denounced by Soviet Latvian authorities as "the Andrei Sakharov of Latvia." March \'Helsinki 86\' leader and founder, Linards Grantiņs was forced to emigrate to the West after serving a 6-month jail term. Grantins chose Dr. Juris Vidiņs to replace him as the head of the human rights group. However, in an unprecedented display of \'glasnost\', a Latvian newspaper revealed that over 43,000 Latvians had been deported by Stalin in 1949. In a ceremony supported by the Latvian Writer\'s Union and sanctioned by authorities, over 15,000 Latvians participated in a March 25 memorial service for the victims of the Stalinist deportations. April During a visit to Leningrad, Gunārs Astra took ill and subsequently died on April 6 following a major heart operation. His wife questioned the circumstances of his death and asked for a Western pathologist to perform an autopsy, but her request was denied. On April 19 over 8,000 people attended his funeral, an extremely emotional event that was highlighted by the singing of the banned Latvian national anthem and the draping of the outlawed Latvian flag over his coffin. Six days later over 12,000 participated in a rally to protest proposed plans to build a subway in Riga. The rally was organized by the Latvian Environmental Protection Club (YAK). The YAK had existed quietly for several years and had a large following among Latvian youth, but this was their first mass public rally. May A Latvian Communist Party official revealed that Latvians now comprised only 47% of Latvia\'s population – the first time in their history Latvians had become a minority in their own homeland. June An explosive month that broke many taboos and shook the very foundations of Communist Party rule in Latvia. On June 1 the Latvian Writer\'s Union held a historic meeting where among other things, Latvian TV commentator Mavriks Vu If sons told a shocked crowd, including Party leaders, that Soviet rule in Latvia came about in 1940 not through a popular revolution, but instead a Stalinist occupation. A week later the Writer\'s Union passed a dramatic resolution asking that Latvia be made a sovereign state with its own citizenship, military units, foreign ministry and national symbols. The NY Times described the resolution as "one of the boldest appeals ever issued by an official Soviet group." On June 14, 50,000 Latvians gathered in Riga to honor the victims of the Stalinist deportations in 1941. A year earlier 5,000 Latvians had defied Soviet authorities by holding the first such rally at the request of \'Helsinki 86. This year, the rally was approved by authorities. The rally, however, turned into a massive nationalist demonstration, as speakers called for Latvian sovereignty and carried signs denouncing Soviej rule in Latvia. For the first time since the Soviet occupation, the outlawed maroon and white Latvian flag was unfurled at a public rally and displayed at the Freedom Monument. Later the flag carrier led thousands of Latvians in an emotional procession through the streets the Riga. Within days, the Latvian Communist Party held a stormy meeting where the leaders and events of June 14 were denounced by top Party officials. Unknown to the participants, their secret meeting was recorded and transcripts were released to the West, revealing that Latvia\'s top Party bosses were outraged by the new openness Gorbachev had imposed on their republic. On June 21, a coalition of 17 nationally known Latvian Party members, intellectuals, clergymen and Political activists announced plans to form a "Latvian opular Front", which would seek to support and implement the resolutions of the Latvian Writer\'s Union. July The momentum begun in June continued building in July as a former underground group known as the \'Latvian National Independence Movement" went public and put forth 20 demands, including an end to the Russification of Latvia, independent political parties and a re-establishment of a sovereign Latvia. On July 13 a Baltic folk festival in Riga turned into a major nationalist rally as the outlawed flags of independent Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia are prominently displayed at all concerts and events. Three days later 30,000 Latvians gathered at an outdoor amphitheater to demand the legalization of the former Latvian flag. Speakers included representatives from Armenia and the Crimean Tatars as well as Lithuania and Estonia. A week later 4,000 flag carrying supporters of the Latvian Environmental Protection Club disrupted an official Party rally organized to commemorate the 48th anniversary of Soviet rule in Latvia. Many of the Russian workers that were bussed in to till out the crowd of the official rally fled at the approach of the Latvians. Pressured by the crowd, the Party officials reluctantly allowed Arvids Ulme, president of the environmental group to address the rally. In front of the Party bosses, Ulme blamed Latvia\'s government for all the country\'s problems. August Having already conceded that Stalinist deportations had indeed occured in in the Baltic States, Soviet authorities were now forced to reckon with the 1939 Stalin-Hitler Pact that paved the way for the Soviet occupation of the Baltic States. On August 23rd, the 49th anniversary of the signing of the pact, over 50,000 Latvians held a rally in Riga, waving Latvian flags and demanding an anullment of the pact. During the rally, a letter sent by 27 U.S. Senators to the Baltic people was read out loud to the crowd. The letter, originating from the office of Sen. Don Riegle (D-MI) had been written in Latvian, Lithuanian and Estonian and had been sent to the three Baltic capitals prior to the rallies. Later that month, \'Helsinki 86\' Juris Vidiņs went to Moscow to meet with U.S. Ambassador to the USSR, Jack Matlock, to discuss developments in Latvia. September On Sept. 3 a massive anti-pollution rally took place in all three Baltic States along the shores of the Baltic Sea. In Latvia alone over 45,000 people formed a human chain over 36 kilometers long. The rally was organized by the Environmental Protection Qub and was supported by international environmentalists in Europe and the U.S. Plans were also being made for the founding congress of the Latvian Popular Front, which was scheduled for early October. In a vote of 8-6, the organizing committee agreed to invite representatives of Western Latvian groups to the congress. October Throughout the year, Soviet authorities in Latvia had backed off each time they were challenged by the nationalistic fervor of the masses. In October the old Party leadership was pushed right out of the picture. Just days prior to the founding congress of the Latvian Popular Front, Moscow replaced the First Secretary of the Latvian Communist Party and the President of Supreme Soviet of Latvia with "reform-minded" Latvians. The Latvian Supreme Soviet then legalized the formerly outlawed Latvian flag and made Latvian the "official" language of Latvia. On Oct. 7 over 150,000 Latvians held an dramatic rally in Riga to celebrate the founding of the Latvian Popular Front. Over the next two days the newly formed Popular Front worked out a far-reaching program, calling for economic sovereignty, environmental protection, greater civil rights, an end to immigration, a free press and freedom of religion. This last demand was dramatically realized when authorities gave the Popular Front permission to use the historic Dom Cathedral in Riga for a church service to "bless" the new movement. This was the first religious service in over 30 years in the Cathedral, which had been converted to a concert hall by the Soviets. Despite all these advances, opposition, this time coming from the Russian migrants in Latvia, began taking shape. An "International Front" of mostly Russians was formed to oppose the Latvian Front. Reports of Russian gang attacks oh Latvian youths in Riga also increased. November As Latvians formed their Popular Front, similar mass movements also emerged in Estonia and Lithuania. In October representatives of all three movements met in Riga to develop a joint strategy. On Nov. 11 the Latvian mass rally reached a new pinnacle when an estimated 200,000 people flooded Riga to commemorate \'Lāčplēsis Diena\' [Bearslayer\'s Day], to honor the veterans of Latvia\'s 1918-19 War of Independence. On that day the maroon and white flag of independent Latvia was ceremoniously raised atop the Riga Castle, where authorities agreed it could now remain \'forever*. Another Soviet taboo was smashed on Nov. 18, when hundreds of thousands of Latvians throughout the country celebrated Latvian Independence Day. Just one year earlier thousands of Soviet miltia had been stationed in Riga to block any ceremonies on this day. In 1988 Latvian Independence Day was celebrated nationwide. Most noteworthy was an official Popular Front sponsored concert, which was held in the Latvian National Theater, site of the signing of the Latvian Declaration of Independence in 1918. During November several Latvian informal groups, including the Popular Front, issued statements condemning proposed changes in the Soviet constitution which would reduce local rule in the Baltic republics. Unlike the Estonian Supreme Soviet, which openly defied Moscow and declared sovereignty, the Latvian \'parliament* took a more conciliatory position. It did so at the urging of its new President, Anatolijs Gorbunovs, who had just returned from a meeting with Soviet officials in Moscow. Nevertheless, Latvians joined Estonian and Lithuanians delegates in protesting constitutional changes at the congress of the Supreme Soviet in Moscow. In 11 months several remarkable things had occured. The traditional symbols of independent Latvia, long banned under Soviet rule, become legal again. The Stalinist crimes in the Baltic States were openly discussed and condemned in the Latvian press. Journalists and historians even began to challenge the legality of Soviet rule in the Baltic States – and their comments were openly published and broadcast for all to hear. The Latvian human rights group \'Helsinki 86\', once viewed as a radical extremist group, seemed almost establishment in comparison to the new nationalist groups that had emerged during the year. Ironically, the \'radical\' proposals it presented to the Communists in January, were the foundation of the demands of the Latvian Popular Front in October. Many in the West have attributed all these changes to Gorbachev\'s policy of \'glasnost\'. While partially true, it should be noted that all the gains made by the Latvians in 1988 came about through the will of the people. The people were not \'given\' their flag, anthem or language – they demanded it. They were not told to hold raļfies – they simply took the streets and forced the authorities to accept them. The Communists did not create the Latvian Popular Front, but simply joined it when they realized they could not stop it. Gorbachev tried to create a revolution from above – the Baltic people ran way ahead of Gorbachev and initiated it, far beyond Gorbachev\'s expectations, from below. 1988 is now over...stay tuned for 1989. The Chicago Latvian Newsletter would like to thank the American Latvian Association and the World Federation of Free Latvians for providing information used in this article. We\'d also like to thank the people of Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia for having the courage to do the things that we\'ve written about', 'US Congress and Latvians face? off against Soviets on the «ost historic November 18th in 69 years. With the two successful mass demonstrations behind then and the unanimous support of the U.S. Congress giving them courage, the people of Latvia were determined to publically observe Latvian Independence Day for the first time since the Soviet occupation over 4 decades ago. The Soviets were equally determined to stop them, and put thousands of armed Soviet militiamen, plainclothes KGB agents, civilian vigilantes and police dogs between the Latvian people and their goal. Perhaps it didn\'t happen in they way they had hoped, but Latvians did succeed in making this November 18, 1987 one that will never be forgotten. Many details remain to be confirmed, but here is a preliminary report of what happened: An angry I8th SEE YOU ON THE 18TH! The first indication that Latvians intended to publically observe Latvian independence day on November 18 came as early as August 23. As the crowd of 10.000 people who had gathered at the Monument of Freedom in KĪga that day broke up, people waved to one another and shouted, "See you again on the 18th!" Although the Latvian human rights group HELSINKI 86 had called for the June 14 and August 23 protests, they hesitated on taking a position on the 18th. Their leadership had been decimated by expulsions and arrests, and they were reluctant to urge others to do something they themselves could not participate in. There was also a new element of danger. While the Soviets reluctantly tolerated the previous two rallies, November 18th was out of the question. To commemorate the founding of the independent Latvian republic a republic destroyed by the Soviets was treason in the eyes of Moscow. It could not and would not be tolerated. THREATS. WARNINGS AND OMINOUS RUMORS The Soviet campaign to nip any Nov. 18 rally in the bud began as early as September 17 when Anatolijs Gorbunovs. a high ranking Latvian Communist Party leader warned that approaching the Monument of Freedo. on Nov. 18 would be viewed as an "anti Soviet act. This warning was repeated over and over again, in the press, in schools and workplaces. As Nov. 18 approached the pressure grew. Parents were warned that they would be held responsible for their children on this day Students were asked to sign documents promising they would not participate. Vigilantes, called "citizen security squads" were recruited among loyal Communists in the country. Rumors, possibly initiated by the KGB, indicated that ex cons, Afghan war veterans and an assortment of thugs were being brought into Latvia and were being trained in small scale terrorist tactics. The word quickly spread that "blood would flow" if the Latvians tried to come out on the streets that day. While some elderly Latvians, especially those who had survived the Stalin era, grew apprehensive and feared a return to bloody repressions. Latvian youth became defiant. The full-scale Soviet effort to thwart a Latvian display of nationalism merely angered and inflamed tJieir passions. "Making some kind of a statement" on November 18 became a «atter of national pride. THE US CONGRESS STEPS IN While tensions continued* to grow in Latvia, members of the United States Senate and Congress decided to take action. The Soviets were put on notice that their actions in Latvian would not go unnoticed. Cong. Uoug Bereuter (R NE) was joined by Dennis Hertel (D MI) and Don Ritter (R PA) in introducing H. Con. Res 209. which expressed support for the right of the Latvian people to peacefully observe their national day. In the Senate, stalwart Baltic defender Don Riegle (D-MI) was joined by Bob Dole (R KS). Robert Byrd (D WV) and Paul Simon (D IL) in introducing a similar resolution. Both resolutions passed by unanimous consent. This action, which could be considered provocative by some, was historic in its own right. It not only had unanimous bi-partisan support, but its co-sponsors included Presidential candidates from both parties, Dole and Simon. Prior to passage of the House resolution, coutless legislators took the stand to eloquently defend it. Among them were the leading human rights advocates in the country: Tom Lantos (D-CA), Steny Hoyer (D-MU), Henry Hyde (R-IL). Gerald Solomon (X NY) and John Miller (R W A). One Congressman, James Bilbray (D NY), was so outraged by Soviet plans to thwart the Nov. 18 demonstration, that he suggested the US Congress send an official delegation to Riga that day to observe the events. He even applied to the Soviet Embassy in Moscow for special permission to make a 48 hour visit to Riga MOSCOW SEETHES All of this was too much for Moscow. Knowing that the Latvian people were aware of all this support through Voice of America and Radio Free Europe broadcasts, they lashed out in anger at "U.S. interference in Soviet affairs." Loyal Communists stood up in meet ings in Riga and condemned the United States. With teachers standing over them, students were forced to sign petitions protesting the Congressional resolutions. Similar petitions appeared outside of workplaces in the hands of Party loyalists. Soviet anger was so great, they sent a delegation of Latvian Communist Party leaders, complete with a Soviet TV crew, to the US Embassy in Moscow, where they planned to lodge a protest with US Ambassador Jack Matlock. They should have known better They were never allowed to see Matlock. After standing on the sidewalk outside the embassy for 45 minutes an embassy official came out and gave the delegation a >rief political and history lesson. He explained the \'.S. nonrecognition policy toward the illegal Soviet annexation of the Baltic States. He said they were olcome to come in as private individuals and talk bout what they wanted. But anyone claiming to be an ■fficial representative of the government of "Soviet atvia" was not welcome. For reasons that are hard to fathom, TASS and Soviet TV later angrily reported this embarassing rebuff froi the US Ambassador. Everyone in the Soviet Union learned what happened. One Latvian in Rīga was asked how Latvians reacted to this incident. "We laughed" he said. NO ROOM AT THE INN The Soviets were not laughing. The Moscow incident, coupled with the Congressional resolutions, had generated a high degree of interest in the world press. A story was brewing and Riga was the place to be. The Soviets responded to this by trying to close off Riga to the outside world. Moscow correspondents were told that Riga\'s hotels were packed and trains were sold out. "There\'s no room in the city," they were told. Oddly enough, it was reported that on November 17, tourists were being bundled up and rushed out of Riga in an attempt to empty the Intourist hotels of potential eyewitnesses. (Perhaps the rooi was needed for the militia and vigilante forces that were being brought in.) NOVEMBER 18 – A STATE OF UNDECLARED MARTIAL LAI» The first reports from RTga came as early as widriight, the night of the 17th. As the first minutes of Latvian Independence Day ticked off, the first flowers already began to appear at the base of the Freedo» Monument This continued sporadically throughout the »orning. By 11.00 AM several hundred gathered at the «onuaent, prompting the militia to appear. One witness reports that around 40 people were arrested. There are also reports that several hundred people had been put under house arrest that same morning Two activist ministers. Juris Rubenis and Māris Ludviks were detained and later put under house arrest. Lidija Doronina was detained on the outskirts of RTga Roberts PoirTtis was taken off the street, bundled into a car and driven around for 8 hours. Janis Baikāns, acting leader of HELSINKI 86 was alsu detained. By noon, Riga came under the full trappings of an undeclared but otherwise comprehensive state of martial law. The city center was delcared a "green zone", meaning that unauthorized persons were subject to immediate arrest. Thousands of armed lili*l3 marched in and formed living barricades around the Freedom Monument. Lenin Monument, and National Theater. City cemetaries, including the gravesite of Latvia\'s first president, Jānis Čakste, were closed off to the public. Despite this extraordinary police presence, thousands of Latvians continued to gather near the Freedom Monument, milling around in the parks and the streets outside the double line of militia. Several eyewitness claim that as many as 7.000 people surrounded t c militia barricade. Some individuals tried to brea through but were stopped. A rumor spread through crowd that a US Congressman was about to «ake an appearance. (Cong. Bilbray never did receive an answer from the Soviet embassy, and thus could not go AN "OFFICIAL" DEMONSTRATION At 4:00 PM the officially sponsored and organized anti-American demonstration began at the Monument to the Red Riflemen near the river. Estimates range from 400 to 2.000 participants in this government-organized rally. These individuals too were surrounded by a phalanx of militia with police dogs. One observer claimed the militia seemed to be there to protect the Communist demonstrators from the Latvian onlookers. Several eyewitnesses claim that Latvian youth, outraged at the anti-American demonstration, tried to break through the militia to get at the Communist protestors. Fights broke out, women were thrown to the ground and during the arrests, many appeared to be injured. Based on information we have to date, this seemed to be the bloodiest confrontation of the day in Riga. PRISON VANS AT NIGHTFALL Sporadic incidents of violence seemed to pick at nightfall in various locations. One report, given by a Russian dissident in Moscow, claims that prison vans, loaded with people were seen rushing through RTga streets in the evening. Other public vehicles had also been commandeered to handle the apparently large load of prisoners. VIOLENCE IN LIEPĀJA RTga was not the only site of unrest in Latvia on November 18. According to Eva Biteniece, daughter of HELSINKI 86 member Raimonds Bitenieks and participant in the June 14 rally, approximately 900 Latvians held a solemn procession to the North Cemetary in the Latviaji port city of Liepāja. (f.iepa\'ja is the birthplace of HELSINKI 86 and its founders, Linards Granting, Martins Bariss and Bitenieks) While the procession began peacefully, at one point a young Latvian emerged from the crowd waving a huge ■aroon and white flag of independent Latvia. The • ilitia closed in to arrest him and bedlam ensued. There were bloody battles between Soviet militia and Latvians. According to Biteniece, 3 busloads of arrestees were taken from the scene. SUMMARY The above reports were all received within hours of the demonstrations. Some came from Latvian eyewitnesses in the city, including Georgs Rubenis, father of the Rev. Juris Rubenis. Despite Soviet attempts to put a news blackout on Riga, some Western reporters, including Washington Post Moscow correspondent, Gary Lee, were able to report on the events. (Lee was present in RTga at the Jūrmala Conference last year and has interviewed Rolands Silaraups in Moscow.) Nevertheless. Soviet efforts to stifle the rally and li«it reporting did succeed in part. HELSINKI 86 »e»bers. who coordinated reports on the previous rallies, were under house arrest or otherwise unable to reach the West. It is also not know if any Western tourists were in the city. If so, it is hoped that in UH coming weeks we will get a fuller picture of what tQok place on this historic day.']

Representative_Docs: ['21. AUGUSTA DEKLARĀCIJA Ko mūsu mītnes zemes vajadzībām sniedzam Rīgā izgatavotā anglisks tulkojumā The Constitutional Law of the Republic of Latvia on Latvia\'s Independence. Being aware of its responsibility to the people, and taking Into consideration its declaration „On the Re nevai of the Independence of the Republic of Latvia" adopted on May 4, 1990, and the results of the all- Latvia advisory vote held on Ma rch 4,1990, and the fad that on August 19,1991, as a consequence of the coup, constitutional structures of state power and government of the USSR have ceased to exist, Article 9 of the Declaration of Independence of the Republic of Latvia, adopted on May 4, 1990 on the restoration of the Republic of Latvia\'s independent statehood by way of negotiations cannot be implemented, – The Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia therefore resolves: 1. To declare Latvia as an Independent, democratic republic, in which the sovereign power of the Latvian State is possessed by the people ol Latvia, and its international judicial status is determined by the Republic of Latvia\'s Constitui tion „On the Restoration of Independence of the Republic of Latvia" defining the transition period for the restoration of the state power. 3. Until the time when the occupation and annexation of Latvia is liquidated and the parliament (Saeima) of the Republic of Latvia is convened, the supreme power is completely to be executed by the Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia. Only the laws and resolutions of this supreme state power are legally in effect in the territory of the Republic of Latvia. 4. This constitutional law is in effect upon Its adoption. A Gorbunovs, Chairman, Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia I. Daudiss, Secretary of the Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia Riga, Latvia, August 21,1991.', 'LATVIAN POPULAR FRONT CALLS FOR INDEPENDENCE! News from the Front: * Popular Front of Latvia (LTF) calls for total political and economic independence * LTF joins with Estonian and Lithuanian movements at historic \'Baltic Assembly\' in Tallinn * LTF board discusses strategy with Western Latvians at summit meeting in France * LTF leaders speak out for Latvian independence The Popular Front of Latvia (LTF), a massive, officially sanctioned popular organization boasting the support of over 1/4 million Latvians, including many Latvian Communist Party members, has called for total political and economic independence for Latvia. The decision came on May 31, when the board of the directors of the LTF voted unanimously to issue an appeal to the Latvian people to support them in this departure from their previous program. At its inception, in October 1988, the LTF had spoken only of seeking Latvian sovereignty within a Soviet federation. The May 31 declaration (printed in full in this issue) states that growing opposition "from the center and internal reactionaries\' made sovereignty within the USSR unlikely. Therefore, the board will seek to establish total Latvian independence through a "parliamentarian struggle" to alter the existing political structure. The LTF call for total Latvian independence is all the more remarkable in that a majority of its board members are also members of the Latvian Communist Party. Since the founding of the LTF, independent LTF board members, representing the National Independence Movement of Latvia (LNNK) and Helsinki 86 have attempted to move the LTF board in a more radical direction. Some Western observers had expected a split between the so-called "Radical Union" of the LTF and the more moderate Communist Party members. Instead, it appears that the moderates have come over to the side of the radicals, forming a unified front in support of independence. Baltie Assembly calls for independence One explanation for the shift in the leadership of the LTF, is the Baltic Assembly, held in Tallinn, Estonia on May 13-14. During this historic two-day conference, leaders of the LTF joined with the Estonian Popular Front and the Lithuanian Reform Movement Sajudis, in approving a series of strong resolutions concerning self-determination in the occupied Baltic States. Among the resolutions adopted by the Baltic Assembly were: l]a call for independent Baltic States within a "neutral and demilitarized zone of Europe"; 2Ja declaration of Baltic economic independence 3]an appeal to the members of the Commission on Security and Cooperation in Europe, and the UN General Secretary calling for the Soviet Union to declare the 1939 Stalin-Hitler Pact null and void. Prior to the meeting of the Baltic Assembly, of the three \'officially sanctioned\' Baltic fronts, only the Lithuanian Reform Movement Sajudis had spoken of total independence. In Estonia and Latvia, the Fronts had taken more moderate positions on sovereignty within the USSR, leaving the calls for independence to unofficial groups like the Latvian LNNK and the Estonian Independence Party. As has routinely happened in the past, the Baits appear to have followed the lead of their boldest and most outspoken member, in this case Sajudis. Having endorsed the resolutions of the Baltic Assembly, the LTF seemed to have no choice except reflect those positions in their own program. Summit meeting in France While some members of the LTF leadership were meeting with Estonians and Lithuanians in Tallinn, other LTF board members met with Western Latvian leaders in France. During the week of May 14-18, leaders of the LTF, National Independence Movement of Latvia (LNNK) and the Rebirth Party of Latvia held formal talks with the board of directors of the World Federation of Free Latvians (WFFL) at the Latvian Cultural Center \'Abrene\', near Chinon, France. The Abrene Summit was the first formal meeting between the board of directors of the LTF and the WFFL. [The WFFL board included Latvians from the U.S., Canada, Australia, West Germany, Great Britain and Sweden] In addition to the WFFL other Western Latvian participants included representatives of the Latvian Social Democratic Party, Daugavas Vanagi, European Latvian Youth Association, Latvian Popular Front Support Groups of Canada and several Western academicians and community leaders. The two groups discussed possibilities of joint cooperation in political, economic and cultural activity. They also discussed the development of a coordinated global campaign to promote sovereignty, self-determination and democratic reforms in Soviet-occupied Latvia. The WFFL for instance, agreed to assist the LTF in achieving representation in such international forums as the United Nations, CSCE Conferences and the International Olympic Committee. Outspoken leadership Two leaders of the LTF, both of whom returned to Latvia from recent visits to the West, have distinguished themselves in the last month with powerful statements on behalf of Latvian independence. Dainis Ivans is president of the LTF and was recently voted "the most popular person in Latvia" by a poll taken by the magazine \'Liesma. Ivāns, a writer, environmental activist and Communist Party member, had just returned from Moscow where he served as a delegate to the National Congress of the USSR, and made a moving speech during a mass rally on June 14 in Riga. Said Ivans: The inevitable collapse of the empire cannot be stopped by clubs, shovels nor tanks, for although the old regime does have power and fanatical priests, it no longer has ideas which can unite and control independent thinkers. [This regime J also does not have the desire to divide power according to principles of decency, justice and equality; this can and must be done by us, in order to guarantee the physical, national, social, political, economic and ecological safety of Latvians and Latvia\'s national groups, so that we can protect our women and children in an independent Latvia. In Moscow we were convinced: freedom cannot be obtained by begging or asking – we must seize and realize it ourselves." 4 Valdis Steins, a former chairman of the LTF board, particpated in both the Ganonoque (Canada) and Abrene (France) summits with Western Latvians, and has been one of the strongest proponents of Latvian independence in the last month. On May 27, Steins spoke at the congress of the National Independence Movement of Latvia (LNNK) in Riga. He called for the formation of a commission, consisting of World War II allies (the USSR, US, France, Great Britain) that would draft an international treaty "concerning the restoration of a free, democratic and neutral Latvia." Steins also emphasized that Latvia\'s citizens alone had the right, to determine the fate of Latvia. According to Steins, the only legal citizens of Latvia were those "who were Latvia\'s citizens on June 17, 1940 and their progeny." Several Latvian groups have begun forming "citizens committees" of individuals who meet the above criteria. It is believed that these groups will then hold a referendum on Latvia\'s future status. Until now, the Popular Front of Latvia, based on the positions of the program which was ratified at its founding congress, has based its activities on the first alternative: the federative principle. However, the events of the last month testify that all Baltic and Latvian efforts to realize this goal have come up against growing political, economic and ideological opposition from the center and from reactionary internal forces, including the open use of brute force, as was demonstrated by the events of April 9 in Tbilisi. Even the developments at the USSR Congress of People\'s Deputies demonstrate that a conservative majority has formed, which has taken a position against the aspirations of the union republics for total national sovereignty. As a result, the Board of Directors of the Popular Front of Latvia has proposed that all branches and member organizations of the Popular Front of Latvia consider a question of vital importance to our people: should the Popular Front of Latvia join the struggle for Latvia\'s total political and economic independence? We see the future Latvia as a democratic nation, where all citizens, independent of their national or social membership, political and religious beliefs, are guaranteed equal rights. Latvia must be a humanitarian nation, ruled by law, economic growth, and the opportunity for broad spiritual and cultural development for all ethnic groups. Relations with other nations, including also the union republics, must be developed on the basis of treaties guaranteeing mutual benefits and equal rights. It is the view of the Board of the Directors of the Popular Front of Latvia, that the Latvian people themselves mustplay the determining role in achieving independence. The Board of Directors of the Popular Front of Latvia foresees the road toward achieving this goal, as one which would involve a parliamentarian struggle, utilizing and modifying the existing structure of the Latvian SSR, according to the demands of the time and the will of the people, as well as one which would involve direct manifestations of the popular will. The goal of the Popular Front of Latvia is to achieve Latvian national independence, and to achieve legal guarantees which would allow the people to choose for themselves the form and mechanism of political power. It must be understood, that the road toward achieving this goal will be difficult, and will demand political responsibility and unwavering dedication from all Latvian patriots, regardless of their national or social membersnip. Our immediate task is to ensure that the June 16 session of the Supreme Soviet of the Latvian SSR ratify a declaration for Latvian national sovereignty, and approve changes in the LaSSR Constitution concerning property rights in the Latvian SSR and ratification of USSR laws, so that the Supreme Soviet of the Latvian SSR can immediately approve a law concerning Latvia\'s economic autonomy. With a view that positive election results are the main prerequisite for paving the way to the development of political freedom and indeoendence, a coalition of all progressive forces must be achieved toward this end. The Board of Directors of the Latvian Popular Front perceives and understands that the idea of the creation of Latvian Citizens Committees is a result of the ultimate consequences of totalitarian activities and as a necessary tool for the protection of the rights of Latvians and other native residents. However, the expansion of activity by these committees will be necessary only then, if Latvia\'s right to national independence and self-determination cannot be guaranteed through parliamentarian measures. The Board of Directors of the Latvian Popular Front', 'THE CONSTITUTIONAL LAW OF THE REPUBLIC OF LATVIA ON LATVIA\'S INDEPENDENCE Being aware of its responsibility to the people, and taking into consideration its declaration "On the Renewal of the Independence of the Republic of Latvia" adopted on May 4, 1990, and the results of the all-Latvia advisory vote held on March 4, 1990, and the fact that on August 19, 1991, as a consequence of the coup, constitutional structures of state power and government of the USSR have ceased to exist, Article 9 of the Declaration of the Independence of the Republic of Latvia, adopted on May 4,1990 on the restoration of the Republic of Latvia\'s independent statehood by way of negotiations cannot be implemented. The Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia therefore resolves: 1. To declare Latvia as an independent, democratic republic, in which the sovereign power of the Latvian State is possessed by the people of Latvia and its international judicial status is determined by the Republic of Latvia by the Republic of Latvia\'s constitution of February 15, 1922. 2. To repeal Article 5 of the declaration "On the Restoration of Independence of the Republic of Latvia" defining the transition period for the de facto restoration of the state power. 3. Until the time when the occupation and annexation of Latvia is liquidated and the parliament (Saeima) of the Republic of Latvia is convened, the supreme power is completely to be executed by the Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia. Only the laws and resolutions of this supreme state power are legally in effect in the territory of the Republic of Latvia. 4. The constitutional law is in effect upon its adoption. – Chairman, Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia A. Gorbunovs Secretary of the Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia I. Daudiss Riga, Latvia August 21, 1991']
