Representative_Docs: ['THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER THE AMERICAN LATVIAN ASSOCIATION DEFINES PRIORITIES AT THE 46TH ANNUAL CONGRESS IN CHICAGO On May 2-4 in Chicago, the American Latvian Association (ALA) organized its 46th annual Congress. Ilmārs Bergmanis, head of the Chicago Latvian society, stated that even though the Latvian community has become smaller over the years people are still active in their communities. Latvian culture and traditions must be introduced into the rest of the world, and passed onto the next generation of Latvian leaders, he concluded. Jānis Kukainis, President of ALA, emphasized the themes of the Congress which were to uphold the Latvian culture in the U.S. and to educate American Latvians about Latvia. In the political realm, Kukainis focused on NATO enlargement and Baltic security as Latvia\'s priority. Kukainis stressed the need for Latvia to solve its own domestic problems. He stated the importance of accentuating the positive when addressing Latvia to the international world. The positive attributes being political stability and low inflation. Einaris Semanis, Counselor from the Latvian Embassy, read a letter from Ojars Kalnins, the Latvian Ambassador to the U.S. According to the Ambassador, NATO unites Europe and the U.S. and Latvia\'s focus is to advance its interests in European structures. The July NATO Summit in Madrid is the beginning of a process, where countries will be invited for accession talks, the Ambassador concluded. In late May, in a letter to Baltic American leaders, President Clinton reaffirmed his commitment to NATO enlargement. "I am committed to pressing our allies to assure that NATO\'s doors will not close behind its new members. NATO should remain open to Europe\'s democracies ready to share the responsibilities as well as the benefits of membership. No country outside NATO will have a veto; no nation will be automatically excluded from joining the June-July 1997 Vol. XXVI. No. 16 alliance. We must not allow a gray zone of insecurity or spheres of influence to reemerge anywhere in Europe." The question still remains, what is the strategy for including countries into the second wave of enlargement, what happens after the Madrid Summit? Thus far, the Administration\'s strategy includes enhancing Partnership for Peace (PfP) and continuing dialogue with countries. In the President\'s letter to Baltic American leaders he remarked about the NATO-Russia Charter. "The NATO-Russia partnership that I have in mind will not in any way damage the interests of other nations. In my trips to Central Europe I have learned one phrase in many languages that sticks in my mind: \'Nothing about us without us.\' This is a good and important principle that the United States respects." The Russia U.S. NATO Charter was signed by Presidents Clinton and Yeltsin on May 27. This is also non-binding, however it serves as a forum for Russia\'s participation with NATO, not in NATO. There is a section in the Charter that outlines the principles that NATO and Russia will base their principles, "respect for sovereignty, independence and territorial integrity of all states and their inherent right to choose the means to ensure their own security." At the conclusion of the ALA Congress, delegates decided to support first tier NATO enlargement for the Baltic countries. Following the ALA Congress, on May 23 in the U.S. Congress, Senator Slade Gorton (R-WA) introduced Senate Concurrent Resolution (SCR 29). This resolution recommends the integration of the Baltic countries into NATO at the earliest possible date. This nonbinding resolution expresses Congresses view that the Baltics are to be commended for their efforts in establishing democratic and economic reforms. This is the Senate version of Representative Gerald Solomon\'s (R-NY) House Concurrent Resolution 10. On June 11, the House of Representatives passed the European Security Act (H.R. 1758). This bill includes text from the Gorton and Solomon resolutions, which recommend Baltic integration into NATO. The European Security Act designates Romania, Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania as eligible to receive NATO transition funding under the program established under the NATO Act of \'94. The passage of the European Security Act in the House demonstrates Congressional support for the Baltic countries before the July NATO Summit in Madrid. At the Summit, NATO Allies will reach a consensus and will invite certain countries to begin accession talks. On June 12, the Clinton Administration chose Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic as the first countries eligible for NATO membership. However, other European allies support the inclusion of 5 countries, adding Romania and Slovenia, in the first wave of enlargement. Thus, a consensus must be reached by the July Summit. As for the Baltics, the Administration remains committed to completing a U.S. Baltic Charter before the July Summit. This Charter will be non-binding but serves to alleviate some of the Baltic countries security concerns. The exact contents of the Charter are unclear at this time. The Administration also supports the passage of the European Security Act. The House passed this legislation parallel to the time that the Latvian Foreign Minister Valdis Birkavs was in Washington, D.C. for a three day visit. Foreign Minister Birkavs met with Secretary of State Madeleine Albright, U.S. Senators and Congressmen to urge that Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania are included in the next wave of NATO enlargement. After talks at the State Department, Albright repeated assurances that the first new members of NATO would not be the last and said the Administration\'s "goal is an integrated Europe and that Europe includes Latvia." The two leaders signed an agreement to cooperate in halting drug trafficking, money laundering and fighting crime. Speaking at the National Press Club in DC, Birkavs said Latvia was ready to share the burden of NATO enlargement and contribute to security in Europe by helping in peacekeeping operations. He said Latvian troops were serving in Bosnia alongside U.S. and Russian forces. Birkavs expressed satisfaction with President Clinton\'s announcement that the United States will allow only three countries– Hungary, Poland and the Czech Republic —to be included in the first wave of NATO\'s enlargement "I hope the summit will not be a once-in-a-lifetime event but more like the Olympics, where the principle is not to be first but to participate every two or four years," he concluded. On June 11, Birkavs addressed Latvian American community members in Rockville, Maryland about Latvia\'s security concerns. He said his meetings with Senators and Congressmen were supportive and highly positive. Some of the Senators he met included Senators Richard Lugar (R-IN) and Richard Durbin (D-IL) and some of the Representatives included Gerald Solomon (R-NY) and Richard Armey (R-TX). Birkavs reminded community members that they should contact their legislators by writing letters regarding their concerns for Latvia. Birkav\'s call for political action is already underway in the Latvian American community. The delegates at the ALA Congress in Chicago reached an agreement to contact their Members of Congress to support legislation for the Baltics. However, in reaching consensus ALA delegates lacked adequate representation and input from the younger generation. The themes of the ALA Congress should not only include passing on Latvian tradition and culture onto future generations and prioritizing foreign policy, but also engaging the youth to participate in the U.S. political process which promotes stability and security in Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia. Aija Straumane', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER GOVERNMENT: 1. The biggest news of course is the invitation to begin talks with the European Union (EU) which Latvia received at Helsinki. Actual talks will begin in early 2000. The Saeima also ratified the Language law, which was duly praised by the EU and roundly criticized by the Russian Federation, whereupon the Latvian Foreign Minister called the Russian Ambassador and strongly objected to their interference. During the TV News conference Prime Minister Šķēle stated that Latvia will end these talks in 2000. This is a fairly tall order, but by diligent application (and ideas as to how the first group of invitees coped) it can be done. 2. The President decided to bring the 18th of November (Latvia\'s Independence day) to the people. There were various concerts throughout the city all day (the weather was reasonable). The President spoke to those present from a balcony of Doma square. There was a short display of fireworks as well. 3. The Russian Federation Duma (Parliament) has unanimously voted sanctions against Latvia, since it "bodes ill for the ethnic Russians living in Latvia". Laughable. There is reportedly little chance of President Yeltsin signing this legislation. Latvian government authorities have criticized this step vigorously. 4. The Latgale Regional Development Authority (LRDA) has fifty strong projects – nearly 8 to 10 million lats worth – to bid for the first Phare funds for the year 2000. It plans to improve roads, infrastructure around them, a network of small companies, tourism object development, etc. 5. The Government has selected Zemgale as the other Phare fund recipient. They intend to clean up the Lielupe river basin, improve drinking water standards and use the money for several other projects. 6. Latvians at a 50% rate favor joining the EU, Estonians at 38% and Lithuanians at 30%. JANUARY 2000 WHAT\'S HAPPENING IN LATVIA by Laimons Streips in Riga POLITICS: 1. Latvian Television\'s evening news "Panorama" showed some young Latvians wearing camouflaged clothing, who had intended to go help the Chechens fight the Russians. This report caused quite a bit of trouble, and indeed censorship raised its ugly head. Luckily, reason prevailed and the Director-General of the Latvian Television did no lose his post. 2. That unnecessary referendum did not succeed. The slightly more than 70% of the necessary number participated. The President proclaimed the new Pension law. EDUCATION: 1. The Rīga Economic University (founded and financed by the Swedish government) is celebrating its sth year. The university has an international teaching staff, and the language is English. There is an enormous interest by the Latvian young in the school\'s programs. 2. Teachers in Latvia are up in arms. The 6th Saeima – with a devil may care attitude – had promised them 29 million lats in salary increases, yet Šķēle government reduced this to 6.3 million. There was a teachers\' demonstration in front of the Ministry building. A jovial Andris Šķēle also attended, and smilingly stated that laws can say this and that, but, unless backed by funding, the laws do not work. He promptly invited the Teacher\'s Union leaders and a couple of teachers to meet with him. However, regardless of the Education and Science Minister\'s Silva Golde explanation to the Union, no agreement was reached and on November 16, teachers were not teaching. There are intimations of more strikes of a full week\'s duration. Some 47,000 teachers also walked out on December 1. The government will allocate over 8 million lats in salary increases, however, the Union demands 28 million lats, which is a huge difference. It is unfortunate that many older and unreplacable teachers will retire, yet the new teachers, graduating form Teacher\'s colleges are not so gullable as to work for santims. 3. On November 17, the Education and Science Minister Silva Golde resigned from her post because she could no prevent the teachers\' strike. Prime Minister Andris Šķēle has allowed Mrs. Golde to resign, though the Teachers Union is backing the Minister. 4. There is now a home page for the Latvian Academy of Sciences, both in Latvian and English. There have been some 12,000 patents issued to Academy members since 1994. It may be reached at http://inventions.lza.lv and the same in Latvian, instead of inventions – izgudrojumi. ECONOMY: 1. There are signs that the austerity, which Prime Minister Andris Šķēle imposed on Latvia, is bearing fruit. Unemployment is slightly down a*id the GNP will be healthier than has been predicted. 2. Apparently due to the above the demographic situation has improved slightly. The number of births is up by 7%, including the triplet girls in Liepāja. Ilze, Ilva and Inga, whose mother is Inese Birzgale, received 3,000 lats from the government and 1,000 lats from the Liepāja municipality. However, the number of deaths still outnumber births by a wide margin. 3. The Computer programming industry is fast moving to the fore-front in Latvia\'s economy. Latvia\'s computer scientists are exporting programs worth 4 billion lats. There seems to be a certain lack of specialists, yet since this is an open economy, it is expected that young people will gravitate to this field. DEFENSE: 1. Latvia\'s "Border Guards" marked its 80th aniversary with a parade in Rīga. So did Latvia\'s armed forces a week earlier, and the police force – two weeks later. 2. Several soldiers of a sapper unit, including a woman staff sergeant Zane Šiliņa, were honored for detonating more than 1000 various weapons littering the Latvian countryside. Several boys nearly blew themselves up with an explosive device, and three of them are still in serious condition. 3. Latvian military has reverted to the attractive uniforms of pre-war Latvia. However no helmets have been seen, since these reportedly look like German Nazi helmets. That caused a newspaper reader to say: "Hitler also wore pants. Shall the military now do without them? And shall we after all remove Adolfs from the names-day roster? HEALTH: 1. Again this year, collection boxes will be placed in stores for money donations. Do not walk past those in need! 2. Narcotics users have but four places to exchange used needles for new ones in Latvia. There are currently 470 HIV-positive cases here, although the real number might be three to four times higher. There have been some 60 AIDS related deaths. 3. World Rotarians have granted $400,000 for building a new hospital in Auce. Rotarians will not only completely equip the hospital, but will also train the medics. FOOD: 1. Sugar is in the news. The Jēkabpils sugar factory has gone bankrupt. The farmers are plowing sugar beets under or feeding them to cattle, yet from Estonia every day (Latvia has a free trade agreement with Estonia and Lithuania) tons of sugar enter Latvia on the backs of people, in hand carts, trucks or whatever have you. 2. The ban on pork imports has been extended for two more years to permit our own pig raisers to recover. The ban has a duty of 1.05 lats ($1.75) for a kg of pork. CULTURE: 1. The 188 nations participating in a UNESCO conference without any debate voted to support Latvia\'s National library building plans. There are indications that by 2005 the library will begin operations. The architect of this edifice is the famous Latvian Gunārs Birkerts. 2. The Latvian National Opera is again in the news. There was a possibility that the Opera house might be closed part of next year if no monetary help from the government is found. However, the government chipped in with additional funding, although very clearly indicating that further budget foul-ups will mean the Director\'s demise. SPORTS: 1. As I pointed out in last month\'s English summary, the Latvian National ice hockey team fell out of the A group of hockey. This year they had to play Kazachstan, England and Ukraine. They beat Kazachstan 6:3, played to scoreless ties with Great Britain and Ukraine, and landed in first place in their group. Latvia and Ukraine now move to group A in European ice-hockey. The next round will be played in St. Petersburg, Russia, in the Spring. The press is giving a lot of credit to the new team manager Herberts Vasiljevs. 2. Daugavpils, the largest city in economically stagnant Latgale region, has recently dedicated an ice-rink building worth 1.5 million lats. The city will have a major league ice-hockey team, hockey school for youngsters, as well as a city championship and cup matches in ice-hockey. Such halls already exist in Liepāja and Ventspils. Quite soon there will be one at the 55th High school in Rīga, as well as in Jūrmala City, Valmiera, Saldus and Jelgava. Such infrastructures are extremely important for Latvian sports development, not only in ice-hockey, but also basketball, soccer, team handball, football and other team sports. 3. Latvia\'s National Volleyball team beat their competitors in Belgium and will probably play in Sydney, Australia if they get by the elimination match in Poland next month. 4. Latvian Anna Orlova was sixth in the luge competition at Lillehammer, but took the bronze at the Sigulda World luge meet. NATURE: 1. Viva Livonia is a tourism project that unites southern Estonia and northern Latvia. Work on the infrastructure (paving of the road) will begin by the year 2000. There is much to see in this meandering 550 km stretch from Peitsama in Estonia to Sigulda in Latvia. There is the Apekalns church, the highest in Latvia, since it sits on a 235 m high hill. Not far from Ape is the impressive Grūbe waterfall, highest in Latvia. From the Drusku castle on a clear day one can see that Estonian showpiece mount Munamāgi. Of course, Sigulda, with the river Gauja and three castles, in itself is a showpiece. It provides ample parking for cars, has scenic bicycle paths, horseback riding facilities, as well as skiing in winter. 2. As a reminder of their concerns for ecology the Latvian "greens" hoisted a black mourning flag upon the Latvian Mobile Telephone tower on Latvia\'s highest hill Gaiziņš 3. The "greens" are up in arms about trees in Rīga. It seems that very many are being cut, but very few replanted. 4. Riga\'s National Zoo has just obtained nine holy ibises from the Dresden Zoo. These birds are black at each end and white in between. 5. In early December, there were big storms in Europe devastating Denmark and attacking southern Latvia. A fishing boat "Sniegs" sank and all six sailors perished. Due to the inclement weather, the bodies were not found. Another fishing boat struck a bridge in Liepāja, ramming the heating duct and cutting off heat and hot water supplies for much of the city. The storm did not spare some inland parts of Latvia. In Smiltene, Central Vidzeme, during the night of December 7, a heavy windstorm damaged the 80 meter high TV broadcasting tower. CITIES: 1. In the economically undeveloped Latgale the Vārkava High school in Upmalu parish managed to have the 12th grade students graduate and all of them proceeded to attend colleges. 2. The maniac of Gulbene, Alexander Kuryakov, who killed three little girls and their guardian was sentenced to life in prison. There is no death penalty in Latvia. 3. At the Hannover exposition sits a glass Catholic church. Vatican has promised this church to Liepāja. Thus the city of Liepāja will acquire a new, round glass church. 4. A taxi driver reported that a nude man jumped into Daugava river for a swim. A bit later he was seen in a downtown bar, warming himself with a hot drink. CRIME: 1. The FBI was very grateful to the Latvian police. A man put bombs in two North Carolina stores in USA, killed thereby five people and demanded $250,000 not to blow up another store. He got the money and decided to put it in an unknown country, namely Latvia. However, his name was quite quickly ascertained and communicated to the FBI, which in turn was very thankful. The perpetrator, who later confessed, was arrested. 2. Vasiliy Kononov – the red partisan, who with his men dressed in German uniforms in 1944 murdered by burning to death several families in the Latgale region, was questioned during his court appearance. He claims that he was nowhere near the crime scenes and points his finger at academician Vilis Samsons, who on TV reported leading 3000 red partisans. On December 6, there were two demonstrations held – for and against Kononov. Abreviated information from Chicago Tribune, Dec. 3, 1999 — A Russian immigrant admitted Friday he had locked five Latvian women in a suburban Chicago apartment and forced them to work as strippers to line his own pockets. Alex Mishulovich, 38, pleaded guilty Friday to federal charges involving keeping people in servitude. In a written statement, he admitted that he lured the women to America, then kept their passports, controlled their movements and farmed them out to work in strip joints six nights a week, pocketing their earnings. He threatened to kill them and have the "Chechen mafia" murder their families if they disobeyed.— — Mishulovich, faces up to nine and half years in prison – – – — Mishulovich, a naturalized U.S. citizen, recruited the women from the Latvian capital of Riga in 1996, promising them pay of up to $60,000 a year if they worked for him as exotic dancers – – – Office of the governor NEWS GEORGE RYAN • GOVERNOR CHICAGO, ILL: Governor George H. Ryan sponsored a tribute to Illinois ethnic museums in Illinois at the James R. Thompson Center. Over 30 ethnic museums had cultural exhibits at the event. The Latvian Folk Art Museum received an award for its participation in Governor George H. Ryan\'s Ethnic Museum Day. Pictured left to right: Paul Markham, Ruta Markham, Penny Kendall, director of International Relations & Chief protocol for Governor George H. Ryan, Roz Plate and Pat Michalski, Assistant to the Governor for Ethnic Affairs,', 'Tf;e clhiiitauqua Conference in occupied Latvia This past September seven Latvian – Americans joined over 270 Americans in a historic "town meeting" between U.S. and Soviet officials in the Baltic resort city of Jūrmala. In Soviet-occupied Latvia. Ojars Kalnlns, Public Relations Director of the American Latvian Association and editor of the Chicago Latvian Newsletter, participated in the conference and gives this special report: TH X IKtBIHIIN <; On Nay 7. Dr Olgerts Pavlovskis. President of the World Federation of Free Latvians and I. were called to the State Department in Washington. D C. to discuss what had been described to us as a potentially "historic event" scheduled to take place in occupied Latvia. The "event" was the Chautauqua Conference: a privately sponsored \'town meeting" where US and Soviet spokesmen could debate superpower relations. A similar conference, involving high ranking U.S. and Soviet officials had already taken place in 1985 in Chautauqua, New York. This year, in a gesture of reciprocity, the Soviet-U.S. Friendship Committee in Moscow had invited the Chautauqua Institute to participate in a followup conference in the Baltic resort riiy of Jūrmala. Latvia. Mark Palmer, then Deputy Assistant Secretary of State and John Hallach, foreign editor of the Hearst Newspapers and coordinator of the conference, explained their views on the event. While they were in favor of such a conference in principle (it promoted the "people to people" exchanges Reagan and Gorbachev had agreed on in the Geneva Summit), they had a problem with the location. The U.S. has never recognized the legitimacy of Soviet rule over Latvia, and generally does not send high ranking officials to the occupied Baltic States. Although this was a private undertaking, the perception of U.S. officials debating Soviets on occupied Latvian soil could be negative. We were told that the State Department had initially refused to approve U.S. government participation at the conference in Latvia. However, after Soviet refusal to change locations, the State Department began to reassess the situation. What If, they asked vi, the US. side agreed to participate in the conference, but would use this opportunity to dramatically call attention to the non recognition policy and other Baltic issues? Into the Lion\'s Den! Wallach and Palmer offered us a number of proposals which they felt would ensure that Baltic interests would be protected. U.S. speakers would clearly restate the U.S policy toward the Baltic States during the debates The American Latvian Association could send ten individuals of their choosing to the conference, who could raise questions and participate in the debates. We would also have total access to the press, speakers and delegates prior to the conference so that we could brief them on our concerns In their view, by openly challenging Soviet rule of Latvia in such a forum, the conference would bring the nontTcognit ion policy "out of the closet" and generate unprecedented press coverage in the West. Palmer saw it v a perfect followup to last years\' Baltic Peace and Freedom Cruise, with one critical difference: Baltic issues would be raised directly to the Soviets on occupied territory, not outside of it. tohm Wallach. as coordinator of the project, gave his personal word that every effort would be made to safeguard Baltic intercuts. In follow up meetings with ALA president Aristids Lambergs. Wallach made a startling offer. To prove his sincerity, he gave the ALA veto power over the entire project. If we could not approve the conference, the project would be called off. It wits an intriguing proposition. Throughout history Latvia had been caught in the middle of Major [tower conflicts Suddenly the ALA was in the middle of a historic event involving the two modern superpowers, only this time the decision of "go" or "no go" rested with us DOUBTS AND DECISION Al.A\'s first decision was "no go". Although disappointed. Wallach kept his word and informed Moscow that the conference could not take place in Latvia. While it seemed that the project had been killed. Lambergs. after conferring with ALA VP. for Public Information. Valdis Pavlovskis. decided to re evaluate the situation. Wallach. the State Department and even the White House (we were told) wanted this conference to take place. We were assured that Latvians would only gain by approving it Weighing the potential pluses and minuses, Lambergs and Pavlovskis ck*:tded that perhaps this was the timetry a bold experiment. For years we had been de-fending" Latvian interests in the West: why not go on the- offensive" and directly confront the oppressor at the scene of the crime, on occupied Latvian soil? They developed a new plan and subsequently presented the State Department with a detailed proposal. If the State Department could guarantee our requests in writing, the ALA would "not protest" the conference. Within days Lambergs received a letter from Deputy Secretary of State John C. Whitehead wriii h met all of ALA\'s requirements. The conference was on PLANS & STRATEGY Over the next three months, we actively began to prepare for the conference, devising an overall press and public relations strategy which would maximize the potential positive benefits of this unprecedented foray into occupied Latvia. We prepared comprehensive briefing books for the American delegates and the press, met personally with Amb. Jack Matlock. Jean Kirkpatrick and other scheduled "debaters" and briefed fr\'ends In the U.S. Congress. During the pre conference negotiations. John Wallach also won two important concessions from the Soviets: per Lambergs\' request, the debates would be simultaneously translated into Latvian as well as English and Russian. Second, the Soviets agreed that the majority of the local audience would consist of Latvians. The key to our strategy was the element of surprise. As far as the Soviets were concerned, the Haiti* issue was not a part of the agenda, and the ALA\'s participation in the conference was not publicly known In our view, the Chautauqua delegation was a Trojan horse entering into occupied Latvia with a bellyfull of Baltic sup rises As a i-esult, the biggest problem we faced was winning the support of the Baltic American community without tipping our hand to Moscow While we did In ief key Baltic American loaders, many in the community had only sketchy information about the conference and were understandably disturbed Even BOOT who knew about the arrangements remained skeptical. As word got out. criticism of our "failure to protest the conference" began appearing in the Latvian American press Whileit was troubling to hear this criticism from our fellow Baits, it did serve a purpose: it was what the Soviets expected and thus kept them in the dark about on r rea I plans. ON TO RIGA\' On Monday, September 15. over 270 Americans and 2,000 Soviets and Latvians gathered in a conference hall In Jūrmala. Latvia to begin what had been billed as "A Journey in Open Diplomacy" Among the American delegation were- seven Latvi,in Americans: Dzintars Kalniņa, Nils Melngailis. Louise Praaaar, Viesturs TJarots. Martins Hildebrants. Benita JaunclaJdei is and myself While none- of us had been horn in Latvia all wen- fluent in the Latvian language and active in the Latvian American community Our task was to help fulfill three objectives: I To protect the U.S. nonrecognit ion policy. 2. To generate press coverage about Latvia and the Baltic States, and 3. To bring a message of support to the people of occupied Latvia. During the following week, amid high drama, deep emotion and a numl>er of unexpected developments, these objectives were fulfilled beyond our wildest hopes and ex pectat ions THE I) EBAT i: S Each day the debates were divided into two parts. In the morning, in an open air theater before an audience of well over 2,200 people, speakers from each side would make opening statements, respond to opponent\'s remarks and then answer written questions submitted from the audience. In the afternoon, before a smaller audience in an enclosed theater, speakers would raise new issues and then respond to oral questions from the audience. While various subjects were discussed U.S Soviet relations, arms control, regional conflicts, etc two unscheduled subjects generated the greatest controversy: Nicholas Danlloff and the Soviet occupation of the Baltic States. The Baltic question surfaced immediately on the opening day of the conference in the ki?ynote address of Ambassador Jack Matlock. As Special Advisor to the President on Soviet Affairs in the National Security Council, Matlock was the official head of the U.S. delegation. He began his speech in fluent Latvian a gesture which was enthusiastically applauded by a delighted, if somewhat suprised Latvian audience. Matlock\'s opening comments contained a subtle reference to a theme that every U.S. speaker would repeat throughout the conference. Praising the Latvian people and nation. Matlock stated that "All of us who ate visiting your land, lie they Americans, Russians or others, owe you a debt a gratitude for your hospitality." It was during the Russian language portion of his spei-ch that Matlock clearly and unequivocally stated the U.S. policy toward the occupied Baltic States "The use of force and the absence of freely given consent are the reasons the United States has neverrecognized and will never recognize the legality of the forcible Incorporation of Latvia. Lithuania ml Estonia into the Soviet Union ." The statement dropped like a bomhshell on the startled audience. In an angry response. Deputy Soviet Foreign Minister Vladimir Petrovsky dismissed Matlock\'s comments as "provorat ion" and accused Ihe U.S of meddling in internal affairs\' of the Soviet Union by referring to the Latvian issue. Yet Ureal Latvians in the audience seemed intrigued by Matlock\'s statement In a written question Matlock was asked why the U.S held such a position. Matlock repeated his earlier remarks, adding that the illegal occupation of Latvia was "a fact "']

Representative_Docs: ["LATVIA'S Q3 GDP GROWTH HIGHEST IN BALTICS RIGA, 20 Dec, BNS – Latvia boasts the highest gross domestic product (GDP) growth among the Baltic states in the third quarter of 2001, reported the Latvian national statistics office. In the third quarter of 2001, Latvian GDP grew 6.3% while provisional estimates give Lithuanian GDP growth at 5.1% and Estonia 4.9%. Latvian GDP grew most rapidly also in the other two quarters in 2001. In the first quarter the growth was 8.3% in Latvia, 5.8% in Estonia and 4.4% in Lithuania, in the second quarter of this year Latvian GDP grew by 9.2%, Lithuanian by 5.7% and Estonian by 5%. Annual GDP growth in 2000 over 1999 was 6.6% in Latvia, 6.9% in Estonia and 3.9% in Lithuania.", 'LATVIA POSTS BIGGEST GDP GROWTH AMONG BALTIC STATES Latvia had the highest GDP growth among the three Baltic states in the first quarter of 2000. In Latvia the GDP grew 5.3% in the first quarter of the year, in Estonia, the figure was 5.2% while in Lithuania 4.1%. In the first quarter of the year the GDP in Latvia was 391 lats per capita, in nominal prices, while in US dollars the par capita amount was $662 in the first quarter of the year. In Latvia the GDP in nominal prices was 947.613 million lats ($U51.579 bin) in the first quarter of the year. According to the Lithuanian statistics department, in Lithuania the GDP in the first quarter of the year was 9.997 billion litas (SUS 2.49 bin). According to the statistics office, the GDP growth by 5.3% in Latvia indicated the return of stability to the Latvian economy. Increasing output in services (up by 5.7%) and the industrial sector (up by 3.1%) also contributed to the upward movement. Wholesale and retail trade, repair of automobiles, motor-cycles and household appliances increased by 6.1 % and was 18.8% of the GDP. The wholesale turnover in actual prices grew by 24% in the first three months of 2000 over the same period last year while retail turnover saw a 5.6% growth year-on-year. The output in transportation, warehousing and communications branch increased by 6.3% and accounted for 16.8% of the GDP. Real estate, lease and commercial operations saw a 12% rise and constituted 8.7% of the GDP in Latvia from January to March 2000. Industry was able to stop the downward slide and achieve a 2.8% growth of output, accounting for 14.8% of the GDP. Output in the extracting industry and mining grew by 55% in the first quarter of this year. Forestry, one of the main Latvian export branches, has developed rapidly and posted a 37% growth year-on-year. In 1999 Latvian GDP increased by 0.1% over 1998. The GDP growth was 3,9% in 1998 over 1997 and 8.6% in 1997 over 1996. in the first quarter of 1999 the GDP dropped 1.5% over the respective period in 1998. in the same period in 1998 the GDP growth was 9.5% but in 1997 the increase was 5%. (SUS 1 = LVL 0.600, $US 1 = LTL 4)', "LATVIA'S GDP GROWTH EXPECTED TO EXCEED 5% IN THE SECOND QUARTER Latvia's gross domestic product (GDP) in the second quarter of 2002 is expected to have been higher than in the first quarter and to have grown by more than 5%. in its forecast, the Bank of Latvia indicates that the rising GDP can be attributed to industrial growth and rapidly climbing retail trade, as well as the more traditional growth sectors – construction and communications. At the same time, GDP growth will be tempered by a decline in the transit sector, notably in port and transportation operations. Although the handling of goods at the Riga port has increased somewhat, this will not offset the overall negative effects on the transit industry from the shrinking cargo turnover at Ventspils, the largest port of Latvia. GDP growth in the first six months of 2002 could be around 4.5%. This estimate, however, may have to be revised once the results of the services sector are assessed and included in the compilations. The Bank of Latvia forecast for annual GDP growth in 2002 remains unchanged at 5%. In the first quarter of this year Latvia's GDP was up 3.8% year-on-year, mostly due to an 8.9% growth of trade (accounting for 19.3% of GDP), a 6.8% growth in construction (4.2% of GDP), a 4.1% growth in commercial services (11.2% of GDP) and a 2.9% growth in processing indusby (11,2% of GDP). The growth of Latvia's GDP in the second quarter of 2001 was 9.3% whiie for the entire year it was 7.7%."]

['(By H. O. DAVIS, President of the Australian Workers* Union). I am happy to be given the opportunity to contact the Australians of Latvian origin through your weekly "Austrālijas Latvietis," and I extend my thanks to the editorship. I am sure that you, as members of the Australian community, have learnt already the environment of this country and I feel sure that you have gained the sympathetic attitude of our community. I hope that the definition of our "Australian way of life is no longer a strange one to you and that you all have found such a way—voluntarily chosen— leads to a life of fulfillment and happiness. I know that this way is for you not an easy way and that you are affected by various factors retarding you, such as the differences in language, culture and customs. Therefore you need patience to overcome these problems. As members of a find national who loves peace, freedom and independence which have been denied to you through long years of war and postwar tragedy, you have all the abilities to join our way of life and find happiness in this country. In this young Democracy, those who are willing to play their part can confidently look forward to the future and I firmly believe that the majority of you are convinced that in coming to Australia you made a very good- choice. I realise the fact that you brought with you to Australia a big amount of useful human abilities and elements of your old and rich culture of which Australia will profit. The results of your work will be Australia's harvest, your individual achievements will be of importance for the whole community, and vice versa: the fate of our people will be your fate and the situation of the working class here will be shared by you. The opportunity you have to gain better knowledge of Australia's conditions and set-up in regard to social and economical matters cap make you acquainted with the vital role played by the Trade Union Movement. Unionism enjoys here the rights and privileges granted to it by the law, This movement helped to create the industrial legislation upon which is based the system of arbitration and conciliation in relations of Labor. Owing to this, the Australian working population formed one of the most progressive methods in the whole world in preserving in dustrial peace and securing social justice. As working people you must discover in the light of your own needs (and they are also common needs) that it is your duty to support the aims of the people organised in the ranks of Trades Union Movement. Sooner or later you will become an active member of this movement. A certain OUR UNITED WAY part of your interest will be devoted to these matters. You will see that your loyalty to these principles will be appreciated by your Australian workmates. At this stage I want to stress that I am proud of being a good unionist. Everyone who knows what Unionism is must feel the same. I participate myself, in the activity of the Australian Workers' Union as its President. Probably many of you have met this organisation at your place of employment and there must be a considerable number of you already members. I The Australian Workers' Union is not only the largest industrial organisation in our country, but I also is one of the strongest pilllars of democracy and social projgress. In 70 years of existence the A.W.U. has gained unending merits for the Australian Nation. For the general welfare of this country the AjWI.U. played a benefitiary part, becoming with the time something like a symbol of the best ambitions and ideas of the working class. It eliminated the hatred in industrial relations for the reason of obtaining improvements for the workers, using the constitutional and legal way only. Carrying this objective A.W.U. gives incomparable services to our homeland. Since the times of Mr. W. G. Spence, who was the first leader of our organisation then consisting only of a few thousand shearers, the A.W.U. made great headway, increasing its membership to 180,000 and becoming the largest Union. A.W.U. embraces a big percentage of unskilled and semi-skilled laborers and with its awards and , agreement, covers many important . industries. A.W.U. disregards differentiations between members which would depend on their citizenship, racial origin, religion, etc., and advocates the spirit of solidarity and mateship among the workers. All members have the same rights and share the same benefits and protectoin of the Union. As a true democratic organisation, the A.WU's attitude towards any totalitarian system of politics has always been very clear—we hate it and we feel abomination towards such outlooks. A.W.U. describes the Communists as the most dangerous parasite and enemies of this democratic Commonwealth. On the other hand, "There is no Union in this Commonwealth which has been attacked as consistently by the Communists and other anti-Labor organisations as has the A.W.U." I am quoting now the words of Mr. Tom Dougherty, General Secretary of the Australian Workers' Union. Under the rules of the A.W.U., a Communist cannot become an officer of the Union. This prevents them from putting intrigues into organisatioal life. The A.W.U. is aware how the devilish conspiracy of Communism prepares its treacherous attack against the whole Free World and against the Christian principles and human values of our life. You know from your very own experience what I mean; you knowhow the Communists robbed many countries of its independence and freedom, placing instead with the use of blood-thirsty terror the extreme slavery, hunger and misery and terrible exploitatoin of the workmen to such extent which was unknown in the past history of mankind. The country where you came from was one of the first victims of this menace. Now in Australia, by telling the pure truth of the happenings there, you give a loyal service to the cause of democracy. Your persoal sufferings under this system in the past, and the suffering of millions of subjugated people today in the Communist dominated countries is a timely warning for the urgent need of tigtening our ranks and strengthening of our will for defence of our country in order to establish the security of our life. A.W.U., through its friendly understanding given to its New Australian members, has a very large number of friends among them. Knowing the right political orientation of the A.W.U. many from our new friends became interested in the Australian Labor Party which represents the political interests of wage and salary earners. The history of the Labor Movement in Australia is very largely bound up with the Australian Workers' Union. It is a sincere wish of the A.W.U and myself that you feel happy here and that you become one of us, sharing with us the love and loyalty to our sunny land of the future, where you and your children have a good opportunity to build up a better tomorrow. And this is our UNITED way we have to go.', 'Latvia’s 71st Anniversary Celebration

The vicepresident of the Latvian Federation of Australia and New Zealand, Mr Vidvuds Koskins, hosted a reception on the 16th November at the Latvian Centre on the occasion of the 72nd Anniversary of the declaration of independence of the Republic of Latvi a. The reception was attended by members of the Australian Latvian community and invited representatives of the wider Australian community in excess of 100 people. Mr Paul Elliott, MP, Federal Member for Parramatta, representing the Prime Minister, the Hon. Bob Hawke A.C. Mr Philip Ruddock, MP, Shadow Minister for Immigration and Ethnic Affairs representing the Leader of the Opposition, Dr John Hewson. The Hon. Sir Adrian Solomons, MP, Deputy President and Chairman of Committees, Legislative Council, represen ting the Premier, the Hon. Nick Greiner. Alderman Eve Dutton representing the Mayor of Strathfield. Alderman Raftan, representing the Mayor of Burvvood Aid. Geof Howe. Mr Aivars Mednis, Chairman Baltie Council of Australia. The reception was addressed by Mr Paul Elliott, Mr Philip Ruddock, The Hon. Sir Adrian Solomons, Mr Kevin Moss and Mrs Tiiu Kroll-Simmul who also proposed a toast to the Republic of Latvia. The four parliamentary Leaders had sent messages with their respective representatives. Message from the Prime Minister It gives me great pleasure to send my greetings to members and friends of the Latvian Federation of Australia and New Zealand on the occasion of the 72nd Anniversary of the Declaration of Indepen dence of the Republic of Latvia. Latvian-Australians are a long established community within our society, well noted for their enterprise and initiative, which are reflected in the signifieant contribution they have made to the

economic life of our country. As well they have ķept up their strong cultural traditions which further enrich our multicultural society.

While you have settled so successfully into your Australian lives it is natūrai that you have also retained an interest in Latvia. The momentous changes that have taken place in Eastern Europe over the last year have led to a fundamenta! transfor mation in East-West relations. Australia has recognised for many years the right of Latvia and the other Baltie States to exist as independent nations. Like many other vvestern governments, Australia has refused to accept in International law their incorporation into the Soviet Union in 1940, rejecting self-proclaimed Soviet sovereignty overthe m. I am heartened by prospects of negoti ations betvveen Moscow and the Baltie States, and I hope they will lead to a settlement which will meet the aspirations of the Baltie people for economic and political independence. I look forvvard to a just and peaceful resolution of this long standing issue. I congratulate you on this important occasion and join with you in the hope that the future will bring security and prosperity, not only for members of the Latvian community here in Australia, but also for families and friends in your former homeland.

R. J. L. HAWKE']

['LATVIAN BUDGET COLLECTS 341 MILLION LATS IN 1995

The Latvian national budget collected 314 million lats in 1995, State Income Minister

Aija Pocatold reporters. "Adding 16.2 million lats that have been channelled to the special health care budget, the total revenue can be estimated at 357.2 million lats," Ms Poca said. In 1994, the budget eamed 328.8 million lats, which is 8.85% less than in 1995. The most important source of income was the value added-tax that accounted for 219.8 million lats in 1995, vvhich is 45.7 million lats more than in 1994. Excise tax income amounted to 52 million lats lastyear, vvhich is 2.2 times more than in 1994, said Poca. 46.8 million lats was paid in corporate income tax last year as com pared to 75.8 million lats in 1994. The big difference betvveen these figurēs can be ex plained by many companies making advance payments of this tax in 1994. The State budget also collected individual income tax worth 131.5 million lats. Unfortunately, Poca said, the basie tax arrears increased to a total of 67.7 million lats during the year.', 'What shape is the Postal Service in? It's hurting. With more and more personal and business communication being conducted via e-mail and socialnetworking sites, mail volume peaked at 213 billion pieces in 2006 and has been declining ever since. The recession has only made a difficult situation worse. Mail volume declined by 25.6 billion pieces this year, or almost 13 percent – more than double any decline in the history of an institution founded before the American Revolution and first led by Benjamin Franklin. Volume is projected to fall by another 11 billion pieces next year. "Simply put," says Postmaster General John Potter, "the Postal Service is in acute financial crisis." How much money is it losing? Losses this year alone totaled $3.8 billion, and by the end of next year, total agency debt is expected to reach $13 billion. The Postal Service's fiscal straits would be even more dire if not for a 2 cent hike on the first-class stamp this year and $6 billion in cost reductions. The quasi-governmental agency has cut 260 million work hours in the past decade and even eliminated 200 000 of its iconic blue mailboxes. But with $80 billion in annual expenses, cuts haven't kept pace with the decline in revenue. "The The U.S. Postal Service loses more money every year. Is there a future for ' snail mail*? business model, quite frankly, is broken," says USPS Chief Financial Officer Joseph Corbett. Why are expenses so high? Labor, mostly. The Postal Service, the nation's secondlargest employer after the federal government itself, employs 630 000 full-time workers with generous benefits packages, as well as tens of thousands of contract and parttime employees. Compensation and benefits account for 80 percent of expenses, compared with about 50 percent at FedEx and UPS. In addition, the Postal Service runs a vast infrastructure of 32 000 post offices and thousands of other retail and processing centers – more outlets than Starbucks, McDonald's, and WalMart combined. Indeed, it has enough capacity to handle a 100 percent increase in mail. "The Postal Service urgently needs to restructure," says Phillip Herr, a government analyst. It's trying. The agency offered early retirement to 150 000 employees this year, but only a fraction accepted. It also targeted 700 post offices for closure, but closing offices has proved to be nearly impossible. Why can't it close post offices? Politics. Although the Postal Service earned a measure of independence in 1971, when it was made a financially selfThe shrinking post office sustaining agency, many operational changes, including branch closures, require congressional approval. There are 2000 post offices that serve fewer than 100 customers each, and the USPS would love to shut them down. But each office is located in someone's congressional district. "Congress puts up roadblocks whenever the Postal Service even mentions that it might be time to close or consolidate some facilities," says Delaware Sen. Tom Carper. The agency also could save as much as $3.5 billion a year by eliminating Saturday mail delivery, but that, too, would require the approval of reluctant politicians. People depend on regular mail delivery and would be greatly inconvenienced by missing a day's delivery," says Rep. Jose Serrano of New York, who chairs a subcommittee that oversees the USPS. With even modest adjustments being politically fraught, prospects for far reaching reforms, such as those enacted in other countries, seem even more remote. What kind of reforms? Germany's Deutsche Post has been completely privatized, as has mail delivery in Japan. Some foreign postal services have improved their financial standing by offering additional services; Poste Italiane sells insurance and other financial services, for instance, while Japan Post operates a savings bank. In the U.S., some post offices have started selling greeting cards in an effort to raise revenue. But the Postal Service is forbidden from offering banking and insurance services, and given the political clout of those two industries, it's doubtful that will change. As for privatization, many policymakers fear that if mail delivery were freed of any government role, rural and poor areas would be neglected and millions of Americans would lose a lifeline. So does the Postal Service have a future? Clearly, something has to give. The Postal Service will have to accelerate its painful downsizing, and complete privatization can't be ruled out. The fact is, with e-mail now übiquitous, Americans simply don' t need the Postal Service as much as they once did, and it often seems that most of what arrives in the mail every day is junk anyway. That sense has been confirmed by a Seattle company that has carved out an unusual niche: It e-mails scanned images of unopened envelopes to its customers, who then decide which ones to open and which to have shredded. Customers shred 90 percent of their mail without reading it. Such indifference to the vast majority of mail leaves postal officials anxious about the future and desperate for solutions. "Urge the public to mail a letter to a loved one and do it weekly," says William Burris, president of the Americans Postal Workers Union. "That would help." A demoralized force In the 1980s and 90s, a series of shootings by postal workers launched the phrase "going postal." Since then, management has been keenly sensitive about worker morale, which by many accounts has taken a beating in recent months. With a longterm hiring freeze in place, employees have seen hours reduced, delivery routes consolidated, and their efficiency meticulously monitored by a management desperate to cut labor costs. "Many of our carriers hate coming into work," says Robert McLennan, a union leader in Buffalo. Postal workers are "frenzied with worry," says Tom Mullahey, a postal worker in Jersey City. "You have different rumors every day, it's like a roller coaster." The Postal Service said such reactions are not surprising, considering the circumstances. "The stress and tension are out there," says Postal Service spokeswoman Karen Mazurkiewicz. "Everyone is under a magnifying glass." THE WEEK December 4, 2009.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER THE FIRST 100 DAYS OF THE NEW GOVERNMENT On March 5, the Prime Minister of Latvia Vilis Krištopans gave a speech concerning the first hundred days of his government. This speech, however, was criticized for the lack of copcrete examples of what the new government has accomplished. Most of the politicians were of the opinion that the greatest acheivement by the government is that the coalition partners in Saeima are still together. THE BUDGET OF 1999 FINALLY APPROVED On February 25, with 60 votes for and 40 against, the budget of 1999 was finally approved by the Parliament of Latvia. The fiscal deficit is going to be 113 million Lats which is 3% of the national Growth Domestic Product (GDP). 3% of the deficit in the budget is the highest percentage allowed by the European Union. Many financiers including the former Minister of Finance Roberts Zīle and the President of the Bank of Latvia Einārs Repše are very skeptical about such a high deficit. A NEW CANDIDATE FOR THE POST OF THE PRESIDENT The Peoples party (Tautas partija) has announced their candidate – Vaira Paegle for the Presidential office. She is a former Chairwoman of the Board of the World Federation of Free Latvians and an active member of the American Latvian Association. So far she is the second officially announced candidate for this office and the only women who has ever run for that office. FORMER KGB AGENTS NOT ALLOWED TO WORK FOR THE STATE'S SECURITY OFFICE The Latvian Parliament Saeima has supported the State's Security Office's changes in the Law that forbids the former KGB agents and secret agents of the intelligence services of other states to work for the office. The only exception to this law is if a person's professional knowledge is essential to Latvia and there is no one else in the country with that kind of knowledge. In that case the Safety police will issue a permit so that that person can work in the Security Office for a certain period of time. APRIL 1999 NEWS FROM LATVIA – reported by Šarlote Līduma in Riga RUSSIANS PICKETING AGAIN On March 3, the mostly Russian organization Līdztiesība (Equality) held a picket in Rīga. Last year a similar picket was held on the same date, after which the relations between Latvia and Russia drastically deteriorated This year the demonstrators were holding signs with predominantly social demands as opposed to last year's which were mainly political in nature. The Rīga City Council permitted the picket because of the request by the leaders of Līdztiesība who claimed that a Russian man named Vladimir Artemjev had died right after the picket last year. To honor this man Līdztiesība invited last year's picket participants to gather in commemoration of Mr. Artemjev. Līdztiesība also claimed that the Latvian police officers who were trying to keep the demonstrators off the street were responsible for his death. However, according to Artemjev's death certificate, he had died not on March 3rd last year, but at the very end of March. RUSSIA'S UNOFFICIAL VETO The materials released by the Congress of the United States show that Russia has de facto imposed veto on Baltics entering NATO. The recent studies done by the Congress show that most of the NATO member-countries consider maintaining good relations with Russia to be their priority, which probably means that the Baltic countries will have to wait for a while before NATO welcomes them. 40,000 DONATED LATS LOST DUE TO THE NEW BANK CRISES The Fund for the reconstruction of the Freedom Monument announced that the first 100,000.-Lats were donated towards the reconstruction. However, due to the crises of the bank called Rīgas Komercbanka, the Fund has lost 43,000.-Lats of the donated money. The Board of the Fund has written a petition asking the President of the Bank of Latvia (Latvijas Banka) to release the Fund's money currently frozen in Rīgas Komercbanka. TRAGEDY IN THE CITY OF GULBENE On February 22, a Gulbene resident sneaked into a kindergarten during the nap-time and killed three children as well as one of the teachers. Later he was caught by the police trying to leave the town. The man admitted to the police that his goal was to become famous and that the best way to do it is to become a serial killer. For a homicide with cruelty according to the laws of Latvia he can be put in jail from 10 to 20 years. The killer theoretically could also be sentenced to death, yet the President of Latvia Guntis Ulmanis granted a moratorium on the death penalty in 1996. Since then no death penalty has been carried out. The most recent statistics show that the population of Latvia is not ready for abrogating the death penalty, especially after this tragedy, where little children were killed. 68% of the respondents are in favor of the death penalty, 33,5% of the respondents are considering the capital punishment as absolutely necessary; only 5,9% of the respondents are absolutely against it. The statistics also show that the most active supporters of the capital punishment are ethnic Latvians. Within one year, the number of supporters of the capital punishment has increased by 1%, but the number of those who are against it has decreased by 4%. DOMESTIC ANIMALS DECREASING The Central Bureau of Statistics in Latvia informs that the number of domestic animals in Latvia tends to decrease. Statistics show that in January of this year there were 434,400 domestic animals, but a year ago – 478,200. In January 1997 there were more than 500,000 domestic animals registered in Latvia. The amount of swine has decreased by 1.9%, domestic fowl by 9.6% and sheep by 27.9%. The number of horses has decreased by 1,000. A LATVIAN BAND WINS THE BBC CONTEST In mid February, a Latvian rock-band "No Respect" won the BBC World Service award "The Next Big Thing". The reward for the group is a tip to London to make a sound track for their CD. ENGLISH SOCCER TEAM WANTS A PLAYER FROM LATVIA One of England's oldest soccer clubs Southampton has signed a contract with a soccer player from the Club Skonto of Riga – Marian Pahar. Southampton has paid 800,000 British pounds for the player, which is the largest amount of money to date paid to recruit a soccer player from Latvia. The British Immigration office, however, has refused to issue a work permit to the player and the deal has been postponed. NEW COIN A teacher from the village of Taurene has won the contest announced by the Bank of Latvia to design a draft for the new 10 Lat coin. The idea to make a new coin came form the international program Millennium which encouraged the Central Banks of Europe to make new coins which would be unique to the country's monetary collection. Maija Bārzdiņa's idea was simple – to make money which will be easy to keep. She designed a coin which is also a button, so the economically-minded people will be able to sew the coin button on their clothes. The new coins are currently minted in England and will be put into circulation the coming fall. It is interesting to note that Ms. M. Bārzdiņa decided to make this sketch because she was unable to sleep on the night when the contest was advertised in the newspaper. A WORLD PAGEANT HELD IN LATVIA In mid February, the President of Mrs. Globe Association Tracy Campbell visited Rīga. She was welcomed by Mrs. Globe '98 leva Bondāre. During her visit Mrs. T. Campbell officially announced that this year's Mrs. Globe '99 pageant will be held in Rīga on September 10th. Mrs. I. Bondāre had the privilege of choosing and announcing the Latvian participant for the pageant. Mrs. I. Bondāre chose Leticija Pone, who was the second Vice-Mrs. 97, the year Mrs. I. Bondāre became Mrs. Latvia. MARIHUANA FOUND ON A LATVIAN TRAWLER On February 16th in the channel of La Manche, French customs officers found about twenty tons of marihuana on a trawler Nemo bearing a Latvian flag. The estimated cost of this huge amount of marihuana is about 700 million French Franks or 70 million Lats. It turned out that a Latvian company had sold the trawler to Estonia, but the formalities to register the trawler under the Estonian flag were not completed at that time. Even though the trawler officially still belongs to Latvia, all the fishermen from Latvia were released by the French customs and only two Poles and an Estonian were kept under arrest. The Latvian fishermen have returned to Latvia and are looking for jobs on different trawlers. LATVIAN ADVERTISEMENT – IMAGE OF THE MONTH An advertisement made by Latvian commercial agency Brand Sellers DDB Latvia for the TV channel LNT has been included in the prestigious magazine -Advertising Age under the heading "Image of the Month". This is the first time a Latvian advertisement gets such attention from the advertising industry's professionals. WILL THE LATVIAN STAGE PLAYS BE PRODUCED IN WESTERN EUROPE? In February a group of nine theater producers from Western Europe visited Latvia in order to find interesting stage plays to produce for the Avignon Festival for the year 2000. The Festival of Avignon with the help of European Union is going to make a new program – Western European Producers Producing Eastern and Central European Stage Plays in Western Europe. A program called THEOREM is specifically designed to get acquainted with the stage plays of the Baltics. During their visit, the producers watched several stage plays at different theaters in Rīga. The main criteria for choosing a stage play to produce in Western Europe is a new and individual approach to directing. The directors from the Jaunais Rīgas teātris (New Rīga Theater) are the most promissing candidates for the project. INTERNET USERS IN LATVIA According to the ADM group official statistics, 67 million people world-wide have been using Internet last year. Estimated figures show that there are between 80,000 and 100,000 regular internet users in Latvia. It is rather peculiar that 56,5% are using the Microsoft Internet Explorer and only 41,8% various versions of Netscape. EUROS INSTEAD OF LATS The President of the Bank of Latvia Einārs Repše in an interview with the British broadcasting company BBC has announced that Latvia will possibly give up its national currency in order to introduce the new money of European Union – Euro even before Latvia enters the EU. LATVIAN GOVERNMENT GRANTS MONEY TO THE VICTIMS OF THE HOLOCAUST The government of Latvia has decided to grant 25,000 USD to the Fund for the victims of Holocaust in London, England. The Fund was founded in 1997 after the conference "The gold of the Nazi's" was held in London. The means of the fund are distributed to the victims of Holocaust. IMPORTED BEERS Last year the amount of imported beer has increased dramatically – by 345%! Most of the beer imported to Latvia comes from Estonia (74%), followed by Germany (4.3%), Lithuania (4%), Netherlands (3.2%), Czech Republic (2.7%), Denmark (2.7%) and USA (1.6%). If this trend persists, the local bear producers will be in seriously jeopardy. The percentage of imported bear sold in Latvia has grown from 4% in 1997 up to 11% in 1998. THE NEW CHAIRMAN OF THE RĪGA LATVIAN SOCIETY ELECTED After a tragic car accident took the life of the former Chairman of the Society Pēteris Pētersons, the Rīga Latvian Society for a short while was without a leader. In February the elections for the position of the Chairman of Society were held and the movie director Jānis Streičs became the new leader of the Society. THE RICHEST LATVIAN POLITICAL PARTIES. According to the data of The Center for Social and Religious Affairs at the Ministry of Justice the political parties that have made it into Saeima have the largest budgets. During the fiscal year of 1998 the Peoples Party's (chairman Andris Šķēle) returns were 672,404 Lats, while expenditures were 644,932 Lats. Latvijas Ceļš has 651,599 Lats in returns and 670,339 Lats in expenditures. Tēvzemei un Brīvībai – 381,000 Lats in returns, and 378,000 in expenditures. Jaunā Partija has 500,000 Lats in returns and 335,000 in expenditures. Most of the expenditures have been spent for the 1998 Parliament election campaign. A CONVICTED BOXER MAKES A LATVIAN HOSPITAL HAPPY A children's hospital in Latvia turned out to be a winner after the country court of Tiergarten (Germany) pronounced a verdict to Graciano Rocky Rockidjani – the former light heavy-weight boxing World champion. The judge read the sentence which states that Rocky has to pay 10,000.- DM to a children's hospital in Latvia. The German newspapers don't say as to why the judge has come up with such an unusual sentence. DONHUAN-ATLAST Despite the financial difficulties that were constantly experienced by the production team of Mozart's Don Huan, the premiere finally was staged at the National Opera. Unfortunately most of the ideas regarding the stage-set of the show had to be altered or abrogated altogether, again – due to financial difficulties. Much of what the set consists of is borrowed from already existing sets from other operas. The cast of Don Huan includes such young stars as Mindaugas Zemaitis from Lithuania, Yelena Voznesenskaja from Moscow's Big Theatre, Pauls Putniņš, a student from Trinity College in London and others. OĻEGS MAĻUHINS WINS ONE OF THE SECTIONS OF THE WORLD CUP A biathlonist from Latvia, Oļegs Maļuhins took the first place in 10 km distance of the World Cup in biathlon. This is the first victory of any Latvian biathlonists in the competitions of such high caliber. RECORD SNOW IN RĪGA On February 25, Rīga was literally buried under the snow. It was snowing non-stop for 37 hours and the layer of snow on the ground grew to 49 centimeters. The previous record registered in 1940 was one centimeter less. INFLATION IN FEBRUARY In February in comparison to January the inflation rate has increased by 0.2%. In the first two months of the year the inflation has increased by 1.4% altogether, (the inflation of the whole last year was only 2.7%)']

['WAR CRIMES ACT: HIGH COURT DECISION AWAITED

The High Court’s hearing into the constitu tional validity of the “war crimes” legislation has run for the allotted three days in Canberra. Judge ment is not expected until the newyear. Lawyers representing Ivan Timofeyevich Polyukhovich who is charged with a series of offences under the War Crimes Act will consider whether to apply for a stay of the (separate) criminal proceedings against him in South Aus tralia. The first man to be charged under the Act, Mr. Polyukhovich, 74, an Adelaide pensioner, has challenged the constitutional validitv of the legisla tien. He is alleged to have murdered 25 people and been knowingly concerned in the deaths of up to 850 Jews in Ukraine during the war. Mr. Polyukhovich is on $20,000 bail and re covering in an Adelaide hospital after allegedly trying to commit suicide. The criminal case against the Byelorussian migrant cannot be decided until the constitutional validity ofthe legislation has first been determined by the High Court. Should that body strike down the legislation on constitutional grounds, the criminal case against Mr. Polyukhovich would disappear With the agreement of ali the parties involved, the Commonwealth, as a defendant, addressed the court first, rather than counsel for Mr. Polyukhovich, as plaintiff. Dr. Griffith, the Commonwealth Solicitor-G en eral, said the legislation had been enacted validly by Parliament, using the constitutional power to legislate in the area of external affairs. Australia was meeting its international obligations and was implementing resolutions and recommendations of the United Nations General Assembly through the war-crimes legislation. By enacting the legislation, Australia was dis charging its obligations and rights under “custom ary international law” to meet an “international concern” about war crimes and crimes against humanity.

Counsel for Mr. Polyukhovich, Stephen Charles, QC, argued that the War Crimes legisla tion was “beyond power” because it could not be based on the defence power so long after the end of World War II; nor on the influx of criminals power, because the plaintiff was nota convicted criminal; nor on the external affairs power because there is no International treaty, covering war crimes, to which the federal government is obliged to adhere. Mr. Charles said the Act provided for prosecu tion of Australian residents who had committed crimes under international law as it stood between 1939 and 1945. He said the Act went beyond war crimes as defined atthetime. It “substantially increased” the area of criminality under international lawand“substan tially reduced” the necessary connection with a war or occupation. The Act prosecuted the offence of genocide. “It is our submission that genocide, abomina ble though ali reasonable persons would regard it, was not, before 1945, a crime at international law,” he said. The federal government unit investigating al leged war criminals living in Australia must finish operations by the middle of 1992 after a direction from the Attorney-General’s Department. TheSpecial Investigations Unit, which isasso ciated with the War Crimes Act, will be provided with funds to investigate before June 1992, with limited resources for up to five other cases. The Attorney-General, Mr. Duffy, said that there had been morethan 650 allegations since the unit was established in May 1987 but the investi gations could not last indefinitely. The age of potential def endants and witn esses was an important factor in prosecutions. News Weekly, September 15,1990', 'Šht J&ril THE GLOBE AND MAIL, TUESOAY, MARCH 4,1986 CANADA'S NATIONAL NEWSPAPER SOVIET VILLAINS OMITTED A flaw in Canada's search to uncover war criminals Crosbie: put matters to rest. BY RON VASTOKAS and LUBOMYR LUCIUK Mr. Luciuk is a post-doctoral fellow at the University of Toronto. Mr. Vastokas teaches anthropology at Trent University. [|L I 1979, Joe Clark, who was then I \| Prime Minister, set up a task force I I on alleged war criminals in Canada. One conclusion of the task force report, prepared by Martin Low, was that "evenhandedness would require that any policy to deal with 'war criminals' should apply to any person regardless of where or when the wrongdoing took place." Inexplicably, the present Government ignored this recommendation when It set up the Commission of Inquiry on War Criminals in February, 1985, limiting its mandate to the investigation of war criminals related to the atrocities of Nazi Germany. To Canadians of East European background, the decision to investigate only alleged Nazis is perceived as selective, even arbitrary. In their view, the Soviet Union is as guilty of genocide as Germany, with which it signed a non-aggression pact in 1939. In the Baltic area, executions, massacres and deportations began during the first Soviet occupation in 1941 and continued when the Soviet army returned in 1944. In Lithuania, some 600,000 men, women and children, a quarter of the population, were shipped out of die country in sealed freight cars and, in the words of Soviet dissident author Alexander Solzhenitsyn, "tipped like dung into the frost-bound soil." A similar fate met half a million Estonians and Latvians and, in proportionately greater numbers, the people of Ukraine. The Soviet secret police, die NKVD, masterminded these mass arrests and executions, set up the concentration camps and even pioneered the use of gas chambers, at Vorkuta in 1938. The NKVD was responsible for die massacres at such places as Katyn, Vinnytsia and Lvov, where a total of some 30,000 Poles and Ukrainians were executed. Together with SMERSH killer battalions, the NKVD also liquidated many of the more than two million people forcibly repatriated to the Soviet Union in 1945 by the United States, Britain and Canada. None of the men responsible for these atrocities has ever been brought to justice. Despite these facts, the Ukrainian Canadian Committee and other East European organizations publicly expressed their support for the decision to establish the Deschenes Commission, believing that the Government would bring all war criminals, regardless of background, to trial in Canadian courts. Unfortunately, this position was not given much attention by the Canadian media. The way the mandate has been interpreted has not allowed the introduction of evidence on Soviet war crimes, although there are lists of alleged Soviet war criminals available and some of them may well be living in Canada. Since the Soviet Union and Nazi Germany were allies from August, 1939, to June, 1941, when many of the atrocities were committed, Uiese lists should be investigated. The contention of Soviet spokesmen that only Nazi Germany can be held accountable for crimes of genocide, or that evidence of Soviet crimes against humanity is "fabricated by Fascist reactionary emigrēs," is nothing more than disinformation. Meanwhile, little thought or consideration seems to have been given to the concerns of the East European community by those who set up the inquiry, despite the fact that Mr. Low's report said, "most, if not all, tfie persons against whom accusations (of complicity in Nazi war crimes) have been made came originally from Eastern Europe." There were no consultations with any of the East European communities before the commission was set up. The question of how many Nazi war criminals might be in Canada appears to have been significant in creating the inquiry. Justice Minister John Crosbie, at a press conference announcing the commission, remarked that "they say there are 3,000 (war criminals) in Canada and we say, no, as far as we know there's no more tban 30 or 40. Many groups do not believe the federal estimates ... so by having this commission ... this should put the matter to rest." Regardless of numbers, many East European community leaders would agree with Professor Irwin Cotler, counsel for the Canadian Jewish Congess, that "if there is one Nazi living in Canada, that is too many," since many of their people also perished in the Nazi concentration camps. The position of David Matas, counsel for the B'nai B'rith, that "all war criminals .. . should be brought to justice" is even more acceptable, since all Canadians would agree that one Soviet war criminal living in Canada is also one too many. However, the selective mandate of the commission, its focus on Eastern Europeans, particularly Ukrainians, the unsubstantiated allegations about thousands of war criminals to be found in these communities, and the decision to seek Soviet evidence of Nazi war crimes, have engendered a perception among many Canadians that die entire process is little more than a witch-hunt. A bitter controversy has also been fanned in the Jewish, Baltic and Ukrainian ethnic press. A full-page advertisement in The Globe and Mail last Sept. 28, based on die Martin Low Report and asking, "Why Discriminate?" evoked a harsh reply in a Canadian Jewish News editorial. It charged that Canadians of East European origin and descent were "harboring" and "protecting persons in their midst who are accused of murder." A November issue of Ukrainian Echo contained a countercharge which read, in part: "You know and we know that a number of Jews worked willingly with the Soviets ... when will we see your community admit to this?" The tragic irony of all this is that the leading participants in the debate, Canadian communities of East European and Jewish origin, have both suffered repression and genocide. All came to this country to build new lives. Yet suggestions that an accord be reached between these groups have been frustrated by the anger generated by media coverage, which has revived old charges. For example, according to Rabbi W. Gunther Plaut, one particularly sore point in Ukrianian-Jewish relations is "the accusation by many Jews that a Ukrainian SS division took part in vicious butcheries of Jews" in the Second World War. This claim has been made before. In 1950, the Canadian Jewish Congress said it possessed "actual documentary proofs" of the Galicia Division's involvement in war crimes. The Ukrainian Canadian Committee asked that these documents be produced. They were not. The controversy surfaced again last year and all the familiar innuendos were repeated. At least this issue has been resolved, for as Yves Fortier, counsel for the Commission on War Criminals, noted on Oct. 3: "I am pleased to put on the record ... that if the only allegation against a resident of Canada is that he was a. member of the Galicia Division, that is not an individual that we consider should be made the subject of an investigation." If the mandate had not been so selective and if careful attention had been paid to the complexities of this issue by the media, then the controversy that now envelops this inquiry might have been avoided or diminished. Again, Mr. Low's report presciently said that "there is ... a serious problem of perception and definition which would have to be resolved in the development of any policy to deal with 'war criminals' in Canada."
