Representative_Docs: ['ANDREW WITER. M P. PARKDALE-HIGH PARK EXTERNAL AFFAIRS—REQUEST FOR LISTING OF HONORARY BALTIC CONSULS Mr. Andrew Witer (Par kdale—High Park): Mr Speaker, on April I i directed a question to the Secretary of State for External Affairs (Mr Clark) with regard to the listing of honorary Baltic consuls in the External Affairs directory of diplomatic, consular and other representatives in Canada. The Minister responded that the question was under review by his Department. This matter has been in limbo since 1972 and clearly should have long since been resolved. For 10 years prior to 1972 the consuls were listed in the directory and it was only on the death of the acting Honorary Consul General of Estonia that year that the listing of the consuls were removed from the directory. The federal Government has received countless representations from the Estonian Central Council in Canada, the Latvian Federation in Canada, the Lithuanian Canadian Community, the Captive Nations Committee in Canada and the Baltic Federation, yet their efforts have produced no results Despite Canada\'s de jure nonrecognition of the illegal occupation of the Baltic states of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania by the Soviet Union, the Baltic consuls are still not listed as the representatives of the pre-Soviet occupation, freely and democratically elected governments of the Baltic States. Over the years there have been many answers given as to who is responsible for the listings. On different occasions the Baltic communities have been informed thai the listing is at the discretion of the Secretary of State for External Affairs, that the responsibility lies with the Parliament of Canada in persuading the Secretary of State for External Affairs to list the Ba/tic consuls, that the question of listing of Baltic consuls must be decided by the Cabinet, and that the question of listing the Baltic consuls should be decided by the inner ( \'binet. With all these conflicting responses, it is small wonder that the Baltic communities are left confused and concerned as to how this matter can be resolved when it cannot even be established who is responsible. In the past, two Secretaries of State for External Affairs have commissioned reports to study the issue, neither of which were ever made public. The Minister\'s assurance that the issue is under review can therefore be of little comfort to the Baltic communities. In discussions with representatives of the Department of External Affairs, representatives have been told that the listings would have a negative effect on "other areas". Such statements have led to the conclusion that the Department of External Affairs is concerned that the listing of consuls would anger the Soviet Union and. consequently, allows that fear to determine our policy on this important matter. There are those in our Department of External Affairs who advocate that Canada should not do anything to ruffle the feathers of the US S R They always recommend the path of least resistance and are proverbial fence-sitters. Such a position is an abrogation and betrayal of our principles as a free and democratic sovereign nation. For us to appease a morally bankrupt Soviet regime, whose only trump? are a mighty military apparatus, an aggressive stance toward the rest of the world, and their traditionally arrogant rhetoric, would constitute a betrayal of all those who gave their lives for freedom and democracy, all those who came to this country believing that Canada was a country which stands for fundamental freedom and human rights, including respect of territorial integrity and the right of self-determination. If Canada continues to sit on the fence by refusing to list Baltic consuls, the Soviets will tell the people of the Baltic States that while Canada pays lip service to the principle of withholding de jure recognition of the captivity of their countries, in practice Canada has conceded Soviet sovereignty and Soviet right to enforce its illegal annexation. This is a discredit to our entire system of government. The United States already accords the Baltic consuls full diplomatic recognition It has led the way, and Australia has followed its lead by recognizing the honorary consul of the Latvian consulate. • (180)1 The time for Canada to act is now. I urge Canada to "tand up and be counted. I urge the Minister to list the Baltic consuls in the External Affairs directory of diplomatic, consular, and other representatives in Canada. In doing so, we demonstrate our continuing commitment to the principles and purpose of United Nations Charter and to the cause of peace and House of Commons Debates Tuesday, May 19, 1987 Today Kremlin\'s quislings rule in Latvia. Canada continues to reject the legality of Soviet control over the Baltic countries. freedom in the world. By our action, we will be sending out a message to oppressive regimes throughout the world that our principles are not and will not be for sale. Given that the issue of listing Baltic consuls has already undergone at least two previous reviews, and in light of the decision by Australia to accord recognition to the honorary consul for Latvia, will the Minister now agree to take advantage of the opportunity to once and for all put an end to continuous studies on this matter and restore the listing of the Baltic consuls? [Translation] Mr. Jean-Guy Hudon (Parliamentary Secretary to Secretary of State for External Affairs): On behalf of the Government, Mr. Speaker, 1 am pleased to state Canada\'s policy with respect to the Baltic States, namely Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. Every Canadian Government since 1940 has firmly refused to give de jure recognition to the forced annexation of these countries by the USSR. However Canada does give de , Adjournment Debate facto recognition to their incorporation into the Soviet (\'nion since the latter country exercises authority. The refusal of Canada to give de jure recognition to the annexation of the Baltic States goes far beyond mere words and includes measures which underscore the special status of the Baltic States as independent nations illegally annexed by the USSR during World War 11. The Government allows the acting honourary consuls of the Baltic States to continue exercising their duties in Canada, although it does not formally recognize them since honournry consuls are not appointed by a government with which Canada maintains diplomatic or consular relations These consuls are Canadian citizens and for this reason have no immunity nor diplomatic privileges. Canadian ambassadors do not travel in the Baltic States. As much as possible, other Canadian officials who do travel there avoid all contacts with local authorities. For purposes of Canadian passport and immigration documents, Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania remain acceptable as references for places of birth Since Baltic passports do not guarantee reentry into Baltic countries, Canadian legislation does not consider them acceptable for admission to Canada This is why extraordinary procedures have been adopted to clear the way for admission to Canada of Baltic representatives of other countries who come here in the discharge of their duties. These measures prove that Canada continues to reject the legality of Soviet control over Baltic countries, a fact which Soviet authorities have duly noted. The Secretary of State for External Affairs (Mr Clark) has already advised the House that he is reviewing the question of adding the names of Baltic acting honourary consuls in the publication of the Department of External Affairs entitled "Diplomatic Corps and Consular and Other Representatives in Canada" The Minister is fully aware of the views expressed in this respect by the Baltic community in Canada; he will give particular consideration to them and to the general implications of such a decision for Canada\'s foreign policy. In conclusion. Mr Speaker, I can assure the House that the Government of Canada does not intend to change this policy under which it does not give de jure recognition to the Soviet annexation of the Baltic States.', 'KEYNOTE ADDRESS. Honoured guests, Parliamentarians, fellow Canadians. It is may honour and responsibility to speak to the issue that brought us all here – the independence of Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia. Dans le monde entier, on se soubiendra de 1989 comme de l\'année de la révolution européenne. Le mur de Berlin s\'est effondre; le rideau de fer s\'est déchiré; des régimes totalitaires sont tombés; et une nouvelle Europe est née. Canadians have gathered in front of that remarkable institution of democracy and tolerance – the Canadian Parliament Buildings to express solidarity with the Baltic States where a historic struggle is taking place. Les événements remarquables survenus en Europe sont vécus très intensément par des millions de citoyens canadiens qui y ont leurs racines. Beaucoup d\'entre eux y ont encore de la famille et parlent la langue du pays. Certains ont été contraints de fuir leur patrie par des régimes qui ont depuis été renversés. La plupart ont des membres de lur famille ou des amis dont les espoirs ont été écrasés ou dont la réputation a été dimuée par ces régimes, mais qui ont aujourd\'hui la chance de refaire leur vie et de bâtir une société nouvelle dans leur pays ancestral. The Soviet Union was and continues to be an empire. The ties that bind that \'Union\' together are not the sharing of common interests and values but are the instruments of repression and armed might. It is the direct spiritual and historical descendent of the empire of the Tsars. Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania were not member Republics of the-USSR. They are colonies of the Kremlin, exploited for their natural and human wealth, their enviroment devastated by gross pollution, their cultures repressed. They did not seek membership in the USSR, nor were they asked if they wished to join. Estonia, Latvia and Lithupr t<)were bartered under the infamous Hitler-Stalin ;-act.They were then annexed by the USSR, despite treaties signed by the USSR itself in 1920, that recognizing their right in perpetuity to freedom, independence and the inviolability of their lands. That is the history. That was the reality of life in the Baltic States. To-day the president of the Soviet Union is a welcome guest of the government of Canada. We want to send a message to him and to Canadian Government. To Mr. Gorbachev we want to say – yes, you have been responsible for the great changes that have occured in Eastern Europe. The people of Poland, East Germany, Hungary, Czechoslavakia thank you. You finally came to the realization that your hold on these people and nations was hollow. Your outer empire was a shell, kept together by armed force. These nations, no matter how repressed by the imbecilities of your communirt ideology, kept alive in their hearts the warm glow of freedom. They have now reclaimed their national dignity and are reentering the mainstream of European civilization. But, what about your inner empire, your \'prison of nations\'. What do you offer them? How do you treat the Baltic States? Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania have not had the wrong committed by your predecessors righted by you. They are the only countries which have not regained their independence since the cîose of the second World War. You are well aware of, for your own congress of deputies has condemned, the 1939 Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, engineered by Hitler and Stalin. This infamous auction of peoples and countries YOU still refuse to condemn.How can you in 1990 fail to abrogate that treaty, how can fail not to condemn your government\'s association with fascist Germany? Why do you not apologize to the people of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania for your predecessors\' criminal acts, as you did over the Katyn masacres or for the invasion of Czechoslovakia or is it that your apologies have purely political purposes. Your morality is skin deep. Mr. Gorbachev, you claim to want to abide by the rule of Law. Show us! In the 1920\'s your revered Lenin, whom you still claim to follow, signed treaties with the three Baltic republics, by which the Soviet Union Would in perpetuity respect the independence of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. Do you now turn your back on Lenin? Where is your rule of Law? Ydu clearly have no respect for the rule of law, for if you did you would understand that the forced annexation of the three Baltic republics was illegal from the moment your Stalinist predecessors manipulated the votes in each of the three countries to get a predetermined result. Mr. Gorbachev, we call on you to muster your courage and repudiate the legacy of Stalin which will haunt you until you come to terms with the just claims of the Baltic States. The whole world is watching. Will you behave as the reformer you claim to be or will you recognize that, as you have done in Eastern Europe, you cannot hold a people against its will? Mr. Gorbachev, you may well be a statesman, – alas you are a paper democrat. The Western media has portrayedyou as the \'Man of the Year\' if notcentury. The real test of wether you can lay claim to such glory is how you treat the nations in your prison, how you treat the Baltic States. Freedom is not divisible, it cannot be apportioned to some and not to all. You cannot let go of your outer empire, bat keep your inner empire in chains – prison is prison. Your so-called law of secession is no more than a new set of chains, chains which you expect the Baltic States to freely put on themselves. They will not. You have already seen the determination of the people of Lithuania to be free of your festering empire. They will notsuccomb to your intimidations. They see through ycur moral hypocrisy. Even when you are offered compromise, as was the case when Latvia and Estonia were prepared to enter negotiations over de facto independence that would extend over some period of time, you rejected those compromises. You have shown the world that your love of empire is greater than your new found commitment to glasnost and perestroika. And what you understand of freedom only the ghost of Lenin knows. Make no mistake, Mr. Gorbachev, we, the Estonian, Latvian and Lithuanian communities in Canada, along with our Canadian friends will continue to bear witness to your shameful handling of the Baltic States. Our message to you is clear: END TM OCCUPATION OF ESTONIA! END THE OCCUPATION OF LATVIA! END THE OCCUPATION OF LITHUANIA! To Mr. Malroney and the Canadian government our message is also clear. While you have steadfastly maintained the non-recognition of the illegal incorporation of the Baltic States into the Soviet Union, for which the people of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania are greatful, you now have a greater task ahead of you. As a nation that values human dignity, that respects human and civil rights as it has consistently asserted in the case of South Africa, that has stood by little nations such as Israel proclaiming their right to national self-determination, the Canadian government, speaking in the name of the Canadian people, should uniquivocally tell Mr. Gorbachev that his conduct with respect to the Baltic States violates all norms of civilized discourse. Mr. Mulroney should make plain the Canadian people\'s disdain for the use of intimidation, veiled threats and the uncompromising aggressivenesss of his behaviour towards the Baltic States. History cannot again be distorted to suit the imperialist dreamers in the Kremlin. The Canadian people know the facts – the Baltic States\' independence was bartered away by two of this centuries greatest tyrants. The legacy of that barter can only be put right by the Soviet Union. Mr. Gorbachev cannot excuse himself or his government from culpability in the tragedy that occured in 1939 and 1940. Mr. Gorbachev is the inheritor of the consequences of those years. The Canadian government must impress on Mr. Gorbachev his moral responsibility toward the people of the Baltic States. He must be reminded that onethird of all Estonians, Latvians and Lithuanians were shot, sent to the Gulag and driven from their homes. He must be reminded of the forced russification, of the forced conaßlption and the forced industrialization that has wrought ecological disaster. Hie Baltic States h&ve been a democratic model of peaceful change to the peoples of the Soviet Union. They have been looked to as a test case to find out whether the parliamentary process can really work. Think of the message the suppression of the Baltic model will send to the people of the USSR. Moderation does not work. Mr. Gorbachev must be told that if he wishes the Soviet Union to join the family of civilized nations, and to be part of the common European home, he must value freedom and human dignity more than his empire. The hope of the Baltic States is not in a confrontation with the leadership of the USSR. They do not want to overthrow President Gorbachev. Their best protection is the more rapid democratization of the USSR. They want a peaceful, humane and progressive neighbour. Mais il y a loin de la coupe aux lèvres, at une période de sacrifices s\'announce qui est A la fois aura des retards et des reculs. Le gouvernement du Canada devroit faire preuve de courage, d\'imagination et de sagasse politique. The Baltic peoples have always wanted to talk. What they refuse to be is the sacrificial lambs offered to a false idol. Canadians gathered before this institution of tolerance and democracy hope you tell Mr. Gorbachev that it is time to let the BALTICS GO! Mr. Mulroney, we call on you to uphold Canadian values when you talk to the Soviet President, you must uniquivocally tell him that Canada will expect no less from the Soviet Union than its withdrawal from the republics of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. History demands it, freedom demands it, human dignity demands it, and the Canadian people demand it. Ed.', "By the right honourable Secretary of State for External Affairs, to the Baltic Federation in Canada on the occasion of the 17th BALTIC EVENING. We have been accustomed to thinking of Canada's multiculturalism exclusively in Canadian domestic terms – the variety it offers; the challenge of drawing the best qualities from different cultures; the tolerance it can teach us here at home. But there is an international dimension to Canada's multicultural character. It provides us direct access to the multitude of countries and societies from which Canadians come. Usually, that helps us understand those countries better, and we can apply that understanding in the exercise of Canada's historic international role as a moderating nation, and a leader in the practical pursuit of human rights. But it can also, in critical times, give us an influence that is not available to countries which lack our links of family and culture and language. Sometimes those links can create problems, as, for example, when foreign disputes are imported into Canada. But more often, they create opportunities, and we should act on them. Take the case of the Baltic communities in Canada. There once was some fear that the presence in Canada of citizens with origins in Central and Eastern Europe would limit our ability to encourage glasnost or perestroika. The opposite is the case. Thousands of Canadian citizens speak Estonian, Latvian, Polish, Hungarian, Serbo-Croat, Slovak or Czech. They give Canada direct and personal connections with the activists of Solidarity, nationalities of the Baltic States, or the faithful seeking to practise the principles of the  Church, or of Judaism in the Soviet Union. Those arc not obstacles to Canadian foreign policy. They are considerable assets, if we have the imagination to make the most of them. Tomorrow, as the Government continues our consideration of our response to the terrible events in China, I am convening a Roundtable of Canadian experts from outside the Government, who have direct experience in China. This will include Canadians of Chinese origin, as well as scholars, business persons, and others who have lived in that society we seek to influence. We are taking the same approach to the different changes that are occuring in the Soviet Union and Eastern and Central Europe. The Prime Minister and I seek the advice of the business leaders who are actually pursuing joint ventures in the Soviet Union. I meet directly with families, like Ala Wclfson or Ivan Shumuk who are trying to bring Refusniks or formerpolitical prisoners to Canada. And we believe there is a creative constructive opportunity to make use of Canada's human links to Europe. We must be sensible and deliberate in the way we use that asset. We must steer between old memories and new enthusiasms, and always be guided by the pragmatism that makes foreign policy effective. But we can help encourage new thinking in the Soviet Union and Eastern and Central Europe by displaying imagination here at home. That is why I come to you this evening – not just out of courtesy to a significant Canadian community, nor simply to celebrate your culture and your history. I am here to ask your help in Canada's contribution to positive and permanent changes in the Soviet Union, and in Eastern and Central Europe. This past year has been momentous in relations between East and West. It has brought elections in Poland and the Soviet Union and the promise of the same in Hungary. In January we signed the Conluding Document A KEYNOTE SPEECH. Joe Clark, to the Vienna Conference on Security and Cooperation in Europe. New conventional arms negotiations for Europe have begun. And last month in Brussels, Prime Minister Mulroney and other Heads of Government commemorated the 40th Anniversary of NATO in a Summit that set out nfy directions for the Alliance. President Bush's dramatic proposals have regained the negotiating initiative for the West. The Prime Minister's proposal of a new scholarship plan – to help sitizens of Central and Eastern Europe learn about democratic institutions – helps assure the dynamism of an Alliance that has safeguarded Western security for four decades. This is a time of profound change in the lands from which many of you and your families came, tike all change, it can be difficult and controversial, and will require both imagination and compromise. We are not spectators in these events. We are part of them – as a nation whose citizens share languages and heritages with Europe; as a country prepared to exercise our influence internationally; as a cociety whose economic system and political traditions offer encouragement and examples to those seeking changes in Europe. As we assess these changes, some of you will counsel caution, and we will want to weigh your advice carefully. Others will see opportunities that do not leap to the eyes of our officials. The developments in China are instructions about how change has been introduced in the USSR. President Gorbachev recognized early reality which has not penetrated the leadership of China economic reform cannot occur without political reform. You cannot ask people to accept the sacrifices of economic reform without also giving them a say in how it will occur. President Gorbachev knew from the outset that the command system had failed. He identified the main cause of failure – all the levers of power were being pulled by individuals protected by repressive laws and police practices. It was impossible for all but the bravest to challenge the system. There were brave challenges. We know the names of many of those heroes – Father Svarinskas, Father Tamkevicius, Mart Niklus, Petras Grazulis, Enn Tarto, Anatoly Scharansky, Josef Begun, Yuri Shukheyvich, Josep Terelya, Stepan Stepeiiak, to name only a few. We worked together – you, other Canadians, and concerned people around the world – to secure their release from prison, from exile and even from the USSR. You kept them in the public eye before there was glasnost. Their courage contributed to the progress we are now observing as glasnot and perestroika because they showed their compatriots how to retain hope, gave them the courage to resist erosion of their cultural identity. Now Soviet authorities are acknowledging that they need the cooperation, not the pasivity, of their population. To gain that cooperation, they are prepared to make concessions. How far is the Soviet leadership prepared to go? I do not know. Perhaps they do not either. But they have already permitted, and even encouraged, expressions of sentiment which no one Wpuld have dared to hope for a couple of years ago. Fundamental change has already occured. More can – and should – be expected. But change should not be so rapid as to provoke a reaction undermining all the successes of the past year. The leaders of the Popular Fronts in the Baltics have demonstrated wisdom in a volatile situation, seeking a peaceful transition to independence. We hope their efforts will be crowned with success, so that Soviet authorities will gradually accept that their best interests are served by granting maximum fredoms to the people of the Baltic States. They have to be encouraged to understand this. The arrival of new leadership in the USSR provides solid grounds for believing that the will of the people can and will be expressed, and that it will have some impact on policy. There is still much to do. But there can be no doubt that great progress has been made in the USSR towards reducing the limitations on individual freedoms. We welcome the release of political prisoners in the Soviet Union. Religious tolerance is increasing and should be enshrined in the new law on religious practice which the Supreme Soviet is expected to pass this year. The effects of this law will be keenly felt in the Baltic States and will be of key interest to us in terms of its impact on the Ukrainian Uniate Church. We will continue to urge the maximum freedom of religion for all. Already, the situation has improved beyond anything we could have expected as little as three years ago. Who could have predicted that more than 1600 new religious congregations would be registeredin 1988 ( as opposed to 104 the year before ), that Jewish cultural centres would be formed in communities around Soviet Union or that religious texts could be imported legally in to the Soviet Union? Bnigration from the Soviet Union is higher now than at any time in the past 10 years. In the first four months of this year, 57,000 exit permits were granted for emigrants. And this before the new laws on emigration have been enacted by the Supreme Soviet. We look forward to even higher figures once that legislation is approved. Canada has no unresolved family reunification cases with the USSR. Private visits from the Soviet Union to Canada have multiplied – from 5,000 in 1986 to 12,000 in 1988. President Gorbachev seems determined to create a society based on law and he is drawing on the experience of Western countries. The Soviet Supreme Court ļftsitadthe Supreme Court of Canada last autumn to discuss a wide range of legal practices, particularly the role of defence counsel. A return visit is planned. Ultimately, of course, it is not the laws but how they are implemented which counts. We will watch closely how the spirit of the new laws is observed. While the democratization process initiated by President Gorbachev is a major step forward, the results are democratic in the sense that we would use that word. But the fact is that the Soviet people were given a choice of candidates for the newly-created Congress of People's Deputies. Where no choice existed, they were given the option to reject the single candidate. This they did. The result wa6 that most of the deputies from the Baltic States were representative of local feelings and local views. That is true also of some deputies from the Ukraine. Many deputies are clearly reformers, most obviously Boris Yeltsin and Andrei Sakharov. But there are dozens more. There will be those who argue that President Gorbachev has secured for himself a compliant Supreme Soviet which will do as it is told. I do not agree. It is not a revolutionary body, but it is reform-minded. Furthermore, the voices of deputies to the Supreme Soviet, and for that matter of deputies to the Congress, will continue to be heard. The politicization of Soviet society is only beginning, but it is a good beginning. No one can fail to appreciate the difficulties facing the Soviet Union. The challenge of reforming the economy, the tragedies of Armenia and the train disaster in the Urals, the legacy of Chernobyl, and the ethnic violence of Armenia-Azerbaijan, Uzbekistan and Georgia. There is a rising tide of demands for greater recognition of the rights of nationalities. These come from around the Soviet Union, from the Ukraine, Belorussla and Moldavia, but most significantly in the Baltic States. Perhaps the most remarkable development in a unique year in the history of the Soviet Union has been the flowering of the popular movements of the three Baltic States and the acceptance of large parts of their agenda by the local Supreme Soviets. The election of popular front representatives to the Congress of People's Deputies gives them a national forum for the pursuit of their objectives. Live television coverage of the entire session of the Congress showed Baltic representatives demanding a renunciation of the secret protocols of the Moiotov-Ribbentrop Pact and independence for the Baltic States. This must have been an eye-opener for most of the country which would have been made aware of Baltic concerns for the first time. The agreement to set upa commision of the Congress to look into the Moiotov-Ribbentrop Pact was an admission, at last, that the issue must be addressed. In the meantime, the Baltic States insist on their independence, refusing to recognize Soviet all-union laws unless they have been approved in their own Supreme Soviets. They have made their languages the official languages of the Baltic States. The Baltic Assembly of Popular Movements which met in Tallinn last month will contribute to the sense of joint purpose of the three Baltic States. The outcome of the contest of wills between Baltic Supreme Soviets and Moscow is still far from clear. The aspirations of the Baltic peoples will not be met all at once. But there has been real progress. This Government applauds the efforts of the Baltic peoples to assume responsibility for their own destinies. Clasnost and perestroika provide a context in which a new reality is unfolding, from which we hope there will be no turning back. Canada continues to refuse to recognize de jure the force incorporation of the Baltic States into the Soviet Union. We give substance to that position in a number of important ways: – First, federal government Ministers and Ambassadors do not travel to the Baltic Republics. – Second, Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania remain acceptable designations as places of origin on Canadian passport and immigration documents, and – Third, as a result of a policy I initiated last year, a disclaimer now appears on all federally-produced maps of the Baltic region reiterating our policy of no de jure recognition of the Soviet annexation. Our position of principle on this matter will not change. The legitimate aspirations of the Baltic peoples must be met. The basis on which the Soviet Union incorporated the Baltic States was illegal and immoral. The situation must be put right. There is now reality in the Baltic States and together we must determine our response to it. Canada continues to recognize facto Soviet control over the Baltic States. To recognize it is not to accept its legitimacy. It is the reality. But we have a choice. We can turn our backs on the Baltic region altogether, thereby denying courageous people the encouragem* ent and contact we can offer. Or we can maintain our de_ position and work within the realm of the possible to improve the life of the people there, many of whom are family to you here tonight. Large numbers of Canadians, particularly those of Baltic origin, are seeking more extensive contacts with the Baltic States, for personal, family, cultural, sports, or commercial purposes. Many of them seek the leadership and assistance of the Canadian Government. So do members of the popular fronts whom officials of my department have met over the past few months, both in Canada and in the Baltic States. A private exchange, under which 28 Estonian businessmen are studying the capitalist market system at York University, has been established and funded by Canadians, most of Estonian origin. Joint ventures between Canadian and Baltic Interests are being pursued. There will be a demand for more exchanges to permit closer contacts and establish joint ventures, so that the people of the Baltic States can rebuild the market skills they traditionally possessed and, broaàen people-topeople contacts. Apart from the key izssues of independence and economic autonomy the Baltic States face three basic problems: the need for economic modernization and development; the threat from environmental pollution; and the need to preserve their national identities. Canada can help with some, perhaps all, of these issues. Our focus could first be commercial and economic, whether in the area of management training, joint ventures, or purely commercial transactions. Some of you are already involved in such activities and my Department is ready to help you. The enviroment may be another area where our expertise could be useful. We do not have established programs of enviromental cooperation with the Baltic States. But Canada would be prepared to share the experience we have developed in an area so crucial to the quality of life. Canadians of Baltic origins are proud to reinforce the sense of national identity of the peoples of the Baltic States. You have shown that they and their cause are not forgotten. You have maintained contacts to the limit of the possible. Now those contacts will be permitted to grow. You should take advantage of the opportunity. Whether the contacts take the form of family visits, now less difficult to arrange, or whether they are sports events, cultural, media or academic exchanges and meetings, you will strengthen the resolve and confidence of peoples whose fate concerns us all. This Government has been paying increasing attention to the Baltic States. More officials from my Department and others will travel to the region to ensure that we have the best possible understanding of developments there. We want to ensure an open two-way flow of information with you, too, for as I said, you represent an important asset in the formation of new Canadian foreign policy responses to the changing Baltic scene. My Parliamentary Secretary has been meeting with you for instance, recently addressing th* Annual Convention of Baltic Veterans League in March, and the Baltic Womens Federation in April. We will not, however, jeopardize our policy of non-recognition de jure. Our role there will be to enhance the potential for contact and communication between the people of Canada and the people of the Baltic States. Joint ventures can greatly assist the development of the market ethic, a crucial element in achieving economic progress under perestroika. Some Canadians have been quick to see the potential of such ventures and more will do so in the near future. There are already 10 Canadian-Soviet joint ventures set up since 1988 and another six are close to completion. A group of Canadian businessmen will travel to the Ukraine in October to identify possibilities there. A Canada-USSR Business Council has been formed led by such companies as Olympia and York, McDonald's Restaurante, Lavalin, Foremost, Francmaster and Alta Cenetics. More than 100 firms should be members of this organization before its first meeting in Moscow in November. More joint ventures can be expected in the Baltic States as new opportunities are identified. The training of managers and the planned folow-up undertaken by Enterprise York are precisely the kind of private initiatives we want to encourage. In November, the Prime Minister will visit the Soviet Union. He plans to take with him a delegation of business people who are keen to identify commercial opportunities. While some of them will find a ready market for Canadian products, many will be looking to the longer term. They will be particularly interested in areas which are likely to enjoy greater freedom to make economic decisions and already have skilled labour forces and a strong work ethic. They should be encouraged to look to the Baltic States. President Gorbachev has shown increasing understanding of the necessity to permit the countries of East Europe to find their own way. In the case of Poland, we have every reason to rejoice in such a decison. That country's problems will not end now that a partially democratic election has been held. But it is already apparent that the people have a new sense that, just maybe, there is hope that the Party will share power with the people and that a way can be found out of the political and economic dilemma which has faced the country for so many years. The decision by the Party in Hungary to permit multiparty elections offers hope for that country as well. Canada and the West will have to seek creatively new methods of encouraging those trends in East European countries which offer a real chance for the people to participate in the decision-making process. At the same time, we must stimulate other East European countries whose leaderships have not yet inhaled the reform spirit to acknowledge that the world has changed and they are out of step. Canada has played a constructive role in creating the atmosphere of reduce East-West tensions which in turn has contributed to greater freedoms in the USSR, in the Baltic States, and in East Europe. Whether on arms control issues, confidence building, Alliance building, or in the CSCE process, the voice of Canada carries weight and conviction. We have a reputation for telling frankly those countries in the Warsaw Pact whose human rights record is wanting, that they cannot be fully accepted as reliable members of the international community until they live up to their commitments. But we now face a new situation. Some Warsaw Pact countries are beginning to make such substantial changes that we are obliged to rethink our relationship with them. We do not forget the human rights concerns which remain to be satisfied; we do not forget the aspirations of nationalities such as those you represent. But we must recognize change where change has occured. We must seek new ways to strengthen the processes already under way We must encourage the Soviet Union and its neighbours to realize that their best interests are served by living within a family of nations, where people are ruled by governments of their own choosing, associated freely according to their own identified national interests. The musical program of the evening was provided by the internationally well known Latvian pianist Arthur Ozolins. Many senators and MPs attended the splendid event. Over a hundred of Estonians, Latvians and Lithuanians had a golden opportunity to meet them. The organizing committee had done an excellent job indeed."]

Representative_Docs: ['Heritage Tours 2006 Visit the birthplace of "Pīrāgi"! Tour the land of your ancestors! Find out for yourself why your grandparents make such a big deal about this small country on the Baltic sea! Since 1997, the American Latvian Association has organized 15 two-week educational tours of Latvia, called "Sveika, Latvija!", geared toward American Latvian young people who have graduated from the Bth grade in Latvian heritage schools. Well over 200 Latvian Americans have visited Latvia on these carefully organized tours to see first hand the land of their ancestors and the subject of their 10-year supplementary education. These trips have focused on places and things that are connected to the Latvian school curriculum and Latvian language proficiency has been emphasized. In recent years, there has been growing sentiment that the American Latvian Association should also offer English language educational tours of Latvia for young people of Latvian heritage who either do not speak Latvian, or speak and understand it only slightly. The tours would be similar to the existing "Sveika, Latvija!" programs, but tour information would be presented in English. The use of Latvian would be encouraged among participants, but not required. The program would concentrate on what is unique about Latvia, its history, culture, environment and geography. The tour might include Riga, Sigulda, Cesis, the Piebalga region, a trip to Latgale to see Aglona and a stop at a potter\'s studio, a visit to a Latvian farmstead, the Rundāle Castle, Liepāja, Ventspils, Kuldiga, to name just a few. The group would visit at least one school in Latvia during their tour to get acquainted with their counterparts in Latvia. Latvia has an amazing and complex history. It has transformed itself in its 15 years of independence since 1991. Riga itself has become an international tourist destination. Other Europeans find Latvia a great place for rural and eco-tourism and the number of tourists is growing each year. This is a great time for American Latvian teens to learn more about their Latvian roots and perhaps kindle an interest that will stay with them for the rest of their lives. The first trip "Heritage Latvia" is scheduled for July 20 – 25, 2006. The group flight will depart from Washington Dulles International Airport. A minimum of 10 participants, ages 13 – 15, must sign up by February 28, for the trip to take place. However, organizers need to guage interest for the trip as soon as possible, so that they can begin to make hotel and transportation arrangements in Latvia. Please send in the name, address, telephone number and e-mail addresses of young people who might have an interest in this trip to author of this article and trip organizer Anita Juberts, American Latvian Association, 400 Hurley Aye., Rockville, MD 20878 (tel: 301-340-8719, e-mail: projekti@alausa. org)', 'Heritage Tours 2006 Visit the birthplace of "Pīrāgi"! Please send in the name, address, telephone number and e-mail addresses of young people who might have an interest in this trip to author of this article and trip organizer Anita luberts, American Latvian Association, 400 Hurley Aye., Rockville, MD 20878 (tel: 301-340-8719, e-mail: org)', 'So, how do you spend a summer with your children if you don\'t want to, can\'t afford to, or don\'t know how you\'d spend a few summer months with your children in Latvia? How can you get them to hear and speak Latvian for a week, two, three or six? Why you can send them to a Latvian summer camp! There are a few Latvian camps, from coast to coast in the US and Canada. So for those who want a Latvian cultural "booster shot" of sorts, summer is the time to tray el and go to camp. From mid-June to mid-August Latvian parRita Pelēce, nts and their children are on the road traveling to camp in hopes that their children will make some new friends and speak a little bit more Latvian than they do at home. There is nothing quite like camp life that can forge life long memories and "forever" friendships. And many of the children do try a bit harder to speak Latvian, if only to other adults and authority figures. But most of all, camp is fun! I never attended the summer camp there, I went further up the road to the outskirts of Holland Michigan, and went to a quaint little camp called "Mežvidi". It was idellyic or at least that is how it stands out in my mind. Even as a young child of 8 I could almost see the entire camp perimeters upon entering. As we drove into the camp down the dirt road the first thing I would see was a large oak tree. It really was old, maybe Mineapole There is a lot more to being Latvian not what they call a "dizozols" in Latvia, but definitely big and old enough that it took about 3-4 of us kias to wrap our arms around it! Under the oak was a crooked bench. At the oak, the road split, but only to get around it. Once we were around the oak it was time to park the car. No one had vans or rocket-boxes back then, but a few smart Latvian families had station wagons and conversion vans to haul us kids from Minneapolis to Holland for 10-12 hours at 55 MPH, the legal highway limit at the time. "Mežvidi" or the "Middle of the Woods" had a few old buildings, a house where the girls slept up on the hill. And very cool multi-car garage that haa been converted minimally to house the boys. Now that was a cool place to sleep! Past these two buildings one could spot the flag mast, where we\'d go each momeing to "salute" the flags of Latvia and America, hear a short morning greeting by the camp director, someone\'s grandmother, I was sure at the time. But the best part was the morning greeting! Looking back it was very "scout" or "militaristic". We\'d march in a line with our cabin mates to the flag clearing and greet the director, who was always there already waiting for us and any other group whod beat us to the punch. LABRIT SORIT!" (Good morning, this morning!) was the main call, but every now and then the older boys or girls would march up they would add "Lai bikses kritf (May your pants fall down!) I couldn\'t wait until I was old enough to punch out that line! About a quarter way around the lake a traditional Latvian sauna, a "pirts", was built. It looked like it had been modeled after a pirts from a Latvian novel or play. My eyes could hardly believe it was real the first summer I saw it. It even had a well next to it, with a hanging bucket. (Though I don\'t remember that being functional. We just went to the lake with a bucket for water instead.) What I do remember is going in the pirts for the first time, and being told to leave my swimsuit on. It was a bit of a shock as I grew up going to a neighboring Latvian family\'s pirts on Saturday nights and knew that one did not wear a polyester swimsuit into the pirts. Thinking back about "Mežvidi" I really think it was the best place one could send their child of 8 or 12 for two weeks 500 plus miles from home. Then comes Garezers. I started going to Garezers when I was old enough to go to the Vidusskola, the Middle School, or High School. Vasara 2011 And frankly, it was quite shock. It was big. It was a real ha mile walk along a tar road to th mess hall. No more dirt roads The Viddusskola was separatee from the camp and it felt like its own world. Except for when w went to eat or swim. We ate i shifts, and I would see my young er sister waiting outside the mess hall as I exited for the Kantine fo treats. She was at camp, I was i Vidusskola. Never the two sha meet. At least that\'s how it fe back then. Fast forward 20 years and I\'m back in Garezers. But this time have my mother, a grandmothe now, and my daughter of 4 alone We have siened ud for 3x2 ily camp, ar so they say. Its really a language and culture camp, and three generations are able to attend. In reality, if you have infants and toddlers along, it turns into is a marathon of sorts. We wake up to the sound of Latvian music being played over the speaker system, someone talking about tne upcoming days events and a gentle push to get out of bed, and get to breakfast at the mess hall. Camp activities start at 9am and go until noon, but if you have a young child I quickly learned to drop any expectations of learning to throw a pot in ceramics or to get informed on the latest political Nometne at Garezers musings from Latvia. By the time we ate, changed a pull-up and walked up to the child-care area, I was ready for a break! (And some "real coffee!) I would drop off my daughter, and a few years later, my son, and take off back to the Vidusskola for a break. I would pick up the room, make myself a stronger cup of coffee and either nap or read until lunchtime. Because after lunch the kids went swimming and I wanted to go along and get some sun and have some fun as well. I could go on about my years of going to 3x3 as my children have grown from infant to preteen, but basically each time it\'s been über tiring and extremely fun and rewarding at the same time. Over the years I\'ve learned to just enjoy hand building with clay a famand made some new and meaningful friendships. At some point I decide I will not bring my children to Nometne at Garezers. What do I do instead? I head to the Catskills in NY state. A friend of mine there, whose children are of similar age, has summoned me there ana I am only too happy to go on this wild three day road trip with my mother and three small children. (Bless your heart mom!) I know that Katskili are smaller than Garezers only due to the fact that I attended a Latvian World Wide Scout and Girl Guide Jamboree at the age of 12. It rocked! Big woods and a small lake I could almost see from the welcome gate. It was like Mežvidi, small and cozy. I decided that it is better for my kids to be at a small camp for their first overnight camp experience and for me to hang with a dear friend for two weeks in the woods. I was right. Katskili was a great camp experience for all of us, grandma included! I do it all again the following year, only this time I look for cheap flights and sack the 3 day drive from MN to NY. Also a good choice. Fast forward another 5 years or so and I am back at Garezers again. This time to send my 12 year old extravert to Sagskola, a middle school version of camp. Not Viddusskola and not Nometne, but a new development, probably started by some smart parent-teacher-volunteer that knew the research on Middle School development. Good call. Like a typical hormonal Middle Schooler she dragged her heels into the van telling me that she never had intendedto go to camp at Garezers. My middle child is in Nometne and with a close friend from our city of Minneapolis. He is hard to please, but in general, even with a broken collar bone and arm in a sling, the boy toughs it out. The result? The two weeks were a hesitant thumbs up. Perfect. My youngest, always trying to keep up with the older two announces to me on day two of the Preschool day-amp, Bizßiz berni, that "this is for babies" and she wants to march straight over to the sleep over part of the camp. Perfect. Or is it? All is perfect, except that I have not registered her for the sleepover camp, but the day camp tor a full 2 weeks. She is only 6 after all and how could I have expected her to sleep in a cabin with girls she didn\'t know for 2 weeks? Never mind that I am in Atbalsis (a private Latvian lodging area just down the road and not a part of Garezers. In fact, people can Rita Pelēcis and Jauno Laiks editor Dainis Mjartāns at 3x3 camp in Garezers fļo to Garezers for years and never md out about Atbalsis. Its a secret resort of sorts. And quieter at night than Dzintari, an area of Garezers. Oops! Now you know. ) and plan to stay the entire 2 weeks they are in camp. I have my mom and our one year old small dog, Rimi (named after our trip to Latvia last summer) along. So enough with day-camp says the 6 year old and she convinces me to "go ask" when she can start sleeping over at "real" camp. So, off I go to speak to the Camp Director. Lucky for us there were a few other 6 year old girls whose parents only signed them up for the first week of the two week camp. We get one of their slots and after a few tears, mainly mine, they are all sleeping away from me. WTiat to do? What to think? How to spend the rest of my time at Garezers and Atbalsis? I really hadn\'t planned for this much "free" time. The next day I took a little walk and saw someone sporting a Tshirt with the wording "Kas notiek Garezera, Lai paliek Garezera". Interesting. A direct translation from "What happens at X, stays at X." A very American quote in my mind and not Latvian at all. Later I see that same Tshirt for sale at the Kantine, a dark little shop with half empty shelves and loads of artificially colored candy and Schwann\'s ice cream for sale. All for the kiddies after lunch while at camp. Is this Tshirt trying to tell me something? Is it precisely this reason I have stayed away from Garezers all these years? There are areas in Garezers, more persisely, Dzintari, that have quite the reputation for heavy partying on weekends. Especially 4/2 Volleyball weekend. I had this "Been there, done that" attitude all these years about Garezers and especially about Vollevball weekend. I just didn\'t fed like I had to go back to all that drinking and partying. And I didn\'t. After I graduated from Vidusskola, I went to work at Kursa, the Washington State High School camp. There is a lot more to Garezers. And there is a lot more to being Latvian. And speaking in Latvian, or at least trying to speak it while in Garezers or trying to learn it, trying to improve your vocabulary, trying to read it (try and modern novel like a Dan Brown translation!), trying to write, trying to keep up on current events and elections and politics (There is a lot to keep up on right now!), trying to learn new songs (from Gustavo\'s rap, Ilgi or Suitu Sievas), is a large part of that. Trying just a little bit, even if its hard. Or because it is hard. Drinking a case of beer and sing ing "Alus Ezeriņš" just doesn\'t cut it in this Latvian\'s eyes. Culural identity is very personal and can be very profound. We all have our own stories and our own voices and we can chose how to use them. Because "Kas notiek Garezera" (What happens in Garezers.) is a lot of really neat stuff that many, many people put a lot of effort in to all year long, not just from June to August. "Lai paliek Garezera" (Let it stay in Garezers.) is cutting ourselves short. Bring it home, wherever your home may be, and be that proud Latvian all year, not just for a few summer weeks. And be grateful that you have a place like Garezers to come to and maybe even take it a step further one summer, and find a summer camp in Latvia.']

Representative_Docs: ['Negotiations between the Russian Federation and Latvia on the withdrawal of former Soviet armed forces from Latvian territory may finally be reaching a decisive stage, if the events of the past few weeks are any indication. Between the visit to Latvia of U.S. Secretary of State Warren Christopher, approval of a resolution renewing the U.N. General Assembly\'s call for the speedy conclusion of agreements, including timetables, for an "early, orderly and complete" withdrawal from Latvia and Estonia, and the Russian delegation\'s proposals to move up their preferred withdrawal date in return for Latvian flexibility on the issue of the Skrunda radar base, the long-stagnant negotiations now seem to have a new urgency. Secretary of State Christopher was in Riga October 26-27, at the end of a tour which took him to Belorus, Ukraine, Kazakhstan and Russia. In Riga, Christopher met with President Ulmanis and Prime Minister Birkavs, all three Baltic Foreign Ministers (Andrejevs, Trivimi Velliste of Estonia and Povilas Gylys of Lithuania), and with representatives of Latvia\'s Russian-speaking population. Christopher\'s trip emphasized America\'s keen interest in seeing a solution found to the Russian troop issue, as Christopher repeated the U.S. position of supporting the immediate, complete and orderly withdrawal of the Russian military from Latvia. However, Christopher was also careful not to take a public stand concerning the Skrunda early warning radar base, saying that was an issue for Latvia and Russia to resolve amongst themselves. During his meeting with Birkavs, Christopher did discuss Skrunda, how long it could take to fully disassemble the radar and needed international guarantees of Latvian security during the period of disassembly. Christopher also tried to walk a tightrope on the sensitive issue of Russia\'s accusations of discrimination against Latvia\'s Russian speakers. Christopher praised Latvia\'s readiness to accept the opening of a CSCE mission and noted that international observers have yet to discover any human rights violations in Latvia. At the same time, he said the U.S. hopes that Latvia will soon deal with "practical issues" tied to the Russianspeaking minority. In light of the fact that Christopher had come to Latvia from Moscow, it is not surprising that he would want to learn more about issues Troop Withdrawal Talks Reach Decisive Stage? raised there, including the discrimination issue. However, one hopes that the Secretary\'s meeting with the Russian-speakers will not be used to lend credence to Russia\'s discredited "human rights" campaign against Latvia and Estonia. Prime Minister Birkavs tried, and succeeded, in returning the focus of debate to the troop withdrawal with a speech before the U.N. General Assembly on November 15, to introduce a resolution on the "Complete withdrawal of foreign military forces from the territories of the Baltics States." Birkavs said "the main obstacle to successful development and the most potent factor of instability in the Baltic region still remains the presence of Russian troops on the territory of two sovereign states — Latvia and Estonia." Birkavs also noted that, in spite of Russian promises and Latvia\'s good faith efforts to reach a reasonable solution through bilateral negotiations, Russia had not yet made "significant progress... in fulfilling these requirements." Russia\'s demands have, in fact, often contradicted their promises: Russia has yet to agree to a withdrawal timetable, blocking an early withdrawal; facilities that Russia has evacuated were left in anything but an orderly condition; and Russia has maintained its demand to keep several bases, giving the lie to the promise of a complete withdrawal. Birkavs paid special attention to the Skrunda radar, noting that "these \'strategic\' facilities are perceived [in Latvia] as military bases which could once again serve as a pretext for the occupation of Latvia, as was the case in 1940." However, Birkavs said Latvia would consider extending the relocation deadline of any such base if it is provided convincing international security guarantees and "a reasonably tight internationally controlled schedule of such relocation." The Latvian government\'s willingness to consider such a compromise was given an additional boost by proposals made by Russia during the latest negotiating session. The Russian delegation, led by chief negotiator Sergei Zotov, said that Moscow was willing to remove all of its troops from Latvia by August 31, 1994, four months earlier than its originally stated deadline of December 31. Russia also dropped its demand to keep bases in Ventspils and Liepāja, while reducing the period of time it wanted to keep the Skrunda radar from 10-12 years to six. As Latvian chief negotiator Mārtiņš Virsis stated, "The Russian delegation has for the first time really made concrete positive proposals." Latvia seems very aware of the limits to i flexibility, however. Prime Minister Birkavs stated that "we do understand Russia\'s strategic need and we are flexible enough to discuss a transitio period, but it cannot be until the end of th century... The way we see it in Latvia, the Skrun da station is a mousetrap, Russia is the cat anc Latvia is the mouse." Latest word has it tha Latvia has extended this latest round of talks unt December 3, in order to fully consider its positio on this latest proposal. As the rhetorical fur continues to fly o the troop issue, the evidence is pointing toward a potential break in the two-year old talks. Bot sides seem willing now to take the steps necessary to secure the withdrawal of Russian troops from Latvia, which will remove an 18,000 person strong barrier to the resolution of critical issues such as Latvia\'s economic crisis and the status of Latvia\'s many non-citizen residents. As long as international pressure remains strong on Russia to withdraw its troops from Latvia, one can finally begin to hope that Latvia will be free of foreign occupying armed forces by the time we celebrate Mārtiņš Zvaners', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER Latvia, Russia Initial Troop Withdrawal Accords The two year odyssey of on-again, off-again negotiations between Latvia and Russia on the withdrawal of former Soviet armed forces from Latvia has come to an end. Negotiating teams representing Latvia and Russia initialed on March 15 the "Republic of Latvia and Russian Federation Treaty On The Regulations, Terms and Procedures For The Complete Withdrawal Of Russian Federation Armed Forces From The Territory Of The Republic of Latvia And The Legal Status Of These Armed Forces During The Period Of Withdrawal". Side agreements on the legal status of the Skrunda early-warning radar station and the social security of retired Russian military personnel and dependents and of active duty Russian military personnel and dependents until their withdrawal were also initialed. Despite several points of intense controversy and lack of clarity, the initialing of the treaty brings the final removal of. the armed forces that have illegally occupied the territory of Latvia since World War II that much closer to becoming a fact. The treaty, initialed by Latvian Chief Negotiator Mārtiņš Virsis and his Russian counterpart Sergei Zotov, commits Russia to the complete withdrawal of its armed forces by August 31, 1994. The withdrawal is to take place according to a timetable prepared by Russian authorities, who say the timetable is already being used to regulate the ongoing troop pullout. A copy of the timetable will be delivered to Latvia when the agreements are signed, possibly in late April. After being signed by the two Presidents, the treaty and side agreements must still be ratified by the respective parliaments — which is not at all assured. Skrunda Base Agreement Questioned The troop withdrawal treaty itself, as significant as it is for the removal of Russia\'s remaining 12,000-13,000 troops and officers, has been overshadowed by intense criticism in Latvia of the three side agreements — especially that concerning the Skrunda radar facility. April 1994 – Vol. XIV, No. 4 The side agreement on Skrunda, which would enter into force on September 1, 1994 only if all Russian Federation armed forces are actually withdrawn from Latvia, would allow Russia to continue operating the radar facility for four years, or until a replacement facility is brought on line, whichever comes first. Russia would then be allowed an additional 18 months to dismantle the facility and remove it from Latvia. During the period covered by the agreement, the radar facility would be treated as a military facility under civilian control, which will be leased from Latvia by Russia for an as-yet undetermined annual payment — not as a military base. The radar facility will be staffed by 798 personnel, of whom, to begin with, 599 would be military specialists and 199 civilian employees. The agreement calls for Russia to work towards replacing the military specialists with qualified civilian employees who are Latvian citizens or permanent residents. External security at the facility will be carried out by Latvian personnel, while Russia would be allowed to employ up to 89 military specialists as an internal security force. Oversight of the Skrunda agreement would be entrusted to a Joint Commission made up of both Russian and Latvian representatives, as weH as a representative or representatives of a mutually acceptable international organization, in which both countries are members. The Joint Commission would have the power to arbitrate any issue referred to it, with its decisions binding on both sides. Both scheduled and surprise inspections by the CSCE will also take place, with no more than two of each per year, as well as three days after the closure of the facility and on the day that the treaty loses force. Surprise inspections would take place on the initiative of Latvia. The Tēvzemei un brīvībai movement has called the Skrunda side agreement a violation of Latvia\'s law on international agreements, which states that the Saeima alone has the right to allow a foreign military force to remain on Latvian soil. It considers the maintenance of the Skrunda facility to represent just such an agreement. Twenty two members of the National Bloc in the Saeima also sent a letter to President Guntis Ulmanis pointing this out. Their letter called on Ulmanis to refuse to sign the treaty and agreements, and instead turn the decision on the treaty and agreements over to the people through a referendum, which could be held in conjunction with the upcoming local government elections. Social Guarantees Unpopular The side agreements on social guarantees are causing no less controversy than the Skrunda agreement. The issue here is the decision by Latvia to allow Soviet military pensioners, who retired prior to Russia assuming control of the armed forces in Latvia on January 28, 1992, and their dependents to reside in Latvia. Latvia would recognize the rights of these people to continue to use their homes, as well as confirm their rights to ownership and, if Latvian law will allow, to purchase their homes. Russia will receive a one-time payment from Latvia for living space freed up by military personnel returning to Russia, to support the housing of these people in Russia. In addition, Latvia will help Russia to build apartments and other facilities with funding from international organizations and third nations. The exchange of living quarters between retirees and military personnel leaving Latvia and Russian Federation residents coming to Latvia will also be encouraged. Russia will continue to pay the pensions of military retirees in Latvia. However, if a military retiree decides to take advantage of any pension they may have earned from the Latvian government, they forfeit their Russian military pension for as long as they receive payments from Latvia. Military retirees would be entitled to the same medical and dental services which Latvia gives its retirees. These services would be paid for by the Russian government, which would also allow Latvia to take over military hospitals in the Riga area for use to serve military retirees. Betrayal. . . Or Realism? The Latvian National Independence Movement (LNNK) board called the agreements a betrayal of Latvian national interests, as they legalize the occupation of Latvia and give Soviet military pensioners who retired before January 28, 1992 the opportunity to remain in Latvia and, in time, apply for Latvian citizenship. LNNK Saeima faction leader and Foreign Affairs Commission Chairman Aleksandrs Kiršteins told Diena on March 17 that "the issue is in the Latvian Constitution. Many have forgotten that four paragraphs of the Constitution were renewed on May 4,1990, which asserted that Latvia is an independent state... In reference to the officers, this treaty is simply unconstitutional.,, The treaty and side agreements were welcomed by the CSCE and European Union, as well as the U.S., British, Finnish, French, Polish, Swedish and Estonian governments. The European Union said it "considers the agreements...an important step towards achieving the complete withdrawal of Russian troops from the Baltic States called for in the 1992 CSCE Helsinki document, and towards the establishment of good, cooperative relations between Latvia and Russia." Prominent Latvian government officials acknowledge how difficult the agreements are for Latvia to accept, but note that the treaty and side agreements offer a last best hope for securing the withdrawal of Russian armed forces and finalizing Latvia\'s break with Moscow. Justice Minister Egils Levits noted that Russia has, by initialing the agreements, firmly fixed a withdrawal deadline and agreed to turn over the two new buildings of the uncompleted phased-array radar at Skrunda to Latvia immediately after the agreements are signed. Levits called the treaty "a notable achievement of Latvian diplomacy... I think future historians will term this treaty an achievement, much as has happened to the 1920 Russia-Latvia treaty." Foreign Minister Georgs Andrejevs, interviewed by ITAR/TASS during a trip to Italy, referred to the international aspect of how agreement was reached. "As far as possible, we heeded to pieces of advice from across the world, above all from the United States. We know that this compromise version was worked out at the Moscow summit between Presidents Boris Yeltsin and Bill Clinton. Then came our visit to Washington. During this visit, we received necessary explanations about why we should not be scared with this version." Andrejevs recognizes how painful the Skrunda agreement is to Latvians, but hopes that realism will win out. "We have the bitter experience of 1939 related to military bases stationed in Latvia. It was not very long ago. However, we need to change Latvian people\'s mentalities. We should explain that times have changed, and Russia needs the Skrunda radar only as a means of obtaining information and not as a military installation. There is quite strong opposition in Latvia... Nevertheless, we should display political realism in our onward advance." Mārtiņš Zvaners', 'The Embassy of Latvia, 4325 17th St. NW, Washington, D. C, U. S. A. «202-726-8213, has released the following: U.S. GOVERNMENT STATEMENTS CONCERNING THE LATVIAN-RUSSIAN TROOP WITHDRAWAL AGREEMENTS Washington – President Clinton congratulated Latvian President Guntis Ulmanis and Russian President Boris Yeltsin April 30 for their agreement on Russian troop withdrawal from Latvia by August 31. 1994 Following is the text of the statement issued by the Office of the Press Secretary of the Whiute House "I applaud today\'s agreement signed by Latvian President Guntis Ulmanis and Russian President Boris Yeltsin that will lead to the withdrawal of Russian military forces from the territory of the Republic of Latvia by August 31, 1994. I have contacted both leaders to offer my personal congratulations for their vision and statemanahip in concluding this historic accord. Since the early days of this administration, among my highest foreign policy priorities has been promoting agreement on an orderly withdrawal of Russian forces from the Baltic countries. I discussed this frequently with President Yeltsin and President Ulmanis. The United States has played an active role with both parties during the course of the Latvian-Russian negotiations. I believe that our engagement with both aides, along with the support provided by other countries, in particular Sweden, has played a constructive role in bringing this agreement to a successful conclusion. Over the course of their negotiations, both the Latvian and Russian governments displayed a pragmatic approach to resolving their differences. The understanding the this document embodies, including the continued operation of the radar installiatlon at Skrunda as a civilian facility, are testimony to the determination of both sides to conclude an agreement that responds to Russian concerns while affirming Latvia\'s full and unrestricted sovereignty and promoting its Mnegration into the world community. The agreement between Latvia and Russia opens the door to a more normal relationship between the two countries. It constitutes an important contribution to overall stability In the Baltic region and to European security as a whole. I hope the this agreement also will help stimulate a speedy conclusion of the troop withdrawal negotiations between Estonia and Russia. " US Department of State spokesman on Latvian-Russian troop withdrawal agreement: "We are delighted that on April 30 Latvian President Guntis Ulmanis and Russian President Boris Yeltsin signed an agreement m Moscow on the withdrawal of Russian troops from Latvia by August 31. 1994. We applauded both the Latvian and Russian Government for taking a constructive, pragmatic approach to resolving their differences We look forward to agreements implemented smoothly The U S. actively promoted the withdrawal of all Russian forces from the Baltics It is our hope that the Russian-Latvian agreement will pave the way for an agreement soon between Estonia and Latvia President Clinton and Secretary Christopher were personally engaged in contributing to an early resolution of the troop withdrawal issues In order to promote the troop withdrawal talks, the U S last year also established a 160 million program for the construction of housing for demobilizing Russian officers returning from the Baltics and elsewhere. Under this program housing vouchers will also be made available fot immediate use by retired Russian officers who want to depart Latvia and Estonia to resettle m Russia A key breakthrough in the negotiations was President Clinton\'s suggested compromise for the continued operation and subsequent dismantlement of the Skrunda radar facility, which provided the basis for the draft agreements that were initialed on March 15 The United States has also commited $ 2 million to assist with the prompt dismantlement of the large phased-array radar at Skrunda Sweden already has commited $ 1 million in the international effort for the same purpose We believe the signing of the agreement is a maio< contribution to legional stability and harmony and also a succesfull example of a concentrated effort both by individual states and by the CSCE to help bring a difficult issue to a satisfactory resolution These statements clearly indicate the pressures which the super powers have applied to The Republic of Latvia No doubt the debate about these signings will be harsh and emotional. Particularly because some of the agreements take effect immediately. The agreements must be ratified by the Russian and Latvian parliaments It is not clear how soon this will be done but it appears that passage in the Latvian Saeima may be secured relatively easily Time will tell. Your comments on the matter are most welcome Ed.']
