Representative_Docs: ['The last two weeks of February saw many of us glued to our television sets, enjoying a once-in-alifetime opportunity to renew our quadrennial Olympic fantasies two years early. Due to a decision by the International Olympic Committee to hold the Winter and Summer Olympic Games in separate years, the XVII Winter Olympic Games in Lillehammer, Norway are taking place only two years after the previous Olympics in Albertville, France. The Lillehammer Games have also proven to be an unique opportunity for Latvia, Estonia, Lithuania and the many other nations emerging from the Soviet shadow to try their hand again at establishing, or, in the case of the Baltic states, reestablishing an independent name for themselves in the world\'s premier sports competition. Latvia recognized by CBS Latvian, Estonian and Lithuanian athletes have yet to medal in the Lillehammer Games and, as a result, have not been prominently featured in CBS\' television coverage. They continued to build on the respect they earned during the 1992 Albertville and Barcelona Games, their first Olympics since renewing their independence in 1991. In a pleasant departure from Albertville, CBS made sure to avoid another faux pas by prominently featuring the Latvian Olympic delegation as it entered the Opening Ceremonies in Lillehammer\'s ski jumping stadium. The Latvian flagbearer was gold-medalist Zintis Ekmanis, who with fellow Latvian Jānis Ķipurs won the twoman bobsled competition during the 1988 Calgary Games, while Latvia was still occupied by the Soviet Union. The CBS commentators also ensured that viewers were aware of the special role many of the Baltic olympians played during the struggle to renew Baltic independence. Ekmanis\' experience as one of the many Latvians who defended Latvia\'s freedom in January 1991 was noted during the Opening Ceremonies, and any time Baltic athletes were shown during event coverage, viewers were told that the athletes represented countries that had broken away from Soviet occupation and domination. The team The team that represented Latvia at the LilleLatvian Pride At Lillehammer Games hammer Olympic Games consisted of 31 athletes and 11 delegation members. Although to date (February 21) Latvian athletes have yet to win a medal, they have competed well — in some cases, surprisingly well. Figure skating — Latvia sent three skaters to the Olympics: Andrejs Vlasčenko in mens, and Jeļena Berežnaja and Oļegs šļahovs in pairs. Berežnaja and šļahovs placed ninth among among 18 pairs competing for the gold medal, while Vlasčenko tied for 21st among the 25 skaters competing for the mens title. Luge — Latvia\'s luge teams repeated their strong performances of Albertville. Anna Orlova led the women with a ninth place finish, followed by Evija Šulce (16th) and Ilūta Gaile (17th). Aivis Švāns and Roberts Suharevs took 11th place in the mens doubles competition, followed by teammates Juris Vovčoks and Dairis Leksis in 12th. In mens singles, Agris Ēlerts took 18th place, while Vovčoks placed 21st. Bobsledding — The four-man race will take place on the last day of the Olympics, February 26. But in the two-man competition, Calgary Games gold medalists Zintis Ekmanis and Aldis Intlers placed 10th. Ekmanis and Intlers are joined on the bobsled team by Boriss Artemjevs, Egils Bojārs, Rodžers Lodziņš, Andris Plūksna, Sandis Prūsis, Otomārs Rihters, Didzis Skuška and Juris Tone. Cross Country Skiing — Latvia sent two skiers to Lillehammer – Jānis Hermanis and Ineta Kravale. Neither expected to be in medal contention in the races they entered – rather, they felt that simply finishing would be achievement enough. LOC President Baltiņš explained their inclusion on the team with the phrase "for faithfulness to sports." Hermanis placed 85th in the 10km classic race and 69th in the 30km freestyle race, while Kravale finished the skm classic race in 61st place and the 15km freestyle race in 52nd place. Speedskating — Latvia\'s one speedskater, Honda Lūse, earned 25th place in the 3000 m race. Biathalon — Latvia sent five biathaletes to the Lillehammer Games: leva Volfa, Aivars Bogdanovs, Ilmārs Bricis, Oļegs Maļuhins and Gundars Upenieks. The level of competition in Lillehammer was exceptional, especially in figure skating, where several former champions returned from professional skating careers to try again for Olympic gold. The Latvian Olympic team proudly and ably demonstrated their commitment to sports and again served as shining exponents of the spirit of Olympic competition. Mārtiņš Zvaners', 'COULDN\'T THIS POSSIBLY INTEREST YOU? During their comparatively short period of independence between the two World Wars, on several occasions the Baltic nations could distinguish themselves in different fields of international sports. ■Estonia. In the European sports press Estonia frequently was called "the land of the strong men", for reason of the achievements in heavy athletics by its nationals, who were among the strongest in our continent indeed. The once world-famous professional wrestler George Lurich has become an almost legendary symbol of colossal strenght, and has set a splendid example for the younger generation of Estonians. At the Olympic games and European Championships, the Estonian boys nearly always won laurels comparable to those of the Finns and Swedes. During the last Olympic games at Berlin in 1936, the Estonian heavyweight Christian Palusalu could claim the title of the "topmost strong man", for reason of his scoring a double victory in wrestling Greek-Roman style and American free-style as well. This is by no means an isolated instance; the fame of other wrestling champions-Vali, Neo, Puttsepp, Kotkes, etc.-in sporting circles is perhaps on nearly the same level. Quite conspicuous are the achievements of the Estonians in weight lifting and boxing too; c. g., in 1938 at Dublin, Raadik became boxing champion of Europe in the middleweight class. The Estonians won quite a number of victories in field and track events too. Shortly before the Second World War, the only serious competitors of the brilliant Finnish javeling throwers in Europe were the Estonians G. Sule and J. Issaac. Next to them came the outstanding shot putters A. Vflding, the champion of Europe in 1938 A. Kruk, and A. Maalstein. In 1945, the last named, now beeing a DP in Germany, still was one of the best in Europe. After the First World War, the Estonian A. Klumberg was the decathlon world record holder; in 1920 at the Antwerp Olympic games, the marathon runner J. Lossman was second, closely behinf the "father of the Finnish runners" H. Kolchmainen. Next to miraclous was the success of the Estonians in shooting; from 1935 till 1939, this small nation could boast of such a list of world record holders and champious as even the Finns and Angentinians were not able to surpass. The Estonian basketball and voleyball players were among the best in Europe. World famous was their young chess champion P. Keres, one of the best players throughout the world. Outstanding were the performances in ice-yachting; several times the European Championship meets took place in their country. In 1937, the European wrestling (Greek-Roman style) Championship meet was held in Tallin, Estonia. Latvia. The Latvian people, counting less than 2 million, have contributed to the list of outstanding athletes several big names. The national sport of the Latvians was soccer football, although they have achieved mor notable results in other sporting events. The former multiple world re. cord holder in walking events, J. Dalins, has spread the fame of his native country throughout the sporting world. Nearly all European star walkers came to compete with him to the Latvian capital Riga. Now this fame has passed over to the Swedes, who once were the most gifted disciples of the notable Latvians-J. Dalins, P. Bernhards, A. Liepaskalns, A. Bubenko, etc. At the Los Angeles Olympic games in 1932, J. Dalins was second behind the Englishman Green in the 50 klm walking event; in 1934 at Torino, he became champion of Europe for the same distance. Another brilliant Latvian was the decathlon athlete J. Dimza who was deported by the Russians in 1940. Perhaps not a few American sporting enthusiasts may still remember his misfortune at the Los Angeles Olympic games in 1932 when, being amongst the leaders after the first half of the decathlon events, he fell so unluckily during the pole vault competition that he had to be carried off the field badly injured. The star page of the Latvian sports roll includes the following names: K. Klava, shooting champion of the world in 1937 at Helsinki-Finland. E. Bietags, wrestling (Greek-Roman style) champion or Europe in 1934 at Rome. A. Berzins speed skating champion of Europe in 1938 at Riga, Latvia. The Latvian national basketball team, the first champion of Europe in 1934 at Geneva. The achievements of J. Dalins were mentioned already. At the Berlin Olympic games in 1936, A. Bubenko was third in the 50 klm walking event, but the lightheaveyweight E. Bietags second in wrestling Greek-Roman style. Quite notable in chess circles ar the Latvian players. At the Paris Olympic games in 1924, the Latvian H. Matison was first, and his compatrist J. Apsenieks second. Several important sports meetings were held in Latvia, c. g.p the wrestling (Greek-Roman style) competition for the European champion-ship in 1926, the basketball, competition for the European championship in 1937, the speed skating competition for the European championship in 1938; besides, several times the European championship iceyachting meets took place there. Big international chess tournaments were organized in Biga, and in the renowned spa Kemeri not far from the beautiful Latvian seaside resorts. Lithuania. Of all kinds of sporting activities, basketball has attained the highest popularity with the Lithuanians. Twice they were champions of Europe, and before the war next to all Lithuanian boys and girls played basketball. To this enthusiasm have mostly contributed the USA star players of Lithuanian origin: Puzinauskas, Norkus, Andrulis, Baltrunas, etc., are names held in highest esteem by all European basketball fans. Good progress was also made in soccer football and other sporting events, but all that had to be abandoned with the outbreak of war. The biggest sports meeting in Lithuania took place in 1937: in thedir capital Kaunas the European basketball championship were held. In exile. At present the athletes of the Baltic states, as displaced persons, are dispersed and dwell in several countries, mostly in Germany and Sweden. With the help of their old and true protectors and friends, the American and English, they try, as far as circumstances permit, to continue their sporting activities, in order to conserve and develop the strenght of their youth for the days to come, when our innermost dreams, of returning to the free and democratic republics of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania, will come true. His small merry face shrank with a sudden sorrow. "I\'m sorry you don\'t speak Czech." "I wish I did, Love", I said, "it it could help you". "Sure, it would help me", sighed Love, "I\'m running after DPs all time but in vain. No Czechs come my way. A Czech is all I need. That\'s why I endured the ETO pretty wellhoping to meet a Czech." This was the time to get suspicious. "Say, what\'s so marvellous about a Czech? What has a Czech .got other DPs haven\'t? Can\'t someone else do as well?" "No", Love said in a rather resigned tone, "not while I\'m married to this wife." "Come, Love, explain it", I patted him on the shoulder, having not the slightest idea of what use the missing Czech could be to Love. He seemed to be so fond of the two Loves he had home already. Yet, who knows? Arthur Thomas Love from Texas raised his eyes. I met a roguish look framed in horn. "You know, my wife is a Czech. She has the habit of calling me names in Czech. You know, she uses them any time, bad names and good names. I\'d just like to know what names exactly. And I\'d like to call her names in Czech too. But there is no hope for me, no Czechs around who could teach me. I\'ll go home and she\'ll kiss me and call me names in Czech." Love pulled a wallet out of his pocket and let me see his wife who called him names in Czech, a smiling American beauty. Then he asked me, "What would you do if you were I?" "Well, Love, I hope you realise your wife can\'t call you Love because everyone else does. So she has to be different. That\'s why. But just to help you, why not think up names to use any time she calls you names in Czech? Tell her it is some European lingo. I bet she will quit speaking Czech". "That\'s an idea," Love seemed to cheer up. "Hm, hm, quite an idea". Then he meditated a little, tilting his lovely nose up with one lovely finger. "Yes", he decided at last. "I\'ll do it if I won\'t find a Czech. Thank you." Private Love got up, knocking over his champagne-glass. He hurried across the room towards the door, and out into the cold German winter night, straight on, as if fate waited for him outside-or a Czech. Love came unexpectedly, Love disappeared unexpectedly. Why didn\'t grandmother tell me?', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER 33 LATVIAN ATHLETES GO TO NAGANO The Latvian Olympic committee confirmed the members of the Latvian National teem for the Olympic games in Nagano. The team includes 33 athletes and their coaches plus 12 accredited members who will not live in the Olympic village. This year s Olympic team is the largest yet from Latvia to participate in the Olympic games. SANDIS PRŪSIS HOPES TO GET A MEDAL IN NAGANO In the opening ceremony of the XVIII Olympic games the Latvian flag was carried by Sandis Prūsis from the Latvian bobsledding team. So far only the bobsledders have had the honor to carry the National flag in the Winter Olympic games. So far, he is the biggest hope to get an Olympic medal for Latvia. JĒKABS NĀKUMS FINISHES FOURTH. In the fifth round of the World cup in Biathlon in the Italian village of Antersalev the Latvian biathlonist Jēkabs Nākums from Alūksne took the fourth place in the 20 km distance. THE NEW PLENARY SESSIONS AUDITORIUM On January 15th the new Plenary Sessions Auditorium at the Parliament was reopened. The oldest parliamentarian deputy Guntars Grinblats from "Tēvzemei un Brīvībai\'VLNNK was the one to carry the flag of the Republic of Latvia into the newly renovated auditorium. After the National Anthem was played, the auditorium was considered to be opened. The reconstruction of the auditorium cost 655 thousand Lats. THE HOMEPAGE OF THE CHARTER BETWEEN USA AND THE BALTIC STATES The U.S. Information Agency (U.5.1.A.) informed that the home-page address of the NEWS FROM LATVIA March 1998 reported by Charter between USA and the Baltic countries is www.usia.gov/regional/eur/baltics/ homepage.htm. One can also find materials regarding this matter on the home-page of U.S.I.A. in Riga. The address of the U.S.I.A. is www.usis.bkc.lv. ONE MORE NEW POLITICAL FORCE IN LATVIA Representing 64 different professions a new political party "The New Party" (Jaunā partija) has announced the date of it\'s foundation. It will take place on March 14th. Many businessmen are linked to this new political force. The members of the party will include such prominent people as composer Raimonds Pauls, the executive director of Varner/Hakon Invest Ainārs Šlesers, neurosurgeon Aris Auders, the former Minister of the Republic of Latvia Jānis Āboltiņš and many others. So far there are 500 active supporters of \'The New Party ", but they hope that they will be the biggest political force in Latvia and will reach 10 000 members. This however seems a very unrealistic number, because the biggest party "Latvian Way" (Latvijas ceļš) has only about 400 members. So far two former KGB agents have joined the supporters of "The New Party". THE MEETING OF PRIME MINISTERS OF THE BALTIC SEA COUNTRIES. On January 22nd and 23rd 11 Prime ministers from the countries around the Baltic Sea arrived in Rīga to the annual conference. Two of them – the Prime Minister of Germany Helmut Kohl and the Prime Minister of Russia Viktor Chernomirdin – arrived in Rīga for the first time. This was one of the most important meetings held in Rīga. The Minister of Justice Dzintars Rasnačs while meeting with the Prime Minister of Russia discussed how Latvia could get back the KGB documents related to the deportations of Latvians. in Riga Šarlote Līduma THE OCCUPATION OF LATVIA According to the letter of Russian Minister of Foreign Affairs addressed to the Duma (Valsts domei) there was no such thing as the occupation of Latvia. RUSSIANS ATTACK LATVIAN HIGH SCHOOL ON THE DAY OF COMMEMORATION On January 20th the main activities of commemoration of the January 1991 barricades took place. The President of the Republic of Latvia Guntis Ulmanis, the chairman of the Parliament of Latvia Alfreds Čepānis and representatives from the government and the town council of Rīga placed flowers at the Monument of Freedom. Many other related activities took place, such as the opening of the Commemoration center of 1991 .This is the first time when the commemoration of the barricades was officially celebrated. The same afternoon about 30 – 40 Russian teenagers armed with sticks and clubs attempted to attack the Latvian high school Nr. 25 on Maskavas St. Thanks to the timely arrival of the National Guards nobody was hurt. LITHUANIA JOINS THE CENTRAL EUROPEAN TIME ZONE Lithuania will join The Central European time zone on March 29th at 2 a.m. After that Lithuania will be in the I Time zone according to the Greenwich time. This time zone includes such countries as Germany, France, Belgium, Netherlands, Italy, Spain, Austria, Switzerland, Norway, Sweden, Denmark, Poland and Czech Republic. For the time being Lithuania as well as Latvia, Estonia, Finland, Belarus, and Ukraine are in the II time zone. The Lithuanian government considers that the Central European time zone will positively affect Lithuania\'s relationships with other countries in the I time zone. Lithuania already was in the I time zone before the occupation by U.S.S.R. in 1940. PRESIDENT NEEDS A NEW BILL The President of the Republic of Latvia might have a right in the future to the amnesty of persons sentenced to death penalty even if they will fail to submit the request for amnesty. The parliament of Latvia, Saeima has to pass this bill as soon as possible because since the changes included in Satversme in December 1997, the process of amnesty has been stopped. Up today the President of the Republic of Latvia has received more than 200 requests for amnesty. However, until the new bill is passed he can not review them. THE NEW ALTERNATIVE TO MANDATORY MILITARY SERVICE The Ministry of Defense has introduced a project which contains a curriculum for teaching the basic military skills in the higher academic institutions. It is supposed to be an alternative to the mandatory military service. If a student will regularly attend the military classes, he will not have to do the mandatory service. The earliest when this project will be effective is 1999. Prior to that, the precise data about the need for specialized military personnel in Latvia has to be gathered. RĪGA WILL HOST MORE THAN 3000 INTERNATIONAL FINANCIERS It is confirmed now that the European Bank of Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) will hold its annual meeting in Rīga, reports the Ministry of Finances of the Republic of Latvia. The proxies of the EBRD almost unanimously voted to hold the meeting in the capital of Latvia. This meeting will take place on May 21 and 22 in the year 2000. Some 3000 – 4000 influential financiers from more than 50 countries are expected to arrive in Rīga for the meeting. THE ALLIES RECOGNIZE LATVIA DE lURE 77 YEARS AGO On January 23rd the celebration of the 77th anniversary of recognition of the Republic of Latvia de iure took place. On August 26, 1921 the independence of Latvia and Estonia de iure was acknowledged by The Supreme Council of The Allies which at that time consisted of England, France, Italy and Japan. The Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Latvia and other representatives from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs laid flowers in the Meža kapi cemetery at the monuments of J.Čakste and Z. Meierovics. MURDERED LATVIANS HONORED NEAR MOSCOW On February 3rd in the city of Butov, near Moscow the first meeting was held to honor the 263 Latvians shot during the Stalin\'s campaign of annihilation. The relatives of these Latvian victims, as well as Latvians living in Moscow and the representatives from the Embassy of Latvia in Russia honored the victims. 73 000 RUSSIAN LATVIANS MURDERED DURING STALIN\'S ETHNIC CLEANSING On February 3rd the Commission of human rights and public sector supported the proposal by the organization of The Latvians in the East about declaring the first Monday of December as a commemoration day of Latvians killed in Russia. However this proposal was not accepted because there is no precise information about the date of December 1937 when the actual genocide against Latvians took place. According to the commission of Latvian parliament Saeima, in a two year period some 73 000 Latvians had been killed because of their nationality. ALFREDS RUBIKS VISITS MOSCOW The former Latvian communist leader Alfreds Rubiks is on a visit to Moscow. In the train station he was met not only by his supporters in Russia but also by Russian top communist leaders. During his visit he met with the chairman of The Duma Genadij Seljeznow and the mayor of Moscow City, Yurij Luzhkow. After a meeting with the fraction of Communists in The Russian Parliament A. Rubiks announced the renewal of a united communist party. THE CONSUMPTION OF ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES IN 1997 According to statistics during the first nine months of 1997, 61% of alcohol consumed in Latvia was vodka and liqueur, 20% – beer, 10% wine, 7% cognac and brandy and 2% – champagne. Amount of consumed alcohol per capita has been 5,4 liters. Altogether the consumption of vodka and wine have decreased, but the consumption of cognac and brandy increased. EU IS INTERESTED IN THE LATVIAN CRAYFISH During the summer of 1996 within the European Union\'s program PHARE research was con■ ducted about the prospects of fish breeding and crayfish breeding in Latvia. The experts came to the conclusion that the crayfish breeding is one of the most prospective fields of aquaculture. Latvia is rich with lakes, rivers and ponds where top quality crayfish abides. Due to the vast areas environmental pollution the population of crayfish remaining in Latvia has decreased. For now there is only an insignificant number of crayfish left, which possibly could be harvested in a single summer. Now a center for crayfish breeding has been founded and this fall crayfish will be available to people who want to begin crayfish breeding. A LATVIAN MOUNTAIN CLIMBER ROBBED IN ARGENTINA Latvian mountain climber from the city of Liepāja, Zigurds Ivanovs was robbed in South America during his climbing of Mt. Aconcagua, which is the highest mountain in South America. The belongings were taken by local Indians who usually are hired to carry the equipment of mountain climbers. The local ranger was informed immediately about the theft, but all search results were negative. A round-trip ticket from Latvia to Argentina which was taken with the equipment, cost $3000 US and the equipment itself was worth another $ 2000 US. However, Z. Ivanovs doesn\'t regret this accident because thanks to this he has met people who are planning to go to Antarctica. As it turned out, to reach the Southern Pole is one of the goals for the Latvian explorer. He also is considering to go to Argentina and try to climb Mt. Aconcagua once again. NEW OPERA On January 23 and 25 the premiere show of "Aida" took place in the National Opera of Latvia. On the opening night the Chinese tenor Warren Mok performed as Radames.']

Representative_Docs: ['The tenth anniversary Baltic philatelic and cultural exposition in Montreal took place on the weekend of May 15 and 16, 1999. This year\'s show was held at the Notre Dame Porte de l\'Aurore – Our Lady Gate of Dawn Lithuanian church hall – a large church with a wonderful hall big enough to play basketball in (which thev regularly do). The weather was excellent, the spirits were high, and with over 700 pages of exposition and good attendance on both days, Saturday and Sunday, it was in every way a cuccess! People from both the English and French speaking communities of Montreal were in atten dance and several car loads down from Ottawa, Kingston, and parts in-between. Dr. Rolfs Sers from the Maritimes and a member of our club and an exhibitor as well came from the greatest distance away. The exhibition\'s "Cafe" was open from 9 AM to 6 PM on both days, with Giselle von Dehn as well as Nora Saletés and Malda Skaistkalns (our members from Cornwall) in charge. The ladies had outdone themselves, with superb cakes and sandwiches, several kinds of teas and coffees available, at phenomenally low prices! The best he has ever seen at any stamp show, in the judgement of Mr. Eric Jānlepp who had driven down from Ottawa for the anniversary show. The Official Canada Post cancel, used on letters mailed at the show, designed by Dr. Francois Brisse (of the University of Montreal) featured two liands open wide with the names of the three Baltic States being gently held. It was very popular and received much usage during the show. The Commemorative envelope of this anniversary Exhibition, in 500 copies, was hand printed in gold leaf by Mr. von Hahn of Montreal West – a beautifylly executed work indeed! With over 700 pages of exhibits, in 14 major collec – tions, to evaluate, Mr. Benoit Carrier, as the chief judge and Henrik Mouritsen helping had their work cut out for them. Ten medals were awarded, with silver medals going to Mārtiņš Štauvers (1) and Dr. Rofs Sers (2 medals), and silver bronze to four other collectors. The medals were designed by our "Grand Old Man" Oskars Vindedzis, who suprised us this year with an exquisite design. BALT-EX X Two exhibits, totally in French, with great computer graphics by club member Francois Rochon were parti cularly commented on; as well as an interesting short exhibit on "Love" by the club president. The most talked about exhibit was again by Dr. Rolfs Sers, our club member froms Antigonish, Nova Scotia, whose "Early Latvian varieties" won the Most Popular Exhibit award. Thematic exhibits were several, the younger viewers particularly liked the one on "Hockey"; the older ones \'Baltic Folk Costumes"; and in the general open class, an exhibit of "Romantic Postcards from Estonia in the PJ2o\'s and 1930\'5". An Extensive Lithuanian exhibit on the "Nobel Prizes" yu Romas Verbyla also was excellent. This year also, we had a dozen "one-pages" – exhibits <>l oji£ Piig£ only, and three pagers or "tryptychs" which seemed to be popular, and will be repeated in the future. In the "Court of Honor" was shown an International level display of Denmark philately (photo copies) by Henrik Mouritsen which was in a class by itself. For our 10th anniversary show, the Montreal City Hall sent the Pro-Mayor, Monsieur Pierre Lapointe, as mayor Bourque\'s official representative, who affably officiated during the Medal Ceremonies held on Sunday – his friendly presence was much appreciated. Next year we will have another 10th anniversary to celebrate – that of the founding of The Montreal Baltic Philatelic Club itself, an amalgamation of those interested in promoting Baltic culture through our club activities and yearly exhibitions. We believe this work to be useful in bringing information in a practical way to the attention of Canadiens of all origins. The club needs your support – should you wish to support this work, give us a call at 486-5717! □ J. J. Raudsepp, President Left to right: Mārtiņš Štauvers, vice-president, J. J. Raudsepp presents free stamp envelopes to young collectors A hot discussion of philatelic and exhibition matters', 'From left to right: John MacEntyre, judge of exhibits, Henn Paabo, exhibitor, Benoti Carrier (at back), judge, president of jury, Rosemary Turpin, exhibitor, Mārtiņš Štauvers, v-p. of the club and exhibitor, George Fiala, gold medal and grand award winner, Elmārs Bārdiņš, exhibitor, Oleg Podymow, gold medal winner, J. J. Raudsepp, president of the club and exhibitor, Oskars Vīndedzis, exhibitor, Eva Pare, Hostess, David Nickson, chairman of Royale 2001, Mrs G. Fiala and Francois Rochon, exhibitor. This year\'s commemorative envelope of Balt-Ex-XII also commemorated two very highly respected philatelists whose names appear on the envelopa, In Memoriam. Mr. Hämar was the longtime president or th Estonian Philatelic Society of America and the Edutor of their "Bulletin"; Mr. J. Ronis also was the Editor od the Latvian Philatelic Society\'s Journal "Krājējs". Both were valued members of our club with Mr. Ronis having exhibited several times and Mr. Hämer was about to do so. The Montreal Baltic Philatelic Club held its annual Spring Exposition (for the twelfth year), BALT-EX-XII in grand style on May 26 & 27, 2001, again in the Estonian church hall, in N. D. G. Exhibited were 600 pages of collections as far afield as Boston and Toronto! In the \'Court of Honor\' one found a highly interesting Exposition featuring the "Great Chuch Organs of the World" ( it included the one from The Dome in Riga) as they appear on stamps, covers and post cards by Benoit Carrier. He also served as the president of the two-man jury, with John MacEntyre, R. P. S. C; both knowlegeable phy late lists, who very conscientiensly reviewed every page of every exhibit, over the better part of Saturday, and awarded certificates and medals impartially and in accordance with the level of proficiency that they found. Both were very helpful in answering detailed questions regardBALT-EX-XII MONTREAL ing exhibiting and our thanks to both for a very impressive job well done. Two very strong colections were the very well crafted thematic exhibit "Collapse of the Evil Empire" by George Fiala, who traced the downfall of the Soviet Union, using philathebc material, with examples from Eastern European countries on the road to regaining their independence; and Oleg Podymow\'s impressive exhibit "Finland 1891-1917: The Era of the Double-Headed Eagle", which also received many favourable comments. Both were awarded a gold medal\' for their efforts, with the former given the "Grand Prix" award for "Best in Show". A high "vermeil medal" was obtained by Mārtiņš Štauvers for his extensive work in the marcophily class of philately – "Latvia – postal cancellations 1910-1945". In the display class the same medals were attained by Viesturs Šķidra from Toronto, for his postal history of Liepāja (Libau); and by Francois Rochon for his history of Latvia in the 20th century through its philately. There were also two very good exhibits on Lithuanian postal history and Lithuanian Postal Stationary usages in the 19905; a fascinating exhibit on the complex tentorial change in Hungary from 1850 to 1945; and a complete 50 page overview of the Baltic Philatelic Club of Montreal and its Expositions, with dozens of colour photographs. At the closing: J. J. Raudsepp (centre) with the 2 gold medal winners Oleg Podymow (left) and George Fiala (also the Grand Prix Award). Hard at work – the two judges reviewing and avaluating the exhibits left to right: John MacEntyre R. P. S. Ca, and Benoit Carrier, president of the Jury. Banknotes from the Baltic countries were exhibited by Henn Paabo who provided catalogs and was on hand to answer questions. Of esoteric interest was a 12 page collection of "Fruit Stickers" (those found on fruits commonly in most stores) by Rosemary Turpin. It turned out that a number of people collect these and that there is collectors\' group on the Internet, as well. Sadly, one important collection – "Estonians in WWI – Oskars Vīndedzis at his artistic greeting card stand Russian Imperial Fieldpost 191:4-1918" by Karl laas of Toronto didn\'t make it to Montreal. On the way, Karl suffered a heart attack and died \'en route1 (at a very young age). This is a big loss to all of us, for he was a fine man and a very friendly, knowledgeable philatelist. The Exposition Cafe was in the competent hands of Eva Pare, helped by Harry Parna. A goodly selection of cakes together with a selection of coffees, various teas and juices dispensed with Eva\'s lovely smile, was appreciated by all! To all those fine ladies who contributed cakes, many many thanks. Special thanks go to Nora Saletés who also had prepared several trays of delicious sandwiches, which were a God-send! Very tasty! There is a tradition in our club that gold-medal award winners buy a bottle of champagne which is/are opened at the end of the show, before the stands are taken down and packed up. This year there were two nice bottles of champaigne (and a tablefull of hors d\'oevres from the charming Mrs. Fiala!) – making it a closing in a grand style! On an extremely limited and minuscule budget, this was a delightful finish! We are already planning next year\'s BAIT-EX Exposition and would like to extend an invitation to all near or far, come visit the Montreal Baltic show next spring! □ J.-J. Raudsepp', 'This year\'s Montreal Baltic Philathelic Club\'s annual Spring show, BALT-EX-XIII took place April 26-28, 2002. It was planned this year as a very special event – the Exposition was held, with all Montreal Baltic Artists and photographers invited to come and expose their work at a joint show. An organizing Committee was set up with members of the three Montreal Baltic Communities and work started in November, 2001, 6 months before the scheduled Exposition. An attempt to hold such a show had not taken place for many tens of years, and in retrospect it is easy to see why – there are three separate communities, and many types of artists, some dead, some living, some professional – but one thing was clear from the outset, from previewing, there was certainly a lot of talent in the Montreal Baltic Community, which should have been and should be in the future, exposed, regurarly if at all possible Baltic painters and photographers in Montreal should hold regular shows and get to know each other – the only problem is that it requires organiza tion and financial support, which is lacking. The Montreal Baltic Community must work together on this. And someone should take it upon themselves to write the overview and history of Montreal Baltic artists\' achievements, before it is to late. The key is to find funding – perhaps there are people in this community who recognize the importance of this? The arts reflect our community rather well! Our Org-committee tracked down, previewed and invited all artists who showed interest in such a joint Exposition; weekly meetings were held over a six-month time period. It was a great pleasure and fantastic opportunity to meet with so many creaative artists and to see so many A SUCCESFUL EVENT The organizing committee (left to right): Romas Verbyla, Oskars Vīndedzis, J. – J. Raudsepp (chairman), Elmārs Bārdiņš and Leonīds Dedelis good works of art before the show and to sit down and talk over coffee! The Balt-Ex-XJII Exposition began with opening ceremonies on Friday evening, 16th April and it was very gratifying to see over 350 guests who came for the wine-andcheese vernissage, together with political aides from Canada\'s finance minister Hon. Paul Martin\'s office, in whose riding the Lithuanian church is located, and Lisa Frulla, the federal Liberal party star candidate in the recent by-elections in the nearby Verdun riding and several Montreal City Councillors. Exposed in the Grand Hall of the Lithuanian Our Lady Gate of Dawn – Notre-Dame-Porte-del\'Amore church were over 100 paintings (30+ painters), a dozen sculptures (2 sculptors) and over 300 photographs (8"xlOM to as large as 3 ftx 4 ft), the artisans were well represented by Zinaida and Oskars Vīndedzis, and there were over 500 pages of philatelic expostions. The large 70 ft x 40 ft Hall was absolutely full of works of art, tables were set so that artists and guests could mix and mingle over coffee, tea and cazkes and sandwiches from the Cafe run by Nora Saletés and her helpers over the three days of the show. Many well known artists works were exhibited, particularly from the Lithuanian community, such as Romualdas Bukauskas, Raymondas Piešina, Alvyra Povuaitis, Vytas Remeika and John Gytis Vazalinskas, among others. Similarly, the Latvian and Estonian communities had their "stars" in oils, acrylics and water colours, best left for more detailed analysis, later. Two young ladies visiting the Exposition, in Latvian folk costumes from left: Rasma Stelps Kiršteins and Biruta Kalacis Photos by Hille Viires Similarly, among the photographers, each of the three communities showed great talents. Many guests suggested that separate exhibitions should be held by individual artists! It was a pleasure finding so many representative works of art from so many participants in one hall, both from the younger and older generations of artists! In the philatelic section, there were several interesting exhibits: "Masterpieces of Canadian Painting" by Benoit Carrier; "A Postal History of Jelgava" by Viesturs Šķidra; "Modern Latvia 1990-2002: Postal History" by Mārtiņš Štauvers; "National Folk Costumes – Estonia, latvia, lithuania from the 19th Century" by John Raud sepp. Two of the exhibits were particularly of a high calibre, meriting the two gold medals awarded by the two national judges John McEntyre and Benoit Carrier: – a superb "19th Century Finland. Coat of Arms Issues 1875- 1885" by Oleg Podymow; and a Superior thematic exhibit of 80 pages "Joseph Stalin – History\'s Greatest Criminal" by George Fiala, which was judged to be "Best-of-show"! This show was a unique experiment – a great deal of work and energy went into it and the experience gained from 12 years of organizing Montreeal Baltic Philatelic Club Expositions every spring served us well. It is to be recommended that the painters of our three Montreal Baltic Communities get together and begin planning for the forming of a club and holding yearly exhibitions – the talent is definitely there! □ J. J. Raudsepp']

Representative_Docs: ['The Latvian Legion (1943-45) Material supplied by the Ministrv of Foreign Affairs, Republic of Latvia * Recent news reports have erroneouslv associated membership m the Latvian Legion (1943-45) with Nazi atrocities committed during the Nazi Germany occupation of Latvia (1941-45). Historical evidence shovvs that the conscripted combat units of the Latvian Legion (designated by Berlin as the Waffen SS) could not and did not participa te m these atrocities. L The Latvian Legion was formed m 1943 after the Nazi occupation forces had completed their systematic destruction of latvia\'s Jewish population. Nazi occupation forces m Latvia created the Latvian Legion m 1943 through forced conscription, m violation of the Hague Convention. 4. Some of these individuāls did join the Latvian Legion m 1943 m order to disguise their criminal activīties. However, the Legion itself was not involved m actions against the Jewish population. Of the 140,000 men who served m the Latvian Legion 50,000 were casualties m combat against the Soviet army on the Eastern Front. On November 28, 1950 the U.S. Displaced Persona Commission Headquarters m Frankfurt, West Germany, APO 757, acknovvledged the above circuinstances m a memorandum concerning Baltie Waffen SS Units. After careful consideration, the Commission finallv decides that the Baltie Wafjen SS units were nota hostile movement within Section 13 of the Act, and membership m these units was nota bar per se." Robert Corkern, tIS DUplaced Persona Commisßion Coordiiiator for Kurope According to Harry Roeenfield, Comrnissioner of the US Displaced Persons Commission, the Commission also approved the follovvingstatement: "That the Baltie Waffen SS Units (Baltie Leģions) aretobe eonsidered as separate and distinct m purpose, ideology, activities and aualifications fbr membership from the German SS, and therefbre the Commission holds them not tobea movement hostile to the Government of the United States under section 13 ofthe Displaced Persons Act, as amended.9 Additional facts about the Vfaffen SS: Despite the "SS" designation, V/affen SS units were not associated with SS activities m the infamous Nazi death camps, nor did they participate m any actions against civilian population. These vvere combat units, often of an international nature. The decision of the United States Displaced Persons Commission m 1950 reinforces this distinetion by stating that the units ofthe latvian Legion "are to be eonsidered as separate and distinct m purpose, ideologv, acitivities and qua!ifications for membership from the German SS." Historical buckground: The Latvian government and armed forces vvere dissolved m the summer of 1940 follovving the Soviet Union\'s occupation and illegal annexation of independent Latvia. Tens of thousands of Latvians vvere deported and/or executed by the Soviet regime by June 1941. Nazi Germany invaded Latvia m July 1941, forcing a Soviet retreat to Russia. A nevv vvave of mass executions and deportations was initiated by the Nazi regime, focussing mainly (but not exclusively) on Latvia\'s Jevvish population. As the Nazi Germany invasion of the Soviet Union began to collapse m 1943, Hitler ordered the conscription of Latvian nationals m combat units known as the Latvian Legion (Waffen SS). Latvian eonseripts vvere sent to the Northern Sector ofthe Russian front m order to cover the retreat ofthe German Wehrmacht. The Legion was forced back to defensive lines on Latvian territory, vvhere major engagements took place m 1944. Latvian troops eventually retreated to Germany vvhere they became POWs under either US-British or Soviet jurisdietion. At no time did units ofthe Latvian Legion engage m combat against United States\' or other VVestern Allied forces. Their purpose vvas to pay tribute to Latvian soldiers vvho had fought and died m defence of their homeland.', 'The concept of the Latvian Legion, its creation and history Reference material supplied by the Ministr/ of Foreign Affairs, Republic of Latvia A. The latvian Voluntary SS Legion On January 23, 1943 the Fuehrer of the German State Ādolf Hitler „approved the idea of creating a Latvian Voluntary SS Legion and ordered such a Legion to be set up". In accordance vvith the order on January 24, 1943 the four Latvian self-defence batallions, included m the 2nd SS Brigāde, vvere joined together under the name of the Latvian Voluntary SS Legion. The formai vvritten order by Ādolf Hitler on the establishment of the Latvian Voluntarv SS Legion was executed only on February 10,1943. In order to clear up all misunderstandings connected vvith the introduction of the term "the Latvian Legion", Himmler had to state the follovving: "The name \'The Latvian Legion\' īsa collective term for all Latvian military units included m the Waffen SS and the police. The Latvian Legion reports to the Reichsfuehrer SS." (See note 1). At this point – May 1943 -- the follovving military units vvere established: t The Latvian SS Brigāde; 2. The Latvian Voluntary SS Division; 3. The Latvian police batallions. (By the order of the Reichsfuehrer SS from May 24, 1943 the Latvian self- defence batallions duc to their steadfastness m action had been honoured to be renamed "the Latvian Police Batallions" and included into the Latvian Voluntary SS Legion.) B. Latvian units m the Vfaffen During the war two Latvian Waffen SS divisions vvere set up. The Latvian Voluntary SS Division vvas set up the first (the establishing order vvas signed on February 15, 1943. From the end of 1943 the unit was involved m military action at the front). From October 1943 until June 1944 knovvn as the 15th Latvian Voluntary SS Division, it later became knovvn as the 15th Waffen SS Grenadier Division (Latvian No. 1). As of December 31, 1943, the total manpovver of the division vvas 15192 men. In June 30, 1944 ir comprised 18413 men. The 15th Division moetly was engaged m defence and retreat battles inßussia: from 18.11.43 til* 5.1.44 it fought m defence battles near Nevel; from 14.1.44 till 15.2.44 it vvas involved m battles north of Nevel. From 4.3 till 12.3.44 the Division vvas engaged m the first defence battles at Ostrov and Pskov, from 26.3 up to 11.4.44 it fought to the north of Opotchka, but from 24.4 till 21.6.44 it vvas involved m the stabilization battles m the territorv of the Commando Group Nord and later – m the retreat battles m the term to ry of Latvia. In September 1944 the remains of the Division vvere relocated to a militarv training centrē m the Western Prussia to be restructured. At the end of 1944 the 15th Division vvas battleready once again. In 1945 it participated m heavy fighting m Vvestern Prussia, m the vicinity of Danzig, m Pomerania, Mecklenburg and Mark Brandenburg. By the end of the vvar a significant part of the soldiers of the division and its supporting units managed to escape from the Eastern front and to capitulate to the VVestern AHies m the north of Germany. The second Latvian division vvas set up on the basis of the Latvian SS Brigāde vvhich, according to the Himmler\'s order from January 7, 1944, vvas reconstructed into the 19th SS Division. From 24.1.43 till 15.3.43 the Brigāde vvas knovvn as The Latvian SS Legion, but later – until 22.10.43 it was knovvn under the name of the Latvian Voluntary SS Brigāde. Then its name vvas changed onoe again and till 7.1.44 it bore the name of the 2nd Latvian Voluntary SS Brigāde. As of December 31, 1943 the to tai strength of the brigāde was 8033 men. Up to May 1944 this military unit was called the 19th Latvian Voluntary SS Division but after that it was known under the name of the 19th Waffen SS Grenadier Division (Latvian No. 2). As of June 30, 1944 the to tai manpovver of the division was 12592 soldiers and on October 20, 1944 it consisted of 12507 men. The Brigāde or the Division was involved m the battles at the Leningrad Front. After the end of the siege of Leningrad some units of the division fought at Wolchow and m the battles south of Pskov. From July till October 1944 the division was involved m the rearguard action at the Latvian border and m the territorv of Latvia itself. In October 1944 the 19th Division was relocated to Kurland vvhere it participated m fiveoutof the six serious battles m Kurland. It fināli}\' capitulated to the Red Army m May 1945 m Kurland. C. Conscription m the Waffen SS m Latvia The soldiers for the Latvian Waffen SS units were recruited by the following methods: L They were recruited by the inci usion of the Latvian self-defence batallions (2) of the Latvian Voluntarv SS Brigāde into the 19th Latvian Voluntarv SS Division. (The self-defence batallions generally were set up by volunteers m 1941 – m to tai sic Latvian batallions were united m a single Latvian brigāde.) Through inclusion of the Latvian police batallions m the Latvian Waffen SS division the Waffen SS acquired 5 additional batallions. The police batallions consisted of volunteers, the Latvian policemen, as wyll as of conscripted persons After the eetting up of the l&th Waffen SS Grenadier Division m August 1944 its personnel consisted of the Latvian border gvards, the Latvian voluntary policemen as well as members of the Latvian professional police (as of 14.5.44 the policemen vvere C\' iced under the command of the tvian Voluntary SS Legion). 2. The conscription [ mobilization (3) ] of whole age groups In the territory of Latvia mobilization effort was continued m the period betvveen March 1943 and August 1944 (m the territorv of Kurland it was also continued m September and October 1944) (4). The first wave of mobilization came m the period betvveen March and August 1943. During this period Latvian citizens from the age group 1919 to 1925 vvere mobilized. The mobilization was carried out according to the regulations of the obligatory labour service m the occupied Eastern territories adopted on December 19, 1941. Under the auspices of the Labour and Social Administration of the Generalkommisar a special mobilization headquarters was set up Lists of the people subject to the mobrlization orders vvere prepared by the police. The follovving figurēs vvere given m the conscription plan: SS Legion needed 15500 men (29.1%); the Wehrmacht had to receive 22000 men (41.5%) and civilian service (obligā-  labour programmēs) -- 15600 (29.4%). According to the mobilization guidelines 0f26.2.43 (the third, amended version of the guidelines, adopted on 16.2.43) the people subject te the obligatory labour service should be able to choose betvveen the service m the Legion, the Wehrmacht or m the civilian service. Nevertheless, already on March 8,1943 the "free choice" clause vvas cancelled because the practice of the mobilization process shovved the unvvillingless of the conscripted persons to choose the military options. Under these circumstances the second version of the guidelines from 16.2.43 vvas set m force. These guidelines stated that "the mobilization authoritv has to convince the conscripts to choose the service m the Legion, and only if the offer is cathegoricallv rejected, to appoint them to the voluntary auxiliary service (5) or to the labour units m the spheres vitallv necessary for the war effort." The heads of the mobilization offices had to divide the mobilized persons according the needs of the three abovementioned institutions. The first wave of the mobilization gave the follovving result: the SS Legion received 17971 men (mainly the Vfaffen SS units) (50.3%) – thus significantly overfilling the initial demand; the Wehrmacht got 12979 men (36.4%) and thetivilianlabour service conscripted 4769 men (13.3%). This time the conscription vvas orgānized under the auspices of the nemv established SS-Einsaizinspektion Ostland (SS-Ersatzkommando Lettland) and it vvas carried out by the Inspector-General of the Latvian Voluntary SS Legion. (5) From 1944 onvvards the Voluntary Auxiliary Service vvas called "the Combat Auxiliary Units". (Dr. hist.) Kārlis Kangeris, Scientific Researcher, Baltic Studies Centre, University of Stockholm April 27,1994.', 'SVEŠVALODU NODAĻA/OTHER LANGUAGES SECTION WHERE HISTORY WAS MADE: THE BATTLE OF CĒSIS – 80 YEARS AGO This year\'s longest day will mark the eightieth anniversary of the Battle of Cēsis, a crucial event in the struggle to secure the independence of the young republics of Latvia and Estonia. The combatants in this threeday military encounter in the vicinity of the old Livonian town were, on one side, an allied force of Estonians and I Avians – to be referred to as the Allies – and, on the other, the joint forces of the landeswehr ("liind-de fence") – consisting of Utvian Balto-Germans as well as volunteers and mercenaries from Germany – and elements of the Iron Division, a voluntary unit formed from troops of the German Army. To understand why and how this conflict erupted, one has to look back at the events and developments that precipitated it. The political and military situation in Latvia in early spring 1919 was, to put it mildly, muddled. Part of the country, including Riga, was in the hands of the (Russian and Utvian) Reds. The rest was held by the small latvian forces (several thousand men) loyal to the government of Kārlis Ulmanis, by the Landeswehr, and by voluteer units of German soldiers ("Freikorps") formed from the German forces that in 1918 had occupied most of the territory of the Baltic lands; these remained in liitvia after the Armistice with the consent of the Western Allies and the Latvian Government to get rid the country of the Bolsheviks. The government of Ulmanis was overthrown on April 16 by a unit of the Landeswehr, which then installed a puppet-government by Andrievs Niedra. Ulmanis and part of his cabinet eluded arrest and took retuge on board a slup anchored in Liepāja harbor under the protection of British warships, retaining their claim to legitimate authority. The real political power was now in the hands of a shadowy group of Balto-German nobles, backed by liindeswehr and the German troups. They hoped to renew their centuries-old domination of Latvia under the aegis of a renewed Russian monarchy after the expected defeat of the Bolsheviks in the Russia civil war then raging. Claiming the landeswehr to be the legitimate Latvian army, they prevented the small Latvian forces under col. Jānis Balodis from expanding, fearing that they, loyal to Ulmanis, would try to reinstate him. After lying low for a while to let the people have a taste of Red terror, the Landeswehr set out to play the role of saviour from it, capturing Riga in a lightning attack on May 22. However instead of joining the Latvians under Balodis in fighting the Reds remaining in Latvia, the main body of the Landeswehr, together with troops "borrowed" from the Iron Divisions, advanced towards the forces of the Estonian Army and the pro-tllmanis Latvian military in Vidzeme (Livonia) near the Estonian border, the Estonians having moved troops there under agreement with the Ulmanis government to aid their ally, the North Latvian army led by col. Zemitâns, a force of a few thousand men recently formed with Estonian aid, in the fight against the Reds. The planner and strategist of the operation was German general Rudiger von der Goltz, who had earlier with German troops helped the Finnish "Whites" defeat the "Reds" in a short but fierce war, and had in February7 assumed command of the Landeswehr and the German forces in l.atvia. A daring, imperialist-minded schemer he now wanted to force the Estonians out of Latvia to have a free hand to settle things there – driving out the Red forces and reining in or crushing the patriotic liitvian troops, and thus gaining control of the entire country for his clients, the Balto-German aristocracy. When the Landeswehr and the Iron Division troops made contact witli the Estonian-Latvian forces near Cēsis on June A, a parley was held at which the Landeswehr representatives demanded that the Estonians withdraw from the latvia territory. When this demand was rejected, a date was set for further negotiations, but before these could take place fighting, for which each side blamed the other, erupted on June 5, in the course of which the Germans seized Cēsis. A ceasefire was negotiated by a representative of the Allied Military Mission in the Baltic. Shortly thereafter the just-appointed head of the Mission, British genera] Sir Hubert Gough, demanded m a telegram to von der Goltz withdrawal from the conflict area, evacuation of half the German troops in Latvia, and allowing Ulmanis to return to power. The German general indignantly refused and advised the political leadership of the Landeswehr and the puppet-government to issue an ultimatum to the Estonians to leave  soil, and in case of non-compliance renew hostilities. This was done, the deadline being set for June 18 and on that date extended till the following day. When the Estonians stood firm, the attack was ordered. Fearing, as a German general, repercussions at the Versailles Peace Conference then just ending, von der GolU saw fit to refrain from being the nominal comander of the operation, launched in the name of the "Latvian government", but he had with his staff worked out the plan for it and a few days before the start of the battle come in civilian clothes from Riga to serve as de facto commander. The opposing forces were rattier evenly "matched Exact numbers have never been determined, but accord ing to best estimates the Allies had a slight numerical advantage (about 6000-5000). Of the Allied forces about one third were Lalvians. The German side was superior in fire-power. They had about twice as many artillery pieces and possessed mortars, which the Allies lacked; this advantage was to some extent offset by the heavier guns mounted on the three Estonian armored trains – cumbersome but often effective vehicles of warfare in that period. The Germans had one such train at their disposal. They also had an advantage in the numbrer of machine-guns and possessed a squadron of aircraft, mainly for reconnaissance, something the Allies lacked. Von der Goltz\'s battle-plan involved separate northward thrusts by the Iron Division and the  at some distance to its right. After deep penetrations of the Allied line the two would then link up, encircling and crushing the main body of the enemy forces. The Iron Division began its advance towards the .Allied positions on June 19 and launched its attack on them the following day in two separate columns. The smaller lefward one made rapid progress against light resistance, but the next morning it encountered stronger forces and suftered heavy losses; with its commander killed, it retreated south to its starting position. The other, larger column initially also made substantial gains but was then stopped cold by fierce resistance and was lett in a vulnarable position, its left flank periously exposed by the retreat of the other column. While the blunting of the Iron Division attack was vital in thet it foiled von der Goltz\'s encirlement plan, the main and decisive action of the battle of Cēsis occured to the east, where the Landeswehr troops, more than twice as numerous as those of the Iron Division, launched their assault on the Allied positions in the twilight of the northern summer light in the early hours of June 21. The fighting in the wooded hill-country of the "Livonian Switzerland" in the vicinity of Cesis was bloody from the start. The three-pronged infantry-attack backed by intense artillery-fire broke through Allied lines and led to an advance of up to 15 km towards the Estonian border, until it was stopped towards evening with the help of Estonian reserves and the intense cannon-fire of the armored trains; the deepest-penetrating force was thrown back, leaving behind some of its artillery. Fighting finally subsided at nightfall. The next morning saw the Estonian-Latvian allies launch a powerful counter-attack. All along the front the enemy was forced to retreat in fierce fighting. A diversionary unit even managed to infiltrate unnoticed behind enemy lines and create panic there. Nor could the Landeswehr expect any relief from the Iron Division, whose outflanked main column had, fearing encirclement during the preceding night retreated south to rejoin the earlier repulsed unit in defensive positions. By the evening of this decisive day the Allies were firmly in control of the military situations. Fearful of having their westward life-line and escape route cut by the enemy forces penetrating through or past the lines of the Iron Division, the battered Landeswehr troops abandoned their positions and the town of Cēsis during the night of June 22/23 and hastened southwest along the Riga highway, according to the memoirs of one of the fighters half-running at 8 km per hour. With the Allies, wary of ambush, slow to follow, the Landeswehr retreated behind a river and two lakes and took up defensive positions there. That day Gune 23) the Iron Division made an attempt to strike at the right flank of the advancing Allies but ran into heavy fire and retreated towards Riga, fighting rearguard actions. The defeat of the German forces was now complete; they had, however, largely escaped destruction and, the Landeswehr and the Iron Division linking up, managed to form a front-line. Casualties in the battle of Cēsis were rather heavy. Of the Allied force 123 were killed, of the Landeswehr 110. The losses of the Iron Division are not known but were no doubt also considerable. What with common estimate of three to four as many wounded as killed, the casualty rate was obviously quite high. By way of epilogue to the story of the battle of Cēsis, it may be mentioned that during the following days the victors, with at times fierce fighting, pushed their enemy towards Riga, intent on driving it from the city and opening the way for Ulmanis to return to power there. On July 2 this force reached the outskirts of the city and began making preparations, backed by bombardment of military installations by Estonian warships, to attack Riga, which Gen. Fodder, commander of the Estonian forces, thought could be taken in 36 hours. At this point the Allied Military Mission stepped in and convened a meeting of all parties, at which it was decided to avoid lurther bloodshed; the landeswehr and its allies would evacuate Riga, allowing latvian troops to enter and the Ulmanis government to assume control there. The landeswehr would then be reduced in size, reorganized, and sent to fight the Bolsheviks; thus reconstituted, it would be commanded by British lieutenant-colonel Harold Alexander (in World War II Field Marshal and later, now Earl Alexander of Tunis, Governor General of Canada). Thus this force came yet to play a useful, if minor, role alongside the Latvian Army, which was now rapidly expanded to become a formidable fighting force, as was proved a few months later when it repelled the attack on Riga of the si/able mercenary army, composed mostly of German war-veterans, of the Russian-Caucasian adventurer Col. Bermondt-Avalov and drove it out of Latvia. In addition to seeing their dreams of hegemony in  and no doubt Estonia as well, dissipate in the battle-smoke over the hills and dales around Cēsis, the Balto-Germans of the Landeswehr suffered a blow to their pride that they found hard to live down and that emerges vividly in reminiscences published by some of them These reflect their shock and bewilderment bordering on disbelief that they, proud German warriors, many of them considering themselves heirs ot the Teutonic knights, and a unit of German troops have been defeated by and forced to flee from soldiers they had thought vastly inferior as fighters. All agree that they underestimated the fighting spirit and ability of the Estonians and Latvians, many of them battle-hardened veterans of World War I, others highly-motivated patriotic teenagers, troops led by officers seasoned by the Great War. In his book Meine Sendung in Filmland uiid Balticum ("My Mission m Finland and the Baltic"), p/ 211, von der GolU blames the defeat mainly on faulty intelligence assesment of the enemy\'s fighting ability, based largely on what was thought a mediocre performance by the Estonian Army against Soviet forces – a rather strange impression in view of the fact that the Estonians had driven the numerically greatly superior invaders from their land and even occupied strategic areas across the Russian border. Some of the Germans involved say they expected the enemy to be no harder to deal with than the Reds, whom they had easily routed. If so, they were obviously in for rude awakening; thus, according to Major Alfred Fletcher of the German Army, the field-commander of the landeswehr forces at Cēsis, the Estonians and fatvians there were "100% better" than the Bolsheviks. And von der Goltz says they "fought with passion" (p. 211). The performance of the Baltic comrads-in-arms gets high marks not only from the losers but also from historians. Thus German historian Claus Grim states in his book Vor den Toren Europas ("Before the Gates of Europe"), p. 258: "Oh* had to conclude, not without surprise, that the Estonians un Zemitāns Ijetts fought with as much skill as fervor". And in his book Estonia (p. 142) J. Hamden Jackson opines that the decisive factor behind the defeat of the German side was "the elan" of their oponents. The Estonian decision to confront the Germans was by no means based solely on desire to help a friendly neighbor; Realpolitik played an important role in it. The Estonians were keenly aware that if the hegemony of the Balto-Germans was established in Latvia, they would be far more likely to suffer the same fate themselves, what with colonialist-minded adventurers like von der Goltz and Bermond-Avalov fishing in the troubled waters of the Baltic and their recruiter of mercenaries dangling before the eyes of unemployed German war-veterans the prospect of land and preferred status in the autonomous Baltic provinces of a renewed imperial Russia. But whatever the exact motivation of the Estonians for extending a helping hand to the Utvians in their hour of need, the co-operation and comradeship-in-arms of the two neighbours in the fateful days of June 1919 is a shining example of the strength in unity of the Baltic countries, worthy to be always remembered and, should the need arise, emulated. □ Martin Puhvel • The battle holds a personal, rather poignant interest for the author – his maternal uncle, a voluteer soldier not quite eighteen years old, was among those killed in the fighting. It must be mentioned also that this year marks the 80th anniversary of this very importand event.']

Representative_Docs: ['BR 4 Edited by Vidvuds Koskins FOR MORE DETAILED INFORMATION ON LATVIA you can look at the following World Wide Web sites: http://www.president.lv/ – Chancery of the President of Latvia http\'7/www.mk.gov.lv/eng/ – Cabinet of Ministers http://wvw.saeima.lanet.lv/ – Saeima (the Parliament of Latvia) http://www.cs.llu.lv/ZemMin/index.html – Ministry of Agriculture http://www.mcxj.tv7- Ministry of Defence httpy/www.lem.gov. Iv/ – Ministry of Economics  – Ministry of Education and Science http://www.varam.gov.lv/-Ministry of Environmental Protection and Regional Development http://fm-ctom-2.fmgov.lv/ – Ministry of Finance http7/www.iem.gov.lv/-fvlinistry of the Interior  – Ministry of Justice http://www.lm.govM- Ministry of Welfare http://www.lda.gov.lv/-Latvian Development Agency http://www.lpa.bkc.iv/- Latvian Privatisation Agency http://www.bank.iv/ – Bank of Latvia http://www.np.gov.lv/- Naturalisation Board http://www.latnet.lv/ – Welcome to Latvia http://www.EUnet.lv/-Latvia Online! http"i/vip.latnet.lv/culture/-Culture in Latvia http://www.rigaBoo.iv/- Riga 800 http://www.latinst.lv/- Latvian Institute http://www.companies.fv – Companies and ■ Shops in Riga http://www.rigathisweek.rv – Guide to Riga http://www.riga.lv – Guide to Riga http://www.rigalatvia.com – Riga Tourism http://re-lab.net – Art centre E-Lab', 'Latvian Tourism Development Programme for 2006 – 2008 approved On 4 July, the Cabinet of Ministers approved the Latvian Tourism Development Programme for 2006 – 2008 drawn up by the Ministry of Economy The goal of the Programme is to promote development of the tourism sector, to ensure annual valueadded increase of 10% -15% and an annual increase in the percentage of tourism sector in GDP of 20%. The Programme was prepared to ensure the achieving of the goals outlined in the Basic concept of Latvian tourism development for 2004 – 2006 and in accordance with the course of action set out in it. The Programme includes events to enhance the image of Latvian tourism and to promote its recognition, to develop and improve the tourism infrastructure, to promote the development of tourism products and services and to improve cooperation between the state and the private sector and on an international level. The programme also anticipates the carrying out of tourism research which would help better determine new courses of action', 'Latvia\'s main task in the tourism sector for 2005 is consolidation of country\'s tourism image According to the Latvian tourism development plan for 2004 drafted by the Ministry of Economics, the country\'s main task in the tourism sector for 2005 is consolidation of Latvia\'s tourism image, including airing of an advertisement clip on CNN, publication of a wide range of tourism information and other tasks. Director of the State Tourism Development Agency (TAVA), Uldis Vitolins, told that the airing of the advertisement clip on CNN is agency\'s main project for this year. It is planned to air the 45-second clip on a regular* basis from March 15 to April 15. The choice of this time of year, Vitolins explained, was based on the presumption that this is the time when tourists make their last decision on where to spend the holiday season". The clip will be broadcasted two to three times a day. Director of TAVA explained that the main idea of the clip is to promote Latvia as a country "where you want to return again and again". Airing of the clip will cost Latvia four thousand lats and it is developed by the advertisement agency "Metro Leo Burnett&Cuba Studio". Another tasks for 2005 include improvement of tourism-related web resources about Latvia, participation in international tourism fairs and organisation of forums and seminars on tourism opportunities in Latvia. 300 roadsigns informing the travellers about sightseeing objects and tourism facilities will be fitted on Latvian roads along with 35 tourism information stands, setting up of special network of parking lots for tourists and other improvements. The Latvian government has allocated more than 310 thousand lats for restoration of cultural monuments. 3 million lats will be allocated to development of infrastructure of green tourism (European Union participates with a 2.2 million co-funding), while the funding for motorway infrastructure improvements amounts to 29.1 million lats. The Central Statistical Bureau informs that in the nine months of 2004, Latvia was visited by 19.6% more tourists than in the same period of 2003, crossing the Latvian border 2.308 million times.']

Representative_Docs: ['Riga airport still leader among Baltic states airports Riga International Airport last year managed to retain its leader position among the three largest Baltic states airports, based on the number of passengers and flights served, and amount of freight loaded. In 2006, Riga International Airport served almost 2,495 million passengers, 32.9% more than in 2005. Tallinn airport served 1,541 million passengers (10% up on 2005), and Vilnius Airport, almost 1,451 million passengers (13.2% up on 2005). 40,162 flights were serviced at Riga Airport in 2005, an increase of 16.2%, while Tallinn serviced 33,989 flights (upl .1 %) and Vilnius served 29,347 flights (up 0.5%). 12,559 tonnes of freight were loaded at Riga Airport (down 21% on 2005), but Riga Airport was stiil the leader in volume of freight loaded. 5,565 tonnes were loaded at Vilnius Airport (up 5.4% on 2005), while Tallinn recorded a growth of 4.3%, loading 10,361 tonnes.', "Riga International Airport maintains leader position among Baltic States Riga International Airport in 2005 managed to retain its leader position among the three largest Baltic airports, based on passenger numbers, number of flights, and freight volumes. In terms of passenger numbers and freight, Riga International Airport led throughout the year, with Estonia's Tallinn Airport leading in some months in the number of flights serviced. Last year Riga International Airport served almost 1.9 million passengers, 77.1% more than in 2004. It serviced 34,552 flights, an increase of 26.4%, and loaded 15,896 tons of freight, 81.6% more than in 2004. In December of 2005, Riga International Airport served 160,571 passengers, 52.7% more than-in December 2004, serviced 2,638 flights, an increase of 14%, and loaded 736 tons of freight, 18.9% more than in December 2004.", "In January Riga Airport records highest number of passengers and flights among Baltic States. Riga International Airport in January recorded the highest number of passengers and flights among Baltic States, while the most freight was loaded in Tallinn. In the first month of this year, Riga International Airport served 160,378 passengers, 585% more than in January 2005, serviced 2,639 flights (16.3% more than January 2005), and loaded 524 tons of freight (32.3% less than January 2005). Tallinn occupies the leader position in terms of freight with 527 tons loaded (up 46.1 % on last year). Riga International Airport managed to retain its leader position among the three major Baltic States' airports, based on passengers served, number of flights, and freight loaded. Last year almost 1.9 million passengers were served at Riga International Airport, 77.1% more than in 2004. Riga"]
