Representative_Docs: ['UNEMPLOYMENT IN LATVIA DOWN TO 8.4% The unemployment rate keeps falling in Latvia and was 8.4% as of 1 July, 2000, the record low rate since 1987, the National Employment Service Unemployment Records, Analysis and Forecast Unit reported on 10 July. In early June the unemployment rate in Latvia was 8.6% of the economically active population. The unemployment rate in Latvia was the lowest in 1997 when this figure was 7% on average. In recent years the rate was usually above 9%. In April 1999 a record high unemployment (102%) was registered in Latvia because many people became jobless as a result of the Russian crisis. Edited by Vidvuds Koskins', 'UNEMPLOYMENT IN LATVIA DOWN TO 8.1% The unemployment rate keeps falling in Latvia and was 8.1% as of 1 September, 2000, the record low rate since 1997, the National Employment Service Unemployment Records, Analysis and Forecast Unit head Ilga Upeniece said on 11 September, In early August the unemployment rate in Latvia was 8.2% of the economically active population. At the beginning of July this figure was 8.4% and in early June 8.6%. The unemployment rate in Latvia was the lowest in 1997 when this figure was 7% on average. In recent years the rate was usually above 9%. In April 1999 a record-high unemployment (10.2%) was registered in Latvia because many people became jobless as a result of the Russian crisis. In January and February 2000 the unemployment rate was 9.1% but in March and April 9%.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER CRISES IN RUSSIA IMPACTS LATVIA Due to the economic crises in Russia the unemployment rate in Latvia at the end of the year reached 8,8%. MINIMAL WAGE IN THE BALTICS The highest minimal wage among the Baltic countries has been in Lithuania. According to the data of the Department of Statistics in Lithuania the minimal wage in Lithuania in the month of November was $107,5. The minimal wage in Estonia – $83.9, but in Latvia it was $73,6. Starting in 1997 the minimal wage in Lithuania has risen by 8%, in Latvia by 11% and in Estonia by 30%. PERSON OF THE YEAR ACCORDING TO THE "BALTIC OBSERVER" Newspaper "The Baltic Times" has awarded "The Person of the Year" title to four people – the leader of the People\'s party (Tautas partija) Andris Šķēle, the President of the Central Bank of Estonia Vahur Kraft, the President of Lithuania Valdas Adamkus and the High Commissioner of OSCE Max van der Stuhl. NO VISAS NEEDED ANYMORE The countries of Shengen treaty (Germany, Italy, France, Spain, Austria, Greece, Belgium, Netherlands, Luxembourg and Portugal) have agreed that there is no need for travelers from Latvia to have visas when they travel to their countries. This resolution will take effect starting March Ist 1999. Latvia already has no-visa regimes with 29 countries, but to travel to the countries of the treaty one had to have a visa for at least one of those countries. EVENTS OF THE YEAR The Minister of Foreign Affairs of Latvia Valdis Birkavs has named the five most significant events in the country\'s foreign affairs in 1998. The events are as follows: the Charter signed between US and the Baltic states, Latvia\'s entering the World Trade Organization, the meeting of the Counsel of the countries bordering the Baltic Sea, the shutting down of Skrunda Radio locator and the "opening of Europe" which is the no-visa regime with the countries of Shengen agreement. February 1999 NEWS FROM LATVIA – reported by Šarlote Līduma in Rīga "LA VIE s" CHOICE OF THE EUROPEAN OF THE YEAR The respectable French Su weekly magazine "La Vie" has announced the President of Estonia Lennart Meri the European of the Year in 1998. This decision was made by taking into consideration L. Meri\'s role in regaining the independence of Estonia and the rebirth of the nation. MILITARY TRAINING IN MICHIGAN The National Guard of Michigan in collaboration with the Armed forces of the Baltic states will organize a joint training called "Partner Challenge". 30 Emergency Volunteers (zemessargi) from each of the Baltic states as well as guards from different squads of the US National Guard will participate in the training. This undertaking will be the first one of such kind for the Baltic countries. ESTONIAN RESERVISTS WELL EQUIPPED The Secretary of Ministry of Defense of Estonia recently announced that in case of a military threat Estonia can equip and arm 53% of all the reservists. There are about 150 thousand reservists in Estonia from which up to 80 000 can be made combat-ready. In Estonia all men up to the age of 35 can participate in the training of reservists. Most of the reservists who are younger than 30 years of age, have done military service in the Defense forces of Estonia. RUSSIAN MILITARY MEN LEAVING SKRUNDA RADIO LOCATION UNIT The Department of Citizenship and Migration has received official information about the Russian military personnel of Skrunda Radio location unit who had to leave Latvia until January Ist, 1999. Their visas were extended several times due to the difficulties of finding housing back in Russia. The last time their visas were lengthened under a condition that they will leave Latvia till January Ist, 1999. It is official now, that all 40 military men with their families have left Latvia. There are still many Russian military personnel in Skrunda who are responsible for dismantling the station. They are allowed to stay in Latvia until February 29, of the year 2000. THE POPULATION OF LATVIA STILL DECREASING The statistics about the critical demographic situation last year haven\'t gotten any better. In Rīga there were 5435 births in 1998. It constitutes 70 less newborns than in 1997. Last year the number of deaths in Rīga has decreased by 42 in comparison to 1997, but still the number of newborns doesn\'t come anywhere near the number of deaths registered in the capital city. The number of inhabitants of Latvia is still decreasing altogether. In January 1, 1998 there were 2 458 403 people living in Latvia, but by October the number had already dropped to 2 444 581. In the first ten months of the last year, 2752 more people have left Latvia, than the ones coming to Latvia. In total numbers 32 360 people have left Latvia, versus the 29 608 that have entered to the country. ON CITIZENSHIP After the new citizenship law has taken effect about 2.5 thousand non-citizens have expressed their wish to become Latvian citizens. In 1998 alone more than 4 thousand non-citizens were granted citizenship. So far this has been the highest number of people to receive the citizenship after the beginning of the naturalization. All together 11 432 persons have gone through the naturalization process and become citizens. INFLATION IN THE BALTICS According to the statistics, the inflation rate among the three Baltic states has been lowest in Lithuania – 2,4%. In 1998 in Latvia the inflation rate was 2,8%, in Estonia – 6,5%. THE NEED FOR ID CARDS FINALLY RECOGNIZED The Ministry of Internal Affairs is working on a project about issuing an entirely new identification document – the ID card. It is anticipated that the new card will work as a substitute for the passport. In Latvia the passport still is the only official document of identif due to its size and importance makes things complicated. The new ID card will be issued no earlier than year 2001. THE ELECTIONS OF THE PRESIDENT COMING UP The Social Democrats of Latvia have announced that it would be very good if the next President of Latvia would be a woman. They think that it might play a big role when EU countries will vote about Latvia entering the EU. As one of the possible candidates for this post they have named Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga – a Latvian from Canada, currently living in Rīga. Other parties also are starting to name their candidates for the Presidential post. So far "Latvijas ceļš" candidate Anatolijs Gorbunovs has withdrawn his name for this post. The same is true for the candidate from "Jaunā partija", Raimonds Pauls. "Tēvzemei un Brīvībai/LNNK" still haven\'t decided who will be proposed for this office from their party, but Guntars Krasts s name has come up as one of the candidates. "Tautas partija" will announce their selection not earlier than in the Spring of \'99. Most likely Mrs. Vaira Paegle from the U.S. will be the one proposed from their party. CERTIFICATES COUNTERFEITED The inspection responsible for controlling of the official language of Latvia has discovered 120 cases when Russian teachers have counterfeited the certificate of Latvian proficiency. Those teachers have been given a month now to pass the exam in Latvian. RESPECTED MEMBERS OF THE SOCIETY ASK TO BAN A BOOK Many well-known Latvians have protested against publishing of the book "Baigais gads" (The Horrible Year) which consists of many photographs featuring the victims of communist barbarity during the 1940/41 Soviet occupation. The authors of the appeal are addressing the President of Latvia Guntis Ulmanis, the Parliament of Latvia, government and other authorities to stop the "nazi-like escapades which do a great deal of damage to the name of Latvia in the eyes of the World". PROPOSAL FROM THE LEFT WING OF SAEIMA The deputies from the left wing of Saeima have proposed to recognize the authoritarian regime of former Latvian president Kārlis Ulmanis as a criminal offense against Satversme (The Constitution of Latvia). However, it seems unlikely that the politicians from other parties will support this proposal. NEW MAGAZINE ABOUT LATVIA The Agency of Development of Latvia together with "Global Media and Publishers" are working on a new project – they are preparing for publishing a magazine called "Doing Business with Latvia". The printing run of the magazine will be 3000 copies and for the most part it will be distributed abroad. Each issue will be dedicated to a different branch of Latvian business. IMPORTS AND EXPORTS TO AND FROM THE EU According to the magazine "Transitions" in terms of export items to the countries of the EU, Latvia is number one among the Baltic states. During the last year Estonia has been exporting to the countries of EU 52% of their whole export and imported from the EU – 60% of all the import. Lithuania has exported 34% from all the export goods to the EU countries and imported from the EU 47%. Latvia, however, has exported 54% to the EU and imported 55%. MORE ESTONIANS THAN BEFORE In the last ten years the share of Estonians in Estonia has grown by 3.5%. In 1989 there were 61.5% ethnic Estonians living in the country. This year the number has reached 65%. However, despite the natural growth of population, the Estonians haven\'t reached the level of natural increase of ethnic survival. A SILVER MEDAL IN BIATHLON Olegs Maluhins from Latvia has won the Silver medal in biathlon in Slovakia in the distance of 10 km. He lost 19.5 seconds to the winner Sven Fischer from Germany THE FIRST NIGHT OF "DON HUAN" POSTPONED In 1787 when Mozart wrote his "Don Huan", the opening of "Don Huan" had to be postponed three times. Now the National Opera of Latvia has to postpone the opening night show of "Don Huan" because of the lack of available resources. The Latvian National Opera, however, has decided to go ahead and perform without the stage decorations and the costumes needed, thus expressing theirattitude towards the poor financing of National Opera by the State. ['COUNCIL OF THE BALTIC STATES ON THE WITHDRAWAL OF TROOPS

The Council of the Baltie States acknow ledges the commencement of dialogue between the Baltie States and the Russian Federation on the issue of the withdrawal of the former USSR military troops, currently under the jurisdietion of Russia, from the territory of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. At the same time, the Council of the Baltie States is forced to acknovvledge that this dialogue is proceeding unsatisfactorily and with this, there stili exists a threat to the State sovereignty of the Baltie States and to the peace and stability in this region. Even now, the October 5 statement of the Council of the Baltie States request for the immediate withdrawal of military troops from the Baltie capitals, as well as the withdrawal of offensive troops, namely paratrooper units and strategie air force units, has continued to be ignored. The January 5,1992 appeal to the heads of State of the members of the Commonwealth of independent States to recall their citizens who are serving in the former USSR’s armed forces which should be withdrawn from the territories of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania has remained unansvvered, with the exception of Azerbaijan.

The former USSR military forces, which are to be withdrawn from the territories of the Baltie States, continue to act as conque rors and disregard the sovereignty and legislation of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. This is furthered by the lack of their clear subordination to the Russian Government. VVithout notice to and the approval of the Governments of the Baltie States, the above mentioned military troops relocate their units and continue to hold exercises in artillery firing and land and sea shelling. The military troop units continue to cause damage to environment. The military troops have begun illegal business activities, namely, the sale of vveapons and property and the clearing of forests. The Council of the Baltie States expresses its protest against the above mentioned violations of the sovereignty of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania and requests Russia to undertake the most decisive measures to terminate such activities. The Council of the Baltie States catego rically protests against the continued presence, in the sovereign territory of independent States, of the former border guard units of the former USSR which hinder the realization of the defense of the

State borders of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania with the Baltie States’ own forces. Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania stili con tinue to demand material compensation for the property, arms and equipment of their armies, which was seized in 1940 by the Soviet Union, and which is presently necessary for the renewal of their State defence capability, as well as compensation for other losses caused by the presence of the former USSR military forces in the Baltie States territory. The Council of the Baltie States invites Russia, which has proelaimed itself suc cessor of the USSR’s rights and obligations, to realize a policy which would promote the strengthening of regional and European security, the reduetion of tensions and the inerease of confidence. The aforementioned is contradicted by the intention to station a great number of military troops, which have

been withdrawn from Central and eastem Europe, as well as from the Baltie States, in close proximity to the borders of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. The Council of the Baltie States eapres ses its hope that the Russian Federation wi!l demonstrate its goodwill and will do everything to actually and immediately withdraw military forces currently under its jurisdietion from the territory of the Baltie States. The Republics of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania, on their part, are ready to eonelude an agreement on the terms and procedures for the withdrawal of the military forces.

Later m the debatē Senator James McClelland deplored the fact thet the Baltie states had been taken over by the Soviet Union. All day long Government senators have deplored the fact that the Soviet Union had taken over the Baltie States. Yet not one, either by his words or his contribution to the debatē, has said that the Government might at least have adopted a more sympathetic, a more understanding and indeed a more honest approach when making a decision on this matter. I turn to the subject raised m the second paragraph of Senator Greenvvood's motion. It refers to the Baltie states. Reference has been made to it by Ministers and by otherhonourable senators who have spoken m this debatē. Almost daily members of Parliament are confronted with problems and issues that arise from the actions of the government of the day. Some of these matters affectpeople m away which may not totally please them. But the Government's decision to recognise the incorporation of the Baltie states m the Soviet Union is the most extraordinary decision I have heard of for many years. It affects many tens of thousands of people m Australia. Of course it affects a great many more people m the states of Lithuania, Estonia and Latvia. Maybe there was no Government decision. From my recollection of the ansvvers vvhich were given to questions that 1 asked m the Senate, I do not know who made a decision, if a decision was made or how the decision came about. The decision, such as it was, was not related to trade or economics or defence or even to international aid. It related to nothing but the people who lived vvithin the Baltie communities. THEIR GOALS ARE VERY HIGH" The Baltie people who live m Australia represent a substantial segment of the population. They are hard-working people. They are succesful and they are contributing to our Standard of life. Their goals are very high- They are people who were driven from their homeland and who re tai nod the hope that one day their homeland at least would be free and that if m the fullness of time this hope was not fulfilled m the way m which they vvanted it to be fulfilled, at least the peoples of the free vvorld would recognise the situation m vvhich they were placed. These people were driven aeross the vvorld liy^CQieJ^eyasni^jnd^he^vjannK ment, for no apparent reason, has by its decision condoned this cruelty. It has accepted the circumstances by vvhich the Baltie people were driven out of their country. It has accepted the circumstances by which the Baltie people were forced to creep dangereously by night and to hide as best they could by day. If honourable senators opposite have ever shared m the experiences of these people as I have, they will know that the things which I say are true. The Government has placed itself m the company of the invader. It has placed itself m the company of the tyrant, the dietator, and the prevented of freedom. It is no wonder that when the event took place I deseribed the decision to recognise the incorporation of the Baltie states by the Soviet Union as cruel and unnecessary. The Government by its decision greatly undermined the work vvhich already had been done by the VVestern powers and this is a very important point for the Senate to remember. The work of obtaining more freedom m intemational arrrangements and undersnatdings by the VVestern powers has been greatly undermined. In the future it wiU make it more difficult for the VVestern powers to continue to work to aehieve the freedom and independence of the smaller states. All the work that had been done m the past has been greatly damaged by the Australian action. "EXAMPLE TO ALL AUSTRALIANS" Because of the involvement of the migrant policy, the Government's decision has denied its own stated policy of ensuring the happiness, reunion and peace of all migu has iett the Estonians, Larvians and Lithuanians completely isolated and completely unrecognised. Senate censure over Baltics Anyone who has had anything to do with the Baltie communities will know that the emphasis which these groups place on freedom is an example to all Australians, and their reminder of the price to be paid for freedom has been completely disregarded by this Government. Most serious at all, the Government has shovvn its own attitude ot the tragie events which led to the dominance of the Baltie states by the Soviet Union. The Government's attitude – and this has been exemplified today – has been one of indifference to smaller states which have been overrun by a merciless great povver. The Government will see tomorrovv what these people think of the Government's decision m this matter. Some 2,000 people will assemble m Canberra to give effect to their own feelings m this matter and to let the Government knovv how strongly they feel about the Government's decision to recognise the incorporation of the Baltie states m the Soviet Union. It is no wonder that many of us have been constrained to deseribe the decision as being cruel and unnecessary. It has caused a great deal of heartache and grief to the Baltie communities throughout Australia. Those people – and I was one of them – who went from spccial meeting to special meeting of Baltie communities on that particular Sunday afternoon will know how these loyal, hardvvorking, honest and decent Australians – former Lithuanians, Estonians and Latvians – were reduced to grief, anxiety, great sorrow and great disappointment because the nation vvhich they had trusted had let them down. VVhen Parliament reassembled 1 sought to get some August last I asked the Minister for Foreign Affairs a number of question m the Senate. The replies to Oiose questions appear from page 781 onvvards of the Senate Hansard of that date. Other questions m relation ot the Baltie sommunities were asked by my colleague from South Australia, Senator Young. I was not at all satisfied with the Minister's ansvvers. I sent telegrams to the Prime Minister (Mt. VVhitlam), and the best that he could do was to send mc a phot ostāt copy of pages of Hansard containing the ansvvers to vvhich I have referred. Among these ansvvers the Minister said: It is unlikely that thes Soviet Government vvill grant independence to these territories. Here is an admission by the Government that the Soviet Union is acting against the charter of the United Nations. One sees m the platform of the Labor Party that its members sobseribe to the policies of the United Nations charter. Here is an admission by the Government that the Soviet Union is acting against the charter of tfie United Nations dealing vvith the self-determination of people. It is also an admission by the Government that the Soviet Union is acting against its ovvn constitution vvhich provides, amongst other things, that every union republic shall have the right freely to secede from the USSR. Surely the Minister for Foreign Affairs vvas not serious vvhen he said that the recognition of the incorporation of the Baltie states by the Soviet Union vvill facilitate consular activities. particularly m relation to the reunion of families. Does he really beUeve it? Surely he must knovv that m relation to any emigration from the Baltie states permits to emigrate are issued not by the puppet governments m the Baltie states but m Moscovv itself. The reasons put forvvard by the Minister m connection vvith both these issues are, m my, vievv, completeh/ unconvincing. I am of the vievv that they dishononr, cleaj\ undertakings. They, . are our of step with the rest of the world. They recognise and condone gravē injustices that have been commited. They deny the Government's claim to champion small nations. To cap it all, they led to a decision vvhich was carried out, as the motion says, furtively and shamefully. So the whole issue is one thet gives reason for sondemnation not only because of its cruelty but also because of its betrayal. When there is betrayal there is cause for very great concern. I now want to read out a letter from the Prime Minister. This letter was vvritten to the President of the Council of the Lithuanian Community. It is written on 17 May which was the day before the last election. It was m reply to an carlier letter from the President of the Council of the Lithuanian Community m Australia. In the opening sentence there is reference to an earlier communication from the Council. The Prime Minister went on to say: The policy of the present Australian Government is that vvhile not formally recognising the incorporation of Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia into the Soviet Union, it must be cognisant of the dc facto situation and deal vvith the government vvhich has effective control of the territorv m question. On a number of occasions the Government has made its vievvs knovvn on the question of civil liberties and on fundamental human rights, its own adherence to them and its vvish that those rights embodied m the Universal Declaration of Human Rights be extended evervvvhere. In an address at the United Nations Association on the occasion of Austraiia's celebration of the 25th Anniversary of the Universal Declaration I said, inter alia, . . . 'the Declaration has come to be recognised as one of the enlightened events m modern history' and that it is a . . . fundamental objective of the Labor Government to ensure that Australia's policies are soundly based on respect for and on the protection and enhancement of civil liberties and basie humaņ rights'. The Soviet Government is avvare of Australian Government's position m this matter, and vve vvill continue to seek opportunities m the United Nations to promote respect for and observance of basie human rights by all members. Senator GEORGES (Lab., Qld.): VVho signed that letter? Senator DAVIDSON: It is a statement by the Prime Minister of this country. It is a statement by the Leader of the Government m this country. It is a statement by the man who, according to the Hansard, from vvhich I have quoted, made the decision m relation to the Baltie states. According to page 784 of Hansard of Tuesday, 13 August, vvhen I asked Senator Willesee vvhether he made the decision he said: 'No, the decision vvas made vvhen I vvas out of the country.' The inference vvas that the decision vvas made by the Acting Minister for Foreign Affairs. Yet here m this letter the Prime Minister conveyed to this Council, vvhich represents some 40,000 to 50?000 peoplem Australia anda vvhole lot of vvell vvishers beyond, an assurance that the Labor Government vvould promote respect for and observance of basie human rights by all members of the United Nations. It is no vvonder that there is also support for the third part of the motion vvhich is novv before the Senate, that is, that the foreign policy alignments are not serving and vvill not serve Australia's interests. The ACTING DEPUTY PRESI DENT (Senator Marriott): I call Senator Greenvvood vvho vvill be closing the debatē. Senator GREENWOOD (Liberal, Victoria) (10.32): . . . The second legof the motion states: (ii) m breach of a clear undertaking to the contrary given by the Prime Minister the Government shamefullv and furtively extended recognition to the incorporation of the Baltie States m the U.S.S.R., the Minister vvithholding any announcement or explanation of the decision. What the Government has failed to acknovvledge m the course of this debatē is why an undertaking given m May vvas dishonoured m August. The basie proposition vvhich vve assert is that m May the Prime Minister (Mr. VVhitlam), m response to questions asked of him, said that the Government did not propose to change the existing policy. STATEMENT TO LATVIAN NEVVS I shall quote from a letter from the editor of the 'Latvian News' vvhich vvas vvritten to the Australian Press. I quote from the Hobart 'Mercury' of 6 August 1974. The Prime Minister vvas asked: Q: Do you plan to touch on the question at ail, during your coming cisit to Moscovv? A: No. Q: So you are, m fact, saying that your policy m the matter is the same as that of the previous Government? A: Yes. Q: Do you intend to change it? A: No. That vvas stated prior to the election on 18 May. Why then did the Government change its policy? VVe have not been given an ansvver, and the anly attempt at an ansvver by the Minister vvas that the Government recognised the realities. VVere not those realities vvhich the Government novv relies upon the realities of May 1974? Has anything heppened betvveen May and July vvhich vvarranted a change? Senator CAVANAGH (Labor, S.A.): Yes, the people voted. Senator GREENVVOOD: Senator Cavanagh says 'yes\ VVhat vvas it? Senator CAVANAGH: The people voted. They locked you out. Senator GREENVVOOD: Senator Cavanagh may be gfving a revealing ansvver. VVhen the Government got the vote of the people it felt it eould make any decision it pleased and so it repudiated the promise. That is the fundamental basis of the Opposition's motion vvhich says that the Minister is deserving ot censure because a promise to uphold the ideal of freedom, cherished by people who came to this country and by theri descendents, was utterly and shamefully repudiated. Therefore it is a matter vvhich we say is deserving of a censure... There is one final point to vvhich I refer. It has been suggested by Senator Steele Hall that he found himself m a predicament m regard to this resolution. As he savv the position – and he expressed it asis his right – he felt that there vvas merit m the second leg of this three-pronged motion and vvould have voted if he had the opportunity against the First and third legs but m favour of the second leg. I suggest to him that it is not easy to be the independent or the individual senator m this chamber. The motion vvhich the Opposition has moved is a resolution of the Opposition parties and m putting it forvvard I am discharging an obligatiok vvhich I have as Deputy Leader of the Opposition m the Senate to put forvvard the Opposition's vievvpoint. If Senator Steele Hall desires to eliminate from the motion any part of it to vvhich he objects he may move an amendment to the motion. But he has not moved an amendment. Alternatively he may ask that the matter be taken sepaeately and if he had 1 am sure that we vvould have conceded the right of an independent senator to have that approach accepted. But as I understand it, it is a matter for the Senate ultimately to decide. Senator DOUGLAS McCLELLLAND (Lab., N.S.VV. – Minister for the Media and Manager of Government Business m the Senate) In vievv of the remarks just made by Senator Greenvvood I suggest that the motion moevd by him be put m three separate parts. Senator GREENWOOD: lf the Minister proposes it or if Senator Steele Hall proposes it vve vvill not oppose it. VVe have put our motion as vve decided. Senator CAVANAGH: I rise to order. After a motion has been moved and the moved has replied, is it not your duty, Mr. President, to put the question? Can the Senate then rearrange the motion? I spoke m this debatē but if the motion is going to be separated it may vvell be that I vvill vvant to speak on it as it vvill be put and m this case it vvill be a different motion from that to vvhich I spoke. Senator VVITHERS: I rise to order. As I understand standing order 130 you have the right to divide a difficult question. That is your prerogative. The Opposition is not asking you to divide the question but I understand that Senator Douglas McClelland... Senator STEELE HALL: I rise to order. 1 understood that Senator VVithers vvas speaking to standing order 130.1n the debatē I expressed the vievv that the motion vvas a complicated one vvith 3 quite divergent vievvpoints. I ask under standing order 130 that you divide the motion into its constituent parts so that the Senate can give an individual and properly based vievv on reach part. The PRESIDENT: I do not classify the motion asa complicated motion and if Senator Steele Hall feels inclined to divide the question I ask him to seek leave to do so as he raised the matter here. Senator STEELE HALL: I seek leave to divide the question. The PRESIDENT: Is leave granted? Senator GEORGES: No. Honourable senators seem to disagree vvith mc. I vvould like an explanation before I vvithdravv my objection. V/hat I object to about this procedurc, although I am prepared to go along vvitht the Senate, is that vve have debated the coropo&ite motion all day. Although the point vvas made by Senator Steele Hall that that part of the motion dealing vvith the Baltie states vvas hidden, complicated and confused and made ridicnlous by the other parts of the motion, nevertheless vve debated the motion asa vvhole. If Senator Steele Hall then vvants to proceed vvith another urgency motion dealing vvith the Baltie states or take some other action he is entiUed to do that, but why make this appear ridiculous? I do not vvant to be standing out like an odd person not granting leave if the rest of the Senate is prepared to grant leave, and if that si the case I vvill vvithdravv my objection. Senator DOUGLAS McLELLLAND: I vvish to speak to the point of order. A colleague of mine anda member of the Government is under attack by the motion that has been moved by Senator Greenvvood. Senator SIR KENNETH ANDERSON (Lib., N.S.VV.): In 3 parts. Senator DOUGLAS McLELLLAND; In 3 parts and they are on 3 different issues. I have discussed this matter vvith the Minister for Foreign Affairs, vvho is the subject of the motion, and he has expressed the opinion that questions should be put on each of the 3 issues. Representative_Docs: ['BOOK REVIEW Dr. H. Biezais\' Review of H. Schreiber\'s Land im Osten (Dusseldorf: Econ-Verlag. 1961), 418 pp. It cannot be said that there is a lack of activity in the investigation of the history of Latvia. The generation of historians, who grew up in independent Latvia, carries on its work both here in exile and in the homeland to the extent that existing circumstances permit. Next, it is Baltic-( ierman historians that exhibit the greatest activity. This group has at all times shown a lively interest in the country where they themselves as well as their fathers have lived. To these c.;n be added yet a third group which is neither Latvian nor Baltic-German and thus is often motivated by purely academic interests. Members of it are to be found in Europe, the USA, and elsewhere. The interests, methods, and points of view of these three groups are so diverse that it is impossible to identify any even partially unified notion of the history of our land and people. The author of the work considered here belongs to the third group. (He is an Austrian and a doctor of the University of Vienna.) Writing of our country, he has significantly entitled one of his chapters "The Much-Desired Bride," an expression which, although figurative, is not without a profound insight into the heart of the matter. His basic concern is the question of German expansionism in the east and its development through the preceding century. He has endeavored to see matters as they are, without any whitewashing of either one side or the other, and to find an answer to the question why the Germans have been so utterly unsuccessful in their eastward ambitions, which have culminated in our day in the greatest catastrophe of their history. The book is written in an engaging style. One has to admire the author\'s by Prof. Dr. Phil, and Dr. Theol. Haralds Biezais THE MUCH-DESIRED BRIDE bibliography and the justness of his conclusions, all the more so. because the differences among Eastern European nations and the conflicts of their interests are so complicated that it is very difficult for an outsider to see through them. Schreiber. however, has managed this commendably. Eschewing a technical style and yet standing on solid foundations, he is capable of interesting even the least initiated reader. Understandably, we are greatly interested in what the author has to say about Latvians. Having discussed the ancient geographical boundaries of the Baltic nations and their situation before the crusades of the Teutonic Order, he first depicts in very clear strokes the tragedy that took place in the conquest of the country of the Old Prussians and in their annihilation. In characterizing this old kindred nation of ours and especially in evaluating their religion as well as the cruelty with which Christianity was forced upon them, Schreiber is far ahead of many of our own scholars. "The religion of this people was just as much or little pagan as, say, Islam, whose adherents the crusaders likewise called pagans. After centuries of pure nature worship, there had developed among the Prussians the cult of a few personified gods who were worshipped in sacred forests and beside sacred waters" (p. 73). After countless destructive battles, the Prussian ration succumbed, but it never accepted the new faith. "The few remaining living warriors did not embrace Christianity; after the quarter-century of desperate battles it remained to them the religion of a savage enemy, a religion whose commandments the Prussians simply could not believe in. The men who after this long war had yet remained alive wandered kiver to Lithuania." (p. 82). The author rfdds: "And despite this, there are historians who dare to maintain that the Prussians were not annihilated but only Christianized and Germanized." (Ibid.) Not without bitterness and irony he concludes the chapter on the Prussians with these words: That which the Germans expelled from these regions in 1944 and 1945 experienced and suffered has been recorded on thousands of printed pages of paper — a testimony of great sorrow and great injustice. The traces of another injustice, which the German lords had perpetrated seven hundred years ago, only the dumb earth has retained in its fateful position between Middle and Eastern Europe..." (p. 84) The author has not lacked courage to look truth in the face and to draw the necessary conclusions. based on the Chronicle of Henry of Livonia, when he comes to speak about Latvians. He arouses admiration for the Baltic nations in their hundred years\' long struggle against the technically better armed German knights, who enjoyed the backing of all of Christian Europe and of the Pope. Henry, the priest, erred when he wrote in his chronicle intended for Western Europe about "the awakening of the idolatrous Livs from their idol-worship and sin through the fire of love" which the German knights had brought. Henry implies that the Livs were backward and isolated, but actually, together with other inhabitants of Latvia, they were already known in the Baltic Sea area as good fighters and pirates on a par with the vikings. They had already established connections with both East and West. Tt was not long before the Pone was forced to send his legate William to settle the quarrels between the Teutonic Order and the bishop, because they tried to take away from each other and acquire for themselves the greater part of the jointly conquered land of the Latvians. This "fire of love" was so strong that even the legate was unable to extinguish it. When in 1226 the legate stepped aboard his ship, he had gained an insight into the ambiguous position in which bishops of Ostland found themselves. Christian, the first bishop of Prussia, died embittered and without achievements in 1245. The bishop of Livonia, Albert, made himself a name of shining glory which no knightly order could darken. However, at bottom their fate was one and the same and had to be, for the men of their times were not yet ripe for a battle with spiritual weapons. Christianity in its lofty medieval form was still burning with a clean flame uncontaminated with bloody vapors from the heretics at the stake; nevertheless, the nations for whom this salvation was destined saw only the blood-red crosses and swords. The men saw an enemy, the women feared the new religion which dissolved their heathen marriages. And of all classes the knights were the one least suited to dispel hatred and fear, to inspire faith..." (p. 105) Further on, Schreiber speaks about how the knights found worthy opponents in the Estonian sea pirates as well as in the cunning Latvian tribe, the Semigallians. He concludes that it was the knights themselves who deprived Germanism of a future in this region. In the thirteenth century and also later, they showed their often obvious incapacity to adapt themselves to their task; they all merely brandished the sword and left thinking to their tutors. Such methods succeeded if clever men led the order, but through the centuries they did not suffice to turn the country into a German province. The knights forever remained foreign masters, and in all countries of the world the days of foreign masters are numbered. The chivalric migrants to Livonia, whose names in later centuries often acquired a high ring, lived under the fateful delusion that one can make the ground fertile with blood, (p. 108) Schreiber corrects also R. Wittram\'s assumption that at least the towns of Livonia were places where the "natives" had free opportunity to work alongside German artisans. He writes: "It is possible that to those who drove to the market the gates were opened; but as citizens in the three largest, and even in the smaller, Baltic towns we find almost exclusively Germans, a few Danes, later Swedes and Russians." (p. 109) In order to characterize the conditions prevailing in later centuries, the author quotes extensively from travel descriptions from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. The impressions of such travelers are in no respect flattering to the rulers of the country. If in some instances the travelers devote too much attention to minor oddities that strike a stranger\'s eye, they cannot for any reason be suspected of ulterior motives. In this connection, Schreiber has devoted much attention to Gottlieb Merkel\'s work on the Latvians and also to those exceptional original attempts to improve the lot of the Latvian peasant linked with the names of K. Schulze, O. Ekesparre, and others. Characterizing the situation of the Latvians in the 18th century, Schreiber writes that it did not have its equal in any other land where Germans lived. "The serfs were sold and traded like slaves; the manor lord who thereby parted a marriage was merely penalized with a money fine. In the beatings, even torture, not only did the lord\'s wife take part, but sometimes their children as well, as if they could not learn something like that early enough." (p. 266) Those are grave words, which, did they not flow from the pen of an Austrian, could easily thought to be chauvinistic. Baltic-German historians have always pointed to such evils as exceptions. It seems that the author was aware of this. His interpretation is almost the direct opposite, namely, that the positive instances are the exceptions: "In certain manors and in certain families the striving old soldiers had achieved a quite considerable personal culture, but on the whole, as a class, they were still unable to see beyond the tips of their swords." (p. 266) The author devotes special attention to the well known German humanists whose names are closely tied to the dissemination of the ideas of 18th century Enlightenment in Latvia, namely, Hamann, Herder, and Merkel. The time had become ripe for the manor lords to realize that through their idleness and simple exploitation of the economic advantages given them by the serf system they had fallen far behind the rest of Europe. Thus an increased migration of tutors from Germany began. With a sense of humor Schreiber writes that the Baroness Barbara Helena yon Budberg, "a lady of sense, beauty, and 20,000 Albert dollars," hired for a few dollars a tutor for her son Waldemar. He was brought to the estate in a four-horse carriage, preceeded by a man on horseback. The tutor was Hamann. Herder and others followed in his wake. These humanists learned to know quite well the life and thought of the country nobility. "The lords had not yet realized that a civilization consisting of towns, towns\' rights, taxes, and laws was not everything. They could not understand why the miserable tutors whom they had hired for a few dollars would rather listen to the songs of the Latvian serfs than to the conversation by the fireplaces of the nobility." (p. 267) Schreiber, in speaking about these carriers of the new spirit, says correctly that "each of them felt that he could not be silent." (p. 264) And, as we know, these men did speak and were therefore forced to leave Latvia or were, like Merkel, condemned to silence for a whole century. But what did the oppressed nation itself do? Perhaps the most significant point if one looks at the book with the eyes of a Latvian is that the author does answer this question, namely, that the nation created its very vast and complex folklore in which it expressed its notions of what life should be like if it were free. As far as I am aware, this is the first instance where a non-Latvian has seen it and expressed it so clearly. Thereafter the author shows that there developed slowly that German type which regarded himself as a superman and the other eastern nations as submen of higher or lower rank. He illustrates this copiously with examples of National Socialist inhumanity in the so-called "Eastern Realm." The author\'s thesis it that through the centuries of their eastward expansionary ambitions, in meeting other nations, the Germans have never seen them as people equivalent to themselves. Often they have been people with character traits superior to the German colonizers. The book is richly illustrated with photographs of Latvia. There is an expressive scene of the entrance way to a Latvian farmhouse with a fireplace from the end of the 18th century. The book contains also many well drawn maps, an extensive bibliography, and an index of persons and places. This is certainly a unique work which deserves attention. It sparks many reconsiderations, because we have not been used to such open language. It is true that now and then among Baltic-Germans one can hear voices which attempt to revise the old overlord mentality. We need recall only the recently voiced opinions of Dr. Marniz and others. We should mention especially G. Rauch, who, in his epoch-making address during the 1961 Baltic-German Culture Days in Liibeck, has tried to turn a new leaf in the understanding of Baltic history. But let us note that these are only single voices without echoes in the official Baltic-German organizations and society. Let us not forget that at the same time the Superintendent of Berlin, P. Kahl, has published a book about the meeting of German Protestantism with Russian Orthodoxy in the Baltic region, wherein he expresses regret that "temporarily" this meeting has been disrupted. Why could he not continue this meeting right there in Berlin? These are circumstances which make one glad about the appearance of Schreiber\'s work. In a provocative manner, it forces the reevaluation of the old and ever-new problems of the history of our native land. We may now do this much less passionately than was possible only a few decades ago, but the problems have not therefore lost their power to attract the interest even of foreigners. Translated by Ojārs Krātiņš', 'Too little has been written about the historiography of the Latvians, and what has appeared suffers from pronounced prejudices or inadequate knowledge. The early period of Latvian historiography was entirely dominated by alien historians who represented the interests and power politics of their respective countries. In their presentation of Latvian history the periods of domination of their own countries were strongly emphasized, periods of domination by other powers ignored and/or belittled, and the Latvian nation as such scarcely mentioned. These observers of historical events were neither psychologically nor nationally related to what they observed. The period of national revival of the Latvian nations during the nineteenth century aroused the interest of the people in their native history. During this period the first academic historians of Latvian origin sought to diffuse knowledge both of the outside world and of the past glory of their country in the hope of stimulating national revival. Latvian historiography, in the scholarly sense, could develop only after 1918 when free Latvia came into being. One of the most distinguished Latvian historians and the founder of the Latvian school of historiography was the above-mentioned Arveds Svabe (who also used the spelling "Schwabe"). Born in Lielstraupe on May 25, 1888 Svabe was already well-known in LatHistorian—ARVEDS ŠVĀBE by DR. EDGARS ANDERSONS vian intellectual circles by the age of twenty-five. At fifty, he was widely recognized as a distinguished scientist of broad interests. A historian and jurist by profession, he was a writer and student of folklore by avocation. His knowledge of languages was impressive, his capacity for work enormous and his personality extraordinary. At the time of his death on August 20, 1959, in Stockholm, he had published more than fifty books and approximately a thousand articles and essays. As a student Svabe sought a solid basis in the natural sciences. Unsatisfied, hi then explored the fields of history, law, sociology, and aesthetics. Later he received rigorous training in historical method at the Shanavsky University in Moscow and studied the publications of British, French and Russian writers of folklore and ethnography, considering it necessary to understand the mental characteristics of nations in order to trace their origins and development on the road to statehood. At the same time he gained success as an orator and as a powerful urbanist in poetry. In 1918 Arveds Svabe became a member of the Latvian National Council in the Far East. After returning to Riga, he became an official of the Foreign Ministry and a member of the Constituent Assembly of the new Republic of Latvia. The War had caused Svttbe to revise traditional judgments and direct his attention away from antiquity to the recent past and current events. As a member of the Constituent A«semh!v he was active in educational reform work and in the campaign to erve historical monuments and docmrnN. The Assembly provided for rrpirinn of the State Library and State Archives. Tempted to continue his historical studies, he was faced by a Department of Histcy in the University of Latvia dominated by Baltic German professors, who, being hostile to the new state and the Latvian national movement, were heavily emphasizing classical studies and the auxiliary studies of history without offering a single course in the history of Latvia. There Svabe, choosing the long way around, entered the more liberal Department of Law. But in 1936 he became the head of the Department of History. With the Latvian nation now thoroughly "history conscious," cities and communal centers organized museums, established local archives and enlarged their libraries. The people demanded texts reflecting not only the history of the aristocracy and ruling minorities, but of the nation as a whole. The Baltic German and Baltic Russian historians,, writing from the angle of their respective nationality and class interests, had ignored the vital problems of the national majorities in the Baltic area. The harm that such one-sided history had already done to the Baltic nations was incalculable. Svabe, after receiving his M.A. in jurisprudence, had been appointed to the chair of History of Latvian Law. He had already published A History of Latvia in three volumes (1921-1923) as a text for schools, two volumes of The History of Latvian Culture (1921-22, the third volume remained unpublished) as well as several articles. Realizing that the former overlords and now the enemies of the new republic, entrenched irļ their ideological positions, were still seeking to control the nation\'s history, Svabe was determined to fight them with historical research. To him this was a continuation of the war of liberation, in a different field, with different weapons. The Latvian agrarian reform was causing friction between the landed gentry and the government. for s< "y of i 1 Svabe wrote his Agrarian History of Latvia (published in English, French and German, 1927-1929) to clarify the bacground for the agrarian reform in Latvia. In the fall of 1926 he finished his History of the Rural Community. After 1927 Svabe devoted much energy to editing the Latvian Encyclopedia of which 21 volumes had appeared by 1939. The Ph. D. degree had been conferred on him in 1932, his thesis topic being The Oldest Feudal Code in Latvia. He was then made full professor of the history of law. While the historians of the old school remained entrenched in the Department of History, a new breed of them was growing up in the Departmets of Law and Economics at the University of Riga. In 1936, however, Svabe accepted the chairmanship of the Department of History, and proceeded to improve seminars and reorganize the curriculum. A new and well-trained generation of historians was prepared to replace the old. He also helped to organize the State Institute of History, assisted in the publication of historical journals and source books, attended international congresses and worked in foreign archives. On his fiftieth birthda} his friends and students honored him with a festschrift entitled To the History of the Nation. Young Latvian scholars published monographs based on new and rich archeological and documentary discoveries. Their activities raised a storm of violent, even scandalous protest from the Baltic barons and their friends—the Nazis, who launched their attacks on Latvian historiography. Nonetheless, they won acknowledgment among scholars in the West. \'From 1939 to 1940 Dr. Svabe was Vice President of the Latvian State Institute of History. He promoted the publication of the monumental History of Latvia and the collection of sources, Monumenta līistorica Latviae. World War II interrupted his work. The Russians allowed him to lecture at the University, but accused him of serving the "rotten democracy." When the Nazis took over they kept Svabe out of the lecture halls. From 1941 to 1944 he was nominally President of the Institute of Historical Sources, but was not permitted to use the archives. His only important production was two more volumes of historical sources. In 1944 he was deported to Germany, where he was rescued by the Allies. After the war he remained in exile. Elected President of the Latvian Central Committee in Exile, he helped to relieve the plight of refugees. Finally, Svabe settled in the Swedish capital where, in association with the University of Stockholm, he devoted himself to historical research. He published several books in English, French and Swedish, completed a three-volume special edition of the Latvian Encyclopedia, and finished the first volume of his monumental History of Latvia, 1800— 1950 {Latvijas vesture, 1800—1944). In the course of his life Svabe worked in five different fields: folklore, the history of law, agrarian history, medieval history and, particularly, modern history. His work in all these areas was unified by the historical approach. It was his initial interest in folklore that led him into history. His History of Latvian Culture combines the study of customs and institutions along with that of documents and relics of historical significance. He traced the evolution of Latvian culture from prehistoric times through to the Middle Ages. Svabe spent much time in the medieval period of Latvian History, exploiting many Baltic, Scandinavian and German legends, and striving for objectivity and candor in handling dangerous problems. Yet his work caused unfriendly critics to accuse him of lack of realism. Reference should be made to his essays in Currents and Sources (two volumes, 1939-40). He brought to light much interesting material concerning Scandinavian-Baltic relations, the struggle between the Teutonic order and the Pope in the Baltic area, and the life and institutions of ancient independent Latvian territorial units as well as the policies of their rulers. Svabe\'s Agrarian History of Latvia bears some resemblance to the works of some German historians, but much of the text results from his own findings. Among many essays dealing with more modern problems the most interesting may be his article on Sigismund Augustus\' Livonian policy. Since he was mostly interested in rural development, few of Svabe\'s essays dealt with the development of Baltic cities, and science and technology were neglected. His later works reveal a growing interest and expertise in diplomatic and political history. One may mention his Histoire dv peuple Letton (1953), The Story of Latvia and her Neighbors (1947, 1949), Baltic Autonomy Until 1918 (1954), Genocide in the Baltic Countries (1949), History of Latvia 1800—1914 (in Latvian). He edited and published two volumes of Fontes Historiae Mcdii Aeii (1937, 1940), Quellen zur Wirtschaftsgeschichte Livlands 1553—1618 (1941) Quellen zur Rechtsgeschichte Livlands 1336—1551 (1941) and the somewhat less important Chrestomathy of the Sources of the History of Latvia 1300—1500 (1940). Some of his critics have maintained that Svabe\'s historical outlook was restricted by provincialism and isolationism and that he evinced no interest in the factors of international co-operation. In fact, he projected on a carefully analyzed, well-balanced and skillfully presented international background. However, he was not expert in some of the questions he treated, and he did overestimate legal, social and economic factors while underestimating the political, diplomatic and military ones. He has been accused of a priori concepts and idees mattresses. Yet his idee maitresse was to discover truth in a violently misused and misinterpreted field of history, hitherto appropriated by certain alien elements and their allies, a caste hostile or indifferent to the Baltic nationals. These accusers have been more violent nationalists than Svabe himself. Undeniably, however, a lack of moderation in his attacks on these elements sometimes seriously weakened the effectiveness of his arguments. He himself admitted he could never be either a diplomat or a politician. Svabe established a new school of Latvian historiography based on modern principles. He greatly raised Latvian standards through his own teaching and writing; he discovered and preserved significant new materials. He wove Latvian history into the pattern of World history. His books and articles have been profoundly influential ; and even his Nazi and Communist enemies have been forced to admit, in their own ways, that Svabe has shed new light on the history of Latvia. He admitted, however, that historians should reflect the prevailing views of their time, their class or their nation. As a combined jurist and historian, Svabe. however, was always critical with his sources, sometimes caustically criticizing other scholars for the misreading of documents. In his writings he followed the rules of simplicity, factual presentation and cautious interpretation. He considered the narrative art indispensable to the writer of history, but never at the expense of truth. His philosophy of history is not original, most of its elements being borrowed from certain French, German, Russian, and British historians. He was particularly indebted to Fustel de Coulanges, Kluchevsky and Aulard. However, Svabe\'s practical contributions to Latvian historiography are so great that he must be regarded as the most important Latvian historian up to the present. Lieven ACCORDING TO HENRY THE LATVIAN AND AT THE TIME OF THE BISHOPS AND MASTERS OF THE ORDER. (THE PERIOD OF ABOUT 1200 — 1562 A.D.). This map is undated. It was designed by W. C. Triebe, drawn by J. W. Krause, and engraved by F. Ramberg. The country of origin of these men is not stated. The left top corner shows a female figure holding a balance, the upper plate of which contains a parchment scroll with the words "Rights of Nations." The scroll is shown as partly torn at the end. A cross lies on the lower plate of the balance. The left bottom corner depicts Latvians or Estonians gathering for a uprising. The background shows a Latvian or Estonian castle earthwork or hill-fort (pilskalns). A cut-down oak tree (perhaps a symbol of the liberty of the Baltic people or their ancient religion, where holy trees played an important role) is growing new branches. The Latvians or Estonians are being joined by Russians, apparently coming to their aid. The top right corner has a table explaining conventional signs showing properties of the Archbishop of Riga and their vassals, of the Bishops of Reval (now Tallin), Dorpat (Tartu or Tērbata), Oesel (Saaremaa or Sāmu sala) and their vassals, of the properties or the Livonian Order (the northern branch of the Teutonic Order) and its vassals; castles of Germans; fortified towns with a castle; minor towns with a castle; as well as the pre-conquest period wooden hill-forts (pilis) of Livonians, Latvians, and Estonians. The left bottom corner shows a heathen inhabitant kneeling before a sacred oak which has been cut down but which is driving new shoots. His hand is stretched in a pleading, protecting gesture over the stump of the tree. A Bishop is interceding for him with a Knight of the Order, behind whom stand some sinister-looking figures with sardonic expressions. The shaded area of the map, excluding the triangle based on the Daugava (i.e., excluding the southeastern part of Latgale), is marked "Lettland" (Latvia; the older, latinized version of this name was Lettia). The narrow coastal area to the west of "Lettland", from the Pernaa river in the north to Kokenois (Koknese) on the Daugava is marked "Livland" (Livonia, in a more narrow sense of the word, i.e. the *) Comments on the period shown by the map, the eastern borders of the Lettigallians (Letts), and the selection of the quoted passages from Henry the Lett\'s chronicle are by Dr. Nikodēms Bojārs. area actually inhabited by the Livs in the 13 century). Curonia (Kurzeme) and Semigallia (Zemgale), south of the Daugava, are left almost blank. Only a few places are indicated there, as the map covers only "Livonia" (i.e., the area of the Baltic nations conquered by the Brethren of the Sword or Fratres Militiae Christi and by their successors after 1237, the Teutonic knights, until about 1242, before the subjugation of the Cours and Semigallians). This, then, approxiamately answers the question as to what period of history is shown by the map; as nearly as one can tell, the political situation of about 1237 is shown. The reasons for this conclusion are the following: 1.) Couronia and Semigallia are not yet conquered by the Teutons. 2.) Estonia is already conquered, including the northern part; however, North Estonia was ceded to the King of Denmark in 1238, and was regained by the Master of the Livonian Order, Burchard yon Dreyerlewen, only in 1343 (de facto, by occupation) or in 1346 de iure, by purchase from the Danish King). Since the Danish possessions are not marked as a separate political unit, the map refers to the situation before 1238. 3.) The Bishop of Riga and the Order took possession of Adzele (Terra Adzelle, or the northeastern part of Lettigallia) in 1224, and this part of Adzele is already shown within the boundaries of "Livonia" (in the wider sense of the word). The Dukes of Pskov dominated the eastern corner of Adzele until about 1340, when they were defeated by the same Master of the Order — yon Dreyerlewen, and the Lettigallian regions of Abrene (now Pytalovo), Augšpils (now Vysgorod), and Sarkani (now Krasnogorodsk) were regained by the state of Livonia. This region of Abrene was again conquered and joined to Russia in 1464-1476 and again after 1945. The map shows this eastern part of Adzele as well as the southeastern part of Latgale as belonging to the Russians; therefore the period after 1224 but before 1340 is shown. 4.) After their defeat in the battle of Saule in 1236, the few surviving Brethren of the Sword joined the Teutonic Order of Prussia in 1237. This situation is shown by the map; the initial German conquests in Courland (1231-2) have disappeared after the battle of Saule. The second important question is the age of the map itself. Is it possible to date it approxiamately ? And who inspired the subject of this map? The map was shown to a specialist, and he dated it approximately late in the 18/ A century. This conclusion also helps to answer the question, as to who inspired the subject of this map. If the dating is correct, then it would fall into the period of the "Enlightenment", a philosophical movement of the 17th and 18th centuries, based largely on rationalism and influenced by the earlier spiritual trends of the Renaissance. The Enlightenment was the time of Diderot and the Encyclopaedists. The source might have been a liberal-minded German Baltic nobleman, but if so, he might have hot made himself too popular among his own because of the nationalist and partly anti-clerical nature of the subject. To Latvian readers this needs no further explanation. It is rather as though an 18th century American had produced a map claiming for the American Indians the right to their ancestral lands. Could the author of the map have been a Russian? First of all, the eastern borders of Livonia (including Lettia) are drawn favorably for the Russians, since the actual borders of Livonia during most of the time of its existence were really farther to the east. Besides, the Russians are shown to be hurrying to help , the uprising of the Letts or Estonians. There were at the end of the 18th century a good number of men and women in Russia who were strongly under the influence of French and English liberalism and anti-clericalism. This movement was headed by Empress Catherine II herself. It is doubted, however, whether these "enlightened" Russians were particularly interested in the rights of minorities at that time. The Empress certainly did not do anything to create an independent state of Latvia or Estonia. The Russification was rather encouraged by the rulers of Russia. If neither a German Baltic nobleman nor a Russian ordered this map to be designed, drawn and engraved, and as the country of its origin is unknown to the commentator, we will have to look elsewhere for a possible source. Who could this person have been? In 1791 Herder published his work on the "Ideas on the Philosophy and History of Mankind", and in this he referred to the Baltic people, which corresponds to his chapter on the Slavonic people. He also published Latvian and Estonian songs in his collection of folk songs.* Another friend of the Latvians and Estonians of that period was Garlieb Merkel. He was born in 1769 as a son of a German Pastor in Livonia who had been born in Weimar, Germany, and was therefore a newcomer to the Baltic. Garlieb Merkel published a book in 1796 with the title "The Latvians". This was the first book in which the nationhood of the Latvians, their rights and traditions as an ancient people, as well as their future formed the theme. He fought passionately all his life for the rights of the Baltic people in the spirit of the Enlightenment. Merkel was a devoted admirer of Catherine II because of her liberal ideas and her intended reforms for Russia. In the first sentence of his introduction to his book on the Latvians, Merkel says: "Reason has triumphed, and the Century of Justice begins." This volume had effects on the German nobility in the Baltic lands. In 1838, Landrat R. J. L. yon Samson confessed that he had experienced a sense of guilt when he read the passionate works of Dr. Merkel.** The commentator has, therefore, no doubt that it was Garlieb Merkel to whom we owe this remarkable document showing the Latvians and Estonians as peoples with a future of freedom before them, after centuries of oppression. It now only remains to explain why the Latvians (Letti seu Lettigalli of the 13th century chronicles) are shown as occupying the southeastern corner of Vidzeme (Idumea of the chronicles), and why Russians are coming to the aid of the Baltic insurgents. This may be due to the fact that the map was based on the annals of Henry the Latvian (Henricus Lettus of the 13th century; a new English translation of his chronicle has appeared recently***). Therefore, the map shows the situation as it was in the first half of the 13th century. At that time, the city-states of Pskov and Polotsk attempted to claim all Estonia and Vidzeme (Livonia) as their sphere of influence, and opposed the Germans as invaders. Besides purely aggressive motives and the tribute which the Russians sometimes exacted from a few minor states of the Baltic people (such as Tālava), the Russian Dukes had a commercial interest in the Baltic. The Russians desired to control this area through which the trade passed from Sweden to the Black Sea, the Volga, and thence beyond to the Far East through Central Asia. Pskov and Polotsk constantly urged the Livonians, Latvians and Estonians to rise and supported these uprisings with armies. However, those or the Lettish (Latvian) princes or castleelders (virsaiši) who concluded treaties of mutual assistance with the German Order, must have regarded the Russian* as a greater menace, because of the interminable warfare of the Lettigallians against the Krivichi tribe in the east (as witnessed by the existence of the "defense zone" of about 300 hill-forts on the Lettish eastern border). Nevertheless, there were some struggles of the Letts against the Order, although not as extensive and tenacious as in Couronia, Semigallia, and Estonia. These struggles and the claims of the Dukes of Pskov and Polotsk were well known to John IV, the Terrible, who tried to "reclaim" and conquer for Muscovy the "lost" Baltic lands. *) R. Wittram, Baltische Geschichte, Munchen, 1954, p. 151. **) R. Wittram, op. cit., p. 157. ***) Henricus Lettus, Origines Livoniae sacrae et civilis, Engl, transi, by J. A. Brundage (Madison, 1961). It may be of interest to discuss why the heathen Latvian, Livonian or Estonian shown on the map appeals for protection to the Bishop against the Teutonic knight. Possibly, because Bishop Albert Buxhoevden, the Bishop of Riga in early 13. century and a Spiritual Prince of the Holy Roman Empire, a friend of both Frederik (Friedrich) II Hohenstauffen and of Pope Innocent 111, was described by Henry the Latvian as a great medieval statesman, and he sought the cooperation of the local population against the Russians. Frederik II declared in 1224 that the converted people of Livonia and Prussia had become the citizens of the Holy Roman Empire and therefore they were free men and not serfs of kings, dukes or princes. The Pope Innocent 111 tried to abolish the slavery in the whole Christian world and defended in his messages the possessions of the Baltic people from the rapacity of the conquerors, while his successor Honorius 111 admitted the Christianized Baits in the community of free peopļev (in libertatem filiorum Dei) in 1225. Bishop Albert, according to Henry the Latvian, "received back .as sons" both Vetseke, the leader of* the Livonians, and some of the Latvian leaders, after their joint and unsuccessful rising against him. The attitude of the Teutonic knights was certainly different. The history of this uprising (in 1212) is best described in the words of Henry the Latvian himself:**** "A great contention arose between the Brothers of the Militia from Wenden and the Letts of Autine, the latter being in the Bishop\'s area, over their fields and their bee trees. Some of the Letts were wounded by the Brothers and the quarrel came before the Bishop. The Lord Bishop rose up with the venerable Lord Philip, Bishop of Ratzeburg, and called the Brothers of the Militia and the Livonians and Letts to a hearing so that, by checking the quarrel, he could restore them to their former harmony. They wrangled bitterly for two days and were unable to enter into any peaceful reconciliation among themselves. The Livonians and the Letts thereupon lefļt the Germans and took oaths among themselves... The Bishop promised to restore everything that had been taken unjustly. As for the things that the Brothers had taken, as they justly deserved, on the account of the Livonians\' excesses, the Bishop made no promise to restore them. The Bishop, acting on the advice of prudent men, demanded the Livonians\' boys as hostages, lest they abandon the Christian faith. The Livonians, however, would neither give up the hostages nor obey the Bishop or the Brothers of Militia ... He (Bishop Albert) called together the pilgrims, the master of the Militia and his Brothers, and the Rigans and Livonians who still remained faithful. Taking all the necessary supplies with them, they marched to Treiden and besieged Dobrel\'s fort... The prince and elder of the latter (Livonians) was Vetseke. The Livonians left the fort from the rear and, after wounding some men in the army, they took to their horses and loot arid returned to the fort, saying: "Take heart and fight, Livonians, lest you be slaves to the Germans." They fought and defended themselves for many days... Others (the Germans) put up a tower which the wind knocked to the ground the next night. At this there was a great noise and rejoicing in the fort and the Livonians sacrificed animals, paying honor to their gods according to their old customs ... The tower was put up more strongly, another wooden tower was quickly strengthened, it was pushed across the moat, and the fort was sapped from beiow. From the highest point in the fort, mean- j while, Russin (the Latvian prince and \\ elder Rūsiņš) called Berthold, the master of Wenden (Cēsis), draugs, that is, his fellow. Russin took off his helmet, leaned down from the wall, and uttered words about peace and their former friendship. Suddenly a bolt from a ballista struck his head. He fell and shortly thereafter he died ... The Bishop, in order to persuade them to return to the sacraments of the ****) Henricus Lettus, op. cit., transi, by J.A. Brundage, sub anno 1212, Chapters 3, 4. faith, sent his banner into the fort... The Bishop had pity on them... They asked the Bishop, as Alabrand had instructed them, to confirm them fully in Christ\'s faith and to lay upon them the annual payment of the tithe as satisfaction for their misdeeds ... The Bishop, however, was at last overcome by their importune begging. He agreed to their request and received them as sons. After giving them peace, he confirmed their promise that henceforth they would be faithful and would pay the annual tithes." It is very possibly this very uprising which is shown on the corners of the above described map. The Pope sent to Livonia Bishop William of Modena who reported adversely to the Pope on the oppression of the native population by the Brethren of the Sword (Fratres Militiae Christi). The hopes and plans of the Holy See to improve the situation of Latvians, Livs however, since the actual power of the Popes in Terra Mariana (Livonia) decreased through the centuries, and the Teutonic knights even ignored a ban of the Church (excommunication) directed at them in the next (14th) century. If the authors of the above map have used other sources besides the well-known Chronicle of Henry the Latvian and the Rhyme Chronicle of the 13th century, it is possible that a careful study might reveal new information such as the exact location of some ancient Latvian, Livonian i and Estonian hill-forts. For example, it ( seems that the capital of the Lettish state of Tālava, Beverīna, has not yet been located with any reasonable degree of certainty. *****) A. Schwabe (Švābe), "Les illusions dv Saint-Siege" in Histoire dv people Letton (Stockholm, 1953), pp. 84-94.']
