['Language law draft regulations completed On 28 July the Latvian Justice Ministry completed draft regulations for implementation of the state language law, which has been sharply criticised by leftist politicians and the Russian-language press. The draft regulations have been sent to the Organisation for Security and Co-operation in Europe (OSCE) High Commissioner for Ethnic Minorities Max van der Stoel, who will give his opinion about the document. The contents of the document will not be disclosed at present. The government representatives earlier stated that the language law regulations will not be discriminatory to ethnic minorities and will meet the OSCE requirements. The regulations governing part of the provisions under the state language law must be adopted by 1 September when the language law takes effect.', 'SVEŠVALODU NODAĻA/OTHER LANGUAGES SECTION DEMOCRACY FOR ALL For some years now, one sentence in Latvia's election law has presented a stumbling block to its membership in the European Union. The United States of America has made the deletion of that sentence a precondition for I.atvia's membership in NATO, and the EU is likely to harden its position. For one sentence to be a deciding factor for Latvia's membership in international organizations, that sentence must be an obnoxious one indeed. Is that the cas? That sentence contains the requirement that candidates for public office must be able to comprehend the state language at a workable level. To any sensible person this would seem to be a very reasonable requirement. The reason given for its removal – that no other nation has such a requirement in its election law – is rather weak. Let's ask a simple question – how many members are there in the legislatures of the EU member states and the USA who are unable to comprehend the respective state languages? I think it is quite safe to suggest that there are none. This means that the unwritten law of the candidates for public office being required to comprehend the state language is being tacitly enforced in every one of those states. Latvia says so openly. It is not hypocritical to require Latvia to remove an honest statement from its legislation? It has been said that requiring candidates to public office to comprehend the state language is discriminatory and in particular discriminatory against Latvia's Russian speakers. Stalinist Russia deported tens of thousands of Latvians to Siberia. Their children and grandchildren mostly speak Russian. Hundreds of thousands of Latvians fled west before the invading Soviet Russian troops. Their children and grandchildren mostly speak English only. By the subject "obnoxious" sentence in the current legislation these  are required to comprehend language ir they wish to participate in public office in Latvia. From this it can be seen that the law applies equally to Latvians as well as to members of any other nationality and it applies equally to Russian speakers as to speakers of any other language. Most definitely it is not a discriminatory law. There are two good reasons why latvia's legislation should continue to have this qualification, although no other Western country has such a requirement. One is that no other Western country has had the use of a foreign language ruthessly forced upon them like the Russian language was enforced in Latvia for a half a century under Soviet Russian occupation. It seems reasonable to suggest that latvian language should be enforced in Latvia for at least an eaually long period of time. The other is that the removal of this sentence would, in fact, grant special privileges to one minority – the Russians – not enjoyed by any other minority in Latvia. Evidence that this is so is seen ir Riga City Council where 15 counsellors are forcing the other 45 counsellors to speak Russian (The Baltic Times Dec. 13-19, 2001). Is this democratic? Or is it, in the view of EU member states and the U.S.A., acceptable for Russians to force everybody in Latvia to speak Russian, but it is not acceptable for Russians aspiring to public office in Latvia to be asked to comprehend the state language? When all is said and done, the requirement of the removal of the said "obnoxious" sentence is nothing more than a proposal to enable a few persons unable to learn a second language to make a nuisance of themselves. It is nothing else, hopefully. To make this a conditon for accesion of Latvia to EU and to NATO is simply ridiculous. □ Letter to the Editor, The Baltic Times, January 10-16, 2002 This letter was attached by Mr. A. Caune to his own which he addressed to The President of Latvia. See the Letters section in Latvian. J. Ziedars', 'LATVIANS REVISE LANGUAGE LAW

On 10 March the Latvian Supreme Council accepted in its first reading a revised version of the 1989 Law on Languages, which when it goes into effect on 5 May, will make Latvian the main language in State institutions, public advertising, and to a lesser extent in private business.

Russian speakers in a wide range of professions will have to undergo attestation in order to determine if their command of the “state language” is up to standards. If they fail, they could face fines or even lose their jobs.

An administrative structure is to be created to enforce the lavv’s stipulations. A new nine-person Languages Commission, led by MP Dzintars Abikis, will supervise the process, accrediting boards from large enterprises and local govemments to carry outthe testing. Other older bodies, set up in 1989, have been rejuvenated: the Languages Inspect orate is to enforce the use of Latvian in the above-mentioned areas, and the Languages Centre is to co-ordinate programs for teaching Latvian in schools and to adults. Ali parties agree that the existing system failed to educate the body of Russian-speakers and assure the primacy of Latvian in the public sphere. A paragraph “recognising that Russian is, after Latvian, the language in wid est use in the Latvian Soviet Sociālist Republic and is a medium of international communication” has been removed. In the new version, not only food labels and product lists, but advertise ments, posters, signs, price tags, instructions and film subtitles must be in Latvian, with other languages figuring secondarily. Other additions harmonise the law with post 1989 legislation, such as the elim i nation

of the two-track system at university. Further changes in the civil, labourand criminal codes will set the levels of fluency reguired in vari ous professions and a system of penalties. Abikis sees the language problem as serious but not insurmountable. Indepen dence has brought a shift in attitudes among many Russian-speakers regarding Latvian. The problem is limited to large cities such as Riga and Daugavpils (in the latter only 15% of the population is ethnic Latvian). In such situations tolerance and a gradual approach are called for.

Abikis considers Latvia’s demography the true cause for alarm making the law neces sary. Ina country where only one third of the capital’s inhabitants speak the State language in the home, “the Latvian language is marked for extinction”. He blamēs his predecessors on the Language Commission, but more generally Latvians themselves for not attempting to improve this State of affairs. Much depends on how stringently the law is read by the ministries and executive bodies involved. Education Ministry official Eiženija Aldermane, also a member of the Language Commission, said three levels of fluency vvould be required, the highest for those in leadership positions, another for mid-level managers and those in constant contact with the public, and a third for low-wage jobs reguiring minimal language use. Some jobs (factory vvorker, for instance) will not require any tests at ali, but the majority of the Russian-speaking population will undergo some sort of tēst. “Draconian measures” such as firing vvould apply only to the first group, if at ali. The commissions could give mid-level employees failing the attestation a second chance, “but if, say, a school principal has

known for three years, but hasn’t studied...” Large enterprises will submit lists of positions divided into the categories to the commissions.

This three-tiered examination system will apply only to employees of State enterprises or the government. Regulation of language use in private business is stili under debate, but those keeping accounts or dealing with the public will probably have to be proficient in Latvian.

Surveys show that roughly one fourth of non-ethnic Latvians speak reasonably fluent Latvian, so that hundreds of thousands of people will be affected. Why, two months before the law goes into effect, has there been so little movement? According to Alexander Maltsev, deputy director of the Nationality Ouestions Department, only 16 million of the 24 million rubles earmarked for realising the 1989 law have been used in three years. The bulk of these funds went to the Ministry of Education for improving the teaching of Latvian at ali levels of the educational system (in Russian-language schools it is notoriously poor). Adult edu cation received only one million rubles for training speciālists usually the enterprises paid themselves fortheir workers who vvished to attend classes at local language centers or cooperatives. Abikis, in speaking with adult education teachers, found that those paying their own way were better motivated. Maltsev regretted that so little had been done with adults, and that no control had been exe rcised over the public use of Latvian.

Chris Hinkle

extract from The Baltie Observer 20-26 March 1992']

Representative_Docs: ['Rotation of soldiers of the Latvian contingent in Iraq concludes In late June and early July, the regular rotation of soldiers of the Latvian contingent in the international peacekeeping operation in Iraq was concluded as 111 Latvian soldiers returned home on 5 July 2004. Among others welcoming the troops home in the Adazi Training Centre of Mobile Troops was the Latvian Minister of Defence, Atis Slakteris, and the Commander of Latvian National Armed Forces, Vice Admiral Gaidis Andrejs Zeibots. Currently, there are 133 Latvian soldiers stili on duty in the international peacekeeping operation in Iraq.', '10th Anniversary of the National Armed Forces On 22 October 2004, the National Armed Forces celebrated its 10th anniState Archives of Film, Photo and Phono Documents. The event was attended by Commander of the National Armed Forces, Vice Admiral Gaidis Andrejs Zeibots, the first commander of the National Armed Forces, retired Colonel Juris Dai bins, and representatives from NAF units. During the event, 91 National Armed Forces soldiers received certificates of appreciation and honour by the National Armed Forces Commander, and documentary films were shown that featured the 85 years of the Latvian Army and 10 years of the National Armed Forces. The National Armed Forces were established at the end of 1994 by combining the Defence Forces and the Republic of Latvia Home Guard; Juris Da!bins was appointed as the first Commander.', 'Overview of International Military Exercises in Latvia during 2010 International military exercises in Latvia – new knowledge and experience. This is a significant year for the defence sector in Latvia – several military exercises for the Baltic States are being held in Latvia and are also attended by NATO and partner countries from the Alliance program of practical bilateral cooperation "Partnership for Peace". 16 military exercises are being organized in Latvia in 2010 including three high level international military exercises. Soldiers from the Latvian National Armed Forces (NAF) are participating in 42 military exercises in Latvia and abroad this year. The aim of military exercises By organizing international military exercises Latvia examines its capability to host allied forces in its territory and improves the professional qualifications of soldiers for international operation in Afghanistan. The main task of NAF and each individual soldier during peace time is to improve their professional skills, therefore the organization of military exercises is so important. The participation of NAF soldiers in international exercises is a significant part of their education which improves the professional skills of soldiers and their ability to cooperate in a multinational environment as well as increases the compatibility of the allied forces. Military exercises in the Baltic States reassure the region about presence of NATO forces. The priority: international military exercises In line with budget cuts in the Ministry of Defence, military exercises which improve NAF skills are the main priorities of the ministry. In 2010 Latvia hosts three high level international military exercises – "Baltic Host" (the beginning of June), "BALTOPS" (the beginning of June) and "Saber Strike" (during autumn). Baltic Host 2010 (May 31 – June 4, 2010) The Baltic States military exercise "Baltic Host 2010" took place simultaneously in the capitals of the Baltic States – Rīga, Tallinn and Vilnius. Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia, U.S. European Command (EUCOM) and Naval Striking and Support Force NATO, as well as Denmark, Poland, Norway and Germany participated in the "Baltic Host 2010". The goal of the exercise is to develop action plans by the Baltic States\' defence sectors together with other state institutions for due and adequate simultaneous reception, staging and onward movement of allied and support units over the territory of all the Baltic States. This year the focus was on cooperation between the defence sector and civil institutions during the Host Nation Support (HNS) operations. Latvia was represented by several civil institutions – for example, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, the Ministry of Economics, the Ministry of Transport, State Border Guard and Emergency Medical Assistance Service. BALTOPS (June 6-22, 2010) The Baltic Operations (BALTOPS) exercise is an annual international naval exercise which takes place in the Baltic Sea and is attended by NATO and partner countries from the program "Partnership for Peace". Over 3,000 servicemen from different countries took part in the international BALTOPS 2010 naval exercise. The aim of the exercise is to improve cooperation among NATO and partner nations from the "Partnership for Peace" as well as to upgrade the navigation safety in the Baltic Sea. Although BALTOPS has traditionally been a maritime exercise, this year an offload operation had been organized during which the equipment offload from the maritime preposition vessels was conducted. The main aim of this operation was to develop the host nation support potential and the ability to cooperate in multinational environment. Sabre Strike (October 18 – 31, 2010) Military exercise "Sabre Strike 11" is organized for autumn 2010. It is a Host Nation Support exercise attended by the armed forces of the U.S. and the Baltic States. About 2000 soldiers from Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia and the U.S. will participate in the military exercise "Sabre Strike 11". The aim of this military exercise is to improve the cooperation between the armed forces of the Baltic States and the USA and to enhance the host nation support skills of Latvia as well as to strengthen the further cooperation in the military operations in Afghanistan. Financing The financing for military exercise of NAF in comparison to 2009 has decreased almost by half (LVLI.I million or EUR 1.57 million in 2009 to LVL 610 thousand or EUR 868 thousand in 2010). Organizing such a wide range of international exercise in light of a reduced budget meant prioritizing use of available finances. All three military exercises in Latvia in 2010 have been organized with financial and material support from the allies. A challenge – host nation support This year has brought a new challenge to Latvia – managing host nation support. Organizing host nation support operations brings valuable experience which improves the professional skills of soldiers and the National guard, gives an opportunity to host allied forces in the territory of Latvia and to collaborate with them. This military training is meant to improve the cooperation between the defence sector and civil institutions as well as to develop soldiers\' professional skills later to be used in the international operations in Afghanistan. Benefits: the view of the Minister of Defence In evaluating international military exercises in Latvia, Minister of Defence Imants Lieģis points out: "Regional international exercises in Latvia have been one of my top three priorities. Working with Allies and being able to receive and host Allied troops is fundamental to our NATO membership and collective defence." Lieģis emphasizes:ulnteroperability – that is the name of the game. Whether in Afghanistan or Latvia, we need to be able to work with NATO partners. That is why the military exercises in 2010 have helped our soldiers to gain new skills in managing host nation support and improving their ability to take part in the operation in Afghanistan."']

["TELECOMMUNICATIONS IN LATVIA TAKE ANOTHER STEP FORWARD, BUT SO DO PRICES On August 11, the new international digital telephone exchange was opened in Riga, markedly extending international telephone communication for Latvia's inhabitants. The new international exchange is linked to the optical cable network (to Ventspils and then by the underrate r cable to Sweden), recently linked to the satellite land relay station, the microwave line (through Estonia to Sweden and Finland), and the long distance communication centre. According to the Technical Director of Lattelekom, the new exchange has 4,200 international trunk lines, which will allow 200,000 international connections per hour. Also on August 11, new tariffs came into effect for international and local calls. Originally the new tariffs were to come into force on August 1, but due to the delay in opening of the new telephone exchange, the tariff rate was postponed. For analogue network subscribers, who make up the largest part of ail telephone subscribers, tariffs will rise on average by 55%. On the other hand, rates for digital subscribers' calls to Europe, the CIS, USA and Canada will only increase by 20%, and calls elsewhere will be cheaper than before. The largest percentile increase for analogue subscribers will be for calls to countries in Asia -1.78 Lats per minute or an increase of 196%; given the joint tariff scheme, digital network users will pay 29% for the same calls. In absolute terms, the highest rate will be for calls to the USA, Canada and Israel (1.88 Lats per minute), while calls within Latvia have increased by 1 santim per minute, but the tariff for calls to Estonia and Lithuania has not changed.", 'Survey: Six in 10 households in Latvia have Internet access

Andris Straumanis - October 20, 2009

Almost six in 10 Latvian households have Internet access, but regional differences reveal that Rīga and its suburbs have greater connectivity while Latgale lāgs, according to data from the Central Statistical Bureau (CSB).

Internet access has inereased 5 percentage points over last year to 58 percent, based on a survey of 3,900 households throughout Latvia, according to an Oct. 15 CSB press release. Sixty-four percent of households in the Capital city of Rīga have Internet access, while the capital’s suburbs have 65 percent. Vidzeme province, at 48 percent, and Latgale province, at 44 percent, have the lowest access, the CSB reported. In Kurzeme, 54 percent of households have access, but in Zemgale, the rāte is 61 percent. Not surprisingly, younger people are the most active users ofthe Internet. Almost ali (96 percent) of people age 16-24 regularly

use the Internet, the CSB survey found, while 85 percent of those aged 25-34 regularly use the medium. Regular Internet use is lowest, 20 percent, among persons aged 55-74. Otherfindings of the survey include: • Forty-five percent of households in Latvia use desktop computers to connect to the Internet, while 20 percent use portable computers and another 20 percent use mobile telephones. • Almost all (97 percent) of Internet users are looking for information, while 90 percent use it for sending e-mail, chatting or other communication. Sixty-six percent use the Internet to access their bank accounts.

• A total of 38 percent of Latvian Internet users bought something online this year, compared to 33 percent last year. Popular purchases included event tickets, electronics, clothing and sporting goods. The CSB survey was taken during the second guarter of this year.', 'Telecoms Modernization Brings New Lines, Number Changes, Sellout Accusations The modernization of Latvia's Soviet-era analog telecommunications system continues to gain steam. Simultaneously, the award of the modernization contract, and thus of part-ownership of Latvia's telecoms monopoly Lattelekom, to the international TILTS Communications consortium of Britain's Cable & Wireless and Telekom Finland continues to be severely criticized by the newspaper Neatkariga Cina and mayinprompt a formal investigation by the Saeima into the cotract awards process. TILTS has installed a fiber optic trunk cable through the western portion of Latvia (Riga-Ventspils-Saldus-Dobele-Jelgava); laid the longest unsupported underwater cable in the world to span the 305 km distance from Ventspils to Nyneham, Sweden; opened a new international telephone exchange and installed a satellite uplink station in Riga. These changes have put TILTS ahead of schedule in achieving their stated goal of a fully digital telecommunications system for business customers within three years, and for all residential customers in Latvia within eight years. As part of this gradual modernization to implement a modern, digital network, Lattelekom recently announced the following changes to telephone numbers in Latvia, effective December 10, 1994: 1) All Riga six-digit telephone numbers which begin with the digits 10, 13 and 14 will become seven-digit numbers starting with the digit "7" (e.g., 109999 will become 7109999, 139999 will become 7139999, and 149999 will become 7149999). To call these numbers from the U.S., dial 011, Latvia's country code (371), and the seven-digit number. 2) All seven-digit telephone numbers starting with "88" will see the first digit in their number change from "8" to "7" (e.g., 8829999 will become 7829999). The procedure for calling these numbers from the U.S. remains the same as it has been: 011-371 and the number. If you dial the old AXE number, you will be connected automatically to the new number until the end of 1995, and both numbers will be in service simultaneously. According to Lattelekom, these changes will not affect telephone rates, which were increased substantially when the international exchange became operational in August. The call for an investigation of Lattelekom was presented to the Saeima on December 22 by Democratic Party faction leader Ilga Grava. Grava, backed by 38 deputies from a variety of factions, is calling for the creation of both a parliamentary commission, as well as one of independent professionals, to look into alleged wrongdoing by officials at the Transport Ministry and the Lattelekom monopoly. On December 23, the Saeima indeed appointed a nine-member commission, with one representative from each of the seated political factions, to audit the creation and activities of Lattelekom. Their report is due March 1. In a related matter, Saeima deputy and Latvian National Independence Movement Chairman Aristids Lambergs resigned his post, and returned all compensation he received, as a director of Lattelekom. The Saeima actions are a response to a petition, sponsored by Neatkarīga Cina and signed by 14,000 residents. The newspaper published a series of investigative reports published in October and November, accusing the government of selling out Latvian interests in the strategic telecommunications industry by awarding the Lattelekom contract to TILTS Communications, and has portrayed TILTS and its Western Latvian backers as foreign exploiters of Latvia and the pawns of the British intelligence service. *']

Representative_Docs:
['Baltie State Prime Ministers: Border Crossing with Russia - a problem for the entire EU

On 3 October, following their meeting at the Dikļi Palace in the Valmiera region, the Baltie State Prime Ministers repeatedly emphasised that border Crossing with Russia is a problem for the entire European Union (EU) not merely a problem for individual organisations in member States. Latvian Prime Minister Aigars Kalvitis announced to joumalists that the Baltie States had agreed to approach the European Commission with a reguest

to direct particular attention to the border-crossing problems with their “Big Neighbour” atthe next EU-Russian Federation Summit.

Estonian Prime Minister Andrus Ansip pointed out that, “this is nota normai situation”, and that border Crossing is nota problem of bilateral relations, but a problem for the EU and Russia. During their working session, the three Prime Ministers agreed that eonstruetive relations with Russia must be developed in the future. The government leaders congratulated Latvia on the ratification of the Border Treaty with Russia, and expressed the hope that it would come into force. The Prime Ministers pointed out that a pilot project on the exchange of customs Information must be implemented, and that the roads infrastrueture in reģions near the Russian border zone must be developed. The long lines of freight transport vehicles become a systemic problem for Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia, and Finland in recent years.', 'RUSSIA WON’T SIGN BORDER TREATY WITH ESTONIA

Russian Foreign Minister Yevgenii Primakov on 9 January said that Russia will not sign a border agreement with Es tonia. He added that Russia should not be “afraid” to impose economic sanctions on Estonia to protect the rights of Rus sian speakers living there. Former Esto nian Foreign Minister Siim Kallas said Primakov’s statement was probably due to a domestic political struggle in Rus sia. Last November, he agreed to drop the Estonian demand that the agreement mention the 1920 Tartu Treaty. His suc cessor, Toomas llves, said during an of ficial vīsit to Sweden that Primakov’s statement was “regrettable” and “incom prehensible”, particulariy because it de marcates the border where Russia had unilaterally fixed it two years earlier. He noted that Estonia was prepared to sign the agreement and expected Russia to do the same as a step toward enhanc ing the security and stability of Europe.', 'President Putin signs law on ratification of Latvia-Russia Border Treaty

On 3 October, Russian President Vladimir Putin signed the law ratifying the Latvia-Russia Border Treaty. On 5 September, the State Dūma (Parliament) of the Russian Federation adopted the Bili ratifying the Border Treaty which was signed on 27 May, and approved by the Upper Chamber of the Dūma, the Council of the Federation, on 19 September this year. Russian Prime Minister Mikhail Fradkov and Latvian Prime Minister Aigars Kalvitis signed the Border Treaty in Moscow on 27 March this year. The Latvian Saeima (Parliament) ratified the Border Treaty on 17 May, and on 29 May the approved treaty was signed by the then Latvian President, Vaira Vike-Freiberga. For ratification to be complete, both parties to the Treaty will need to exchange the Ratification Instruments. With the signing by Russian President Putin of the law ratifying the Border Treaty, Russia has fulfilled the promise made to Latvja, said Latvian Foreign Minister Artis Pabriks. “The next step is to agree with the Russian side on the exchange of these Instruments, about who will do what, and at what time,” said Minister Pabriks, indicating that prior to that, the instrument may possibly also need to bear the signature of the new Latvian

President, Valdis Zatlers. “VVe can start serious diplomatic discussions on the exchange of the Border Treaty instruments,” acknowledged Mr Pabriks, pointing out that the possible exchange of ratification instruments had already been discussed during a meeting in NewYorkwith Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov.']

Name: 52_latvian citizenship_naturalisation board_citizenship naturalisation_naturalisation process
Representation: latvian citizenship_naturalisation board_citizenship naturalisation_naturalisation process_non citizens_naturalization board_naturalised citizens_naturalization applications_granted citizenship_together parents
Representative_Docs: ['106,324 persons receive Latvian citizenship since beginning of naturalisation process Since the beginning of the naturalisation process, regional departments of the Latvian Naturalization Board had received 113,640 applications for naturalisation in respect of 126,237 persons. Latvian citizenship had been accorded to 106,324 persons. By decree of the Latvian Cabinet of Ministers, 12,413 under-age children have been accorded Latvian citizenship, being naturalised together with their parents. Since 5 February 1999, the Naturalization Board regional departments had received 5,074 applications in respect of citizenship for children born in Latvia after 21 August 1991 to parents who are non-citizens or stateless persons. Citizenship was granted in 4,748 cases.', '113,972 persons gain Latvian citizenship by naturalisation since 1995 Since the naturalisation process began on 1 February 1995, a total of 113,972 persons have received Latvian citizenship. Since 1 February 1995 the Naturalization Board of the Republic of Latvia (NB) has received 117,886 applications for Naturalisation, in which 130,663 persons expressed the wish to become Latvian citizens. By order of the Government, 113,972 persons, including 12,852 minors who naturalised together with their parents, have been awarded Latvian citizenship. Since the naturalisation process began the NB has also received 5,757 applications for Latvian citizenship in lespect of children born in Latvia after it regained independence, to noncitizens and to stateless persons. Of these applicantsthere were 5,365 children who have now been recognised as Latvian citizens.', 'Naturalisation Board head: in 10-years time, the number of non-citizens has decreased almost by 50% The Latvian Naturalisation Board (NB) is proud to notice that the 10 years of its operation has bore fruit as almost half of Latvian non-citizens have chosen to receive Latvian citizenship. While in 1995, there were 735 thousand non-citizens in Latvia, now the number is 452 thousand, the Head of the NB, Eiženija Aldermane, told in a press conference. If the naturalisation process continues at the same pace, it can be completed in the next six or seven years, estimated the Deputy Head of NB, janis Kahanovics, adding that some 130 thousand people would still choose to retain the non-citizen status for the rest of their life. According to Kahanovics, 85,352 people have been naturalised since the beginning of this process almost 10 years ago. 10,770 of these people are underage childen who received the Latvian citizenship together with their parents. Since 21 August 1991, 3088 children of non-citizens and stateless people have received the Latvian citizenship. Through international cooperation, NB has prepared and published over 50 informative, educational and methodological materials for candidates for Latvian citizenship, developed and implemented 45 different projects, for example, the study and action programme "Towards Civic Society"; established the Information Centre, developed and planned the campaign "Provision of Information and Raising of Awareness on Citizenship Issues". NB is the main actor in the field of implementation of the Latvian Law on citizenship. Initially, the Board performed only the tasks related to acquisition of citizenship through naturalisation, however, since 1999, it is responsible also for registration of citizenship status, granting of Latvian citizenship to children born in Latvia after 21 August 1991, matters related to cancellation of citizenship and society integration issues.']

Representative_Docs: ["VENTSPILS HANDLES 10.4% MORE VENTSPILS, 5 September, BNS – Latvia's largest port Ventspils in the west of Latvia has handled 26.476 million tons in eight months this year, which is 2.496 million or 10.4% more yearon-year. The Ventspils Freeport deputy administrator, Guntis Tirmanis, reported that in August the port handled 3.028 million tons, which is 0.411 million tons less than in July, and 0.3 million tons more than last August. Half a million tons less crude oil and oil products were handled in August at the port, seeing 2.265 million tons of liquid cargo, 0.714 million tons of dry-bulk cargo and 0.049 million tons of general cargo. Tirmanis said that optimistic forecasts still prevail in expectation of the port handling a record annual amount this year, handling around 38.4 million tons of cargo. Last year Ventspils handled 34.757 million tons of cargo.", 'CARGO TURNOVER IN LATVIAN PORTS UP 13% IN 7 MONTHS RIGA, 22 August, BNS – Latvian ports reloaded a total of 34.97 million tons of cargo in seven months of this year, up by 13% year-on-year, reported the Transportation Ministry. Of the above total, 33.435 million tons were outgoing cargos and 1.535 million tons were incoming cargos. Two-thirds of shipments reloaded in Latvian ports over the period were handled by Ventspils port in western Latvia which posted sevenmonth cargo turnover of 23.447 million tons, up by 10.4% year-on-year. Riga port, the second largest in Latvia, reloaded 9.027 million tons from January to July this year, a 19% growth over 2000. Liepāja port, which ranks third among Latvian ports, reported seven-month cargo turnover of 1.963 million tons, an 8% increase year-on-year. The aggregate cargo turnover of seven small Latvian ports reached 533,300 tons in seven months of 2001, up by 32% from 2000.', 'CARGO TURNOVER IN LIEPĀJA UP 31.3% lbs port ot Liepāja in western I_3tvi3 in tbs first eleven montbs of 2002 bsncllscl s totsl of 3.91 million tons of cargo, a 31.3°/» growtb over tbs corresponding figure for 2001. In November tbs port rslosdsd 474.500 tons of cargo, including 425,100 tons ot outgoing cargos and 49,400 tons of incoming cargos. General csrgo sccountsd for 2.463 million tons or 63°/o of all cargos rslosdsd in tbs port of Liepāja over the period, an increase by 29.9% from 2001. Ferrous metals and timber were the leading cargo in this category at 892,000 tons and 701,000 tons. The amount of ferrous metals handled by the port rose by 63.9% from January-November 2001 and for timber by 7.7%. The amount of liquid cargo reloaded at the port was up by 52% over last year, reaching 814,200 tons in the first eleven months of 2002 or 21% of the total cargo turnover at the port so far this year, including 674,000 tons of oil products, an increase of 37.5%. Bulk cargo handled reached 632,900 tons or 16% of total cargo turnover in the same period, up by 38.9% from 2001. In this category, grain and grain products took the lead with 302,200 tons, rising 3.8 times compared to last year. The stevedoring company Liepājas Osta LM boasted the largest cargo turnover in the port, reloading 1.065 million tons from January to November 2002, a growth of 71.9% from 2001. Terrabalt ranked second with a cargo turnover of 641,800 tons, up by 13.9% compared to the first eleven months of last year, followed by Glen Oil, which reloaded 500,900 tons of cargo to the end November, up 0.6% from 2001. In 2001 the annual cargo turnover at the Port of Liepāja was 3.26 million lats, up 10% from 2000. It is the third largest port in Latvia after Ventspils and Riga.']

['Negotiations with International Lenders Review Mission Successfully Completed On April 14, review mission negotiations between the Latvian government and International Monetary Fund (IMF) and European Commission (EC) were successfully completed. Prime Minister Valdis Dombrovskis expressed his satisfaction with the completion of the mission: "As regards the government, we have reached the mission-level agreement. [..] In addition to [..] provisional amount of consolidation of the 2012 budget [..] we have also specified the amount of the share of the financial sector's stabilization reserve to be exempted – EUR 300 million. The main precondition here is to submit the strategic plan for sale of Citadele banka to the European Commission. Afterwards the Economic and Financial Committee will decide on this issue." The negotiations have resulted in the draft Letter of Intent of Latvia to the IMF and supplemental Memorandum of Understanding between Latvia and the EC, as well as a number of agreements on which final decisions at the IMF and EC management level will be made in the nearest future. Dombrovskis emphasised: "During the last two years Latvian government has been implementing serious, though unpopular programme to stabilize economy, resume competitiveness and strengthen sustainable growth. [..] If in 2012 Maastricht criteria are met, in 2014 we will be able to introduce the euro. It will be a new incentive for further development, attraction of investments and increase in living standards for all Latvian population." In view of Latvia's strategic goal – to ensure economic stabilisation and growth revival, as well as the plan to introduce the euro in 2014, the agreed budget deficit for 2012 is under 3% of GDP. Taking into account the consolidation measures to be performed and rising growth, Latvia could achieve even a better result – budget deficit in amount of 2.5% of GDP. The main 2012 budget consolidation directions and further action directions have been agreed with the lenders, focusing the consolidation on expenditure cuts, combating shadow economy and reform of real estate tax, evaluating possibilities to make state subsidies more efficient, ensuring implementation of sustainable social insurance system conception. The government will continue implementation of structural reforms in education and health care sectors, ensure development of the social safety net strategy for 2012 and 2013 by August 2011 and a number of other measures. During the negotiations the lenders accepted Latvia's initiative to use gradually for general government budget deficit financing goals part of the international loan which is now reserved in the Treasury in amount of EUR 650 million for ensuring stability of the financial system. If financial sector related issues are solved successfully, it is planned that already in June it will be possible to transfer almost half of this reserve to finance the 2011 state budget.', 'EU Recognises Improved Latvian Economy and Approves Continuation of Loan Program On July 20, as the international loan program review mission was successfully closed, the Commissioner for Economic and Monetary Affairs of the European Commission Olli Rehn signed the new Supplemental Memorandum of Understanding between the European Union and Republic of Latvia. According to the payment schedule of the international loan program in September Latvia will receive the next payment from the European Commission amounting to EUR 200 million. "The program is on track, financial conditions have largely stabilized and the economic situation is showing signs of improvement," said Olli Rehn. According to Rehn, the Memorandum "puts strong emphasis on steps to improve the business environment, competitiveness, public procurement, public sector efficiency, EU funds absorption and other elements important for Latvia's economy to be efficient and competitive." Finance Minister Einars Repše stated: "The signed Memorandum with the European Commission is the outcome of government's work, as well as a proof that Latvian economy stabilizes. Implementation of fiscal policy is aimed at budget deficit reduction to ensure fastest possible recovery of the economy and improvement of competitiveness in international markets. Despite the already reached achievements we have to continue targeted work to reduce excessive state budget deficit." Edited by Pēteris Kļaviņš', 'EU Recognizes Improved Latvian Economy and Approves Continuation of Loan Program

On July 20, as the international loan program revievv mission was successfully closed, the Commissioner for Economic and MonetaryAffairs ofthe European Commission Olli Rehn signed the new Supplemental Memorandum of Understanding between the European Union and Republic of Latvia. According to the payment schedule of the international loan program in September Latvia will receive the next payment from the European Commission amounting to EUR 200 million.

“The program is on track, financial conditions have largely stabilized and the economic situation is showing signs of improvement,” said Olli Rehn. According to Rehn, the Memorandum "pūts strong emphasis on steps to improve the business environment, competitiveness, public procurement, public sector efficiency, EU funds absorption and other elements important for Latvia’s economy to be efficient and competitive” Finance Minister Einars Repše stated: “The signed Memorandum with the European Commission is the outcome of government’s work, as well as a proofthat Latvian economy stabilizēs. Implementation of fiscal policy is aimed at budget deficit reduction to ensure fastest possible recovery ofthe economy and improvement of competitiveness in International marķēts. Despite the already reached achievements we have to continue targeted work to reduce excessive State budget deficit.”

The Memorandum includes an evaluation of the current economic situation in Latvia and concludes that the Latvian economy shows signs of recovery. The document

fixes an agreement regarding the work to be done to prepare the State budget for 2011 and necessary amount of consolidation, approximately LVL 395 - 440 million (EUR 562 - 626 min). The Memorandum also provides for the continuation of a tight fiscal policy and structural reforms, which have been undertaken by the Prime Minister Valdis Dombrovskis’ governmentforthe last year and a half. The policy was designed to return the Latvian economy on a stabilisation and grovvth path. It entails improvement of EU funds acguisition and regulation of public procurements, implementation of employment measures, and has provisions for combating the shadow economy. On the Latvian side it was signed by Prime Minister Valdis Dombrovskis, Minister of Finance Einars Repše, Governor of the Bank of Latvia Ilmārs Rimšēvičs, as well as Chairwoman of the Financial and Capital Marķēt Commission Irēna Krūmane.

This week at the meeting of the International Monetary Fund Board it is also planned to approve the Letter of Intent of the Republic of Latvia to the IMF.

The Latvian Institute']
