Representative_Docs: ["Mila and our children join me in extending our warmest wishes for a joyful and festive holiday season. As we celebrate with family and friends, we can take this opportunity to reflect on the blessings we share as Canadians, and to rejoice in the good will and promise this festive season holds. At this special time of year, it is my hope that Canadians will have an opportunity to reflect on all that we have accomplished together, and all that remains to be done to build a stronger and more prosperous country. To each of you, our very best wishes for a joyous holiday season and good fortune in the coming year. Mila et les enfants se joignent ā moi pour vous souhaiter d'agreables Fetes. En cc temps de joyeuses retrouvailles entre parents et amis, nous avons I'occasion de mediter sur les bienfaits dont nous sommes comblēs en tant que Canadiens et de nous rējouir dv climat d'espoir et de bonne volontē gui regne en cette saison. Je formulē le voeu que tousles Canadiens pensent ā tout cc que nous avons accompli ensemble et ā cc qu'il reste ā faire pour bātir un pays plus fort et plus prospēre. Nous vous souhaitons ā tous et ā toutes de trēs joyeuses Fetes et une trēs bonne annee. OTTAWA 1991 PRIME MINISTER – PREMIER MINISTRE", "SPEECH FROM THE THRONE To open the first session of the thirty-seventh Parliament of Canada January 30, 2001 Honourable Members of the Senate, Members of the House of Commons, Ladies and Gentlemen: It is my great pleasure to greet you on the first day of the 37th Parliament since Confederation. The vital relationship that exists between the Canadian people and Parliament is what we celebrate today through history, custom and symbolism. I also have the plesaure of welcoming new members to the House of Commons, and I want all parliamentarians to know how deeply I appreciate the ideals that motivate you to serve your country. I also appreciate the sacrifices brought on by your task and made in the name of the common good and of leadership. And in a healthy democracy, leadership can come from everyone, because it is a sense of really knowing what you want and what you can contribute. A little more than a year ago, I became Canada's 26th Governor General. I set out as the main objective of my first year in office to visit every province and territory and to meet as many Canadians as possible, wherever they live and make their lives – to engage in a true dialogue with them. I have seen many places that Canadians have decided to call home, from big cities to small hamlets, from the Island of Montreal to Ellesmere Island. But what we call home is more than a place name on a map. It is our belonging to a community of ideas and ideals; it is the knowledge that we can say something and be listened to, and the conviction that we can respect, rely upon and help each other. Meeting Canadians across the country has given me the opportunity to hear about the different challenges we face. Looking at these issues, we of course remember that we have demonstrated, time and time again, that we have the self-confidence to act and to act successfully. Change does not frighten us – we have always harnessed it to our advantage. It is often said that our country's strength lies in diversity. Why is this so? Because diversity imposes serious responsibilities. Indeed, if we accept our place in a rich and successful society, we must also acknowledge and engage with the parts of our society that are less advantaged. The Government has been given a third mandate by Canadians. In leading Canada into the new millennium, its overarching goal will continue to be to build a stronger, ever more inclusive Canada and secure a higher quality of life for all Canadians. In pursuing this aim, the Government will carry out the commitments set out in its election platformCanada is proud, optimistic and strong. The Government has achieved the critical objective of restoring the nation's finances. The economy continues its longest period of economic expansion since the 19605. Inflation and interest rates are low and stable. More than two million new jobs have been created since 1993. Significant new investments are being made in children and youth, in innovation and skills, in health care, and in the environment As we enter this new century, Canada and Canadians (ace the challenges of competing in a faster-paced, technology-driven world economy. Of responding to economic uncertainty among our trading partners. Of continuing to strengthen the fabric of our society in an era of increasing globalization. And of advancing our Canadian interest and values in the international arena. We must ensure that every region, every province and territory, every community, and every citizen has a strong voice and can contribute to building our nation. To bring the benefits of our prosperity to all communities, whether urban, rural, Northern or remote. To promote innovation, growth and development in all parts of our economy, including our agricultural and resource sectors and our manufacturing and service industries. Canadians must rise to these challenges. Success in our more interdependent and complex world require the contribution of all Canadians. The Government of Canada, for its part, will focus on: * building a world-leading economy driven by innovation, ideas and talent; * creating a more inclusive society where children get the right start in life, where quality health services are available to all, and where Canadians enjoy strong and safe communities; * ensure a clean, healthy environment for Canadians and the preservation of our natural spaces; and * enchancing our Canadian voice in the world and our shared sense of citizenship. In fulfilling its responsibilities, the Government of Canada will be guided by the values of Canadians. It will work with other levels of government, the private and voluntary sectors, and individual citizens. It will continue to set bold goals and work toward them in a pragmatic, step-by-step way. It will continue to be a prudent steward of the nation's finances as it focusses on the priorities of Canadians. Its efforts will be affordable and sustainable. The Government reaffirms its commitment to balanced budgets. To assist the Government in fulfilling its reponsibilities, Canada must have a public service distinguished by excellence asnd equipped with the skills for a knowledge economy and society. The Government will seek bright, motivated young women and men to accept the challenge of serving their country in the federal public service. The Government is committed to the reforms needed for the Public Service of Canada to continue evolving and adapting. These reforms will ensure that the Public Service is innovative, dynamic and reflective of the diversity of the country – able to attract and develop the talent needed to serve Canadians in the 21st century. The Government will help to create opportunity for Canadians and ensure that opportunity is shared by all. This is the Canadian Way for the 21st century. Creating Opportunity An innovative economy is essential to creating opportunity for Canadians. An innovative economy is driven by research and development It requires a highly skilled work force and investments in new technology. A businesss environment and tax policies that encourage smart risk taking and entrepreneurship and that reward success. An attractive environment for investment And a strong global brand for Canadian excellence. An innovative economy is one where the benefits of new ideas are shared by every sector end every region – from East to West to North, from office workers to farm families. Canada has laid a solid foundation for success in the new economy. Our economic fundamentals are among the best in the world. Spiralling debt and deficits have been replaced by social and economic investments, tax cuts and debt repayments. On January Ist of this year, most elements of the Government's comprehensive and broad-based package of 100 billion in tax relief took effect. We are better positioned than at any time in the last three decades to seize the opportunities of the global economy and to weather the short-term slowing of growth experienced by Canada's major trading partners. Innovation To secure our continued success in the 21st century, Canadians must be among the first to generate new knowledge and put it to use. Our objective should be no less than to be recognized as one of the most innovative countries in the world. Achieving this will require a comprehensive approach and the support and participation of all governments, businesses, educational institutions, and individual Canadians. We must strive for Canada to become one of the top five countries for research and development performance by 2010. This is a challenge for all Canadians, but in particular for the private sector as the largest research investor in Canada. As its contribution, the Government will at least double current federal investment in research and development by 2010. In making new investments, the Government will: * continue to pursue excellence in Canadian research by strengthening the research capacity of Canadian universities and government laboratories and institutions; * accelerate Canada's ability to commercialize research discoveries, turning them into new products and services, and * pursue a global strategy for Canadian science and technology, supporting more collaborative international research at the frontiers of knowledge. New federal investments will include strategically targetted reseach that is co-ordinated with partners. These investments will directly benefit Canadians in areas such as health, water quality, the environment, natural resources management, and oceans research. Among its investments, the Government will increase support for the development of new technologies to assist Canadians with disabilities. Research in life sciences will benefit all of Canada, particularly our agricultural and rural economies. The Government will help Canada's agricultural sector move beyond crisis management – leading to more genuine diversification and value-added growth, new investments and employment, better land use, and high standards of environmental stewardship and food safety. Skills and learning Canada will only realize its full potential by investing aggressively in the skill and talents of its people. To succeed in the knowledge economy, Canada will need people with advanced skills and entrpreneurial spirit Canada's youth are optimistic, technologically savvy, globally connected and most highly educated generation in our history. The Government will continue to help young Canadians contribute to their country, gain employment, and apply their business and creative skills. Building a skilled work force must be a national effort. The Government of Canada will work with provinces and territories and with non-governmental organizations to ensure that all Canadians, young and old, can achieve their learning goals. Canada must see at least one million more adults pursue learning opportunities during the next five years. The Government will also help adults who want to improve their skill, but who may face difficulty in finding time or resources to do this while providing for themselves and their families. It will create Registered Individual Learning Accounts to make it easier for Canadians to finance their learning. And it will improve the loans that are available to part-time students, so more workers can learn while they earn. Some Canadians face particular challenges upgrading their skills and enchancing their education. The Government will take steps to make it easier for them to access skills and learning. * Youth at risk are among the most likely to drop out of school or to have difficulty in making the transition from school to work. The Government will work with its partners to ensure support for youth who particularly need help staying in school or getting their first job. * Today, many Canadian adults lack the higher literacy skill needed in the new economy. The Government of Canada will invite the provinces and territories along with the private sector and voluntary organizations to launch a national initiative with the goal of significantly increasing the proportion of adults with these higher-level skills. •Persons with disabilities face barriers to full participation in the economy and society. The Government of Canada will work with the provinces and territories and other partners toward a comprehensive labour-market strategy for persons with disabilities. * Increasing numbers of Aboriginal people are developing their business skills and competing in the new economy. The Government will work with Aboriginal people to help strengthen their entrepreneurial and business expertise. Immigrants have enriched Canada with their ideas and talents. The Government will take steps to help Canada attract the skilled workers it needs. It will also work in co-operation with the provinces and territories to secure better recognition of the foreign credentials of new Canadians and their more rapid integration into society. The Goverment will re-introduce changes to immigration legislation to streamline and improve the immigration system. Connecting Canadians The Government has helped to make Cnada one of the most connected countries in the world, yet the speed of change continues to accelerate. Canada must continue to develop and strengthen its information infrastructure. The private sector today is expanding high-speed access to the Internet in many regions. The National Broadband Task Force will advise the Government on how Canadians together can achieve the critical goal of making broadband access widely available to citizens, businesses, public institutions and to all communities in Canada by 2004. The Government will continue to support the Community Access Program and School Net, ensuring that Canadians, their communities and their schools can have an on-ramp to the information highway. These programs are critical to Canada's effort to close the digital divide, particularly in rural, remote, Northern and Aboriginal communities. The Government will also enchance School/Net, focussing on creating more and better learning content on-line. The Government will continue to work toward putting its services on-line by 2004, to better connect with citizens. It will also modernize federal privacy law to safeguard the personal information of Canadians and provide better copyright protection for new ideas and knowledge. Trade and Investment The Government's investments in innovation, skills and connectivity, as well as its reduction of corporate taxes and improved treatment of capital gains, are making Canada one of the most attractive places to invest and to do business. In addition, the Government will: * ensure that Canadian laws and regulations remain among the most modern and progressive in the world, including those for intellectual property and eompetiveness; and * re-introduce legislation to promote a strong and efficient financial services sector that will benefit the Canadian economy and all Canadians. The Government will work closely with the United States, Canada's most important trading partner, to maintain secure and efficient access to each other's markets. It will continue the joint work toward creating the Free Trade Area of the Americas. The Government will also launch a branding strategy to raise awareness of the advantages of investing in Canada. As part of this effort, the Government will continue its successful Team Canada trade missions and launch Investment Team Canada missions to the United States and Europe. Sharing Opportunity The Canadian Way recognizes that economic and social success must be pursued together. We cannot build a prosperoeus society in the absence of economic growth. We cannot lead in innovation and new ideas without healthy and secure citizens. We must not pursue our interests in the world without strengthening our distinct culture and values here at home. Nowhere is the creation and sharing of opportunity more important than for Aboriginal people. Too many continue to live in poverty, without the tools they need to build a better future for themselves or their communities. As a country, we must be direct about the magnitude of the challenge and ambitious in our commitment to tackle the most pressing problems facing Aboriginal people. Reaching our objectives will take time, but we must not be deterred by the length of the journey or the obstacles that we may encounter along the way. The Government is committed to strenghening its relationship with Aboriginal people. It will support First Nations communities in strengthening governance, including implementing more effective and transparent administrative practices. And it will work to ensure that basic needs are met for jobs, health, education, housing and infrastructure. This commitment will be reflected in all the Government's priorities. Children and Families Securing a good start in life for children is the only way to ensure that they are ready to learn, to seize opportunity as adults, and to contribute to the building of their country. There was time in Canada when retirement often meant facing a new life of hardship. A generation ago, Canadians set a national goal to eliminate poverty among seniors, and we have made significant progress. There was time in this country when falling sick meant risking one's life savings. Working together, Canadians built a national, publicly funded health care system to ensure access to quality care for every citizen regardless of income. There was a time when loosing a job also meant immediate loss of income for workers and their families. And so Canadians created Employment Insurance. Now Canadians must undertake another national project – to ensure that no Canadian child suffers the debilitating effects of poverty. Canadians and their governments have already taken significant steps in this direction. A strong economy and job creation have been essential to reducing poverty nd ensuring that families have the resources to care for their children. But esonomic frowth alone is not enough. Governments alse have a key role to play in helping families left behind and in improving support to families and children. All governments have put in place a range of measures to help families and children. The National Child Benefit is the cornerstone of our collective efforts to provide children with a better start It is the single most important social program to be introduced in this country since medicare in the 19605. The Government of Canada's contribution to the National Child Benefit will continue to rise over the next four years. Most recently, the Government of Canada and provinces and territories launched the Early Childhood Development initiative to expand and improve access to services for all families and children. The Government of Canada is investing more than $2 billion in this initiative over five years. As part of this agreement governments will begin reporting to Canadians on the outcomes of their programs and services for children. These reports will give the Government of Canada and its partners the information they need to take whatever additional steps are necessary to provide Canadian children with a better start in life. The Government of Canada will also take immediate action with its partners where the challenges are greatest * Single parents and their children often face special challenges overcoming poverty. The governments of Canada, New Brunswick and British Columbia have been testing new approaches to help single parents become more self-suffiecient. The Government of Canada is prepared to test innovations with other provinces and territories, with the longer-term aim of developing new measures that help these parents overcome poverty. * The Government will work with its partners on modernizing the laws for child support, custody, and accesss – to ensure that these work in the best interests of children in cases of family breakdown. * It will improve the support available to parents and caregivers in times of family crisis. No Canadian should have to choose between keeping their job and providing palliative care to a child. The Government will take steps to enable parents to provide care to a gravely ill child without fear of sudden income or job loss. * In securing a better future for Aboriginal children, the government will work with First Nations to improve and expand the early childhood development programs and services available in their communities It will also expand significantly the Aboriginal Head Start program, to better prepare more Aboriginal children for school and help those with special needs. * The Government of Canada will also co-operate with Aboriginal communities and provinces and territories on the measures required to reduce the number of aboriginal newborns affected by fetal alcohol syndrome. No child should experience this syndrome, but Canada's immediate aim must be to significantly reduce its incidence in the Aboriginal population by the end of this decade. Good Health and Quality Care A healthy Canadian society is built on the behalf and well-being of individual Canadians and the health of our communities. Canadians place a high priority on good health and on their health care system. We know that our systen of medicare, which ensures access to needed services regardless of income or place of residence, is vital to our quality of life. It is Canadian advantage and deeply valued by all citizens. The Gouvernment of Canada will uphold the Canada Health Act It will work with the provinces and territories to ensure that all governments continue to fulfil their commitment to the principles of medicare. Governments in Canada have come together to strengthen and renew Canada's health system. Last September, First Ministers affirmed the commitment of their governments to the principles of the Canada Health Act and endorsed a health action plan that will enable them to move forward in building a modern, integrated and sustainable health system for Canadians. Over the next three years, governments will take concrete action to reform and support innovation in primary care, to adopt modern health information technologies, and to purchase needed diagnostic and medical equipment. For its part, the Goverment of Canada is commiting more than $21 billion in new funding to the provinces and territories over five years through the Canada Health and Social Transfer. The Government will also champion community-based health promotion and disease prevention measures. * It will strengthen its efforts to encourage physical fitness and participation in sport, and take further steps to combat substance abuse, reduce tobacco consumption, prevent injuries and promote mental health. * It will advance progress on disease prevention, focussing in particular on reducing the incidence of preventable diabetes and tuberculosis – especially among Aboriginal people, who suffer disproportionately from these diseases. * The Government will also provide a further major increase in funding to Canadian Institutes of Health Research. The new funding will enable the Institutes to enhance their research into disease prevention and treatment, the determinants of health, and health system effectiveness. Building on the health action plan's commitment to public reporting, the Government of Canada will work with the provinces and territories to create a citizens' council on health care quality. This council will ensure that the public's perspective ir considered in developing meaningful indicators of health sysrtem performance. As public reporting begins on how the health system is meeting the needs of Canadians, governments will use this information to continue to move forward with the renewal of medicare. A Clean Environment Canada is blessed by the beauty of its vast landscape and the wealth of its natural resources. But with the blessing comes the responsibility to ensure its preservation. A healthy enviroment is an essential part of a sustainable economy and our quality of life. The Government's focus will be on the three priorities of clean air, clean water, and the consevation of Canada's natural spaces. Last December, the Government of Canada signed an agreement with the United States to significantly reduce the emissions that cause smog. This agreement will lead to a 90 percent reduction im smog-causing vehicle emissions by 2010, bringing cleaner air to millions of citizens in both countries. The Government will move quickly to implement this agreement and other mesures, working with the provinces and territories to achieve cleaner air. It is Canada's responsibility, as steward of one of the world's largest supplies of fresh water, to protect this critical resource. Safeguarding our water is a shared task among governments, industry and individual Canadians. The Gouvernment of Canada will fulfil its direct responsibilities for water, including the safety of water supplies on reserves and federal lands. * The Government will also lead in developing stronger national guidelines for water quality by enhancing scientific research and continuing its collaboration with partners. Drawing on expertise within the Government and from across Canada, it will significantly strengthen the role of the National Water Research Institute. It will fund improvements to municipal water and waste water systems through the federal-provincial-municipal Infrastructure Canada program. It will also invest in research and development and advanced information systems to enable better land use and protect surface and ground water supplies from the impact of industrial and agricultural operations. Canadians are the guardians of a significant percentage of the world's wilderness and wildlife. The Government will invest in the creation of new national parks and implement a plan to restore existing parks to ecological health. It will work with its partners toward more integrated, sustainable management of Canada's oceans. And it will re-introduce legislation for marine conservation areas and to protect species at risk. As part of its efforts to promote global sustainable development, the Government will ensure that Canada does its part to reduce greehouse gas emissions. It will work with its provincial and territorial partners to implement the recently announced first national business plan on climate change. To safeguard Canadians from toxic substances and environmental contaminants, the Government will also strengthen laws, research efforts and other measures for Health protection. This will include he development of appropriate standards that reflect the special vulnerabilities of children. Strong and Safe Communities Strong and safe communities are an essential part of the fabric of our society. They are critical to providing Canadians with the security to build a better future for themselves and their families. They are also important to attracting talented people from around the world to come and make their home here. Canadian communities of all sizes – whether urban or rural, Aboriginal or multicultural – face diverse challenges and have unique needs. The Government of Canada will strive to ensure that, wherever possible, its actions and programs are co-ordinated to help build local solutions to local challanges. It will work with partners across Canada to launch a dialogue on the opportunities and challenges facing urban centres. It will co-operate with provincial and municipal partners to help improve public transit infrastructure. And it will help to stimulate the creation of more affordable rental housing. Ensuring that Canada's communities are safe is an important element of fostering and attracting people needEd for success in the new economy. Crime rates in Canada have fallen steadily for almost a decade. The Government of Canada will continue to work with provinces and territories, communities, and all its partners to implement a balanced approach to addressing crime – focussing on prevention as much as punishment, strengthening penalties for serious crime, and considering the needs of victims. The Government will focus on safeguarding Canadians from new and emerging forms of crime. It will take agressive steps to combat organized crime, including the creation of stronger anti-gang laws and measures to protect members of the justice system from intimidation. It will provide enhanced law enforcement tools to deal with emerging threats to security, such as cybercrime and terrorism. It will act to safeguard children from crime, including criminals on the Internet The Government will take steps to ensure that our laws protect children from those who would prey on their vulnerabily. The Government will re-introduce legislation to change how the justice system deals with youg offenders. It will encourage alternatives to custody for non-violent offenders, emphasizing rehabilitation and re-integration into society, while toughening consequences for more violent youth. Working with the provinces, territories and communities, the Government of Canada will strengthen the capacity of local communities to deal with conflict, prevent crime, and address drug abuse. It is a tragic reality that too many Aboriginal people are finding themselves in conflict with the law. Canada must take measures to significantly reduce the percentage of Aboriginal people entering the criminal justice system, so that within a generation it is no higher than the Canadian average. Vibrant Canadian Culture Canada is defined by far more than its political boundaries or economic relationships. In these times of rapid change and globalization, it is more important then ever that we know who we are as Canadians and what brings us together. The focus of our cultural policies for the future must be on excellence in the creative process, diverse Canadian content, and access to the arts and heritage for all Canadians. Both the English and French networks of the Canadian Broadcasting Corporation have long been cornerstones of Canadian cultural policy. The CBC helps connect Canadians to each other, their history and their country. It reaches all parts of Canada, from big cities to small towns to Nothern and Aboriginal communities. It provides a distinctive Canadian voice in both official languages and important opportunities for our creative people. The Government will increase its support to help the CBC fulfil its distinct role as a public broadcaster serving all Canadiazns. The Government will assist the book-pubushing and sound-recording sectors to make the transition to the new economy. It will continue to support the development of digital content for the Internet and other new media in French and English. And it will work to expand international markets for Canadian cultural products and services. Communities across Canada are increasingly recognizing the importance of arts and heritage for their quality of life and ability to attract talent, investment and tourism. The Government of Canada will continue to work with the private and not-for-profit sectors and other governments to strengthen Canada's cultural infrastructure. It will help communities to develop arts and heritage programs that are sustainable and relevant to their diverse circumstances and aspirations. Creating and Sharing Opportunities Globally The well-being of Canada and Canadians depends on global human security, prosperity and development. The Government of Canada is commited to working with its international partners to promote international peace and security by enhancing the mechanisms for conflict prevention and resolution. It will work to strengthen global governance as well as existing and new multilateral institutions. These include the G-20 – a new forum of which Canada is the first Chair – which is striving to enhance the stability of the world economy and ensure that globalization benefits all its participants. The Government will increase Canada's official development assistance and use these new investments to advance efforts to reduce international poverty and to strengthen democarcy, justice and social stability worldwide. Canadians have become leaders in harnessing the power of technology to build a more inclusive society. This experience serves as a powerful model for the world. Through its participation on the Digital Opportunies Task Force established by the G-8 nations and through its own investments in developing countries, Canada will contribute to closing the global digital divide. The Government will continue Canada's proud record of peacekeeping. In Budget 2000, the Government provided funding increases for the Canadian Forces to help ensure that they are equipped and prepared to respond quickly to calls for help at home and abroad. The Summit of the Americas this year presents an exceptional opportunity to promote a balanced and coherent vision for deepening co-operation among the nations of the Western hemisphere. The summit declaration and action plan will support Canada's interest in strengtening democracy snd human rights, expanding commerce through the Free Trade Area of the Americas initiative, increasing people's access to the benefits of growth, and providing opportunities for all nations in the Americas to improve the quality of life of their citizens. At the Summit of the Americas and as chair of the G-8 in 2002, Canada will work to expand opportunities for more countries to participate in the benefits of globalization, while pressing for peace and security in the world. Celebrating Our Canadian Citizenship Canada was born of a noble vision and an act of will. Our Canadian citizenship has been built over time through the experiences we have shared... ...when we visit other parts of our country or when we travel abroad and see ourselves through the eyes of others. ... when every year thousands of new Canadians stand proudly with their families to take on the responsibilities of Canadian citizenship. ... when we come together to help each other in tough times. And when millions of Canadians voluteer their time and energy to make their communities a better place. The Government will help Canadians to strengthen their bonds of mutual understanding and respect, to celebrate their achievements and history, and to exercise their shared citizenship. * It will continue to expand exchange programs for young Canadians to reach its goal of 100,000 exchanges each year. * Canada's linguistic duality is fundamental to our Canadian identity and is a key element of our vibrant society. The protection and promotion of our two official languages is a priority of the Government – from coast to coast. The Government reaffirms its commitment to support sustainable official language minority communities and a strong French culture and language. And it will mobilize its efforts to ensure that all Canadians can interact with the Government of Canada in either official language. The institutions of Government will continue to be strengthened. Since 1993, the Government has taken a range of measures to enable members or Parliament to more effectively represent the views of their constituents. MPs have participated in pre-budget consultations, at the end of which recommendations were made to the Government. Moreover, private members' bills from the House and Senate have been taken into account more often and considered with greater attention than at any time in the past In this new session of Parliament, the Government will make further proposals to improve procedures in the House and Senate. Among other measures, voting procedures will be modernized in the House of Commons and, to assist parliamentarians in carrying out their duties, the Government intends to increase the resources of the library of Parliament to better serve the research needs of standing committees of the House and the Senate. Every Canadian is called upon to make contribution to building our country. To ensure that the promise of Canada becomes an even greater reality in the 21st century. And to ensure that our Canadian Way remains the best example of what is possible when women and men of every race and creed come together in community in search of a better future. □", "OTTAWA 1991 PRIME MINISTER. PREMIER MINISTRE Mila and our children join me in extending our warmest wishes for a joyful and festive holiday season. As we celebrate with family and friends, we can take this opportunity to reflect on the blessings we share as Canadians, and to rejoice in the good will and promise this festive season holds. At this special time of year, it is my hope that Canadians will have an opportunity to reflect on all that we have accomplished together, and all that remains to be done to build a stronger and more prosperous country. To each of you, our very best wishes for a joyous holiday season and good fortune in the coming year. Mila et les enfants se joignent â moi pour vous souhaiter d'agréables Fêtes. En ce temps de joyeuses retrouvailles entre parents et amis, nous avons l'occasion de méditer sur les bienfaits dont nous sommes comblés en tant que Canadiens et de nous réjouir du climat d'espoir et de bonne volonté qui règne en cette saison. Je formule le voeu que tous les Canadiens pensent à tout ce que nous avons accompli ensemble et à ce qu'il reste à faire pour bâtir un pays plus fort et plus prospère. Nous vous souhaitons à tous et à toutes de très joyeuses Fêtes et une très bonne année."]

Representation: vike freiberga_vaira vike_vīķe freiberga_vtķe freiberga_president vaira_president latvia_latvian president_vaira vīķe_ms vike_latvia vaira
Representative_Docs: ['NEW IRON LADY\'S HERO IN AMERICA Latvian president tough on Iraq By PETER WORTHINGTON Friday, 21 February, 2003 (Received from a reader in Toronto, Canada, published in a Toronto newspaper) During this week\'s controversy over possible war with Saddam Hussein, anti-war protests, and France\'s threat to veto UN action against Saddam, Americans have discovered a new hero. She is an unlikely hero, but hero nonetheless. Vaira Vike-Freiberga was elected president of Latvia in 1999 after she revoked her Canadian citizenship. On this visit to meet President George W. Bush, she is being compared to Margaret Thatcher – America\'s favourite British PM of the Reagan administration. America has fallen for her. All week, President Vike-Freiberga has been wowing them on TV talk-shows. Whereas most foreign leaders give careful, qualified answers to hot questions, not this no-nonsense 64-year-old redhead who was educated at the University of Toronto and was a psychology professor at the University of Montreal. WENT HOME IN 1998 When she returned to visit her native Latvia in 1998, she had no idea she would be running for president "otherwise I\'d have taken more than two suitcases," she said. Her husband and two grown kids closed up their Montreal home after the 100-seat Saeima (parliament) elected her to head Latvia\'s 2.5 million people. American talkshow hosts and phone responses aren\'t used to political leaders who say what they think and don\'t hedge. She\'s an outspoken supporter of President Bush\'s efforts to bring Saddam to heel – or go to war. She explains that she comes from a country that has lived under oppressive dictatorship and knows that you cannot bargain or give in to people like Saddam. She has little patience with the anti-war elements, who have never known dictatorship. They should smarten up. So there. She was seven years old when her family fled Latvia to escape the Soviets. She lived first in Germany, Morocco, France and Canada and is fluent in Latvian, English, French, German, Spanish and some Portuguese. She doesnt (yet) speak Russian, which 40% of Latvians do. One of her first Thatcher-like moves in office was to reject language law legislation to make Latvian mandatory for all businesses. \'Too intrusive," she said, possibly recalling Quebec\'s language laws that make English a crime in certain circumstances. When the Russians started to lean on Latvia, V-F leaned right back and would have none of it. She explained that Russia is "extremely unpredictable ... not very stable and its democratic basis questionable. It must come to terms that we are no longer a Soviet republic but a sovereign state." But it\'s her unqualified support for Bush and his efforts to disarm Saddam that has won her fans. Before being elected as a nonpolitical president (Latvia\'s had eight governments since independence in i 991) President V-F dkJnt dabble in politics. Certainly, her adamant stands on foreign affairs owe nothing to Canada, unless it\'s appreciating what not to do. Ironically, Canada is perilously close to being regarded by Americans as France is – despised by many Americans who see France betraying its bngtime ally over Iraq. Conservative talk-show host Rush Limbaugh is scathing about France: "How many Frenchmen are needed to defend Paris? Dunno, they\'ve never done it." Fox News\' Bill O\'Reilly, whose TV talk show has passed Larry King Live, urges Americans to boycott Canada, not visit or holiday there, because he feels Canada is dragging its feet in the war against terrorism and because it supports France. Pro-America rallies are now planned across the country in response to the antiwar protests last weekend. LAME PROTESTS What\'s working in Bush\'s favour is that "peace" protesters don\'t object to what Saddam Hussein has done to his people, nor oppose the bio-chemical weapons they insist he doesn\'t have. Significantly, polls show over two-thirds of Americans support war against Saddam – roughly the same percentage that polls show Canadians oppose war. If only we, instead of Latvia, had Vaira Vike-Freiberga in charge, perhaps Canada wouldn\'t be the wimp of the western world.', 'LATVIA HAS A NEW PRESIDENT On June 17, 1999 (on the same day in 1940 – 59 years earlier – The Soviet Union occupied latvia under a secret amendment of The Ribentropp-Molotov pact) the Latvian parliament – Saeima – elected a new president – Vaira VĪķe-Freiberga. She received 53 votes on the seventh ballot. Memorable is the fact that Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga is the first woman in the Eastern Europe to hold the office of President of the State. She took office on July 8, 1999. The current term is 4 years. She replaces President Guntis Ulmanis who had served his 2 terms allowed by the Constitution of Latvia. On that same day Saeima passed a historic Language Law. Both events have been widely covered in the European and Western media. On July 10, 1999 "Globe and Mail" covered both in a lead editorial Titled "Latvia imports a Canadian President" and subtitled "Can Vaira Vike-Freiberga serve a a role model for language tolerance?" The editorial comment follows: Vaira Vike-Freiberga isn\'t canola or telephone parts, but she still has the potential to be one of Canada\'s most important exports. After living 44 years in this country, the former University oif Montreal psychological professor returned last year to her native latvia. This June, with the country\'s presidential race deadlocked, she was asked if she would stand for the largely ceremonial office. She did, and she won. Now Ms. Vike-Freiberga talks about bringing a Canadian sensibility to a country whose history bears the scars of conquest and colonization. Since 1237, with only few interludes of independence, Germans, Poles, Swedes, Russians, Germans again, and Russians again have ruled the Baltic country. Russian rule after the Second World War was particularly brutal, with 60,000 Latvians deported to Siberia and hundreds of thousands of Russian colonists brought in. Since 1991, Latvia has been free and strugling. The most trying question has been how to amalgamate the 40 per cent of the population who are Russianspeaking with a Latvian speaking majority. The Latvians initial instinct was vengeful. They passed a law proclaiming that only those Russian speakers who themselves, or whose ancestors, had been in the country m 1940 could become citizens. Everyone else was a stateless, non-voting resident. These provisions have since been reversed, but almost simultaneously with Ms. Vike-Freiberga\'s inauguration the latvian Parliament passed a Draconian language law. Among other things, it requires all signs, posters and advertisments to be written only in lavian. It demands that public speeches be only in Latvian. It severely restricts the ability of Russians to conduct business with the government in Russian and prohibits showing films to  children unless the movies a dubbed in latvian. The law is seen as so discriminatory that latvia has been warned by several European countries that its provisions may torpedo its hopes of joining the European Union. The legislation has also produced protests from Russia and Russian-speakers. The latter have marched under ominous placards announcing "A Ruling Dictatorship I>eads to Kosovo." Enter Ms. Vike-Freiberga. She has declared that Russians are welcome members of the state. She says she will enroll in Russian language classes so she can speak with the minority, and as well as show them that even a 61-year-old can learn a new tongue. She has also argued that Russians must learn the "state language" of Latvian. She likens this to people living here learning either English or French. All of this is good and soundly Canadian. But she also says she will never give a speech in Russian while president She has declared that the Russians ar colonizers who did not do the honourable thing and return to their homeland after the end of empire. In this too she sounds Canadian, but, alas, of the Lord Durham-Parti Québécois militant school. Ultimately, she and Latvia are going to have to decide wluch Canada they will be – the place where two language groups make peace with one another or the place where one kind of linguistic colonialism attempts to supplant another. It is a choice which will determine whether Latvia prospers in a region where it will always have to accomodate its huge and nationalistic Russian neighbour. And it is also a choice which will indicate how good this country has been at teaching Vaira Vike-Freiberga the intrinsic worth of tolerance. □ President V. Viķe-Freiberga has invited two-time premier Andris Šķēle, the leader of the People\'s party CTautas partija) to form the Bth gouvernmet of the renewed Latvian State. It will be a majority gouverment supported by the 3 major parties (Tautas partija, Tēvzemei un Brīvībai un Latvijas ceļš) in the Latvian Parliament (with 62 combined members ol 100). U appears that this will happen quickly and will form a political stability for some time to come. It will replace the minority gouvernment of Vilis Kristopâns. who resigned upon learning that one of the junior parties of the coalition was talking to the oposition.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER A NEW PRESIDENT FOR LATVIA After four unsuccessful tries, Anatolijs Gorbunovs, the some-time ideological chief of the Latvian Communist Party, but since then a staunch supporter of democracy and the people\'s choice for President, Raimonds Pauls – the well-known composer, Vaira Paegle – an immigrant and some-time President of the World Association of Free Latvians, Jānis Priedkalns – another immigrant, Latvian Envoy to the UN, the Latvian Saeima (Parliament) with a three-party consensus managed to elect Mrs. Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga as the second Latvian President of this independence period (the sixth all together). Mrs. Freiberga moved to Latvia in 1998 from Montreal, Canada in order to assume the Presidency of the Latvian Institute. She has a doctorate in experimental phychology, speaks Latvian, English, German, Spanish and, during her first news conference, stated that she would also learn Russian. She firmly announced that Latvia\'s movement to the European Union and NATO are her first objectives. Russia immediately objected for not being mentioned. Touchy, those Russians, or what? She was inaugurated on July 8, 1999. In her fine speech, she urged the Latvian people to care for each other and to enrich each other\'s lives. That night an inaugural ball was held for 900 at the Rundāle castle (qv.). LATVIA\'S GNP DROPS The Latvian GNP, which was upward-mobile last year during the first quarter of 1999, has dropped by 2.3%, when compared to the first three months of 1998. However, both Latvian and EU authorities are not disturbed by this, and expect the GNP to rise to + 1.5 – 2.0 by the end of the year. THE SEA BORDER TREATY FINALLY SIGNED The Prime ministers of Latvia un Lithuania – Vilis Krištopans and Rolandas Paksas – signed the long overdue agreement on the sea-border. It is a 22,2 km (13.9 mile) broken line boder, that does not concern economics or the possibility of oil on the bottom of the Baltic Sea. FIRST AND ONLY BROADCAST PRESS CONFERENCE The Prime minister Vilis Krištopans (The Latvian Way) staged a news conference on television during prime SEPTEMBER 1999 NEWS FROM LATVIA – reported by Laimons Streips in Rīga time, with his press secretary next to him and then selected media representatives to ask questions. The questions for the most part were vicious attacks on the Krištopans government, and this ascerbic man managed to answer them all with a smile and very gently. THE NEW LANGUAGE LAW IS PASSED The new Language law, which has been debated internally and with the European Union for five years, was passed by the Saeima. Since then the law has been severely criticized by Russian authorities as well as by Russians residing in Latvia, of which many simply do not wish to learn Latvian. Moreover, the European Union has also questioned some of the paragraphs of the law. The new President returned the bill to the Saeima, outlining some paragraphs that have to be clarified. ANDRIS ŠĶĒLE -THE NEW LATI VAN PREMIER On July 11, 1999, the President, Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga, asked that Andris Šķēle, the Chairman of Tautas partija (The Folk party) form the new Latvian Government. In another positive move, the reigning parties – Tautas partija and Tēvzemei un brīvībai (the Folk party and for Fatherland and Freedom) – asked that Latvijas ceļš (the Latvian way) join the government. Šķēle is the sole owner of the largest Latvian food, sweets and liquor producers. He has promised to yield control to another party for the duration of his premiership. Latvian law so far has nothing like a blind trust. As the first act, the new government changed the State budget, eliminating much of income and expense, in order to balance the books more closely. The government also increased excise taxes on alcohol, tobacco, black oil as well as gambling. Since the government coalition has a solid majority in Saeima, it is expected that all these provisions will become law. "GREENS" ATTACK LITHUANIA\'S OFFSHORE PUMPING STATION The Latvian "Greens" delayed the opening of Lithuania\'s offshore oil pumping station at Būtiņģe. They occupied the platform and hancuffed themselves to it, but were later arrested by Lithuanian police. The "Greens" accuse the Latvian government of ignoring the dangers inherent in Būtiņģe. Next day the oil pumping station was duly inaugurated with many speeches, and a tanker was filled, but the Lithuanian police were on the lookout for more protests. UNIVERSAL TESTING METHOD FOR HIGH SHOOL SENIORS Four years ago the Latvia\'s education system instituted a universal testing method for high school seniors. During the four years, media have reported that the tests may be bought at the "Laima" clock (a very common meeting place for all sorts of people) in downtown Rīga for about Ls 20.- (appr. 35.- USD). This year four shools were accused of failing math tests, because 80% of the students answered the same questions wrong. Clearly something is rotten, since the tests were sent to school principals in sealed envelopes. The Public prosecutor\'s office has begun an investigation, and several hundred students, who say that they did not cheat, have begun a class-action suit, and are also complaining to the Citizen\'s Protection Authority. Some 2,500 students are reported to have cheated. SUMMER CAMPS FOR LATVIAN ORPHANS Several hundred orphans will have the opportunity to get some sun and fresh air this summer in camps provided for them. This is part of an effort to get these children off the streets. RUSSIAN POLICY PLAYS HAVOC WITH LATVIA\'S ECONOMY The Russian political attacks against Latvia for misusing the colonists they themselves shlepped into Latvia, and which of course is arrant nonsense, was soon coupled with an embargo against Latvian food imports, especially by the Mayor of Moscow, Yurii Luzhkov. And then the collapse of the Russian economy made it a tripple whammy against Latvia. While there are some Latvian food exporters that have successfully made agreements with some governors of Russian provinces, this is of little help. Heretofore 90% of Latvian food production could be exported to Russia. FISHING INDUSTRY HIT SEVERELY Most severely hit by the embargo is the fishing industry. Many fish canneries have gone out of business, and fish production has dropped to below 50% since 1995. Food producers fault the government for not signing a free trade agreement with Ukraine with whom we have no political problems. The 40 million Ukrainians would certainly make a difference in fish production industry, had a free trade agreement been signed. FOOD PRODUCTS TAINTED Health matters mostly come from the European Union. Chicken, eggs, milk and all sorts of products made from these have come from Belgium, tainted with a class of Chemicals known as the dioxins. At higher concentration, dioxins may be carcenogenic. It appears that Belgian authorities sat on this discovery for a while. Belgian, French and German food products of the kind mentioned were culled from our stores and samples sent to Germany and Finland for dioxin analysis. So far this contaminant has been found in imported beef liver. Right on the heels of the dioxin scare appreared another one, again, originating in Belgium. Coca-Cola drinks made several hundred students ill. The explanations of the company that has manufactured Coke cans for many, many years, were quite unconvincing. Again, Latvia removed Coca-Cola products that were manufactured in France and Belgium from the store shelves, but not those made in Lithuania. What is wrong with those Belgians anyway? EU officials commended the Latvian agencies for their fast and complete work removing the tainted products from the market. AMERICAN MILITARY TO ADVISE LATVIAN DEFENSE FORCES It is reported that Latvian defense authorities have invited a group of American military for the purpose of instructing Latvian defense forces on NATO requirements. The tenure of this group might be from 6 to 12 months, although the Latvian Defense minister Ģirts Valdis Kristovskis has said that the time might be much longer, since not all Latvian officers know what is required of them. LATVIAN WOMAN IN THE AIR FORCE ACADEMY A young Latvian woman, Pfc. Ērika Neimane will begin studies at the US Air Force Academy this fall. SUGAR BEET CROP Latvia is again going to have a bumper crop of sugar beets, but – alas! – 30 thousand tons of sugar of unknown origin have come into the country and are being sold in unmarked plastic bags, which is against the law. This is presently being investigated by the authorities. LATVIAN FARMERS PRODUCE TOO MUCH FOOD FOR LOCAL CONSUMPTION It seems that Latvian farmers produce twice the amount of food that Latvia\'s inhabitants are capable of consuming. Obviously the rest has to be exported, preferably to the vast Russia, quite familiar with Latvian products from years past. However, Russia cannot pay, even though several Russian provinces have entered into agreements with Latvian food producers, which are mutually acceptabe, and by paying in kind. Latvia also exports food to the European Union and other countries, even to the USA, for I myself went to Maier\'s Delicatessen in Chicago and bought Latvian manufactured sprats (šprotes). Latvia\'s excellent beer is also exported to the USA and sold for outlandish prices. Likewise – Latvia\'s champagne. Latvia was the first country to mature this bubbly wine in stainless steel tanks. Several other countries have followed suit. I do not think the French will buy this manufacturing manner. CRAYFISH – LATVIAN AND CAJUN DELICACY It has been reported that in Latvia the crayfish are now going to be grown commercially. A gentleman by the name of Egils Tinte – the Vice-president of the Latvian Crayfish Growers Association – is quite involved in this project. In his ponds he has harvested about 70,000 young crayfish this year. It apparently has begun well, since this Association has been invited to join the World Crayfish Growers Association. SANITARY OFFICIALS CLOSE THE FISH PAVILLION State Sanitary officials descended upon the Fish pavillion of the Great Market halls in Rīga. The air temperature in the pavillion at that taime was 31 °C (88°F). They demand that the temperature be lowered to 10° – 15° C (50° – 59°F) even though to air-condition such a huge edifice may not be an easy proposition. They shut all operations in the pavillion because the freezer temperatures which should have been -15° C (5°F) were found to be on the order of -2,9° C (27°F) – a world of difference for \'The very easily spoilable fish products", as the leader of the State Sanitary group said. MUSIC FESTIVALS IN LATVIA Latvia – like, to be sure are many other lands, – is a land of music festivals. But, since you are reading this, you have some affinity to Latvia, not Edinburgh or Verona. The first is the Ballet Festival, followed by the Rīga Opera Festival in June; a month later is Sigulda Opera Festival, held in the ancient Sigulda castle, which shows the best of Latvian opera singers and the young European singers; Rīga Dome Cathedral Organ festival, as well as the a Chamber music festival in August. In the fall, there is the Baroque festival, which is held in Rastrelli\'s built castle at Rundāle; Jazz festival, Rock festival in the provincial town of Valmiera. You name it, and Latvia probably has it. PĒTERIS VASKS\' 2. SYMPHONY IN LONDON The 2. symphony of the Latvian composer, Pēteris Vasks will be premiered at the Royal Albert Hall in London. This work was commissioned by BBC. It will be broadcast from a recording by the Latvian "Klasika" radio on August 26. SKONTO IN EUROPEAN SOCCER CHAMPIONSHIP The Latvian leading team in soccer started the World Championship first level well, beating Luxemburg\'s Jeunesse Esch 2:0 (0:0) in Luxemburg. The return match was played at the very, very wet Daugava stadium in Riga, and this time Skonto beat the Luxemburgers 8:0 (2:0). Skonto next played Bucarest\'s Rapid and in Bucarest managed a draw – 3:3 (2:1). They won again in Rīga 2:1 (0:1) and then for the third round played against the premier-league team Chelsea of England. Skonto is the first Latvian soccer team to go to a third round. EUROPEAN YOUTH FIELD AND TRACK CHAMPIONSHIP IN RĪGA The European Youth Field and track meet was held in Rīga\'s Daugava Stadium. This is the first time such an event was staged in a former Soviet-block country. AUTO-SPORT TRAGEDY There was a sports related tragedy in the Latvian provincial town of Madona, where two competing cars ran into the crowd, killing five on the spot, two died on their way to the hospital and two are still on the critical list. The blame seems to be with the arrangers of the event. The Latvian Automobile Federation (LAF) has decided not to allow any auto races before all the tracks are inspected and certified. The accident claimed another victim – a 9 year old boy, who never regained consciousness. The two drivers who caused this tragdey left auto sport and one of them is creating a fund to help victims of accidents caused by sporting events. The LAF has barred five of the officials and referees from the sport for life. WWF INTRODUCES WILD HORSES IN LATVIA At a behest of the World Wildlife Fund (WWF) a herd of wild hoses of the genus Konik polski were introduced at Pape in Latvia. For fence building and animal maintenance WWF earmarked $30,000 – this year and again for the next two years. It has been intended to add other animals to the horses, e.g. the blue cow that has stemmed from aurochs that lived in Latvia in earlier centuries. Another species for introduction is the presently living aurochs (in Poland); however, for these animals, that weigh nearly a ton, a large territory and very stout fencing is needed. Latvia is the only European country where alongside grass eaters live predators, such as wolves, foxes and lynx. THE RĪGA CARD IS AVAILABLE Attention all travelers to Rīga! The city now has a Riga Card that features discounts at sundry restaurants, travel on public transportation, including trains, free admittance to museums, e.t.c. The card may be obtained at the airport, Old town, tourism agencies. It will cost Ls 8.- for one day, Ls 12.- for two and Ls 16.-for three days. There are also children\'s cards. THE RĪGA CANAL WILL BE DREDGED The Riga Canal, which meanders so very prettily through the city in its 3,2 km length, is going to be dredged (last time it was done, was in 1932) to a depth of 2 m. Nowadays, the depth in some places is only 70 cm. There are construction debris, car parts and – who knows – perhaps the skeleton of a suicide victim! The city also plans to introduce stabilizing vegetation and to improve the banks as well. LIBERAL PARTY LATVIAN WAY LOSES POWER During the presidential election, the here-to-fore most potent political force, Latvijas ceļš (The Latvian Way) lost much of its power. A three-way consensus by the far-right party Tēvzemei un brīvībai (For Fatherland and Freedom), Tautas partija (Folk Party) and the Social Democratic Workers Party allied themselves to elect a fine President and may play a more formal role in the future. And, indeed, Mr. Krištopans, the Prime minister, resigned on July 5, 1999, accusing his coalition partners of treason. LATVIAN SOCCER TEAM PLAYS BRASIL The Latvian National soccer team was invited to a friendship game against Brasil. The game duly took place in Curitiba. The Brasilian National team won by a score of 3:0 (1:0). According to sports news, this was a demonstration lesson for the very young Latvian team, where some teens were on the team for the first time. The Brasilians, however, considered the Latvian National team as worthy sparring partners and would like to expand relationships. FK VENTSPILS IN THE EUROPEAN INTERTOTO SOCCER MATCHES More successfull was the Latvian soccer team FK Ventspils, which in the European Intertoto matches beat Norwegian Valerenga with a two-game total of 2:1. They next played the Turkish team Kocaelispor to a draw 1:1 (0:0) and lost a game in Turkey 0:2 (0:1). Thus they were eliminated from further games. CHICAGO, ILL: Governor George H. Ryan and Illinois First Lady Lura Lynn Ryan hosted the 1999 Ethnic Media at the James R Thompson Center on July 22. Over 60 ethnic groups attended the reception where Governor Ryan issued a proclamation declaring July 22.1999 as Ethnic Media Day in Illinois. Children representing ethnic communities presented Mrs. Ryan flowers.']

Representative_Docs:
["EU SUITABILITY FOR LATVIAN INSTITUTIONS COSTING 1.2 BLN LATS Latvian institutions will need 1.2 billion lats (SUSI.9 bin) in the next ten years to fully meet European Union (EU) member status, according to calculations made by the Finance Ministry. The Ministry of Finance, by order of the European integration Council, has collected for the first time information on the activities and costs of all 12 government ministries required by Latvia according to the National EU integration program and promises made during EU accession talks. Integration costs this year and in 2002 are expected to be the highest, when the implementation of EU requirements and standards will cost 268 million and 296 million lats respectively. A sharp fall in costs is expected in 2003, which undoubtedly shows the Latvian government's intention of completing as much as possible before the next round of EU expansion in 2004. The largest part of these costs is expected to be covered by the state budget – 646 million lats. Only 168 million lats are expected to be received as financial assistance from the EU and most of this is expected to come from EU structural funds after accession, mainly for environmental projects. 202 million lats are expected to be received as bilateral assistance, including donations and co-financing from other countries. The rest will be obtained from loans. In terms of costs, top of the list is the Ministry of Environmental Protection and Regional Development, planning to spend 800 million lats on putting in order the country's garbage collection industry, ensuring tap-water quality and cleaning of drainage water, as well as the control of air and industrial pollution. Almost half of these funds are expected to be received from the state budget, most of the costs expected to be charged to local governments. Considerable costs will also be required by the Ministry of Welfare for carrying out EU requirements, planning to continue the reform of health care, social security and assistance systems, and to ensure the public with healthy and environmentally friendly food over the next four years. The lowest costs are planned by the Ministry of Transportation, set to increase the control of hazardous cargo and establish a plane-crash incident investigative service. In terms of planned costs at least, the Defence Ministry doesn't seem to be focusing too much attention to the development of the common EU external and security policies, as the ministry plans to spend only 440,000 lats over the next 10 years for establishing a Latvian coastal observation system prepared to link up with similar systems in Estonia and Lithuania. Edited by Vidvuds Koskins", 'Latvia Dsssatisfied with Proposals for EC EU Funds Regulation for 2014-2020

Latvia is dissatisfied with proposals for the European Union (EU) Cohesion Policy (EU funds) regulation for the period 2014-2020 published by the European Commission on Thursday, October 6. The proposals provide principles for setting the amount of financing available to EU Member States and potential areas of support, as well as EU funds management and implementation issues for the period after 2013.

Deputy State Secretary of the Ministry of Finance Aleksandrs Antonovs pointed out: “Latvia is not satisfied with current proposal, because it envisages reduction of the Cohesion Policy or EU funds financing to Latvia. Latvia also believes that the EC proposal does not ensure focused enough Cohesion Policy providing support to limited numberof priorities allovving achieving more visible results.”

Maintaining EU funds financing at least at current amount after 2013 or more is Latvia’s priority in negotiations on future Cohesion Policy and one of its priorities in negotiations on the EU multi-annual budget

for 2014-2020, taking into account that EU funding is the only considerable source of investment for development in Latvia, as well as stili there are notable social and economic differences betvveen EU Member States and reģions.

Latvia wants to improve effectiveness of the Cohesion Policy stating that Member States should report on achievements, rather than the amount of expenditure, thus reducing the expenditure control and focusing on effectiveness and added valued of this policy. For Latvia it is also important to have clear rules for EU funds implementation before drafting national programming documents, in order to avoid a situation when further interpretation of these rules hinders launching of EU funds acquisition on January 1, 2014, without retarding investment inflow in the Latvian economy and facilitating its further development.

“Negotiations on issues related to future Cohesion Policy vvill be difficult, but Latvia vvill not be alone in its position, as was proved by the joint letter sent last week by prime ministers of the Baltie States and Hungary to the European Commission on unfair rules for distribution of Cohesion Policy financing among Member States. The Ministry of Finance is looking for allies among other Member States to ensure that negotiations on the EU multi-annual budget for 2014-2020 bring the most favorable result for Latvia,” emphasized Antonovs.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER GOVERNMENT: 1. Latvia is assured to be invited for joining -m talks with the European Union (EU) in December, alongside with Lithuania, Malta, and Slovakia. Apparently Romania and Bulgaria are in a bit of different situation. The government's feeling is that Latvia will soon catch up with the first group of admitees. We expect to become full EU members by 2005. This is by far the most important story this month. The Polish foreign minister, when recently visiting Latvia, offered his help on how to negotiate with the EU. Latvia will get some serious money from the EU as a country that is on the verge of joining it. Latvia will gain gift funds of about 30 – 35 M Eiro from the ISPA program for arranging the infrastructure and environment; 21.6 M Eiro form the very far-reaching PHARE program funding. Of course, Latvia has to match part of these funds and they have to be well developed. Latvia has submitted a Development plan to the EU. 2. In October the State Central Statistics Bureau started a major study of Latvian households. At the same time this study will be conducted in Estonia and Lithuania. The study will concern itself with the living space, members of households, their education, income, health, occupation, and it will be financed in part by the Norwegian government. 3. The right-wing party For Fatherland and Freedom and the centrist party Folk Party have agreed to call for the publication of the data in the so-called "Cheka sacks", namely – information on Cheka informants, which had been so far impounded by the government. Seems that information on the most vicious chekists has been pulled, and they themselves have fled to Russia. Good riddance! 4. Latvia has joined the UN International Migration Organization. Right now the Latvian government is trying to get 1.2 M dollars for the purpose of assisting Russians living in Latvia to move to Russia. POLITICS: 1. That unnecessary and costly (over 800,000 lats) referendum on the pension law will take place on November 13. DECEMBER 1999 WHAT'S HAPPENING IN LATVIA 2. The leading coalition has decided to increase the year 2000 budget deficit, and not within the EU guidelines. Such items as a helicopter for the armed forces, help for the new and pretty Valmiera theater building (I've been there!), 800,000 lats for that unnecessary referendum, 130,000 lats to pay Parex bank for the Neoplan bus (see October ČZ). 3. The Saeima refused to censure one of their own – physician Saeima deputy Pēteris Apinis, who in a 70 km/hr zone was caught driving 122 km/hr. Only the Folk Party and some deputies of other parties voted for the censure. EDUCATION: 1. During the last four years, the number of students studying Latvian has increased – form 209,947 in 1995/96 to 234,476 in 1998/99. In 1995/96 the number of students that were studying in Russian was 136,740, but in 1998/99 this number dropped to 126,073. Likewise the number of Latvian schools is increasing, but the number of schools that teach either Russian or both languages is dropping. In 1998/99 there were 12 foreign language schools (other than Russian) in Latvia. In several Latvian parishes there are no Latvian schools whatever, mostly in the Eastern part of Latvia. From September 1 of this year, all foreign language schools will start teaching Latvian from the first grade, with the intention that a grade school graduate speaks perfect Latvian. 2. Five young Latvians replete with fur hats and long woolen stockings went to Siberia to teach the Latvian language to Latvians living in the Krasnoyarsk region and in the autonomous Republic of Bashkir. For the coming winter they plan to purchase warm foot-wear and thick jackets. Their way is paid by the PBLA and the Latvian Lutheran Church abroad. ECONOMY: 1. Well over a million of Latvian Gas common stock was rapidly sold at a high price for Latvian stock certificates: 2.11 lats each. 2. Mac Culpa! I made a mistake. The Rīga Bank of Commerce was named The First Bank of Commerce of Latvia, not of Rīga. 3. Iran is dickering to buy the Rīga Coach factory, which at one time built railroad coaches for the entire Soviet Union. These coaches still run in Latvia. It seems that Iran would gut the factory and bring the inventory to Iran. 4. There are signs that Latvia and Ukraine are close to a logical free-trade agreement (see September ČZ). DEFENSE: 1. The first Latvian military units went to Kosovo to maintain that uncertain peace. 2. The President visited Luxembourg, where she met government leaders, including the Great Duke. From there she went to Brussels and conferred with the new North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) Secretary General George Robertson. He assured the President that Latvia, if the defense budget reaches 2% of the GNP, has a good chance to join the NATO. 3. There was a de-mining operation in the waters of Rīga Bay. Nineteen ships from seven countries took part in the operation and recovered 18 live mines near the shores of Vecāki and Saulkrasti. HEALTH: 1. Many of the debilitating illnesses may be treated by the interesting process of Reittherapy, namely on horseback. There is a horse farm for this purpose at the Vaivari Rehabilitation Center near Rīga. There seems to be a lack of blacksmiths in Latvia, however. 2. Authorities are urging Latvia's inhabitants to get immunization for influenza. 3. It seems that men's socks from Slovakia contain traces of substances that irritate the skin and cause other foot disorders. FOOD: That formidable manufacturer of excellent sweets – the confectioner LAIMA, is going to produce its goods in Novgorod, Russian Federation. CULTURE: 1. The Pedvale Museum received this year's UNESCO International prize for maintaining a civilized landscape and for sucessful management. In Pedvale there are frequent exibits and artist plenaries. The prize is named after the famous Greek actress and Culture Minister Melina Mercouri. 2. The Latvian Opera will soon stage Prokoffiev's ballet Romeo and Juliet. 3. Jānis Sermuliņš brought home a gold medal from the Informatics Olympics. Four other boys got the silver. SPORTS: 1. As some of the readers might know, the Latvian national hockey team managed to be demoted from Europe's First Division. Since there is little hope to get anybody from the NHL the fight for promotion will be done by Latvia's European hockey players. They are in a group with the powerful Kazachstan, somewhat powerful Ukraine and Great Britain. The games will be held in Sheffield, England. 2. A scandal in volleyball! It seems that several of our volleyball players went to Finland and Poland with counterfeit transfer certificates. Nobody knows into which pocket the transfer money landed. The Finns do no fool around, and apparently Latvian volleyball players will be sent home... 3. Both the Brocēni – LMT basketball team and the University Sports Female volleyball team will be in the States to play Colleges. Unfortunately not even close to Chicago... In their first US game, Brocēni – LMT beat the University of Miami 92:91. 4. During the ten-year anniversary celebration of the Latvian Sports Museum it is intended to form a Latvian Sports Hall of Fame. The famous Olympics gold-medal winner in javelin Jānis Lūsis will be the first to be inducted. 5. There is now a female hockey team in Liepāja, manned by high school students, that intends to become a farm team for the Latvian female hockey team. NATURE: 1. For the second time in two years, a Latvian woman was honored by the World Women's Summit. It was Zenta Krastiņa, a decorative gardener and florist. 2. The State bird for the year 2000 is going to be the peewit. This pretty bird is in trouble, since many of its favorite nesting places are endangered. It lays eggs in just-plowed fields, which are then cultivated, thus destroying the nests. The birds also like to build nests in wet meadows with hummocks, if not overgrown with bush. Next year ornithologists will also study the bird's distribution in Latvia. 3. The World Bird days, observed also in Latvia, discovered a record number of bird species. The Latvian Ornithologist Society reported that 132 bird species were observed, far surpassing previous years – 108 last year and by 96 the year before that. Altogether 46,555 birds were observed, among them the rare lesser diver and the partridge hen. 4. The Rīga National Zoo is celebrating its 87th year. To commemorate this occasion, the Zoo has built a large wooded pen for the bushy wolf, a native of South America – an endangered animal. It is a gentle animal and subsists on rodents. Next is a tropical roomfull of birds and other species that like hot, damp climate, also orchids and a breeding ground in the Zoo-farm near Kalvene for all sorts of birds of prey. CITIES: 1. 12-year old Sandra Norkute became a hero by saving a 61-year old woman who fell into the swift running Daugava river. Sandra jumped into the chest-high, cold water, grabbed the woman by her clothing and started pulling her ashore. By that time some State Firefighting and Rescue Service men had arrived and pulled both females to safety. The woman who suffered from exposure, was hospitalized. For her heroism Sandra will receive 50 lats, which is a rather huge amount of money for a 12 year old from the Rīga Lithuanian high school. She will spend it on – guess what? Clothing, of course. 2. Parking in downtown Rīga is getting to be expensive. Along Basteja Boulevard the going price for parking will be 2 lats or $3.50 an hour. The price decreases as one gets further from center of the town. CRIME: 1. The major criminal authority Jānis Kļava (nicknamed Kops) finally was sent to jail for running down a cyclist with his Lincoln Town car with the license plate Lucky. He was also mildly drunk. A chase through Rīga stopped him in front of Hotel de Rome. 2. A Jelgava policeman, who first celebrated his superior's birthday, then went to a liquor store, bought a bottle of brandy, polished that off on the spot, bought a bottle of vodka, did likewise, then went to a bar, drank a couple of beers, and started shooting. He shot two people dead, wounded others, rambled home, asked that his girl friend to help him flee, but passed out. His name is Dairis Filipovs. In court he said, that he accepts the indictment, but cannot remember any of it. Small wonder. He is looking at a lengthy incarceration. 3. I am presently reading a book – Narimas hronikas (The Chronicles of Narima) which depicts the unspeakably horrible conditions that deported Latvians, Lithuanians, Estonians found in Siberia. My problem is that this happened in the middle of the 20th Century, not in the 16th, when such things were commonplace! 4. By now eight old men have been indicted on charges of genocide against the Latvian nation. Three of them are native Latvians.']
