Representative_Docs: ['Latvia grants EUR 318 000 in foreign aid in last five years Latvia has granted over LVL 215 000 (EUR 318 500) in foreign aid over the last five years, including LVL 20 000 that were allocated this year to Iran to help it deal with consequences of the devastating earthquake. In 2003, Latvia granted LVL 55 000 to Iraq as humanitarian aid and contribution to the renewal of the war-ravaged country. In December 2002, Latvia also helped Spain to deal with the consequences of the environmental disaster caused by the tanker Prestige, sending technical assistance worth nearly LVT 18 000. In August 2002, Latvia sent medicine in the amount of LVL 11 500 to the Czech Republic to help its population in the aftermath of major floods. Latvia has also helped the population and refugees in Afghanistan by sending about LVL 9 000 worth of blankets and other humanitarian aid in November 2001. Nearly LVL 7 000 in medical goods were sent to Ukraine in April 2000 in the wake of floods. Latvia has also helped victims of a natural disaster in Romania, sending over 26,000 LVL worth of medical goods in August 2000. Earthquake victims in Turkey in August 1999 received from Latvia humanitarian aid worth USD 19 000, which were allocated from the contingency funds. In April 1999 Latvia also sent USD 100 000 worth of blankets to Macedonia to help Albanian refugees who had fled Kosovo.', 'Senate proposes $25 million in foreign aid – House kills it during all-night committee meeting. It was Oct. 26, the last day of negotiations between House and Senate over the 1991 Foreign Aid Bill. It was 5:00 AM, the last hour of a heated all-night session in the Foreign Operations Appropriations conference committee. It was the last chance for the U.S. Congress to approve a Senate recommendation that $15 million in AID grants be provided to the Baltic States. It looked like a lock. Senator Robert Byrd (D-WV) had introduced and fought for the Baltic aid. The entire Senate Foreign Relations committee had backed it. All it needed was approval from the House members of conference committee. The approval never came. The House members rejected the Baltic amendment, and went home to run for re-election. Actually, two Senate amendments for aid to the Baltic States had been offered to the joint House/Senate conference committee. Originally Sen. Alfonse D\'Amato (R-NY) had introduced an amendement calling for $10 million in humanitarian aid for the Baltic States, while Sen. Byrd asked for an additional $15 million in economic assistance. Since there had been no Baltic aid amendments originating from the House side, the D\'Amato amendment was rejected out of hand by conference committeee chairman Cong. David Obey (D-WI). This was initially viewed as a House/Senate compromise, since the Senate had offered two Baltic bills while the House had none. It was thought this would guarantee that at least the $15 million Byrd amendment would go through. Yet even this second proposal was further compromised when some committee members complained that U.S. aid to the Baltic States would appear to support \'secessionist\' movements in the Soviet Union. Thus, an agreement was reached to split the $15 million: $7.5 million would go to the Baltics, and $7.5. to support democracy and privatization in other Soviet republics. This way it would not appear that the Congress was singling out the Baits. Ironically, this compromise was also rejected, because other committee members did not want to give aid to the Soviet Union. Among those who opposed the compromise were Cong. Charlie Wilson (D-TX), a major supporter of the Afghan freedom fighters, Cong. Robert Mrazek (D-NY) and Cong. Matt McHugh (D-NY). CONGRESS REJECTS FOREIGN AID TO BALTICS While all the details of the committee debates and dealings are not known, observers of the comittee proceedings feel that Cong. David Obey directly, and Cong. Lee Hamilton (D-IN) indirectly, worked to prevent the Baltic aid package. Obey sprang the compromise proposal on the committee without forewarning, perhaps knowing that conservatives would reject any amendement that included aid to the Soviet Union. Hamilton, while not a committee member, has a great deal of influence on House Democrats, and reportedly argued against any foreign aid that would appear to destablize Gorbachev and his Soviet government. By combining Baltic and Soviet aid in one package, conservatives and liberals gave a 1-2 knockout punch to Baltic aid. Aid to the Baltics was bad because it would weaken the Soviet Union. Aid to other republics was bad because it would strengthen the Soviet Union. The solution: no aid for anyone. "I HAVE NOTHING FOR YOU MY DEARS"', 'FROM RFE/RL DAILY REPORT $ US SEMTE LINKS 3USSIAN AID TO BALTIC TROOP WITHDRAWAL. On July 1 the US Sen.ite voted 92 to 2 to impose restrictions on aid to Russia after a year unless made significant progress toward withdrawing its troops from the Baltic States, Reuters reports. By a vote of 60 to 35, the Senate defeated a stronger alternative bill that would have imposed the restrictions immediately, without the 12 month grace period. The bill authorized $ 620 million ( $ ISO million for 1992 and $ 470 million for 1993 ) for economic aid to Russia plus $ 18 million in State Department ezpenses and $ 6.8 million for US Information Agency programs. The bill also included $ 400 million aid for 1992 and $ 450 million for 1993 to Eastern Europe nations, including the Baltic States. The Senate also approved authorization of a $ 12 billion contribution to the IMF that will be part of its $ 24 billion aid program to Russia ( Saulius Cirnius ). * LATVIAN, RUSSIAN EXPERTS DISCUSS TROOP WITHDRAWAL. At their third meeting, which began in Jūrmala on June 30, Latvian and Russian experts continued discussions on the withdrawal of ex-USSR troops from Latvia. Radio Riga reported on July 1. Latvia\'s deputy defense minister Dainis Turlajs said Russia was disregarding previous accords; he noted that in the February 1 communique, signed by representatives of both countries, Russia had agreed to discuss the withdrawal of all ex-USSR troops, adding that now Russian experts insist the communique had only mentioned the pullout of some troops. Latvian group member Juris Dobelis said that the Russian side had, however, presented a list of 84 military objects in Latvia that the military could vacate soon and said that this would be compared with a similar list compiled by the Latvians. Dobelis said that Latvia wants to take advantage of any positive steps made by the Russians ( Dzintra Bungs ). § 450 PEOPLE TURNED BACK AT ESTONIAN BORDER. Some 450 people were refused entrance into Estonia at the Narva border with Russia on July 1 because they did not have Estonian visas. BNS reports that four train loads of people wishing to enter Estonia were checked for documents at the Narva rails station. Estonia began requiring visas of former Soviet citizens on July 1. All otherforeigners have been subject to a visa requirement since last October. After Estonia\'s currency reform, the cost of a single-entry visa jumped to $ 60 ( RUna Kionka ). * POLISH-LATVIAN TREATY OF COOPERATION SIGNED. While visiting Riga, Polish Foreign Minister Kryzaztof Skubiaszewski signed a treaty of cooperation between Poland and Latvia – the first such treaty to be signed with one of the Baltic States – and accords on trade, travel and legal protection for citizens of the two countries. Skubiszewski told the press that he was pleased with the development of Latvian-Polish relations. He praised in particular Latvia\'s generous policies toward its many national minorities. While in Riga, the Polish foreign minister also met with representatives of the approximately 60,000 Poles living in Latvia, Radio Riga reported on July 1 ( Dzintra Bungs ). * LATVIAN FILM DIRECTOR JURIS PODNIEKS FOUND DEAD. Radio Riga and Reuters reported on July 1 that earlier that day the body of Juris Podnieks had been found in Zvirgzdu lake, in the vicinity of Kuldiga. Podnieks disappeared on June 23 while participating in a fishing and underwater exploring expedition of Latvian lakes. Although the cause of death was not announced, initial findings suggest that he probably died as a consequence of an accident. Podnieks gained international acclaim with his film "Is It Easy to Be Young?"; the film, a searing indictment of the Soviet system, consists of interviews with young people who speak openly about their disillusionment and their indifference toward the future. In Latvia Podnieks was best known for his documentary films, especially those chronicling the path toward the restoration of Latvia\'s independence ( Dzintra Bungs ). # SWEDEN TO CEOMPENSAIE ESTONIA, LITHUANIA FOR THEIR GOLD. Sweden has decided to compensate Estonia and Lithuania for gold that the Swedish government had transferred to the USSR in 1940, after the Soviet Union occupied the Baltic States. The gold, now worth about 46,6 million dollars, had been deposited in Sweden before World War II by Estonia and Lithuania. The Swedish government statement of July 1 said that the two countries can decide if they want repayment in gold or in cash and indicated that Sweden would like to complete the transactions as soon as possible. Estonian and Lithuanian officials indicated that they would like to have the gold back, Western agencies reported on July 1 ( Dzintra Bungs ). THE MONTREAL BALTIC STAMP CLUB held its Exhibition "BALT-EX-111" on May 23 & 24, 1992 at the Montreal Estonian St. John\'s Lutheran Church hall, Marcil Avenue, NDG. It featured the new stamps of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. The visitors judged Dr. R. Sers study of the Latvia\'s Nr. 1 as the best in the show. M. Stauvers\' "Latvian Postal Saga 1990-1992" was a close second ( by one ballot ) and by far the most extensive ( 75 pages ).']

Representative_Docs: ['Banking Crisis Grinds On, Leads To Much-Needed Reform The governments of Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania are tightening their nation\'s banking laws after the catastrophic series of bank failures in Latvia sent the country reeling into a financial crisis. The longoverdue reform is one — perhaps the only — positive result of Latvia\'s banking debacle, according to an editorial in the August 10-16 issue of The Baltic Observer. The failure last May of Banka Baltija, the largest commercial bank in the Baltics, rocked Latvia and sent shock waves throughout the region. About 20,000 businesses and half a million depositors lost their money. The bank\'s losses are estimated at $400 million. Two of Banka Baltija\'s directors have been arrested on charges of fraud. The Latvian parliament rammed through tough banking regulations in late June, which the prime minister, Māns Gailis, hailed as providing "a stable basis for structuring the banking system." The regulations will establish controls on commercial banks, including urgently needed bankruptcy rules. The laws also prepare the way for getting depositors of Banka Baltija some of their money back, in a country that lacks any deposit insurance (another item in the legislation). Expected cost of the cleanup: 30.4 million lats/$60.8 million. The legislative reforms may be too late to help the thousands of depositors and businesses which lost their money. But it is, as the saying goes, better late than never. ♦', 'Baltic Banking Crises Rumble On; Lithuania, Lavents Dominate News Banking instability finally hit the third Baltic state, Lithuania. After suffering the closure of several smaller banks and beginning to feel like the worst may be over, the Lithuanian central bank suspended operations at Lithuania\'s two largest commercial banks, Innovation Bank and Litimpex. The two together hold one quarter of the country\'s total bank deposits, according to an article in the latest issue of The Economist (96.20.1). The article linked the banking crises that have swept each of the Baltic states now since 1992 with a series of causes, ranging from inexperience on the part of new bankers, overly lax regulation (especially in Latvia), and the fact that many of the new commercial banks simply lived beyond their means. Many banks were set up as a mechanism to provide cheap insider financing for large companies; others offered unsustainably high interest rates in an effort to lure new depositors. Some, like Latvia\'s Banka Baltija and its boss, Aleksandrs Lavents, did both – fostering the collapse of about 40% of Latvia\'s banking industry last summer. The Economist does not paint an absolutely bleak picture of banking Baltics style: the newspaper cites Estonia, the first to sustain a banking collapse in 1992, with reforms so successful that banks there "are now arguably the soundest of any ex-communist country." Latvia and, belatedly, Lithuania are hurrying to join their northern neighbor in toughening bank capital requirements and other banking regulations, while banks in each country engage in badly needed consolidation. Baltija Failure Lingers Speculation and cheap political theatrics continue, however, to dog the worst offender in Latvia\'s banking collapse, Banka Baltija. Months after the Bank of Latvia and the government of former Prime Minister Māris Gailis took over the failed bank, jailed Oversight Council Chairman and factual bank boss Aleksandrs Lavents continues to find politicians willing to make accusations that the man who single-handedly wiped out the savings of tens of thousands of Latvian citizens is being held behind bars simply because he is a Jew (Siegerist Party leader Odisejs Kostanda). Popular Harmony Party member Mavriks Vulfsons echoed this accusation, and presented a Lavents-offered plan to bring Baltija out of bankruptcy that would only serve, according to the Bank of Latvia, to further increase the failed bank\'s debt load while cheating depositors out of any chance to receive any of their funds. The Bank of Latvia, in a statement released January 22., called on Lavents instead to return the nearly 100 million dollars currently estimated to have been transferred from Baltija accounts to his personal holding company, in order to reimburse creditors and depositors. The Bank feels that the bottom line of the various plans offered by Lavents and his supporters to bring Baltija out of deserved bankruptcy are nothing more than ploys to get Lavents out of jail. On a related matter, the new government of Prime Minister Andris šķēle has decided to forgo continuation of compensation payments to Baltija depositors until additional funds are recovered by the bank\'s appointed administrator, Uģis Grūbe. In fact, because state budget funds were used to begin the payments (just before the October Saeima elections), accusations are now flying that former Prime Minister Gailis used the payments as a political ploy to regain voter confidence, and votes, for his party, Latvia\'s Way. The net result: 17 seats in the 6th Saeima for Latvia\'s Way and one million lats of borrowing from the state budget to compensate 6,000 Baltija depositors. The banking situation may be stabilizing, but the revelations are only beginning. Stay tuned. #', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER Was Banka Baltija Blown Up With Skrunda "Monster"? May A, 1995 was a day for celebration in Latvia, with the spectacular demolition on Latvia\'s fifth anniversary of renewed independence of one of the most infamous symbols of the Soviet military occupation, the unfinished long range passive array radar station at Skrunda. However, recent reports in Moscow and Latvia have led to speculation that the final destruction of the Skrunda "monster", as the radar was "affectionately" known, may have played an indirect role in the downfall of Latvia\'s largest commercial bank, Banka Baltija. Banking Big, Banka Baltija Biggest Latvia\'s economic rebirth has been largely based, so far, on phenomenal growth in the financial services sector. As part of its drive for independence, Latvia claimed the role of the former Soviet Union\'s banking center—rivaling, in ambition at least, the bankers of Switzerland. More than sixty banks opened throughout Latvia, each competing with the others for deposits, customers and a share of the huge amounts of capital flowing out of Russia. Several have either been declared bankrupt, or come perilously close to bankruptcy, over the past half-year—among them Tautas Banka, Centra Banka, Latvijas Depozītu Banka and, most spectacularly, Lainbanka, which barely survived a run on its deposits following the arrest of senior bank officials on fraud charges that were later dropped. However, Latvia\'s biggest bank was, perhaps, also its most vulnerable—Banka Baltija. Built by Chairman of the Board Aleksandrs Lavents and President Tālis Freimanis, Banka Baltija was the largest commercial bank in Latvia, with 37 branches throughout the country. Banka Baltija controlled assets in excess of 2\\l million lats—nearly three times the assets of the next bank, the Latvian Universal Bank. As of April 1, more than 196 million lats were on deposit. More than 500,000 residents—20 percent of the total population of Latvia— had an account at Banka Baltija, drawn by the extremely high interest rates on deposits offered by the bank. Depositors included such prominent politicians as Prime Minister Māris Gailis, former Finance Minister Andris Piebalgs and Saeima chairman Anatolijs Gorbunovs, who had "hoped to get my 50 percent on May 20." In order to support the high interest payouts, Banka Baltija aggressively pursued loan business, managing just less than one third of the total volume of loans distributed by all of Latvia\'s commercial banks combined. Banka Baltija took on a number of risky, but lucrative loans, which often caused borrowers to default and become dependent on the bank for their survival. State stock companies such as the electric monopoly Latvenergo, the natural gas monopoly Latvijas gāze, Latvian Railways, July/August 1995 — Vol. XV, No. 6 Latvian Airlines and the Dautex chemical fiber plant in Daugavpils, Riga Central Market owner Turība, and the popular newspaper Labrīt have all suffered as a result of loan defaults to Banka Baltija, By this spring, rumors were circulating in the halls of the Saeima and government about cash flow problems at Baltija—but no one was prepared for the implosion of the banking colossus. Baltija Collapses, Officials Gut Bank The first visible signs of trouble at Baltija began to appear in mid-May. Following the murder of a minor stockholder in the bank, Moisejs Gurēvičs, bank officials began to make engage in an unusual series of interviews and public relations efforts. On May 15, Diena columnist Aivars Ozoliņš speculated as to whether the interviews were an indication of the involvement of Baltija officials in Gurēvičs\' murder, and noted an unconfirmed report in the Russian-language paper SM-Segodnja that Bank of Latvia President Einārs Repše and then-Finance Minister Andris Piebalgs had been asked by Baltija officials for an 80 million lats in financial aid. The request quickly became, by May 20, a deal in which the Latvian government would bail out the bank in return for 51% of Banka Baltija stock and total control of all bank operations. Latvian American Uldis Klauss, a consultant with the Bank of Latvia, was appointed the bank\'s new chief executive. On May 22, having spent the weekend examining the bank\'s financial reports, Klauss shut down the bank, and Repše suspended all banking operations. By May 23, the government bailout had become a full-scale takeover, in order to give the government unquestioned authority to restructure Baltija. The next week were spent exposing illegal transactions by bank officials, which emptied the bank\'s coffers before the bank was taken over by the Latvian government. Lavents and Freimanis became the subjects of an investigation into the malicious destruction of their business on June 1, based on the sale of 80 million lats of Baltija loans to a small bank in Moscow in exchange for Russian state treasury bills with a current market value of only 24 million lats. Other deals were completed either just before or after the government agreed to bail our Baltija: the bank mortgaged all of its physical, real and intellectual property as a guarantee to secure nearly 45 million lats in long-term deposits from the state shipping monopoly Latvian Shipping Company. Loans worth 6.3 million lats were sold to Pareks banka, while another 5 9 million lats in loans were sold to the Riga Oil And Chemical Bank. By June 5, it was clear that losses at Banka Baltija were at least in the range of 115-196 million lats. Klauss termed the situation at the bank "a pit that keeps growing deeper and deeper". Repše called the actions of the Banka Baltija officials deliberate sabotage for which, if convicted in the United States, Lavents and Freimanis "would never get out of prison... They openly lied about the bank\'s shortterm problems while, at the same time, signing agreements which emptied the bank. They managed to deceive regulatory agencies and international audit companies. It is villainy and sabotage against the Latvian state." Was Russia Involved? Several sources point to the involvement in the Banka Baltija crisis of "not just the first/second person at Baltija [Lavents and Freimanis—MJZ) but also of third, fourth and even fifth parties." The daily Monitor, published by The Jamestown Foundation, reported on May 30 that massive withdrawals of Russian funds from Latvia led to the collapse of the Baltija Bank. According to a report on May 23 in the Moscow paper Delovoy mir, Russian depositors were angry about Latvia\'s destruction of part of the Skrunda radar site and what it said were Riga\'s anti-Russian policies. The fact that the funds were drawn out all at once suggests that there might have been some coordination by the Russian government itself. When asked about the possibility of a "Russian Scenario", several commentators in Latvia were skeptical. However, high-level officials within the Bank of Latvia are well aware of how real such a scenario is, in light of Russia\'s oft-expressed willingness to use economic means to destabilize the Baltic countries. "It looks like Baltija has been robbed bare. Literally... The total emptying [of bank assets) took place just recently. Estimated losses are nearing 200 million lats. It is not possible that something like this could be done by accident or stupidity. Either a well organized mafia is involved, or the Russian secret services (if they aren\'t one and the same). Everything has been carried out in a cold-blooded and thorough manner..." The unnamed high-ranking Bank of Latvia official stated that the fate of Lavents and Freimanis will serve as a litmus test for the involvement of other parties—"lf we are able to arrest and convict them, then it is very possible that they are simply criminals. If they flee, are vindicated or are removed\', then it is likely that [securityl services are involved." The official agreed that "there is very good reason" to suspect the involvement of Moscow in the downfall of Banka Baltija. The Latvian government has set June 26 as its date for developing a plan to halt the crisis that exploded in its financial system in the wake of the Banka Baltija collapse. Bankruptcy proceedings have begun against the bank in Latvian Commercial Court. In the meantime, the Baltija crisis has brought local government activities to a standstill, threatened pension payouts and caused the respected Financial Times to comment that the "Latvian Zurich" has now become a banking Las Vegas. The government is trying to restore normalcy to the once-thriving Latvian financial sector. But the question remains—is Latvia\'s economic security buried in the rubble of the Skrunda early warning radar station? *MJZ']

Representative_Docs: ['# fho Sun\'s European bureau chief Paul Stanway and photographer m McKenna have Ju§t returned from a three-week assignment In ffļs Soviet Union where they wttnetted the Incredible changes In the crumbling empire. They observed first-hand the opsn contempt for the past regime backed with a determination by tot republics to move In a democratic direction without any Interference from the ghosts of the old guard. In this fifth of she parts, Stanway examines Latvia. ■■■RAlUjl! STAN WAY RIGA, Latvia – The Baltic countries may be independent but there remain more than 300,000 Soviet troops in the region, and they seem in no hurry to leave. During the attempted coup in Moscow units of the notorious Soviet Interior Ministry troops, the Black Berets, stormed through the centre of the Latvian capital seizing government buildings, television and radio stations and killing three people. In the days since most have deserted or quietly left for home, but the regular army remains – surrounded by a resentful population and trying to keep a low profile Thai\'s not easy. There are more than 25 Soviet army camps in the Riga region and although none appears on any civilian map they\'re not hard to find. "The woods are full of Russians," one helpful lady informs us. "Just look and you\'ll find them." And indeed we do. Just 10 km outside Riga, buried in the forest and surrounded with barbed wire, we happen on the Ardazi Infantry Training Centre. The three soldiers at the gate don\'t know quite what to make of two Canadian journalists who show up in a cab and ask for a tour. Only days before they would surely have ordered us away at gunpoint, but the confusion of changing times seems to have percolated down through the ranks and a phone call is made to the guard commander. A few minutes later a burly paratrooper, Col. Alexei Palenko, arrives with a smile on his face to ask what we want. He thinks it\'s a huge joke that we want to visit the base but he sends for another officer to give us a guided tour while he impresses us with his knowledge of the Canadian military. Lt. Col. Mikhail Kirilov arrives at the gate and is told to escort us around and show us whatever we want to see. "Everything?" he asks. "Why not?" says a beaming Palenko. Over the next couple of hours we\'re shown around the camp facilities and driven out to see a unit practicing a lake crossing with infantry fighting vehicles, and another unit training with the same vehicles over an assault course. There\'s no attempt to keep anything from us and everyone is smiling and relaxed. "Why not?" asks a veteran infantry major with two gold teeth who\'s supervising the vehicle training. "The old times are gone We\'re all friends now." What about the coup? Were they on alert? "We were," says Kirilov, "but we did not leave camp. We waited to see what would happen." Did he approve of the coup? "I am not a politician," he says diplomatically. "I am a soldier. I follow orders." As we tour a half empty barracks I ask him about the large number of empty The Baltics may be free but more than 300,000 Soviet troops are still in the region, and many want to make their home there beds. "This is a problem," he answers. "Many conscripts are refusing to serve now." There are only 43 men in this particular communal barrack room when it should house 80. There are very few Baits left on the base and those that are still here will be leaving by November. But what about the rest of them? When will the base close and troops leave Latvia? "I have heard of no such plans," says Kirilov. "We have no orders to go anywhere, so we stay." The conscripts are subdued with officers around but one, from Tashkent, tells me he\'s not anxious to go home. "I don\'t know about the future," he says, "but right now I have this security. We really don\'t know what\'s happening back home. It\'s confusing." Kirilov is from Omsk in Siberia and like everyone else seems in no hurry to go home. "There will be great changes in the Soviet forces," he says, "but I have been in the army for more than 20 years and I\'m not too worried." "It\'s not bad with the local people. Not bad at all. And from what I hear our living conditions are much better than at home. We will not go hungry." There have been reports in local papers that some Soviet officers have offered their services to the new armed forces being set up by the Baltic states, but Kirilov smiles and says he knows nothing of such things. "If I stay here it will not be as a soldier. I think this would be a good place to live." Not all Russians in Latvia are so sure. After the Soviet occupation tens of thousands of Latvians were killed in fighting and hundreds of thousands more * TOMORROW: Estonia photos by transported to other areas of the Soviet empire. Russians were shipped in to replace them and man the new Soviet factories. As a result one in three Latvians citizens today is of Russian origin and many are concerned about independence. At Riga Railway Carriage Works No. 1 most of the workforce is Russian. Many have marched in protest against Latvian independence and some actively supported the coup attempt. Like 43-year old office worker Antoniņa Perutchikova, they worry about anti-Russian sentiment and about their jobs — previously guaranteed for life. "Yes, I was in favor of the coup," she tells us tearfully. "I am frightened by the changes. I don\'t want them. My grandparents came here and this is my country, but I want it to be part of the Soviet U.iion." "But I don\'t know what is going to happen. If the coup had succeeded we could have gone back to the old ways and perhaps everything would have been okay. Now I don\'t know. I am frightened." Not all Russians in Latvia feel that way. Engineer Andrew Oleinik thinks the changes are wonderful. "I came here as a teenager in 1944 and this is my country now," he says. "I will become a Latvian citizen as soon as I can. The changes have come too late for me but my children have grown up here and they have a better life now." His family was forcibly resettled in Riga after his father was jailed for 10 years for some petty political crime. Two older sisters who remained in Russia disappeared after the war and are LATVIA The Toronto Sun, Thursday September 26. 1991 presumably dead. "The Communist Party has caused my family great pain and I have no love for them," he explains. "The sooner we forget their philosophy and their divisions the better for us." That may not be easy. The Latvian parliament, dominated by the nationalist Popular Front, will soon debate a new citizenship law and there is support for restricting automatic citizenship only to those Russians resident before 1940 – the year Stalin annexed the country. The Popular Front argues that granting universal citizenship could create chaos without an accurate census, with hundreds of thousands of Russians swamping the country in search of the legendary Baltic good lire. With Latvians already a minority in their own capital city, it\'s an argument which attracts a lot of support. Latvians are endowed with the passivity and easy going nature of their fellow Scandinavians and there\'s a noticeable lack of tension on the streets of Riga with none of the outward demonstrations of nationalism favored by the more passionate Lithuanians. But it doesn\'t help relations between Russians and Latvians that when angry nationalists pulled down the giant statue of Lenin in Riga city centre after the failed coup in Moscow, Russians placed flowers on the fallen idol. And as the lady said, the woods are still full of Soviet troops. The Red Army remains an army of occupation, and one which seems in no hurry to pack up and go home. * SOVIET troops, above, practise amphibious landings just outside Riga. Soviet descendant Antoniņa Perutchikova, left, who lives In Ļatvia, wipes a tear from her eye after a conversation with Sun reporter.', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER January 20, 1991 – As we go to press, over a dozen people have died in Lithuania, barricades are up in Riga, and all three Baltic States are bracing for an imminent assault on their people and governments by the Soviet armed forces. With new developments every day and every hour, it is difficult to prepare a newsletter that will only be read weeks later. The following issue will try to provide some background and additional information about events in Latvia during the first part of January. Late last year, Latvia\'s Foreign Minister Janis Jurkans predicted that 1991 would be the year of the Baltic States. In spite of the tragic events that have already occurred in the Baltic States, or perhaps because of them, it can only be hoped that 1991 will also be the last year of the Soviet Union... A new year, new fears The dress for rehearsal for Lithuania\'s Bloody Sunday (Jan. 13) took place in Latvia on January 2, when Black Berets used force to seize the Latvian Press House in Riga. While there were no fatalities, force FEBRUARY 1991 Vol. XI No. 2 BLOODSHED IN THE BALTICS was used and some shots were fired. The shooting occured on Vansu Bridge, and was directed at a vehicle belonging to the Latvian Interior Ministry. On January 7, Soviet Defense Minister Yazov announced that paratroopers would be deployed to the Baltic States and other repubics, in order to round up individuals that hadn\'t reported for the draft. After discussions with the Latvian and Estonian governments, the deadline for the paratroopers was set for January 13. Troops begin arriving in Lithuania immediately. On January 10 Soviet President Mikhail Gorbachev gave an ultimatum to the government of Lithuania, demanding that it repeal all "anti-Soviet" laws and abide by the Soviet constitution. An estimated 10,000 anti-independence demonstrators rallied in Riga. On the following day, Soviet troops moved into Vilnius. The first gunshots were fired, the first buildings were seized, the first victims fell. In the early morning of January 13, the assault of the Lithuanian TV tower in Vilnius began. As the casualty count in Lithuania rose, Latvia\'s Vice President Dainis Ivans went on national TV with an appeal to the populace to come to Riga to defend the Latvian government. On Janury 13, over 700,000 people gathered on the banks of the Daugava River in Riga, in a demonstration of support for the Latvian Government. No incidents were reported and no paratroopers were seen. By Monday, hundreds of trucks, buses, snow plows and other vehicles had been brought to Riga from around the country in order to protect the government buildings. Tens of thousands of Latvians took up a 24-hour vigil on the barricades, prepared to resist any assault. On Tuesday a coalition of Communist Party members, Soviet military personnel and other Moscow loyalists – held another anti-government rally in Riga. This time, only 8,000 participated. Later than day, Soviet troops attacked and seized the Latvian Military Academy in Riga, seizing weapons and beating some of the students. On January 16 the first Latvian casualty was confirmed. Roberts Mūrnieks, a driver for the Latvian Ministry of Transportation, was shot in the back of the head and killed by Black Berets. That same day, Andrejs Krastins, Vice President of the Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia stated, "The Latvian Government wants the world to known that Gorbachev is personally responsible for orchestrating this manipulated chaos." An appeal for help On January 14, the government of the Republic of Latvia issued the following appeal: "The events in the Baltic States are entering a tragic and decisive stage. Ignoring all legal and moral rules and norms, the reactionary circles of the Communist Party of the USSR and Soviet Armed Forces are preparing a murderous coup d\'etat and liquidation of existing constitutional systems of the Baltic States. From the information available to the Government of the Republic of Latvia, it follows that the coup in the Baltic States can take place in the nearest 24 to 48 hours. Armed troops under the central administration of the USSR have started to disarm the militia of the Republic of Latvia, thus making the government of the Republic unable to maintain order and exercise control over gangs of hooligans which are being formed by the adversaries of the legal government. Irrespective of the fact that support for the Government of Latvia was expressed by over one fourth of the population who took part in the manifestation on January 13 in Riga, and in written form, by over one million people of various nationalities, the aggressive forces of the Communist Party are ready to restore totalitarian dictatorship at any cost. The actions to be taken are bound to produce violence, even bloodshed, a new enslavement of the nation. The threat of danger is corroborated by the present policy of the leaders of the USSR. Besides, the bulk of mass media in the USSR spread deliberate niisinformation about processes taking place in the Baltic States, thus deluding society in the USSR and in the world. At this severe hour we are addressing all the national and governments of the world with an appeal not to allow another Kuwait to happen, with an appeal to prevent murderous international crime in the Baltics. We ask all the governments of the world who praise values common to all mankind, who praise ideas of humanism and democracy to oppose unanimously the reactionary forces and support the rights of Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia to survival and restoration of their statehood in a democratic way." The Government of the Republic of Latvia, January 14, 1991. How the world reacted If Gorbachev had calculated that the world\'s preoccupation with the Gulf crisis would give him a free hand in the Baltic States, he was greatly mistaken. While no concrete action was immediately taken to halt the attacks on the Baltic governments, there was no shortage of condemnations and threats to cut off economic assistance. On January 11, the European Economic Community (EC) expressed its first concerns and urged Gorbachev to begin negotiations with the Baltic States. NATO held an emergency meeting and issued a similar expression of deep concern. The United Kingdom\'s Foreign Office summoned the Soviet Charge d\'Affaires and told him there was no justification for the Soviet action. Poland\'s government and populace expressed strong support for neighbouring Lithuania. The Polish parliament denounced the Soviet action and large anti-Soviet demonstrations were held in Warsaw and Krakow. Denmark issued a stern warning to the USSR and the Canadian Government expressed strong opposition to the use of military in the Baltic States. Prime Minister Mulroney expressed "the abhorrence of all Canadians at the unwarranted violence used against the people and democratically elected Government of Lithuania. Sweden backed its words of concern with a concrete expression of support for the Baltic States when it allocated 1 million Kroner to cover the expenses of Baltic government officials visiting Stockholm. This money paid for a trip to the United States by Latvian and Lithuanian vice presidents, Dainis Ivans and Bronius Kuzmickas. The United States weighs in The United States also made its feelings known in no uncertain terms. Sen. Bradley (D-NY) introduced, and the Senate unanimously passed, a Sense of the Senate Resolution in which it called upon President Gorbachev "to refrain from further use of coercive tactics against the democratically elected governments of Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia. Such coercive tactics are unacceptable among the community of democratic nations and especially so at a time when the world, including the Soviet Union itself, is united in opposition to the forcible annexation of another small nation, Kuwait, by its brutal neighbour, Iraq." It should be noted that the Senate interrupted its debate on Iraq at 2AM in order to pass the resolution. On January 13, both President Bush and Secretary Baker made statements on the Baltic situation. "I have been following the situation in Lithuania and the Baltic States closely. The turn of events there is deeply disturbing. There is no justification for the use of force against peaceful and democratically elected governments. And the brave people and the leaders of the Baltic States have, indeed, acted with dignity and restraint." said President Bush, adding, "We condemn these acts which could not help but effect our relationship." The President came close to what could be considered recognition of the Baltic States when he said, "I urge the Soviet Government to return to a peaceful course of negotiations and dialogue with the legitimate governments of the Baltic States." Speaking from Turkey, where he was preparing for the allied action against Iraq, Secretary of State James Baker said that he was "deeply disturbed and saddened by these developments", reaffirmed that "the United States has never recognized the forcible incorporation of the Baltic States into the Soviet Union" and offered this warning to Moscow: "Only with continued reform will progress and hope for the Soviet Union be possible and in the end, enduring U.S.-Soviet cooperation, indeed partnership, depends upon continued reform -- for partnership is impossible in the absence of shared values." To monitor the rapidly developing events, the State Department set up a special Baltic Working Group, which was manned around the clock and headed by Paul Goble, the State Department\'s Desk Officer for Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. Goble, who put in an average of 18-20 hour work days, worked closely with the American Latvian Association, Joint Baltic American National Committee, Lithuanian organizations and the Lithuanian, Latvian and Estonian Legations. On Monday, Jan. 14, the Charges d\'Affaires of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania met with numerous State Department officials, including Ray Seitz, Assistant Secretary for European and Canadian Affairs. The empire acts up There was also a strong response from within the Soviet Union as well. Demonstrations in support of Lithuania were held in Moscow, Leningrad and Kiev. Independent trade unions and coal miners called for a strike. Many Soviet deputies, including Moscow\'s Deputy Mayor condemned Gorbachev\'s action. The most forceful and significant show of support came from Boris Yeltsin, President of the Russian Republic. The day after the first assault on Lithuania, Yeltsin went to Tallinn, Estonia and signed a mutual cooperation treaty with the leaders of Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia. Then, Yeltsin went even further and issued a statement to all soldiers of the Russian Republic based in the Baltic States, urging them not to use their weapons against unarmed civilians. "Before attacking civilian targets in the Baltic States, remember your home – think about your republic\'s and people\'s present and future. Violence – against the rule of law, against the Baltic nations – will likewise bring about new serious crisis in Russia, as well as crises for Russians living in other republics." Yeltsin warned the soldiers not to become "pawns in the hands of dark reactionary forces," and added, "We believe in you, the officers and soldiers of Russia, who cherish high moral values, honesty, bravery, boldness, idealism and patriotism, as did the previous generation of the Russian military. We turn to our experience of history which tells us that a misstep today may affect not only those who take the step, but also forthcoming generations." Yeltsin\'s appeal had its affect. A Soviet tank division based in Vitebsk, Bieolrussia had been deployed to the Latvian city of Dobele when the crisis began. But when they were ordered to move on Riga, they refused. There are reports that many tore up their Communist Party cards and that their commander defected. There is also a report that one of the victims in Vilnius was a Soviet soldier, who was shot to death by the Soviet troops when he refused to participate in the massacre and stood in front of a Soviet tank. And in Riga, eyewitnesses report that many Russians came to barricades that had been set up around the Latvian government buildings and offered food and drink to the defenders. It is also worth noting that on January 13, the day after the assault on Vilnius had begun, over 700,000 people rallied in Riga in support of the Latvian government. Latvia has a population of 2.5 million. Of those, 1.5 million are ethnic Latvians. On January 15, the National Salvation Committee – a coalition of Communists, Soviet army officers and other Moscow loyalists – held a rally to call for the resignation of the Latvian government. They claimed to be defending the interests of Latvia\'s non-Latvian nationalities. Of the 1.1 million non-Latvians they were supposedly defending, only 8,000 showed up. Riga alone has over 600,000 non-Latvian residents who could have simply walked or hopped on a bus to the rally. The figures speak for themselves. The pen, the fax and telex are mightier When the Soviets seized the Latvian Press House they moved against one of Latvia\'s most powerful weapons in the war for independence. The Press House, located across the Daugava River from downtown Riga, houses all of Latvia\'s main newspapers and printing facilities. The building had been illegally controlled by the Communist Party for many year. When the new Latvian government was formed it passed a decree which made this facility the property of the Republic of Latvia. The Press House was converted to a stockholding company and was made available to all publications, including the Communist press. Communist Party Boss Alfreds Rubiks vowed to retake the building, and on January 2 carried out his threat. Nevertheless, the Latvian press continues to operate from alternative facilities. It has also played a critical role in providing information to the west during the January crisis. Inexplicably, telephone and telex lines to the west remained open, and Latvian journalists as well as the government were able to provide almost hourly reports on events in the country to Western journalists in and outside of Latvia. This information flow was coordinated with Latvian organizations in Europe, the American Latvian Association (ALA) and the World Federation of Free Latvians (WFFL) in Rockville, MD, and the Legation of Latvia in Washington, D.C. The ALA and WFFL maintained a 24-hour watch from their offices and were able to convey information almost instantaneously to the State Dept., Congress and press. When Roberts Mūrnieks was pronounced dead in a Riga hospital, the Legation of Latvia had a press release out to the wire services 15 minutes later.', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER i .1 J MARCH 1991 Vol. XI No. 3 ill On the night of January 20, Soviet Interior Ministry troops, known as the Black Berets\', attacked the Latvian Interior Ministry in Riga. They sprayed the building with gunfire, then occupied it, and after several hours, left. When they were through, four people lay dead and several were injured. Among the dead were Latvian film maker Andris Slapins, and two members of the Latvian Interior Ministry – both ethnic Russians. Several weeks later, Gvido Zvaigzne, another Latvian cameraman struck by the gimfire, died from his injuries. At the time of the attack, authorities in Riga were convinced that this was the beginning of Moscow\'s attempt to seize and topple the Latvian Government. Black Berets had been terrorizing Riga for several days, firing on buildings and assaulting rivilians. Although one Latvian had been killed by the Black Berets earlier, the attack on the Interior Ministry on January 20 was the first full scale assault on a Latvian government institution. The attack started and stopped within a matter of hours. Why did the Black Berets attack the building, only to leave it shortly thereafter? According to the Black Berets, they had no intention of seizing the building that night. They had gone to there, they say, to discuss the alleged rape of a Black Beret\'s wife. Upon arriving at the building they were fired upon, and assuming that the fire came from Latvian Militia, attacked the building in self defense. In a further investigation of the incident, it was confirmed that the Black Berets had indeed come under fire, but not from the Interior Ministry. Sources within the Latvian prosecutor\'s office claim that KGB sharpshooters located at nearby Bastejkalns and on several neighboring rooftops, had opened fire on the Black Berets. It is believed that KGB bullets were the ones that killed the Latvian film makers. WHO DID THE KILLING IN RIGA? Although there is no confirmation of these reports, some in Latvia believe that the KGB did indeed instigate the incident – firing upon the Black Berets and the bystanders – in order to provoke the gunfight. Their purpose was to give the impression that Latvia\'s militia was attacking the Black Berets. There is also evidence that the Black Berets may have been part of the charade. Peter Maass of the Washington Post reported on Feb. 18, that a Black Beret machine gun nest had been set up across from the Interior Ministry, and that a flare was fired just before the attack, presumably as a signal to begin. What is known, is that the Latvian militia did not provoke the attack, and that sniper fire from nearby locations did occur. Whether it was the KGB, Black Berets or someone else who pulled the triggers, the responsibility for the attacks lies with Moscow. A blessing in disguise? by Qjars Kalnins Most people view the January bloodshed in Lithuania and Latvia as a failed attempt by Moscow to topple the democratic governments of those two countries. According to such hardline defenders of the Soviet empire as Col. Viktors Alksnis, Mikhail Gorbachev is fully responsible for the failure: he had promised to depose the governments, but lost his nerve when resistance proved greater than expected. For people in Lithuania and Latvia, these events were tragic. At least 21 people lost their lives, and scores were injured. But it is becoming clear that those 21 did not die in vain. Their deaths have strengthened the Baltic independence movement, in a way that was unimaginable last year. At the end of 1990, while the rest of the world was pre-occupied with the crisis in Kuwait, the Baltic governments were pre-occupied with their own economic and political struggles. Still committed to independence, the Baltic governments were stiffering a crisis of confidence, as their restive populaces began grumbling about economic problems and the stalemate with Moscow. Few, if any Western reporters or government officials were paying much attention to the Baltic States. The January crackdown changed all that. When the tanks rolled into Vilnius, the Western press followed. As the death count rose in Riga, so did the anger of the world community. The Baltic people rallied behind their governments and took positions behind the barricades. In a matter of days, despite the outbreak of war in the Persian Gulf, the Baltic States were back on the front pages of newspapers around the world. If Moscow thought it could rely on the Gulf War to distract the world from its attack on the Baltics, it was dead wrong. Columnist William Satire said it all when he wrote, The free-world media will not treat the story as secondary news in light of the gulf crisis; we are able to cover two wars and chew gum at the same time." With hundreds of print and electronic media reporters in Vilnius and Riga, every shot fired against the Baltic people was heard and seen around the world. And every foreign government which once had expressed nominal symbolic support for Baltic independence, was looking for ways to express its outrage in the strongest possible terms. President Bush condemned the Soviet actions. The Canadian Government and European Economic Community suspended aid to Moscow. Sweden allocated funds to support the activities of Baltic government officials in the West. Iceland took steps to recognize Lithuania and establish diplomatic ties with all three Baltic governments. Delegation after delegation, from the US Congress, European Council, Eastern Europe and the Soviet-ruled republics, began arriving in a steady stream in the Baltic States. For the first time ever, world government leaders began insisting that the status of the Baltic States was an international issue which had to be settled on an international level. Cautious, pragmatic statesmen, who had sought to walk a fine line between their support for the Baits and their friendship with Gorbachev, began, for the first time, using the T word: independence. During the first two years of the Bush Administration, no Administration official had ever openly supported Baltic "independence." But on February 10, Secretary of State Baker told a nationwide TV audience, "WeVe made it very clear that we want to see the aspirations of the Baltic people for independence fulfilled." By cracking down in the Baltics, the Kremlin thought it could crush Baltic independence once and for all. It accomplished the exact opposite. William Safire, whose writing on the Baltic States has been consistently brilliant, wrote, "The terrible truth is that fre  is most often won on the barricades, after sacrifice, when real soldiers come to the rescue of countrymen being savaged by dictator\'s killers, and when outsiders are shamed into diplomatic support." Safire then added this quote from Thomas Jefferson: "The tree of liberty must be refreshed from time to time with the blood of patriots and tyrants." Mikhail Gorbachev could have paved the way to Baltic independence through peaceful negotiations. He chose to drench the streets in blood. Those who made the ultimate sacrifice in the streets of Vilnius and Riga have made the tree of liberty stronger than ever. U.S. Congressional delegation in Riga On February 12, the largest U.S. Congressional delegation to ever visit the Baltic States arrived in Riga, Latvia. A dozen members of the U.S. Congressional Commission on Security and Cooperation in Europe (CSCE), headed by Chairman Steny Hoyer (D-MD) met with Latvian legislators and political leaders, and then headed for similar meetings in Vilnius and Tallinn. The visit was prompted in part by a request made in Washington two weeks earlier by Dainis Ivans and Bronius Kuzmickas, Vice Presidents of Latvia and Lithuania. The two had testified before the CSCE and met with Secretary of State Baker. Both Latvian and U.S. officials viewed the visit as meaningful and productive. The CSCE members also met with Alfred Rubiks, Ist Secretary of the Communist Party in Latvia. Four hours after the meeting, a bomb exploded in the Communist Party headquarters where the U.S. legislators had spoken to Rubiks. Latvian authorities said it was similar to bombs placed in other locations in Riga, and attributed it to anti-independence forces in Latvia. Latvia\'s shuttle diplomacy During the height of the crisis in Latvia in January, a number of Latvian government officials headed to Western Europe and the United States to solicit support for the beseiged country. Latvia\'s Vice President Dainis Ivans spent a busy week in Washington, meeting with Secretary of State Baker, members of the National Security Council and numerous U.S. Senators, including Senators Kennedy (D-MA), Biden (D-DE), Bradley (D-NJ), Warner (D-VA) and Wallop (R-WY). He also appeared on NBC\'s Today Show, the McNeil-Lehrer Newshour, and National Public Radio. Ivans\' visit was also featured in the PBS-TV program "Frontline", hosted by Hedrick Smith. The program, entitled, "Guns, Tanks and Gorbachev" was broadcast nationally on Feb. 19. Ivans also made a one-day trip to Ottawa, Canada where he met with Foreign Minister Joe Clark. While Ivans was making the rounds in the Northern Hemisphere, Latvia\'s Foreign Minister Janis Jurkans was covering the European Continent and Great Britain. He met with British Prime Minisler Major, Germany\'s Foreign Minister Genscher, France\'s President Mitterand and with the foreign ministers and political leaders of Sweden, Denmark, Holland and Belgium. Jurkans\' numerous visits received extensive TV and newspaper coverage throughout Europe. Rubik s Cube Last year, Alfrec s Rubiks received a rar honor in Latvia. The 1 Secretary of th Communist Party Latvia came in first in poll conducted by th Latvian youth magazin liesma. He was votec Latvia\'s most despisec personality. Through the years, Rubiks has done a great deal to deserve this prestigious honor. You could say he was a visionary. Back in 1987, when Gorbachev wad building a case for glasnost, Rubiks, then the mayor of Riga, suggested that tanks be brought in to stop peaceful street demonstrations. It took Gorbachev 3 years to act on Rubiks\' suggestion. Rubiks was eventually voted out of office and retained the only position that didn\'t require democratic elections: the leadership of the Communist Party. He watched in frustration as his Party split and then was forced out of power in the spring 1990 elections. But that didn\'t stop him. Throughout 1990 he vowed he would retake the power that was taken from him. In December he went on Latvian TV and predicted that Latvian TV and radio "would soon be our hands again." On January 2, Soviet Black Berets, operating under Rubiks\' orders, seized the Press House in Riga. A week later, as Soviet tanks began crushing civilians in Vilnius, Rubiks assumed the leadership of the socalled "Latvian Salvation Committee" and announced that he was ready to take over the Latvian government. As usual, he was ignored by the populace. Within days, Rubiks announced that his Salvation commitee had already taken over the government. But the democratically elected Latvian government was still in place. Later, after the Black Beret assault on the Latvian Interior Ministry, Rubiks held a press conference and announced that he had been misunderstood. His Salvation Committee, he said, never really intended to seize the government. It just wanted to have a voice in Latvia\'s affairs. Rubiks denied having any role in the Black Beret terror campaign in Riga. Soviet President Gorbachev also denied responsibility for the violence. Gorbachev even denied having anything to do with Rubiks or his Salvation Committee. Shortly thereafter, Latvia\'s president, Anatolijs Gorbunovs went to Moscow to meet with Gorbachev and discuss the crisis in Latvia. When he arrived, Gorbunovs found two people sitting across from him at the table: Mikhail Gorbachev and Alfreds Rubiks. Perhaps Gorbachev found something in common with his fellow Communist Party boss. Gorbachev, you see, also has this problem with democratic elections and popularity polls. Chicago has a heart Usually, infants like Krista Liepniece rarely have a chance to survive in Latvia. Born with a rare form of congenital heart disease, Latvian doctors concluded that they could do little to help her. Her x-rays were sent to major cardiac surgery centers in Europe and the United States, and specialists there concurred. But Dr. Christopher K. Zarins, a Latvian American Professor of Surgery at the University of Chicago never gave up hope. He examined the two-year-old Krista during a medical mission to Latvia in December 1990, and returned to Chicago with an appeal to the University of Chicago for assistance. Dr. J. Deane Waldman, Chief of Pediatric Cardiology, and Dr. Robert B. Karp, Chief of Cardiac Surgery at the University of Chicago agreed with Dr. Zarins. On January 23, thanks to the University of Chicago, Scandinavian Airline Systems and the Chicago-based Latvian Mercy Lift, Krista and her mother Inga arrived in Chicago. Successful heart surgery was performed on Krista on Feb. 1 at Wyler Children\'s Hospital in Chicago. Chicago condemns crackdown On February 6, Mayor Daley and the Chicago City Council passed a resolution condemning Soviet violence in Lithuania and Latvia. The lengthy resolution called on Gorbachev to cease the use of force in the Baltics, and stated, "we support President Bush\'s condemnation of the recent Soviet use of force and intimidation in Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia, and call on him to convey this message directly to the Soviet Government, to make this issue a priority item on the agenda of future summit meetings between the United States and the Soviet Union." The City Council urged the Soviet government to "enter into peaceful and meaningful negotiations" with the Baltic governments, and suggested that the CSCE utilize "new conflict prevention and resolution mechanisms" to resolve the Baltic crisis. Reiters — The 700-Year History of the Latvian Flag Historical references to the red-white-red flag of Latvia can be found as far back as 1279 in the German language Reion chronicle. The use of the red-white-red colors was revived by Latvian students at Dorpat University in 1890 and used in the National Latvian Song Festival in 1910. During World War I, a commission of artists was formed to decide the "official" shade of red and the flag\'s proportions. This "official" national flag graced the stage of the National Theater at the declaration of statehood ceremonies on November 18, 1918. KULTŪRAI – CULTURE Alīda Clrule — Friča Bārdas simtgade 7 Alida Cirule — Fricis Barda Hundredth Anniversary This year marks the hundredth anniversary of the birth of Fricis Barda, Latvian\'s finest neoromantic poet. This critical summary traces his romantic vision through his poems, their subject matter, style, and metaphor. Many of his poems have been set to music and are often presented at choral concerts. Komponista Alfrēda Kalniņa simtgade 9 Composer Alfreds Kalnins Hundredth Anniversary Alfreds Kalnins is one of Latvian\'s most beloved composers. His works include the first Latvian opera and ballet, more than 400 solo songs, 130 choral works as well as instrumental compositions. Edmunds Zirnītis — Dzejas mākslas meistars Edvarts Virza 12 Edmunds Zirnitis — Edvarts Virza: Master of Poetics (1883-1940). A Review of the contribution of Edvarts Virza to Latvian poetry. His poems published in 8 volumes from 1907 through 1942, show unparalleled virtuosity of form. A master of versification, Virza represents the classical school of Latvian verse. Julieta Rumberga — Latviešu teātrim svešumā jādzīvo! 16 Julieta Rumberga — Latvian Theater in Exile The theater is an ideal way to preserve the Latvian identity of our youth in exile. It strengthens their language skills, introduces them to our national characteristics, history and ideals. Working in the theater, Latvian youth become active participants in the Latvian community. Interviews with members of the 3rd Drama Workshop, reinforce the hope that Latvian theater in exile will survive. Julieta Rumberga — Trešo teātra kursu izrāde Garezerā 17 Julieta Rumberga — Third Drama Workshop in Garezers Reviews of the final presentations of the drama students at the Third Drama Workshop. Antonija Millere — Valoda un valodnieki 24 Antonija Millere — Language and Linguists Although linguists are looked to as authorities of language, the final arbiter of acceptability is the consensus of the people who speak it. In a living language, linguists only determine the facts, people determine what new words will be created and accepted into the language. Projects for 1980 sponsored by ALA\'s Cultural Bureau include a traveling theater, a documentary film project, two books, but the most ambitions project is planned for August 10 through 17, 1980 at Garezers: a family week of culture, courses in crafts, seminars on the family, children\'s activities. The aim is to provide activities where parents and children can participate together. ALA mūža biedri 26 ALA\'s Life Members Jānis Riekstiņš — Latviešu Institūts, Latvju enciklopēdijas projekts 27 Jānis Riekstiņš — Latvian Institute\'s Latvian Encyclopedia Project The 2 volume encyclopedia of Latvian history over the past quarter century is 90% written. Publication will be insured on the strength of subscriptions sold, and a new drive will begin in 1980. TRIMDAS SKOLA – EDUCATION Liga Ruperte, Ph.D. — Mācīsim skolēniem latvietību 28 Liga Ruperte, Ph.D. — Let\'s Teach our Students Latvianism. A paper presented to the Eastern Region Teachers Conference in March 1980. The aim of a Latvian education is more than learning a given set of facts. The true aim is to teach the new generation a Latvian value system and morality so to better develop a strong Latvian personality, that is both independent and self-confident. While parents are the most important in partners of values, schools can do their share by going beyond the facts and relating the subject matter to the students\' own feelings, opinions and behavior. SKOLAS GAITAS – SCHOOL\'S ACTIVITY Emils Avots — Gadu simteņa ceturksnis veltīts jaunatnes audzināšanai 31 Emils Avots — Youth Camp Marks its 25th Year. The Grand Rapids Lutheran Church sponsored Summer camp for Latvian youth, celebrated its 25th year last summer, 68 children gathered from all over America to Mezhvidi, Michigan, from July 8 to 21, 1979. Vilis Miķelsons — Dižens jaunatnes latviskais saiets 33 Vilis Miķelsons — Fifth Latvian Youth Conference — Central Region 300 youth gather in Indianapolis to celebrate a folk festival of song, dance, arts and crafts. APSKATS – REVIEW J. Utināns — Atskats uz DV ASV darbības 30 gadiem 37 J. Utinans — 30 years of DV activities in the USA. This charitable organization began its activities in the USA with the first immigrants who arrived from Germany after the war. DV in USA is part of an international organization operating in 12 countries in the free world. A woman\'s auxilliary DV Vanadzes has been active since 1955. Together, the DV and the Vanadzes have done more for helping needy Latvians, promoting Latvian cultural programs, schools, books and magazines than many other organizations. Vilis Miķelsons — Kā radās Kalamazū latviešu pagasts? 42 Vilis Miķelsons — Origins of the Latvian Settlement in Kalamazoo Today about 1200 Latvians live in Kalamazoo, or about one Latvian for every 80 residents. This unparalleled density of Latvians can be attributed to the dream ofE. Dinvalds, who came to Kalamazoo from Germany in 1948. Dinvalds wanted to keep the D.P. Camp Latvian chorus, "Dziesmu Vairogs" together and resettled in Kalamazoo. He enlisted the efforts of his countryman, J. Laupmains, a Methodist minister in Kalamazoo, who organized the 60 Kalamazoo churches in finding sponsors for the "famous" chorus. The chorus became the nucleus of cultural life in Kalamazoo and attracted other Latvians to settle in the area. 2. R. Ribenieks — Kvekertaunas latviešu kolonija 45 Z. R. Ribenieks — The Latvian Colony in Quakertown, PA What started as a Latvian Baptist colony seeking relief from the Depression of the thirties in Philadelphia, is now home to 400 Latvians, both Baptists and Lutherans. It is home to some distinguished Latvian artists and craftsmen and the site of the Latvian Summer School, Beverina.
