Vanagu Kārlis — Tā zeme ir mūsu! 55 Vanagu Kārlis — That Land is ours An historical journey down the river Daugava from Staburags to Koknese, Latvia. A. Spurmanis — Technikas apskata 25 gadi 60 A. Spurmanis — The Technical Journal\'s 25th Anniversary Technikas Apskats, the journal of the Latvian Engineers Association celebrates its 25th year of publication in the free world, but continues the tradition of Latvian scientific publication which began at the University of Latvia in 1921.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER AT THE END OF NOVEMBER – A NEW GOVERNMENT ESTABLISHED On November 26th the new government formed by Vilis Krištopans was accepted. 59 deputies voted for the government, 24 were against it, the deputies from the communistically orientated party "Tautas Saskaņas partija" didn\'t participate in the vote, thus guaranteeing acceptance of the new government. THOUSANDS OF CANDLES LIT IN REMEMBRANCE OF LATVIAN FREEDOM FIGHTERS In memory of the battle against the army of Bermont which took place on November 11, eighty years ago, many events occurred all around Latvia. The most moving event was when people gathered on the bank of Daugava river near the President\'s Palace to light candles in remembrance of the Latvian fighters for freedom. THE CELEBRATION OF NOVEMBER 18TH On November 18th the 80 anniversary of the proclamation of Latvia\'s independence was celebrated. This year more than before, various events dedicated to the anniversary took place. The day started with religeous service in the Doma Cathedral, then the army parade took place. One of the main events was the concert in Latvian National Opera house. Many official delegations from abroad congratulated Latvia on it\'s independence day. Because of the sudden drop of the temperature (it was about 8 degrees Fahrenheit even during the day) there were some problems with the fireworks later in the evening. It turned out that some fireworks don\'t work when it s cold, but nevertheless thousands and thousands of people showed up and enjoyed the "remaining" fireworks display. THREE STAR ORDERS PRESENTED On November 17th the Three Star Orders (Triju Zvaigžņu ordeņi) were presented to many people who have honored the name of Latvia. Among them was also the President of the Republic of Latvia – Guntis Ulmanis. THE NEW CHANGES IN THE LAW OF CITIZENSHIP TAKE EFFECT After the new changes in the Law of the citizenship NEWS FROM LATVIA – January 1999 reported by took effect the number of people asking to grant them citizenship of Latvia has increased. During the first week of the Law 280 inhabitants of Latvia asked for the country\'s citizenship. Before the Law took effect there were only about 100 people weekly applying for the citizenship. Most of the new applicants for the citizenship are in the 41 – 50 age bracket. EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT ON LATVIA The European Parliament has voted on Latvia entering the EU as soon as possible. However, it only means that next time Latvia will be invited to join the EU, most likely will will be a year from now. LATVIAN POLITICIANS GO TO MOSCOW The delegation of the pro-communistically orientated party – "Tautas Saskaņas partija" – have gone for a visit to Russia by the invitation of the mayor of Moscow. The Minister of Foreign Affairs Valdis Birkavs is of the opinion that it is inadmissible for the deputies of TSP to meet with such high officials of Russia and that those kinds of visits give Russia the opportunity to interfere in Latvia\'s internal affairs. The politicians from TSP have agreed with the Mayor of Moscow that Moscow will collaborate with businessmen from Latvia who support the TSP. This is an open example of corruption and the other deputies of Saeima have expressed their indignation of such a move by their colleges. EUROPE GETS INTRODUCED TO THE HISTORY OF LATVIA. An exhibition "Latvia is returning to Europe: from Occupation to the Independence" was held in Europarliament in Brussels. It was opened by a speech of Prime Minister at that time, Guntars Krasts who informed that the intention to make such an exhibition was to introduce other European countries to the recent history of Latvia. The exhibition was organized largely due to the efforts of the deputy of Europarliament Ernesto Kakavale who is responsible for reports about Latvia. It was his idea to organize such an exhibition. The Latvian government gave 40 000 Lats towards the installation of the exhibition. It is planned to show the same display in Strasbourg and in other cities of EU countries. Šarlote Līduma t in Rīga LATVIA THE THIRD IN TERMS OF FOREIGN INVESTMENT In 1997 the amount of foreign investments in Latvia took the third place among the Central and Eastern European countries. The amount of investments on one inhabitant of Latvia in 1997 has been 169 USD. The first place with 208 USD per capita went to Hungary. Estonia with 180 USD on each inhabitant of the country took the second place. In terms of investment into a foreign country Russia received the first place with investments worth 2,5 billion USD, Hungary – 431 millions USD and Estonia – 130 millions USD. NEW BOTTLES-NEW BEER Starting mid – November the Latvian beer brewery "Aldaris" has changed the old bottles to a new and prettier design. It has also put out a new beer just for Christmas season and it is called the "Christmas beer" (Ziemssvētku alus). 22 YEARS TOGO The living standard in Latvia could reach the level of the poorest country of European Union – Greece – only in 22-25 years as reported in "Business Central Europe". For Estonia the prognosis is to reach the level of Greece in 16-18 years, for Lithuania – 32-33 years. STATISTICS During the last three years the death rate in Latvia has decreased. It might be the first indication of positive changes in the Latvian economy. There are 59,4% people of working age living in Latvia (ten years ago – in 1989 – there were 57,7% people of working age in Latvia). The ethnic situation has also changed. In 1989 there were 52% Latvians living in Latvia, this year the number has increased to 55,5% Latvians. Currently in Rīga 38,7% residents are ethnic Latvians. The average age for marriage is 23,6 years of age for women and 25,7 years of age for men. The number of marriages per year has increased by 0,5%, however, the amount of divorced marriages is also on the rise. In comparison with EU countries, the accidental death rate is large and it makes 11,6%. One of the main reasons of death still is the hindered circulation of blood. VISAS FOR RUSSIAN MILITARY MEN LENGTHENED AGAIN The visas for 205 Russian military men currently stationed at the Skrunda radio locations unit were extended by the Latvian Immigration service till the end of September 1999. For 7 Russian military men with their families who were formerly working at a Skrunda Radio location their visas had expired, but since they claim that they have no place to go in Russia their visas have been lengthened again, this time till January Ist 1999. THE FIRST HELP FOR PEOPLE WITH LOW INCOME In November the first "social house" – an apartment building for the low income people – has been opened. So far only 10 one bedroom apartments and 40 studios are available but soon four other buildings for such purpose will be renovated in Rīga. The rent in those houses will be very low (0,04 Lats per one square meter) and the costs for heating in the winter time will be lowered by 25%. Till the middle of November 75 people had already applied for rights to live in this house. However many others are considering to apply later, because the information about applications has not been out for more than a couple days before the completion of the building. IN LATVIA – COLD WHEATHER DOMINATES This November and December have been unusually cold in Latvia. The first snow which fell on the ground in the end of October hasn\'t melted yet. The temperature in November was mostly 17 to 8 degrees Fahrenheit. In the period of time from November first till November 18th 11 people were found frozen to death. On November 18th – the day of the Proclamation of Latvia\'s Independence – temperature this low was registered only more than a hundred years ago. The snow on the city streets has piled up. The Rīga City Counsel spends about 11 thousand Lats each day to clean the snow from the streets. PEOPLE ARE RESPONSIVE TO DONATING The Fund for the Reconstruction of the Monument of Liberty (Brīvības piemineklis) has informed that the largest amount donated to the Fund is 10,032,83 Lats. It was collected from November 14th till November 17th in the "Statoil" chain of gas stations. Each "Statoil" customer donated half a santim for each liter of gas purchased. When the four day campaign of money collecting for the Monument began, the number of "Statoil" clients increased by 16%. THE DEMARCATION ON THE LAND BORDER – COMPLETED On November 27th the demarcation between the land border of Latvia and Lithuania was completed in its entirety. The land border between the two countries is 540 kilometers long. GOOD NEWS FOR EMPLOYEES During the last year the number of enterprises which provide health insurance for their employees has doubled. LATVIAN CHILDREN GET HIGH PRIZES Two gold medals and three silver medals were won by the children of Latvia at an international arts contest in Taiwan. The event gathered several thousand contestants from over thirty countries. Altogether 60 drawings from Latvia were entered in this contest. BRONZE MEDAL IN LAHTI In the championship of weight lifting in the Finnish city Lahti, Viktors Ščerbatihs from Dobele in Latvia won the bronze medal in double contest (divcīņa) and the small bronze medal in pull-lifting (raušana). Both his results are new records for Latvia as well. SONORA VAICE\'S SUCCESS IN AUSTRIA The performance of Latvian soprano Sonora Vaice in Austria has been recognized as a great success. She performed as Violetta in Verdi\'s "La Traviata" and with the first aria got the ovations of opera goers. The Austrian critics like her voice as well as her appearance. In all the articles that have been written about Sonora it is always emphasized that she comes from Latvia. TRAIN ACCIDENT RULED SABOTAGE A leak of 59 tons of diesel fuel after an explosion at Mangaļi train station outside Rīga on November 29th was caused as the result of sabotage. The country\'s Forensic expertise center has determined that TNT explosives were attached to a cistern causing the blast. The fuel polluted the surrounding area and caused 30,000 Lats damage. DENMARK HELPS LATVIAN CHILDREN The Latvian branch of Save the Children Foundation and the Christmas Charity Marathon staged by the Latvian state television received 200,000 Lats donation of medical supplies from Denmark\'s Maersk Medical company and Sollerod\'s Lions Club. FISHERY LAYS OFF WORKERS Due to the financial crises in Russian the "Kaija" fish cannery will lay off 220 employees. Their warehouse is stacked full with unsold goods which Russia can\'t afford to buy. LATVIA PREPARES FOR FULL MEMBERSHIP IN WORLD TRADE ORGANIZATION. Additional 7,000 Lats are needed for Latvia to officially join WTO. Currently, Latvia pays 17,000 Swiss franks per year to WTO as an observer nation. Full membership, however, requires the additional money in annual fees. The membership brings access to the markets of 131 member states as well as reduced tariffs with many countries.', 'RED ARMY VICTORY CELEBRATED BY THE RUSSIANS On May 9th, many Russians living in Latvia gathered to celebrate the Victory of the Red Army over Germany. Many leaflets were distributed during this meeting, including leaflets against Latvia joining the NATO. The celebration In Rīga ended with a magnificent show of fireworks which wasmorejpectacujax than the one on November 18th. ~""e LATVIAN POETS HONORED On May 9th, two Latvian poets – Vizma Belševica and Knuts Skujenieks – were granted a prestigious award in Sweden. This award is presented only to highly recognized poets who live near the Baltic sea. IN THE PARLIAMENT On May 10th, the Parliament of Latvia decided to keep the death penalty. In May, the Parliament\'s Commission on European Affairs asked the Ministry of Foreign Affairs to include the visit of the Museum of Occupation in the official protocol of the government. Until now it was only on the list of recommended sites to visit. Now all the official delegations coming to Latvia will have an opportunity to learn facts about the Occupation of Latvia. NEW AND UNEXPECTED PASSPORT CHANGE In 1992, the Republic of Latvia released the new Latvian passport. Now it has turned out that the Latvian passports do not meet the international safety requirements. The new passports will be issued as soon as the year 1999. RĪGA SLOWLY BECOMES WHEELCHAIR ACCESSIBLE Finally at the Central Train Station in Rīga there is an elevator installed for people with moving disabilities. So far for people in wheelchairs the only way to get to the train was by being carried by somebody up the stairway. BUSINESS NEWS According to the magazine "Business Central Europe the total investment of foreign capital in Latvia last year was 635 million Lats (about 1,07 billion USD), slightly more than in Estonia or Lithuania but significantly less than in some Eastern European countries. The 10 Ls coin which was introduced last year was a winner in the international contest of small gold coins. In the influential numismatic magazine "Deutches NEWS FROM LATVIA September 1998 – reported by Muenzenmagazin" it is mentioned between the 15 best coins of 1997. in the international exhibition of seafood products in Bremen, Germany "Fish \'98" many wholesale seafood dealers from Czech Republic, Germany, Norway, Netherlands, USA and Canada were interested in buying the products of Latvian seafood companies "Kaija" and Salacgrīva \'95V More than 800 enterprises were presenting their seafood products at this exhibition. LENIN SOLD AT SOTHEBYS The monument of former Soviet dictator\'and tyrant Vladimir Lenin from the city Preiļi, Latvia was sold in May at Sotheby\'s for 23,350 British pounds. LAILA PAKALNIŅA\'S MOVIE AT CANNES FILM FESTIVAL During the 51st International Film Festival in Cannes, France in the category of the "Golden Camera" the film of Latvian director Laila Pakalniņa "Shoe" ("Kurpe") was shown. The film has been mentioned in such prestigious movie magazines as "La Monde" and "Liberalization". The rights to show the movie were bought by the European film channel "arte" as well as many movie theaters in Paris. For Laila Pakalniņa this was the first motion picture. She has participated in Cannes Film Festivals before as a director of documentary films. THE FIRST MULTI-LEVEL PARKING LOT STARTED In May the construction of the first multi-level parking lot in Rīga was begun. It is scheduled to be completed by May of next year. STATISTICS In 1997, 79,2% of Latvians were registeres as living, below the accepted level of the standard of living, which is 72 Ls per month. In the last four years the number of foreign citizens working in Latvia has increased 1,6 times. Most of them are employed in Rīga and Ventspils – the two economically more developed cities. The highest number of illegal workers has been registered near the boarders of Latvia and mostly in the summer months. The fine for the employer, if caught, employing illegal imigrants is 150 Ls. It is too small a fine to stop employers from hiring illegal workers. So far there is no legislation in Latvia that controls the number of foreign employees entering the work force. Šarlote Līduma in Rīga "LIELAIS KRISTAPS" (THE LATVIAN "OSCAR") On June sth, the Latvian movie award "Lielais Kristaps" ("The Big Kristaps") was presented. Three movies were nominated for the best Latvian motion picture. For the firs time in the twenty year history of "Lielais Kristaps" the award was presented to a TV movie – "Izpostītā ligzda" ("The Destroyed Nest"), starring popular Latvian actors such as Eduards Pāvuls, Zane Jančevska, Milēna Gulbe. This was the first movie directed by the actor Uldis Pūcīts. However, there are debates among the movie critics of the country whether a TV movie can be classified as a motion picture. A NEW U.S. AMBASSADOR The President of the United States has appointed a new ambassador to the Republic of Latvia. The newly appointed ambassador James Holms will arrive in Latvia shortly after the elections of the Parliament of Latvia this fall. JUNE 14TH – THE DAY TO REMEMBER THE VICTIMS OF COMMUNISM On June 14th – the day of Commemoration of the victims of communism, many events took place. The largest gatherings were at the railroad station – Torņakalns, at the Monument of Freedom and at the Museum of Occupation. Many ambassadors from the EU countries, as well as the ambassador of the United States Larry Napper took part in those events. ENVIRONMENT On June 20, the nuclear reactor in Salaspils, near Rīga was shut down. It was built 37 years ago and was the only civil research nuclear reactor in the Baltic states. It will take 7-11 years to dismantle it and the whole process will cost 50 million USD. RUSSIAN TEACHERS The Russian teachers were picketing at the Ministry of Education and Science in Rīga against the regulation which states that starting September Ist this year the Russian teachers are required to speak Latvian fluently. Out of 19,190 non-Latvian teachers only about 2,200 have attended Latvian language classes which were free of charge. It is estimated that about 500 teachers will find it difficult to pass the Latvian language examination. NON-CITIZENS ARE SLOW TO EXCHANGE THE OLD USSR PASSPORTS More than a half of the non-citizens of Latvia have not bothered to obtain the non-citizen passports. According to the law, the old USSR passport has to be exchanged for the non-citizen passport of Latvia by the year 2000. Currently there are over 650,000 non-citizens in Latvia. Only 221,517 of them have obtained the new passports. IN PARLIAMENT On June 22, the Latvian Parliament passed a bill to grant citizenship to all children who were born in Latvia after August 21st, 1991 to non-citizens. These parents can request the citizenship to be granted to their children up to the age of 15. In that case the children don\'t have to take the examination in the official language, which is Latvian. However, if the parents decide not to request citizenship for them, after the age of 15, the children themselves have the right to request the citizenship. However, then they will be requred to take the examination in the official language. 38 deputies opposed this bill. According to the Latvian Constitution, if 34 members of the Parliament oppose a bill, the President must put a stop on the bill for two months, so that changes can be introduce. It needs 10% of the voters to support the idea in a form of a referendum. A MOVIE ABOUT THE BALTICS A new documentary film titled "One world: the Baltic countries" is being made about the Baltic states. The movie is produced by "Ward Television Corporation" and it will be aired by the Public Broadcasting Stations in the United Stated of America and by some national TV stations in other parts of the world. One of the sponsors is Motorola Company. COST OF LIVING STATISTICS Of 172 different cities around the world Rīga was ranked 45th in regard to the cost of living. This research project was conducted by scientists in Geneva, Switzerland using the prices of more than 200 products and services on the scale from 1 to 100,100 being the most expensive. Rīga scored 88 points. However, according to this survey Tallinn, the capital of Estonia, has one of the lowest cost of living ratio. SONG FESTIVAL 31,000 singers, folkdancers and musicians participated in this year\'s Song Festival in Rīga. The City Council of Rīga spent 18,000 Lats to refurbish concert halls and parts of the city for this event, and the state granted about 390,000 Lats for the purpose of staging the Song Festival. MISS AND MISTER LATVIA On July 4th, a pageant of "Miss and Mister Latvia *98" was held on National TV. Evija Ručevska from Rīga was crowned "Miss Latvia \'98" and Kaspars Patašs from Jelgava became "Mister Latvia \'98". NEW REGULATIONS REGARDING REFUGEES On July 4th, the Cabinet of Ministers set new regulations on the allowance for refugees. If a person has obtained the status of a refugee and doesn\'t have any other source of subsistence, she or he is entiled to receive a 50 Ls allowance as well as additional 35 Ls for acquiring basic knowledge of the Latvian laguage. "BALTIC CHALLENGE \'98" On July 16th, near Klaipeda, Lithuania an international military training called "Baltic Challenge \'98" took place. Eleven countries participated in this training. Latvia participated with two military companies "Latbat" and "Baltbat" and the national guard "Zemessargi" unit. LATVIAN PEACE-KEEPERS – BEST IN THE BALTICS The Latvian peace-keepers from the Baltic Peace battalion "Baltbat" have been rated the best guards in the Baltics. Especially the Latvian mechanics have been praised for their knowledge of repairing a variety of armored vehicles. For Latvians this was not the first peace-keeping mission that they have participated in. MONETARY REFORM On July 20th, the Bank of Latvia has put in circulation 500 Lats domination notes. This concludes the monetary reform that in 1992 introduced the Lats as the Latvian currency. The 500 Ls notes were printed in Germany at the printing house "Giesecke & Devrient". Besides all the safety marks featured in all paper bills of Latvia, this 500 Ls has an additional safety mark – a change in color – depending on the angle from which the light is coming. ENVIRONMENTALISTS AGAINST OIL TERMINAL The Latvian environmentalists, mostly from The Green Party are protesting against Lithuanians building an oil terminal along the Baltic Sea coast, stating that the coast of Kurzeme will be totally destroyed. People can pettition and voice a protest against the building of the terminal. A number of the Green Party members have gone on a hunger strike near the Lithuanian embassy in Rīga. Many well known politicians have signed the petition. AN AWARD PRESENTED TO THE FORMER U.S. AMBASSADOR TO LATVIA The Council of Orders awarded the former ambassador of the United States of America the Honorable Larry Napper with the Second degree or the Three Star Order. LATVIAN ROCK BAND GETS THE GRAND PRIX From July 16th – 18th, the rock festival "Baltic Song Contest" took place in Sweden. In a fierce competition with bands from Sweden, England, Poland, Norway, Lithuania, Netherlands and Denmark the grand prix was awarded to the band from Latvia "Prāta vētra" ("The Brainstorm"). The lead singer of the band Renārs Kaupers was praised in Swedish press and was even named the new Jim Morrison. ROLLING STONES IN TALLINN On August Bth, the legendary rock group the "Rolling Stones" had a concert in the Tallinn, Estonia. According to the border guard information about 6000 Latvians crossed the border to hear and see the group perform.']

Representative_Docs: ['THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER UNITED NATIONS DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM IN LATVIA The United Nations Development Program (UNDP) assists people in 174 countries, including Latvia, in promoting sound governance and market development and helping to rebuild societies in the aftermath of war and humanitarian emergencies. The UNDP focuses in the following areas: 1 )democracy building 2)transition to market economy 3)environmental regeneration, 4) conflict resolution and s)the advancement of women. In administering its programs, UNDP relies on the expertise of developing country nationals and non-governmental organizations, the specialized agencies of the UN and research institutes in every field. In the fall of \'93, UNDP prepared a proposal for democratization in Latvia which stimulated many initiatives in the area. DEMOCRACY BUILDING An important element in promoting democratization is to facilitate the involvement of non-governmental organizations (NGO\'s) in civil society. UNDP will provide assistance to Latvian NGO\'s involved in social and human development. UNDP technical support is being combined with assistance from the American Bar Association\'s Central and East European Law Initiative, the United States Agency for International Development (USAID), the United States Information Service (USIS) and the Soros Foundation to establish a Judicial Training Institute under the Latvian Judges\' Association. The goal is to provide training for new and experienced judges in law and judicial practice. TRANSITION TO MARKET ECONOMY A World Bank-financed study on economic management, executed partly by UNDP, provided an analysis of the economic management of the Latvian public sector. As a follow-up, UNDP is helping finance a capacity-building program with the main objective of enhancing the analytical capacity of the government in the implementation of economic policies. ENVIRONMENT According to UNDP reports, the principal environmental problems in Latvia consist of air and water pollution. The two main bodies of water- the Gulf of Riga and the Daugava River- are heavily polluted. In the early 90\'s, 80% of urban settlements and about 95% of rural villages failed to provide for high-grade drinking water and adequate sewage purification. March 1997 – Vol. XXIII, no*l3 Soil and ground water have been severely contaminated near military bases, due to chemicals and petroleum products. Thus far, information about UNDP efforts to reduce water and air pollution has not been available. In the energy field, a joint initiative with Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia is underway with the International Atomic Energy Agency. Protection from radiation and nuclear safety are the main focus points. CONFLICT RESOLUTION The recent passage of the Latvian Citizenship Law requires that people seeking to naturalize have sufficient knowledge of Latvian. UNDP is assisting the government in the development of a comprehensive national program for Latvian language training. This project is intended to facilitate the integration of the Russian minority. ADVANCEMENT OF WOMEN The Gender in Development program (GID) emphasizes women\'s roles and needs in all operations-not just in traditional women-oriented projects. GID is an underlying element of all UNDP supported programs. Specific GID programming efforts under way in the Baltics include: 1) establishment of national Women in Development (WID) units for capacity-building, to defend women\'s interests and to assist in women-oriented legislation; 2) development of gender statistics; 3) support to NGO\'s and other advocacy groups in civil society; 4) training and the retraining of women for the transition to market economies, and in environmental protection. Many governments in the world contribute to UNDP funding. Recipient country governments cover more than half of total project costs through personnel, facilities, equipment and supplies. Funding for UNDP programs from \'92 to \'96 in Latvia totaled over $3 million. Thus far, the 97-99 programs in Latvia have been allocated over $1 million. For more information regarding UNDP and their programs contact: UNDP, Regional Bureau for Europe and the CIS, 304 East 45th Street, NY, NY 10017. "Change is under way. This doesn\'t just mean doing things differently. It means breaking the mold, starting over, and questioning everything that was believed to be real. It means taking great risks and charting unknown territory," UNDP Administrator. —Aija Straumana', "MINISTER OF FOREIGN AFFAIRS ADDRESSES UN COMMISSION ON HUMAN RIGHTS On 21 March in Geneva, the Minister of Foreign Affairs Mr Indulis Berzins participated in the 58th Session of the UN Commission on Human Rights (UNHCR). In his speech the Minister noted that protection and development of human rights is among the priorities of today's international relations, and expressed confidence that the UNHCR has to continue with an effective work in this area worldwide, Mr Berzins noted that Latvia appreciated the assistance and support provided by international organisations during the process of streamlining legislation, and expressed satisfaction over the decision reached at the end of last year by the OSCE Participating States that the presence of the OSCE Mission in Latvia should be discontinued. In his speech, the Minister also emphasised that the priority of Latvia's Government is the development of an integrated society. The National Programme Integration of Society in Latvia tirat covers various fields – citizenship, language training and education – has been adopted to facilitate this process. The Minister of Foreign Affairs informed the participants that in the previous year, Latvia's Government launched the information campaign Citizenship of Latvia: Make Your Choice, tine aim of which was to familiarise the genera! public with the opportunities of naturalisation, as well as introduced the activities of the Society Integration Foundation, established in 2001. The Minister noted that the Foundation would be one of the key mechanisms in facilitating tie building of a harmonious society.", "MINISTER OF FOREIGN AFFAIRS ADDRESSES 58TH SESSION OF THE UN COMMISSION ON HUMAN RIGHTS On 21 March 2002 in Geneva, the Latvian Minister of Foreign Affairs Indulis Berzins participated in the 58th Session of the UN Commission on Human Rights, where he gave a speech. In his speech, the Minister emphasised that Latvia's work, from 1999 to 2001, in the Commission on Human Rights, which is one of the most significant international human rights bodies, has proved Latvia's commitment and ability to be an active participant of international processes and to contribute to the decision-making concerning global issues. Mr Berzins noted that protection and development of human rights is among the priorities of today's international relations, and expressed confidence that the UN Commission on Human Rights has to continue effective woric in that area in any part of the world. Mr Berzins noted that Latvia appreciates tine assistance and support provided by international organisations during the improvement of legislation, and expressed satisfaction over the decision reached at the end of last year by the OSCE Participating States that the presence of the OSCE Mission in Latvia should be discontinued. In his speech, the Minister emphasised that the priority of Latvia's Government is the development of integrated society. The National Programme Integration of Society in Latvia is the means to achieve that objective, and it covers various sectors – citizenship, language training and education. Mr Berzins informed the participants that, In the previous year, Latvia's Government iaunched the information campaign Citizenship of Latvia: Make Your Choice, the aim of which was to familiarise the general public with opportunities of naturalisation, as well as give an introduction to the activities of the Society Integration Foundation established in 2001. The Minister noted that the Foundation would be one of the key mechanisms in development of harmonious society."]

Representative_Docs: ['Olgerts Liepins The blak-and-white graphic arts, including that of wood-engraving did not begin to flourish before the 16th century. The history of wood-engraving is marked by two distinct periods. The first may be called the period of woodcut, and the artist then worked with his knife on a piece of board split lengthwise, using as a principal means of expression the black line which was obtained by THE LATVIAN WOOD-ENGRAVING a double cut into the wooden printing-block. In the 18th century the wood-engraving proper appears, for which a piece of wood cut crosswise was used! and a line (appearing white on the print) produced by a single draw of the style. However a high virtuosity in wood-engraving was not reached until the 18th century when an English engraver, Beuick (1753—1828), began using Fishermen – woodcut by A. Junkers Illustration from Ed. Virza\'s Straumeni – woodcut by A. Junkers for his graphic procedure cross-sawn wood blocks where the gTain no longer presented any oostacie to the free application of the engraver s tool. In the 19th century xylography developed to such perfection that by combining the technique of hatching, cross-hatching and stippling a variability of expression was attained which made this particular graphic art admirably suited for the reproduction of paintings- This stage however, marks a stop in the development of xylography as an independent and original art, and gradually the mechanical process of photography and phototyping begins to supersede the wood-engraved reproductions. , The renaissance of xylography begins towards the end of the 19th century when the draughtsman and wood-engraver becomes one again and the latter no longer aims at and indulges in the secondary work of reproducing other artists\' paintings. Abandoning the exclusive use of a network of fine lines which subsequently was outdone by the screen of the phototype, the woodengraver now turns to apply and make the most of black and white patches and surfaces and their contrasting effect to shape the graphic masses. The softness and succulence of the line cut into the wood-plate now again serves pure artistic purposes, as it had done in the past. The particular medium is well suited to attain a great power of expression. In Latvia the beginnings of wood-engraving, coincide with the development\'of Latvian press in the second half of the 19th century. Our pioneer in this domain was Augusts Daugulis (1830—1899), who began by engraving the headings for the "Peterburgas Avize"t a well-known newspaper of the period of the Latvian national rise, and its supplement "Zobugals " t and making woodcut portraits, being one of the most popular wood-engravers in Petersburg at that time. Subsequently, Ernests S tenders (1893—1919), impressionist, worked in this medium too, woodengraving was also practised by the cartoonist Indrikis Z c be r i n s (born 1882) and Janis S p r i n g i s , but they applied this technique as a means of reproduction rather than as a particular branch of art. The actual unfolding of Latvian xylography took place in a very brief space, during the years of independent Latvia, towards the end of the twenties of this century. The results obtained reached such a high standard of quality and diversity that the works of this branch of Latvian art soon were able to rank with the best wood-engravings abroad. A decisive share in the evolution of Latvian xylography belongs to Romans Suta\'s studio of art. Not being a wood-engraver himself, he knew how to initiate his students into the secrets of composition. Amongst his students was Nikolajs Puzirevskis (bornlß9s), the oldest representative of Latvian wood-cutters of the living generation, he too an exile in Germany now, and a former pupil of the German artist Emil Orlik and the Russian Nikolai Masjutin who was teacher of wood-engraving in Suta\'s studio. In his landsapes Puzirevskis still clings to realism, concentrating his entire attention on the contrast of black and white, and emphasising form with primitive sharp-cut patches. His numerous bookplates show him to be a very able composer. He particularly delights in historical and exotic topics. Next by age comes Aleksandrs Junkers (born 1899), another of our very capable wood-engravers. He worked, in 1928, in the studio of Mr. Suta, devoting, however, most of his time to self-training and studying the graphic works of Eastern European artists. The style of his engravings is characterised by a rustic gravity and form of expression. Corot and Van-Gogh are his favourite painters. Exhibiting his works abroad, Junkers is among the first to carry the art of Latvian woodengraving outside the borders of his country. Illustrations from Pushkin\'s "The Queen of Spades" – woodcut by O. Noritis In 1933 Junkers jointly with Puzirevskis and Sterns shows his engravings at the First Exhibition of Wood-Engravings in Warsaw. In 1937 he takes part, with Plepis, in the Second Exhibition there, and, in the same year, he presents his woodcuts with those of Plepis at the International Exhibition of Wood-Engravings and Lithographs in Chicago, organised by the Chicago Institute of Art, where, once more, at the7thShow he exhibits his engravings, already after the outbreak of the Second World War. However they were not Junkers\' separate graphic prints — mostly landscapes — which brought success and recognition to him, but his book-illustrations. In 1942 Junkers illustrated Edvarts Virza\'s prose poem "Straumeni", which he accomplished with extraordinary success He succeeded in attaining a certain robust vitality in depicting the scenes of rustic life and farmwork. By presenting his figures and architecture in sombre patches and by placing them obliquely and at different perspective angles towards each other, he obtains strongly contrasting effects, whereas much tenderness and poetic feeling is expressed where idyllic natural scenes are rendered by means of white contouring lines very sparingly used on a dark background. His apex Junkers reaches in 1942 when his woodcuts for "Straumeni\' appear ir\\ a separate portfolio, and a series of town views and scenes from the fishermen\'s life. In 1944 Junkers added new laurels to his fame when he exhibited at the First Latvian Exhibition of Wood-Engravings, Riga. Having turned from his former rather flat and decorative style to serious studies of form, he now concentrated upon expressing relief, depht of space, and on giving a purely picturesque rendering of still-life ("Flowers", "Fish") in a rich manner of surface treatment. Oskars Nori ti s, a highly versatile and talented graphic artist, who died at an early age, was the artistic manager of the "Zelta Abele" publishing company which had quite an important part, since 1935, in the development of the Latvian book-art and book-trade. Noritis was a very capable woodengraver and showed in his vignettes for Pushkin s "Pika dama" ("The Queen of Spades") a smart interpretation of period style. In a similar manner combination of woodcut vignettes with illustrations in other graphic techniques was very deftly applied by Voldemars Xra st in s ("Daphnis and Chloe") After Noritis\' death Janis Plepis (born 1910) was the most representative artist who associated with the "Zelta Abele.". He, too, was a pupil of Suta\'s and began exhibiting in 1930. At first he took a fancy to lino-cutting. In 1936 Plepis found his vocation when the "Zelta Abele" publishing office engaged him to prepare a series of woodengravings for their publications. Very subtle and delicately composed are his figures and the historical colouring of the illustrations for Ed. Virza\'s poem "Hercogs Jekabt" ("Duke James"). Plepis woodcuts for the literary works of A. Grins are his most interesting works, because his ever-growing virtuosity in shaping and moulding bodies here meets with a very expressive way of handling line and form. He very ingeniously contrives to obtain the strongest Illustration – woodcut by J. Plepis contrast between dark spots and hatched surfaces of an extreme delicacy. The movements of his animals are strongly dynamic. The horse is his favourite animal. Wonderfully expressive are his woodcuts of wild beasts for A. Kivi\'s novel \'The Seven Brothers". In a later stage Plepis reaches in his engravings a perfection in decorative and ornamental technique not easily surpassed by anyone. To this period belong his woodcuts illustrating the miniature pocket-edition of Virza\'s Straumeni". Contrary to his former soft though sappy contour line he now" strives towards a geometrically accurate drawing and not unfrequently applies a "gloss" to his contrasting ornamental cut. He delights in representing the sun and the starred skies, obtaining a wonderful luminosity, and he is very scrupulous about the composition of his drawing. Pauls Sterns (born 1901), yet another modern representative of the elder generation of Latvian wood-engravers, who more or less sticked to the traditional technique of the twenties, has produced portraits and heads, still-lives and factory scenes in his conventional manner. Peteris U p i t i s was the first wood-engraver of flowers in Latvia. He had a way of intermingling black and white surfaces in his still-lives that resulted in a tremulous brilliancy which helped to set out the variegation of the blossoms. This effect was obtained by his treating the wood-plate in a certain way with casein before working on it with his tools. The landscapes cut by Margers V i to 1 i n s , who received the Culture Fund Prize of 1943, are rich in impressions and show a good technique. Perhaps the most powerful talent among our youngest generation of xylographers is Olgerts A be I i t c, he too a pupil of Suta\'s. He is a restless and versatile spirit with a liking for the plastic qualities of the human body and an affinity to the vigorous breath of the baroque art. His Illustration from Ed. Virza\'s "Hercogs Jekabs" – woodcut by J. Plepis favourite artist is Agostino Carracci, but he is equally fond of the French modernists. About 1930 Abelite establishes his reputation with his largestyle etchings showing a classical approach, which are of unusual expressiveness due to a certain disproportion of his figures. Later on he takes an interest in lithography, but gains his popularity and becomes widely known by his woodengravings. The lithographs and wood-engraved vignettes for Cervantes\' "Sample Stories" admirably convey a true flavour of Spanish vigour of movement, types and scenery in a Renaissance atmosphere. Abelite\'s next chefs-d\'o-euvre were his wood-engravings for Ilona Leimane\'s novel Tilkacu mantiniece" ("The Heiress of Vilkaci"), where the spirit of a true ethnographic Latvian milieu is rendered with a dynamic display of his flourishing personal style. Of particular interest are the frontispieces, exuberant in their baroque conception, and some powerful figure groups in the vignettes. Abelite very cleverly makes use of different technical tricks such as the flourishing lines in the vignettes of his cocks and jugs, resembling brush-strokes. His six illustrations of O. Liepins\' short stories "Ilozainais smaids" ("The Roseate Smile") 1943, are real masterpieces where he gives very subtly rendered glimpses of ancient Florence and a series of decadent types of modem society in a brilliant technique. Very rich and reminding of classical etchings are his elaborate initials for this collection of stories. His figures, as a rule, are very tall and show elegance of line. In 1944 Abelite takes part in the First Show of Latvian Wood-Engraving with a series of largesized prints. There we find his wonderfully impressive "Still-Life" which shows some very rare ways of handling the wooden printing plate, and his bold, distorted "Nude", remarkable for the contrasting of the tender shading of the flesh with the vigorous drawing of the hand. There we hit upon his "Flowers" so rich in shades, his "S. Peter\'s", reminding us of Renaissance masters, the exceedingly dynamic "Horseman", the sombre "Girl\'s Head", and his spirited selfportrait. That Abelite has not yet reached the apex in his artistic development is evident by his subsequent illustrations, where he seems to abandon his picturesque style to return to a comparatively primitive, Masareel-like manner. If, in conclusion, it comes to summing up the characteristics of Latvian wood-engraving, one can state in the first place that the Latvian woodengraver fully realises the possibilities offered by this medium. He tries to make the most of the values of the black-and-white surfaces, he uses the soft line without imitating pen-drawings. Ingenuous and inexhaustible are the ways he exercises, in order to obtain a balance of surfaces, to express the play of rhythm by means of demi-shades and lines of different quality. Generally, the Latvian wood-engraver very rarely faces Nature with the eyes of a naive realist, but delights in composition. In this latter respect our wood-cutters differ from those of our neighbouring nations. As to national and psychological qualities emanating from Latvian woodcuts, mention must be made in the first place of the Latvian vitality, a rustic healthiness and a general cheerfulness which are reflected in the works of our woodengravers. Dynamic of movement, fulness of form, and richness of ornament are the visible artistic results of those qualities. Further, the Latvian Illustration from J. Leimane\'s "Vilkacu mantiniece" – woodcut by O. Abelite wood-cutter goes in for accuracy in shaping, sciupulousness of finish, and a sincerity and sensibility of line. It is with exuberant joy that the Latvian graphic artist realises and dives into the beauties of his country\'s Nature, glorifying and giving his artistic interpretation to the peculiar traits of her scenery, her animals, her people. Translated by Harolds Kundzins.', 'LATVIS A GROUP OF LATVIAN PAINTERS AND SCULPTORS IN CANADA Established in 1954, the Latvis society of painters and sculptors has 21 members and 13 associated members. The center of activities is Toronto, where annual group showings take place, but membership extends as far as Montreal and even Halifax, Nova Scotia. In Spring, 1964, a large exhibition was held in Montreal with a total of 103 works, and this showing, held at The Gemst Gallery, won unanimous praise from the critics. What is rewarding to the spectator . visiting a group showing of the "Latvis" painters and sculptors is the discovery of some common, basic underlying art truth. It seems as if at some levdl of inspiration or moment of creation, there is a universal acknowledgement of similar ideas. This common frame of reference may be the "social subconscious", as Jung describes it, since all the members were born in Latvia and came to Canada as political refugees. Such generalizations, however, are not useful in trying to denote a common quality of art form, which pervades the canvasses, bronze, stone, ceramics, wood, prints, watercolors and drawings. As if stimulated by a similar experience the works have a cohesive thread, a sense of common reverberation in the act of seeing. The artists know their craft and have mastered their techniques. What is also meaningful to the critical observer is a feeling of communication: the work of art is not only the product of the artistic process — but also speaks to the spectator, reveals new, unseen worlds, sometimes mythical, strange and personal. In the paintings the polychromatic colors are strong and lively; they sparkle in a variety of compositions, textures and glazes. They speak of clarity and truth. The discoveries, feelings and sensations of the artist are communicated to the spectator, who will not be shocked or stare in horror, but, on the contrary, will be greeted and drawn closer. This amiable quality of restrained, lyrical coloring is an element often forgotten today, when the acrobatics with a paint brush or palette knife display a sardonic countenance. The artistic quality of the "Latvis" group of painters and sculptors, when seen as a whole, constitutes a totally congruent collection of works of art and as critic Raymond Heard (Montreal Star, Montreal) noted: "I found the standard of work surprisingly high." Other critics attempted to use a criterion of style and as Carol Chapman (Globe and Mail, Toronto) puts it — "some (artists of the group) are primarily stylists, some haven\'t attained this, others transcend it." A less significant and rather heteroclite attribute is the sign of an "ebullient extrovert" attitude that, according to one critic, "puckishly peeks through." (D. Pfeiffer, The Gazette, Mont.). This curious observation reflects the philosophical attitude of an artist, who is not swept along the path of modern extremes, even if these happen to be the slogan of the day. With the "Pop art" high on a pedestal, the apostles of anti-art are undermining the few remnants of artists\' craft, as if expressing a complete degeneration of art as a skill and profession. Such spasmodic outbursts in the name of "dynamic expression" are not the trademark of the "Latvis" artists. It may be due to their inheritance of ideals and ideas practiced in Latvia that there is a universal respect for materials, techniques, style and composition. The modern trends in Latvian aft at the turn of the century saw the advent of post-impressionism, futurism, influences of the French symbolists as opposed to EDUARDS DZENIS, born in Latvia, has travelled to Belgium, France, Germany and Sweden. He loves to depict swift movement of forms, penetration of light and perhaps, most convincingly, stylized horses. By using a variety of techniques of which a mixed dry point is most original, he engraves a flowing, decisive line that explores the possibilities of depth. In addition, mottled areas increase the surface quality and provide a rhythmic cadence. A nonchalant, stylized expression is based on a thorough understanding of oils, tempera and emulsions. JĀNIS ZUNTAKS, born in 1906 and once Head of a Department of the Art College in Riga, works alia prima and with a polychromatic palette depicts landscapes — snows, trees, skies. His work is characterized by a tonal perspective, harmony and contrast. His most recent paintings show a distinct transfiguration and new harmonies. Swift in execution he produces pulsating and vibrant oils that are always in demand by the collectors. the Russian school of realism (Narodniki). Following the First World War, painters J. Kazaks and J. Grosvalds were the leaders of the new Parisian school of cubism, expressionism, but in a typical Latvian manner, avoided calculated uncertainty and non-motivated deformity that marked the "fin de siecle" of the postimpressionism era. The trends of constructivism, neoplatiscism and purism were experienced, modified, and often discarded as they tended to be flat, poster-like and imitated mechanical models. The years during the twenties and thirties saw a gradual consolidation and sobriety, avoiding surrealism and nonobjective experimentation. Such general background was experienced by the older members of the group when they received their training at the Academy of Fine Arts in Riga, Latvia. ARNOLDS NULITIS, has exhibited in Germany, France and Holland; he also belongs to the Colour and Form Society of Artists in Toronto. His everchanging apnroach explores abstract, non-objective compositions that change from angular, gothic, contrasting lines to frottage-like experiments of texture. In "the Nets" he depicts an interplay of geometric forms, well-balanced and intelligible. ZIGFRĪDS JURŠEVSKIS, b. in 1910, once headed the Department of Ceramics at the Art College in Latvia. His stylized, monumental clay figures are plastic, clearly defined. Using a unique technique of sculpting clay on a turntable, he achieves geometric clarity and symbolic meaning, but there is also an earthly, jocund softening of what otherwise might have been classical severity. PĒTERIS POLNA, b. in 1912 sculpts and carves in several media. His "Old Troll", a bronze, with an elongated, simplified torso reminds one of Don Quixote — it is a languid figure with a definitely literary connotation. HUGO MERCS, b. in Riga, exhibits floating, polished sculpture in wood. VALIJA MĒRNIEKS, has just returned from Paris. On her canvasses she clearly defines areas and subjects them to a massive composition, rationally constructed. Her careful delineation of geometric forms speaks of vigor and solidity, which is sometimes modulated by grisaille-like effects. LILIJA TREIMANE, has her ceramics studio in Val David, Quebec. She has exhibited framed terra cotta plaques, utilizing glazes over a surface indented with lines. EDGARS KRŪMIŅŠ, b. in 1922, earns his living as a television scenic designer in Toronto. His paintings and, even more so, his watercolors manifest dreamlike apparitions, often kaleidoscopic glimpses of city roofs, birds and sail boats. A poetic, transparent, delicate coloring sifts through an extremely personal landscape. His imagery is simple, but not overt and bombastic. In contrary — his lyrical restraint and deeply felt decorative elements provide a fascinating paradox. VISVALDIS REINHOLDS, b. in 1924, has built his studio on high land overlooking a deep cataract, known as the Devil\'s Glen, near Duntroon, Ontario. In his secretly placed studio, surrounded by dense bushes, he works on his non-objective canvasses, using imprimatura, followed by broad impasto strokes. His art transcends style, his colors speak of Northern lights with an unusual luminosity and reflection. An architectonic clarity defines the composition, sometimes quite severe and yet simple. Rectangular areas of thick color are unmistakenly placed with precision. R. does not have a high output: his work seems to be completely conceived in advance, after a period of meditation. A new concept of space encompasses his canvasses and he transforms shapes into structures that often seem as reflections of another world. ĒRIKS DZENIS, b. in 1925, can be envied for his immaculate draftsmanship. Endowed with an ability to do an effortless rendering, he takes particular interest in the human, figure in motion. Swift in execution, his pastels display an instantaneous vision precise and balanced. Today, when the ability to draw accurately is gradually disappearing, his drawings are a pure delight. ALFRĒDS BRŪVERIS, b. in 1922, is a painter for whom expression is a necessity. flii nervous, abrupt, coarsely laid brush strokes speak of anguish and sometimes of sheer force. KARL LANGENFELDS, b. in 1925, is employed as a scenic artist for television. He likes to portray objects of genre: still lives, clowns, owls, devoting his interest to textures, tonal contrasts in terms of the spectrum. His style is planimetric and his knowledge of materials methodically proper. The theatrical element is interwoven with his rendition of living objects. An element of "joie de vivre" permeates his visual transcriptions. DAINIS, b. in 1929, who for simplicity signs only his first name, is an instructor at the Ontario College of Art, in Toronto. His complicated techniques of watercolors take him to secluded regions of Northern Ontario, where he executes a formidable number of watercolors in a month\'s time. His romanticism evokes nature as an essence for existence and for this reason his pathetic orchestrations of nature are really metaphorical allusions about the grandeur of this world. His approach is direct, intuitive, without premeditated composition or rationalization. His method, using the wet technique, requires absolute sureness of execution — no corrections are possible afterwards. ILGVARS ŠTEINS, b. in 1925, lives in Halifax, Nova Scotia, where he is employed as a TV artist. His graphic art, of which multicolor wood cuts are typical, is fascinating. It borders on surrealism, and at times is satirically grotesque. Occasionally he vivisects the human folly and draws demon-like, strange, uncouth creatures that depict the dehumanization of our age. A careful and unceasingly ardent engraver, etcher and draftsman, he has his favorite imagery that he has mastered to the greatest detail. His knowledge of anatomy of figures and animals is invariably erudite, technically without any flaws. His greatest talent is his everchanging invention of new subject matter: it has a life of its own. ĢIRTS TILTIŅŠ, b. in 1935, shows a tonal, plastic quality but without reverberating nature forms into a "grande paysage". In contrary — he reduces nature to basic nomenclature: a branch, a leaf, a stem; these constitute a tree. His portraits are solidly constructed without chiaroscuro effects, sometimes thinly painted. His showing at the Roberts Gallery, in Toronto, prompted one critic to say that he has the technique "to make his love contagious in conservative realism." GUNTA UPĪTE, b. in 1933, prefers delicate, restrained coloring that finds its best application in genre, and still life. AGRIS ANŠMITS, b. in 1938, young and talented, paints impressionistic landscapes using a heavily loaded brush. He achieves a thick impasto, vibrating texture that saturates his canvasses with a heavy atmosphere. He likes a variety of greens, whites and some of his landscapes are reminiscent of the Dutch masters. A. PUĶĪTE is an able watercolorist, K. KRONBERGS shows balanced composition and luminosity, A. KALNIŅŠ paints stylized still lives, L. KALNIŅŠ has exhibited landscapes, A. KERANS has been noted for his miniature plastic sculpture, A. JAUNZEMS has exhibited concentrated, dense renderings in tempera. It is impossible to account for every artist in the group, since new painters and sculptors annually join the Latvis society of artists. Canadian art today, with few exceptions, is greatly influenced by U.S. art, and the new "formulas" are eagerly imitated. One only has to attend an annual exhibition or see the works of the graduating class of the Ontario Art College to confirm this view. In contrast, the exhibition of the Latvis society of painters and sculptors, when viewed as a whole, is un-Canadian in the sense that the general outlook does not reflect the "New York" or "Chicago" schools. This difference has been noted by several critics, and one is almost sure that we are looking at a European standard of art for what it is worth. Standing from left: P. Polna, A. Kerans, J.
