Kronbergs — "Sun In The Forest" — Oil', 'Janis Silins The purpose of this article is to give a concise characteristic of the development of Latvian art during the latest period, i. c. beginning with the eighties of the last century up to the present time. During these sixty years Latvian culture as a whole, and painting, sculpture and the graphic arts as essential branches of it, succeeded in shaping independent national features of their own and in fitting in to the common pace of evolution of the spiritual life in Europe. The making and development of these arts took a length of time, and they have a history of their own. * Coming from the West, the official period styles in art, from the Romanesque style onwatjd, including the rich unfolding of the baroque and ncoclassic art, used to reach Latvia, with some retardation. In ecclesiastic and profane architecture, in sculpture and painting, the forms of Western Europe acquired, in our country, a certain local colouring which corresponded to the feeling and mentality of the Latvian people At the same time when foreign masters, hailing usually from Germany, but since the Renaissance period also from Sweden, Poland and other Western countries, continued to come to Latvia, local schools and art centres were gradually developing, particularly m the Duchy of Kurzeme (Courland) (1561—1795) and in Riga. Wars and fires have destroyed countless monuments of Latvian art and for the same reason literary data about our early artists are scarce and scanty. Nevertheless, from the 15th century onward, but particularly in the 18th century, traces and indications of Latvian artists and craftsmen are numerous. Besides the period art which had some influence on the development of the Latvian nation, the importance of popular art must be stressed as having been far greater. The roots of it go back as far as pre-historic art tradition exists. This rich source of artistic inspiration has not dried up yet in spite of the ever growing strength of the forms of modern civilised life. We have but to mention the rural architecture of Latvia, so expressive in its simplicity and matter-of-course objectivity, the everyday Latvian household furniture and equipment: the earthenware and working implements, the rich textile fabrics, brilliant in hues and abundant in their peculiar national ornamentation and design, and the silver-wrought jewelry. The Latvian surface ornament discloses in its strictly geometrical design, fine colour scheme and rhythm of the separate elements, a world of its own, differing from both Slavic and Scandinavian ornamental design. It is this tradition which, rooted in and grown out of the life of the people, consciously or subconsciously inspires the present artists\' work and serves as a starting point to modern applied arts and crafts. The same close community exists between the works of the modern THE NEW LATVIAN ART Latvian painter and sculptor and the manifestations of Latvian popular art. They have both one\' root in common: the peculiar Latvian conception and aspect of life and life\'s phenomena, formed by the fundamental structure of the Latvian soul. Let us try briefly to characterise the spiritual trend of the Latvian artist and its expansion in terms of form, by defining the common and unifying elements which despite all idiosyncrasies permeate the artist\'s personal style. We are aware, . in the mentality of the artist, of two contrarious elements which are welded together in a queer way. One is his rustic proximity to the soil, to the world of realities. He never loses touch with life, even if he is carried away by the images of his dreams or by abstract form. But once adrift outside his own country the Latvian artist will invariably get uprooted, lose his artistic sap, and wither away. The tendency for realism interlaces the whole of our art. It accounts for a certain heaviness evident in the works of Latvian art. But it is this unsophisticated approach towards the natural phenomena and life\'s events which enables the Latvian artist to view and interpret them in that warm light of intimate simplicity and sincerity which prevails in the works of our best masters. But there is yet another spiritual attitude which has contributed to the forming of the character of Latvian art. Besides the ability for treating and artistically expressing life from a realistic angle there is a strong propensity conceivable for a soaring of the artistic mind into the realms of transcendental and visionary ideas. This specific northern quality of plunging into meditative absorption, of intensifying the expression of sentiment, of conceiving the monumental entity of Nature lends a note of exceptionality to the artist\'s images, of everyday subjects and sights. This peculiar dualism in Latvian art is most characteristic of some of our poets, too (Rainis, Poruks, Skalbe), who like to interweave reality with threads of their artistic imagination. To the Latvian artist life means a full-sounding reality, and, at the same time, a symbol. Under such an angle the peculiarities of form in Latvian art are more easily comprehended- The Latvian sculptor simplifies the forms of Nature. Heavy and massive, as the solids of K. Zale\'s "Dying Horseman" \'in the Warriors\' Cemetery, Riga, and the figures and heads of T. Zalkalns\'s Latvian Mothers appear, they represent a fusion of symbolic simplicity and realistic form. The rustic in the painter and sculptor tends towards- creating and shaping compact, clear and simple forms, a tendency typical also of the Latvian national ornament and rural timber architecture. A constituent principle in Latvian sculpture and painting is the characteristic rhythm of forms, line and surfaces, binding together the contrasting elements. The Latvian painter has a positive liking for diffused outdoor light which seems to penetrate the bodies and emanate from them, enwrapping everything in. a soft silvery twilight, thus creating that lyrical mood so frequently hinted at in the folk-songs whenever the "silvery grove" is mentioned. This does not, however, imply that Latvian painting is lacking the shine of bright, unmitigated sunlight which, breaking forth, touches and lights up the forms in glowing yellow and warm pink and reddish hues. Let us now turn from these general reflections on our artists\' mentality to the historical outline of the development of Latvian art. In the 19th Christ on the Cross, by J. Rozentals The Jelgava Market, by J. Valters Vernal Waters, by V. Purvitis century Latvian art was strongly affected by the social  which took place in Latvia as well as elsewhere in Europe. The culture of the courts and privileged nobility circles which had reached a climax in the Duchy of Kurzeme (Courland) came to an end, and the middle-classes began to stress their claims and rights. The prevailing cultural forms gradually changed in expression according to a new conception of life. In this period of transition the native artists, living in narrow, provincial circumstances, had to fight hard to subsist, and, as often as not, the more talented went west or east to make their way there, rather than put up with the narrow-minded and artistically uninterested society at home. As long as the thirties of the last century, when a neo-classic tendency in art was prevalent, Jelgava, the principal town of the province of Kurzeme, had the lead in artistic life. Towards the forties it shifts to Riga, the metropolis, where a more realistic view, tinged by romanticism, begins to develop. The middle of the last century produced a number of master-painters of a romantic-realistic trend who gained recognition both in Western Europe and in the Russian capital. It is interesting to note that while pursuing their studies in the Academy of Fine Arts in Petersburg they were oriented towards Western European art. Of the most important painters of that period ought to be mentioned Karlis Huns (1831—1877), a painter of historical subjects and portraits, and Julijs Fedders (1838—1909), landscapist. After graduating from the Petersburg The Mower, by T. Uders Academy with the Large Gold Medal, Huns visited the art centres of Europe in 1863, including England, and settled down in Paris where he became influenced by the art of Delaroche and Descamps, and soon drew attention by his historical paintings representing scenes of St. Bartholomew Massacre. In his "Old Catholic Warrior" who tries with clumsy fingers to fix the sign of the White Cross on to his casque, Huns combines an accurate draughtsmanship with colour effects. The realistic way in which he presents the figure, interior and accessories, are typical of the taste of late romanticism. Huns\'s works appeared on view both in the Paris saloons and in Vienna and at international shows in London. Some were acquired by the art museums of Petersburg and Moscow. However the most touching are his intimate sketches depicting the simple Latvian peasant taking his meal, or folkfestivals and market scenes. At the age of forty Huns receiv;d a professorship at the Petersburg Academy of Fine Arts. Julijs Fedders, then a student of the Academy, was strongly influenced by the Barbison group. In his paintings an atmosphere of romance, silvery-green hues and blue and violet-hazed distances prevail in his landscapes representing the scenery of his country: the picturesque valley of the river Gauja, the banks of the river Daugava, the lonely parish cemeteries. His style is realistic, but blended with a poetical interpretation of Nature. Incidentally, the paintings of Fedders met with success at the International Art Exhibition, London, 1874, and they are to be found in several museums in Russia (Petersburg, Moscow, Odessa). Some decades had to elapse before a new enthusiastic generation rose to create modern Latvian art. From the seventies onward the spiritual life of the Latvians developed at a vigorous pace despite the resistance and opposition met on the part of the minority of the German inhabitants. A drift of Latvian country people into the towns, especially Riga, set in. They hoped to secure an economic footing there. This rush to the towns resulted in the development of an independent. Latvian urban culture. A new approach to art opened wider aspects, and promised richer experiences to the young artists, mostly peasant youths eager with an intrinsic desire for developing their artistic abilities. They would leave their farmsteads in order to go to Petersburg to get their education there. The majority of the Latvian painters of that period were born in the seventies. Fostering the desire for an art of their own they developed their national mind and enthusiastic spirit in a luring light of national romanticism which swept Latvian literature at that time and tinged in a peculiar way the spiritual life of the Latvians. Petersburg at that period witnessed a heyday of art. A host of talented artists, unsatisfied with the routine of a stagnating academic naturalism, were striving after fresh impulses and new ways of artistic interpretation and were ready to submit to the influences of Western European art. Art exhibitions of the most notable masters of the Western and Scandinavian countries took place in Petersburg, and it was particularly the young Finnish art which showed signs of a peculiar national vigour. In the nineties a national romanticism, led by such eminent painters as Zorn, Berg, Edelfelt, Jarnefeld and Gallen-Kallela, was prevalent in Swedish and Finnish art and proved that even such small nations as the Finns were Smokers, by J. Kazaks able through their art to gain world fame. All this was apt to inspire the young Latvian artists, fill them with enthusiasm and hopes, and strenghthen their belief in the future of their country\'s art They turned wholeheartedly to studying and de picting their country, its scenery, its popular types, its rural life, scenes of the glorious struggles for freedom in the past, and of fabulous motifs. The father and ideological leader of this movement was Adams Alksnis who was born in 1864 and died prematurely (1897). He emphasised the importance of national topics in painting. In his drawings and water-colours Alksnis shows the toiling Latvian peasant, one of his favourite motifs being the ploughman and the wood-cutter who on a winter morning, with his shouldered axe, is crossing the snow-covered fields on his way to the forests. In these pictures the human figures melt into one with the milieu, embodying the virtue and will to work of the Latvian peasant. Alksnis also likes to dwell on the raids by sea of the ancient Cours (kursi), the struggle for freedom against the Teutonic Order, he even rambles occasionally into the world of fairy-tales, depicting a rather goodnatured and silly sort of devil. Unfortunately, most of Alksnis\'s work remained unfinished. His contemporary, Richards Zarrins (1869—1939) was a • known graphic artist, sometime Head of Department at the Imperial Russian Mint and Bank-Note Printing Office, Petersburg, afterwards professor at the Latvian Academy of Fine Arts, Riga His favourite media were the engraving-plate, the lithographic stone, and charcoal. Basically romantic, he discloses the fairy-world of the Latvian folklore, visualising the mysterious dusk of wood From the Oriental Cycle, by J. Grosvalds thickets, the abode of witches, hobgoblins and bogies, where Kurbads, the hero, subdues the nineheaded devil and accomplishes his numerous feats. Among the Latvian artists Alksnis and Zarrins enjoy the reputation of national romanticists of local significance. Another group of that generation of artists is represented by a number of painters, whose artistic maturity grants them a place on a level with other masters of European fame and standing. Three of them. Janis Rozentals (1866—1917), Vilhelms Purvitis (1879—1945), and Janis Valters (1869—1932), were graduates of the Petersburg Academy of Fine Arts. Coming of indigent country families, the first two had, during their early age, to suffer life\'s hardships, but possessing true Latvian perseverance and toughness, they managed to push their way and succeeded in attaining their aim. Endowed with rare impulses, they stirred the social circles of their time by the freshness of their artistic temperament and by the sureness of their ideas. Their style of painting is a realistic and decorative impressionism. In the later decades of their development Valters and Purvitis turned to a more expressive and free manner in painting, characterised by a crescendo of decorative colour effects. Exuberant in creative impulses and spirit was the talent of Janis Rozentals, painter of genre and portraitist. At the so-called Spring Exhibitions in Petersburg, where young artists used to make their debut, he" drew attention by his refined colour harmonies and the lyrical atmosphere of his paintings. Jointly with Purvitis, they were, for several years successively, appointIlze, by V. Tone Ed members of the Committee of Jurors of that exhibition. The evolution of Rozentals\'s art was determined by Finnish, not Russian painting. land became his second home-country. He frequently sojourned there, and there he died too. Rozentals excelled in painting scenes from the life of the Latvian farm-people, giving visions, in scales of silvery greenish, pink and bluish hues, of the atmosphere of sunlit summer Sundays, with the country people lingering between whiles, after service, in front of the church and in the churchyard. He has produced quite a gallery of rural types\', presenting them, full of warm sentiment, in a romantic atmosphere. His genre has become classic and left a lasting imprint on Latvian art. Rozentals\'s figures are for the. most part taken from among the peasantry and reflect the rhythm immanent in the soul of the rustic Latvian and the serene sincerity of his life. Rozentals\'s portraits are alive with an expression of deep feeling which communicates itself in a direct way to the looker The artist had a wonderful ability to com bine the bodily reality and psychological likeness ofc his model with a highly expressive and decorative colourfulness. Rozentals has also painted quite a number of fine landscapes and several altar-pieces which have become widely popular. The works of Vilhelms Purvitis, one of the most remarkable landscape-painters in Europe of his time, are to be found in nearly all the large* art museums of Europe and in many private collections (in London, Paris, Bruxelles, Leningrad, Moscow, Stockholm, Oslo, Malmo etc)- Under the influence of his masterful pencil and highly Mother and Child, by O. Skulme artistic personality several generations of Latvian landscape-painters have developed and worked out their style, creating a "Purvitis school" in painting. The son of a miller, Purvitis studied painting in the Petersburg Academy of Fine Arts\' in the studio of Kooindzhi, and in 1897 got the Rome Prize for his competition work "The Last Rays". Very soon the \' young painter gained a reputation in Western Europe. His landscapes were awarded first prizes at the World Exhibition in Paris (1898), in Paris in 1900, gold medals at Munich (1901) and Lyons, with Cross of Honour (1902) for his "Northern Night" and "Day in March." In 1905 the "Studio" published an article about him. In 1913 the Petersburg Academy of Fine Arts conferred on him the title of Acade mician for his outstanding merits as an artist. Purvitis began in a plein-air manner of. painting, turning afterwards to a decorative impressionism and creating a peculiar style of his own of a synthetic comprehension of Nature. Latvia\'s scenery acquires in his perception a solemn sublimity, and the most commonplace object: a clump of birchtrees, the bank of a brook, a snow-covered stretch of land, seen with his eyes and interpreted by his brush, reveal the deeper essence of Irbene Lane, by K. Ubans Nature\'s life Purvitis\'s melting snow and vernal waters, and particularly his birch-groves, are wide ly known as some of his most favourite and finest topics. So are his stretches of hilly countryside alternating with brook valleys dwindling out of sight towards far distances and still chequered here and there with patches of half-molten snow; or river-banks and meadows covered with thawing snow and ice glowing in the slanting rays of the afternoon sun in March; or lowlands flooded by water-courses overflowing their banks; or, again, a cluster of white-trunkcd and purple-topped birchtrees set off against the deep limpidness of a blue vernal sky — each motif a glorious hymn to the majesty of Nature\'s revival. To his subtle composition of lines and surfaces the master adds a luminous colouring. In the last decades of his lifetime Purvitis sought for less conventional landscape motifs, which he interpreted in an uncommonly vigorous and dynamic style. As a pedagogue Purvitis held the post of director of the Riga Municipal School of Art from 1909 to 1915 and became the head of the\' Studio of Landscape Painting at the Latvian State Academy of Fine Arts in Riga from 1920 to 1934. Under his efficient guidance a great number of young Latvian, Estonian and Lithuanian painters received their artistic training. janis Valters, a contemporary of Rozentals and Purvitis, was an exceedingly sensitive master of hues. He exhibited in Petersburg, Munich, Berlin. Impressionistic in treating light and colour problems and in composing form, Valters imbued his genre- paintings, portraits and landscapes with much intimate feeling, half-suggested im – pressions and an undertone of colours. We find among his genre- paintings a market scene, rope-makers, sheep-shearing, bathing boys on a sunlit bank, and interiors in subdued harmonies with delicate female figures reading, playing music or lost in reverie in a dusky room. Valterss artistic conception is more akin to the contemporaneous French and English art of painting. His excellent portraits are of a refined colouring and admirably convey that sensitive and soulful atmoAt the Seaside, by A. Annuss sphere in which his models were living. A certain boldness which he sometimes knows to restrain, coupled with a charming poetic feeling, is evident in his treatment of the glitter of the March sun, the play and reflexion of light on shallow waters, or when he paints the soft greenish trunks and orange foliage of autumnal trees. Valters spent the last decades of his lifetime abroad where he began to appreciate expressionismTwo main directions in conceiving and giving shape to form were prevalent among the national romanticists: the one — a lyric and intimate style, the other — dramatic, agitated and rather harsh Rozentals and Valters are the most conspicuous representatives of the first style of painting- The latter is best perceived in the works of Teodors Uders (1868—1915) who was an artist of very strong individuality. His expressive charcoal-drawings mostly treat types of fishermen and peas ants. The figures of his mowers, boat-builders, young fisher-women are monumental,\' full of elementary vitality, and seething with passion. Being a philosophical-minded symbolist, Uders views life as a process of motion, as an unceasing struggle. His types, genuinely Latvian, one feels, are imbued with the tragicalness of life. His figures are earth horn, rustic people who are suffering under the yoke of Inexorable Fate. Uders discloses in his drawings a great deal of his personal religious credo, in depicting sufferings and the. solitary and forsaken figure of Christ on the Cross. The generation of national remanticists was followed after a short interval by a number of young Latvian painters born around 1880. Though still impeded by the adverse attitude of the Germans in the Baltic, Latvian art gradually gained ground, developing a new multiformity and activity. 1910 saw the first show of paintings by Latvian artists in Riga on a more extensive basis. It was followed soon after by three more exhibitions of paintings and one of graphic arts which gave an opportunity to the rising talents to make their public appearance. Some of them, like Janis In the Field, by K. Miesnieks Roberts Tilbergs (born 1880) and Aleksandrs Romans (1879—1911), continued to move along the paths of academic tradition. Tilbergs, a well-known portraitist, attains a pleasing outward arrangement in composition by a refined flow of line and grouping of surfaces and succeeds in enhancing the attractiveness of the persons he portrayed. In his style of painting he is rather close to some English portraitists, for instance Sir John Layer. Grand in conception is Tilbergs\'s portrait of Rainis which shows the great Latvian poet as a solitary walker, resting in a meditative attitude on a hill-top. Romans is rather mournful and dreamy in his moonshine landscapes and white horses by the riverside, and sensitive, discriminating and delicate in colouring in his portraits. The works of the rest of the contemporaneous painters more or less reflect the restlessness of the life of that time upset by the revolution of 1905 which in art gave rise to a wave of mysticism and decadence. This unrest particularly characterises the "Bohemians" of that period, of whom Voldemars Zeltins (1879—1909) with his agitated colour scales is perhaps the most inciting. Another kind of artistic temperament is represented by the artist-dreamer who seeks to retire from reality to a visionary realm of imaginary beauty. Rudolfs Perle (1875—1917), a, water-colour painter, is one of them. He displays in his watercolours a colourful phantasmagoria of roses, lilies, fairy-tale rocks and castles. The Paris studies by Peters Kr as tin\'s (born 1879) reveal influences of the French "1 c s fau v c s." A melancholy mood permeates his misty Latvian bogs and delicately framed female figures. Voldemars Matvejs (1877 -1914) was the first to introduce the aspirations of expressive modernism to our art of painting. Archaic in manner; he used dim and transparent colours to evoke visions of the Latvian folklore: youths on horseback on their way to the rose-coloured castle of Romance. The beauty of bygone times is sump tuously represented in Janis Kuga\'s (born 1878) painted scenery for Rainiss and Aspazija\'s dramatic plays. His stage-decorations initiate a new phase in the development of the Latvian theatre Mythological Painting by A. Cirulis Fairy-Tale illustration, by N. Strunke During the years of Latvia\'s independence he greatly contributed to the enrichment of the stage productions of the Riga National Theatre and Opera by creating a series of stage-decorations, and conducted the Studio of Decorative Painting at the Latvian State Academy of Fine Arts, Riga, holding the office of Rector since 1934. Professor Kuga is also a landscape-painter who looks for his favourite motifs on the rocky banks of the Daugave which disclose vistas of far, violet-hazed distances. The first world war marked the beginning of a new period in the development of Latvian art. A young generation, born about 1890, which had declared war to the academic naturalism and pro claimed the ideas of post-impressionistic modernism, was in the making. It was yet a period of fermentation for them, with the striving after new means of expression, a focussing of their creative minds on pure artistic values, spiritual experience and a free display of imagination rather than on the reflexion of realities. These young artists oriented towards modern French painting and were passionate in defending the right of unimpeded creation. During the war many of them were fighting for Latvia\'s independence, alternately using their rifle and their pencil to prove their idealism and enthusiasm, and their belief in the future of their nation, thus providing strong impulses to the growing new art. Two artists who died prematurely clearly reflect in their works the spirit and atmosphere of this period of fighting and the manner of their early efforts in expressionistic painting: Jazeps Grosvalds (1891—1920) and Jekabs Kazaks (1895 to 1920). Grosvalds introduced new thematic problems to Latvian art. With a taste for monumentality, he depicted the hardships and sufferings of the Latvian refugees in simplified synthetic forms, as, for instance, the greyhaired Latvian peasant, huge, imperturbable in his spirit, looming on a background of houses on fire, and consoling a boy who, frightened, is clinging to his side. Seeing the events with the eyes of a true artist and being a soldier himself, Grosvalds reproduces the glorious fighting of the Latvian Riflemen. As an officer of the British expeditionary forces Grosvalds travels through Mesopotamia and the Iran, and it is there that he creates his magnificent Oriental Cycle. Kazaks, who died at the age of 25, is more expressive and feverish in his paintings of the life of refugees and soldiers. In the last years of his lifetime he turned to study colouristic problems. During the two decades of independence Latvia\'s art experienced a rise unknown before. The newly Etching by V. Krastins founded Academy of Fine Arts in Riga gave the future artists an opportunity of moulding their artistic faculties in their own country, thus laying the foundations for the development of independent traditions in Latvian art. The number of active Latvian artists may be estimated to some 60 at the outset of the first world war, this number having increased to about 500 in 1940. Exhibiting abroad, our most proficient masters obtained notable successes at Helsinki, Tallinn, Warsaw, Cracow (1936), Vienna, Budapest, Prague, Kaunas (1937), Copenc hague (1939), and Paris and London (1939). Their works were acquired by foreign art museums. In a comparatively short time the new Latvian art went through its stage of pioneering, and joined the living art of the senior European nations. To go into details of the complex process of the making of Latvian art would mean to exceed the compass and scope of this survey. A few most characteristic examples will therefore do to illustrate this period of Latvian aitf. 1920—1924 marked the period of a general enthusiasm for abstract constructive art. The Latvian master-artists, however, being firmly rooted in their native soil, did not become wholly engulfed by purely formal experimentation, nor did they get lost in the void of abstractions. In 1924 Latvian art veered towards post-expressionistic realism. The interpretation of reality became the task of the artist. The significance of personality and the elaboration of mastery grew essential. Konrads Übans (born 1893) is an impressive painter of lyrical, intimate landscapes. Refined colouring and an elaborate technique qualify him as one of our best modern landscapists. He excells in the dreamy silence of Latvian country-side scenery, the provincial towns and outskirts. Like Übans, Voldemars Tone (born 1892), too, has a troop of enthusiastic disciples among the younger generation of artists. Tone is a portraitist who delicately shapes his models, dipping them into a visionary atmosphere of subdued deep brown and grey shades, contrasted and brightened up by yellow and red accents. His female figures and heads, merging into shadowy forms, are filled with a hushed melancholy. If Übans and Tone represent the more lyrical mood among the younger painters, Leo Svemps (born 1897) and Janis Licpins (born 1893) go in for an expressive dramatic and dynamic style. Both have common lines of contact with Vlaminck and the modern, sensually tense, Belgian painters. In his landscapes and flower-pieces Svemps indulges in a display of shining colours and picturesque Wood-Cut by A. Junkers strokes. In just the same way*he handles his portraits. Liepins is harsher, more melancholy, when depicting the poise and heaviness of his peasants or potato-diggers, or in his still lives. Somewhat in the tradition of Belgian art are the paintings of the romantic and phantastic Janis Tidema ni s (born 1897). If Tidemanis is led by his instinct, Ludolfs Liberts (born 1895) is pushed by his will which sets the aims to his romantic temperament. Liberts is a versatile and productive master of the younger generation. He is equally successful in painting Latvian scenery, German landscapes and Paris boulevards at night with a brilliant skill. Lately he has turned to portraying. Liberts is a well-known painter of stage scenes, and in his decorative settings for the operatic and ballet stagings in Riga and abroad he has developed a style of his own. Liberts has exhibited in Paris, Bruxelles, Berlin and Stockholm, and won a gold medal at Barcelona and a Grand Prix in Paris (1937). Quite a number of his paintings are to be found in foreign museums and collections. In the genre field Karlis Miesnieks (born 1888) and Augusts Annuss (born 1893) have contributed a number of large compositional paintings. Miesnieks, a painter of an unsophisticated, rustic mind, sincere and observant, favours in his works scenes from the Life of farmworkers and peasants. He is also quite a good portraitist; he represents his landscapes in a condensed manner, very often in the colourful and warm red glow of the setting sun. Annuss likes to paint healthy fishermen\'s wives in hues permeated with light, or farmers and their folks taking their afternoon meal, and the sinewy and sturdy bodies of longshoremen. There is quite a lot of delicate lyricism in his painting and a tendency to the essential and monumental in his composition. Looking at the world with the eyes of a child is Ansis Cirulis (1883—1943) who shows in an amusingly simple and idyllic style decorative images and the noble beauty of Latvian Tore. Then there areErasts S veics(born 1895), Janis Cielavs (born 1890) and Otto Skulme (born 1889) – all mature painters and artistic personages representing a lyrical and sensitive style in art. From the young generation quite a lot have become known as talented and very promising painters, amongst them, just to mention a few, the genrist Fridrich s Milts (born 1906), the landscape-painter Eduard Kalnins (born 1904), Janis Gailis (born 1903) and the scene-painter Peteris Rozlapa (born 1906). An unfolding of the graphic arts was evident particularly in the last decade of Latvia\'s independence when our graphic artists specialised in the different media and techniques of engraving, Sepulchral Monument, by T. Zalkalns etching, xylography, lino-cut, lithography, and pen and chalk-drawing, attaining notable results. Two outstanding figures amongst them are S i g i s – munds Vidbergs (born 1890) and Nik lavs Strunke (born 1893), the first — an accomplished aesthete and especially skilled in pen-andink drawing, the latter — known as an illustrator of books, and graphic composer excelling in images of the Latvian past and lore. Worth mentioning are also the graphic artists Francis Bange (born 1895), etcher, and Karlis Krauze and Janis Sternberg s. In the province of sculptural art Latvia boasts achievements in both monumental and decorative sculpture, which perpetuate the memory of the dead fighters for Latvia\'s independence (the Warriors\' Cemetery, Riga) and embody the idea of Freedom (the Monument of Liberty, Riga). Teodors Zalkalns (born 1876) and Burkards D z c n i s (born 1879) are the veterans amongst our sculptors. Zalkalns has developed along independent lines, working synthetically and simplifying form, and always respecting the intrinsic qualities of his medium. His silent, intiovertive Latvian mothers have been modelled in general, compact forms which are monumental, but do not lack a note of intimacy. Simplicity and a peculiar rhythm mark his statue of Rudolfs Blaumanis, and his sepulchral monument for the poet Rainis. Both Zalkalns and Dzenis have worked in Paris in Rodin\'s studio. Dying Horsemen, by K. Zale Dzenis is the author of many a portrait bust, realistic and expressive of an inner life, as well as of several memorial sculptures. The mOst notable representative of monumental sculptors is Karlis Zale (1888—1942). In collaboration with several architects he created the sculptural part of the Warriors\' Cemetry and the Monument of Liberty in Riga. His work is a hymn to and a glorification of heroism. He possessed a style of his own, grand and strongly rhythmical, but not devoid of a certain feeling of romance. His national symbols: the Ancient Horsemen, which silently guard the entrance gates to the Warriors\' Cemetery, the Dying Horseman, the statue of Mother Latvia — are full of an archaic solemnity. In his Monument of Liberty in Riga Zale perpetuated the symbolic figures of the Chain-Breakers, tearing asunder the fetters of serfdom, the Lacplesis (Bear-Killer), the ancient Player of the Cither (kokle), and the Statue of Liberty. Zalkalns and Zale have provided strong impulses for the development of Latvian sculptural art. Of the younger sculptors Emils Melderis (born 1889), Karlis Zemdega (born 1894) and Karlis Jansons (born 18%) in a rather short time succeeded in attaining, in their art, a level of European standards. Translated by Harolds Kundzins']

Representative_Docs: ['In Commemoration of Latvia\'s Independence Day November 18, 1918 On November 18th, thirty-four years will have passed since the day when the Latvian people proclaimed their independence and established the Republic of Latvia. This event is solemnly commemorated by Latvian exiles in emigration and is remembered with silent devotion by the people in Sovietenslaved Latvia. The memory of Latvia\'s independence is a source of endurance and a ray of hope for the return of freedom, for which the entire Latvian nation is today united with the free world. The Latvian people, and their Lithuanian and Estonian neighbors, have inhabited their present territories on the eastern shores of the Baltic Sea from a time iunmemorial. Because of their georgraphical position exposing them east and west to the unruly energies of their expansive neighbors, they have suffered frequent invasions and periods of foreign domination. Conditions in Latvia and Estonia, which countries were from the 13th to the second half of the 16th century parts of the Livonian confederation, were in some respects comparable to the situation in Britain after the Norman invasion. Lithuania\'s destiny, linked since the 14th century with Poland because of a personal union, took a different course, until its loss of independence following Poland\'s partition. At the close of the 18th century the Baltic nations had been brought entirely under Russian rule. But they held on to their language and ancient customs and never merged with their alien overlords into a new nation. Russian domination of the Baltic countries brought the farmers under extreme servitude and strained to the breaking point the antagonism of the people against their alien rulers. Finally Russia\'s liberalizing Czar Alexandr I restored personal freedom to the farmers of Latvia and Estonia in 1817-1819. But Czar Alexandr II enacted in 1861 the liberation of the peasants in all Russia. Even though spiritually the Baltic nations had never been completely subjected by foreign domination, the abolishment of agrarian serfdom gave them powerful impetus for the advancement of their social, economic and national aspirations. The First World War, with German invasion, inflicted much suffering on Latvia and caused a great exodus of refugees and evacuees to Russia of whom i many never returned. In Febru- i ary 1917 revolution broke out in Russia, but on November 7 the Bolshevik Leninn regime ascended to power as a result of ■ a coup d\'etat against the demo- ļ cratic government headed by Kerensky. With Russia\'s military resistance unreparably collapsed, Lenin signed the Brest-Litovsk peace treaty with Germany, by which the Baltic countries were detached from Russia. The Baltic nations were faced with the alternatives of being absorbed in the whirlpool of i Soviet communism or German imperialism unless they could establish their own independent statehood. When Germany\'s attempts to incorporate them in the Reich were frustrated by its defeat in the West, the time was at hand for the Baltic countries to achieve complete independence. i On November 18, 1918, a Latvian National Assembly convened in Riga and solemnly proclaimed the independence of the Republic of Latvia. However, having renounced Brest-Litovsk following Germany\'s acceptance of the Armistice on November 11, the Soviets invaded the Baltic republics which had yet been unable to orgaize effective military resistance. Although most of Latvia had been occupied for half a year, the Red forceš regime signed a peace treaty with Latvia on August 11, 1920. Article II of this treaty contains the following provisions: By virtue of the principles proclaimed by the Federal Russian Socialist Republic of Soviets which establish the right of self-determination for all nations, even to total secession from the empire with which they have been incorporated, and in view of the desire expressed by the Latvian people to lead an independent national existence, Russia unreservedly recognizes the independence, self-government and sovereignty of the Latvian republic and voluntarily and forever renounces all sovereign rights over the Latvian people and territory which formerly belonged to Russia. Self-determination of nations, a principle advocated by President Wilson at the close of World War 1, was not only introduced in the covenant of the League of Nations, but it was also upheld by the Lenin regime. It is, as a matter of fact, incorporated in the Stalin Constitution of 1935, Article 17, of which states: "To every Union Republic is reserved the right to secede freely from the USSR." As early as 1913 Leninn had A TYPICAL WINTER SCENERY OF LATVIA branded Russia "a prison of nations." Since the dominant Great Russians constitute only 43 per cent, of the Empire\'s population, he reasoned that the Bolshevik Party must exploit the nationalistic sentiment of the non-Russian peoples to bring about a social revolution. This he recognized without limitations the right of all nations to self-determination — even to complete secession. The adoption of Lenin\'s theses secured for the Bolshevik Party the support of great numbers of non-Russian soldiers and workers and with their assistance it gained political control of Russia in November 1917. Unfortunately for the non-Russian peoples, Stalin was then Peoples Commissar for Nationality Affairs. When rather than the anticipated world revolution Russia was plunged into civil war, no less than eleven European and Asiatic nations declared their independenc from Russia. Stalin\'s reaction to this development was marked by contempt to the principles inherent in his office. At. the Convention of all-Russian Soviets held in January 1919, he succeeded in gaining acceptance of his thesis that questions of self-determination of nations are to be determined solely by the Soviets in the interests of proletarian class struggle. By this motion the right to self-determination of non-Russian peoples was subjected to the arbitrary interpretation of the Moscow dictatorship and thus completely deprived of its essence. Having overruled Lenin, Stalin emerged from the convention with vastly increased power, "Workers\' and peasants\'" governments of the seceding nations were formed in Moscow and Red troops were dispatched to reconquer them. Stalin took personal charge of the conquest oi his homeland, Georgia, where he acted with exceptional cruelty. However, he failed with Finland, the Baltic States and Poland. When in 1923 Lenin had passed away after long illness and infirmity, Stalin seized command of the Communist Party and Soviet Government. Having mummified and sanctified Lenin, and after having ousted his chief rival, Trotzky, he promoted himself to the position of Lenin\'s uncontested heir and the undis• puted "idol" of the Soviet people. In all this he effectively utilized the apparatus of the NKVD, which holds the key to his power. Stalin also succeeded in the creation of a powerful war\' machine which enabled him to win World War 11 with Western assistance and to enslave subsequently millions of previously free people. This raises the question whether the system which he created is going to prevail over so-called capitalism which, in spite of its shortcomings, leaves a wide scope for personal freedom, enterprise and human progress. In order to form an objective opinion, it is useful also to consult ideologies which deal with the subject of Stalin\'s own grounds. In a recently published article, Milovan Djilas, a leading theoretician on Marxism and a member of Yugoslavia\'s politbureau, calls Stalin "stupid" and ignorant in matters concerning Marxism. He draws the conclusion that the Soviet system has lost all its advantages over contemporary capitalism because it has deserted socialism for state capitalism and has devised the most cruel form of labor exploitation. To conceal its weakness, Soviet propaganda resorts to the obvious method of blackening Russia\'s capitalist rivals. Stalin and his associates ignore any change in the world of free enterprise for fear that any objective comparison would make it too obvious that Stalin\'s "socialism" is the most reactionary system to be found today. The first two years following (he declaration of Latvia\'s independence were marked by invasion and war, death and insecurity. Kārlis Ulmanis, Latj via\'s first Prime Minister who I guided the nation through this I stormy period, referred to it on occasion of the Republic\'s tenth anniversary as follows: Most of us still remember the deplorable state of affairs in our country at the end of 1918, caused by a great war, the revolution and German occupation. The people had neither bread nor security, and they were harrassed by the soldiery, the Bolsheviks, or common marauders. Under such circumstances they naturally looked for protection from the government of their own young republic. However, the government at first had neither funds nor real authority because the power still rested in the hands of a foreign occupation regime. All the government could depend upon was the justice of its cause and its impertaurbable faith in the people\'s will to become free and independent. At the time these words were written, the Latvian Republic j was nationally and internation-I ally already standing on jinn I foundations. The majority of the people were well-satisfied and proud of being politically inde-I oendent. Reconstruction of war devastation had been completed. A sweeping agrarian reform had eliminated the power of landlords, who formerly had owned 48 per cent, of the land, and it provided more than a hundred thousand new settlers with land for tillage. The reform blunted the edge of communist subversion and it stimulated agricul-I tural production to yield a growing surplus for export. Latvia\'s i independence was recognized by the entire civilized world, and the creative genius of the nation was boosted to unprecedented heights in all branches of industry, science, literature and the arts. The democratic Constitution adopted by the Constituent Assembly in 1922 may be called a model of liberalism. However, its electoral franchise, devised during a period of genuine repulsion against autocracy, tilted to the opposite extreme to promote dispersal of political representation in a polyarchic Saeima, thus undermining government authority. With more than twenty parties and shades of opinion represented in Latvia\'s house of representatives, several times a year one or two members came to hold the balance in the formation or overthrow of coalition cabinets. When the world wide economic crisis of the thirties, and the augmenting menace of tolitarian aggression from East and West clamped down on Latvia\'s economic life and political security, Prime Minister Llmanis dissolved the Saeima and established in May 1934 as an emergency measure his authoritarian regime. Nevertheless, the Constitution remained legally in force. Ulmanis, who later became President, was successful in restoring prosperity and a favorable foreign trade balance, and in promoting\' reasonable unity of purpose in national life. It cannot be denied that Ulmanis played a most priminent part in Latvia\'s political life throughout its independent existence. But it was beyond his power to prevent the catastrophy of Soviet aggression and occupation of Latvia in 1940. He himself was deported to Russia and nothing is known of his fate. When Stalin and Hitler concluded their treaty of friendship and non-aggression in August, 1939, which set off World War 11., the Baltic nations at first hoped that their neutrality would be respected by the contracting parties. They had then no knowledge of the secret clauses in the agreement which delivered the Baltic countries and Eastern Poland into the sphere of Soviet imperialism. After Poland had been divided between the two aggressors, Moscow imposed in September and October of 1939 treaties of "mutual assistance" on Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania by which the Soviets gained military bases on Baltic territory. The Kremlin, however, pledged itself not to interfere in Baltic interior affairs. Barely nine months later the Baltic states were suddenly and without the slightest provocation on their part occupied by overwhelming Red forces. As soon as the Soviet occupation regime and its political police had gained full control of Latvia, the now universally known Andrei Vihsinsky arrived from Moscow and took charge of preliminaries for its annexation. A "people\'s cabinet" of Soviet puppets was installed and it imposed on the stunned population a few weeks later Soviet-style "elections" with only a single pro-communist slate of candidates presented to the electorate. This fraudulent election farce was on August 5, 1941, • used by the Ail-Union Supreme Soviet assembled in Moscow as a pretext to "accept" Latvia into the USSR. Identical procedures were jused to annex Estonia and Lithuania. The United States Government imediately expressed its condemnation of the devious processes perpetrated by the Kremlin to absorb the Baltic countries. In his declaration of July 23, 1941, Under Secretary of State, Sumner Welles, having outlined his Government\'s principles and reasons for opposing Soviet military intervention and anticipated annexation of the Baltic Republics, concludes :— The United States will continue to stand by these principles, because of the conviction of the American people that unless the doctrine in which these principles are inherent once again governs the relations between nations, the rule of reason, of justice and of law— in other words, the basis of modern civilisation itself—cannot be preserved. Great Britain and the other western democracies adopted thereafter an essentially similar attitude, and today the continued legal independence of the Baltic Republics is generally recognised by the free world. Since, as is shown above, the principle of selfdetermination of nations has never been theoretically contested even by Stalin, it is clear that Soviet claims to sovereignity in the Baltics are based solely on brutal force and are, therefore, null and void in terms of international law. On this consideration, and the fact that the divided world cannot forever remain under the tension of the present cold war, hinges the hope of the Latvian people that freedom must and will be generally restored and consequently also to them — unless the entire globe falls under the sway of total communist tyranny, as has already happened to one third of humanity.', 'K. Zariņš, Latvian Minister in London. Since 1940 Holder of State Emergency Powers Abroad K. Zariņš, Latvijas sūtnis Londonā. No 1940. g. valsts ārkārtējais pilnvarnieks ārzemēs No 19kO. g. valsts ārkārtējais pilnvarnieks ārzemēs If the name of Latvia is not written on the roll of the states at present belonging to UN, that does not mean that our homeland has ceased to exist forever. It is there, and nobody could tear it away from the shores of our Amber Sea where it will remain for all times to come. If we still have our native land, we have our Latvian people too. At present, the Latvians are perhaps one of the most unfortunate peoples on earth due to the trick fate played them in ancient times settling them on the coast of the Baltic sea between two mighty nations, the Germans and the Russians. On July 28th, 1920, the Secretary of State of USA, Charles E. Hughes, stated that the governments of Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania had been constituted by the original inhabitants of these countries and were supported by them. That was quite true for, in our land, 77 p. c. of the total papulation were Latvians. Many historical relics prove, besides, the fact that they by no means had settled there in recent times, but at least since the bronze age, 1500 years B. C. The Latvians belong to the Baltic group of peoples and are neither German nor Russian kinsmen. They speak their language belonging to the Baltic branch of the Indo-European language family. Many archeologic excavations have amply proved the fact that they had their peculiar culture, technical skill and civilization. In our abundant folk-lore there are many traces of the ancient Indo-European sun myth, the genesis and the pantheistic cult. The ancient Latvians had no idols; they believed in an eternal life after death, and monogamy was a strict rule in their family life. Since times long forgotten the Latvians have diligently tilled their, on the whole rather infertile, land until it yielded satisfactory crops. Their farms were surrounded by orchards and flower beds. They were great bee-keepers, and they knew the art of selecting seeds and medicinal herbs. They cultivated one of the best flax brands in Europe, acclimatized and raised cattle 1918 1947 herds and horses, were smart huntsmen, fishermen and brave seamen. Always they were famous as skilled tradesmen and artisans, as weavers, smiths, ship constructors and carpenters. The Latvians have created their original decorative art with peculiar designs. The Latvian folk-songs are enchanting and melodious, the Latvian dances are full of charm. The Latvians had their musical instruments too: pipes, lutes, horns, flutes, etc. The Latvians together with the other Baltic peoples were already on the best way to found their national kingdom and thus to unite their tribe states when, in the 13th century, their land was invaded by the German colonizers in the disguise of preachers of the Gospel. Those organized several crusades in order to subdue the Latvians and to put a stop to their endeavours to develop their national state. As it was stated by the chronicler Ilenricus, in 1215, Latvia was called Livonia and was composed of Couronia, Lettgallia and the Latvian tribes of Talavia. At the beginning of the 16th century, the Latvians still successfully repelled the attacks of the Muscovites, but shortly afterwards they were weakened by the nobility. Thus, simultaneously with the conquest of "White Ruthenia by Poland, which was united with Lithuania and began to unfold its great power, Livonia was partitioned too. In 1562, the Latvian provinces of Couronia and Semgallia southwards of the river Daugava, which formerly had been tribe states, became duchies under the sovereignty of the Polish king. The territory northwards of the Daugava which was called Livonia proper, was annexed to Poland in 1564. At the beginning of the 17th century this territory was divided between Poland and Sweden. In 1621 Northern Livonia with our capital Riga was annexed to Sweden. Due to the peculiar political circumstances on the shores of the Baltic Sea, a close alliance between J a Latvijas vārds nav to valstu sarakstā, kūpas šodien pieder UN organizācijai, tad tas nenozīmē, ka Latvijas nav. Latvija ir! Latvija kā zeme arī paliks un neviens to nespēs atraut no Dzintarjūras krasta, tū tur paliks mūžīgi. Ja ir Latvija, tad ir arī latvju tauta. Latvija un līdz ar to latvieši, varbūt, šodien ir viena no nelaimīgākām tautām pārējo pasaules tautu vidū tāpēc, ka liktenis viņus sirmā senatnē nometinājis Baltijas jūras krastā, līdz ar ko latviešiem sava dzīve bija jāveido starp divām lielām kaimiņu tautām — vāciešiem un krieviem. 1920. g. 28. jūlijā ASV ārlietu ministrs Ch. E. Hjūgs konstatēja, ka Latvijas. Igaunijas un Lietuvas valdības ir šo zemju iedzīvotāju sastādītas un atbalstītas. Viņam bija taisnība, jo latvieši savā zemē bija 77% no visa zemes iedzīvotāju skaita. Pēc ticamiem zinātniskiem datiem, latvieši šajā zemē nav šīsdienas iedzīvotāji, bet savas līdzšinējās dzīves vietas apdzīvo vismaz kopš bronzas laikmeta (1500. g. pr. Kr.). Pēc izcelšanās latvieši ir balti un nepieder ne ģeriņūou, ne slavu tautu grupai. Latvieši runā valodu, kas iedalīta šatemu nozarojumu baltu zarā indoeiropiešu valodu saimē. Kā to pierāda zinātniskie izrakumi, latviešiem bija sava īpatnēja kultūra, technika un civilizācija. Latviešu bagātā folklora • saglabājusi seno indoeiropiešu saules mītu, pasaules radīšanas mācību un panteistisko dievticību. Senie latvji nekalpoja elkiem, tie ticēja dzīvībai pēc nāves un viņiem bija viensievu ģimenes. Kopš neatminamiem* laikiem latvji čakli kopuši savu nabadzīgo zemi, kuru tie padarīja auglīgu. Viņu saimniecības ir augļu dārzu un puķu dobju ieslēgtas. Latvieši pazina biškopību, viņi pazina sēklu un medicīnisko augu selekcijas mākslu, viņi audzēja vienu no labākām linu šķirnēm Eiropā, aklimatizēja uin audzēja ganāmpulkus un zirgus, bija labi medinieki, zvejnieki un drošsirdīgi jūrnieki. Latvieši vienmēr bija pazīstami kā veikli amatnieki, audēji, kalēji, kuģu būvētāji un galdnieki. Latvieši radījuši savu dekoratīvo mākslu ar īpatnām iezīmēm. Latvju tautasdziesmas ir apburošas un melodiskas, latvju dejas — grā1947 A. Bīlmanis, Latvian Minister in U.S., Deputy Holder of State Emergency Powers Abroad A. Bīlmanis, Latvijas sūtnis ASV, valsts ārkārtējā pilnvarnieka vietnieks ārzemēs cijas pilnas. Latvjiem bija arī savi mūzikas instrumenti: dodas, kokles, ragi, stabules v. c. Latvieši līdz ar pārējām Baltijas tautām bija jau ceļā radīt nacionālu karaļvalsti, tā sakausēt savas cilšu valstis lielākā vienībā, kad 13. g. s. latviešiem uzbruka vācu kolonizatori, slēpdamies evaņģēlija sludinātāju maskās, organizējot vairākus krusta kara gājienus, lai iekarotu latviešus un tādā veidā pārtrauktu latviešu nacionālo valsts izveidošanas procesu. Ap 1215. g., kā to konstatē chroniķis Indriķis, Latvija bija pazīstama kā Livonija un ietvēra sevī Kurzemi, Letgaliju un Tālavas latviešu ciltis. 16. g. s. sākumā Livonija sekmīgi atvairīja maskaviešu uzbrukumu, bet drīz to vājināja muižniecība. Tai pašā laikā, kad Polija — kas savienībā ar Lietuvu bija savu spēku atraisīšanas stadijā, sāka iekarot Baltrutēnijas grāfistes, arī Livoniju sadalīja. Latvijas apgabali Kurzeme un Zemgale, kas atradās dienvidos no Daugavas un viduslaikos bija cilšu valsts, 1562. ig. kļuva hercogistes, pakļautas Polijas karalim.
