H w« now cast a retrospective glance on the period previous to this occurrence, it seems clear that, in March, 1939, already, our situation was in a sense similar to that we lived through in 1918, when Russia ceded Latvia to the German sphere of influence in accordance with the treaty of Brest-Litovsk. On March 28th, 1939, the Latvian Minister for Foreign Affairs received from the Foreign Commissar of USSR, M. Litvinov, a solemn declaration, where. USSR anew acknowledged the Latvian-USSR Peace Treaty, the Briand-Kellogg Non-Agression Pact, and their obligations in respect to the League of Nations. In consequence of this declaration the Latvians, at least for the time being, dared to look into their future with some amount of confidence. They could not but believe that USSR, by this declaration, would continue to demonstrate by deed too, that they were willing to acknowledge the existence of Latvia as a politically and economically wholly independent state. This state of affairs took a quite unexpected turn when, in May, 1939, the Foreign Commissar Litvinov who was friendly disposed towards the Latvians resigned, and V. M. Molotov took over his duties. Already in the summer of 1939, during the discussions held between V. M. Molotov and the British emissaries, yon Ribbentrop visited Moscow. At present we know that the Moscow diplomats demanded free hand in their political relations with the Baltic republics. Hitler complied with those wishes of USSR; he bartered away what was not his, as it was said by Lord Halifax in a speech on December sth, 1939. The solemnly declared neutrality policy of the Baltic states was of no avail to protect them against the pressure of their mighty neighbour, in spite of the principles declared by USSR in March, 1939. USSR demanded lo lease them naval and land bases and to station Soviet garrisons in our country. The Baltic states, among them Latvia, were isolated and their position rather difficult, although on September 4th, 1939, the German ambassador, yon Kotze, declared that he considered the non-agression treaty with Germany, concluded on June 6th, 1936, as the basis of the German-Latvian relations, and that Germany had concluded no agreement with the Soviet Union contrary to this treaty. At present this declaration seems to be no more than a devious and smart statement. Be that as it may, on June 17 th, 1940, the Soviet armies entered Latvia, and soon afterwards it was annexed by USSR. In January, 1941, Germany and USSR concluded a frontier agreement, thus assenting to the incorporation of the Baltic states into the Soviet Union, and the Latvians were under the rule of the Slav people again. The Roman Code establishes the principle „Volenti non fit imperia", but nobody could provide real proofs that the Latvian people had voluntarily acquiesced to the dictatorship of the Soviet Union proletariat. This dictatorship under the guidance of the Communist party, the various acts of nationalisation, etc, not only inflicted upon the Latvians terrible material losses and degradations, but cost them many lives, for more than 36,000 Latvians — men, women and children — were deported to USSR. For those who remained, life became more and more intolerable with every coming day. On June 21st, 1941, Germany attacked USSR and, in quite a short time, occupied Latvia. The independence of the Latvian state was not restored by them. Instead of that, they incorporated our land into the occupied „Ostland" territory. Streams of blood and persecutions which were directed particularly against the Latvian citizens of Jewish origin, followed, and the Latvians were under the yoke of a foreign power again. In 1944 the Red Army entered Latvia as „liberators". Disregarding the various declarations and resolutions passed at\'several conferences of the Allies between 1941 and 1944, the dictatorship of the proletariat and the rule of a single party was established anew in our land. As a result of those quite undeserved misfortunes, two considerable parts were split from the. Latvian nation; one of these parts was deported to USSR proper, the other scattered throughout Western Europe. We Latvians can, therefore, testify before the whole world that we are staying in Western Europe contrary to our wishes, and that the burden\' of supporting us now would never had fallen on anybody, if our big neighbours had respected our sovereignty and had not deprived us of our right to live peacefully in our country, as the Belgians, Danes, Dutch and other small nations in Western Europe can do., But, if we had the misfortune, contrary to our wishes, ro become DPs, i. c. refugees without elementary human rights in the eyes of the world, we at least may ask, for how long will this last. Perhaps nobody who did not get through all above said, might fully understand and grasp our feelings and state of minds. Our present life in the camps is in no way comparable with our former life at home we were accustomed to, it is no more than a drab and gloomy improvisation. We did hope that, after the weapons- were laid down, our sufferings and losses " would be mitigated by effective international laws which would guarantee, to big and to small peoples alike, sovereignty, jiistice, lasting peace and, most of all, freedom from fear and want. It would seem that now, after the principal agressor — Germany — is disarmed, there could be no reason whatever for the Latvians who, due to their religious and political convictions, are not willing to return to their homeland, to lead this improvised life for more than two years already when, at the same time, working hands\'are lacking in several7countries. We have almost lost our hope that the rights put down in the Atlantic Charter might he put into effect in the near future, although there is declared that „ ... they desire to see no territorial changes that do not accord with the freely expressed wishes of the peoples concerned; they respect the right of all peoples to choose the form of government under which they will live; and they wish to see sovereign rights and selfgovernment restored to those who have been forcibly deprived of them; they hope to see established a peace which will afford to all nations the means of dwelling in safety within thejr own boundaries, and which will afford assurance that all the men in all the lands may live in freedom from fear anA want." At the Yalta Conference, oh February 12 th, 1945, President Franklin D. Roosevelt, Premier Winston Churchill and Marshall Stalin declared anew their adherence to the Atlantic Charter and to the Declaration by the United Nations pledging themselves to cooperate with all peace loving peoples to ensure a world order in which peace, security, freedom and universal well-being would be established. There scarcely could, be any doubt whatever that the Latvians belong to those peoples who became victims of agression. This, by the way, is more or less expressly established in the statement of Undersecretary of "State, Mr. Sumner Welles, released tq the Press on July 23-rd, 1940, that ,/... the political independence and territorial integrity of the three small Baltic republics — Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania — were to be deliberately annihilated by one of their more powerful neighbors." At present our only sincere wish would be that the high moral and political principles laid down in the above mentioned and many other declarations, should be applied to the relations between the nations in a more happy position and our degraded people and, if temporarily no more could be done, at least to the part of it who at present, as emigrants, are practically scattered amongst enemies. We never could be happy again if the words of Winston Churchill — that there would be no sense to try the\' Hitlerites for their crimes, if no law and justice would reign again, and if totalitarian and police regimes would replace the German agressors — should come true. Our daily life would become unthinkable if it should last forever in its present manner, if we should be deprived of humane warmth, compassion and the right and opportunity to work productively. We patiently have waited and expected that the public mind of the world would not deny us this right, we feel that we cannot and would not accept our old age dole forever, we most urgently wish to build up our life anew. If we may not do this in our homeland, vve, at least, expect that we should not be compelled to do this on the ruins of Germany. I Our determination, our energy, our joy of working are not broken yet, and they never will be broken, for they are the essence of our existence. We are quilt\' prepared to emigrate further. We can testify before the whole world that our thoughts and our work will be applied immediately in order to secure our existence and to get along without further help, that we are willing to work amongst every people wrho do not look upon us as on slave laborers, and that we would strive to raise their and our prosperity. We would dedicate all our strength, our knowledge and our thoughts to the high ideals put down in the Atlantic Charter, we would willingly, follow all freedom loving men. We claim only one right — to do all in our might that some day the Latvians could build and live their lives as they were accustomed to do it before in their native land on the shores of the Amber Sea. We are believing Almighty God will not deny us the fulfilment of this our prayer, and that freedom loving mankind would lend us a hand to achieve this. Grfd Bless Latvia. \\ Ed. Raudupe', 'A. CEICHNERS The happy Age of Freedom of the Baltic Peoples * * * Cultural Autonomy of Minorities in Latvia THE HAPPY AGE OF FREEDOM OF THE BALTIC PEOPLES The collapse of Russia and Germany at the close of World War I gave new life to the many peoples of Europe whom they had exploited and surpressed. Among the foremost of these were the long-suffering natives of the three Baltic lands, whose grants of political independence were the costly, but prized culmination of centuries of struggle and strife. At last Latvians, Estonians and Lithuanians could shape their own destiny and solve economic, social and cultural problems from the viewpoint of their own interests. Political independence proved to be the motivating dynamic for twenty years of continuous progress. The economic and cultural forces of the Baltic nations, which had been so long suppressed by dominating foreign peoples, were suddenly released, and the abilities of these hardy people were proven in many different fields. The example of Latvia can show what the Baltic peoples had accomplished during the short period of their political independence. The Soviet troops were turned out of Latvia in the years 1919—1920, and the War of Independence against Germany and Soviet Russia came to an end. In August 1920, a peace treaty was concluded between Soviet Russia and the Republic of Latvia, allowing the new state of Latvia to form and develop its own economy. The economic life in Latvia was based on a modified free enterprise system of private property and private initiative. From the beginning, the state owned only the forests, the railroads, some industrial and commercial enterprises, and some few state banks. The land and all farming were in the possession of free independent farmers. Industrial, handicraft, and commercial enterprises belonged to private persons and Joint Stock companies while the houses in towns were also private property. The new Republic faced very great tasks. Having separated from Russia, Latvia had to base her economic life on other A. CEICHNERS foundations. The industry of Riga, which formerly was producing for Russia, was destroyed in 1915. At that time, all industrial enterprises were evacuated to Russia, at the threat of German troops. All machines, raw materials, as well as the workmen with their families were evacuated to Russia. There remained in Riga only empty factory buildings. The agriculture of Latvia was also devastated by the war : one fourth of the arable land lay untilled; 10% of all farm buildings were entirely destroyed; only about 80% of the pre-war live-stock were left, and that of inferior quality. Likewise the farming equipment was partly destroyed. The population of Latvia had also decreased: in 1920 Latvia had 1,6 million inhabitants, vhile before the war she had about 2,5 million. (In 1925 when the refugees had returned from Russia there were again 1,84 milion.) To restore a healthy, happy and productive Latvia became the challenging aim of every citizen. AGRICULTURE. In spite of the great devastations caused by the war, only some years of hard work by the free Latvian farmers were necessary to change the agricultural productivity. The task of the rural population was very great, because simultaneous with the reconstruction of farms and the raising of production, was enacted a radical agrarian reform. It was necessary to erect a great many new buildings on the old farms, as well as on the new ones which were established according to the Law of Agrarian reform. There were in the year 1920 about 700.000 various farm buildings in Latvia. In the years 1920 to 1937, 440,000 new buildingsdwelling houses, stables, barns, etc. were built; so extensive was the building activity in the country. 70,000 new established farms procured in dependent existence for 300,000 people—2s% of the whole rural population. The break-up of the large landed properties was of great political, social, and economic importance. The social peace was made more secure in Latvia, as the number of landed proprietors became larger, when there were no excessive differences as to the sizes of the real estates, and no more latifundia. Already some years after the beginning of the agrarian reform in Latvia the number of votes polled for the leftist, Marxist parties, diminished compared with the year 1920. when the Law of Agrarian reform had not yet been passed. In Parliament elections in the year 1931, the Communist vote was only but 7% of the total vote cast. As an agrarian country where the great majority of the rural population consisted of independent farm owners and their family members, Latvia had no occasion for developing extreme political trends. The agrarian reform had accentuated the intensive and highly qualitative work of the small farmer and his family members. Through their diligent and careful work, the soil was utilized to a hingher degree. The acreage of arable land in the year 1939 was 1,315,572 acres more, i. c. 30% more, than in 1913. The agricultural production was furthered by the state in different ways: the rural population was diligently trained; agricultural knowledge was propagated by instructive articles in magazines and newspapers, by courses and lectures, radio and books. The general educational level of the population was also raised. Agriculture received state credits, and export of agricultural produce was furthered. All these measures, coupled with the application of the Latvian farmers, who were proprietors of their soil and could freely develop their abilities for their own benefit, had led to a high increase in the agricultural output and raised the export of food and other agricultural products. The total corn crop amounted in the years 1909 to 1913 on the average to 896,500 short tons in a year, but in the years 1934 to 1938 to 1,369,500 short tons, i. c. it increased 50%. The entirely new culture of beetroots was introduced: a purely Post-World War I phenomenon. In the years prior to the second World War the production of beetroots increased to such a degree, that there was no need any more to import sugar from abroad because of the sufficiency of domestic sugar and grain. The sowing acreage was widened and the crops per acre increased. For instance, the average rye crop per acre in the years 1934 to 1938 was 47% more than before the First World War (1909 to 1913). The crop of potatoes increased 63%. Likewise the cattle-breeding was raised: the number of horses increased from 320,000 in the year 1913 to 400,000 in the year 1938, the number of horned cattle—from 912,000 to 1,224,000, etc. The average quality of the live-stock also improved. The result was that the milk production rose from 679,800 short tons in the year 1920/21 to 1,834,300 short tons in the year 1937/38; the meat production from 107,800 short tons in the year 1923/24 to 184,800 short tons in the year 1936/1937. The increase in the agricultural production is all the more remarkable, because at the same time the number of the rural population had remained nearly without any changes (about 1,2 million). In comparison with 1913, the population had even diminished. This indicates that the standard of life of the population in Latvia had risen, the supply of the farmers\' families had become richer and the farmers were able to sell more agricultural products to the urban population and for export. The export of butter rose from zero to 25,802 short tons in 1938. The cooperative dairies widened their activities more and more, so that in 1938 they converted 798,600 short tons of milk, chiefly into export butter. That totals twenty times more milk than in the year 1914, i. c. before the establishment of the Latvian state. Although the crops per acre and yields of milk per cow in Latvia were considerably lower than in Western Europe, they were relatively high in comparison with some East and South European countries, especially with the Soviet Union. For instance, the average corn crop (all sorts of cereals comprised) amounted per acre in the years 1933 to 1938 in the Soviet Union to 4 quarters a year, in Latvia to 5 quarters. So in Latvia 35°/o more corn was harvested per acre than in the Soviet Union. Likewise the crops of potatoes and beetroot in Latvia were 50% higher. The milk output per cow amounted in Latvia in the years 1937 to 1938 on the average to 8 quarters, on the collective farms of Soviet Russia in the year 1938 to 5 quarters, so that in Latvia the milk produce was nearly twice as great as in the Soviet Union. Not only in the quality and productivity of the cattle, but also in the number of the cattle there was great difference between these two countries. For instance, in the year 1938 the number of cattle per 100 inhabitants in Latvia was 61, in the Soviet Union—37. The number of pigs was 41 and 18. Latvia had relatively twice as many cattle as her huge neighbouring country. For comparing the agricultural produce of both the countries it is necessary to calculate the production per head. Shortly before the Second World War, in 1937, the corn crops and beetroot crops in both the countries were per inhabitant the same, the crop of potatoes in Latvia was twice as much. The cattle-breeding shows quite a different picture. In 1938, according to official statistics, the meat production in the Soviet Union was 46 lb, in Latvia 187 lb per head of the population. The milk production 374 lb and 1840 lb respectively. The meat production in Latvia was relatively four times, the milk production five times greater than in the Soviet Union. The chief cause of this tremendous difference was that there in the Soviet Union was no reasonable and productive foundation of the cattle-breeding and agriculture. In the same years when in the Soviet Union forcible collectivization of the farming and destruction of the independent peasantry were carried out, in the small neighbouring country, where the principles of state economy were quite different, the agricultural output rose to a high degree. The farmers of the Soviet Union whose soil and equipment had been expropriated and who were obliged to be only obedient slaves of the Communist party, had no pleasure and no interest in their work, especially in the careful breeding of the live-stock. Quite different were conditions in Latvia where the free-proprietors of their soil could work and produce with full energy and great care, having constant prospect of a good profit. The government of the Republic of Latvia was endeavoring to secure good prices to the farmers for their produce. INDUSTRIALIZATION OF LATVIA. After the evacuation of Riga by the Russian military authorities in 1915, only empty factory buildings remained there. It is estimated that at that time, approximately 30,000 boxcars with goods (machines, raw materials of the industry of Riga), worth 1300 million Lats ($ I.— = Lats 5,18) were carried away to Russia. The number of workmen and their family members, who were evacuated then to Russia, is estimated at about 80,000. Instead of 93,000 workmen employed in the big industry in Latvia in 1910, there were employed only 21,000 in 1920. The evacuated industry machines were never returned from Russia. The former extensive economic relations with Russia were broken off. Nevertheless, new foundations for the development of the Latvian industry were created and new markets for Latvian produce found. Protective duties and other restrictions of import insured the activities of the Latvian industry. The industrial enterprises gradually received large state credits; different foreign investments also helped to increase the industrial output. The most important basis of the Latvian industry was the inner market, chiefly the rural population. The purchasing power of the farmer was strengthened; the continued increase of agricultural production and the income of the farmers had a furthering influence. Also the export of industrial products was furthered by the state creating by it new employment and production possibilities for the industry and the workmen as well. New machines and various materials were imported from abroad. Latvia herself supplied her industry with important raw materials, such as wood materials, agricultural produce, grain, milk, meat, flax, leather, wool, potatoes, beetroot. Being supported by the said measures of economic policy, the number of workmen employed in the industry (in enterprises operated with machines or which had at least 5 workers) rose from 21,000 in the year 1920 to 63,000 in the year 1930, and to 100,000 in the year 1938. In the year just before the World War 11, there were employed in the industry five times more workmen than in 1920, approximately the same number as shortly before the World War I. Considering that the population of Latvia on the eve of the World War II was only 2 million, half a million less than it was before the World War I, it is clear that Latvia in 1938 was more industrialized than in 1915. This was effected despite the fact that commercial relations with Russia, formerly the most important basis of the Latvian industry, had become quite unimportant during the existence of the Republic of Latvia. Not only the number of workmen but also the technical equipment of the factories was considerably enlarged and modernized. For instance, in the year 1922 steam engines and steam turbines were employed in the industry with total 36.000 H.P.; in the year 1938 the number of H.P. was already 141,000. The number of electro-motors rose in the same time from 1246 to 14,733, resp. from total 13,000 H.P. to 107,000 H.P. The largest industry branches in Latvia were the metal industry, food industry, textile industry, and wood working industry. New industry branches came into being, for instance, the production of radio sets (in 1930 in Latvia only 1340 radio sets, in 1930/31—31,987 radio sets were produced), sugar industry etc. More and more cotton textiles, venler, bricks, cement, sugar, etc. were produced. Whereas the population in the years 1925 to 1937 had risen only 7%, the number of persons employed in the industry and trades had increased 65%. The conclusion is that in the Republic of Latvia the industry had very much developed. More and more industrial articles, formerly imported from abroad, were manufactured in the home country, as Latvia became more and more industrialized. BUILDING ACTIVITIES. Lively building activities flourished in Latvia for these 20 years of freedom adn independance. Vestiges of the war in the country were effaced, buildings erected on the devastated and on the new established farms. In the last years before the World War II the government built great power stations and erected Communal buildings. As it was mentioned before, in the years 1920 to 1937 440,000 new houses were built on the farms. In only 10 years (1920 to 1930) 10 million cubic meters of timber were assigned in state forestries to the new farms and to the farms destroyed during the war. This number illustrates the extent of the building activities. In the years 1926 to 1939 the increase of dwelling space in the towns amounted to 58,000 dwelling rooms. In the same period 8,000 buildings for other purposes were erected in the towns. Many new schools were built, as well as government offices, hospitals, sanatoria, railroad stations, postal and telegraph offices. Municipal buildings, bridges, railroads and highways were built. In the last years before the World War II the great power station Kegums was built at the river of Daugava. It was planned to build Kegums with 100,000 H.P. The construction of it was begun in 1936 and the costs of building shortly before the occupation of Latvia by the Soviet Union amounted to about 12 million dollars. The power station Kegums was to further the consumption of electric power in the country, industry, and towns, and to save a considerable quantity of imported coal, as the import of coal was to be reduced. The power of this new station was supposed to be twice as great as that of the power station of Riga, which up to that time was the largest power station in Latvia. In the last years before the World War II many monumental governmental buildings were erected in Riga, for instance the Palace of Justice, the building of the Ministry of Finance, etc. The extensive constructing activities in the last years of the independence of Latvia were characterized by a great increase in the production of construction materials. The production of cement rose from 77,000 short tons in the year 1930, and only 55,000 short tons in 1932 to 170,500 short tons in the year 1938. The consumption of cement was so large that a second cement plant had to be built in the years 1936 to 1938. The production of inland cement was not large enough, therefore, in the year 1938—26,400 short tons cement was imported (net import). The production of bricks rose from 47 million bricks in the year 1930 to 126 million in 1938. The Soviet Five Year plans and building activities in the Soviet Union have been praised by the Communist propaganda and glorified as an economic miracle which has never before occured. The small country of Latvia, however, can match the Soviet Union, and in the production of some building materials even surpass her. For instance, in the year 1938 there were produced per head of the population: cement—in the Soviet Union 75 lbs, in Latvia 162 lbs, bricks 51 and 63, respectively. The production of cement was in the year 1938, and also in many other years, about twice as much as in the Soviet Union. Also the use of timber was lower in the Soviet Union. That indicates that also in Latvia a brisk building activity has existed. FOREIGN TRADE. What have the Baltic states given to the world? What is their importance in the world economy? The rise of the Latvian agricultural and industrial production was connected with the rise of the foreign trade. The total export and import of Latvia in the year 1921 amounted only to 396,000 short tons, in the year 1937 to 3,536,500 short tons. During the existence of the Republic of Latvia a lively exchange of goods developed between Latvia and the rest of the world. Latvia exported agricultural products (about 50% of the total export), wood materials, and some industrial products. The export of butter rose from zero to 25,850 short tons in the year 1938, export of wood materials (beams, planks, etc.) amounted in the year 1937 to 1,760,000 short tons. Considerable quantities timber, paper and pasteboard were exported. Furthermore flax, meat and cattle were exported. Imports consisted of all kinds of raw materials, for the Latvian industry (cotton, wool, rubber, iron, steel, coal, tobacco, etc.), fuel, machines for agriculture and industry, automobiles and various consumers\' goods. Already before the great world economic crisis (1930 to 1933) the foreign trade in Latvia had reached a relatively large extent. The export of goods from Latvia amounted in the year 1930 to $ 25.— per head of the population. In comparison with some other countries that was a big export. The export of many other countries in the same year was much less: export of Poland amounted to $ 9.— per head of the population, of the Soviet Union only $ 3.—, Hungary 19, Spain 20, Portugal 7, Italy 16, Jugoslavia 9, Rumania 10, Bulgaria 8, Greece 12. The export of the other Baltic states was: Estonia $ 24, Lithuania $ 13 per head of the population. The importation of goods into Latvia and the other Baltic states also was larger than in the above mentioned other European states. The following comparison shows what an enormous difference in the foreign trade existed between the huge neighbouring country, the Soviet Union, and the Baltic states. In the year 1938 the Latvian export amounted to 45 million American dollars, the Lithuanian export—4o million dollars and Estonian export—29 million dollars, totaling 114 million dollars. The export of the Soviet Union in the same year was 253 million dollars (reckoning the nominal value of the rouble 0,19 dollar). The small Baltic states whose territory was only 64,478 square miles and total population only 5,6 million had approximately only half as small an export as the huge Soviet Union with an area of 8,360,000 square miles and 170 million inhabitants. The export per head of the population in the Baltic states was about 20 dollars, in the Soviet Union only 1,5 dollars. The same difference existed in the import figures. The importance of the small Baltic states for the world economy was only twice inferior to that of the Soviet Union. The surprising result is partly explicable by the great natural resources of the Soviet Union (many important raw materials need not to be imported and to be paid with the export), but chiefly by the very small productivity and great poverty of this country caused by its peculiar political and economical conditions. The production and export of butter can serve as a small example of the difference existing between the capability of production in the Baltic states and the Soviet Union. In the year 1937 the export of butter in Latvia amounted to 21,132 short tons, in Estonia 14,498 short tons, in Lithuania 16,564 short tons, in the Soviet Union 14,662 short tons. Latvia alone with her 2 million inhabitants had exported considerably more butter than the Soviet Union (in the year 1938 the Latvian export of butter amounted to 25,796 short tons). The total export of butter of the three Baltic states amounted to 52,193 short tons and was four times greater than the export of butter of the Soviet Union. Whereas the Baltic states delivered to foreign countries 19 lbs of butter per head of the population in a year, only 0,2 lbs of butter export per head fell to the population of the Soviet Union, i. c. a 100 times smaller quantity. The three Baltic states were delivering nearly 8 per cent of the world butter export, whereas the Soviet Union only 2.4 per cent. Not only the export but also the production of butter was uncomparably lower in the Soviet Union than in the Baltic states. For instance, the production of butter in Latvia (in the dairies) in the year 1938 amounted to 32,627 short tons, 33 lbs per head of the population. In the same year, in the Soviet Union 217,470 short tons of butter were produced, only a little more than 2 lbs per head of the population. TRANSPORTATION. Also here during the existence of the Republic of Latvia great progress was recorded. The railroad economy which had been destroyed in the war was very much enlarged and improved. 537 miles of new railroad lines were constructed, so that as of January I, 1938, the total length of the railroad lines amounted to 2082 miles. The government spent also large sums for construction of highways. In the year 1919 there were only 6 civil automobiles registered in Latvia, but in the year 1939—6,687. One out of every 298 inhabitants held an automobile. The number of automobiles was not large in comparison with West European countries or America, but at the same time the number of inhabitants to which 1 automobile fell in some other countries was much greater than in Latvia, for instance in Bulgaria— 1484, Yougoslavia—lo34, in Greece—496, in Poland—lo3o, in Rumania—777, in Hungaria—43B, in Estonia—226. In the year 1920 only 10 per cent of the Latvian prewar merchant fleet was left; but already in 1931 the merchant fleet was at 40 per cent larger than before the World War L In the year 1939 Latvia had 73 merchant vessels of total 199,058 gross tons. The merchant fleet of Latvia per head of the population, for example, was twice as large as in Belgium or Germany, much larger than in France, Portugal, Spain etc. The merchant fleet of Estonia was 176,376 gross tons, Lithuania 4,330 gross tons. The total merchant fleet of the three small Baltic states with its approximately 400,000 gross tons was only three times smaller than that of the Soviet Union (1,135,783 gross tons). Also the data pertaining to the development of the post, telephone, telegraph and radio prove the rapid successful economic and cultural progress of Latvia. During the independence of Latvia the number of post offices increased from 298 to 1428, the total length of the telephone lines from only 6214 miles in the year 1920 to 334,921 miles in the year 1939. In Latvia one post office fell to 1,335 inhabitants (in Sweden to 1,455, in Estonia to 1,551, in Finland to 1,054). In Latvia one telephone apparatus fell to 27 inhabitants, in the Soviet Union to 125, in Italy to 62, in Finland to 21, in Belgium to 20 inhabitants. The number of radio subscribers amounted in Latvia in the year 1939 to 140,000. To 14 inhabitants fell one radio set, in comparison with 11 inhabitants in Finland, 40 in Italy, 43 in the Soviet Union, 8 in France etc. FINANCE AND CURRENCY. The peculiarity of Latvia (as well as of the other Baltic states) was sound and well regulated finance. Latvia was one of the first countries which after the World War I introduced the gold standard or returned to a gold standard system. Already from the year 1922 the money unit was the "Lats" which contained 0.0102397 oz of fine gold and was made equal to the Swiss gold franc. The Currency Law of the year 1922 provided that the Bank of Latvia must change banknotes for gold at any time it was asked for by their holders. The rate of exchange of the lat was kept stabilized for many years, even after England had devaluated the pound sterling in September 1931 and many other countries had followed the example of England (in April 1933 the dollar of the USA was also devaluated). Only in September 1936 the lats was detached by a law from the gold and equalized to the Hu of the English pound sterling. The finances of the state in Latvia, upon which the development of the whole economic fife depends, were always in good order. In the budget the receipts and expenditures of the Government were always well balanced, so that the budget usually was closed with a surplus. In the last years about 15 per cent of the total government expenditures were used for the national education; about 13 per cent for the effecting of social legislation, the national defence required 23 per cent. Latvia, as well as the other Baltic states had small public debts. The total internal and foreign debt in the year 1938 amounte only to 15 dollars per head of the population. STANDARD OF LIFE OF THE POPULATION. Consumption of food, clothing, shoes and other goods was considerably raised during the existence of the Republic of Latvia. This is proved by statistics of the produce of crops and live-stock, and also by the amount of import. After restoration of the agriculture, which had been destroyed by the World War I, and the Law of Agrarian Reform having been carried out, the agricultural production rose considerably, especially in the years from 1931 to 1939, the result of which was the qualitative and quantitative increase of food consumption in Latvia. Food consumption (meat, butter etc.) in the last years before the World War II rose just in the needy families, in the families of town workmen, the small employees etc. The consumption of some food stuffs rose considerably also in the farmers\' families, as it is shown by data of the agricultural book-keeping. Regarding the consumption of milk and meat products Latvia took one of the first places in Europe and in the whole world. According to statistics of the League of Nations, the total meat consumption (except poultry and venison) in Latvia in the year 1935/36 amounted to the average of 187 lbs per head of the population, in Australia 200 lbs, in New Zealand 236 lbs, in Belgium 90 lbs, in Denmark 125 lbs, in France 75 lbs, in Great Britain 141 lbs, in the USA 136 lbs (the figures in the before mentioned countries refer to the average of the years 1930 to 1934). Consumption of milk products was at the same time: in Latvia 150 gallons of milk (all dairy products reckoned as milk) per head, in Switzerland 134 gallons, in Denmark 110 , in France 83, in Belgium 90, in Italy 27, in Great Britain 106, in Poland 59, in the USA 97. Particularly great was the difference between Latvia and the Soviet Union. According to official Soviet data the total consumption of milk in the Soviet Union in the year 1938 was 49 gallons of milk, in Latvia 150 gallons per head, meat consumption was 46 lbs and 187 lbs respectively. Although a considerable part of the milk production, in the form of butter, was exported, the consumption of milk and milk products per head in Latvia was three times, and the consumption of meat four times greater than in the Soviet Union. As a further sign of the rise of the standard of life of the population of Latvia the following must be considered: the increase in consumption of clothing and luxuries (tobacco, beer and other drinks), the consumption of electricity; the transportation of passengers by railroads and autobusses which increased considerably; the better housing conditions in towns and in the country. Although Latvia in this respect could not be compared with some Western European countries or America, Latvia surpassed the Soviet Union (and some Eeastern European countries). For instance, according to official data, the use of wool textiles per head in Latvia was about three times and use of silk textiles (chiefly artifical silk) four times larger than in the Soviet Union. As the number of live-stock in the Soviet Union was very small for many years after the collectivization of agriculture, the consumption of shoes there could also be very scarce. Likewise, in the year 1938 the population of Latvia had at its disposal, i. c. there were produced and sold for the inland market, nine times more radio sets and eleven times more bicycles than it was the case in the Soviet Union. One new radio set soled in Latvia fell to 100 inhabitants, and one bicycle to 40 inhabitants, in the Soviet Union—to 850 and 440 inhabitants respectively. According to official Soviet data an average living space of less than 5 sqare yards fell to one inhabitant in the towns of Soviet Union, but in the towns of Latvia 10 square yards. CULTURAL PROGRESS. The national education was very much raised in Latvia during her political independence. At the end of that period there were nearly no illiterate people in Latvia. An exception was the province of Latgale, at the border of the Soviet Union, where a lot of Russians lived, of whom a part were still illiterate. Shortly before the World War I, in the year 1911, during the Russian domination, the total number of pupils in all the schools of Latvia was 170,000, but in the year 1937—270,000 (as already mentioned, the population before the World War I was by half a million more than in the year 1939); the number of teachers was in the same years 7,577 and 12,935 respectively, so having increased at 70 per cent. The greatest progress was achieved by the Primary schools and the schools for higher education. Hundreds of new fine school buildings were constructed. The University of Latvia in Riga which was founded soon after the proclamation of independence of Latvia, comprised not only the departments of philology and philosophy, medicine, law etc., but also the technical schools: mechanics, chemistry, agriculture, etc. Whereas the sole school of higher education in Latvia during the Tsarist time, the Politechnical Institute of Riga, had only 2,000 students (in 1911), the number of students of the University of Latvia amounted to 7,000 —8,000. During the time of the Republic of Latvia about 7,200 persons were graduated from the University of Latvia, 1619 of them completed the course of the School of Medicine, 907 the School of Agriculture, 577 the School of Chemistry, and so forth. According to some estimates there were in Latvia in the year 1939 about 10,400 persons with university education. The way to schools in Latvia was not blocked to any gifted poor person. Shortly before the invasion of Soviet troops into Latvia, in the summer of 1940, 3800 students of the University of Latvia (the total of the students was 6500) received loans (fellowships) from a state bank, which enabled them to study. Even some students of the first semester received loans, if they had very successful completed the course of the Secondary School. In this connection it also can be mentioned that small Latvia in the last years of her existence accorded every year the "Fatherland\'s Prize" to 6 to 7 prominent writers, artists, scientists, composers, etc. This prize was founded by the last government of Latvia and amounted on the average to 8.000 lats. In the huge Soviet Union, with its population greater nearly 100 times, in the year 1941 a little more than 200 "Stalin\'s Prizes" were accorded for scientific, technical and cultural achievements. Each prize amounted to 25,000 to 100,000 roubles. If one considers that in the year 1939/40 the purchasing power of the lat in Latvia was equal to that of 10 roubles in the Soviet Union, it appears that the small Latvia accorded to her prominent men and women a state prize, which was equal to the first class "Stalin\'s Prize" (100,000 roubles). But in comparison with the population the number of prizes in Latvia was much greater than in the Soviet Union. In different public libraries of Latvia about 6 million books were available to readers. This number must be regarded great comparing it with the population of only two millions. During the existence of the Republic of Latvia approximately 26,000 books were published. In the year 1936 in Latvia 1601 various books and pamphlets were printed; each book was on the average of 144 pages. I the same year, for instance, in Sweden 2886, in Austria 1510, in Denmark 3505. in Great Britain 16,572 new books were published. In the year 1936 in Latvia 149 magazines and 54 newspapers were issued. There were comparatively many people in Latvia who mastered several languages. According to data of the census in the year 1930 there were in Latvia 1387 inhabitants who mastered six or more languages; 17,129 inhabitants mastered five, 66,636—four, 237,359—three languages. Consumption of paper in Latvia and in the Soviet Union shows to some extent the cultural level of the population in both these countries. According to official data in the year 1938 in Latvia 9 lbs of news-print, 4 lbs of printing paper and 3 lbs of writing paper per head of the population were used. In the Soviet Union in the year 1937 these figures were respectively: 2 lbs, 1 lb, and 2 lbs. Consequently in Latvia three times more newspapers, books and periodicals could be printed and more writing paper used. Also the sanitation improved in Latvia. The government and municipal authorities did not spare means for raising health of the people. This is proved by data about the number of physicians and about the extent of hospitals. The number of physicians amounted in Latvia in the year 1921 to 699, in the year 1938 to 1589. The number of dentists was 351 and 837 respectively. To 1 physician there fell in the year 1938: in Latvia 1,247, in Estonia 1,183, in Lithuania 2,727 inhabitants. The number of beds in the hospitals and sanatoria amounted in Latvia in the year 1922 to 4,767, in the year 1938 to 13,218. To 100 inhabitants there fell in the year 1938 the following number of beds: in Latvia 6.7, in Estonia 4.3, in Lithuania 1.4 beds. The number of hospitals and sanatoria in Latvia rose from 75 in the year 1922 to 141 in the year 1938. The increase of the medical personnel and the enlargement of hospitals and sanatoria in Latvia was not a sign of deterioration of the health conditions but, on the contrary, a sign of a greater attention bestowed to the medical care. SOCIAL CONDITIONS. Also in this respect the place of Latvia and the other Baltic states was not among the last ones in the world. During the period of the political independence of Latvia the social structure of the population underwent radical changes. Shortly before the World War II about two thirds of the population of Latvia consisted of owners of farms, trade and industry works, commercial enterprises, apartment houses, and of their family members. Only a third of the population were wageearners (workmen, employees, officials) and their families. But many employees and workmen often also owned a small house in town or had savings in the credit institutions. In the distribution of national wealth and national income there were no such great contrasts as there are in some other countries, where the land is chiefly owned by the great landlords and where the industry and commerce is in the hands of the big companies. Radical changes were made here by the agrarian reform. Before the World War I more than a half of the private estates belonged to about 1,300 big landlords, and 60 per cent of the country population were landless people, with their family members. After the agrarian reform 70,000 small farms were created from these latifundia and in the year 1930 nearly 80 per cent of the total population who lived on agriculture, were landowners, i. c. farmers\' families; only 20 per cent were landless people with their families. Also a great part of industrial and commercial enterprises were owned by small and middle enterprisers, tradesmen, petty industrialists and dealers. The state could greatly influence the economic life and social conditions by the many large and important state enterprises (factories, wholesale enterprises, banks, railways, state forestries), which were much enlarged, especially in the last years of the existence of the Republic of Latvia. So the social peace in Latvia could not be so much disturbed as in many other countries. But much care was taken also for the wage-earners, whose life conditions were improved and standard of life raised. Very important for the workmen was the fact that thanks to the energetic economic policy of the government in the years just before the World War II such good opportunities for finding job were created, that there was no unemployment in Latvia. Rather shortage of labour existed and up to 50,000 farm hands had to be brought in from the neighbouring countries of Poland and Lithuania. Therefore everybody could find job and the wages were on a comparatively high level having a rising trend. SOCIAL INSURANCE. Besides, the life of wage-earners and their family members was eased and secured by the sick-insurance, insurance against accidents and professional diseases ,the old age insurance, and a fund for combating against unemployment. Moreover, the social welfare of the municipalities cared for needy old people and small children. All the workmen and employees were compulsory insured with the sick-funds. Only the civil servants and the greatest part of the farm workers were not insured with the sick-funds (they had a special medical care). The means of the sick-funds came from the payments of the members of sick-funds (two per cent of the wages), payments of the employers (4 per cent of the paid wages) and contribution of the state (1 per cent); the sickfund received altogether 7 per cent of the sum total of wages of the workmen and employees. The medical treatment of the members of sick-funds and of their family members practically was free of charge. The members of sick-funds only paid 15 per cent and their family members 30 per cent of the price of the medicaments. The sick-funds also provided their members with special medical treatment in sanatoria, rest homes, and gave balneal treatment at health-resorts etc. Moreover, every summer the sick-funds sent children of their members aged from 6 to 12, to the country for recreation. Those members of sick-funds, who supported their families, received in case of incapacity for work, due to illness, for 26 to 52 weeks yearly (depending on the time of belonging to the sick-fund) the following subsidies: in case they were attended at horne—80 per cent, if attended at hospitals or sanatoria 60 per cent of their wages. Single persons received 80 per cent and 40 per cent. A female member of the sickfund received in the case of child birth a subsidy to the extent of her wages for 4 weeks before and 8 weeks after the delivery; thus total for 12 weeks. There were also special subsidies for feeding the new-born during the first 8 months. In the case of death of a member the sick-fund paid a subsidy for the funeral to the amount of 40 days wages of the deceased person. Almost only town inhabitants were insured with the sickfunds. The expenses for medical treatment of the country population were covered by the state and municipalities, according to a special law. The country population needed not to make payments in advance, but they had to pay a certain tariff for each treatment received. The needy country population did not pay anything to the physicians and hospitals, all those costs were paid by the state and rural communities. The well-to-do people (the law referred to all the country population: wage-earners, employees, farmers, tenants and their family members) paid the full costs of the medical treatment themselves. The farm workers during incapacity for work, due to disease, received from their employers food and lodging for 6 months. In case the sick farm worker was not able to visit himself the doctor, the employer had to convey him to the doctor and bring him back home, or had to take the doctor to the patient. All wage-earners and employees, and also all farmers, tradesmen, owners of small workshops, and fishers, and their family members as well, were insured against accidents and professional diseases. Nearly all means for this purpose consisted of payments of the employers and the state. The subsidy during a temporary incapacity for work was 70 per cent of the day\'s wages of the victims, and 50 per cent in case the patient was treated in a hospital and did not support a family. In case of 100 per cent of incapacity for work a pension was paid to the amount of 70 per cent of the wages. In particularly serious cases the victim received a pension to the amount of full wages. According to the Law of Pensions old-age pensions were granted to civil servants, officers, employees of the state enterprises, teachers of all schools, medical nurses, priests etc., also to scientists, writers and journalists, artists, permanently working artisans and workmen of the state enterprises etc. These persons were entitled to a pension, independently of their ability for work, in case they were discharged from employment, had served at least for 25 years and were not under 55 years of age. The pension fund consisted of deductions from wages and of payment made by the employers. After 25 years of service the pension amounted to 50 per cent of the salary; for each next year of service the pension rose at 2 per cent until it reached 70 per cent of the salary. Officials and employees of the municipal authorities were insured in special pension-funds. About 100,000 state and municipal officials and some other smaller groups were entitled to pension. Farmers, industrialists, merchants, professional men (physicians, lawyers, etc.) were usually sufficiently secure in old age by their property and sawings. For the time being the workmen had no old age benefits in that way; they and some other population groups that were incapable for work received subsidies from the municipal social welfare. The relief of the poor was for the most part open, in asylums there were living only about 10,000 adult persons. The last Latvian government had great plans about securing the old needy people and those unfit for work. In the year 1938 a law was passed concerning saving of funds for old age insurance. But this new plan was not carried out, because already in the next year the World War II began, and a year later the break-down of the Republic of Latvia came. For combating unemployment (for creation of various employment opportunities) a special fund existed at the Ministry of Social Welfare. The workmen and employees had to pay for that fund, usually 1 per cent of their wages; the employers had to pay the same amount. Besides the owners of real properties in towns and professional men had to give a certain contribution. But in the last years there was no unemployment in Latvia, but rather a shortage of manpower, especially in the agriculture. Moreover, some other means were used for clearing social contrasts in order to further social peace, for raising the standard of life and for family subsidies to the workmen. In the year 1937 the government adopted extra pay for families of the farm workers. The farm workers received additional pay from the state for all children who were up to 11 years of age: for the first two children 4 lats a month each, for the rest 5 lats a month. Farm workers who had one child received 4 lats, who had two children 8 lats, for 3 children 13 lats, for 4 children 18 lats, and so on. As, for instance, 1 lb of butter costed 1 lat and 1 lb of bread 0,10 lats, a farm worker\' family could buy for 4 lats 40 lbs of bread or 4 lbs of butter in a month. These additional payments were received also by the parents and grandparents of the yearly workers, if they were 65 years old and were maintained by the worker. In the year 1938 a further law eased the family life of farm workers by providing a fund for building farm workers\' homes. That fund appropriated more than 2 million lats annually. This sum was used for loans (at 1 per cent a year) to farmers for building farm workers\' houses. After completing the buildings, half of the loan was cancelled. During the Soviet occupation extra payments to workers\' families were not paid; also the steady improvement of housing conditions of the farm workers ceased in the Soviet time. FINAL REMARKS. Although the three Baltic states, as agricultural countries were not thickly populated, their entire territory (65,300 square miles) was comparatively large. It was, for instance, a little larger than Czechoslovakia (53,054 square miles) or a little larger than the half of Italy (119,691 square miles), or only a third smaller than Great Britain (86,486 square miles). Latvia alone is as large as Belgium (11,583 square miles) and Holland (13,327 square miles) put together. The Baltic states had no great natural resources, no coal, no iron ore, no petroleum etc. They had only their moderately fertile soil and forests. Nevertheless the national wealth in these countries was comparatively high. Considering that the whole world economy and mankind have profit in a small area of the globe being diligently and carefully tilled and utilized, one can say that the establishment and existence of the Baltic states has been an event of positively international importance. Comparing the economy and culture of Latvia with those of the Soviet Union we can judge from what a dreary fate the population of the Baltic states had escaped by separating themselves from Russia, fighting for their freedom in the years 1918 to 1920, and obtaining their political independence, be it only for a period of 20 years. The achievements of the Jiher Baltic states in this respect were to some measure equal to those of Latvia. During the Soviet occupation the very foundations of the economic life and productivity of the labour of the Baltic states were destroyed: the joy for work of the farmers, workmen and intellectuals was annihilated, the private initiative and industry of the enterprisers were entirely eliminated. The most diligent and prosperous farmers and enterprisers were regarded by the Communist regime as criminals and were dealt with as sueh—deported to Siberia for forced labour and executed. The forcible collectivization of the farms in the year 1919 and the deportation of the most diligent and prosperous peasants to Siberia has destroyed the most important basis of the economy and welfare of Latvia and other Baltic states. It will result in a total break-down of the agricultural production and cause hunger in the towns. The Baltic peoples can regain their happiness only when their political independence is restored. The Republics of Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania must be re-established again in order to save these peoples from the lowest misery, unhuman oppression, and gradual annihilation.']
