['THE DANGER OF A MILITARY COUP IN THE USSR

Lithuania has become the apparent testing ground of the West’s attitude to the Gorbachev regime and to the Soviet leader himself. The demand that the US and NATO should take unspecified drastic steps to force the Soviet out of Lithuania is understandable in the case of Lithuanians who are as much entitled to their freedom as any other nation. Hovvever, precipitate action may set at risk ali the gains vvhich have been made and thereby jeopardize the prospects of freedom itself. The danger now exists of a serious clash betvveen Gorbachev and the so called “liberāls” on the one hand, and an Army/Party group of so-called “reaction āries” on the other. The continuation of Gorbachev’s rule, at least for a limited period, appears necessary if the dissolu tion of communism as an ideology, as vvell as that of the Party as a major political force are alike to be completed. This vievv is not based on the assump tion that Gorbachev is a “good guy” or a “bad guy”. He is an extremely clever poli tician, fighting for his political life. The continuation of the Gorbachev re gime promises the continuation of “de mocratisation”, not necessarily of a parlia mentary system of govemment, but in the sense of the continued vveakening of the Communist Party and the strengthening of the “popular forces”. The only present altemative is an Army coup d’etat vvhich, promises the end of Gorbachev possibly a rough one and a reversion to what existed before. That means martial law, arrests, sentencings, far tougher action against the Baltie peoplcs and the other ethnic rebcllions, and the restoration of the Party. The fact that the pre-Gorbachev sys tem cannot be restored, least of ali by a

proclamation of martial law, vvill not dimin ish the attraction this formula offers to high military officers who today judge Gorbachev’s policies as doing little more than to dissolve the Soviet Empire. Within the Soviet Union, the internai political situation is novv delicately poised. There is a deep and quite open opposition to Gorbachev and his revolution. While he has been able to gain the general support of the intelligentsia, the people at large have lost confidence in the economic re strueturing by vvhich he promised them food and other basie necessities, a promise vvhich has not materialized. The opposi tion group vvhich really counts arises from the ranks of the Party and military leader ship vvhich is stili capable of action, and must realize that time is running out for it. An anonymous pamphlet circulated throughout Moscovv at the beginning of March elaiming to be the vvork of the KGB Headquarters Staff threatened direct action if the “sociālist system” vvas imperilled. It regarded Gorbachev’s “ne glect” as inexcusable. An article by Col. Gen. Boris Gromov (dated February 1990) the former com mander of the Soviet Forces in Afghani stan, and novv commander of the Kiev military distriet, challenges Gorbachev’s vvhole vievv of the intemational situation and states that in proportion as the Sovicts vvithdravv from Eastern Europe, the US is strengthening its ovvn military position. Ligachev vvho has long since been re garded as Gorbachev’s most formidable critic vvithin the Politburo itself, in a speech to the Central Committee on March 16 openly attacked Gorbachev and his poli cies. Gorbachev’s constitutional reform of March 13 vvas dirccted to taking out of the

hands the Politburo (and of the Communist Party) the instruments of coercion the KGB and the defence forces which might be used to overthrovv him in a repetition of the coup against Krushchev in 1964. The new constitution placed con trol of the Army and the KGB in the hands of the President i.e. himself as head of the govemment rather than as head of the Party. On March 29 Pravda reported further on the measures taken by Gor bachev to protect himself the extraordi nary step of abolishing the position of General Secretary of the Party, the en largement (and consequent diffusion of power) of the Politburo. There is no doubt that, whatever he intended at the begin-

ning in March 1985, Gorbachev is novv intent on dissolving the Party so that it cannot challenge his position as head of the govemment. With the forces coalescing, probably the vvorst thing that could be done in the subject republics today vvould be to precipi tate the final clash betvveen Gorbachev and the Army/Party group, or to force Gor bachev to become the instrument of the latter. It would give the Soviet defence forces the pretext to use military power not merely against the rebellious republics but against the leader vvhom they clearly re gard as the architect of the dissolution of the communist system. Neivs Weekly, April 14, 1990', 'SOVIET EMPIRE EXPLODES - WHY?

The Soviet Union is degenerating into chaos and possibly into civil war. How has this come about in a brief five years? When Gorbachev came to power in March 1985, the Soviet Union had reached a condition of economic paralysis not dissimilar from that of the Old Regime in France in 1789. The Soviet economy, already staggering under the weight of an arms build-up taking about 20% of the GDP, crashed under the strain of attempting to complete with Reagan’s accelerated rearmament program. The seemingly inevi table growth of Soviet power was reversed. Gorbachev proposed a set of slogans glasnost and perestroika but he had no economic program. Six years of failed promises have now ended in a nationvvide confiscation of a great part of the people’s savings. The Soviet collapse has extended from the economic to the political and administrative fields, particularly in the

Centre’s relationship with the Republics. The resultant disorder is not dissimilar from that which afflicted the French revolutionary regime when Napoleon ended it in 1799. When the Soviet military forces were used to crush demonstrators in Tbilisi in Aprit 1989, followed by the bloody repres sion in Baku and, most recently, in Lithuania and Latvia, the internai rift became irrepar able. Prophecies of a coup d’etat, follovved perhaps by civil, war, now abound. The indications point strongly in this direction. Following on the military crack down in the Baltie States, the censorship has been re-imposed in order to cut the links binding elements of the liberal oppo sition in different paris of the Soviet Union. Newspapers like Argumenty I Fakty (circulation 24 million) and Moscow News were invaluable information sources vvhich ķept the democratic forces in hand. Will the crackdown end in dictatorship?

Yes, said former Foreign Minister, Shevard nadze, previously one of Gorbachev’s closest collaborators. “I think that a dictatorship is unavoidable.” (Moscow News, January 6-13,1991). In his resignation speech (Radio Moscow, December 12, 1990) Shevard nadze attacked “two colonels”, obviously Colonels Alksnis and Petrushenko. These, he said, having boasted that they had got rid of the Interior Minister, Bakatin, had threatened to “settle scores with the Foreign Minister” i.e. with himself. Four days after Shevardnadze’s resig nation, Alksnis proved the point by calling for a “parliamentary investigation with criminal consequences” into Shevard nadze’s policies, particularly for having negotiated German unification, the entry of a united Germany into NATO, and the vvithdravval of Soviet troops from East Germany. If Shevardnadze expected that his old friend, Gorbachev, vvould rise to defend him against these attacks, he vvould have been disappointed. “Why did no one speak against them?” he asked: but it was already clear that Gorbachev was abandoning his liberal allies, and moving in the direction of an alliance vvith elements of the military, the KGB, and the Party. Ali things are possible. Nevertheless Gorbachev vvould appear to have few friends among the military. In an intervievv vvith Argumenty I Fakty Colonel Alksnis seemed to put paid to that possibility: Alksnis, claiming to speak vvith a majority of the Supreme Soviet behind him although his party Soyuz itself can actually claim only one-third of the vote had already claimed that the chaos in the

economy vvas due to the fact that Gorbachev had no economic program when he came to povver, and stili had none today, six years and many “plāns” later. Alksnis went further. He stated that Gorbachev himself had approved the estab lishment of the phoney “National Salvation Committees’, made up of old hardline party “apparatchiks” in the Baltie States, vvhich, vvith the support of the Black Bērēts (a vvell armed ex-officer “free corps”), had attemp ted to overthrovv the elected governments in Lithuania and Latvia. When, as a result of national resistance and the rise of protests in the West, the attempt stalled, “the President betrayed us” said Alksnis. “He is afraid to take the responsibility himself. Imagine a surgeon starting an operation, making an incision on the body of a patient, and then leaving him”. The military, added Alksnis, vvas made the scapegoat for the violence in both countries. Nor did Alksnis hesitate to call openly for an army crackdovvn vvhich vvould almost certainly end in civil war. “I do not exclude that the Army vvill become autonomous (i.e. refuse to accept orders from the govern ment)... It vvill inevitably esealate into a civil war vvhich vvill eneompass the Soviet Union. The result vvill be terrible chaos and complete anarchy” As a result of a civil war, a “new authority” vvould emerge. It vvould neither be Gorbachev nor Yeltsin but “a completely unknovvn person who in the current situation may by chance come to povver.” That a military officer should call on the Army to mutiny in a nevvspaper vvith a circulation of 24 million, and stili remain at liberty, instead of being arrested and charged vvith treason is an eloguent

indication of Gorbachev’s impotence. If the situation predicted by Alksnis were to eventuate, the Soviet army would split. While the officer corps is Russian and speaks Russian, the conscripts come from ali of the Soviet’s myriad ethnic groups. This year 40% of the conscripts who received their call-up papērs did not report. The three Baltie States refused transports for their own consripts. The Ukrainian Par liament has insisted that its soldiers must not serve outside the Ukrainian borders. Georgia has actually secured agreement on that principle from the Soviet Government for its married conscripts. From the vievvpoint of the outside world,

one of the major questions would be who had control of the 30,000 nuelear vvarheads presently in the hands of the Soviet military. From 1906 to 1911, the Tsar’s Prime Minister was Piotr Stolypin. He was a modernizer, in many ways not unlike Gorbachev. To retain political support for his modernization program, he oscillated betvveen the conservatives and the liberāls. He won the gratitude of neither. One day, an assassin (probably a member of the Tsarist secret police) put an end to his oscillation by blovving off his head. Unlike Stolypin Gorbachev may ride the storm: but an empire in chaos is capable of anything. News Weekly, February 16,1991', 'Conclusions oi the Independent Military Experts of the Public Organisation "Shield" on the Events m Vilnius, January n-13.1991: At the invitation of the State Commission of the Republic of Lithuania created to investigate the events of January 11-13, 199* (Chairman, Prime Minister G. Vagnonus), a commission of independent military expertß. including a captain of the firstrank in the reserves of the USSR Army, A. Yevstigneyev (Commission Chairman. People's Deputy of the RSFSR, Ph.D. in Engineering), a lieutenant-colonel in the reserves of the USSR Army, I.V. Bychkov; a colonel ofthe USSR Army. S. M. Kudinov (People's Deputy of the RSFSR); a captain ofthe second rank m the reserves of the USSR Army, G. M. Melkov (Doctor of Law, Professor of Law); and a major m the reserves ofthe USSR Army. N. M. Moskovchenko. conducted the following investigation from February 3 to 12,1991: a) studied documents made available to the commission, reviewed three video films ofthe events of January 11-13,studied the tapes of ladio intercepts of conversations of the military,studied the documents and conclusions oflegal-medical experts, as well as the notoc and affadavits ofmedical and auxiliarypersonnel inhospitals and the morgue, and reviewed statements given to the press by various government, military, Party, and pubhc officials, and other relevant documents; b) held detailed discussions with members of the Government ofthe Republic ofLithuaii.a.members of the Lithuanian Procuracy (prosecuting authorities),with heads ofdepartments, and other officials; c) had meetings and detailed discussions with members ofthe Communist Party ofLithuania (Secretary ofthe CPL Central Committee Jujmalavichus —the main contact forthe CPL and the National Salvation Committee; Secretary of the CPL Central Committee General Naudziunas and a member of the National Salvation Committee according TASS reports m "Izvestia"; Secretary of the CPL City Committee Khadunkin; member ofthe CPL Central Committee Shurupov; and others);with the [Soviet] Procurator General ofthe "Lithuanian SSR "Petrauskas: with a representative ofthe USSR KC3, Rukshenas; and with managers of the Vilnius radio factory; d) had discussions with the vice chairmen of the Democratic Labor Party, V. Cerzhonas and V. Beriozovas; e) had meetings and discussions with soldiersand officers of the Vilnius garrison and then family members, and with Vilnius residents of various ages and ethnic backgrounds; and f)had a telephone discussion with the military commander of the Vilnius garrison, Major-Gcneral Uskhopchik. who refused to have an officialmeeting, saying that he did not recognize the Government of the Republic of Lithuania, and stated "your official mission means nothing tome. " The Military Commander of the Vilnius garrison. Colonel Relous. refused to meet, maintaining that allavailable documents had already been handed over tothe investigator. The head of the garrison's politicaldepartment. Colonel Smokarev. refused to have a meeting Repeated attempts toestablish contact with him and others were fruitless. SHIELD A discussion with the head of the political department of the Military Commissariat for the Republic of Lithuania. Lieutenant-Colonel Gohibev, was held, m which he asserted that he did not recognize the Government and the laws of the Republic of Lithuania. President Lands bergis and Prime Minister Vagnorius received the commission members. Conclusions and Recommendations On the basis of the investigation, the Independent Military Experts arrived at the following conclusions: LOn January 11-13. Vilnius, an attempt was made to stage a coup d'etat with the aid of armed forces, internal troops of the USSR Ministry of Internal Affairs and of the KGB with the aim of restoring the political power of the CPSU as represented by its constituent part—the CPL The USSR President cannot claim ignorance of the planned joint actions by the Soviet Armed Forces, internal troops of the USSR Ministry of Internal Affairs and of the USSR KGB. Such actions could not be carried out without his personal permission. a. The participation of USSR military units m the coup d'etat had been planned, coordinated and approved by the Centre. 3. The organiser of the coup d'etat was the CPL Central Committee. 4. For carrying out its designs, the CPL Cential Committee established the so-called "National Salvation Committee" whose membership and leaders are being kept in secrecy so that in the event of failure they could evade responsibility. Secretary of the CPL Central Committee Jarmalavichus, authorised to maintain contact between the National Salvation Committee and Vilnius garrison, is the only officially known person linked to the National Salvation Committee. The Independent Military Experts consider that the following persons constituted the nucleus of the National Salvation Committee: first secretary of the CPL CC Burokevichus. Secretary of the CPL CC Jarmalavichus, Secretary of the CPL Major-General Naudziunas. head of the department of the CPL CC and colonel m the reserves of the the USSR Army Shurupov, First Secretary of the Vilnius City Committee of the CPL Lazutka, Second Secretary of the Vilnius City Committee of the CPL Khadunkin. Secretary of the CPL primary organisation of the Vilnius radio factory Dostal. and military commander of the Vilnius garrison Major-General Uskhopchik. 5. The National Salvation Committee and the CPL had counted on the support of the people dissatisfied with deteriorating living conditions, as well as the Russian speaking population and the military whose rights had been curtailed by certain laws of the Republic of Lithuania. With this aim in view, members of the CPL had carried out deliberate agitation and ideological propaganda. Political bodies and CPSU organisations had carried out analogous propaganda m military units and among family meinbers of the military. In this way an atmosphere of ill-will and animosity was artificially created between the native population and the army. At the present time the Government of the Republic of Lithuania is using all its energies to improve relations with the army units stationed on the territory of the Republic, and to normalise the situation. However, military commanders categorically refuse to establish contact. 6. Convinced of its victory and impunity, the National Salvation Committee, relying upon military units of the Vilnius garrison and units of the USSR Ministry of Internal Affairs and the KGB sent to Lithuania by the Centre, attempted to stage a coup d'etat,which caused great casualtiesamong civilians and significantmaterialdamage. 7.There isno evidence toindicatethe usage ofarms on the part of civiliansand guards of public buildings. Shots were firedonly by the military.As a result.l 3 persons were Killedand 604 injured.All the victims were civilians.Among 13 killedwas a lieutenantof the USSR KGB who was accidentally wounded by hisown men and died because the militarydid not render him timely qualifiedmedical aid 8. The militaryused s.4scalibre dum-dum bullets thatgreatly increase the sufferingsof tho wounded and arebanned under internationalconventions. 3. The coup d'etatwas aborted by the people of Lithuania who rosem defense oftheirlawfullyelected government. The resultsof the population poll held on February 9, 1991emphatically and convincingly testifiedto the choice of the people of the Republic ofLithuania. M. Responsible farthe attempt to stage the coup d'etat,and forthe numerous victims and material damage are: I)The USSR President who failedto perform his constitutionalduty; 2)Minister of Defense Yazov, Minister oflnternal Affairs Pugo, and Chairman of the USSR KGB Kryuchkov who gave permission touse the military forces under their command in pursuing anticonstitutional,criminalgoals; 3) Deputy Minister of Defense Colonel-General Achalov, militarycommander ofthe Vilnius garrison Major-General Uskhopchik, regiment commander ofthe Paratroop Forces Colonel Kustnuk. regiment commander ofthe 107thMotorised RifleDivisionColonel Astakhov, who directedthe actionsof the troops under theircommand incarrying outthe criminaldesigns ofthe National SalvationCommitteeand CPL Central Committee; 4) Soldiers,sergeants and officerswho shot at civilians. The USSR President,the USSR Ministry of Defense, the USSR Ministry of Internal Affairs, the USSR KGB and the CPL are responsiblefar the materialdamage caused to the Republic of Lithuania, itsstateinstitutions,aswellas tolegal and natural persons ofthe Republic. 13. The National Salvation Committee isan unconstitutional,criminalorganisation,the activitiesof which must be banned, and proceedings must be institutedagainst itsmembers forhaving committed an especiallygrave crime againstthe state. 13.Taking intoaccount directcontact between the members ofthe National Salvation Committee and the CPL incarrying out the coup d'etat,theactivity ofthe CPL must be suspended untilthe end ofthe investigation. Lļ.Central newspapers, radio and Central Televisiongave a biased interpretationofthe January 11- :3 events in Vilnius,adhering to the stand of the CPSU and CPL :g.ltwould be expedient torepeal the laws restnetmg the rightsofthe Russian-speaking population,of the militaryaridthen familymembers. r6.Taking intoaccount the current politicalsituation,when the laws of the USSR are not being enforced on the territoryofthe Republic ofLithuania, whereas the laws adopted by the legislativebody of the Republic run counter tothe laws ofthe USSR, it isnecessary towork out a specialpicvisional legal document protecting the rights,honour, dignity and property ofthe militaryand their family members untilan appropriate agreement between the USSR and the Republic ofLithuaniais concluded 17.F0r the coordinationof effoitsbetween republicsaimed at protectingtheir sovereignty,political and economic independence from the dictateofthe Centre, and forthe coordinationof jointactions in situations analogous to the January 11-13 events in Lithuania, horizontal agreements between the republics enabling them to resist this dictate should be concluded. 18. In order to maintain state sovereignty and to enfore* law and order, it is necessary to form national guard units in the republics. ï&. Supreme Councils of the republics must adopt legislation defining the notion of a "criminal order" and establishing that legal proceedings may be instituted for executing such an order, ao. The events in Vilnius rank with other cases when the Soviet Army was used against its own people: m Novocherkask in 196 a, in Tbilisi in 198 g, in Baku in 1990. a: The events of January 11-13 are not related to any international conflicts and are of a purely political character. The events showed that : —the Party leadership is striving to turn the Soviet Army into a hostage of its criminal political designs and its soldiers—into mindless and submissive executors : —depoliticisation of the Armed Forces, the Ministry of Internal Affairs, and the KGB of the USSR is an objective necessity and must be carried out without delay; —all nationalities are equally endangered by the plot hatched by the party and military leadership who are striving to retain power by all possible means, impudently violating laws and condemning peoples to poverty, hunger and death. The Independent Military Experts deem it their duty to bring these Conclusions to the notice of Poeple's Deputies of the USSR and of the RSFSR, other republics and the world community, governments of foreign states and the United Nations Organisation. Representative_Docs: ['CRISIS SUBSISTENCE LEVEL REPORTED AT 55.44 LATS IN JUNE The Latvian Free Trade Unions Association reports that the crisis subsistence ieve! in Latvia was 55.44 fats in June. Compared to June last year, the crisis subsistence level has gone up by 13%. Subsistence level was 90.26 lats per resident in June, 95.14 lats per employed resident and 72.19 late per pensioner. The estimates show that on foodstuffs each resident shouid spend 32.91 lats per month, a worker-36.51 lats and a pensioner-30.37 lats. The minimum wage has been set at 38 lats since April and average pension – at 39 lats since May.', 'Living Scale in Estonia "Every worker must participate in so-called \'community work\'— about 100 hours a summer—repairing streets, planting trees, helping with construction jobs, etc-, for which he receives no salary. This is \'voluntary,\' as is participation in parades, meetings, etc., which, together with \'community work,\' take up Sundays and most free time. "Workers are entitled to a twoweek vacation yearly. But it is wrong to think that workers spend their vacations at a seashore resort. Hardly one in a thousand can enjoy such luck and then it is reported in all the newspapers and broadcast on the radio. Usually workers are \'requested\' to spend their vacations working in a factory-adopted kolkhoz. "The salary or a worKer is about 400-700 rubles per month, from which 11 per cent .is deducted for direct taxes and 10-15 per cent, as a \'loan\' to the State (average—one month\'s salary). Single and married people who do not have children have to pay a 3 per cent, tax which is popularly called \'bachelors tax.\' One source of support for the kolkhozs is the small (i hectare) strip of private land. Until recently every family was even allowed 2 cows. Since 1950, the situation has grown steadily worse. Because of the lack of food, the kolkhoz cows died of hunger and private cows were sold or slaughtered. Last summer, some kolkhoz chairmen permitted the remaining private cows to enter the kolkhoz paddocks along with the kolkhoz cattle. But they were replaced and punished for violation of the kolkhoz constitution. The damage w hich the private cows had done to kolkhoz economy in eating kolkhoz grass was estimated at more than the value of the cow. So the cow was taken over by the kolkhoz and the former owner had to pay the rest of the damage with more workdays. "Estonians never forget the fate of those who are declared kulaks or relatives of kulaks. They are deported to Siberia, and the passports of their families are stamped \'ineligible for work,\' and neither kolkhozs nor factories are allowed to employ them. Many have committed suicide, collapsed from hunger, or have been deported to Siberia. If a kolkhoz chairman or Party secretary dislikes a person, they can stamp him as kulak or \'bourgeois nationalist\' and the unfortunate person has no future in Soviet Estonia. "Relatively, the best conditions exist in the fishing kolkhozes. Fishermen are paid according to the government regulated prices for fish, and the more fish they catch, the more money they get. It is said that some fishermen earn up to 1,500 rubles a month. But according to stories now circulating, the present income of fishermen will be reduced by new regulations, which will lower the price of fish- Present rates will be paid only for fish caught in excess of the norms." He included the following price list in his report: Rubles Rye bread (1 kg.) 2 White bread (1 kg.) – 3-4 Butter (1 kg.) 40 Margarine (1 kg.) 25 Meat 15 Pork 20 Smoked ribs 35 Potatoes 1-2 Cigarettes 3J Vodka (h liter) 30 Suit 800 Raincoat 1200 Shirt 100 Men\'s shoes 450 Women\'s shoes 400 Hat 125 Socks 15 Wrist watch 250-400 Bicycle 800 Radio 800 "There is also a blackmarket in sugar, white flour, groats, and high quality bread."', 'AVERAGE SALARY IN SEPTEMBER EQUALLED 97.03 LATS According to the Central Statistical Bureau, the average salary in September in Latvia equalled 97.03 lats in state and local government enterprises, establishments, organisations and companies with state or local government capital shares, which is a drop of 2.6% since August. While the average pay in budgetary establishments was 79.77 lats in September, a decrease of 5.7% since August, it equalled 110.30 lats in enterprises, organisations and companies with state or local government capital shares, which represents a 4.1% increase compared to August. When compared to September of 1994, salaries in state and local government enterprises, establishments, organisations and companies with the state or local government capital shares have gone up by 22% while consumer prices in the same time period have increased by 24%. it must be noted that up until October 6, salary payments totalling 5.3 million lats were also delayed for employees of some state and local government establishments, enterprises, organisations and companies with state or local government capita! shares.']

Representative_Docs: ['BUSINESS OUTLOOK: LATVIA A tough-minded approach to banking stabilisation has paid off. After a fallow year, prospects look increasingly good Western imports will exceed last years $900 m, which was more than forecast and on the evidence of the first quarter could exceed $Ibn. The figure is much lower figure than in Estonia (especially on a per capita basis) but the iatter country\'s "tourism" sector distorts the picture. Latvian wages are around $180, compared to $230 in Estonia. Both consumers have been squeezed during the recession – although villa construction around Riga continues apace. Unlike Estonia, the current account appears to be narrowly in surplus, largely thanks to the strongly positive effect of Russian oil transit fees <SD> (which brought in around $I.Bbn last year). As a result, the international reserves are at a healthy $580 m; import cover should be a comfortable 3.1 months at the end of this year. Foreign investment is some way behind Estonia especially when measured on a per capita basis. Cable & Wireless (UK) in partnership with the Finnish telephone company remains easily the biggest foreign investor. Keliogs (USA) makes cornflakes, and there is some activity in luxury hotels, petrol stations and forestry. The weakest area Is state finances; an area which highlights the weakness of the state administration. Taxpaying is regarded as optional by many businesses and smuggling of excisable goods is a serious problem. Western companies, which do not bribe or threaten tax collectors, pay more than their fair share. However increasing privatisation revenues should enable the government to meet its target $110 m budget deficit for 1996, although not the IMFset goal of $72m). interest rates at the weekly Treasury bill auctions have fallen sharply and are now under 10% annually, indicating the return of confidence in the stability of the lat which is unofficially pegged to the IMF\'s Special Drawing Right. Privatisation has lagged in Latvia, because of the cosy political links between ministries and state industries. The new government of Prime Minister Andris Skele is proving quite effective at accelerating the pace of sell-offs – several of the country\'s most important companies, including the national gas, shipping and oil concerns are up for sale to foreign investors in the next two months.', 'Baltic States continue to be regarded favourabe for investments. The Baltic States continue to be regarded as favourable for investments, according to results of the latest survey of investors conducted by the German-Baltic Chamber of Commerce in Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. Companies surveyed regard the elimination of customs and other trading barriers as the biggest contribution to future integration of the Baltic States into the common European market. No less important to businessmen are the smaller administrative costs, and it is important that following accession to the EU it be possible to orientate more readily through legislation. In the opinion of businessmen, the Baltic States will continue to remain attractive to business, since that is the region of Europe which is developing most quickly. German businessmen, in selecting a place in which to invest, take into account its economic prospects, political stability, overall level of education, membership of the EU, low tax level, geographic location, availability and costs of workforce, and state support for investments. German and Baltic Chamber of Commerce in Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania (27.02.2006)', 'Baltic States continue to be regarded favourabe for investments. The Baltic States continue to be regarded as favourable for investments, according to results of the latest survey of investors conducted by the German-Baltic Chamber of Commerce in Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. Companies surveyed regard the elimination of customs and other trading barriers as the biggest contribution to future integration of the Baltic States into the common European market. No less important to businessmen are the smaller administrative costs, and it is important that following accession to the EU it be possible to orientate more readily through legislation. In the opinion of businessmen, the Baltic States will continue to remain attractive to business, since that is the region of Europe which is developing most quickly. German businessmen, in selecting a place in which to invest, take into account its economic prospects, political stability, overall level of education, membership of the FIJ, low rax level, geographic location, availability and costs of workforce, and state support for investments. German and Baltic Chamber of Commerce in Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania (27.02.2006)']

Representative_Docs: ['IKXJ 1 1 IRA Novel about Latvian family in Milwaukee (half-Latvian aiiu  was born in Seattle, Washington, on July 4, 1976 . He received his MFA in poetry and his PhD in English Hterature from Cornell University. His writing has appeared in Sports Illustrated, The Crab Creek Review, and The Yemen Observer. What is Red Weather 9. The setting is Milwaukee, Wisconsin – if not America\'s heart, then at least its liver – home to an array of breweries and abandoned factories and down-on-their-luck Eastern European immigrants. The year is 1989. As Yuri tells us: "In Latvian, Balodis means pigeon. We were a small roost of Soviet immigrant pigeons – just the three of us – huddled together amid the urban decay." Revolutions are sweeping through the nations of the Eastern Bloc. Communism is unraveling. And nobody feels this unraveling more piquandy than Yuri – whose parents mortify him with their stubbornly foreign accents and habits. But when Yuri falls in love with Hannah Graham – the daring and dangerous daughter of a prominent Milwaukee socialist – chaos ensues. Within weeks he has joined the staff of the Socialist Worker, and his ideological betrayal will have more serious consequences than breaking his parents\' hearts. As Hannah Tinti (editor of One Story Magazine) also says: "In Red Weather, Pauls Toutonghi introduces a wonderful new character to the world of fiction – Rudolfs Balodis – a hero, a thief, an ex-communist, an alcoholic, a janitor, a Latvian, a singer of the blues and above all else, a father." Who is Pauls Toutonghi? Or as Darin Strauss (Chang and Eng, The Real McCoy) writes: "Pauls Toutonghi is such an original, it seems almost blasphemous to try comparing him to others, but here goes: Gary Shteyngart meets David Sedaris meets Frank McCourt. In other words, he\'s whipsmart and hilarious and Red Weather is a guaranteed knockout." Or Adam Langer (Crossing California, The Washington Story): "Pauls Toutonghi s humor is imbued with a rare generosity of spirit. And, with his debut novel, he has written a moving and enPauls Toutonghi tertaining love letter to youth, to family, to his heritage, and, perhaps most important, to Milwaukee, a city that is woefully underrepresented in contemporary fiction." Or Ken Kaifus (Thirst, PU-239, The Commisariat of Enlightenment): "Pauls Toutonghi\'s vibrant first novel, lyrical and rich in human insight, celebrates the essential experience of the first-generation American – whose struggle for full nativeness is always joined within the dizzying, tragic and exuberant campaign to become an adult." The author will read from his work: June 26 at 7 p.m. in the Harry W. Schwartz Bookshop, 2262 S. Kinnickinnic Aye., Bay View, a suburb of Milwaukee, Wis., and June 27 at 7:30 p.m. as part of the Bookslut Reading Series in The Hopleaf, 5148 North Clark, Chicago. For further information on the author, visit www.paulstoutonghi. com. Hidden within this coming-ofage story is a tender, sad portrait of Yuri\'s alcoholic father, Rudolfs Balodis, who dreams of being a country-and-western singer and works nights as a janitor at a car dealership. Rudolfs carries the very real scars of his own youthful rebellion – two fingers and the thumb missing from his left hand, chopped off by the Latvian secret police – and in a lovely scene he takes Yuri on a "drunken midnight field trip" in which they steal a watermelon-red Corvette and go for a joy ride to the accompaniment of "Moon Mullican, the King of the Hillbilly Piano." Rudolfs s sense of magic and his sense of humor save the novel. On Halloween, he dresses up as Stalin ("Is the scariest thing I could think of) and he crashes noisily through this assemblage of pastiches. Rudolfs is a full character, awash with contradictory desires: wistful for yet furious at his motherland, and in love with the adopted country he so clearly sees has abused him. The stunningly quiet ending reverberates back through the whole book, throwing fresh light on both its weaknesses and its real ambition. The final dated section takes place on Friday, May 4, 1990; although Toutonghi never mentions it, that was the day the Supreme Soviet Council announced the restoration of Latvian independence. This correlation goes unremarked by the characters. But in ending his novel on this day, Toutonghi silently allows Yuri to disown his ideological posturing, and reconciles the politics of the father with those of the son. It\'s a touching act of homage from a novelist who is foolish and brave enough to sacrifice his own prospects of writing an original novel for the greater good of a love letter to hisfather. The New York Times The relationship between fathers and sons is a motif much honored in the West by writers from James Joyce on, and one to which Russian Ivan Turgenev had given something of a political cast. In truth, there\'s always a mixture of the personal and the political in a novel that pits one generation against another. In Pauls Toutonghi\'s nicely etched story about the education of a first-generation Latvian-American in beer-soaked, bourbon-filled, working-class Milwaukee in autumn 1989, teenage narrator Yuri Balodis takes up the cudgel of the upstart generation. His father, roughly but lovingly depicted Rūdolfi Balodis, stands up (and more often than not takes a drink of bourbon and sits down, or lies down, or eventuaOy falls down) for the older way of life. When we first meet Rūdolfi he seems to be nothing more than a hard-working, hard-drinking, sentiment-spouting refugee from Eastern Europe. But because of Toutonghi\'s cunning representation, we eventually see him both as the man his son believes him to be and as a character who has within himself the same possibilities for heroism that led him (with his wife) to escape Iron Curtain life. Quite a lovely technical feat for a first-time novelist – and Rūdolfi is quite a memorable character. If this was all the bookaccomplished, it would have been enough for a reader to give it some attention. But Yuri\'s story about himself and his father renders in a particularly appealing and interesting way the old template of young versus old. The plot seems necessary rather than contrived, and though it is slow to unfold, the book never tries the reader\'s patience. This is largely because of the clarity of Toutonghi\'s prose and the distinctness of Yuri\'s observations, beginning with those of Milwaukee at night. "I walked in the middle of the sidewalk," Yuri tells us after he has sneaked out of the family\'s factory-district apartment as he is wont to do, so late at night it\'s nearly the edge of morning, "observing the shadows of objects and buildings, listening for the distant hum of the awakening city. The sidewalks were broad. Awnings hung down from every facade. The city felt like a gelatin silver print, black and white and a little mysterious." Yuri is just as sharply observant in daytime, as in his description of "a beautiful, clear day on Lake Michigan," when, "if there\'s been a recent rain and the air is relatively free of smog ... the water of the lake can give you the sensation of endless life. It offers you this sensation just for looking, just for considering its body, its aquatic span, which vanishes into the distant horizon. On clear mornings in the spring, Fd often stand on any one of the numerous concrete piers of downtown Milwaukee and just look into the watery distance. It gave me a sensation of smallness, of insignificance, a sensation that would simultaneously scare me and also make me feel hilly alive and sad." But that\'s spring. For most of novel, sensitive young Yuri suffers in the autumn of his discontent, running the gamut of new emotions – from infatuation to bravado to rebellion to contrition – as he falls in love at first sight with one of his schoolmates, the daughter of a local college professor and dedicated socialist; goes behind his hardworking father\'s back and commits a foolhardy felony; and suffers the consequences, nearly losing his father and his own freedom in the aftermath. The more Yuri suffers, the more engrossing the book becomes, as he deals with his emotions and those of his father, his dutiful mother and his visiting relatives from Latvia (among whom is his cousin Eriks, who contributes to Yuri\'s education in a most surprising way). And the more Yuri suffers, the more lyrical the book becomes, with sentences that suggest a serious and gifted new writer at work – the kind of "[pļerfect, precise sentences" that Yuri himself revels in when, in his misery ancTsolitude, he shuts himself off from the family and lets his reading pour over his head, sentences "that sang and resonated and rang with the solace that only well-structured language could provide." That\'s mostly what we get in this bittersweet love letter to a father and a city. It\'s prose fiction that might just make Milwaukee famous again. The Chicago Tribune', 'THE PRESS IN OCCUPIED BALTIC STATES While in the free countries the press serves the purpose of informing and entertaining its readers, in the U.S.S.R. it is a means of "educating" the people in the doctrine of Leninism-Stalinism and of spurring it on to greater efforts in production. Hence, despite the shortage or lack of every conceivable article in the U.S.S.R., the state sees to it that a comparatively large number of newspapers are printed. ESTONIA. According to the latest official date (cf. RAHVA HAAL, May 6, 1951) there are in Soviet Estonia, six national, 34 raion, three town and 23 tractor station newspapers and 10 magazines. Of these two national, two town and two raion papers are published in the Russian language. The total is larger than the number of papers published in independent Estonia. In the last months the number of newspapers has increased considerably. While at the end of 1950 Soviet Estonia had no more than 30 newspapers (RAHVA HAAL, Nov. 25, 1950) their total number is now 66, i.e., the number of newspapers has more than doubled in the last half year. Nevertheless, the plan has not been fulfilled in this respect, for according to it every raion (of which there are 39) and every tractor station (61) should have its own paper. In other words, five raions and 38 tractor stations still lack a paper. The number of magazines, on the contrary, has decreased; last year there were 11. The total issue of all papers i and magazines was 550,000 copies in November of last year (RAHVA HAAL, May 5, 1951). Although the number of papers has doubled, the total issue is roughly the same. The only conclusion that may be drawn from this is that the issue of the national newspapers has decreased considerably in the last six months. The main organs of the CC of the Estonian CP and the council of ministers (i.e., the government) are RAHVA HAAL (Voice of the People—Estonian) and SOVIETSKAIA ESTONIA (Russian), which occupy about the same position in Soviet Estonia as PRAVDA does in the Soviet Union. Both appear six times weekly. RAHVA HAAL has a supplement—TODE KODUMAALT (Truth from the Homeland) which appears twice a month and is devoted to repatriation propaganda. It is distributed exclus-i ively among the Estonian refugees abroad, not in Estonia itself. The other national newspapers are TALURAHVALEHT (The Peasants\' Paper) for the kolkhozniks, NOORTE HAAL (The Voice of the Young) which is the organ of the Komsomel, SIRP JA VASAR (Hammer and Sickle) which is devoted mainly to literature, art and culture and OHTULEHT (The Evening Paper) which carries mainly home information. The latter is the only paper which accepts advertisements even from private persons (e.g., offering secondhand clothes and furniture for sale). The provincial papers, i.e., the\' town and raion papers, are the organs of the local CP committees and the town or raion executive committees. The tractor station papers\'are edited by the! political department of the sta-! tions and contain almost exclusivively only party and farming i propaganda. The more important magazines are EESTI BOLSHEVIK (Eston-1 ian Bolshevik) and ABIKS AGI-\' TAATOREILE (Assistance to Agitators), which have their exact counterparts in Moscow and are published by the CC of the Estonian CP. Other larger magazines are STALINLIK NOORUS (Stalinist Youth) published by the CC of the Estonian Komsomol EESTI NAINE (The Estonian Woman) published by the CC of the CP, SOTSIALISTLIK POLLUMAJANDUS (Socialist Agriculture) for the kolkhozes, NOUKOGUDE OPETAJA (The Soviet Teacher) for the teachers, LOOMING (The Creation), a literary journal published by the Association of Estonian writers, and PILT JA SONA (Picture and Word), an illustrated news journal. The national, town and raion papers and the magazines are published by the Publishing House for Newspapers and Magazines subordinated to the Polygraphic, Publishing and Bookselling Board which is an agency of the Council of Ministers of Soviet Estonia or, in other words, they are State-owned. No data are available on who is the publisher of the tractor station papers for hitherto none of these has been seen this side of the iron curtain. All papers and magazines are j subjected to severe preliminary censorship which is exercised by | the Board of Literature and Pub-1 lications, an agency of the Soviet Estonian Council of Ministers. At the lowest corner of the last page of every paper and magazine there are the letters "MB" and a number, which mean that the censor, i.e., the abovementioned board, has given its permission for publication under the printed current number. The press is distributed in Soviet Estonia by the National Press Union which owns all the existing newspaper stands and kiosks. This Press Union is a local enterprise of the Ministry for Interior Affairs. All printing establishments are nationalised, belong to the State and are subordinated to the abovementioned Polygraphic, Publishing and Bookselling Board. The papers have four pages as a rule and are printed on rather inferior paper. The total newsprint allocated to the newspapers and magazines is fixed in the five-year-plan. The newsprint is divided among the various papers and magazines by the State Planning Commission. The foreign news and news about other parts of the USSR come to the papers from TASS, the Soviet Telegraph Agency. As for home news, they are in the overwhelming majority reports on the progress of work and Socialist competitions at kolkhozes and enterprises. Of the four pages of every paper at least one is devoted to purely Communist and Russification propaganda. According to Western ideas the Soviet Estonian papers (as well as the All-Union ones) are insufferably dull. Incidentally, the former Estonian Telegraph Agency ETA still exists in name, but is actually only a local office of TASS. Besides translating TASS news from the Russian into Estonian ETA supplies the national (Tallinn) papers with a small quantity of provincial news and the provincial papers with Tallinn news. This Soviet ETA has no foreign contacts whatsoever. Every Soviet Estonian citizen is coerced by propaganda and the agitators (though not legally obliged) to subscribe to at least two papers: one national and one local one. And the State likewise sees to it that these newspapers are not only printed and subscribed to but also read. Everywhere where there is a "collective," i.e., several people working together, be it in a factory, kolkhoz, school, etc., there are collective newspaper readings eitber daily or several times a week. An agitator or politruk convenes the workers, reads the more important articles to them, makes the appropriate comments and puts questions to his hearers to see whether they have remembered and understood what the government requires of its subjects, for the papers never contain anything of any entertainment value—only the speeches of tbe great, leaders on the current party line on various subjects and accounts of fulfilment, everfulfilment or non-fulfilment of plans and nerms. From a mine of knowledge and a means of relaxation, under the Soviet regime the newspaper and its compulsory reading have become a scourge for the people. LATVIA. What applies to Estonia, also applies to Latvia and Lithuania. Owing to the very character of the Soviet press the people do not read it unless compelled to and subscribe—most unwillingly —only to the absolute minimum. By this attitude they put the press distributing agencies into a very difficult position, for in the planned economy of the USSR whatever is printed must be sold. The less important papers and magazines, however, sell very badly in the Baltic States. S. Vikentiev, manager of the Riga department of "Soyuzpechat" (Union press), e.g., complains bitterly in SOVIETSKAIA LATVIA (22/4/51): "The Bolshevik press plays a most important part in the Communist education of the toiling masses. The Soviet man finds in the papers and periodicals his best friends which help him to work, learn and live. Therefore the distribution of the Soviet press is of an enormous political importance. Unfortunately distribution of some of our republican publications is bad. Very often the entire edition of the paper SKOLOTĀJU AVISE (Teacher\'s Paper) is not distributed and the same applies to the journal BERNIBA (Childhood). KAROGS (Banner) and especially PADOMJU LATVIJAS SKOLA (Soviet Latvian School) and FIZISKA KULTŪRA (Physical Culture)." Comrade Vikentiev believes that this state of things is due to the lack of interest towards the distribution on part of various organisations. He points out that in the whole of Riga, a city with over 400,000 inhabitants FIZISKA KULTŪRA has only 794 subscribers. "The large collective of the institute for physical culture subscribes to 10 copies only. In tti*e VEF factory there are over one thousand organised athletes, but less than 10 subscribe to FIZISKA KULTŪRA." The situation is similar with regard to the other publications mentioned and Vikentiev calls upon the appropriate organisations to take the distribution of these magazines in hand. The explanation is that the above publications are not among those to which subscription is compulsory and only very few people in the Baltic "states absorb Communist propaganda voluntarily. There is no doubt that their distribution will not become satisfactory unless trade union or other organisations oblige their members to subscribe or agitators threaten them into it by hinting that refusal might be regarded as disloyalty to party and State.', 'Izgi c z t ! By Manukla Hoelterhoff RIGA. Latvia – The faded snapshot shows a buxom, neatly dressed woman of about 50 years old holding a watering can in front of a house with a lace-curtained veranda. A hanging ivy tumbles down a sun-dappled corner. Oak and birch trees cast silvery reflections on the windowpanes. The door is open. This woman is my grandmother. In 1941 she tended the roses and geraniums, ivy and hedges for the last time in her house here. Many years later, following stops in Altenburg. Schwerin, Lodz. Berlin and Hamburg, she died, uprooted and unhappy, in the U.S. Eventually, there will be none left who remember her. So it is with Latvia and the other Baltic States: Estonia and Lithuania. The data exist, but every day these small countries, tucked away between the northeast corner of Poland and the Soviet Union, slip more from the collective memory. Increasingly only those with family ties can point to their place on the world globe and remember their histories. Latvia\'s fate stuns even in a century numbed by mass destruction and death. The country-about the size of Belgium and the Netherlands combined-lost about 250,000 people in World War I and a bloody battle of independence fought in 1918 against the new Soviet regime and Germany. In 1939, after a mere 20 years of freedom. Latvia was consigned to the Soviet sphere by the Hitler Stalin Nonaggression Pact. Then, in 1941. the Nazis declared war on the Soviets and invaded Latvia, where they killed 90.000 people, primarily Latvians and Jews, at the camp in Salaspils, a short drive from Riga. Three years later, the Soviets "liberated" Latvia and gobbled it up: one of the many bloody morsels tossed to Stalin at Tehran. A Journey to Riga Lithuania and Estonia, whose experi ence with freedom proved similarly brief, were also forcibly incorporated into the U.S.S.R. Some 600.000 Baits were deported to Siberia or murdered between 1941 and 1949. The U.S. has never formally recognized the annexation of the Baltics, but treats it as a fait accompli. Of our family friends or relatives who stayed behind, none survived the jugger naut of fascists and communists. Still, I had this photograph and like my mother, who is now in her 70s, often wondered what had happened to the house and garden. Together we journeyed this fall to Riga, whose nickname, she insists, was "little Paris." Such journeys are potentially maudlin. Reality rarely stands up to memories. But after one shaky afternoon, Mother put away the handkerchief and calmly surveyed a city in which she was a total stranger. Anger and amazement took the place of sentimental reveries Unlike in most other towns of the Western world (and Riga, however distant, was once part of It). the changes are not so much brought on by modernization as by primitive Sovietization that seeps into every nook and cranny of the 800-year-old city. Visiting Riga was like visiting an archaeological site: Beneath the gloomy layers of socialism that choke the city like soot, there are vivid signs of a once sophis ticated society. Riga\'s statue of liberty-a slender damsel-still holds aloft her three gilded stars, though she now has a statue of Comrade Lenin to keep her company and the street s name has been changed from Freedom Boulevard to Lenin Street. But the street is still handsome-lined with trees and elegant belle epoque mansions, and even big red banners do not completely disfigure the classical-style opera house (whose company Richard Wagner once directed). Lielā amerikāņu laikraksta The Wall Street Journal 19. decembri ievietotā raksta Baltie Memories autore Manuela Hoelterhoffa ir šī izdevuma mākslas daļas redaktore. Viņa nav latviete, un tieši tādēļ autorei nevar pārmest eventuālu noslieci, aprakstol patiesos apstākļus šīsdienas okupētajā Latvijā. Mēs pateicamies autorei par mūsu tautas un valsts ciešanu atgādināšanu miljoniem amerikāņu lasītājiem. Nododam M. Hoelterhoffas rakstu musu laikraksta lasītājiem ar lūgumu iepazīstināt ar tā saturu angliski runājošos draugus un paziņas. Tāpat lūgums dot to lasīt jauniešiem, kam latviešu valodas rakstu lasīšana sagādā grūtības. Baltic Memories In the old section of town, two well-kept guild halls remind the visitor of Riga\'s mercantile past. Already in the 13th century, a Bremen bishop and troupe of Teutonic knights set up a trading station here on the banks of the river Duna. also called Uvina and Daugava, depending on whether you spoke German. Russian or Latvian – one of the oldest European languages. Af ter several flourishing centuries as a citystate with a dominant German upper class, Riga was incorporated in the 18th century into Czarist Russia, which, however, avoided throttling Latvia\'s individual cosmopolitan mixture of different peoples and religions. Our family grew up bourgeois, trilingual and eating enormous quantities of bread, butter, bacon and eggs-the Baltic States were a major exporter of grain and dairy products particularly to England and Germany. A few years ago. Latvia experienced a milk shortage. "The struggle of man against power is the struggle of memory against forgetting." writes Milan Kundera. the Czech emigre, in "The Book of Laughter and Forgetting." Few know this better than the Soviets, and decades ago they implemented a policy of Russification to dilute memories of better, long-gone days that an embittered older generation might pass on to the next. So many Soviets have been sent to live and work in Latvia that Riga\'s population is now only 35% Latvian (53% in the entire country). Russian is the language of the bureaucracy. When the Soviets are not shipping people in. they are shipping them out. The major newspaper. Rigas Balss (Voice of Riga), recently ran an article describing how 400,000 young people up to 40 years of age throughout the Soviet Union are annually impressed to help industrialize farflung areas of the empire. Siberia in particular. The article does not mention that the native Baits are often prevented from and certainly not encouraged to return home. The Latvians we met (mostly friends of emigres) seemed determined to postpone their ethnic disappearance, but resentment mixed with resignation. "Our only form of protest is to not speak Russian and to remember the facts." said a technician who was born during the last war. Other forms of protest are summarily dealt with. A 1982 report on the implementation of the Helsinki pact in Latvia (published by the World Federation of Free Latvians) lists long prison terms for dissidents guilty of the usual "crimes against the state" and reports various botched assassination attempts (such as shoving a former political prisoner under a snowplowl. Every day we made deliveries, rolling out of the hotel, all bundled up and looking like the onion-domed cupolas on the Russian Orthodox Church (now a planetarium) outside our hotel window. In cars\'and apartments we would strip off one coat or two sweaters, mufflers and gloves for our new acquaintances or pull out of our pockets ampules against arthritis. (Few packages reach Latvia these days, since the Soviets, in another move to cut off the Latvians, no longer permit the sender to pre-pay the exorbitant duty.) And all the time, the city\'s physical past indicted the impoverished present. How often during our melancholy walks would my mother stop in front of a building and remember an era as remote as paradise. The 17th-century building in which she worked for a thriving cement company (and once occupied by Peter the Great); the boarded-up shop where my grandmother sold far better things than the stuff we had carted with us; the gloomy cafes, or the 16th-century castle that is now partly occupied by Pioneers-a kind of scout outfit that turns little boys and girls into pillars of communism. In a recent Pioneer publication (reprinted by the U.S. Latvian News Digest) a local educator explained to his perplexed little brood why Soviet stores are empty while those in capitalist countries are full. "People in capitalist countries do not earn enough money to buy such products and therefore they remain on the shelves," wrote the patient pedagogue. "The income of the Soviet peoples has been rising steadily so that now they can buy everything they desire. It is the buying power of the Soviet people that keeps the store shelves empty." Like Night and Day The empty-shelf syndrome might have something to do with the forced industrial ization and militarization of this agrarian land. Instead of expanding Latvia\'s proven potential as a breadbasket, the Soviets are building factories and military installations (exploiting Latvia\'s proximity to European targets). Visitors cannot stray beyond the Baltic capitals, but it is no secret that two of the Soviet Union s eight airborne divisions are stationed in the Baltics; and, given the number of nuclear submarines, large and small surface war ships, transport ships and amphibian landing units said to be parked here, it is surprising they are not bumping into one another. Still, the Latvians hoe and grow as best they can. We ate well at an atmospheric, tiny restaurant in back of the cathedral (inaccessible to the ordinary Latvian without connections). And in the market halls the difference between the products coming from the collective farms and the tiny private plots worked by those who have filled their quota was like night and day. One cheerful cow owner happily treated us to a tasting of cheese samples; another old woman sold us a dripping piece of honeycomb. "The best." she said to her elderly customer, "the best. You remember." Fortified by such tidbits, we slowly drove to the suburb of Imanta, near the once famous Riga beach and knocked on a peeling door. The lace curtains were gone; the ivy had died long ago. Once one small family had lived here; now there were four mailboxes and clearly no new plumbing. The bathtub my grandmother had just been ready to install more than 40 years ago, was standing in front of the veranda, filled with plywood and dirt. After a few minutes, a young man in striped pajamas came down a dilapidated staircase. Martians wouldn\'t have sur prised him more When we told him who we were, he carefully buttoned his top. smiled and introduced himself as Litvinov of the Soviet air force. Then he walked over to a tumbledown thicket and gave his visitors two roses. Ms. Hot\'lterhoff is fhe Journal\'s -irfs prfitor. Izplatīt!']

Representative_Docs: ['SOVIET AIM . . . languages. Party Secretary A. Pelshe, who is also corresponding member of the Latvian Academy, outlined Stalin\'s ideas for the creation of a universal tongue. It would be reactionary, he said, to adhere to the pecularities of specific languages. He advocated, instead, a merger of languages, indicating that the future universal language ought to be based on Russian. Soon after the Conference, Prof. Sherebrennikoff published on February 24th an article in "Sovietskaja Latvia," in which he complains of a shortage of language experts  ideologically adequate textbooks for Baltic schools and higher institutes of learning. He demands that new dictionaries, grammars and textbooks be prepared and that the entire programme for Baltic languages be revised. To cope with Stalin\'s new directives in the linguistic field, entirely new "cadres" of linguists, free from bourgeois-nationalist prejudices, must be trained. Also he found considerable similarity between the Russian and the Baltic lan guages. Conference discussions were unmistakably geared to introduce increasing numbers of Russian words into the Baltic languages, and several thousand such words have already been suggested. Before adjourningl, the Conference adopted resolutions proposed by the Moscow philologists. Despite their lack of knowledge of the Baltic languages, they demanded from their Baltic colleagues "greatest accuracy" in the translation of Marxian-Leninist-Stalinist ideological writings. This accuracy, it appears, consists mainly of introducing full Russian words into the translations. Russian being the world\'s richest language, Baltic linguists would thus enrich their own languages, and at the same time "purify" them from their "bourgeois jargon" and "religious archaisms!" The application of this method of translation would also be considerably simplified, and mutual understanding between the Soviet peoples would be improved. We may add that this method would also transform Latvian and Lithuanian into an artificially created "Russian dialect," although these languages belong to an entirely separate branch of the Hindo-European language group. Despite the dismal forecast for the Baltic nations, as indicated by these frantic Soviet attempts to eliminate their languages, they serve as proof that, if ever, it will be a long road until such a goal will be reached. In all the present social and cultural life of Latvia there prevails yet a deep rift between the Soviet occupants and the Latvian people. Soviets\' Aim to Extinguish Baltic Languages The Soviets are carrying out their Russification policy in the Baltic countries directly or indirectly, in every possible way. They have now put forth a new effort to achieve their aim to exterimnate Latvian "bourgeois nationalism" through pollution ot the Latvian language with Russicisms. The signal for this was given at the Eleventh Convention of the Latvian Communist Party which took place in Riga in December 1951. Latvian writers, editors, teachers and educators were severely criticised for the neglect of ideological instruction and indoctrination. Scientists and linguists were blamed for having failed to grasp the superiority of Russian culture and language. A top official of the Organisation on Safety and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE), the Swiss Gerard Stoudmann, criticised a provision in the election law of Latvia, which states that everybody who is a member of an elected administrative body has to speak Latvian on "the highest level". In 1939, Hitler and Stalin signed the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, on whose basis the Russians occupied Latvia in 1940. In 1941, the Germans occupied Latvia and in 1944 the Russians occupied Latvia again, remaining there until 1991. During the first and second occupation the Russians deported, murdered or made disappear at least 243,000 persons. A further 115,000 persons fled in 1994 on account of the second invasion of the Russians. All this occured to a population of some 2 million people. The number of Latvians (Latvian-speakers) is even nowadays lower it was in 1935. On basis of an article of the Penal Code of the Soviet Union, Latvians (including infants) were deported on account of the following Grime: to be a Latvian. Furthermore, the Soviet authorities conducted an aggressive policy of Russificatlon. Everyone in the Soviet Union was indoctrinated by the State that the Russians were the Great Brother Nation which had brought culture and civilisation to the Small Brother Nations (such as the Latvians). This suggests a cosy family relationship, in reality, the Great Brother Nation and Small Brother Nations corresponded more to Oder-and Untermenschen. Regularly, Russians told Latvians that they must speak the language of human beings, and not thatoī pigs. Around 1990 only one Latvian Ministry (the Ministry of Forestry) used Latvian in stead of Russian. Latvians who declared openly for the use of Latvian on basis of the Soviet law were deported, even right into the 1980s. All countries of the world, with few exceptions, never recognised the de jure occupation of Latvia (and Estonia and Lithuania) by the Soviet Union; and so in international law the Baltic countries remained independent countries, which were de facto occupied by the Soviet Union. The High Commissioner on Minorities, Mr. Max van der Stoel, wrote on 6 April 1993 in his first letter to Mr. Georgs Andrejevs the Latvian Minister for Foreign Affairs: "I think, for instance, of the long years Latvia suffered under Soviet occupation (...)" ['WHAT IS RUSSIA UP TO IN THE BALTICS?

Latvia, the Baltie Republic finally rid of the Soviets and enjoyi ng its first taste of freedom in half a century, is experiencing problems with its large and povverful Russian neigh bour. The most recent example of the bizarre and nuanced circumstances created by the meltdovvn of the Soviet Union was the news that unknovvn to Latvian authorities a submarine Libya had purchased from Moscow was undergoing work in a Russian controlled factory based in a Russian occupied zone in Latvia. Were the Russians merely continuing some of the less attractive features of Moscovv’s old foreign policy? After ali, the U.N. sanetions were imposed on Libya 15 April. The Russians, ostensibly like the Americans voted to comply with these sanetions. The submarine refitting effort would appear to be an attempt both to evade sanetions and, perhaps, to let the blamē fall on Riga. Moreover, the Russians actually used the same Latvian port last year to train the crew of an I raqi missile boat despite the public front of U.S.-Soviet cooperation in the war against Saddam Hussein. And that isn’t ali. At the moment, according to a Latvian official, the Russians are outfitting another submarine in the very same Latvian factory this one intended for shipment to Iran.

When the newly formed Latvian govern ment protested to the U.N. Security Council regarding Russia’s use of one of its key ports in order to continue its military relationship with Libya an international pariah the Russians immediately halted work on the submarine in guestion.
