But the Russians have refused to set a deadline or timetable for the vvithdravval. And Estonia even elaims that Moscovv has recently added troops to those it previously deployed there. Ojārs Kalnins, an official of the Latvian Embassy in VVashington, notēs that the Russian troops, beyond creating a measure of domestic instability, also damage Latvia’s potential to improve itself economically by dravving Western investment. In particular, Kalnins points out, the Russian military presence causes American businessmen to hesitate before investing in Latvia. The episode involving the Libya-bound

submarine raises fairly basie questions. Is Russian President Boris Yeltsin manifesting simple duplicity regarding his willingness to take part in U.N. imposed sanetions? Or is Yeltsi n unable to control his own mi I itary ? Is a rogue group of former military officers men Yeltsin is unable to control endea vouring to maintain a Russian military presence in the Baltics (perhaps even to embarrass Yeltsin)? Senator Larry Pressler deems the issue sufficiently important that he has asked Secretary of State James Baker to urge the United Nations to send observers directly to Russian held military bases throughout the Baltie region. Pressler wonders whether the difficulties that Latvia is now confronting also plague other Baltie States. Needless to say, Pressler and others are especially concerned that in spite of sanetions —there may be other Libya bound vessels under repair in Russian occupied Baltie ports. It should be noted that the governments of ali three Baltie States have joined in urging U.N. inspectors to take a close look at just what is going on inside the Russian controlled bases on theirown soil. Latvia secured its independence last fall. But VVashington, even though it established formai diplomatic ties with Riga, quickly turned its attention to other concerns, perhaps forgetting that the Latvians would not truly be free until Russian troops agreed to depart. Pressler and Senator DeConcini now plān to introduce an amendment to the president’s bill to provide assistance to the independent States of the former Soviet Union. The Pressler—DeConcini measure would link financial assistance for Moscow with

“signifieant” progress on Russian troop withdrawal from the Baltie States. The president’s effort to provide support to the newly independent republics of the former Soviet Union merits support. But so does the Pressler—DeConcini amendment. Assistance to Russia only makes sense if Moscow desists from ali efforts to deprive the Baltie States of their full independence. Similarly, before any such assistance is provided, VVashington needs to be certain that Moscow has abandoned any notion of trans ferring technology and weaponry to former Soviet elients like Libya.

VVashington Post, 7May 1992.', 'According to the latest issue of "Fighting Ships," Russia is reported at present to have 37 0 submarines in active service, 120 under construction, while a new building programme which has been launched makes provision lor a further 1000 underwater craft. Britain has 53 submarines on the active list and another 18 on immediate reserve. A statement on the strength of the United States fleet shows 194 submarines, but it is estimated that by the end of 1952 only 100 submarines will be on the active list. In the foreword, it is pointed out by Jane's that the trend by the principal naval Powers during 1951 has continued towards the improvement of aircraft carriers and in the offensive against submarines. Larger aircraft carriers are being constructed so as to be capable of operating bigger and more effective aircraft, and smaller aircraft carriers for antisubmarine warfare. Large destroyers are also being adapted as submarine "killers" of a fleet of anti-submarine escorts, while smaller destroyers are being converted into antisubmarine frigates. Jane's describes the new Royal Navy anti-submarine frigates ReWorld's Submarine Fleet lentless and Rosket, as a new conception of submarine "killer" ships. Two other destroyers, Rapid and Roebuck, are being similarly converted. They have completenew armament. Great use has been made of aluminium. In discussing the fleets of the various Powers, Jane's says that it is extremely difficult to obtain information regarding the Russian navy, and emphasises that details of new construction in Russia are presented with the greatest reserve as no official confirmation can be obtained. The strength of the Russian navy is given as 520,000, excluding the navy flying corps. Royal Navy officers and ratings now number 143,500, not including naval air services, while the strength cf the United States navy is 705,000 officers and ratings. The marine corps is not included in the United States figures. The new programme of Russian naval construction is reported to include three battleships, 20 cruisers, 120 destroyers, and 1000 sea-going submarines. Construction of one to four battleships of a new powerful type in Russian dockyards is persistently reported. These ships, it is said, will have a displacement of between 42,000 and 45,000 tons — equal in size to the Royal Navy's biggest battleship, Vanguard, and the United States battleship Missouri. One of these new Russian ships named The Soviet Union, is re» ported to have carried out trials late this year. This class is said to be equipped with catapult towers for firing radio-controlled aerial torpedoes, and rocket-firing apparatus and radio-controlled guns. These vessels are also believed to carry very elaborate radar installations and anti-aircraft and anti-submarine protection devices. No Russian aircraft carriers are listed by Jane's, although Russia is known to have a strong naval air force. Russian submarines listed by Jane's vary from long-range 1500-tonners to small two-men craft. Britain's whole naval programme, says Jane's, is largely directed towards the underwater menace. The United States new naval construction programme includes a giant aircraft carrier, which will be the biggest in the world, two ocean escorts, seven submarines, 106 mine sweeping boats, and about 100 other vessels. The United States navy at present comprises about 5000 vessels, including 120 aircraft carriers, 15 battleships, 69 cruisers, 384 destroyers, 263 escort destroyers and frigates and 194 submarines. Less than a quarter of these are on the active list. . Jane's says that by the end of 1952, the United States active fleet will have three very large aircraft carriers, nine fleet aircraft carriers, four battleships, 19 cruisers, 340 destroyers and escort vessels, and 100 submarines. The United States navy will soon have in service three different cruiser types of novel design. They will be submarine, hunters and "killers." France and the Netherlands also are building powerful antisubmarine units, and converting old vessels into submarine chasers. Jane's  however, that many of the rearmament measures in the navies of the United States, Britain, France, the Netherlands, and Italy, which have been engendered by the inter-1 national tension, are still largely in the project stage.', 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER "Diplomacy is the Continuation of War by Other Means" On August 19, Latvians around the world looked back on that fateful day two years ago, when Russian armored personnel carriers stormed through the streets of Riga in an echo of the abortive putsch attempt taking place in the Soviet capital of Moscow. Since the failure of the putsch, the Baltic governments have taken huge steps towards reestablishing the sovereignty and independence that Soviet tanks robbed from them more than fifty years ago. The Soviet Union, which occupied the Baltics, is no more, having been replaced by 12 new nations, each moving at its own speed to establish their own independence. The days of repression, fear and intimidation seemed over—the former Soviet Union was much too busy trying to emerge from the rubble of a crumbled empire to bother with old dreams and desires. So it seemed. Today we know better. Russia, which has selectively declared itself the direct successor to the Soviet state, has made clear its wish to take up also the renewal of a greater Russian empire. In many parts of the former empire, it is doing so by stirring up armed conflict, backing one faction against another in bitter civil wars now raging in the Central Asian republics, the Transcaucasus republics and Moldova. In Ukraine, Russian lawmakers make demands for the return of Ukrainian territory while the governments play an upwardly spiraling game of "chicken over the former Soviet Black Sea fleet. However, armed intervention by the new bosses in Moscow is not taking place in Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania. Are these three countries any less threatened? Clearly, the Baltic states remain of interest to those planning to reconstitute the Soviet/ Russian empire, people like Russian Vice President Alexander Rutskoi and legislators Ruslan Hasbulatov and Sergei Stankevich. How else to explain the insidious, ongoing campaign concerning the alleged violation of the "human rights" of ethnic Russians in the Baltic states? September 1993 – Vol. XIII, No. 7 What other meaning can there be to the suspension of Russian troop withdrawal from Lithuania, where only 2,500 active duty soldiers remain? How else to interpret the hero's welcome in Moscow given to recently extradited former Riga "Black Beret" deputy commander Sergei Parfyonov, who was convicted by a Latvian court of involvement in the events of both January and August 1991, where a one-hour interview was broadcast on the Russian parliament's television channel? Russia seems bent on using diplomacy, veiled threats and public relations tactics to achieve in Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania what it is using tanks and guns to achieve elsewhere in the former Soviet Union-the renewal of Russian domination over the area its leaders continue to call the "near abroad". And, it seems, the American government, led by the American media, is more than willing to lend its collective credibility to these efforts. The Baltics continue to take a pounding on editorial pages over their citizenship policies and over other laws, which are collectively referred to as "human rights" issues. This, despite 15 multilateral investigations of the rights situation in the Baltic countries, which have found the Baltic governments blameless of violations of human rights. The fact that individual transgressions do take place against laws and procedures set by these governments is, to Russian eyes, a sufficient reason to tar them with the same brush used by the international community for nations like Myanmar (Burma) and Cuba. That such prominent columnists as Kremlinologist Dmitri Simes feel free to lend credence to Moscow's pleas of disappointment "that the Clinton team expressed concern over the slow pace of withdrawal of the few Russian troops remaining in Latvia and Estonia, but would not publicly criticize the disenfranchisement of the vast majority of Russian-speaking residents living there" (The Washington Post, 08/10/93) gives U.S. Government officials all the leverage they need to state that "ethnic Russians are not being treated fairly in Lithuania and the other Baltic states" (The Washington Post, 08/24/93). There is an old saying about men, or in this case governments, that live in glass housesthat they should be careful what stones they throw. About 200,000 ethnic Latvians live in Russia, 100,000 of them in the Siberian regions of Omsk and Tomsk. Are these people receiving the same minority rights the Latvian government has granted its minorities? No Latvianlanguage schools exist in these areas to educate this minority group. No Latvian books or newspapers are published in these areas, as opposed to the wide range of Russian-language materials available in Latvia. Ethnic Latvians wishing to reactivate their Latvian citizenship are being harassed and frustrated by Russian tax and property laws. Russia claims to have the interests of the Russian minorities in the Baltic countries in mind when it complains of discrimination and resists troop withdrawal. However, the Russian policy of preventing the completion of troop withdrawal from the Baltics is keeping many military officers and their families trapped on plundered military bases, with no heat, fuel or food. These people want nothing more to return to Russia and find a roof over their heads and food to eat. If this is how Russia treats its own defenders, how can the world take seriously its protestations of mistreatment? The only language the Russian government seems to understand is that of force—in this case, the force of a threatened cutoff of American foreign aid if the Russian and Baltic governments do not reach agreements on troop withdrawal, with definite dates of completion, by October 6, 1993. Both Russia and Lithuania seem to be moving back to the negotiating table. How successful, however, can these negotiations be if the Lithuanian side can no longer trust the Russian side to live up to its bargains? Troop withdrawal negotiations are also at a standstill with Latvia and Estonia. Latvia cannot get a response to its request that negotiations be resumed on August 29. The reason: the Russian side pleads "technical difficulties" involving delegation members out on vacation. It strains credibility to state that delegation members would be unavailable, especially at a time when desperately needed aid could be placed in jeopardy by American law and Russian inaction-or worse. What can we, as concerned Americans, do in this situation? I believe it is time to dust the cobwebs off of our press contact lists and renew old friendships with congressional offices. Although a major battle in the renewal of Baltic independence was fought, and won, in the late August days of 1991, the war against Baltic independence started by Nazi Germany and Soviet Russia on August 23, 1939 has yet to end. The legacy of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact continues to haunt Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania, in the form of Russian desires to reestablish its "sphere of influence" over the Baltic countries. It is time to once again start calling our Congressmen and Senators, the White House and the State Department, and the local newspaper editor and television reporter, to remind them about America's promises, and America's legal obligations, to the Baltic countries. I believe it was the German statesman yon Clausewitz that said "diplomacy is the continuation of war by other means". Today, we see the truth of this saying in Russia's attempts to win the upper hand in Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania. We, as people who care about the Baltic countries and their people, cannot and must not permit America to abandon the Baltics just when they need them most. □']

Representative_Docs: ['(Contd. from previous issue! On September 1, 1939, Lithuania declared her neutrality in the war that broke out between Germany and Poland, and on October 10, 1939, under the pressure of war circumstances, she concluded the Treaty of Mutual Assistance, with the USSR, the Preamble of which ouce again re-affirmed the principles of respect for national independence and- non-interference in internal affairs, and recalled the Treaty of Peace of 1920, and the Pact of Non-Aggression of 1926, with the provisions for the peaceful solution of conflicts. The infamous Molotov-Ribbentrop deal of August 23, 1939. amended by the secret Soviet-German Protocol of September 28, 1939, and the subsequent occupation of Lithuania, June 15, 1940, by the Soviet armed forces was an act of unprovoked aggression perpetrated for the political aims of the USSR, and a flagrant violation of all bilateral and international treatries and conventions binding both States. By this aggression the Soviet Government committed an international crime and, thus entire responsibility rests with the Soviet Union, obliging it to re-establish the violated right and repair the damage caused. The one-sided anu forcible incorporation of Lithuania into the soviet Union having taken place under illegal conditions and not having conferred any rights to the latter, the situation existing hefore the occupation and the socalled incorporation remains valid, and the legal status of Lithuania is the same as she enjoyed before the occupation. Lithuania remains a member of the interantional community and is subject to rights and obligations. All treaties concluded by Lithuania with other States remain in force and are binding on both parties. Lithuanian laws still remain in force and international tribunals in cases where one of both parties are Lithuanian citizens, apply— according to the rules of private interantional law — Lithuanian laws. The Lithuanian diplomatic and consular representatives accredited to foreign governments continue to function and assume the defense of the rights and interests of Lithuania and of its citizens. Only the exercise of the sovereign rights of Lithuania is temporarily suspended in Lithuania, by the fact of military occupation (occupatio bellica). Wherever ascendency by force ceases, the Lithuanian authorities legally exercise this power. All the States, except those which had adhered to the Axis and those within the Soviet sphere of influence, have refused to recognise the legality of the act of Soviet rule in Lithuania. Thus, the Soviet Union is an illegal occupant of the Lithuanian State and a fraudulent usurper of the inalienable rights of the Lithuanian people. As long as this situation will last, til: Gf,\'-ernment of the Soviet Union is bound by the law of nations which governs the rights and duties of the occupant State. Such acts as (a) forcible sovietisation of the country; (b) extension of the territory of the Lithuanian State; Soviet Union legislation to the (c) the Soviet electoral law and the compulsion exerted on the population of the occupied country to particpate in the elections of the constitutional organs of Soviet Russia; (d) the forcible recruitment of Lithuanian youth in the armed forces of the USSR- (c) the spoliation of the country and population for the benefit of the I Russian State; (f) the forcible russification of the younger generation; (g) physical annihilation of the enslaved nation by means of torture, murder and mass deportation into Siberia and the Arctic Regions of the Soviet Lmon—all that constitutes a continuous and flagrant contravention of this law. a By signing the Charter of the United Nations and later by adhering to the Universal Declaration of Human Rights—the Convention on Prevention and Pv> ishment of the Crime of Genocii lias equally been signed by the Soviet Government but not yet ratified — the government of the Soviet Union has assumed some explicit understandings in regards HUMAN FREEDOM IS BEING CRUSHED , to their citizens, namely: (a) an individual\'s right to life, freedom and personal security (Art. 3 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights); (b) an individual\'s right to human treatment (Art. 5); (c) an individual\'s right to conditions enabling him, in the case of arrest and trial, to defend himself in a public process (Art. 11, I); (d) the principle according to which nobody can be condemned for actions which, at the moment they were committed, did not constitute an offense from the viewpoint of the national or international law (Art. 11, II); (c) an indiviual\'s right to freely choose his residence inside a country (Art. 13, I), and his right to return to his country (Art. 13, II); (f) the right to freely found one\'s family (Art. 16); (g) the right to manifestly confess one\'s religion (Art. 18.) A fortiori and implicitly the Government of the Soviet Union is bound to grant and to respect these rights and fundamental freedoms in regard to the citizens of the occupied Lithuanian State. 11. Realities in Occupied Lithuania. A. —Contravention Against The Provisions Of The UN Charter And The Law Of Nations In General. The pre-arranged aggression against Lithuania and the two other Baltic States has been carried on by the Government of the USSR at a time when the political and military equilibrium of Europe collapsed, France being defeated and Paris occupied by Hitler\'s armored divisions. These circumstances permitted the rulers of the Kremlin not only to invade the Baltic States but also to stage their incorporation into the Soviet Union. Sovietisation followed. Sovietisation or Soviet colonialism means, on one hand, the enslavement of men and their transporation into Soviet mass man by (a) spoliation and annihilation of all educated and comparatively well-to-do classes of the nation; (b) non-respect of all human rights and fundamental freedoms; (c) destruction of families; (d) breaking up national traditions; i ( (c) the communist indoctrination of the younger generation; territory of the Lithuanian state; (f) uprooting the people, and, on the other hand, the ruthless and reckless exploitation of the economic resources of the occupied country and its manpower for the benefit of the Russian State and its armament program. The Soviet rule in enslaved Lithuania Is but a mockery of all principles of the law of nations, of the provisions of the United Nations charter, and of the established principles of international morality. A spontaneous and general uprising of the Lithuanian people against the communist oppressor took place in Lithuania on June 2 3, 1941, following the outbreak of hostilities between Germany and the Soviet Union. All larger cities were cleared of Russian garrisons, and the restoration of Lithuania\'s independence was proclaimed. At some places the Soviet administration was overwhelmed so suddenly that even the agents of NKVD and NKGB had to flee without taking or destroying their archives. Thus a number of top secret NKVD and NKGB files were seized by the Lithuanian insurrectionists in Kaunas. Siauliai, Alytus and other cities. These documents, some on the stationery of the Moscow headquarters of these nefarious institutions, revealed the inner operation of the NKVD-NKGB forces which maintain the Communist Party in power and extend Russian control over non-Russian areas and countries. Here are some of these documents pertaining to the mass deportations from Lithuania: (1) Order No. 0054 of November 28, 1940 of the nominal head of the NKVD for Lithuania, Guzevicius, directing the listing of all "people\'s enemies, including all non-Communists and aliens." Twice this document refers to the Order No. 00122 3 of the NKVD of the USSR, dated: 11th October, 1939. That proves that Moscow Headquarters of the NKVD had issued Its first order directing the preparations for mass liquidation of "the anti-Soviet and anti-social elements" of the Baltic States at a time when Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia were independent, but shortly after the signature of the second secret Molotov-Ribbentrop Proto. col which sealed the fate of Lithuania. "The index account must cover all those persons who by reason of their social and political past, national-chauvinistic opinions, religious convictions, moral and political inconstance, are opposed to the socialistic order and thus might be used by the intelligence services of foreign countries and by the counter-revolutionary centres for anti-soviet purposes. "These elements include: (a) All former members of the anti-soviet political parties, organisations and groups; trotckyists, rightists, essers (socialist revolutionists), mensheviks, social-democrats, anarchists and the like; (b) All former members of national chauvinistic anti-soviet parties, organisations and groups; nationalists, Young Lithuania, voldemarists, populists, christian democrats, members of nationalist terroristic organisations ("Iron Wolf"), active members of student fraternities, active members of Riflemen\'s Association, Catholic terrorist organisation! "White Horse"; (c) Former gendarmes, policemen, former employees of political and criminal police and of the prisons; (d Former officers of the Czar, Petliura and other armies; (c) Former officers andf members of military courts at the armies of Lithuania and Poland; (f) Former political bandits and volunteers of the white and other armies; (g) Persons expelled from the Communist Party and Commyouth for anti-party offences; (h) All deserters, political emigrants, re-emigrants, repatriates and contrabandists; (i) All citizens of foreign countries, representatives of foreign firms, employees of offices of foreign countries, former citizens of foreign countries, former employees of legations, firms, concessions and stock companies of foreign countries; (To be Continued)', 'THE TORONTO STAR Glasnost unleashes debate in Baltic states By Ron Vastokas Political ideas seldom work out the way they are supposed to. When Mikhail Gorbachev unveiled his program of glasnost (openness) three years ago. he intended to expose the problems of Soviet society to public scrutiny, debate and reform. Today, glasnost has started challenging the very legitimacy of the Soviet empire. Nowhere is this challenge more apparent than in the Baltic states where it centres around a sensitive chapter in Soviet history. On Aug. 23. 1939. foreign ministers Vyaeheslav Molotov and Joachim Yon Hibbentrop signed a Soviet-German non-aggression pact in Moscow. Attacned to the * pact was a secret protocol that placed Estonia. Latvia and Lithuania within the "sphere of influence" of the Soviet Union. The pact gave Hitler the green light to march into Poland and start World War II and the secret protocol left Stalin a free hand to seek a "solution of the problem of the Baltic countries." The "solution" turned out to be a military occupation of the Baltic states and their incorporation into the Soviet Union. The West, including Canada. *has never recognized the seizure of the Baltic states as legitimate. The Soviet Union, on the other hand, has remained silent about Stalin\'s accord with the Nazis and has kept a tight lid on the subject of Baltic independence. With the coming ol" glasnost. however, these issues have become difficult to avoid. At a writers\' seminar last year Vilnius, the capital of Lithuania, emigre authors were told they could have their articles published in a state-run weekly fco long as the incorporation of Lithuania into the U.S.S.R. is not questioned. There are no taboos (j about other themes." And the Soviet president An-W viromyko put the official line on the subject more bluntly. On a visit to Vilnius in early February, a few days before the anniversary marking the independence of Lithuania, he said that Soviet power.in Lithuania "is stronger than ever" although "possibly there ore some lunatics in some places abroad who fail to understand that." Despite official threats. Baltic activists put glasnost to a test last year and staged demonstrations on Aug. 23 in Vilnius, Riga and Tallinn. Speakers denounced the Hitler-Stalin pact, commemorated mass deportations of the earlyyears ol Soviet occupation and raised the question of self-determination. As today\'s 49th anniversary of the pact approached, there were renewed calls for demonstrations to repudiate it. A remarkably outspoken "appeal" signed by 25 Lithuanian activists described the pact as an instrument by which the Baltie States "became new colonies of Russian imperialism." These nationalist aspirations have received support from activists in Moscow. The shortlived Democratic Union that challenged the supremacy of the Communist party on May 8, also called for the withdrawal of Soviet torces from eastern Europe jnd the Baltic States. The most reeen! development is the publication and broadcast (Aug. 12) of the Stalin-Hitler pact un state-run media in Estonia. Activists who have been pressing for an independent Estonia say that this first official disclosure of the secret protocols portends a change in Kremlin policy. The full text of the pact was also made public in the Lithuanian News on Aug. 5. What is surprising about these developments is the aijeed with which they ore taking place, the blunt language that is being used and the expanding range of critiJ. Mounting public pressure has led to an unprecedented official permission to hold a rally in Vilnius today to commemorate the signing of the non-aggression pact between Germany and the Soviet Union. The pact is but one of a number of issues that deal with economic and cultural autonomy, the dangers posed to national survival by Russification and the worsening state of the environment. Up to now, the Kremlin\'s response has been somewhat ambiguous, giving no clear indication as to how Gorbachev intends to cope with the rising tide to national assertiveness. There appears to be an uneasy stand-off as Soviet authorities allow some national sentiments to be aired and then selectively crack down, in the heavy-handed manner of the past, on those who organize or steer them. At the same time. Gorbachev has said that "many of the problems the Soviet Union is facing now are a result of a too harsh and insensitive nationalities policy in the past." and has promised to deal "very substantially" with this "most fundamental and vital question." Whether Gorbachev is thinking of forcible seizure of sovereign territory when he speaks of a "harsh policy in the past" is not clear. But the Baits are no longer watting to find out. They are telling Gorbachev they know what happened 50 years ago and that they want amends. It is ironic that glasnost. meant to restore the legitimacy of the Soviet state and party, has now placed the initiative in the hands of the people. □ Ron Vastokas is Associate Professor of Anthropology at Trent University. Treaty ofNonaggression Between Germany and the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics The Government of the German Reich and the Government of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics desirous of strengthening the cause of peace between Germany and the U.S.S.R., and proceeding from the fundamental provisions of the Neutrality agreement concluded in April 1926 between Germany and the U.S.S.R., have reached the following agreement: ARTICLE I Both High Contracting Parties obligate themselves to desist from any act of violence, any aggressive action, and any attack on each other, either individually or jointly with other powers. ARTICLE II Should one of the High Contracting Parties become the object of belligerent action by a third power, the other High Contracting Party shall in no manner lend its support to this third power. ARTICLE 111 The Governments of the two High Contracting Parties shall in the future maintain continual contact with one another for the purpose of consultation in order to exchange information on problems affecting their common interests. ARTICLE IV Neither of the two High Contracting Parties shall participate in any grouping of powers whatsoever that is directly or indirectly aimed at the other party. ARTICLE V Should disputes or conflicts arise betwen the High Contracting Parties over problems of one kind or another, both parties shall settle these disputes or conflicts exclusively through friendly exchange of opinion or. if necessary, through the establishment of arbitration commissions. ARTICLE VI The present treaty is concluded for a period of ten years, with the proviso that, in so far as one of the High Contracting Parties does not denounce it one year prior to the expiration of this period, the validity of this treaty shall automatically be extended for another five years. ARTICLE VII The present treaty shall be ratified within the shortest possible time. The ratifications shall be exchanged in Berlin. The agreement shall enter into force as soon as it is signed. Done in duplicate, in the German and Russian languages. Moscow, August 23, 1939. For the Government With full power of the of the German Reich: Government of the U.S.S.R.: v RIBBENTROP V MOLOTOV Secret Additional Protocol On the occasion of the signature of the Nonaggression Pact between the German Reich and the Union of Socialist Soviet Republics the undersigned plenipotentiaries of each of the two parties discussed in strictly confidential conversaions the question of die boundary of their respective spheres of influence in Eastern Europe, These conversations led to the following conclusions: 1. In the event of a territorial and political rearrangement in the areas belonging to the Baltic States (Finland. Estonia, latvia, Lithuania), and northern boundary of Lithuania shall represent the boundary of the spheres of influence of Germany and the U.S.S.R. In this connection the interest of Lithuania in the Vilna area is recognized by each party. 2. In the event of a territorial and political rearrangement of the areas belonging to the Polish state the spheres of influence of Germany and the U.S.S.R. shall be bounded approximately by the line of the rivers Narew, Vistula, and San. The question of whether the interests of both parties make desirable the maintenance of an independent Polish state and how such a state should be bounded can only be definitely determined in the course of further political developments. In any event both Governments will resolve this question by means of a friendly I agreement. v s STATE DEPARTMENT 3. With regard to Southeastern Europe attention is called by the Soviet side to its interest in Bessarabia. The German side declares its complete political disinterestedness in these areas. 4. This protocol shall be treated by both parties as strictly secret. Moscow, August 23, 1939. For the Government Plenipotentiary of the of the German Reich: Government of the U.S.S.R.: v. RIBBENTROP V MOLOTOV German-Soviet Boundary and Friendship Treaty The Government of die German Reich and the Government of the U .S. S. R. consider it as exclusively their task, after the collapse of the former Polish state, to re-establish peace and order in these terrotiries and to assure to the peoples living there a peaceful life in keeping with their national character. To this end, they have agreed upon the following: ARTICLE I The Government of the German Reich and the Government of the U.S.S.R. determine as the boundary of their respective national interests in the territory of the former Polish state the line marked on the attached map, which shall be described in more detail in a supplementary protocol. ARTICLE II Bodi parties recognize the boundary of die respective national interests established in article 1 as definitive and shall reject any interference of third powers in this settlement. ARTICLE 111 The necessary reorganization of public administration wHI be effected in the areas west of the line specified in article 1 by the Government of the German Reich, in the areas east of this line by the Government of the U.S.S.R. ARTICLE IV The Government of the German Reich and die Government of the U.S.S.R. regard this settlement as a firm foundation for a progressive development of the friendly relations between their peoples. ARTICLE V This treaty shall be ratified and the ratifications shall be exchanged in Berlin as soon as possible. The treaty becomes effective upon signature. Done in duplicate, in the German and Russian languages. Moscow. September 28, 1939. For the Government By authority of die of die German Reich: Government of the U.S.S.R.: J RIBBENTROP W MOLOTOW Confidential Protocol The Government of the U.S.S.R. shall place no obstacles in die way of Reich nationals and other persons of German descent residing in the temtories under its jurisdiction, if they desire to migrate to Germany or to the territories under German I jurisdiction. It agree* that such removals shall be carried out by agents of the Government of the Reich in cooperation with the competent local authorities and that the property rights of die emigrants shall be protected A corresponding obligation is assumed by the Government of the German Reich in respect to the persons of Ukrainian or White Russian descent residing in the territories under its jurisdiction. As soon as the Government of the U.S.S.R. shall take special measures on Lithuanian territory to protect its interests, the present German-Lithuanian border, for the purpose of a natural and simple boundary delineation shall be rectified in such a way that the Lithuanian territory situated to the southwest of the line marked on the attached map should fall to Germany. Representative_Docs: ['A Nation in the Making AUSTRALIA ACHIEVES NATIONHOOD The Anzac landing at Gallipoli on April 25, 1915, made Australia a nation in the eyes of the world. She went into the! first world war as a part of the 1 British Empire, not even making a separate declaration of war; by 1 the time the Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1919, she had become an independent, self-assertive nation, even among the great powers of the world. It is true that after the war, Australia tended to go into isolation again, but the second world war proved to her finally that she was only a section of struggling humanity. Aiinough in the years before 1914 Australia had frequently made it clear that she resented interference from the British government, and wished to control her own army and navy, no Australian thought it surprising that she automatically entered the war against the German Empire at the same time as the moiner country. The British government had wisely kept Australia fully informed about the diplomatic moves by which she sought to prevent hostilities in Europe while at the same time maintaining her treaty obligations. "The crimson thread of kinship" proved far stronger than political squabbles, either within Australia or within the Empire, and both the Prime Minister (Cook) and the leader of the Opposition (Fisher), supported Britain without qualification. The outbreak of the first world war found Australia in the midst of a political crisis. On the request of the Cook Government, in strife with a Labor-controlled Senate, the Governor-General, Sir Ronald Munro-Ferguson had granted a double dissolution on July 30, only five days before Britain declared war on Germany. A bitterly fought election campaign followed during the next six weeks, so that Australia entered the war under conditions which might have caused uncertainty in foreign policy. But no one contradicted the Prime Minister when he said tht day after the double dissolution "Whatever happens, Austral k is part ot the Empire and is ir the Empire to the full; when th< Empire is at war, Australia v at war." Indeed, the leader of the Op position was equally emphatic "Should the worse happen aftei every aung has oeen done tha honor will permit, we Austral ians will help and defend thi mother country to our last mai and our last shilling." The Great War was not th< first in which Australian troop had fought alongside the Brit ish. In 1885 a contingent iron New South Wales had been sen to the Sudan and in 1900 a smal naval force had gone to China More serious had been Austral ian participation in the Boe War (1899-1902) in which 518 o our troops had been killed. There had been many oppo nents of Australia\'s part in thes campaigns, especially in Soutl Africa. But even during the hea of the election controversies, n responsible politician suggeste> that Australia should not joi: in the struggle against Germany It was a unanimity that came t a surprise to many, including th German government, which ha been led to believe from i 1 agents that British dominion would act independently, an jealously refuse to be forced int war. The glorious records of the Australian volunteer army in Gallipoli, France, Palestine, andļ eiswhere earned the respect of i the whole world. By the end of the war, Australia had enlisted 417,000 troops, sent 330,000 of I them overseas, suffered 227,000 casualties, and had lost nearly 60,000 dead. This was an amazing record, especially when it! is remembered that the Austral- ļ ian people twice refused by referendum to endorse conscription. W. M. Hughes Prime Minister Fisher resigned and his office was taken by his brilliant and energetic Attorney- i General, William Morris Hughes.! Hughes was a member of the Imperial War Cabinet, in Lonao.i and his forthright opinions on a number of vital issues came as a shock to many British.  He certainly made i it impossible for anyone to forget mat Australia was playing an important part in the war, and that her views must always be considered. Although very provocative and sometimes rather too dramatic, Hughes gained many points against Mr. Lloyd George, the great British war leader, just \'as Mr. Curtin differed from Mr. Churchill during the second \' world war when he believed that the best interests of Australia ļ were concerned. Both Hughes and Curtin proved that loyalty I and difference of opinion can exist together. A delegation from Australia, j led by Hughes, took part in the discussions which led to the j Peace oi Versailles in 1919. Again the fiery little Welshļ man crossed swords with great s statesmen, and he gained for Australia, probably against Jap| an, the mandadtes for what had been German New Guinea and , inc. island.al Kauxu. Australia, like the other Dom| inions, signed the Treaty of Versailles as a separate nation, although she had not declared v. ur independently in 1914. -She j was aiso given a place in the League of aNtions and the Internation Labor Office. Thus the whole world gave i legal recognition to the nationhood of Australia which had oeen proved and earned during the war. This had been done without destroying the British Empire though its character had altered very much and has drastically changed since then. It is now understood that the Dominions could enter into separate treat-1 ies, appoint their own ambassadors, and generally follow an ! independent foreign policy from 1 Great Britain if they so desired It is interesting to note thai [ Australia has moved slowest ir • this direction, the Anzus Pact be-  ing the only important treatj ■ which she has negociated outsid* ■ the framework of the British j i Commonwealth relationships. M Always resentful of any kinc of imposition, Australia has nevertheless \'ertheless kept very close to tht ! mother country. The ties 1 blood have made understanding t and harmony the most remark ļ able features of relationshir I Britain ever since Federation. 1 It would not be right, how • ever to ignore another opinior J about the effect of the firs – ļ world war on the developmen • of Australian nationhood. It i; s expressed by Professor G. V • ļ Portus in his excellent pam phlet "Britain and Australia 3 (Longmans). He writes: "It is an exaggeration to sa> that Australia became a natioi at Gallipoli. Nations are no born in a day—not even in . day of battle. "There were many who sai< that the 1914-18 war brough Australia into world affairs an< taught her her place in th • comity of nations. But in trutl 3 the war had very little perman ent effect on the Australia: external outlook. The pre-wa belief that Britain and th British were more interested ii European politics than in th Empire tended to be confirmee "This encouraged the belie that Australians could go oi regarding themselves as a com munity that might fashion it own standards in isolation an let the world go by. "The war did not dispel thi illusion. It took the depressio: n to convince Australia that sh was bound in the bundle of ir 5. ternational life."', 'MORE AUSTRALIANS JOIN ARMED SERVICES Recent events In Korea having caused a real "boom" in recruiting for the Australian Regular Army, many re emit ung depots report increases 9 to 400* 0f wouldbe -soldiers. The challenge of the Korean war-a possible precursor to another world conflict between democracy and totalitarianism – has spurred Australian recruiting. More men are seeking to join the armed services than at any time since the end of World War 11. This the public knows, but the average man in the street (and still fewer women) don\'t know much about the tests which open the gates for a recruit seeking into the armed forces. There\'s quite a bit for a recruit to go through from the time he enters a recruiting centre until he sets the seal on a goodly portion of his future with the fateful words of the oath of enlistment: "I swear that I will well and truly serve our Sovereign Lord the King, in the Military (or Naval or Air) Forces for a period of – years (six in the Army, six or 12 in the Navy and Air Foroe) or until sooner lawfully discharged dismissed, or removed, and that I will resist His Majesty\'s enemies and cause His Majesty\'s peace to be kept and maintained, and that I will in all matters appertaining to my service faithfully discharge my duty according to law." Recruits for the Army musJ be natural-born or naturalised British subjects. The Air Force and Navy, not requiring the same mass enlistment, can affor( to be slightly more selective and demand that the recruit must also be of substantially European descent and have British-born or naturalised parents. Before they enlist any man the three services first searcn his military record (if any) to see if he has been convicted of any military offence, check police files to see if he has any civil convictions, and ask security services to find out if he has ever engaged in subversive activity or shown allegiance to the subversive doctrines of foreign Powers. Whether men with civilian or military convictions will be permitted to enlist is left to the discretion of recruiting officers. There\'s little hope for a man convicted, of a serious offence. A man who has a security record or has lied about his" nationality will also be rejected. Many of the men who want to join up are under 21 and these have to obtain the written consent of their parents or guardians signed by a justice of the peace. The R.A.A.F.-technical of the services-demands a relatively high standard of education from recruits. Air-crew recruits must hold an intermediate certificate or its equivalent. Recruits fo ground staff must have comple ted their second year of hig school. The Navy also asks for a intermediate certificate, bu applicants who did not reac this standard can oualify -fo enlistment by passing a fairl simple education test. Fleet Air Arm recruits mus hold an intermediate certi-Pioa te with a pass in four subjec ts, including English and ma thematicB. The Army is more easily satisfied. A completed primary education is all that is required and. aa in the Navy, those who failed to reach the standard are admitted if they pass a simple education test which should be a push-over for anyone who can read and write. The first thing a would – be recruit has to do is to fill out an application form. This asks for basic information on such subjects as place and date of birth, occupation, marital status, and is designed to give an indication of the type of post for which the applicant is best suited. Then, after they have brought in their birth certificates and two character references , they are sent for medical and dental examinations and educational or aptitude tests. rtecruits for the Air Force and Fleet Air Arm are put through a stiffer medical examination, special attention being paid to eyes, ears, nose, and throat. Although the education and aptitude tests for the services are relatively simple, some applicants muff them badly. Many of the men aplying to join the services did not ,go beyond the final year in primary school. A few men who want to join the Army can\'t read or write, and are unable to fill out their application forms. i\'He tt.A.A.i1. aptitude test, given to all applicants, whether for air-crew or ground staf is the most thorough. It is specially designed to br-rpg out abilities required in different sections of the service. The test consists of five parts. The first is designed to establish whether a man can follow a simple direction and contains points similar to this: "Write down the last three letters o-f the alphabet, put a  round what you have written and underline the middle letter." The second part, concerned with elementary calculations, is designed to show the applicant\'s proficiency at simple addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division-essen – tial knowledge for men who are going to have technical training. One of the questions involves the division of a sum of money. Another might be along these lines: "If ten men in a room were joined by another five men and then three men went out, how many were left in the room?" The third section is a test of verbal ability and general knowledge. Because essay writing takes too much time, applicants are asked a series of short questions, such as: "What is a word similar in meaning to happy?" General knowledge may t>e t». { sted by a question such as: I .-5 "Is France in Asia, Europi V Africa, America, or The Indies! The fourth series of ques. tions is designed to test > man\'s proficiency at routint tasks. A problem in this section might be to assess the postage for a given weight of letters addressed to different cities. The last part of the test assesses mechanical and oractical aptitude and is mainly diagrammatic. One of the tasks may he to select from four shapes the two which form a perfect circle. Many of the men who sit for this aptitude test have forgotten all about their schoolwork and are inclined to get the .jitters over simple questions J well within their knowledge and when tney are in a state of normal composure. About half the men applyinj to join the Army have not coiik pleted their primary education and are sent for an elementary education test. First they are son* simple dictation. This is followed by a "reading cornprehen- < sion test" designed to show wh- / ether a man can read and whether he really understands the meaning of words. After they have enlisted,all Army recruits are given a psychology test designed to reveal special aptitudes. The test is similar to vacational guidance tests conducted by employment agencies and large firms of employers . When the recruits enter camp they are given an examination in primary standard English, arithmetic, geography, and history. Those who fail are sent to an Army school until they reach the required standard. Some may leave the school and start their basic training within a week, but others may have to stay at school for as long as two months. The two education tests for the Navy are harder than the Army\'s,but are still relaticely simple. Intended seamen, stokers, cooks, stewards, musicians,butchers, painters,blacksmiths,naval airmen, and skilled air mechanics are given the easier of the two tests. They are given such tasks as: "Subtract £534/15/9 from £1246/16/6" aor are asked:" How nuch greater is .75 of £3 than two-fifths of 553?" A slightly higher standard is demanded of intended communications branch men,sick-berth attendants, dental mechanics; shipwrights, joiners, plumbers, and others. Their test contains such questions as: "Find the change out of £10 after buying five yards cloth at\' 32/6 per yard, three yards of silk at 6/11 per yard, and 32 buttons at 5/6 per dozen." Or: "A triangular shaped lawn has a base of 18 feet and a perpendicular length of eight yards. Find the area in square yards." Representative_Docs: ['Baltic Freedom Day 1986 Baltic Freedom Day Resolution introduced In US House and Senate – Baltic community bēgtu» campaign to win full Congressional support. On February 7 Senator Don Rlegle (D-MI) introduced Senate Joint Resolution 271 (S.J. Res. 271). calling for the designation of June 14. 1986 as "Baltic Freedom Day". The resolution was co sponsored by 30 other U.S. Senators, including Illinois Senators Paul Simon and Alan Dixon. In his remarks to the Senate. Sen. Rlegle stated: " With this resolution, the Congress focuses the attention of the world on the plight of the Baltic people and demonstrates solidarity with them in their continuing struggle for freedom. This year. June 14 will mark the 46th year of Soviet subjugation of the Baltic States. Despite steady erosion of the national identities of the people of Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia over the past four and a half decades, their determination to regain their lost freedoms has not been weakened...The past 46 years have shown that all the military strength of the Soviet Union can never succeed in rrushing the spirit and the drive of the Baltic people." Sen. Rlegle concluded his statement by drawing a comparison to South Africa. "Just as the South African policy of apartheid, which denies basic rights to a majority of its citizens. Is unacceptable, so too Is the continuing Soviet occupation of Latvia. Lithuania and Eston 1a... Express ion s of support for the aspirations of tl« citizens of the captive nations from the free world give hope to those still fighting to secure their fundamental human rights. Prompt Senate approval of this resolution will demonstrate the solidarity of the American people in their continuing struggle for freedom." At the same time, Cong. William Carney (R-NY), along with Congs. Brian Donnelly (D-MA) and Don Ritter (R-PA), Introduced a companion bill In the House. House Joint Resolution 500 (H.J. Res. 500) states in part, " The purpose of this resolution is to set aside a day of rememberance for the continuing plight of the people of Estonia. Latvia and Lithuania, who have endured 46 years of foreign domination and suppression by the Soviet Union." The Baltic Freedom Day Resolution Is of key importance to Baits on both sides of the Iron Curtain. It sends a clear signal to Soviet Union and to the world that the United States government still supports Baltic aspirations for freedom by publicly reinforcing the longstanding U.S. non recogn it ion policy toward the illegal Soviet occupation of the Baltic States. Perhaps even tore important, the passage of this resolution will be broadcast to the people of occupied Estonia. Latvia and Lithuania via Voice of America and Radio Free Europe, letting them know that they have not been forgotten. Thus, it la critical that Baltic Americans do everything in their power to ensure full Congressional support for this bill. While Senate support is already strong, in order to pass both the House and Senate versions of this resolution, a majority of U.S. legislators must sign on to these bills. While organizations such as the American Latvian Association (ALA), the Joint Baltic American National Committee (JBANC) and the Baltic American Freedom League (BAFL) will be working in Washington to win broadbased support for these resolutions, it is critical that Baits and others write directly to their Congressmen, urging them to co-sponsor H.J. Res 500. WHAT YOU CAN DO Write to your Congressman or Congresswoman and ask them to co sponsor H.J. Res. 500. indicating that the passage of this bill is extremely important to you. Ask your family members, relatives, neighbors and colleagues to send letters to their representatives as well – the more letters each legislator receives, the better our chances of winning his or her support. WRITING TO THE CONGRESS: Address your letter to: The Hon. (congressman\'s/woman\'s name) United States House of Representatives Washington O.C. 20515 If you don\'t know who your U.S. Representative is, call AlglB Silas of the Joint Baltic American National Committee at (301) 340- 1954. ALSO... JBANC and ALA have placed requests with the White House asking for a special Presidential signing ceremony for the Baltic Freedom Day Resolution. If this is approved. President Reagan will meet with Baltic-American leaders In the White House. In your letters to your Congressmen and Congresswomen. (especially the Republicans!) ask them to personally write the President, urging such a public reception. After you\'ve written a letter to your Congressman, you can also write to President Reagan, asking that he hold such a Baltic Freedom Day reception in the White House. Soviet leader Gorbachev is coming to Washington D.C. this year, and a public Baltic Freedom Day Ceremony would send hla a clear signal that the ongoing illegal Soviet occupation of the Baltic States has not been forgotten.', 'Baltic Freedom Day, 1991 and 1992 BY THE PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED ST A TES OF AMERICA A Proclamation During the past year, the long struggle of the Baltic peoples to recover their freedom has been marked by both triumph and tragedy: triumph in their bold calls for liberty and independence; tragedy in the bloody events of January 1991. The United States and, indeed, all freedomloving nations have long denounced the infamous Molotov-Ribbentrop pact that led to the forcible incorporation of the independent Baltic States into the Soviet Union. The peoples of both the West and the Baltic States have continued to believe that the freedom of Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia must and can be restored. Recent events suggest that our hopes have not been misplaced. In peaceful, democratic referendums, the peoples of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania have asserted overwhelmingly their desire for freedom. Toward that aim, they have sought to enter into meaningful negotiations with Moscow about their status. Despite the tragic events of January 1991, which resulted in the deaths of at least 21 Lithuanians and Latvians and many more injured, a dialogue between the Baltic governments and the Soviet Union has begun. We hope that it will bear fruit, and we urge the Soviet Union to move forward with the talks. However, the United States remains deeply concerned over the continued application of intimidation and force by Soviet authorities. Such actions are incompatible with the process of peaceful change through fair and constructive negotiations. The United States has never and will never recognize the forcible annexation of the Baltic States by the Soviet Union. I reiterated this policy during my recent meeting with Lithuanian President Landsberģis, Estonian Prime Minister Savisaar, and Latvian Prime Minister Godmanis. This was my sixth meeting with the Baltic leadership during the past 12 months. The Administration will remain in close contact with the Baltic leadership in the months ahead. As we commemorate ~Baltie Freedom Day," we reaffirm our support for the right of the Baltic peoples to live in peace and freedom. The Congress, by Public Law 102-17, has designated June 14, 1991, and June 14, 1992, as ~Baltie Freedom Day" and has authorized and requested the President to issue a proclamation in observance of this event. NOW, THEREFORE, I, GEORGE BUSH, President of the United States of America, do hereby proclaim the days of June 14, 1991, and June 14, 1992, as Baltic Freedom Day. I call upon the people of the United States to observe these days with appropriate ceremonies and activities to reaffirm their commitment to human rights and to freedom and democracy for all oppressed peoples.', 'By the President of the United States of America A Proclamation During the past year, the long struggle of the Baltic peoples to recover their freedom has been marked by both triumph and tragedy: triumph in their bold calls for liberty and independence; tragedy in the bloody events of January 1991. The United States and. indeed, all freedom-loving nations have long denounced the infamous Molotov-Ribbentrop pact that led to the forcible incorporation of the independent Baltic States into the Soviet Union. The peoples of both the West and the Baltic States have continued to believe that the freedom of Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia must and can be restored. Recent events suggest that our hopes have not been misplaced. In peaceful, democratic referendums, the peoples of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania have asserted overwhelmingly their desire for freedom. Toward that aim, they have sought to enter into meaningful negotiations with Moscow about their status. Despite the tragic events of January 1991. which resulted in the deaths of at least 21 Lithuanians and Latvians and many more injured, a dialogue between the Baltic governments and the Soviet Union has begun. We hope that it will bear fruit, and we urge the Soviet Union to move forward with the talks. However, the United States remains deeply concerned over the continued application of intimidation and force by Soviet authorities. Such actions are incompatible with the process of peaceful change through fair and constructive negotiations. The United States has never and will never recognize the forcible annexation of the Baltic States by the Soviet Union. I reiterated this policy during my recent meeting with Lithuanian President Landsbergis, Estonian Prime Minister Savisaar, and Latvian Prime Minister Godmanis. This was my sixth meeting with the Baltic leadership during the past 12 months. The Administration will remain in close contact with the Baltic leadership in the months ahead. As we commemorate "Baltic Freedom Day," we reaffirm our support for the right of the Baltic peoples to live in peace and freedom. The Congress, by Public Law 102-17, has designated June 14, 1991, and June 14. 1992, as "Baltic Freedom Day" and has authorized and requested the President to issue a proclamation in observance of this event. NOW, THEREFORE, I, GEORGE BUSH, President of the United States of America, do hereby proclaim the days of June 14, 1991, and June 14, 1992, as Baltic Freedom Day. I call upon the people of the United States to observe these days with appropriate ceremonies and activities to reaffirm their commitment to human rights and to freedom and democracy for all oppressed peoples. IN WITNESS WHEREOF, I have hereunto set my hand this 13th day of June, in the year of our Lord nineteen hundred and ninety-one. and of the Independence of the United States of America the two hundred and fifteenth. Baltic Freedom Day, 1991 and 1992']

Representative_Docs: ['Asylum seekers in Latvia to get work permits The Ministry for Welfare plans to introduce changes to regulations concerning work permits for foreigners to enable the granting of Work permits to asylum seekers, pending a decision on their status as refugees or "alternative*. Previously work permits were only issued to refugees and other persons who have been accorded "alternative" status. The changes to the Regulations will bring them into line with standards contained in EU Council directive 2003/9/ EX, which prescribes minimum standards for reception of asylum seekers, and will avoid previous inconsistencies encountered in the application of the regulations. In future foreign persons who are students at accredited Latvian tertiary education institutions, or who are full-time students, may be granted work permits for situations when their Employment Contract does not exceed 20 hours work per week. It is planned to repeal the Regulation requiring EU Family Members to obtain work permits, since that requirement does not apply to citizens of the EU or the European Economic Zone (EEZ). Ministry officials remind Latvian citizens intending to work in EU or EEZ countries that while they do not require a work permit, they must nevertheless have signed a Work Agreement which confirms the deduction of Social Insurance Tax from their wages, for payment of income support and pensions in the future.', "Ministry of Foreign Affairs urges Latvian residents to register their stay in another country The Latvian Ministry of Foreign Affairs urges residents of Latvia to register in the Consular Register before leaving the country or while staying abroad. The Ministry's Consular Department has  a Consular Register with an aim to compile information about residents of Latvia who are staying abroad. The register will be kept and maintained in Latvian embassies, consulates and the Ministry's Consular Department The recent events in the tsunarpi-stricken Southeast Asia have proven the necessity to gather data about people and their location in a particular region of the world to be able to provide timely consular assistance to people who have suffered in an emergency. The Ministry of Foreign Affairs would like to repeatedly invite Latvian residents to register in the respective Latvian embassy or consulate while staying abroad. This procedure would ensure that the registered persons receive adequate protection of their rights and interests, as well as assistance in emergency situations. Latvian embassies and consulates register Latvian residents who are staying in the respective country. If Latvian residents are leaving for countries where no Latvian mission has been established, these persons are registered in the Consular Department of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. In order to register, travellers should provide the Latvian embassy, consulate or the Ministry's Consular Department with a completed questionnaire containing basic information about the traveller. This can be done in person, via e-mail, regular mail or otherwise. Data of personal character can be furnished at will. Information on a person's stay in a foreign country is kept as long as the person stays there.", "LATVIA'S EMBASSIES REPORT 70,000 VISAS ISSUED IN FIRST HALF OF 1996 At a press conference on 19 August, Minister of Foreign Affairs Valdis Birkavs spoke about Latvia's foreign policy activities in the first half of 1996 including consular work in Republic of Latvia embassies abroad. During this time period, the embassies have issued approx. 70,000 entrance visas, more than 21,G0G of which have been issued in Moscow, more than 8,000 in St. Petersburg, around 6,000 in Helsinki, and more than 5,000 in Minsk and Vitebsk. Mr. Birkavs told reporters that the introduction of a visa-free regime in co-operation with Nordic countries is one of Latvia's priorities. In this connection, Norway is ready to send its experts to assess the situation in Latvia. As of 1 May, a visa-free regime between Latvia and Ireland has come into force, and the Minister of Foreign Affairs foresaw that a visa-free regime with Bulgaria could be introduced by the spring of 1997, and noted that a draft agreement on visa-free travelling has also been submitted to Slovenia. The minister said that the Baltic states have jointly proposed to Luxembourg, as the presiding state of the Schengen agreement countries, to begin talks on visa-free travelling."]

Name: 83_credit rating_standard poor_rating agency_long term
