Representative_Docs: ["STANDARD & POOR'S UPGRADES LATVIA'S RATING The international credit rating agency Standard & Poor's upgraded the long-term foreign currency issuer credit rating of the Republic of Latvia lending from 888 to BBB+, according to the Latvian Finance Ministry. The short-term foreign currency issuer rating was raised from A-3 to A-2. Latvia was also given the long-term local currency issuer rating of A- and the shortterm local currency issuer rating of A-2, and the outlook was evaluated as Stable. Standard & Poor's said Latvia's ratings were upgraded in view of its economic development and resilience during the recent slowdown of world economic growth. The rating agency also noted that Latvia is a potential member of the European Monetary Union (EMU) and that after accession to this union the foreign and local currency ratings would no longer be different. Latvia's ratings were also upgraded owing to its good macroeccnomic indicators and the steps taken by the government to strengthen the market economy. Investment growth in Latvia is stable, structural reforms continue and the medium-term GDP growth is expected at 6%. The national debt is likely to be low at 17% of GDP in 2002. In addition, Latvia will probably get full EU membership in 2005 at the latest. According to Standard & Poor's, Latvia's economy has become increasingly flexible and competitive in recent years, and the EU preaccession process has contributed to the growth. Some negative aspects were pointed out as weii, such as the high current account deficit, although it is largely covered by foreign direct investments. Standard & Poor's expect Latvia's current account deficit at 9.3% of GDP in 2002. The agency said it expected prudent macroeconomic policy from the Latvian government, which, combined with EU membership and accession to EMU, would contribute to the development of a sustainable economy and further growth of ratings.", "STANDARD & POOR'S UPGRADES LATVIA'S CREDIT RATING FORECAST TO POSITIVE The international credit rating agency Standard & Poor's has upgraded Latvia's credit rating forecast from stable to positive. Reasons for this include the country's progress towards EU membership, stable economic growth, the continuing inflow of foreign investments and structural reforms. Standard & Poor's has approved a credit rating for long-term foreign currency loans of BBB+, for long-term local currency loans of A, and for short-term foreig/i and local currency loans of A-2. The forecast for loans in foreign currencies is positive. The agency noted Latvia's high current account deficit as the major drawback, expected at 8.5% of GDP this year – although this will mostly be covered by foreign investments.", 'FITCH RATINGS UPGRADES LATVIA\'S CREDIT RATING The international credit rating agency Fitch Ratings has upgraded Latvia\'s rating for longterm loans in foreign currency from 888 to BBB+. For long-term loans in local currency, the rating has been raised to A, while for short-term loans in foreign currency the rating is now F3. The ratings agency also evaluates Latvia\'s economy as having "a stable outlook". In its report, Fitch Ratings points out that Latvia\'s rating has been increased in response to the country\'s stable economic development, structural reforms, strengthening of the banking sector and stable foreign investment. It says Latvia has one of the highest foreign investment ratios of European Union applicant countries. The international rating agency Standard & Poor\'s has already affirmed the Latvia\'s longterm foreign currency credit rating at BBB+, while Moody\'s has assigned a rating of A 2.']

Name: 84_life desires_oral history_man desires_looked upon
Representation: life desires_oral history_man desires_looked upon_pan movements_one hand_pristine state_marvin harris_human beings_effective life
Representative_Docs: ['svešvalodu nodaļa/other languages section INDIVIDUAL AND SOCIETY: A SHIFTING SOCIETAL PARADIGM Second part (See first part in MLB Ziņotājs Nr. 2.(611.), February, 2000, page 9-11) Marvin Harris, Cannibals, and Kings (Vintage Books, 1991) Marvin Harris (1927 ) has taught anthropology at Columbia University, and lectured by invitation at most of the major universities in the United States. His other Imants Steprāns books include: Rise of Anthropological Tlieory; Cows, Figs, Wars and Witches; and Cultural Materialism. In the introduction Harris says that his, ... purpose in this book is to replace the old onwards-andupwards Victorian view of progress with a more realistic account of cultural evolution... [becausej... (sltone age populations lived healthier lives than did most of the people who came immediately after then, (p.x) Harris wanders, moreover, about the permanence of the unquestionable technical gains that our civilisations have been striving to enjoy for the last 150 years, and if they are not just a "...bubble-like protuberance on a curve that slopes down as often as it slopes up: (p.xii). The contrast between our food-gathering forebears and us – apart from healthy living and ecological awareness – can be seen in terms of polarity on a continuum of societal dominance. Towards one pole this dominance delines, since people with open spaces around them cannot be easily intimidated. Even if the ancient food gatherers too had some internal family hierarchy and its corresponding domination, it was something different from following commands from above demanding servile toil and drudgery (21). Towards the opposite pole we see the steady growth of domination based on "...a capacity to organise labour on behalf of communal enterprises" (p. 112). Harris sees this continuum as "...the decisive difference between pre- and post-date political systems"(p. 155), and explains it as a development of the pristine state from which the political state from which the political state would later asise. The pristine state itself is a political organisation already a notch above the kinship groups of the ancient food gatherers, but still very much grounded in local tradition and a concept of justice, rather than a hierarchy of dominance based on violance and threat. In ntost band and village societies before the evolution of the state, the average human being enjoyed economic and political freedoms which only a privileged minority enjoy today... (butj... with the rise of the state all this was swept away., as ordinary men seeking to use nature\'s bounty had to get someone else\'s permission and had to pay for it with taxes, or extra labour (pp. 101-2). Harris explains the rise of the state in terms of a need for increased food production and illustrates this with anthropological data gained from the study of the big man, the big provider or mumi, and his role as organiser of the muminal feast. Such a mumi – in this case in the Trobriand Islands – is a hard working man who, by setting up a bigger and better traditional feast, gradually establishes for himself and his helpers a priviledged status. This status further enables the mumi to edge towards the status of war chief and judge; thus the mumi were "... changing little by little into permanent rulerls) with coercive control over production and consumption.."(p. 108). The status of a mumi provides for the feast, but is not indispensable for the daily food production, yet. The next level of political organisation is illustrated by the role of the mukama, who rules over a large population – the Bunyoro people in this case – as a king. The mukama alone could grant or withhold permission for blood vengeance, and failure to contribute to the mukarna\'s income could result ir the loss of one\'s lands, banishment, or corporal punishment... The mukama also spent a considerable portion of the state treasury on what we would today call \'image-building\' and public relations (p. 114). Thus the development of the pristine state brings with it fundamental chaznge in the relationship between the individual and society within a political framework. While this development later reaches a level of despotism, at this early stage Harris demonstrates how the slowly incrementing erosion of kinship traditions and values begins to deprive individuals of their political independence. At this stage the concept of justice based on a communal belief system in the hands of the stale also becomes an instrument of coercion. As this ir Harris\' key concept, I will quote him more fully, What I find most remarkable about this evolution of the pristine state is that it occurs as the result of an unconscious process: The participants in this enourmous transformation seem not to have known what they were creating. By imperceptible shifts in the redistributive balance from one generation to the next, the human species bound itself over into a form of social life in which the many debased themselves on behalf of the exaltation of the few. To paraphase Malcom Webb, at the beginning of the lengthy process no one could foresee the end result. Tribal equalitarianism/egalitarianism should gradually vanish even as it was being appended, without awarenss of the nature of the change, and its final achievement of absolute control would at that point seem merely a minor alteration of established custom. The consolidation of governmental power would have taken place as a series of natural, beneficial, and only slightly (if at all) extra-legal responses to current conditions, with each new aquisition of the state-power representing only a small departure from contemporay practice.\'(p. 122) At the next stage we see the seeds of the pristine state flowering into the extraordinary bloom of the mighty empires, about which historians rather than anthropologists speak. Such empires – like China, Egypt, and Mesopotamia – were governed by a highJy centralised system of overlords, who claimed a heavenly mandate for their priviledges. Here the role of the great provider of the pristine state, who used their role to organise communal work projects, was perfected in the role of despot, where the subjects of the empire were only, ... little better off than their oxen and were no less subject to the commands of superior beings who knew how to keep records and who alone had the right to manufacture and use weapons of war and coercion (p. 235). Harris, following Karl Wittiogel, shows that these ancient empires which began as so-called hydraulic societies (referring to the irrigation projects first used by the kings to provide food for increasing populations), turned, over time, from using tools to organisation to weapons of coercion. As population levels increased to a point where die food resources could provide only bare subsistence, the population became completely dependent on the food stored by the emperors, who, thus became great providers on the level of gods. Once this system of coercion had been established and the concept of the heavenly mandate created, it was ready to begin a life of its own. An aura of higher wisdom and secrecy became part of the techniques of coercion, which had to be taken for granted. In this regard I again quote Harris more fully, According to Wittfogel, the hydraulic theory has omnious implications for our own times While he traces the origin of the agro-managerial form of despotism to specific ecological conditions, he emphasizes that once in existence it was spread by conquest far beyond its semiarid riverine homelands. He insists, for example, that the Mongols transplanted the agromanagerial form of despotism from China to Russia and, in the aftermath of the Mongol conquest, to Central Asia and the eastern part of Europe. In czarist Russia the same system of \'Oriental Despotism\' lingered on into the twentieth century, file Bolshevik Revolution and Lenin\'s dictatorship of proletariat were not, in Wittfogel\'s view, \'transient steps along the way toward restoration of liberties that human beings enjoyed before the evolution of the state\' rather they led to the restoration of centralizing powers of government and an increase in czarist tyanny through the development of industrial means of exploitation and control (pp. 245-6). This analysis should lead us to search for shadows of agro-managerial despotism not only in the Chinese/Mongolian empire and the Russian state, but also in the Mesopotamian and Egytian imperial practices that have most likely been incorporated in western European – and, by extension, Atlantic-civilisation. In all of these cases, \'military necessity\' and imperial prerequisites for domination have substituted the former agro-managerial principle. Indeed, it was in our own times and in the very heart of world power, America, that the postulate of the militaryindustrial complex was proclaimed-first, as a threat to be avoided, but used as a powerful international tool more and more now, apparently. In our times the ecology, the riches of the nature (mis)-spent and industrial techniques (especially biological technology) are presenting new needs for adjusting traditional values and setting up new communal and societal organizations. But how can we be sure that these new needs won\'t breed new powers and priviledges of coercion? In this respect we are in the same situation as those who laboured under the managers of the irrigation projects of old, those from whom greater intensification of production was required for reasons that were seemingly mysterious and hard to understand. As Harris points out, ... Wittfogel\'s theory suggets... that when certain kinds of state-level systems of production undergo intensification, despotic forms of government may arise which can neutralize human will and intelligence for thousands of years. This implies further that the effective moment for conscious choice may exist only during the transition from one mode uf production to another. After-the society has made its commitment to a particular technological and ecological strategy for solving the problem of declining efficiency, it may not be possible to do anything about the consequences of an unintelligent choice for a long time to come (p. 246). In a footnote Harris writes about the "... slightly (if at all) extra-legal responses..." that augur this process (p. 122). He points out that the capacity for electronic tracking of individual behaviour by centralised networks of surveillance and record-keeping computers already exists (p. 288). As in the name of political necessity our legal principles are adjusted to international understandings and deals, the extra-legal responses are slowly permeating our traditional approach to justice. This sort of thing, for example, transpired when American Legal Realism began to question the value of the rule of law, saying that in reality the judge is acting on behalf of the political power and not the books of law (that for the i>egal Realism were sheer pretences anyway). In this respect I quote the legal scholar William W. Fisher 111, During the period between the two world wars, Legal Realism enjoyed a substatial following in American law schools... (but] ...the Realists\' thoroughgoing positivism and flirtation with ethical relativism were helping to erode Americans\' faith that the law could and should be measured against any rational moral standard, thereby weakening the nation\'s intellectual defences against the fascism overrunning Europe. And some of the response derived from domestic politics: the support most Realists had lent the New Deal – in particular; their denunciation of the decison of the Supreme court invalidating early New Deal statutes – aroused wrath of those who regarded FDR\'s policies as prototalitarian. In the face of thes attacks many Realists... publicly disavowed position they had taken... in the early 19305... and embracing a revived natural law theory (22). Here legal proposals were being restrained overtly as injunctions against the totalitarianism of the enemy but covertly against the totalitarian policies of the FDR himself. Both approaches in themselves were grounded not so much in the principles of the law as in political power strategies to which – logically – the Realists themresponses that now were dressed up by the Realists in the finery of natural law; and in the shade of which the very enemy, against which international American politics appearently were designed to fight, found shelter. Once a society – in this case the whole Atlantic civilisation – had reached a stage where political power became not only the ultimate tool but also a basic principle, the slide towards coercive domination was inevitable. If we think that these matters are merely historical accidents we have to heed Harris\' admonition all the more, I urge those who feel that my explanation of the evolution of culture is too deterministic and too mechanical to consider the possibility that at this very moment we are again passing by slow degrees through a series of natural, beneficial, and only slightly... extralegal\' changes which will transform social life in ways that few alive today would consciously wish to inflict upon future generations. Clearly, the remedy for that situation cannot lie in the denial of deterministic component in social processes; rather, it must lie in bringing that component into the arena of popular comprehension (p. 122). While dealing with such subjects as the determinilic components of social processes could be seen as matters of interest only to anthropologists, bringing these matters into the arena of popular comprehension concerns us all, espacially on the occasion of this symposium, because of our endavour to develop further our conception of humanness. Contusion Introducing the concept of a shift of the social paradigm from societal towards individual values, I pointed out not only the abstract qualities of the concepts with which we are dealing, but also the historic developments that led to the deterioration of our heritage accepted mainly as Western Christendom. The Enlightenment had torn apart the traditional garment that had been corrupted by superstition and degraded by tyranny. The individual now was free, but also nearly naked, as in the state of nature, adorned only in the old soiled underwear of political power. As a way out I pointed to individuation, the becoming aware of the individual and social forces; as a Self, being an organic part of the community, which exists in the spectrum of organisations from subjective certitude to societal comprehension to totalitarianist coercion, as elucidated by Michael Polanyi and Marvin Harris. John Stuart Mill had already recognised a century ago that ... in the present age – which has been described as \'destitute of faith, but terrified at scepticism\' – in which people feel sure, not so much that their opinions are true, as that they should know what to do without them – the claims of an opinion to be protected from public attack are rested not so much on its truth, as on its importance to society (23). Sociologists have recognised that we are worse off today as, ... with enormous nation-states, international diasporas, wide realms of personal choice, unstable and heterogeneous networks of social relations, mass media for the proliferation of cultural transmissions and the sheer multiplicity of discourses attempting to name or constitute persons, the social basis for recognition has come under particular challenge (24). The old shell of culture is gone, the new is not yet ready, and individuals are left as solitary and vulnarable as they ever have been, as ptiilosophers would tell us – they are in the state of nature. Without any morallygrounded authority, justice and education, the law and uprightness are fading fast. If we understand humanness as both the foundation of the Self and the normative guide for our society, it should be our most important concern. £NI) NOTES 21. Conclusion INTRODUCTION The objects of sociological study do not present themselves in nature any more than farmland presents itself. Farmers may look at plains that have been tilled... and see rich fields... Just as the farmers must win the fields from nature, so the sociologists must win the objects of their research. (1) In this paper I am going to search for clues of the dominace of social norms being shifted toward individual values. In the introduction I will review the developments of sociology from the middle of the 19th century onwards. The main body of this paper will examine the work of two authors that illustrates this shift, approaching the objects of sociological study from new perspectives. I hope this also will shed some light on the theme of this seminar – of what does humanness consist. THE OBJECT OF SOCIOLOGICAL STUDY The commentary of Aristotle that "people live in groups" conceals the problem of the very object of sociological studies – the group. Is it stastical concept, a SVEŠVALODU NODAĻA/ OTHER LANGUAGES SECTION INDIVIDUAL AND SOCIETY: A SHIFTING SOCIETAL PARADIGM Imants Steprāns moral domain, a concept created by tradition, not so much a thing out there as something in the eye of the beholder, or a combination of all of the above? Before our sociological concepts became more firmly established in the 19th century, the enlightenment mood showed society as having been, ... slowly formed by the natural process of civilisation... then seized and corrupted by superstition, and degraded by tyranny and the minds of men deadened under the burden of misery and fear,... yet little by little day breaks again and eyes long condemned to darkness catch a glimpse of light. (2) This glimpse of light turned ot to be the principles of rationalism and the methodology or natural sciences. August Comte (1798-1857) construed this as scientific positivism that bestowed on sociology (his term) a certain universality, at the same time developing the scope of empirical observations of the particulars. Emile Durkheim (1858-1917) extended this to the assertion that sociology is a distinctive science with a distinctive subject-matter dealing with social facts, and stated that "... a social fact is every way of acting, fixed or not, capable of expressing on the individual an external constraint. "(3) Tlirough his methology he more clearly conceptualised the importance of society, opposing the practice of simply studying the psychology of individuals in social settings. Max Weber (1864-1920) elucidated the scientific nature of the ideal type; at the same time, however, he also pointed out its limitations since the observers themselves are/were individuals grounded in society. All these theoretical efforts and the richness of the empirical material have been epitomised in the general theory of society, which dominated sociology till the middle of our century. This general theory, though, had been too deeply immersed in the tradition of the Enlightenment to keep up with the devastating political and cultural shanges that came after WWII. As Jurgen Habermas said, The dialectic of enlightenment, once it had attained its goal, had exausted the impulse toward a critique of the age that originally set it in motion. (4) This had been already stressed by Nietzche\'s assertion that our modernity with its strivings for rationality had been nothing but a pretentious rhetoric of power under which the individual had to suffer. These buds of Nietzchean nihilism blossomed later on in the post-modern criticism of scientific rationalism, "claiming that knowledge is made valid not by its relation to its objects, but by its relation to our pragmatic interests, our communal perspectives, our needs, our rhetoric..." (5) (Therefore! for positivism, which represented the court of judgemets of enlightened reason, to digress into intelligible worlds is no longer merely forbidden, but meaningless prattle,... (and in this sort of societyl... [tjhe individual is reduced to the nodal point of the conventional responses and modes of operation expected of him. (6) This critique was further accentuated by the Marxists decreeing the significance of praxis that now became presented in terms of the prominece of revolutionary organisation. (7) All this resulted in the critical theory that, ... exists largely to facilitate a constructive engagement with the social world that starts from presumption that existing arrangements – including currently affirmed identities and differences -do not exhaust the range of possibilities. (8) The critical theory was very much the child of the 50\'s that thrived on the passion for crisis in order to change these existing arrangements the more. After a while, when this enchantment had faded (9) social scientists began to realise that, ... a good critical social theory must be able to tell the difference between crisis ideologies and the valid experience of real crisis. But how. [they wandered 1, can such validity be confirmed, given the indeterminacy of action, norms, and interpretations? (10) The followers of Habermas now avowed that, ... the effects of the Enlightement I Because J... the totalized critique of reason undercuts the capacity of reason to be critical. (11) The followers of Heideger, however, approached societal problems by mediating on the Being – whom they saw expressed, one way or another, in language (12) – replacing sociology with methaphysics. They couched this turn in concepts of hermeneoutics, which had also been shifting towards the individual as the evaluator of social existence. This splintering was further intensified by the students of societal difference (i.e. gender), asserting on the one hand that notions of social being begin from differences, but on the other hand that, ... feminist theories focusing exclusively on gender oversimplify their situation, since gender is just one of other fundamental facets of identity and difference, such as poverty, class, ethnicity, race, sexual identity, and age. (13) All these perceptions shifted the equation even more from the group towards the people asking if there is anytliing at all in the makeup of people as individuals that make "people live in groups". This led Claud LevkStrauss to focus on the human mind instead of organisations and groups of any particular society or class of society. This approach became known as structuralism, asserting that it is the human mind that constructs community by using symbols, which are much more given than developed. This idea of deeply rooted – almost biologically rooted – Self did not escape the notice of anthropology, literary studies, linguistics, and religion. When, for example, students of religion come to the conclusion that, ... Dionysus dances not in heaven but in our heads... (they follow it up by a statement that]... it would be premature at best to hold that biology is irrelevant. Kven if symbolic-cultural systems do arise in virtue of human social interaction, that clearly cannot be a sufficient condition for creation and use of symbols. (14) And when cosmologist Carl Sagan wonders whether "... we are capable of constructing a cosmology that is not some mathematical encrypting of our personal origins?" (15) Then these personal origins, too, direct our attention away from society towards the deeply – even genetically – rooted core of the individual.. At this point the mood from which critical theory sprung shifted towards the conviction that society was to important to be left to the sociologists, and allowed the term sociology to slide in favour of social sciences. The individual, the Self, is now much freer than during the Enlightement, simply because society has lost its cultural moorings – it is very much freedom by default. Moreover, this trend, "became inevitable in the wake of late medieval thought after the traditional source of meaning dried up land! the self had no choice but to function as a \'subject\'. (16) Therefore, we must search for a new qualifier, besides the general and the critical social theories. I propose to use the concept of individuation as a way of searcliing for a renewed role of the individual within society. Carl Jung best elucidates this conepL Individuation means becoming an individual, and in so far as individuality embraces our innermost, last and incomparable uniqueness, it also implies becoming one\'s own self. We would therefore translate individuation as coming to selfhood of selfrealisation. (17) To develop this theme further I am going to analyse two authors in more detail. First, Michael Polanyi, who sees selfhood as being rooted in deeply felt, personal convictions that may be repressed, however, by societal dominace, be it religious dogma or scientific rationalism. And second, Marvin Harris who sees the growing role of the state, and not society in general, as delimiting the individual. And that this coercive expansion of the state can be counteracted by our civic consciousness. MICHAEL POLANYI, PERSONAL KNOWLEDGE (CHICAGO, 1974) To quote from the book-cover, Michael Polanyi (1891- 1976) was a Fellow of the Royal Society of England, professor of physical chemistry and of social studies at the University of Manchester and a Fellow or Merton College, Oxford. He was the author of many books; including Science, Faith and Society; Meaning; and Knowing and Being, all published by the University of Chicago Press. It should be mentioned that Polanyi refers to what is generally called modernity as the critical movement, which is the subtitle of this book. Knowing this makes it easy to appreciate his notion that the, ... critical movement, which seems to be nearing the end of its course today, was perhaps the most fruitful effort ever sustained by the human mind. The past four or five centuries, which have gradually destroyed or overshadowed the whole medieval cosmos, have enriched us mentally and morally to an extent unrivalled by any period of similar duration (p. 265). This appreciation, however, is restrained by the awareness that exactly these most fruitful efforts – this modernity – are now being superseded by post-modernity, which has come with aporias of its own. These aporias originate from the basic values and convictions we hold not only as members of society but also as individual beings; though, ... if we believe that we hold these convictions merely because we were taught them, they may appear to be external to us; while to the extent to which we acknowledge that we have actively decided to accept them, they will tend to appear arbitrary, [and)... these unsettling reflections will now challenge also the framework of society (p. 203), He proposes to solve this dilemma by "... fusion of the personal and the objective as Personal Knowledge..." (p. viii). If this sounds somewhat simplistic, Polanyi is the first one to point out that he is dealing not with formulas and dictums but the growth of the self. (18). It is a process within which each of us has to develop this fusion individually, personally; as he says in the preface, "... ultimately, it is my own allegiance that upholds these convictions...11 and this is his only dictum. The reader has to study this book as a whole to absorb it, in a way it is as much art as science. To underline this I quote the introduction to his final chapter in full, I have arrived at the opening of this chapter without having suggested any definite theory concerning the nature of things; and I shall finish this chapter without having presented any such theory. This book tries to serve a different and in a sense perhaps more ambitious purpose. Its aim is to re-equip men with the faculties which centuries of critical thought have taught them to distrust. The reader has been invited to use these faculties and contemplate thus a picture of things restored to their fairly obvious nature. This is all the book was meant to do For once men have been made to realise the crippling mutilations imposed by an übjectivist framework – once the veil of ambiguities covering up these mutilations has been definitely dissolved – many fresh minds will turn to the task of reinterpreting the world as it is, and as it once more will be seen to be. 0 Polanyi sees the need for this reinterpretation as evolving out of an everdevoloping and changing universe. Thus this universe is seen as incomplete. Our mind demands that the incomplete be interpreted in terms of completeness – this is the principle of Gestalt Psychology. (19) The drive to completion can be seem as a drive for intellectual control, far down the scale of life as the worms and even perhaps amoeba, we meet a general alertness of animal, not directed towards any specific satisfaction, but merely exploring what it is there; an urge to achieve intellectual control over the situations confronting it (p. 132). Tlus universal urge itself, however, is seen by Polanyi forking in two directions – 1) the affirmation of our intellectual freedom, (so basic to individuation and development of personal knowledge), but also, 2) the societal drive to politacally harness this urge by pretending to complete the open form for and on behalf of individuals authoritatively, dogmatically, and, finally, totalitarienary. Intellectual freedom may be understood to function the same way as the notions of tolerance in technology and mechanics: if the moving parts of a machine are built without a certain leeway, that is, too rigidly, the machine may not function. Polanyi finds this leeway within our psychological makeup and shows that, besides the strict framework of logic, it also contains belief, tacit approval, and a heuristic methology. In his opinion tacit approval, a heuristic approach, belief, and even faith provide for this leeway and all are on tlie same continuum of indeterminacy and change "... for truth is but the external pole of belief (see p. 133). This can be illustrated by the indeterminacy in mathematics where, ... spectacular gains, achieved by the speculative use of mathematical notations for purposes not originally entertained, remind us that the major fruitfulness of formalism may be revealed in its uncovenanted functions, precisely at points where the peril seems greatest of its drifting into absurdity... we must commit ourselves to the risk of talking nonsense, if we are to say anything at a 11... (p. 94). This tacit risk-taking is a basic element of language. Communication assumes not only a certain intellectual rift between partners that has to be bridged, but our thought processes also have to find room for new concepts as "... languages are the product of man\'s groping for words in the process of making new conceptual decisions, to be conveyed by words" (p. 112). While the heuristic approach is nothing but rational extension of this tacit risk-taking function in a scientific methology, faith is "... the power for the deliberate holding of unproven beliefs" (p. 268). In Polanyi\'s opinion, the price we pay for rationality\'s thorough discrediting of belief is the burn-out that our civilisation is experiencing, so that, ... modern man lost his capacity to accept any explicit statement as his own belief. All belief was reduced to the status of subjectivity; to that of an imperfection by which knowledge fell short of universality (p. 266). This statement is softened by the acknowledgement that, ... we should be grateful for the prolonged attacks made by rationalists on religion for forcing us to renew the grounds of the Christian faith, but this does not remotely justify the acknowledgement of doubt as the universal solvent of error which will leave truth untouched behind. For truth is but the external pole of bebef, and to destroy all belief would be to deny the truth (p. 286). Polanyi follows this statement by showing that faith has been discredited by the inversion of truly held belief, thus thwarting its growth as, ... the extensive dogmatic framework of Christianity arose from ingenious efforts, sustained through many centuries, to axiomatize the faith already practiced by Christianity. In view of the high imaginative end emotional powers by which Christian beliefs control the whole person and relate him to the universe, the specification of these beliefs is much more colourful than are the axioms of arithmetic or the premises of natural science. This wish to axiomatize for Polanyi is the antithesis of personal knowledge. Polanyi knows that most people would agree that this is the state of affairs within Church. He therefore devotes much energy to shed a similar light on the same affairs in science, which he calls false objectivity because "... modern scientism fetters thought as cruelly as ever the churches had done" (p. 265). He starts by taking the scientific approach to the solar system to task, which puts the sun, instead of the earth, in the centre of this system. He warns, ... no one – scientists included – looks at the universe this way, whatever lip-service is given to \'objectivity\'... [for]... as human beings, we must inevitably see the universe from the center lying within ourselves and speak about in terms of human language shaped by the exigencies of human intercourse... Copernicus gave preference to man\'s delight in abstract theory, at the price of rejecting the evidence of our senses... In a literal sense, therefore, the new Copernican system was as anthropocentric as the Ptolemaic view, the difference being merely that it preferred to satisfy a different human affection (pp. 3-4). However, while the urge itself was still anthropocentric the result was not. It was an opening of vistas, commanding respect by their very rationality, but superseding the witness of our senses. We understood it as leaving our subjectivity behind and arriving at an objectivity that could be much better communicated. (ThisJ... objectivity does not demand that we estimate man\'s significance in the universe by the minute size of his body, by the brevity of his past history or his probable future career. It does not require that we see ourselves as a mere grain of sand in a million Saharas. It inspires us, on the contrary, with the hope of overcoming the appalling disabilities of our bodily existence, even to the point of conceiving a rational idea of the universe... This is not, however, what we are taught today. To say that this discovery of objective truth in science consists in the apprehension of a rationality which commands our respect and arouses our contemplative admiration... would be generally shrugged aside as out-dated Platonism: a piece of mystery-mongering unworthy of an enlightened age... (pp. 5-6). For Polanyi, a scientist himself, the growth of the individual (individuation) is endangered not by society but by science, albeit a science out of joint. He fully appreciates the role of society. For him, ... articulate systems which foster and satisfy an intellectual passion can survive only with the support of a society which respects the values affirmed by these passions (p. 203). This society, however, has been colonised (20) by science whose rationalism has robbed the individual of his centre, and denied him "seeing the sun rising in East". This belief in rationalism that once had helped the individual to throw off the chains of dogmatism is now turning itself into chains. To throw of these chains, we should again see the sun, the moon, and the stars as part of a universe in whose centre we have been allowed to stay for a while – this is Polanyi\'s Personal Knowledge. /To be continued in the next issue of MLB Ziņotājs / END NOTES 1) Craig Calhoun, Critical Social Theory (Blackwell, 1996), p. 65. 2) Marquis de Condorcet, Sketch far an Historical Picture of the progress of Human Mind, quoted from L Cahone, ed.. Modernism and Postmodernism (Blackwell, 19%), p. 71. 3) Emile Durkheim, The Rule of Sociological Method (Chicago, transl/repr. 1962), p. 37. 4) Jurge Habermas, The Philosophical Discourse of Modernity (Cambridge, Mass., 1991), p. 37. 5) Lawrence Calhoon, ed., From Modernism to Postmodernism, p. 17. 6) Max Hornheimer and Theodor Adorno, Dialectic of Enlightemenl (quoted: From Modernism to Postmodernism), p. 245 & 247. 7) Louis Dupre, Passage to Modernity (New Haven, Ct: Yale University Press, 1993), p. 250. The recent self-destruction of the Marxist social systems that dominated half of Europe can be seen as the latest explosion of a revolt against the modernist dogma, from which Marxism drew the most extreem conclusions. 8) Craig, p. xviii. 9) Bernard-fiend Levy, Barbarism With a Human Face (Harper & Row, 1980). 10) James Bohman, New Philosophy of Social Science (Cambridge, Mass.. 1991), p. 227. 11) Thomas McCarthy in introduction to Hamabermas\', The Philosophical discourse, p. xvii. 12) Gianni Vattimo, The End of Modernity (Baltimore, 1991), "... Heidegger is generally considered to have provided the basis of hermeneutic ontology insofar as he asserts a connection-almost, one might say; an indentification-between Being and language", p. 115. 13) Margaret R. Somers and Gloria D. Gibson, Reclaiming the Epistemological Other" quoted from C. Calhoun, ed., Social Theory and the Politics oflndentity (Blackwell, 1995), p. 55. 14) E. Thomas Lawson and Robert N. McCauley, Retiiinking Religion Cambridge University Press. 1993), p. 184. 15) Carl Sagan, liroca s Brain (New York, 1979), p. 366. 16) Louis Dupre, ibid., p. 114. Dupre sees this growing from "the interludes of nominalist theology, intensified by the exposure to non-Christian cultures of classical antiquity." 17) Carl Jung, Two Essays on Analytical Psychology (Princeton, NJ: Princeton University IVess, 1966), p. 173. 18) Though he follows Jung very indirectly – his is much closer to the Gestalt Psychology. 19.) Same as we instinctively wish to complete unfinished forms, like a partially finished 0 form, even finishing the circularity of an U – though Polanyi has some reservations about the theories of Gestalt psychology too. 20) In terms of Habermas. * This paper was delivered at Humanness 1999 seminar, held at St. John Estonian Lutheran Church, May 28 – May 30, 1999. The text was edited by Reverend Norman Henning. The seminar convener was Rev. Heino I^aneots. It also demonstrates the in-depth studies some of our Seniors are doing in order to widen their horizons in knowledge. Our intent is to publish several of these in order to show the diversity of our members of the community. Anyone interested should contact the editor.']

Representative_Docs: ['ENGLISH LESSONS ARE FREE Free lessons in English are available to all people who have come to live in Australia and cannot speak the language. If you have relatives or friends who need help to learn English, you can assist them by telling them that they can have free lessons. Classes Classes are held in any place where there are six or more people to form a class. There are two classes at night, lasting for two hours each. A free textbook is supplied. Attendance at classes is the quickest way to learn the language. Correspondence Lessons If you live in a place where no class is held, and where there are not enough people to form one, you can have lessons sent to you by post. These are called correspondence lessons. The pupil sends his work to a teacher who corrects it and returns it to the pupil. This is not as quick a way of learning English as going to a class, and you should not ask for correspondence lessons unless you cannot attend a class. Radio Lessons Lessons on the radio are broadcast each Saturday and Sunday over the Australian Broadcasting Commission stations all over Australia. you to learn to speak English. Booklets telling all about the lessons are sent out each month, and you may have them sent to you even if you are also learning English at a class or by correspondence. ['Xii 11 ii r a „Thinking through film" has been the motto for the Institute of Documentary Film's (IDF) Festival and East Silver Industry Market for a decade now. This thinking, and feeling, through the medium of film has turned a small local Czech event into an international annual checking of the pulse of Eastern Europe, showing the world what topics and issues are current in society from such far flung places as Estonia or Montenegro, from Armenia to Slovenia. The festival is helping the rest of the world gain insight into the "thinking" that goes on in documentary filmmaking from this distinctive and vibrant region, uncovering moving stories about such diverse topics as young men exploited in the Romanian gay porno industry (All Boys), or a small Slovakian town where the mayor tragicomically battles a diminishing population (Matchmaking Mayor), or human and intimate portraits of Russian border guards in the snow covered no man's land of the Arctic, hundreds of miles from the nearest settlement (At the Edge of Russia). From October 25 -31, the oldest silver mining town in the Czech Republic, Jihlava hosted the 10th Institute of Documentary Film (IDF) Festival and the 7th East Silver Market. The documentary film programme hosted over 300 East Lūkass T. Rozītis un Māra Pelēcis at Jihlava Film Festival European documentary films and the Industry section wel corned over 400 film professionals – sales agents, distributors, film fund and festival representatives and commissioning editors from Europe and North America – in search of new films to screen met with East European directors and producers seeking to find support for future documentary projects or new audiences for already completed films. This year, among the 300 film titles, traditionally dominated by Polish, Czech, German and Austrian productions, more than 40 films were Baltic productions, Doc Dreams on Silver Screens 16 of those Latvian. Currently the Latvian feature film industry is on hold, awaiting more cuts in state spending and ever diminishing support for filmmaking, as a result a sudden boom in documentary filmmaking can be noticed. Fortunately this is not only a boom in quantity but also in quality as 3 Latvian films – Souvenirs: Healing after War, by director Māra Pelēcis; Working class ballets by Roberts Vinovskis; Family instincU by Andris Gauja – gained further recognition of the IDF and were nominated for Silver Eye Awards, the Industry sections highest accolades. Andris Gaujas film about brothers and sisters incestuous relationship which offers a tragic but highly authentic insight into the bleak reality of the Latvian countryside went on to win a Silver Award in the midlength film category. 1 ne two films I took part in the festival and market with were very different in tone and execution, but both dealing with the theme of war as a destructive force and the never ending aspiration of man trying t right historical wrongs. Māra Pelēcis' Souvenirs – Healing after War A h/rical, intimate and moving eulogy in film about a daughters search for answers after her fathers Andy's unexpected death , a film about Ivars "Andy" Pelēcis a Vietnam Veteran who never truly returned from the war. This is their story, a journey through alcoholism, mental illness and misunderstanding. The story of a man she gets to know better in death than in 30 years of her life. And The Execution Show, by Ilmārs I at km skis and Jurģis Tūbelis (produced by HansaMedia) This is a historical documentaryabout mans naked relationship with major historical events. In the winter of 1946 the Soviets, aiming to match Nuremberg, were organizing a huge public trial in the city of Riga for seven Nazi war criminals. For three centuries, Riga had not witnessed a public execution. Yet, the medieval theater of public punishment and retribution was about to be reborn. This is a film based on the collective historical memory of the World War II which unites countries that experienced a double occupation of two empires and two ideologies. By living with the myth that in World War II total good vanquished total evil, the consequences of this war continue to haunt us to this day. Lūkass T. Rozītis Producer for TV and Film production company HansaMedia', 'Cabinet of Ministers Award Presented to Five Distinguished Individuals

On November 17, 2011, a day before Latvian Independence Day, Prime Minister Valdis Dombrovskis and the members of his cabinet presented the Cabinet of Ministers

Avvard to five distinguished individuāls. This is one of the highest avvards given by the Latvian government for a remarkable contribution to the country’s development. The Cabinet of Ministers Avvard is presented only once a year, a tradition that stretehes back to the 19305. Nominated by members of the government, recipients of the avvard have represented a vvide array of professional fields, ranging from scientists and doctors to artists and vvriters. This year, the avvard vvill be presented to film director Jānis Streičs, flovver expert Jānis Rukšāns, scientist Edvards Liepiņš, Rundāle Palace Museum director Imants Lancmanis, and Occupation Museum of Latvia chairman Valters Nollendorfs.

Jānis Streičs is undoubtedly the most celebrated and prolific Latvian film director of ali time. He has directed more than 20 avvard-vvinning films over the course of his more than 40-year professional career. Over four decades, Streičs’s vvork has both reflected and help to form the Latvian national psyche; memorable scenes and dialogues from his many films have become part of everyday conversation, regularly quoted and passed on from generation to generation in Latvia. Streičs’s most notable films range from the 1967 comedy Captain Enrico’s VVatch, to the 1981 classic tāle of rural life Limousine in the Color of Midsummer Night , to the blockbuster 2010 historical drama Rudolf’s Gold. His 1992 film Child of Man vvas the first and only film ever to be shot entirely in the Latgallian dialect, spoken in eastern Latvia. In addition to his vvork as a film director, Streičs is also a renovvned vvriter and painter. Most recently, he played in active role in developing the concept for the Letonika and National ldentity programs, outlining our national priorities in the field of culture.

Edvards Liepiņš is the head of the Latvian Organic Synthesis Institute’s

Physical Organic Chemistry Laboratory and a professor at Rīga Technical University’s Department of Material Science and Applied Chemistry. He is also a pioneer in a r.evv field of scientific research in Latvia, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy. Led by Professor Liepiņš, the Physical Organic Chemistry Laboratory conducts research in several scientific fields: public health (biomedicine and pharmaceuticals), materiāls Science, and clinical diagnostics.

Professor Liepiņš’s research has helped to bring us closer to understanding the processes that are important for maintaining the life functions of organisms. His studies have revealed the mechanisms behind many new reactions and the precise structures of the resulting products. These have been important not only for Science, but also for new pharmaceutical manufacturers ali over the world. Therefore, his work has vvorked directly toward improving the lives of everyone on the planēt.

Ever since he was twelve years old, Jānis Rukšāns has been fascinated by flovvers. Today he is a world-renowned expert on flower bulbs, with numerous publications to his name, including Crocuses: A Complete Guide to the Gēnus and Buried Treasures: Finding and Growing the VVorld’s Choicest Bulbs, both published in English by Timber Press in the United States. To gather his extensive knowledge, Rukšāns has travelled the globe in search of rare flowers, discovering new species on far-flung expeditions to countries ranging Uzbekistan to Greece.

Rukšāns also tends an extensive nursery of flower bulbs near Cēsis, one of the best and largest collections of its kind in the entire world. As a lecturer, he has been invited to speak at conferences and seminārs ali over Europe, bringing honorto Latvia and promoting intemational awareness of our country. He has also

created various decorative bulbs - new types of flovvers vvhose pure beauty brings joy to vvhoever lays their eyes upon them. Likevvise, Rukšāns’s popular books and academic papērs teach others about what he has learned and discovered, effectively passing on his knovvledge to future generations.

After a long and distinguished career as a university professor in the United States, vvhere he was an active member of the Latvian community, Valters Nollendorfs turned his efforts to the Occupation Museum of Latvia, vvhere he now serves as chairman of the board. Under his care, the museum has become one of the most popular museums in the country, teaching intemational visitors to Latvia about our history. He has vvorked to raise funds for the museum, led the building’s reconstruction project, and overseen the strategy for the museum’s future development.

Since 1996, Nollendorfs has guided numerous delegations of foreign dignitaries through the Occupation Museum, vvorked together with history teachers and students, edited academic papērs by museum employees, as well as lectured on Latvian history at intemational conferences across Europe. His tireless efforts to interpret and explain Latvia’s history have helped enlighten us about the past. They also ensure that the vvorld knovvs what Latvia has been through. Nollendorfs has helped ķept our h istory alive, so that we may never forget it.

Imants Lancmanis has devoted his life and work to the Rundāle Palace, vvhere he has served as museum director since 1975, though his connection vvith the eighteenth century palace stretches back as far as 1964. Over the decades, Lancmanis has led the efforts to restore and preserve the museum and study its history. An art historian by trade, Lancmanis has received numerous avvards and recognitions for his

devotion to the conservation of Latvian culture. His passion for the palace has helped keep the building look as beautiful as it did when it was first constructed, in the 17305, as a summer residence for the Duke of Courland.

Thanks to Lancmanis’s work, Rundāle Palace - one of Latvia’s greatest historical monuments - has become famous throughout the world. The museum not only attracts thousands of tourists each year, but is also the site of important national celebrations and balls attended by foreign dignitaries and officials, becoming a radiant symbol of our country. The restored museum is celebrated by art lovers as an object of sublimē architectural beauty, serving as an important window into our collective past.', 'BALTIC FILM AT THE MONTREAL WORLD FILM FESTIVAL tach year in the fall, for 10 days, the Montreal World Film Festival is a veritable cultural happening, and a wonderful opportunity to sample different cultures of the world as exposed by the film makers. Over 400 films were presented this year – and judging from the attendance the Festival was a popular success! Last year two I.atvian films were presented; this year there was one Estonian film, "Out of the Cold", a USA – Russia – Estonia co-production, with well known U. S. stars. The film is a dramatic love story spanning 20 years from 1939-1959, showing clearly the havoc wrought by the war and Russian occupation on peoples lives. In the story, a once-celebrated American cabaret singer-dancer, Dan Scott who has seen better days, accepts a contract in Tallinn, Estonia and happily finds he is still a major star, there. The war breaks out in Europe and Germany invades and then Russia invades Estonia. None of this is of any concern to Dan, but the lives and loves of the cabaret people get caught in the brutality. In the process of naively trying to find out what has happened to his pregnant love. Deborah, Dan Scott's American passport is confiscated, burned and new papers made for "Daniil Skokov", a citizen of the Soviet Union, and he's sent to a Siberian labour camp for 15 years, where life is quite different from what he has known. There he loses his leg in a grizzly accident but survives! After Stalin's death and 6 years more in exile, Dan comes to Moscow and attempts to connect with the U.S. Embassy and to retrace lus life. The film is entirely believable, well crafted and intelligent combining the best aspects of American film making and technology, and Russian storytelling. The locations are in Estonia (Tallinn) and Russia (St. Petersburg); the main stars are American, and the script writer/director, Sasha Buravsky is Russian – there is a nice balance and things are pretty well accurately depicted for the time period The UM is excllent. Keith Carradine. with about 40 feature films and more than 20 cable-TV and network-TV movies to his credit and also an Academy Award as singer-songwriter stars as Dan Scott (note: his older brother David starred in the memorable "Kung-Fu" TV-series). Mercedes Ruehi, who won an Oscar for her supporting role in "The Fisher King", and Canadian born actress Mia Kirschner, who starred in Dennys Arcand's "I>ove and Human Remains" and Atom Egoyan's "Exotica" play the roles of a cabaret performer and love interest. Brian Dennehy, always an intelligent and well-read actor, with over 45 motion picture credits and major performances in theatre productions, co-stars as the American Ambassador to the Soviet Union. He was also in "Gorky ParkI'. Judd Hirsch, with a distinguished track record in a long string of performances in the theatre, both on and off Broadway, plays the Cabaret owner; Bronson l*inchot, with a number of feature films and TV roles to his credit, also plays in the film. The art directors of course had to have a keen eye to recreate time and place over a period of several decades, and I believe they were succesful at it. A former Soviet army barracks site was only slightly modified to serve as a Siberian labour camp. Truckloads of dirt had to be spread on the paved roads as no paved roads led to these camps in Siberia in 1940. With the aid of props and cars Dan Scott (Keith Carradine) left, Tina (Mercedes Reuhl) and Leon Axelrod (Judd Hirsch) prior to a performance in Axelrod's night club in Estonia in Sasha Buravsky's film "Out of the Cold", a story of a down and out American singer/dancer who gets trapped in Europe at the start of World War II. from that period, a location in St. Petersburg, Russia, was made to look like the Manhattan lower east side of New York. Reading through the movie credits, the cast of minor characters and film rew used in Tallinn feature about 100 Estonian names, including about 25 or more minor roles and job titles from art directors to gaffers to 3rd assistant directors, location managers, illustrators, set designers, coordinators, construction managersm, accountants, costumers, hairdressers, makup, special effects men, electricians, transportation co-ordinators, drivers and office – and set-prodution assistants, securitu and medics – all neceessary to the making of a feature film, and they all get paid! The local Estonian film industry; shaky at best, obviously got a nice shot in the arm (About one and a half million dollars went into the local economy.). Filming started on April 20, a 998 and took 54 days to complete; the last week of shooting was in St. Petersburg, Russia, using a seperate local Russian filming unit. The film was funded by both – American and Russian investors. It's definitely worth a look. □ J. J. Raudsepp']
