Representative_Docs: ['THE BALTIC QUESTION IN WORLD POLITICS The following is a summary of an article by Dr. Boris Meissner on the Baltic question in world politics, published in the German Baltic monthly magazine Baltische Briefe, July/August, 1966, No. 7/8. Dr. Meissner,. of Estonian descent, who is being considered in Germany as one of the outstanding authorities in East European affairs, covers in his article a wide field ranging from the emergence of the three independent Baltic Republics in 1918 to their absorption into the Soviet Union and their present colonial status. The same article was also published in the September issue of the German monthly Europaeische Begegnung. We shall dwell in this summary particularly on the part of the article which deals with the legal aspects of the Soviet annexation of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. * The East European nations, having emerged as independent states after the First World War, were immediately concerned with safeguarding their freedom by forming a defensive alliance against future encroachment on them by their big neighbors east and west. Attempts to form a bloc of mutual assistance comprising the nations from the Baltic to the Black Seas failed due to the diversity of size and interests of the states involved. The greatest and final obstacle arose over the Polish-Lithuanian conflict concerning Wilno. Of all attempts of creating a common defense policy there remained only a Latvian-Estonian alliance which was ratified in November, 1923. The Baltic Republics signed nonaggression pacts with the Soviet Union in 1926 and 1932. Similar pacts were concluded with Nazi Germany in spring, 1939. But, as subsequent events show, they were to no avail. In 1936, Stalin\'s lieutenant, Zhdanov, announced in a speech the Red Army\'s readiness to widen Russia\'s window to the West in the direction of the Baltic States. With Hitler coming to power in Germany, the postwar period of reasonable security for the East Central European nations had come to an end. In 1939, Hitler selected Poland as his next target of aggression. This led to an attempt of Britain and France, who had obliged themselves to defend Poland\'s integrity, to seek a common front with the Soviet Union, being convinced that only the creation of a bloc of formidable resistance would discourage the aggressor before the shooting started. It turned out later, however, that the Kremlin was simultaneously and secretly negotiating with Hitlerite Germany. Soviet demands in both sets of negotiations centered on special arrangements, or "guarantees" regarding Poland and the Baltic States. But Poland was not prepared to grant the Red Army rights of transit through its territory in advance ol war. The Baltic States, on the other hand, were fearful of the Soviet conception of "indirect aggression which they rightfully considered to be a pretext put forward by Moscow to occupy them at will when the time is ripe. While Britain hesitated to sacrifice the freedom of the Baltic States, Hitler was less scrupulous, and thus he won the contest concluding on August 23, 1939, the infamous Nazi-Soviet pact of friendship, which was accompanied by secret protocols dealing with partition of Poland, and turning the Baltics over to the mercy of Stalin. Dr. Meissner dispels subsequent Soviet allegations of a British-German collusion at the expense of the USSR, by sayingIt is conclusively plain that the Baltic question, viewed particularly in connection with the Polish issue, did play a substantial part in the negotiations prior to the Second World War. The research, here presented, serves unquestionable evidence that, next to Hitler, it was Stalin who took an active part in unleashing the holocaust of World War 11. In the fall of 1939, after Germany and Russia had invaded Poland, the Kremlin compelled Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania to sign so-called pacts of mutual assistance with the Soviet Union, granting the latter military bases on their territories in exchange of Soviet pledges not to interfere in their internal affairs and sovereignty. But the following year in June, the Baltic States were served with unprovoked ultimata which led, first, to unlimited Soviet military occupation, and soon thereafter to annexation and sovietization. Says Dr. Meissner: The methods of communist seizure of power and of subsequent sovietization are nowhere more plainly visible than they were in the case of the Baltic States. The author also observes that today the "federal" Baltic Republics of the USSR enjoy less autonomy than they did under the tsarist regime, when they were mere provinces of the Russian Empire. * The last part of Dr. Meissner\'s paper is devoted to the legal aspects of the Soviet annexation of the Baltics, examined in terms of international law. The claim of the Kremlin that the Baltic nations have voluntarily joined the USSR are groundless. They were arbitrarily occupied by Red armed forces. So-catied elections on June 15 and 16, 1940, with only one pro-communist slate of candidates permitted, were a violation of democratic procedures, Soviet-Baltic treaties, and of the constitutions of the independent Baltic States. The pressure of the Soviet armed forces and secret police on the voters and the candidates alike precluded any exercise of free choice and preservation of national sovereignty. The United States reacted swiftly to the "devious processes" of the Soviet invaders in the Baltics. On July 23, 1940, acting Secretary of State, Sumner Welles, issued a strong declaration refusing to recognize the Soviet absorption of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. This initial attitude of justice prevails to the present day. Each year hence, the Government of the U. S. has come forth with renewed condemnation of the Soviet rape of Baltic independence. Soviet apologies, says Dr. Meissner, that the occupation of the Baltic Republics in 1940 was justified by Russia\'s concern of selfpreservation, is contradicted by the fact that her armed forces were already stationed on the Baltic territories in accordance with the treaties of October, 1939. Besides, Hitler\'s intention of attacking the Soviet Union was far from evident at that time. Even granting validity to later Soviet fabrications as to why it was necessary to occupy the Baltic countries while Russia was at peace with Germany, they fail to provide an explanation why Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania are still being held in colonial captivity long after the menace of German aggression has been eliminated. Moscow, surely, had no legal justification, other than the law of the jungle, to impose on the hapless Baltic nations unwanted revolutionary social changes shaped on the Soviet pattern. The Western community of free nations, having never accepted the Kremlin\'s claim that the Baltic peoples joined the Soviet Union voluntarily, is still principally adhering to the Sumner Welles declaration of nonrecognition of the Soviet absorption of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. During the most crucial period of the war there was, indeed, a tendency in the United States and Britain to seek an accommodation regarding the future of the Baltics on Stalin\'s terms. But it came to nought, because of the unsaturable appetite of the Kremlin for much bigger gains in East Central Europe and the Far East. At the present time the United States is still in the lead of upholding the legal prerogatives of the formerly independent Baltic nations, while Britain and France have compromised by recognizing the "de facto" inclusion of the Baltic Republics in the Soviet Union, but not their annexation "de jure, " Western Germany\'s stand in relation to Baltic sovereignty is interesting, insofar as the Hitler-Stalin agreement still looms in the background. According to Dr. Meissner, A German-Soviet Russian covenant of January 10, 1941 on border regulations was never ratified owing to the outbreak of the German-Russian war some months thereafter. Thus Germany has never recognized the Soviet annexation of the Baltic States "de jure"; and the present Bonn Government adheres to this fact in relation to Baltic nationals. Dr. Meissner\'s conclusions regarding the future of the Baltic nations are worth while quoting: Restoration of national independence of the Baltic States may seem to be irrelevant today, because the existing balance of power and the unbroken will of the Soviet Union for expansion seemingly excludes such possibility. However, power constellations are transitory and the intensity of extreme political doctrines tends to erode in time. It is entirely possible to envisage a situation where the interests of Russia, concerned with renovation of its image, may welcome a solution of the Baltic question according to the principles of international law, or at least grant the Baltic Republics the right of genuine self-determination.', "ALL WE ASK IS FREEDOM American Latvian Association Latvian Federation of Australia and New Zealand European Committee for Latvian Freedom Latvian National Federation In Canada Latin American Latvian Association The following is the text of the opening remarks given by Dr. Oļģerts R. Pavlovskis, president of the World Federation of Free Latvians, during the Baltic Tribunal in Copenhagen, July 25th and 26th. For almost 4000 years the Baltic people have lived on the shores of the Baltic Sea. For 4000 years Baits have tilled the land and prospered. For 4000 years Baits have been able to repulse attempts at foreign domination, in order to further the'development of their culture, 4000 years of heritage. Until the two greatest tyrannies of modern times — the Soviet Union and Nazi Germany — joind forces. 46 years ago, in the fall of 1939, the Soviet Union and the German Reich signed a secret protocol. Within a few months, between June 15—17, 1940, while the Western world was stunned by Hitler's actions in the West, in Eastern Europe Soviet armored divisions quietly crossed the borders of the Baltic countries and occupied Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. Today the Baltic countries — once free and independent republics — are under Soviet military occupation. The Soviet government has eliminated almost all human, national and religious rights of the Latvian people. To the mass arrests and deportations in the 1940*5, 1950's and early sixties new tactics and methods have been added in order to carry out the Soviet government's destructive Russification and genocide policies against the Baltic peoples. Incarceration in psychiatric institutions and mind control by drugs are only two of the latest and most insidious developments in the Soviet prison system. Today, after 4000 years of existence, only 53 percent of the population in occupied Latvia is Latvian — a direct result of Soviet government's policies. Baits have been made second class citizens in their own countries. Their culture, their land and their industry are exploited and subjugated to the goals of the Soviet empire. Baltic youths are forced to fight and die in Afghanistan for Soviet interests. The Baltic Sea, once peaceful and prosperous, has been turned into a huge Soviet nuclear naval base for its growing armada, where boarding of foreign vessels and intrusions in foreign waters by the Soviet navy has become almost commonplace. We, the Baltic people, do not ask for territorial gains. We do not ask for power. But we DO DEMAND for Estonians, Latvians and Lithuanians, the right to live as free and peaceful people in our lands and to exercise our God given rights. All we ask is FREEDOM. WHEREAS the armed forces of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (hereinafter referred to as the USSR or the Soviet Union) have occupied Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, and WHEREAS the USSR has established a totalitarian dictatorship in Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania and has deprived the peoples of the Baltic States of the possibility of expressing their will and managing their own affairs in political, economic, cultural, and religious fields, this indictment is submitted on behalf of the Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian nations by the Baltic World Conference (hereinafter referred to as the BWC). The BWC is a federation of the three Supreme Executive bodies of each of the Baltic nationalities and their representative organizations in the Free World, namdy, the Estonian World Council, the World Federation of Free Latvians, and the Supreme Committee for the Liberation of Lithuania. Each of these three central organizations defends, respektively, as negotiorum gestor the interests of the Estonian nation, the Latvian nation, and the Lithuanian nation, in concurrence with the diplomatic representatives of the independent republics of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, who are accredited in the United States of America and other countries, and seeks, as the ultimate goal, an end to the illegal Soviet occupation with the subsequent re-establishment of the independent and democratic states of Estonia. Latvia, and Lithuania. ALL WE ASK IS FREEDOM... INDICTMENT The BWC accuses the Soviet Union, under international law, of the following crimes against humanity and against the States and Peoples of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. A. Conspiracy, Aggression, and the Illegal Annexation Baltic States 1. On August 23, 1939, the Soviet Union, in collusion with Nazi Germany, signed a mutual nonaggression treaty, known as the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact with secret protocols attached and supplemented on September 28, dividing Eastern Europe into spheres of influence. According to the terms of the pact, Finland, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Bessarabia, and Northern Bukovina came under Soviet Russian domination. By this act of conspiracy, the Soviet Union violated Article 10 of the Covenant of the League of Nations and Article 1 of the Kellogg-Briand Pact, and it shares the responsibility with the German Reich in causing World War 11. 2. By implementing the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, the Soviet Union, by use of military force, coerced the signing of the Mutual Assistance Treaties with Estonia on September 24, 1939; with Latvia on October 5, 1939; and with Lithuania on October 10, 1939. The Mutual Assistance Treaties provided for the garrisoning of Soviet troops in the respective neutral and sovereign Baltic republics and were instrumental in carrying out the military occupation of Lithuania on June 15, 1940; Estonia on June 16, 1940; and Latvia on June 17, 1940 (occupatio bellica). These military acts were in direct violation of a number of international treaties which the Soviet Union had signed with Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania in 1920, 1926, 1929, 1932, 1934, and 1939. They were also a willful violation of the Convention for the Definition of Aggression of July 5, 1933. 3. The Soviet Union established the Soviet system of government in the Baltic States, annexing Lithuania on August 3, 1940, Latvia on August 5, 1940, and Estonia on August 6, 1940, thereby incorporating the three countries into the U.S.S.R. B. Exploitation of the Baltic States 1. The Soviet Union has deprived the Baltic States of their status as countries committed to the ideals of law and the promotion of peace in Europe. Through the installation of rocket and submarine bases which far exceed any normal national needs, the U.S.S.R. has turned the Baltic States into a place d'armes. The implementation of such measures forces the Baltic States into serving Soviet offensive designs against other peaceful European countries. 2. In violation of international law, the Soviet Union is drafting Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian youths into its armed forces. They are compelled to serve outside of the Baltic States and are forced to take part in military actions against other countries, i.e., Hungary in 1956, Czechoslovakia in 1968, Afghanistan since December 27, 1979. 3. The Soviet Union is economically exploiting Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania. Accelerated industrialization is used to gain greater political control over the Baltic States, but not to improve their economies or to make them more efficient. 4. The Soviet Union, in exercising its colonial-master's powers, discriminates against the ethnic Baits in employment, housing, education, communications, the arts, and other endeavors and services. 5. Soviet colonial rule in Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, based on alien Russian domination and exploitation, constitutes a violation of the United Nations Organization's Charter and is especially a violation of the Declaration on the Granting of Independence to Colonial Countries and Peoples of December 14, 1960. C. Deportation and Systematic Russification of Estonians, Latvians and Lithuanians with the Purpose of Eliminating Their National Identities, Cultures and Languages. 1. The Soviet Union has deprived the Estonians, Latvians, and Lithuanians of their national self-determination, destroyed their democratic way of life, and subjected them to a totalitarian, imperialistic regime. 2. The Soviet Union is steadily decreasing the proportions of the native populations in the Baltic States. a. During the 45-year Soviet occupation of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, approximately one million Baltic nationals have either been deported to Soviet forced labor camps or resettled to distant areas within the Soviet Union. b. The Soviet Government is systematically resettling Soviet Russians and other non-Baltic nationalities into Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, while coercing the Baltic people by various means to relocate to distant lands within the Soviet Union. 3. The Soviet Union is destroying the phusical and national-political existence of the Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian people. It is deliberately imposing conditions of life which are designed to rob the people of their potential of resuming their status as sovereign nations, to which they are entitled under the right of self-determination. 4. The Soviet Union is systematically pursuing a policy of Russification of the Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian languages and the cultures of the Baltic nations. D. Violations of Human Rights 1. The Soviet Union has deprived the peoples of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania of basic freedoms and fundamental human rights, such as the freedom of thought, conscience, religion or belief, freedom of opinion and expression, freedom of peaceful assembly and association, and the right to a fair and public hearing and independent tribunal. 2. Threats of imprisonment or placement in psychiatric institutions are used to quell all forms of dissent. INTRODUCTORY SUMMARY The Baltic World Conference will present detailed evidence, eyewitness testimony and documentation, to support the abovementioned charges. Our purpose is to demonstrate to this Tribunal, and thus to the world, that the actions and policies undertaken by the Soviet Union and its leaders, have been designed with one primary goal in mind: to inflict on Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania conditions of life calculated to bring about their destruction as nations. Furthermore, we will demonstrate, that these actions are an assault, not only on the well-being of the Baltic peoples, but all freedom-loving peoples throughout the world. We will show that Soviet policies in the Baltic States are contrary to international law, and thus all universally recognized codes of human decency and justice.", 'Dr. Edgar Anderson on GREAT BRITAIN AND THE BALTIC PROBLEM AFTER WORLD WAR I In two recent articles for the Journal of Central European Affairs* professor of history Edgar Anderson has analyzed the role of Britain in the creation of the Baltic republics after World War I. At the collapse of the Tsarist Russian government in November, 1917, the Western Powers were faced with a thorny dilemma: the previously subject nations of the Russian empire showed aspirations to self-government, basing their claims on Allied declarations of people\'s rights to self-determination. These declarations, however, had not been formulated with these nations in mind. On the one hand, the Allies were afraid of creating a precedent for empire-breaking which affected a former and possibly a future ally and which might eventually affect themselves. On the other hand, they were glad about any anti-German uprisings, because after their peace treaty with the Bolsheviks, the Germans had to fight on only one front. Consequently, the Allies took no stand on the issue. In January, 1918, the British were contacted by the first representatives of Latvia and Estonia. They assured these representatives of the sympathy of the British government and of its unwillingness that these countries should fall under German rule. However, very soon the whole Baltic States region did fall under German occupation, legalized in subsequent treaties with the Bolsheviks. The necessary precedent for interference in Russian affairs came about with the Allied landings at Murmansk and Vladivostok to secure their stores against the Germans. The strength of anti-Bolshevik and anti-German attitudes among Baltic nationals influenced Britain to grant a temporary de facto recognition of Estonia as early as May 3, 1918. Britain granted such recognition to Latvia on November 11, 1918. The prickly question of de jure recognition of the Baltic republics was postponed until the Paris Peace Conference. Meanwhile, the British representatives asked assistance in the form of military supplies for the Baltic States from the Scandinavian countries, which were, however, afraid to interfere. Dr. Anderson writes: "In his memoirs the British diplomat Herbert A. Grant-Watson says that Great Britain was the first of the Western Allies to take a practical interest in the struggle for freedom of the three Baltic peoples and to realize that they had advanced sufficiently to form independent nations, capable of deciding their own political future." (I, p. 278) When the German empire suddenly collapsed and the Bolsheviks set up their own Baltic governments and invaded the area, the Allies formulated a clause in the German peace treaty whereby German forces would remain in the Baltic until no longer needed. In November, 1918, a British naval force under Rear Admiral Edwyn S. Alexander-Sinclair was dispatched to the Baltic to gain reliable information about conditions there and to aid the Baltic nationals in fighting the Bolsheviks. The force reached Liepāja in December, established there its main base, and procee*"The British Policy Toward the Baltic States 1918-1920," JCEA, XIX (1960), 276-289; "An Undeclared Naval War: The British-Soviet Naval Struggle in the Baltic, 1918-1920," JCEA, XXII (1962), 43-78. hi the abstract, page references to the first article will be preceded by I, to the second by 11. ded to Tallinn, arriving in time to prevent the collapse of the Estonian republic and to deliver badly needed arms to the Estonians. Thereupon the fleet turned back to Riga. An official communique issued at a conference between British officials and the Latvian government on December 17, 1918, stated that "a part of the British fleet will remain permanently at Riga. The British Government will also provide the Latvian Provisional Government with arms and other needed supplies for the organization of the Latvian State and its struggle against the Bolsheviks." (11, p. 47) The expected collapse of the Bolshevik government did not take place. Instead, the Bolsheviks, in the wake of the retreating German armies, proceeded with their occupation of the Baltic States. In view of the confusing situation there and the unwillingness on the part of the other Allies to intervene physically, Lloyd George ordered the British naval forces to leave Riga in January, 1919, and subsequently relieved Rear Admiral Alexander-Sinclair of his command. The Bolsheviks occupied most of the Baltic area, while a German general, yon der Goltz, established himself in the western corner of Latvia and Lithuania with designs of converting the Baltic into a German province and of eventually restoring the Imperial German government. In view of these developments, the British made an about-face and dispatched another naval squadron, now under the command of Rear Admiral Sir Walter H. Cowan, to the Baltic in order to check the Bolshevik occupation and to aid the anti-Bolshevik and anti-German forces. The British government had deliberately left Cowan without specific instructions,  he proceeded to reestablish the British base at Liepāja, much to the chagrin of the Germans and the pleasure of the Latvian government. A British military mission was established in Liepāja and through the efforts of a Major Keenan and Sir Stephan Tallents, who had seen conditions at first hand, the Allied Supreme Council organized several commissions in April, 1919, to deal with the political and economic situation in the Baltic. However, in a surprise move, the Germans overthrew the Latvian government and re-conquered much of the Bolshevik-occupied territory. In May, 1919, the Allied Commission on Baltic Affairs, because of the conflicting interests of various Allies, including, now, a White Russian representative, decided to regard the Baltic States as a buffer between Germany and Russia, but not to extend to them official recognition. During this time the chief of the British Military Mission in Finland and the Baltic States, General Sir Hubert Gough, in the absence of any official British policy of intervention, exerted all his influence for the good of the legitimate Baltic governments. The Baltic troops managed, with British assistance, to defeat the Germans, who signed an armistice treaty in July, 1919, and retreated from northern Latvia. A British officer was then appointed commander of the German forces in the Baltic, the so-called Landeswehr, and was instructed to use them against the Bolsheviks. Because of the impossibility of obtaining common agreement among the Allies, the very necessary\' economic assistance to these countries was meantime left to private organizations. When the efforts of General Gough to gain de jure recognition of the Baltic States went too far, Lloyd George relieved him of his duties. He was replaced by General Marsh who favored White Russian aspirations and attempted to create a common anti-Bolshevik front composed of the forces of the Baltic States, Poland, the White Russian Yudenich, and an army of German renegades (formerly under yon der Goltz and including some Russian prisoners of war) under Bermondt. While the Allied heads of state repeatedly failed to as;ree on a common and consistent Baltic policy, British naval activities in the Baltic Sea had almost succeeded in putting an end to the Bolshevik navy, and the Baltic forces had almost succeeded in expelling the Bolsheviks from their countries. The British government, however, while not holding out any promise of assistance or recognition of independence, instructed its military missions to discourage strongly any peace treaty between the Bolsheviks and the Baltic States which might not coincide with Allied wishes. Dr. Anderson gives the following illustration of the conflicting attitudes among British heads of state themselves in the fall of 1919: "On September 20, Churchill wrote to Lloyd George asking him to recognize the independence of the Baltic States at once... \'for in a little while it would be too late to secure terms for these small, free states which had given aid against Bolshevism.\' Lloyd George answered with unusual frankness on September 22: You want us to recognize their independence in return for their attacking the Bolsheviks. They would ask us to (1) guarantee it, (2) supply them with equipment and cash to fight with. And in the end, whoever won in Russia, the Government there would promptly recover the old Russian (sic) Baltic ports. Are you, Mr. Churchill, prepared to go to war with perhaps an anti-Bolshevik Government of Russia to prevent that? If not, it would be a disgraceful piece of deception o:i our part to give any guarantee to those new Baltic States that you are proposing to use to conquer Russia... You won\'t find another responsible person in the whole land who will take your view, why waste your energy and your usefulness on this vain fretting...?" (I, p. 286) Accordingly, the British government withdrew any stipulations on a Baltic-Bolshevik peace treaty. In October, 1919, Bermondt\'s German forces, which were supposedly part of the common anti-Bolshevik front, turned on the Latvian troops. The Allies immediately declared a blockade of Germany in the Baltic and it was enforced by Admiral Cowan and his naval force. When Bermondt ignored an ultimatum to withdraw from the fortress Daugavgriva, he was fired upon by combined British-French naval forces in the neighborhood of Riga. With the help of these naval detachments, the Latvians defeated Bermondt\'s forces at Riga and Liepāja and the remnants of these forces were evacuated under an Allied commission. In December, 1919, the British removed from the Baltic all warships except for a cruiser and several destroyers. The policy of the British government by this time had been settled in favor of an independent block of Baltic nations. The British thus had achieved a satisfactory disengagement in the Baltic. In view of the British attitude, the Baltic States signed peace treaties with Soviet Russia and renewed their efforts to secure de jure recognition and admission to the League of Nations. This recognition was extended by the Allied Supreme Council to Latvia and Estonia on January 26, 1921, but to Lithuania only in December, 1922. All of the Baltic countries were admitted to the League of Nations on September 21, 1921. Through the material and military assistance Britain rendered the Baltic States in the years 1918-1920, and through the watchful presence of a token naval force in the Baltic afterwards, she earned deep gratitude on the part of the Baltic peoples. In summing up, Dr. Anderson writes: "The zigzagging, meandering and wavering of the British policy toward the Baltic States could be partly explained as the result of the considerable disagreement among the Allies in regard to the future of those countries, and partly as a result of the disagreement between the British military men and diplomats and even among the diplomats themselves. Those who fought for the independence of the Baltic States finally won. Grant Watson proudly says in regard to the Baltic States: *We gave.. .active and practical assistance in the moment when their fate hung in the balance.\' The Baltic States needed no more than that." (I, p. 289) 0. Krātiņš']

Representative_Docs: ["Andris Teikmanis and State Secretary of Swedish Prime Minister's Office discuss EU future Strategy for Baltic Sea Region On 27 May, Secretary of State of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Andris Teikmanis, met with the State Secretary for the European Union at the Office of the Prime Minister of the Kingdom of Sweden, Maria Asenius, in order to discuss the issues related to the development of the forthcoming EU Strategy for the Baltic Sea Region (Strategy) and the preparations for Sweden's EU Presidency. During the meeting, the Foreign Ministry Secretary of State assessed in positive terms the contribution by Sweden to the elaboration of the Strategy and the exchange of opinions among the interested parties, as well as expressed hope that the Strategy and its Action Plan will be approved during the Swedish EU Presidency starting 1 July. Mr Teikmanis and Ms Asenius had an in-depth discussion regarding the vision by Latvia and Sweden on the administration, implementation, funding and a regular update of the Strategy Action Plan. The energy and competitiveness aspects of the Strategy in their turn were debated when the State Secretary to the Swedish Prime Minister's Office met with the Minister for Economy, Artis Kampars. As part of her visit, State Secretary Asenius took part in a debate on the Strategy, organised by the Foreign Ministry with the Latvian political scientists and representatives of stakeholders in order to provide an insight into the vision of Latvia's NGO sector regarding the measures of the Strategy. The agreement regarding the development of the EU Strategy for the Baltic Sea Region was reached at the European Council of December 2007. The goal of the document is to co-ordinate the efforts by the region's EU Member States, the Baltic regional Organisations, financial institutions, NGOs and stakeholders towards the promotion of a balanced development in the Baltic Sea macroregion. The draft Strategy and its Action Plan prepared by the European Commission are scheduled to be published in June this year.", "11th Baltic Economic Forum to Focus on Raising Regional Competitiveness On September 23 – 24, Rīga will host the yearly Baltic Economic Forum. On the agenda of this year's forum are discussions among business people, politicians, and experts on the experience gained by the Baltic countries when dealing with the global economic and financial crisis, on the possibilities and challenges for raising the competiveness of the region according to the European Union's Strategy for the Baltic Sea Region. On September 24, invited to participate in a special roundtable discussion are the Prime Ministers of the Baltic states – Prime Minister of Latvia. Valdis Dombrovskis, Prime Minister of Estonia, Andrus Ansip, and Prime Minister of Lithuania, Andrius Kubilius. The Commissioner for Regional Policy of the European Commission Johannes Hahn is scheduled to open the discussion, and the Commissioner for Development of the European Commission Andris Piebalgs will also participate. On September 23, the Minister of Economics Artis Kampars and the Governor of the Bank of Latvia Ilmārs Rimšēvičs will give presentations. The Minister of Economy and Communication of Estonia Juhan Parts is also expected tc participate. Business leaders from the Baltic countries will share their experiences on obstacles that hinder a full use of the opportunities provided by the common EU market, and will discuss what should be done to improve conditions for business in the region. During the Forum a debate will be held on the impact of the global crisis on the banking sector and future scenarios, organised by the Association of Latvian Commercial Banks and hosting bank directors and financial experts from the region. The role of corporate governance in state enterprises will be also be discussed from different aspects, for instance, the options to privatize a company or to run it more efficiently. Discussions on several co-operation priorities under the EU Strategy for the Baltic Sea Region are also planned. In a separate session, Ministers of Transport from Latvia and Lithuania will engage in a dialogue with business representatives and experts regarding the issue of how to facilitate the Baltic Sea Region's availability in the sector of transport. The closing session of the Forum will be on the availability of the financial resources and the role of the public and private funding. Registration is open until September 15 and the number of participants is restricted. For further information and registration, please, visit: www. balticeconomicforum.com", "11th Baltic Economic Forum to Focus on Raising Regional Competitiveness On September 23 – 24, Rīga will host the yearly Baltic Economic Forum. Representative_Docs: ['LATVIAN PRIVATIZATION CHIEF CRITICISED An ad hoc committee in the Latvian parliament investigating the country\'s privatization efforts scolded the head of the Latvian Privatization Agency (LPA), Janis Naglis, for making statements detrimental to the privatization of Latvijas Kugnieciba (Latvian Shipping Company, LASCO). The committee called the statements, in which Nagjis argued that share prices for LASCO were too high, as "destructive" for the process, LETA reported on 24 January. Attempts to privatize LASCO failed several times, most recently in December. This comes as Economics Minister Vladimirs Makarovs refused to extend the contract of Nagjis, whose work will terminate at LPA in March. Alongside growing criticism from the government and parliament, Economics Minister Vladimirs Makarovs reiterated that he will not extend the contract of Latvian Privatization Agency (LPA) head Janis Naglis when it expires in March. An ad hoc parliamentary committee investigating privatization reprimanded Naglis on 24 January for making statements detrimental to the privatization of Latvijas Kugnieciba (Latvian Shipping Company, LASCO), The committee called the statements by Nagjis "destructive" to the privatization of LASCO, as during the time bids were being accepted on LASCO, Naglis publicly said the price per share was too high. No proper bids were received for LASCO, resulting in another failed attempt to privatize the country\'s shipping fleet.', 'Latvian technology business group opens office in Silicon Valley Latvians Online February 01, 2011 The Latvian American Business Association of California (LABACA) has opened an office in Silicon Valley, the group announced Jan. 31. The office, located in the Plug and Play Tech Center in Sunnyvale, Calif., is part of the association\'s efforts to link technologyrelated businesses in Silicon Valley and Northern Europe, especially Latvia. LABACA is a non-profit organization formed in August to foster cooperation between U.S. and Latvian intellectual and financial resources to form a hightech "ecosystem." It particularly aims to popularize Latvia\'s information and communication technology sector. "I am confident that the Plug and Play Tech Center community is the right place to initiate exposure and networking for Latvian startups," Richard Grant Gailums, a LABACA board member, said in a press release. "I anticipate that the Plug and Play community and its valued partners, like Silicon Valley Bank, Greenberg Traurig, Berger Lewis and others will help create long-term relationships for successful highgrowth young businesses from Latvia." The Sunnyvale facility is one of a halfdozen locations run by the Plug and Play Tech Center. Formed in 2006, Plug and Play now hosts more than 200 start-up companies in California. It also works with the companies to secure funding. The office in Silicon Valley will help LABACA accomplish a number of goals, according to the press release. They include: • Creation in Silicon Valley of a base of Latvian high tech companies, projects and ideas. • Bringing innovative ideas from Latvia and Northern Europe to Silicon Valley, enabling transformation and exchange between economies. • Using the Silicon Valley ecosystem, "business angels" and venture capital for launching Latvian startups. • Transferring Internet security solutions to the United States from Europe and particularly Latvia. In addition to opening the Silicon Valley office, LABACA in recent months met with Jared Cohen, head of Google\'s think tank, and co-sponsored a conference in Rīga for Latvian iPhone application developers. Further information about LABACA is available on the association\'s website, www.labaca.org.', "EBRD LAUNCHES NEW STRATEGY FOR LATVIA RIGA, 21 Dec, BNS – The European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) has developed a new strategy for operations in Latvia. The EBRD will do more power, rail and municipal infrastructure deals in Latvia, moreover, the bank will in particular seek to do more infrastructure deals that do not require a guarantee from the central government, said the EBRD director in the Baltic states George Krivicky. The bank will also focus on supporting the privatisation of remaining large-scale businesses in the shipping and oil transhipment areas, subject to suitable conditions being met, and other large-scale projects to help attract foreign direct investment. The EBRD has offered Latvian officials that it would join privatisation, for example, of the Latvian Shipping Company (LASCO). Krivicky said the aim of the bank's offer is to create a situation in which there is higher investor confidence in LASCO privatisation and conviction that it is safe and will proceed well. He noted though the bank has not yet discussed with government officials the new situation created by approval of the new privatisation model envisaging to sell 51% of LASCO shares on bourse forcash. Krivicky said the bank also has ideas about privatisation of Ventspils NaftaoW terminal but the EBRD would not make them public for tine time being. The EBRD is keen to provide selective support to banks to assist in consolidation of the financial sector. The bank also aims to support the development of non-bank financial institutions, particularly in areas such as leasing, mortgage finance, insurance and private pension funds. The EBRD will give priority to the development of small and medium-sized enterprises throughout Latvia. Presently the EBRD's credit facility for small and medium-sized businesses in Latvia is served by Latvijas Unibanka. Krivicky said the co-operation with Unibanka is successful which is indicated by the fact that the EBRD has extended to Unibanka he second credit facility of 10 million euros. He noted though that in the area of lending to small and medium-sized businesses the EBRD on principle wants to co-operate with more than one institution in Latvia. Assessing Latvia's development in general Krivicky said that on the one hand the country shows big progress while on the other hand many things still should be done. He especially noted Latvia's external vulnerability which remains high given Latvia's current account deficit. The genera! vulnerability of developing markets towards external shocks has grown lately in the worid. The EBRD cited improvement of the investment environment, completion of privatisation, introduction of the new commercial law, elimination of corruption, small and medium enterprise development, promotion of local authorities' work and improvement of regulatory bodies work as important factors for Latvia's development. The EBRD to date has joined 26 projects in Latvia worth 300 million euros and helped to mobilise an additional 740 million euros in investment from sponsors and co-financiers for a total project value of over 1 billion euros."]

Representative_Docs: ['THE KINGS OF THE BALTIC PEOPLE Prehistoric Time The time of the heroic tribal Princes and famous Kings of the Baltic people is centered mainly around the 13. and 14. Centuries after Christ. Yet, to understand the origin of the problems these rulers of the Baits had to face — in particular, how to repel the Slavic and Germanic aggression, — we should look to the prehistory, to the two millenia before Christ, since in this ancient and vast period vital decisions were made by unknown leaders of the Baits which determined the future course of the history. The foremost among these decisions was the choice of the geographic location of the Baits, the migration to their original home. The comparative philology has succeeded, to a considerable extent, in the difficult task to reconstruct the original Indoeuropean grammar and to classify the languages as to their relative age, partly on the basis of their degree of similarity to the original language. Another criterion is a historical and archeological proof of the earliest known existence of a certain branch of people. Thus, the Baits already lived in their historic homeland — roughly speaking, the present-day territory of Latvia, Lithuania, East Prussia, North Poland, and White Ruthenia (Byelorussia) — at the time of Herodotus (5. Century 8.C.); the Baltic country (Balcia) is mentioned by Plinius the Elder (1. Century A. D.) ; and Tacitus1 describes in some detail the life of the Baits (Aestii or Aestyorum gentes) at the end of the 1. Century A. D. The most desirable product of export from the Baltic country to the Mediterranean cradle of Graeco-Roman culture was amber, the "Baltic By N- E. Bojārs, Ph. D. gold." It was already known the the Greek philosopher Thales (about 600 8.C.). These and other historical data about the Baits are well substantiated and adequately described in books of Latvian and Lithuanian history.23,4 Therefore, it is wrong to say that the Baltic people entered the history only at the time of the German attack upon the Baltic lands in the 13. Century A. D. The Germans are neither the first to bring the Western culture to the East ("Kulturtraeger dcs Ostens") nor the first to preach Christianity in the Baltic lands. On the basis of archeological finds, it has been shown that the early ancestors of the Baits lived near the Baltic Sea already around 2,000 B. C.2 Therefore, the differentiation of the earliest Baits — the so-called Prebalts — from the common Indoeuropean stock must have taken place in the Third Millenium B. C. We do not know whether this differentiation was very gradual, or a sudden migration of a tribe. The great age of the Baits as a separate unit of the White race is also confirmed by the age of their common language. The Baltic (or the common Latvian — Lithuanian) language is older than Sanskrit.3 Thus, if we classify the main branches of the Indoeuropeans (i.e., the White race or Aryans) according to their age, the older branches being listed first, the following approximate order is obtained: 1.) The Prebalts, coming from the South, settle in South Latvia around 2,000 B. C. introducing innovations like beautiful polished stone battle axes, square log buildings instead of the round huts of the nomads, domestic animals and agricultural plants in the land previously sparsely inhabited by nomadic FinnoUgrians whose only domestic animal was the hunting dog;2 2.) The fair-haired Achaeans, coming from the North, penetrate the mountainous country in North Greece around 2,000 8.C., and by 1,900 B. C. the Middle Helladic invasion of Greece is in full swing, the Hellenic people (Danaoi, Ahhiya) being also mentioned by contemporary Egyptians;*"l 3.) The Hittites, coming from the West, invade Asia Minor around 1,900 B.C. and establish the first Empire of the White race (ca. 1640-1200 8.C.); the Hittite Kings 3 (Labamas, Hattusilis, Mursilis, Telepinus, Shubbililyuma, and Tudhaliyas) conquer present-day Turkey, Syria, Palestine, Iraq (Babylon), and attack Egypt; 4.) The Indoiranians, of the same migratory wave from the West, occupy Iran and parts of India; 5.) The Celts, Romans, Germanic and Slavic people appear later on the scene of history, as a result of other waves of migration. The direction of the migratory waves combined with the relative age of languages and other evidence point clearly to East Central Europe, a land somewhere between the Black and Baltic seas, as the original home of the White race ("die Urheimat der Arier," a concept coined by German researchers which has caused hot disputes in the past). The old theory that the White race came from India is no longer tenable. The ancestors of the Baltic people, therefore, lived consecutively in the following three areas: 1.) in the original homeland of the White race somewhere between the Black and Baltic seas around 2500 B.C. and earlier (the origin of the very first White people—according to the Biblical tradition, the descendants of Noah\'s son Yaphet—being shrouded in the mystery of the past) ; 2.) in the original homeland of the Prebalts, from the Vistula to the Daugava and the upper basins of the Pripet, Dnieper, and Oka; 3.) in the historic homeland of the Baits (see Map I, the maximum expansion of the Baits around the first century A.D.), which had expanded to include Latvia to the Estonian border and regions of Scythia and Sarmatia (present-day Ukraine and Russia) almost to Kursk and Moscow. Colonies of Prebalts also settled in Estonia and South Finland, but were absorbed after 1500 B.C. by Scandinavians, and by later waves of Finns and Estonians who came from the East.2 While the expansion of the Baits from the above described 2.) to 3.) homeland has been well established, at least in general terms, the distance and the process of transition from the 1.) to the 2.) area of settlement remains mysterious. Some German researchers have expressed the view that the Prebalts did not travel at all before 2000 8.C., i.e., that the Baltic area is the original homeland of the whole IVhite race, and that the Baits, at least before the Christian era, were the direct descendants of the Aryans (original Indoeuropeans) who had the least amount of admixture of other races. As it is well known, the race-conscious Germans claimed later, in Hitler\'s time, the doubtful honor of the "pure Aryans" or Nordic race for themselves. Other researchers have expressed the view that the Prebalts came from a Mediterranean country, settled for a while somewhere north of the Black Sea, and then proceeded to their 2.) and 3.) homelands.3,6 The dainas (Latvian and Lithuanian folk songs, some of them of very ancient origin) and other folklore materials contain numerous allusions to rich cities and castles, to kings and heroes in splendid armor, and to fair princesses resplendent with gold, silver, and jewels.3 Ancient Latvian decorative designs are sometimes similar to decorative motifs from excavated Troy.3 It should be remembered that several cities successively flourished for centuries in the same location of Troy in Asia Minor before the Trojan War (1194-1185 B.C.)5 described later by Homer. Thus, the ancestors of the Baits might have had a splendid period of culture in a rich southern land in the remote past of the Egyptian, Cretan and Trojan civilizations. What was the catastrophe of nature 7 or who was the enemy that forced the ancestors of the Baits to abandon this rich and legendary land? Who were their kings, their leaders that directed the long march to the north, to the shores of the Baltic Sea? The Hebrews have their Moses who led them toward the Promised Land, but about our leaders history is silent. There is some very interesting but scanty information about the earliest writings of the Baits, an alphabet derived from the Greek independently of the Latin and the Cyrillic script with entirely different meanings of the letters.8 Such partly deciphered inscriptions in ancient Baltic letters have been found on a flag of ancient Prussians; on a silver statue of the god Perkunas or Pērkons (the deity of thunder) ; and on very old musical instruments (Lith. and ancient Latv.: kankle; Latv.: kokle). Thus, with deep regret about the rich cultural heritage destroyed in the land of the Baits by the ceaseless attacks of the rapidly multiplying and aggressive Slavs and Germans, with only a tantalizing glimpse of rich cities and ancient writing, of destruction and migration, of forgotten tribal princes who led the people to a new homeland and to the Baltic Sea which got its name from the Baits (or the "Whites," since "baltas" in Lithuanian or "baits" in Latvian means "white"), we leave behind us the legendary time of the Bronze Age. We leave the two happy millennia before Christ, when the amber fishers grew rich from their commerce, astonished at the high pricesl paid for it in Greece and Rome; when the Baits cultivated their fields and drank their medalus (mead or honey-ale) in peace; when no alien hand dared to cut the holy oaks and sacred birch grovesß of Romove (Rāmava in Latvian, the residence of the krivu krivs, the High Priest of the Baltic religion). We enter the two millennia of the Christian era—not a time of peace, Pax Christi in regno Christi, deceitfully preached by the alien conquerors who used the baptizing of the Baits as an excuse to loot and oppress them.9 On the contrary, the last two millennia are full of ceaseless struggle for liberty. It is a time of spilt blood and burnt cities, of atrocities committed by the Teutonic Knights, by Ivan the Terrible and by Stalin in the fair land of the Baits. It is a time of the fire and the sword. If the Baltic land is Terra Mariana even as Palestine is Terra Domini, as it was solemnly declared9 by Pope Innocent 111 on November, 1215, then there is more reason to cry: "Dievs, Tava zeme deg!" (God, Thy land burns!). Out of the darkest night of this struggle the names of the kings and princes of the Baits stand out and radiate through history their message to us: to have courage and to love freedom. Early History: The Kurs At the time of the persecution of the early Christians in Rome, during the I period (0-400 A.D.) of the Iron Age in the Baltic lands, the new iron weapons are put to their use in the land of the Kurs in defense of their country. Significantly, the scene of the first (2nd century A.D.) and the last (May 1945) battle in the Baltic lands to date is Kurzeme (also called Curonia or Kurland), the large Latvian peninsula in the Baltic Sea. Our source of information about the first historical conflict is the Hadinga Saga.to It tells eloquently about the bravery and chivalry of the Kurs. The attempt of the Danish Prince Hading to invade Kurzeme ends in a disaster. The Prince is captured by King Lokher of Kurzeme. When the Danish prince\'s wounds heal, the Kuronian king permits him to return home. A century later, another king of Kurzeme, Dorno, defends his people against a Danish invasion at the Duna urbs (Daugava city; presumably Riga).ll Archaeological evidence also points to the existence of a traders\' town at the site of Riga (pronounced "Riya" by Low Germans) a long time before it was allegedly "founded" by Germans in 1201 A.D. The ancient name of Riga is probably derived from the Latvian word "rija" (also pronounced "riya"). It means a threshing place for grain; or, in this case, probably a storage granary for the merchants of Riga.3 The derivation of this name from the Latin word "rigare" (to irrigate) by Henricus de Lettis12 seems to be just a witty play on words. The Latvian chronist admits that the natives showed the city Riga to Bishop Albert in 1200 and that its name is the same as that of a local lake.12 Therefore, Bishop Albert is not the founder but just a conqueror of Riga who converted the town of wooden buildings to a stone cityfortress. The Kingdom of Kurzeme (or Kursa) existed for the amazingly long period of 1100 years (c. 100-1231 A.D.) and successfully repelled numerous Danish and Swedish invasions throughout its history. Since Kurzeme is smaller than either Denmark or Sweden, and presumably had a smaller population than the Scandinavian countries, the Kurs had to fight, as a rule, against heavy odds. Their preservation of freedom and their glorious victories in the intermittent naval warfare and land attacks during 11 centuries are indisputable facts that speak, volumes about the courage of the Kurs and their efficient political and military organization. The Kurs were able to meet the Vikings as equals in battle, the dreaded scourge of the seas. The Vikings (also called Varyags or Normans) were able to conquer the vast lands of Russia, possessions of Byzantium, southern Italy, Normandy, and England—but they never broke the resistance of their much closer neighbors, the Kurs. In fact, the Kurs often retaliated and took the offensive, so frequently ravaging the Danish coast that the services in Danish churches in the 11th century were concluded by the prayer: "For Kurerna bevare ossj milde Herre Gudi" (From the Kurs save us, merciful Lord God.)13 But what about the nationality of the Kurs? Did they really belong to the Baltic branch of the Indoeuropean race linguistically? This question has been emphatically answered with a "yes" by German historians who were contemporaries of the Kurs,14 and by later philological studies.15 Even until World War II (and perhaps to this day) direct descendants of the Kurs lived on the Peninsula of Kurs ("Kurische Xehrung" in German) on the Baltic coast of Prussia. In this isolated, sandy and narrow strip of land (south of the large and fertile Latvian Kurzeme), the Kurs were not exposed to incursions of aliens, and therefore they preserved their own dialect to our times, which is closely related to the Latvian language. Since the ancient Prussians and Lithuanians essentally were not seafaring peoples, the Kurs dominated the whole Baltic coast between the estuaries of the Vistula and the Daugava and hotly contested the dominion of the Baltic Sea with the Vikings. That is why some historical data concerning the Vikings or Varyags of Denmark, Sweden, and later Russia are included in the following brief compilation of Kuronian history (Table I). TABLE I Some Historical Data Concerning Kursa Event%>3,B Year Prebalts in Kursa (Prehistoric) c. 2000 B.C. Sweden, Estonia) ruled by king Ivar until c. 675 Danes, Kurs and Livs (Ivar\'s grandson Harald) lose the great naval battle of Bravalla to the Swedes (Sigurd Hring) ; Danish chronicle extolls the bravery of the Kurs c. 750 Kurs reconquer Seeburg, expel the Swedes and Danes c. 800 Varyags conquer Russia: Ladoga (Aldeigjuborg), Ilmen (Holmgardr or Novgorod), Polotsk (Pollteskia), Smolensk, Kiev; the Kurs (Kuryanyi) elite troops and bodyguards of Varyag kings 820-880 Kurs defeat the Danes, capture their fleet, gold and silver 853 Swedish kings Olaf and Eric invade Kursa (Seeburg, Apule) 854, 860 Egil Skallagrimson of Iceland is captured by Kurs; escapes 925 Danish king Swreyn Astrittson builds a church in N. Thus, the Kurs were the bulwark of the Baits against the Scandinavians; the Zemgals and Lithuanans fought pitched battles against the Teutonic Order of the Germans; and the Letgals defended the eastern frontier of Latvia against the Russians by their 186 castles found in their territory east of the Daugaval6 and many more fortified places in the regions of Adzele and Polote, east of the modern Latvian-Russian border.18 Around the year 1000 A.D., the Latvians west of the Daugava (Zemgals, Sels and Kurs) had 135, and the Estonians had 160 castles.16 A map of numerous Lithuanian castles, chiefly a bulwark against the Teutonic aggression, is available in Mr. Jurgela\'s book4 (p. 85). It should be remarked that Map I of this paper, showing the maximum extension of the Baltic people (at the time of the Roman Empire) is chiefly based upon extensive research by the famous Lithuanian professor K. Būgal7 and upon other ethnographical investigations.lB Each castle (Latv.: pils, Greek: polis) and the settlement around it was governed by a Seigneur (Latv.: virsaitis or, in the dainas, kundziņs; chronists\' Latin: senior). The seigneurs or elders had their members of the household, the servants, and the bodyguard (Latv.: draugi, draudze; Russ. druzhina; Gothic: driugan; Latin: drungus)?> which for the major castles assumed the role and the size of a standing army (e.g. 15,000 men in the Kuronian castle Apule and 7,000 in Seeburg or Jūrpils). The seigneurs of several castles were responsible to a prince (Latv.: kungs; Lith. kunigas; chronists\' Latin: princeps) who ruled a country district (Latv.: novads; chronists\' Latin: terra). The most powerful of the territorial princes assumed leadership over several terrae, particularly in time of war, and thus became kings (Latv.: lielskungs -or ķēniņš; Lith.: didikunigas ; chronists\' Latin: dux or rex). Within this pattern, the evolution of tribal kingdoms became inevitable. All Latvian tribes were close to the point of a final unification into one state3 before the German invaders, particularly the shrewd and ambitious Bishop Albert, set one tribe against the other and carved out a state for himself and the German knights. The Latvian neighbors were more fortunate; thus, the Lithuanians were united by Mindaugas around 1225, and the Swedes in 1222 by the Folkjunger jarls. According to the testimony of the chronicles,9-19 the following rulers of ancient Latvians definitely attained the rank of king (rex) in the 13th century: Visvaldis and Tālivaldis of the Letgals, Viesturs and Nameitis of the Zemgals, and Lamikis of the Kurs. They kept their royal power in the time of war and peace; the royal line tended to become hereditary; they had large armies which could successfully engage in battle the aggressive neighboring states; and their status was on the verge of becoming a national kingship. Thus, the ancestors of the Latvians had four distinct levels of office or status within the nobility or "upper class:" 1. ) Bajāri or labiesi, well-to-do landed gentry of freemen; 2. ) Chieftains (seigneurs, elders) of individual castles; 3. )Princes of country districts; and 4. ) Kings of provinces or tribes. Since all Latvians were free men (except for prisoners of war; e.g.. the Kurs employed captive Danes as farm workers), the lower levels of the nobility often intermarried with the middle class or with the poor. Thus, the dainas tell us about the nobleman\'s love for the orphan girl (bārenīte). However, the kings usually married within their own class or they took home foreign princesses. The chronist Henry the Lett (Latvian)20 tells us that the kings Visvaldis had married the daughter of king Daugerutis of Samogithia (lower Lithuania). There is some information about the marriage of a German-Livonian prince Lieven with a daughter of a king of Zemgale; and a legend about the love of the Kuronian prince Indulis for Aria (Ārija), a daughter of a German count. The close friendship and probably blood relationship of the king Nameitis and the noble Lithuanian family who were kings of Samogithia and later grand dukes of Greater Lithuania is fairly well established.2!-22 Some Latvian writers of our times who call the above named kings merely local chieftains (virsaiši), do a disservice and misrepresentation of the glorious and heroic past, when even the enemies of the kings of the Baltic people accorded them the title rex. Would their claim to royalty have been more valid if they had received a crown from the pope or from the emperor of the Germans? In fact, Lamikis (Lammekinus), the king of the Kurs, concluded a treaty with the pope Gregory IX. an act of pacification between equals, on December 28, 1230; the text of this treaty has been preserved.3 The treaty preserved Lamiķis\' title of king, and assured to his subjects the possession of their lands and property. The treaty was ratified by the pope in February, 1232, and the king of the Kurs was expected to appear in Rome in 1233 for his coronation. This event never took place because, ignoring the treaty, the Brethren of the Sword attacked Kurzeme, ransacked and burnt the castles and intercepted the Kuronian messengers to the pope,3 while the loyalty of the Kurs was split between those who wanted to fight and those who expected a peaceful solution, a ban on the Germans. In many other instances the actual power of the pope and the emperor in the Baltic was overestimated by the native kings, who relied upon solemn treaties. Time and again the Germans invited Estonian, Zemgallian, or Prussian leaders for a peace conference and thereupon murdered them all,21 or forced the Livonian elders to give their sons as hostages who were deported to Germany and educated to be subversient to the German cause.12 A sad fate was in store for the king Visvaldis, who concluded a treaty with the German bishop in order to get his wife released from German prison.2o In short, treaties with the pope or the emperor did not actually secure the royal power of the local kings, but merely tended to make them vassals and subjects of the Germans. For this reason, the king Viesturs of the Zemgals, "in the hardness of his infidelity," did not listen to the enticing promises, to "many discussions and long sermons" of the legate of the pope, Bishop William of Modena.2"\' The Hundred Years\' War of the Baltic The kings of the Baltic people commanded armies of many thousands. During a century of almost constant warfare (from 1198 to 1290 in Latvia, and much longer in Lithuania),2 34 919 they often inflicted crushing defeats on the German Orders of Sword Bearers and Teutons, the most powerful military organization oiL late medieval Europe. The Germans could keep their foothold in Latvia only by a constant stream of crusaders from many lands of Christian Europe. According to a report by a master of the Livonian Order in 1351, the Order had lost in battle against the Zemgals and Kurs alone 117,000 men, among them 6 masters of the Order, 28 dukes and counts, 49 barons, and 11,000 noble knights and gentry.2l The total losses of the Germans in the conquest of Latvia and Estonia over the period of a century may amount to 300,000; it seems to be a conservative estimate. Bishop Albert went from Riga to Germany each winter to preach the crusade. He returned with numerous ships every spring, laden with ironclad knights and soldiers. He was often joined by dukes and margraves who brought along to Latvia and Estonia (then called Livonia) the better part of their armies. It is ridiculous to assume or to imply that, on the Latvian side, this hundred years\' war of the Baltic of the 13th century could have been fought or directed merely by local chieftains (virsaiši), by a handful of wild and unruly barbarians. On the contrary, the Latvians and Lithuanians, as well as their Estonian neighbors (who do not belong linguistically to the Baits but nevertheless have close geographical and historical ties with them) should be proud of their great kings who led their ancestors to many splendid victories. Even if the Baltic region came later under the domination of foreign powers, the national spirit had been awakened and the thirst for independence had been created by this heritage of a heroic past. Seven centuries after the period of the great kings of the Baltic people, the free republics—Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania—were formed. The spirit of independence, which survived the dark centuries of foreign domination, is not going to be crushed by just another occupation under which the Baltic states now are. History knows of even worse threats to the very existence of the Baltic people which were overcome, with time. The road to freedom to be taken in the future will be illuminated by the light of the past: the immortal fame of the kings and heroes of the Baltic people. REFERENCES 1. Henricus de Lettis, op. cit., sub anno 1225.', 'THE KINGS OF THE BALTIC PEOPLE By N. Bojārs, Ph.D. The German Conquest of Prussia. Livonia, and Estonia While the Yatvings, in the numerous wars of liberation from the Slavs and Tartars, became associated with the Lithuani ans and formed with them a single nation, the fate of the Prussians was to be conquered by the Germans. Since the ancient Prussians and their heroic battles for liberty have been described in numerous chronicles and documents, the knowledge of their early history is more complete than that of the Yatvings and Galinds. In fact, it would be a difficult task to condense to a brief summary a description of the life and customs of the ancient Prussians, their religion and politics, the depressing story of their conquest by the Teutonic Order in the 13th century, their desperate revolts and escape to Lithuania. However, our task is facilitated by a lucky coincidence : in an earlier issue of this magazine several articles deal with questions related to the ancient Prussians, and we do not have to repeat the information here. E. Brastiņš\'9 discusses the Prussian religion and the early attempts to convert them to Christianity; L. Lieven20 describes the history of Prussia in the general framework of the medieval history of East Europe and gives interesting samples of the ancient Prussian language; while H. Biezais2\' reviews the description of the German conquest of Prussia and Livonia originally given in H. Schreiber\'s new book.22 While all these articles appear to be interesting and largely objective, dis- (Continued from Zintis Nr. 11/12) closing a sympathy toward the ancient Prussians and their tragic fate, and while these papers mostly complement and confirm each other, there is a note of discrepancy and perhaps even contradiction concerning the methods of conquest in Prussia, the treatment of natives by the German knights of the Teutonic Order, and the fate of the descendants of the Prussians. Quoting Dr. Schreiber\'s book, Dr. Biezais talks about the "annihilation" of the ancient Prussians; he writes that "after countless destructive battles, the Prussian nation succumbed, but it never accepted the new faith." He further quotes from p. 82 of the book:22 "The few remaining living warriors did not embrace Christianity .. . The men who after this long war had }ret remained alive wandered over to Lithuania .. . And despite this, there are historians who dare to maintain that the Prussians were not annihilated but only Christianized and Germanized." L. Lieven defends precisely such a view; he writes20 that at the end of the Great Uprising in Prussia (1260-1282) "probably one third of the Prussian people had perished or had sought freedom by leaving for Lithuania and thence for Russia, but two thirds of the Prussians remained to form the bulk of the population." In his article, he attempts to refute "that it was the policy of the Teutonic Order ... to exterminate the Prussian people in order to settle an empty land with German peasants."2o While it appears somewhat futile to argue about the number or the percentage of Prussians who remained in their land after the German victory, since no impartial census was then taken, the methods of conquest of the Baltic lands by the Teutonic Order are well known. We do not need the testimony of non-Germanic historians who might be said to be prejudiced against the Germans. It is sufficient to quote German chronicles written by people filled with religous zeal, who on the whole approved the conquest and subjugation of the Baltic nations. Such a person was Henricus de Lettis (either a German or a Germanized Latvian, a hostage grown up and educated in Germany), since he was happy about "the awakening of the idolatrous Livs from their idol-worship and sin through the fire of love."21 22 In what manner was this "fire of love" expressed by the friars of the Sword Bearers and Teutons? Was Christianity preached in the peaceful manner of Christ, or with blood and fire and sword? L. Lieven apparently does not believe that the conquests took place according to the "sword, blood and fire" doctrine, since he attempts to refute this "19th century German nationalist doctrine."20 Let us turn for the answer to this question to Henricus de Lettis. He describes 23 the arrival of Bishop Berthold and his German army in Riga in 1198. The Livonians made a camp beyond the Riga hill. The chronist says further: "Nevertheless, they (the Livs) sent a messenger to the Bishop, asking why he had brought an army against them. The bishop replied that as dogs return to their vomit, so they had returned too often from the faith to paganism. The Livonians then said: \'We shall do away with this reason. If you send back your army, you may return with your people to your bishopric in peace, and you may compel those who have received the faith to keep it. You may lead others to accept it, but with words, not with blows.\' " That was not Berthold\'s way to preach Christianity: he did not accept the peaceful proposal of the Livs,. but elected to wage war and lost his life, since "those who raise the sword will perish by the sword." He was killed in battle by Ymaut on July 24, 1198. Under the name of Imanta this Livonian was popularized as a national hero by Latvian writers in the 19th century. The behavior of the German knights of the Order of Sword Bearers in Estonia is described by Henricus de Lettis24 as follows: "When we arrived there, we spread troops over all roads, villages and districts of the country; we set on fire and destroyed everything; we killed all that was of masculine sex, captured women and children, and took away much cattle and horses .. . and returned to Livonia with great rejoicing ... We divided among u< the whole booty ... and returned joyful] y ... as the victors exult when they divide booty." In this manner, the German militant friars conquered the relatively small territory of the Livs by 1212 and Estonia by 1227. Both Livs (Livonians) and Estonians belong to the Finno-Ugric family of languages. Although geographically they live in the Baltic region, linguistically they do not belong to the Baltic people or to the Indoeuropean race. Later (13th-16th centures) the whole territory of Latvia and Estonia was named Livonia by the German conquerors, since the first German strongholds in the Baltic were built in the land of the Livs, although originally the Livs had only about four castles and had played no important part in the early history of Latvia7 (p. 54). Bishop Christian\'s and Prince Konrad\'s Polish crusaders were the first to attack the Prussians,4 but without much success. Konrad invited the Teutonic Order to Prussia in 1230, hoping to profit in the deal. Thus, a Polish ruler is to blame for creating this menace to the Baltic lands and Poland.25 In a way, though, Konrad\'s design has been realized now: with Prussians destroyed in the 13th century and the Germans exiled in 1945, Poland has ultimately profited, since it has now occupied most of the old Prussian lands. The Teutonic Order started their first full-scale "crusade" in Prussia in 1231. The chronicle of Oliva describes the torture and horrible death of Pipin, a ruler of a Prussian castle who was captured by the Teutons.26 Innumerable times, balked by the valor of the Prussians, the Germans resorted to scorched-earth tactics and imposed impossible taxes upon the survivors. The elders of Varmians and Natangians in Prussia sought some relief from the grain taxes levied by the Teutons, designed to crush the Prussian spirit by starvation:27 "You know that our poor brothers worn out by the many working days on your estates, have no time left to till their own fields, and their fields remain unfilled. Their children are suffering from hunger, and now you want to take their last piece of bread from their mouths." Friar Wolradt "Mirabilis," the voigt of  invited fifty Prussian elders to a feast to hear his answer. The guests were treated in a most hospitable manner. Suddenly, he left his guests, all exits were locked and barred, the building was set on fire, and the elders burned alive.27 This atrocity (and not so much the remote battle of Durbe, as indicated by Lieven20) ignited the Great Prussian Insurrection, in which the Teutonic Order was very nearly expelled from Prussia. The greatest leader of the Prussians in this insurrection was Prince Monte. The German chroniclers never cited a single deed of chivalry of their own knights but extolled the chivalry of Monte and of Zemgallian princes in 1260, and, a century later, the chivalry shown by King Kestutis of Lithuania.4 Other Prussian princes who directed the Great Insurrection were Glappas, Auktums, and Divane. The Prussians defeated the Teutons in many pitched battles and destroyed all their fortresses except Kdnigsberg, Balge, Elbing, and Kulm.4 The siege technique of strongly fortified stone castles had not been sufficiently developed; there was no artillery as yet, and catapults and ballistas were not effective against huge walls; and Mindaugas, the Lithuanian king, hesitated too long, because of his baptism by Germans, and denied his help to his Prussian kinsmen at a crucial time. This omission was later called treason by the revolting princes who killed the king in 1263. Instead, Mindaugas unnecessarily sent an army against Bryansk, deep in Russia. Prince Monte had been kidnapped by the crusaders as a young boy and had been reared by the Teutons in Brandenburg. His Catholic name was Hinrik (Heinrich). An idealist and a true knight, he once offered his life for that of a captured enemy knight whom he had known in Germany. A constant stream of misinformed crusaders arrived in Prussia from Western Europe, and as years went by the Prussian struggle for liberty became exceedingly difficult. Prince Monte was ambushed by Hermann yon Schonenberg, the commandant of Christburg. He had Monte tied to a tree and then pierced his brave heart.4 The Great Insurrection was largely suppressed by 1274, but sporadic battles continued until 1282. In 1281, while storming a German castle in Prussia, King Nameitis of Zemgale was killed; he had previously defeated the Teutons in many battles in Latvia and then had hastend to help his unfortunate kinsmen in the south.2B Prussia was so completely devastated in this desperate struggle for freedom that it took the Germans the next fifty years to rebuild their chain of fortresses and settlements.4 The Teutonic Order, now securely entrenched in its castles in Prussia, continued its policy of extermination in the 14th century, principally against the Samogithians (Zhemaitians) of Lithuania. More than a dozen chronicles written by Germans and enumerated by Jurgēla29 are replete with terrible stories of Teuton atrocities. For example, a Teuton raid surprised a peaceful Samogithian village at the time of a wedding in 1337. The village was burnt, a bloody "wedding dance" was staged, and the people were hunted down and killed by the Teutons, "just like foxes and rabbits are hunted."30 In another raid, "by the Marshal\'s orders, all pagans of both sexes were killed, the old and the young, and the district (of Streva) was laid waste by fire."3l As a German historian scornfully writes,32 "the Order changed in the shortest time to a colony of convicts, where vagabonds and criminals from all Germany took refuge." Numerous Papal warnings did not have any effect on the Teutons.4 6 7 On April 4, 1325 the Archibishop of Riga excommunicated the Teutonic Order. The Teutons did not pay any attention. Even bishops were held imprisoned in the high towers and underground cellars of their castles. The bishop of Oesel was murdered in 1381.35 Bishop Theodore of Samland was sentenced to death by starvation by the Grand Master of the Teutonic Order in 1477.36 When killing someone, the Teutonic knights derided the victim with the words: "Papa te iubavit!—The Pope will help thee !"33 More complete data on the behavior of the Teutons in the Baltic TABLE V.
