Representative_Docs: ["PUBLIC SUPPORT FOR LATVIA'S ACCESSION TO EU STABLE A public opinion poll carried out in September by the European Integration Bureau, in co-operation with the State Chancellery and ihe market and public opinion survey company Latvijas Fakti, reveals that support for Latvia's accession to the European Union (EU) is stable. if a referendum on Latvia joining the EU had taken place in September 2002,46.2% of Latvia's residents would have voted for EU membership (46.6% in July) and 35.8% against (35.3% in July); 17.9% of residents were undecided (as compared to 18.1% in July). The number of EU supporters has therefore remained stable over the past months. According to the poli, people aged 18-39, ethnic Latvians and people with higher incomes and higher education are generally more positively inclined towards EU membership. Among Latvian citizens, support for accession was at 48.4% in September, the highest result so far in 2002. Among non-Latvians, Latvian citizens were more positive about joining the EU than non-citizens. Residents of the Vidzeme region of Latvia are most strongly in favour of EU accession, while in the Latgale region the percentage of euro-optimists is lower than in other parts of the country.", 'CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER April 1991 Vol. XI No. 4 "Do you support the democratic and independent statehood of the Republic of Latvia?" That was the question that 1,666,128 residents of Latvia responded to on March 3, when the Republic of Latvia held an advisory vote on independence. The respondents represented 87.57% of all those eligible to participate in the advisory vote. It was open to all people, age 18 or over, who were permanently registered in their internal passports as residents of Latvia. Soviet army personnel, who do not possess such passports while on active duty, were not eligible to vote. The results in Latvia exceeded the most optimistic predictions. Ethnic Latvians represent, at the most, 53% of Latvia\'s population. Latvian government officials had hoped that enough non-Latvians would support independence to achieve a \'yes\' vote between 60%-70%. Support for indpendence exceeded 50% in ever Latvian city and rural district. Even in the heavily Russified city of Daugavpils, where Latvians represent only 12% of the population, the \'yes\' vote was 51.29%. In Riga, where Latvians make up approximately 36% of the population, pro-independence votes reached 60%. In rural regions, were ethnic Latvians predominate, the cumulative vote total was 85% \'yes. YES – 73.68% NO – 24.69% Unexpected support, predictable reactions. Anatolijs Gorbunovs, President of the Supreme Council of the Republic of Latvia said that the vote was a strong demonstration of support for the parliament and its pro-independence program. Jorgen Vinter, chairman of the Danish parliament and an observer of the vote in Latvia said, "I am very happy about the results." Anatolijs Aleksejevs, leader of the antiindependence Interfront group claimed the vote did not reflect public opinion and said that it could only be viewed as a "preliminary phase" to a Soviet referendum. Mikhail Gorbachev said nothing. Last year Gorbachev had insisted on referenda in the Baltic States, claiming that the pro-independence governments were operating without a demonstration of public support. When Lithuania planned its February vote, Gorbachev changed his mind and declared all Baltic votes \'illegal. A public relations poll? Gorbachev did have a point in claiming the Baltic votes (Estonians voted 77% for independence, Lithuanians 90%) had no legal status. In the eyes of the Baltic governments, these votes had nothing whatsoever to do with referenda called for in Soviet secession laws. Since the Baltic States are not a legal part of the Soviet Union, they do not have to follow Soviet procedures to secede. Nor, as occupied countries, do they need referenda to establish their legal right to independence, no more so than should Kuwaitis have held a referendum on whether they wished to remain under Iraqi occupation. The votes were held, largely because many Western governments, including the United States, had encouraged the Baits to hold them. It was felt that a public show of support would strengthen the Baltic cause and take another argument away from Gorbachev. Until now, Gorbachev had claimed that a majority of Baltic residents were against independence. It was on this pretext that he had threatened presidential rule, i.e. to defend the rights of the silent majority. But a majority in the Baltic States has been far from silent, and used the votes to register their views. Independence for ALL the people. In Lithuania, where over 80% of the population is ethnic Lithuanian, a large pro-independence vote was anticipated. In Latvia, no one was really sure what to expect. The results suggest that between 1/3 and 1/2 of all non-Latvians in Latvia are firmly pro-independence. Another sizable segment did not vote, suggesting indifference or neutrality on the issue. What of the 24% who voted against independence? One group that no doubt espoused this view was Latvia\'s large collection of retired Soviet army officers. It is estimated that at least 60,000 Soviet veterans have retired to Latvia, bringing with them another 120,000 family members. Many of them reside in Riga and in nearby Jūrmala, and because they are retired, were elgible to vote. (Nevertheless, 68% of Jurmala\'s voters endorsed independence.) They make up the bulk of the anti-independence Interfront movement. Soviet government bureaucrats and family members, and many factory workers who have emigrated to Latvia only recently, probably made up the remainder of the \'no\' votes. Despite this core of hardline Soviets, the vote tally indicates that there is no broadbased anti-independence sentiment in Latvia, not even among Russians or other non-Latvians. Non-Latvian supporters of independence have either lived there for generations and feel loyal to their adopted homeland, or simply feel they will have a better life in an indpendent Latvia. The non-voters may be waiting to see who wins in the tug-of-war between Riga and Moscow, and probably are willing to back the winner, regardless of who it turns out to be. Get me to the polls on time. According to the Associated Press, one 102-year-old woman voted on Feb. 24, when balloting opened for people who could not make it to the polls on March 3. She was voting early, she said, because she was afraid she might die before Sunday. German parliamentarians saw two women, one 92 and the other 90, walking to the polling stations to vote. Anatolijs Gorbunovs, President of the Latvian parliament, voted at the polling stations located in the the Zubar Candy Factory in Riga. While waiting for him to arrive, election officials passed out sweets to reporters and photographers on the scene. Gorbachev\'s referendum Mikhail Gorbachev\'s March 17 referendum on preserving the Soviet Union brought out the anticipated majority, but barely. The Baltic States, Moldāva, Armenia and Georgia all refused to participate in the Soviet referendum. The Latvian parliament ordered all government agencies and branches to not participate in the organization or implementation of the vote. It stated, however, that it would not prevent Soviet citizens in Latvia from voting in the referendum if they chose to do so. According to one Latvian reporter, those who did participate had ample opportunity to vote as many times as they wished; to test the system, the journalist voted 6 six times. The Soviets reported that 500,000 voted in Latvia, 90% of them supporting preservation of the Union. Since several hundred thousand Soviet miltary are stationed in Latvia, and were elgible to vote in Gorbachev\'s referendum, the vote total sounds plausible. It is not known how many times each soldier voted.', 'INCREASING SUPPORT FOR LATVIA\'S MEMBERSHIP IN THE EU AND NATO A public opinion poll carried out in July by the European Integration Bureau in co-operation with the State Chancellery and the market and public opinion survey company Latvijas Fakti shows a gradual increase in the number of EU supporters. If a referendum on Latvia\'s joining the European Union had taken place in July 2002, 46.6% of Latvia\'s residents would have voted for accession (45.5% in June) and 35.3% against it (38.5% in June); 18.1% of the residents were undecided (16.0% in June). Thus, the number of EU supporters has increased, while the number of sceptics has gone down; on the other hand, the number of undecided residents has grown. Young people aged 18-39 tend to favour Latvia\'s joining the EU, whereas residents over the age of 64 tend to have a negative view. According to the poll, people with high Income, higher education, citizens of the Republic of Latvia, urban residents and ethnic Latvians are generally more positively Inclined toward the EU. Unlike the public opinion poll results in May and June, the results in July show that mors female respondents (48.1%) support Latvia\'s EU accession than male respondents (44.8%). Euro-optimists are in the majority in the city of Riga and the regions of Zemgale, Latgale and Vidzeme. Only in the region of Kurzeme are there more euro-sceptics than euro-optimists. An opinion poll carried out by Latvijas Fakti in July 2002 found that support for Latvia\'s membership in NATO had risen since the previous poii conducted in March: 66.1% of respondents were in favour or did not oppose Latvia\'s membership in NATO, compared with 63.9% in March (14.8% replied that they did not oppose Latvia\'s membership in NATO, but wished to receive more information on this issue, compared with 16.7% in March). The most frequently cited reason for supporting membership was better security for the country and its people (30.7%, up from 21% in March). The proportion of "undecideds" fell from 11.5% in March to 7.3% in July. Meanwhile, 26.6% of respondents believed that Latvia\'s membership in NATO is not necessary (compared with 24.5% in March). The only sociodemographic groups where more than a third of respondents did not support NATO membership were non-Latvians (43.8%), non-citizens (40%) and residents of the region of Latgale (41.1%).']

Representative_Docs: ['INDUSTRIAL OUTPUT IN LATVIA GROWS 7% Y-O-Y IN AUGUST RIGA, 10 Oct, BNS – This August the industrial output in Latvia grew 7% year-on-year, reported the Latvian National Statistics Office. In August 2001 industrial output in Latvia reached 145 million lats ($U5233.87 mln) in actual prices. The statistics office said that this August most industries were able to boast growth yearon-year. Production grew by 51.2% in the extracting industry and mining, by 7.4% in the processing industry and by 0.5% in electric power, gas and water supply. In the food industry the production increased by 7.9% this year. Likewise, a 29.8% growth was registered in production of miscellaneous machinery and equipment not falling into any other classification (agriculture and forestry machinery, machine tools, electric tools). Furniture production was up by 29.1% year-on-year, production of non-metal minerals items (ceramics, cement, etc.) grew by 24.4%, chemical industry output rose 23.7%, production of rubber and plastic items jumped 13.1%, output of timber and wooden articles was up by 7.2%, production of pulp and paper increased by 6.1%, etc. At the same time, this August production fell year-on-year in the following industries: textile output was down 7.6%, the publishing, printing and reproduction industry suffered a drop by 4.2% and production of leather items shrank 3%. In August 2001 industrial output in Latvia grew 6% over July. Month-on-month, the production fell by 8.4% in the extracting industry and mining while there was a 6.8% growth in the processing industry and a 1.7% rise in the electric power, gas and water supply sector.', 'SOVIET ECONOMIC EXPLOITATION OF THE BALTIC STATES The Baltic Republics are given particular jobs to do within the framework of the fifth Soviet Five Year Plan (1951-55). Some of these jobs pertain to all three countries, but individual projects are also listed. The organ of the Latvian Communist Party, Cina (Riga), October 28, 1952, printed a two-page illustrated article detailing the percentage increases aimed at in the new ] Plan: ". . . the tishing fleet (must be increased) 3.8 times. .. ~. ] The production of naphtha—os per cent, electrical energy—Boi per cent . . . the naphtha industry will double work productivity. The capacity of electric olants will be doubled and hydro-electric plants will triple their output. . . . "Twice as many building materials will be produced as in 1950. Brick production will be increased 2.3 times, plaster—2.6 times, glass—4 times, and cement—2.2 times. "The production of grain will increase 40 to 50 per cent, cotton—ss to 65 per cent, sugar beets—ss to 65 per cent, potatoes—4s per cent, meat and bacon—Bo to 90 per cent, milk— 45 to 50 per cent. "Farming will be mechanised as follows: Plowing and sowing 90 to 95 per cent, threshing of grain with combines—Bo to 90 per cent, reaping of sugar beets —90 to 95 per cent. ". . . work productivity must be increased 50 per cent, in industry, 55 per cent, in construe- I tion and 40 per cent, in agriculture. . . . "National income will be increased 60 per cent. . . . wages will increase. . . . (and) the income of kolkhoz peasants will increase in cash and goods produced. . . ." LITHUANIA The following quotations are from the Soviet fifth Five Year Plan as broadcast by the Moscow radio: "To insure an increase of 2 to 2.5 times in the production of electric power in the Lithuanian SSR. ... To insure the further development of machine building: shipbuilding and the manufacture of turbines and machine tools. . . ." (Note: The manufacture of turbines had already been developed at Kaunas, in a prewar factory, which apparently was enlarged and accommodated for this job. Theas have been many announcemems in the press related to the shipment of turbines made in Kaunas to construction sites of the hydro-electric plants at Stalingrad and Kuibyshev in the USSR.) "To build the Narva hydroelectric station (Estonia), the Riga steam power plant (Latvia), and to develop the construction of the Kaunas hydroelectric station (Lithuania)." (Note: The Plan is worded: "To build" the Narva and Riga stations and "to develop the construction" of the Kaunas station. Since construction of I the Kaunas station has not begun, the "development of the construction" can only mean the development of blueprints). "To carry out extensive fish breeding in order to increase fish stocks especially in inland waters. To increase the catch of fish . . . approximately 3.9 times in five years." (Note: The fishing plan was not achieved in full in 1951. The Ministry for the Fish Industry was sharply criticised during the first half of 1952 and • Minister. E. Bilevicius-Sarin, was appointed.) "To insure the further development in the Lithuanian, Latvian, and Estonian SSR\'s of animal breeding, particularly milk cows and pigs ... to raise the milk yield per cow in the county collective farms in non-Blackearth areas to 1,800 to 2,000 kilograms. . . ." (Note: The average annual pre-war yield per cow in non-Blackearth areas was 2,653 kilograms.) "To reclaim marsh lands in the . . . Lithuanian SSR. . . . To increase the area of reclaimed lands by 40 to 45 per cent." (Note: This directive no doubt applies more to the other Baltic countries as the marsh land area in Lithuania is negligible.) "To increase the network of machine-tractor stations . . . and equip them with tractors and agricultural machinery." (Note: There are many indications that the government is particularly anxious to carry out this part of the plan in order to release more manpower for such jobs as wood-cutting in northern areas of the USSR.) "To carry out the reconstruction of railway lines in the Lithuanian, Latvian, and Estonian SSR\'s." (Note: So far there are no indications that work has begun.) "To improve shipping ana increase passenger and freight transportation on the waterways of the Nemunas and Daugava Rivers. To provide for the building of bridges over the Nemunas River at Kaunas (Lithuania) and the Daugava River at Riga (Latvia)." (Note: The bridge over the Nemunas River at Kaunas was blown up in June 1941. It has not yet been rebuilt, eight years after the war.) "To insure the further development of sea transport in the Lithuanian, Latvian, and Estonian SSR\'s; to enlarge the Riga (Latvia) and Klaipeda (Lithuania) ports." (Note: The bridge over the Nemunas River at Kaunas peda. The manufacture of loading cranes has been increased at Klaipeda and according to the Lithuanian press, the Soviet ports of Odessa, Murmansk, etc., are also being supplied with such cranes.) ESTONIA Particular attention is given to Estonian slate mines. According to Radio Tallinn, October, 1952, the government has ordered a 2.3 per cent, increase in slate production as well as an 80 pet cent, increase in production of artificial gas and synthetic gasoline made from slate. At the beginning of the fourth Five Year Plan, 1946-50, the slate industry was ordered to produce 8.4 million tons of slate by the end of 1950, to build lour new slate oil distilleries and three large gas plants. Over 200,000 people were to be employed. However, at the end of 1951, slate production was little more than three million tons, not one oil distillery had been built, and only one gas plant (which supplies the city of Leningrad by pipeline) had been constructed. It is possible to estimate from these statistics that slate productoin at the termination of the fifth Year Plan should be about seven million tons—one million tons less than required by the end of the fourth Five Year Plan. No mention is made in the new Five Year Plan of the gas distilleries other than the gas pipeline from Kohtla-Jarve to Tallinn which is supposed to supply cheap household gas. ("Considerable attention nas been paid this pipe-line which, according to Radio Tallinn, was completed on February 14. It is 166 kilometers long and supplies both Leningrad and Tallinn with shale aas. Although the price has been set at "only 20 kopeks instead of one ruble per cubic meter," housewives are still not being supplied. Only "in the near future, I will 1 54,000 workers\' apartments in Tallinn" be supplied.) Estonia is included in the plans to increase electric power output by 2.5 per cent. Besides the Narva hydro-electric power station, there are innumerable programmes for highway and harbor development, expansion of the fishing and farm machine industries, increase of animal husbandry, and a phosphorous industry to be built in Mardu near Tallinn (a plan considered for over 15 years.)', 'LATEST STATISTICS In June 2003 seasonally adjusted industrial output compared with June of the previous year had increased by 8.6%. An increase in output was registered in the manufacturing sector (+9.4%), while decreases were observable in mining and quarrying (-6.4%) and the provision of electricity, gas and water (-8.2%). Compared with June of the previous year, industrial output in June 2003 increased in the manufacture of food products and beverages (+11.4%), fabricated metal products (+28.6%), machinery and equipment (+22.2%), rubber and plastic products (+13.7%), non-metallic mineral products (ceramic products, cement, concrete and other products) (+20.2%), wood and products of wood (+17%), furniture (+15.2%), basic metals (+12.5%), pulp, paper and paper products (+11.6%), and electrical machinery and equipment (+2.1%). A decrease in industrial output was observed in the processing and preserving of fish and fish products (-20%), in the manufacture of grain mill products, sta! rches and starch products (-3.7%), chemicals, chemical products and man-made fibres (-34.1 %), transport equipment (the repair of ships, boats, locomotives and rolling stock) (-6.8%), and textiles and textile products (5.8%). In the first seven months of this year, passenger turnover at Riga International Airport was 389 138 passengers, up 10.7% over the same period last year, when 351,563 passengers were handled. However, the number of flights (10,647) decreased by 2.2% in comparison to the same period in 2002. Cargo turnover remained unchanged for the period -3258 tons of cargo against 3256 in 2002. Passenger turnover for July was 68,009 or 9.5% up over July 2002.1603 flights were registered at the airport that month, up 5.4% over July 2002, while cargo showed an increase of 5.9%, reaching 375 tons. The Port of Riga handled 171,245 passengers in the first seven months of this year, two times more than for the same period last year. In the first seven months of last year the Port handled 68,980 passengers. This year the port registered 84,507 arrivals of passengers and 86,738 departures. In July the port handled a total of 45,533 passengers, 1.4 times more than in July of last year. The Port of Riga handled 12.608 million tons of cargo in the first seven months of this year, up 22.6% on the same period last year. The port reported that dry-bulk cargo remains the most handled cargo type in Riga on 5.358 million tons, up 37.5% over the same period last year. The major contributor to this category was coal with 2.863 million tons (an increase of 2.2 times), and chemical cargo (mineral fertilisers) with 1.16 million tons (up 13.2%). General cargo handled at the port was also up, reaching 4.075 million tons (an increase of 22.2% over the period), most of this being timber (2.728 million tons – a growth of 16.2%). Liquid cargo handled in Riga from January to July was 3.175 million tons, up 4.2% over the same period last year, most of this being composed of oil products (3.096 million tons – up 6.3 %). This included 1.089 million tons of diesel fuel, down by 25%. Last year the Port of Riga handled 18.109 million tons of cargo, 21.7% or 3.2 million tons more than in the previous year. The Port of Riga is the second largest port in Latvia in terms of cargo turnover after the port of Ventspils.']

Representative_Docs: ['It is envisaged that a museum about Latvian emigration and the life of diaspora Latvians will be opened in five years time. To achieve this goal, an association, „ Latvians Abroad – Museum and Research Center" (LA-MRC) has been founded. In November the organization launched a website, www.diasporamuseum. iv, which provides information about the development of the museum. The web page also includes information about a suitcase exhibition currently being developed, which will display the personal belongings of Latvians Work on a Latvian Diaspora museum has begun who emigrated in different historical periods. One object, a suitcase, illustrates the broader story of the emigration of the Latvian people in the 20th century. The Simson family used this suitcase a number of times: the first time, they traveled with the suitcase to Latvia from Ukraine, where the family had sought refuge during the First World War. The second time, the suitcase accompanied the familvs escape to Germany during the Second World War when the Russian front line advanced over Latvia. The suitcase was used by the Simson family during their time in Displaced Persons camps, and later was taken with them when they moved to their new home in the state of lowa, USA. It is envisaged that the suitcase will return to Latvia in autumn 2009 as an exhibit in LaPa\'s first traveling exhibition. The story of the Simson family is one of the many stories that have come to the attention of the association "Latvians Abroad – Museum and Research Center". Information about emigrants from various different eras is currently being compiled – about their motivations, travels, contribution to Latvian society and life in the diaspora. The association is governed by a board elected at the annual general meeting. One employee in Riga and volunteers in Latvia and abroad are working towards finding a site for the museum outside of Riga, developing traveling exhibitions, recruiting members and supporters, securing funding and developing a collection policy. Amongst supporters of the museum are the Latvian Minister of Culture, Helēna Demakova, and other Latvian government, local council and cultural workers, and representatives of international migration organizations, such as Knut Djupedal, director of the Norwegian Emigrant Museum. The web page is in three languages – Latvian, English and German – and has been funded by The Secretariat of the Special Assignment Minister for Social Integration and the Cultural Foundation of the American Latvian Association. Further information: Marianna Auliciema, Office manager Tel: +371 29175510 E-mail: lapaintb </ umail.com wuw.diasporamuseum. I v', 'Latvian Occupation Museum to receive 33,000 euros for video project The Latvian Occupation Museum has received a letter from the European Commission Educational and Culture General-Directorate promising financial support of around 33,000 euros for its project "The collection of video evidence from persons who have suffered deportation". On 18 December 2004, the European Commission Educational and Culture Organisation General Directorate advertised an annual competition offering financial support to develop projects for the preservation of significant sites and archived records associated with deportations. By 28 February two projects had been received from Latvia – one each from the Latvian Occupation Museum and from Cesis Municipal Council. The main aim of the Museum Project is cooperation with Latvian municipal councils to ascertain those persons in various Latvian districts who suffered deportation, to establish a collection of interview materials, to analyse those materials and placement of that video evidence archive on the Occupation Museum website. The project will conclude with a conference entitled "Video evidence in history, society, education, art and museums", at which Project results will be presented, together with methodology developed for utilisation of such evidence in various roles. For the duration of the Project, interested persons will be able to follow Project activities in a newly created section of the Occupation Museum website: "Video recordings bear witness..." in both Latvian and English. Video evidence collected during the project will be available as part of other Museum exhibits shown regularly elsewhere in Europe. The aim of the Project is to further develop and deepen the understanding of Europe\'s complex history by preserving word-of -mouth historical evidence as a valuable resource for future historical research, and to secure the opportunity for future generations to understand, with the help of sound and image, the experiences of deported persons. However, the aim of the Cesis Municipal Council Project "Remembering the lost stories of the Deportation" is to establish a data base to include information about repressed persons deported in 1940--41 and who still live in Latvia and Russia, as well as to explain to modern society the events of that "Terrible Year", creating discussion about that period in history and involving young persons, repressed persons, and their relatives. It is planned to include future projects on deportation and its victims as a separate category in the new EU programme "Culture 2007". 29 July – 5 August 2005', 'Occupation Museum Opens in Riga The Latvian Fifty-Year Occupation Museum, which opened July 1 in the Warfare Museum in Old Riga, is dedicated to showing the results of totalitarian occupation on a small country—in this case, the fifty-year occupation of Latvia by Soviet Russia and Nazi Germany. The museum\'s founder, Prof. Paulis Lazda of the University of Wisconsin-Eau Claire, envisioned it as a clear and dispassionate exhibition of the fate that overtook the Latvian state and its people during the last fifty three years. In light of the revisionism which Russian officials and western Russian scholars have engaged concerning Latvia, Prof. Lazda was convinced that a museum showing the unvarnished facts of recent events in Latvia, similar to those in Warsaw, Berlin and Yad Vashetn in Jerusalem, would effectively undermine the credibility of revisionist arguments, and help to ensure the future existence of the Latvian nation. This conviction has now become a powerful and moving statement of the right of the Latvian nation, and the Latvian state, to live. The opening exhibit at the Occupation Museum is called \'The First Year", which is known to Latvians as "The Terrible Year". It chronicles the conspiratorial background of the June 17, 1940 occupation of Latvia by the Soviet Union and the first year of this occupation, which began a half century of systematic repression, deportation, alienation and extermination of the people of Latvia. Exhibits show documents and testimonies about the secret German-Soviet pacts of 1939; the Soviet ultimatums to the Baltic states, first demanding military bases, then total submission, and ended with their "voluntary" membership in the Soviet Union. The Sovietization of Latvia is chronicled here, as is the suppression of Latvia\'s leaders, intelligentsia and common residents, the alienation of Latvia\'s cultural life, the torture, political murders and deportations that marked this first year of many under Soviet control—a period during which Latvia lost approximately one third of her original population. Future exhibits will look at everything that has happened since The Terrible Year. In 1941, one conspiring power\'s occupation was replaced by that of another-Germany. Hitler\'s plans for Latvia were no less brutal than Stalin\'s. They called for the Germanization of Latvia, which Hitler\'s followers were only just able to begin. Latvia\'s Jewish population was exterminated; political opponents were dealt with, to include representatives and defenders of independent Latvia. With the return of Soviet power, many fled the new Soviet terror by going West. Many Latvian warriors died in the armies on both sides. With the second Soviet occupation of Latvia, a new wave of "cleansing" and deportations began. The deported Latvian residents were replaced with immigrants from throughout the Soviet empire, which began a transformation of Latvia\'s ethnic demography to the detriment of the native population. The Latvian Fifty-Year Occupation Museum is dedicated to all of the ethnic groups that make up the Latvian nation-to commemorate, to recall, as a threat and as a warning. It is dedicated to those who died, those who survived, but even more, to those who live on and are creating a new state. □']

Representative_Docs: ["Bank of Latvia pegs lat to euro The Bank of Latvia on December 30, 2004, has fixed the peg rate of the lats and the euro at 1 EUR = 0.702804 LVL, which takes effect on January 1, 2005 in line with the government approved plan for Latvia's preparation for full-fledged membership in the Economic and Monetary Union (EMU). The exchange rate of the lats against the euro was calculated by applying toda/s market rates set by the European Central Bank (ECB) to the SDR rates formula, similarly as it has been done every day since the beginning of 1994 when the Latvian currency was pegged to the SDR, the unit of accounting of the Internationally Monetary Fund (1 XDR = 0.7997 LVL). As of January 2005 the Bank of Latvia will unilaterally limit the lats' exchange rate against the euro to ±1 % of the central rate. The hitherto existing width of the fluctuation band against the peg currency, which is easy to understand and traditional in the financial markets, would thus be maintained. The actually fixed exchange rate regime, which has been favourable to the small open economy that Latvia is, will not change. After pegging the lats to the euro, the Bank of Latvia will set the daily lats exchange rates against other currencies according to the fixed lats/euro rate and the rates of the respective currencies at the particular moment. Edited by Ēriks Zvirbulis", 'Bank of Latvia releases new 10-lat banknote Andris Straumanis – September 30, 2008 A new 10-lat banknote that looks essentially the same as those already in circulation, but with better protection against counterfeiting, is to be released Oct. 1 by the Bank of Latvia. The anti-counterfeiting elements already are used in the latest 5-lat and 20-lat notes, according to a Sept. 30 press release from the central bank. The front of the new 10-lat note includes two "10" symbols imprinted in relief above the translucent oak leaf. Also on the front, the large "10" is printed with a special ink that gives the illusion of changing colors when the bill is held at different angles. The need for the new 10-lat note was driven by demand for the currency, according to the press release. Due to normal wear and tear, the average lifespan of a 10-lat note is 1.6 years. Last year alone the central bank removed from circulation and destroyed 4.6 million 10-lat notes. The new notes were printed by Giesecke & Devrient, the same German company that has printed other Latvian banknotes. Holders of earlier 10-lat banknotes issued in 1992 or 2000 may continue to use them as they remain legal tender.', 'Latvian coin featuring beer mug \'amounts to hidden alcohol advertising\' By Andrew Osborn, – 22 Jun 2011 A new Latvian coin featuring a beer mug on one side has sparked a spat between the country\'s health ministry and central bank amid accusations it amounts to hidden alcohol advertising. One million coins with a frothy stein of beer on one side have been produced The silver coloured one Lat coin (which is worth about £1.30) was released earlier this month to mark the Baltic country\'s midsummer solstice festival where beer drinking looms large. But the former Soviet republic\'s health ministry took exception to the new coin, writing an angry letter to the Bank of Latvia alleging that releasing one million coins with a frothy stein of beer on one side amounted to illegal alcohol advertising. "The coin contradicts our efforts to cut down on the consumption of alcohol in society," the health ministry complained. It seems that the Bank of Latvia\'s press release accompanying the issue of the new coin caught the health ministry\'s eye too. It waxed lyrical about beer\'s positive qualities, noting that it was a well known cure against insomnia and had been used in the past for semi-medical purposes. "After water and tea, beer is the third most popular beverage in the world," the Bank of Latvia enthused. "The builders of the Egyptian pyramids were given three vessels of beer a day." A spokesman for the bank dismissed the health ministry\'s criticism as "not serious" however. "We might as well ban national traditions and the midsummer solstice itself (according to their logic)," he said. Strongly denying that it amounted to hidden alcohol advertising, he said the health ministry\'s criticism had only increased interest in the new coin and that Latvians were queuing up outside the bank\'s branches to get hold of one. In the throes of an anti-alcohol campaign, the health ministry also wrote to a school which tested final year pupils on the history of Scotch whisky as part of an oral exam this year, complaining that the topic was "unacceptable."']

Representative_Docs: ['To the Editor LIFE Magazine Time-Life Building • , Rockefeller Center New York, New York 10020-1393 I am aghast at the article "Soon They Will Come For Us" by Edward Barnes in the December, 1992, issue of LIFE magazine, accusing the Latvian people of everything inhuman and uncivilized . I am equally troubled by the editorial board that this seven page article appeared in the December issue when this month "men of good will" will seek peace with each other and not inflame the passions of their forefathers, be they Latvian or Jew. The Republic of Latvia is barely a year old since it has regained independence after 50 years of Nazi and Soviet occupation; yet the author, in an inflammatory manner, condemns the entire Latvian nation for the complicity of the few who coopted with the Nazi and communist governments. No Latvian was charged at the Nurenberg war crimes trial, and no international tribunal has yet convened to judge the Soviet crimes against Latvians or Jews. To reply to every paragraph would entail restating historical facts that would only underscore the author\'s lack of knowledge of who the victims were during this brutal occupation period. The world has acknowledged the illegality of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, yet the Soviets, now the Russians, find it very difficult to relinquish their once privileged position as occupiers. Unfortunately, the world press continues to convey the Russian allegations of human rights violations in Latvia, ignoring the findings of the U.N. commiseicn that such charges are unfounded. In search of ân answer to this perplexing question of how irresponsible, reporting finds its way to the pages of respectable publications, some insight can be gained from a number of prominent editors of the U.S. media. As a member of the Consular Press Organization in Los Angeles, I note that we frequently query the principals of leading publications on the subject of biased reporting. Not so surprising is the consensus regarding news stories from eastern Europe. Unquestionably all publishers economize to compete. Not all publishers have the resources to staff full time journalists around the world. People sell stories and journalists buy stories; people pay to have their story told, especially to large readership audiences. The disinformation or propaganda machine in Moscow has not stopped. Any stringer arriving in Moscow is not only assisted but facilitated to cover issues that are of interest to the government. Journalists who do not speak the local language, or aspiring freelancers, have copy material and photographs supplied to them. Less critical journalists even write human interest stories without leaving the comforts of their hotel rooms. Staged photographs are not different from quotes out of context. Journalists can get paid twice, by the sponsor and by those who sell the printed medium. The media resources will continue to play a crucial role in world politics through the molding of world public opinion. The present reality of one military super-power has not diminished the interdependency on the economic scene in the maneuvering for economic advantage. If U.S. aid to Russia is being hampered by the legal preconditions of removing Russian occupational troops from the Baltics, then the forces in play work hard to build a false case against a sovereign nation, to which the aforementioned legal criteria does not apply. Whether the above mechanisms have impacted this feature article will remain an open question, but for the more serious readers of Latvian-Jewish relations I recommend journalist-writer Frank Efrayim Gordon and his recent publication, "Latvians and Jews between Russia and Germany" in which the author follows the association of Latvians and Jews dating back to the 1500\'s when Jews started to settle in the present territory of Latvia. Through his research and analysis, Mr. Gordon concludes that, "First, Latvia never was an xanti-Jewish\' state. Second, I myself, being an Israeli, an ethnic Jew, and a Social Democrat, openly declare that I am anti-communist. It is not a shame. The paths of Latvians and Jews have often crossed, but only recently did the good relationship between both peoples deteriorate. In the dramatic year 1940, tragic misunderstandings occurred, whose consequences are still felt today. These consequences are deepest not in Russian-occupied Latvia or in the Jewish state of Israel, but in the Latvian diaspora, which in part coincides with centers of the Jewish diaspora." Representation: curtain satire_iron curtain_coffee spoons_nowa huta_make jokes_two two_secret police_bathing suits_said teacher_saint peter
Representative_Docs: ['Iron Curtain Satire EXPERTS IN NUMBERS Jokes satirising the muchvaunted Soviet "aid" to Hungary have recently become popular there. One of them is quoted below: "A master in an elementary school asked his pupils, \'Which of you can introduce politics into an arithmetical problem?\' "One of the children got to his feet and said, \'Two and two are four, teacher.\' " \'Of course that\'s right, but there are no politics in it,\' replied the teacher. "Two and two are six," volunteered another. " \'That is not right and, besides; there are no politics in it.\' said the teacher. "Finally little Maurice raised his hand. "•Well,\' said the teacher, \'let\'s see what Maurice has to say.\' " Two and two make three, and  four*TM* " \'Very good, Maurice,\' the teacher said." ONLY JOKING After his execution, Rudolph Slansky was greeted at the gates of Heaven and taken on a tour by Saint Peter. Slansky found the quiet, pious life there unbearable and asked to be transferred elsewhere. Saint Peter then led him to the gates of Hell and turned him over to Lucifer. Slansky was 1 taken to a noisy bar where there was dancing, singing, and much revelry. \'This is for me!" declared Slansky, but Lucifer\'s reply was to lead him into the kitchen where he was dropped into a kettle of boiling oil. Slansky protested and demanded to be returned to the bar. "Brother," replied Lucifer, "that was just propaganda . . .! copied from your own system." \' DRILLING COFFEE SPOONS Drilling holes in coffee spoons is th<J latest step taken to halt the petty thefts which are on the rise as consumer goods become increasingly scarce in Czechoslovakia. Thefts of spoons in the cafeteria of the Main (formerly Wilson) Railway Station in Prague, reached such proportions that the management was forced to devalue its coffee spoons by drilling one-inch diameter holes in them. The spoons can be used only to stir, not to taste, the coffee. At the famous Karlovy Vary spa, now a workers\' resort, soup spoons were similarly treated. REFRIGERATOR CARS The Prague newspaper Prace, January 20, published an article under the headline "Refrigerator on Wheels, With Every Comfort." Describing the hardships of travelling over long distances in unheated railway cars, the paper declared: "These luxurious new coaches now in use on the main inland lines have only one shortcoming: they* are heated by electricity. As there is not yet one single electrified railway line in Czachoslovakia and the coaches have no steam heating, the travellers arrive at their destination frozen stiff, cursing the railways and the designers of the new coaches, wishing them nothing worse than a trip around the world—on the northern route —in this beautiful icebox on wheels." 1 HIGHER LITERACY 1,075,000 copies of the works o: Stalin have been published in Bui jaria, according to official statistic; quoted in the Sofia newspapei Scmedelsko Zname, December 20 Hi is means one copy for every sixth Bulgarian, including babies !literates, and old people.', 'HUMOUR HAS GONE UNDERGROUND Things are pretty grim these I days for humorists behind the Iron Curtain. The Soviet government newspaper, Izvestia, complains that clowns, particularly funny clowns, have almost vanished from Russian circuses. i "Things are going badly for clowning," says the August 14 issue of Izvestia, "not only because there are few good clowns, but because the Soviet Circus Administration rarely produces good jokes for the circus artists." In Hungary a playwright has written the Budapest newspaper, Szabad Nep: "Muct has been said about the lack of humor on our stage. Since 1945 there have been no true comedies. All this is the more to be regretted as our public is simply thirsting for laughter. As it is, th epeople stay away from our plays in droves. "We have theatre conferences at which is laid down a strict formula for making people laugh. We are told to make jokes about capitalism, bourgeoism, absenteeism, Trotskyism and other political subjects. But the public refuses to be told what it shall take for humor." Unde rthe communist formula playwrights and comedians may not make jokes about the regime, its leaders, or its laws. Nor may they be funny about political commissars, secret police, fiveyear plans, political meetings, factory managers, or unrest among the people. Russia\'s great humorist, Mikhail Zoshchenko, found it impossible to write unde rthese rules. Soviet shortcomings had been the subject of his gay and gentle satire. His books outsold all other writers hut Gorki and TVmyan Bedny. He made jokes about ignorance, cruelty, green, lack of culture, the housing shortage, povety, the gap between the high sounding pretensions of propaganda and the painful Soviet reality. He knew the weakness in high places and low, the tragedy of human condition that makes men laugh when they might otherwise cry. In 1946 Moscow put an end to Zoshchenko\'s career—his writings were termed subversive. Bim and Bom, famous Russian clowns, also made the mistake of failing to follow the communist By Paul L. Ford laugh formula. Instead they made the people laugh too heartily at themselves, their troubles and at their rulers. They have been barred from public appearances. Humor has gone underground in the Soviet orbit. The communists would do well to listen to what the people themselves are saying. The people make their own jokes in private. The following story, for example, is being quietly circulated in Russia: Swedish custom officers were examining the luggage of a Russian traveller: First they found a bottle of medicine. "That\'s just a remedy against sea sickness," explained the Russian. Then they discovered a box of white pills. "That\'s for air sickness," he said. Finally they pulled out an enormous picture of Malenkov. \'That\'s a sure cure for home sickness," said the Russian. Then there is the story about the businessman who went to the Leipzig People\'s Bank in East Germany with 1,000 marks to open a new bank account. But first he asked the manager about the bank\'s solvency. "Well," the manager said, "our institution is backed by the government." This did not seem to satisfy the businessman. "Just between you and me," he said, "It might happen that the government ..." The manager interrupted him. \'Now listen." he said; "surely that would be worth a measly thousand marks to you . . . !" Iron Curtain Satiro A Budapest factory guard repeatedly challanged a worker leaving the plant with a wheelbarrow of sawdust. Each time he found nothing but sawdust. Finally the guard exclaimed: "Just between us, it is known that everyone steals — the engineers, the accountants, the director. I myself steal. You want me to believe you are only carrying sawdust?" "Between us," explained the worker, "it is not the sawdust! I\'m stealing the wheelbarrow!"', 'Iron curtain satire Some time ago, the story goes, a footloose young Greek set out on a hunting trip, tie lost his way one foggy night, and when the fog cleared he discovered he had blundered across the frontier and was In Bulgaria. Seeing an opportunity to learn Bulgarian he signed up in the Communist regime\'s army. After several bewildering months he was iriven leave to visit the capital, Sofia. Thinking this an excellent chance to practise his newly acquired tongue, he attempted to en-I gagp several civilians in conversa/ tion. But the Bulgarians could not understand him, they simply stared. No more could he understand tliem—for the language he had been taught was Russian. PERMANENT headachf: A drunk in a Prague streetcar jostled a Communist, who flashed his Party badge. "Watch what you\'re doing!" stormed the Communist. "A line thing it is to get drunk during the Five Year Plan! Look at me; I\'m never drunk!" The dsmk smiled good-naturedly, touched the Party badge, and whispered, "But I can sleep it off!" FINE POINT The following anecdote is reported circulating in Central Europe: "In a small border town, an American ran into a friend, an old Czechoslovak tailor he had known during the war. " \'You are still alive\' exclaimed the surprised American. \'How do you manage to survive?\' " \'Very simple,\' replied the old man. \'When the Americans came, I told them it was wonderful that they were here. When the Russians came, I also told them how glad I was that they had come.\' " \'In other words,\' said the American, \'you make no distinction between us and the Communists?\' . ."The old tailor smiled and said, 1 \'Oh, yes, there is a difference. You ■ see, I would never tell the Communists that 1 said the same thing • to them as I said to the AmeriI A Ql ESTION OF IDENTIFICATION Two strangers were admiring a handsome new car on a street in Prague. One said to the other, , "Isn\'t that beautiful? Another superb achievement of the unexcelled Soviet industry!" The other man stared at him and said, "But that\'s an American car. Don\'t you know one when you see one?" The other replied, "Certainly I do. But I don\'t know you!" RUNAWAY REDS The poor quality of consumer goods is an everyday target of criticism in today\'s Hungary. A I recent escapee gives the follow-\' ing example of the situation: "At Budapest swimming pools \' it frequently happent that when . people emerge from the water their skin is streaked with color \', running down from their bathing suits. This is particularly the case with bright red suits. Outraged ! customers protest to the State stores, but the fact is that onceused bathing suits cannot be returned. The sales people are able to offer only one piece of advice: don\'t go into the water." TOTAL BREAKDOWN In a letter to the Lithuanian Communist daily TIESA (Vilnius) November 27, a would-be student of motor cycling described his ex| periences in a State training school: "It was April, tļMR, when the Council of the Sports Association of Vilnius announced tha a training class for motorcyclists was being organised. "Thirty boys appeared for the classes on May <i. Comrade I\'elec* kis was the instructor. The studies went very well. We finished the course in theory in two months. Onl- the practice remained to be completed. But . . . practice was the main stumbling block, because the organizers of the school had neglected to provide even one motorcycle to use for practice. We gathered at the stadium many times, each time only to hear Comrade IVleckis s;i>, \'Sorry, there is ! still no motorcycle. We will have to "\'■ home and reconvene in two days . . .\' "Much precious time was wasted until finally the motorcycle was received. We paid !.") rubles apiece to the Auto Ins|>ection for examination and started to practice. But i disappointment came very soon. The motorcycle was so old that it fell to pieces after two lessons. "However, Comrade Peleckls found a way out of this trouble. He collected 23 rubles from each student for repair of the motorcycle, and has not been seen by us since that day ..." DEAD LANGUAGES According to a Munich correspondent the last private language school in Budapest, the Beritz School of Languages, was forced to close on October 1(5. Continued ■Mtests on the part .of the French "fjmbassy, which had unofficially taken the school under its wing, l proved useless this time although the Embassy\'s intervention had saved the school on previous occasions. Representative_Docs: ["Latvian Cabinet of Ministers accepts informative report on losses inflicted by the Soviet totalitarian occupation regime On 27 December, the Cabinet of Ministers accepted the informative report prepared by the Latvian State Commission on Establishment of the Number of Victims and Locations of Mass Graves of the Totalitarian Communist Occupation Regime of the USSR, Collection of Information about Repressive Measures and Mass Deportations and Calculation of Losses inflicted on the Latvian Nation. The Commission notes that, judging from previous Latvian and foreign experience, its work is unlikely to be completed in less than four or five years' time, i.e. circa 2010, provided that adequate financing is available (the planned amount for 2006 is 134,000 LVD. The protocol decision attached to the informative report calls for a draft regulation to be developed by the State Chancellery to enable the Commission to continue its work, and submitted to the Cabinet of Ministers by 1 February 2006. During the three-month long preparation of the report, the Commission has met on seven occasions, and Commission members have studied the experience of Estonia and Lithuania, which have previously undertaken similar work. The Commission has worked in cooperation with the State Archives of Latvia, Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Ministry of Economics, other state institutions and external experts. The informative report notes that one of the Commission's main tasks is to create a common database of persons politically repressed by the Soviet occupation regime. The report refers to the possible lack of resources (human, intellectual, information and financial) as the most problematic issue confronting the further work of the Commission.", "1st volume of conference materials about Latvia in World War II published Materials of the international conference Latvia in World War //, which was held in Riga on 14-15 June, 1999, have been made available in a separate volume in Latvian and English. The current volume comprises reports covering the following themes: Molotov-Rtobentrop Pact and its Consequences; Collaboration and Resistance Movement in Latvia; Military Formations during WW I! in Latvia and Other Occupied Countries; Crimes Against Civil Population in Latvia during WW 11. The reports – many of them revealing contradictory approaches -- vividly illustrate the interest this topic holds not only among history researchers in Latvia, but also abroad. The conference proved that the fate of Latvia during the WW II remains not only a historical problem, but has proven its topicality in Latvian policies today, affecting her internationa! relations and aspirations towards integration into the EU and NATO, as weii as exerting influence on image of Latvia in the world, and – in terms of domestic affairs – on social integration issues. The publishers have planned to continue these series under a common title Symposium of the Commission of the Historians of Latvia, The second volume is to be released after the Holocaust conference, which is going to take place in Riga on 16-17 October, 2000. The third voiume is expected to be published next year and will focus on the issue of mass deportations, the theme of the conference planned for June 2001. The conferences are prepared and organised with the active support of Latvia's History Commission, Institute of History of Latvia at the University of Latvia and Latvian State History Archives. Latvia's History Commission, Dr. ESga Zalite, Adviser io the President on History Commission issues, 3 Piis Square, Riga, LVI9OO, Latvia; Tel.: (+371) 7 092 109; Fax: (+371) 7 325 800; E-mail: eiga@presideni.iv.", "LATVIA'S HISTORY COMMISSION SUMS UP ACTIVITIES IN 2000 On 4 December, Latvia's History Commission evaluated the results of the research work carried out in 2000. Four task forces of historians were working on 26 different history themes. The first research group, comprising Latvian State Archives' specialists, is investigating crimes against humanity committed in the territory of Latvia in 1940-1941. The task force worked on preparation of the collection of documents The Deported, which would contain the most important documents – pieces of evidence of the deportations of the Latvian population carried out by the Soviet regime in 1941. In addition four research projects on the repressions against Latvian diplomats, ethnic minorities of Latvia, army officers, and civilians as performed by the Soviet authorities during the first weeks of the Second World War have been completed. The work in the Holocaust research task force has yielded most substantial results. Marģers Vestermanis, Director of the Museum and Documentation Centre Jews in Latvia, is presently working on an extensive monograph about resistance against the Holocaust in Latvia. Materials on those people in Latvia who rescued Jews during the Second World War are yet to be collected. The mass execution of Jews in Latvian towns and villages, the punishment of the members of the Arajs' commando during the period of the Soviet regime, the fate of Jewish refugees in Latvia in the period of 1933-1940, and the red terror against Jews in 1940-1953 are the themes under the scrutiny of a number of historians. Investigation of the crimes committed in Latvia during Nazi occupation in 1941-1945 is the third research theme being studied. Work on this project will also be continued in 2001. Research on the operation of the Latvian selfgovernment during the Nazi occupation period, the national resistance movement, and the elimination of Latvian intellectual potential has been accomplished. The fourth theme being studied is entitled The crimes against humanity committed in Latvia during the second Soviet occupation in 1941-1956. A research on the formation and operation of the state security institutions of the Latvian SSR has already been completed. Part of the research is dedicated to the activities of the authorities of the Latvian SSR state security institutions and their role in genocide against the Latvian nation. It is hoped to publish these findings in 2001."]

['HOT SPOTS

The main environmental “hot spots” in Latvia are centred around Latvia’s largest cities and Industries. One of the chief problems is hazardous waste produced by large Industries developed in Latvia after 1945 as a part of the Soviet industrialisation, urbanisation, and Russifi cation policy for Latvia. There are more than 350 enterprises in Latvia generating hazard ous waste. The yearly volume generated exceeds 140,000 tons. They are stored, for the most part in municipal dump sites not designed to accept hazardous wastes. These sites are often very close to populated areas. Another problem is with the safety of people living near high power radar or telecommunication facilities. Studies on 10 much military objects, undertaken in 1989, showed that people living nearby regularly feel week, uncomfortable, and under stress. The main “hot spots” in Latvia are: RĪGA the Capital of Latvia. Rapidly industrialised, urbanised since 1945 vvithout regard for environmental consequences. Half of the population of Latvia lives in Greater Riga. The major problems are: large, obsolete factories, often noisy day and night; the large volume of industrial and municipal waste (the total wastewater volume in 1990 was over 160 million of that amount going com pletely untreated); three large dump sites located near

population centres; air pollution (the common amount of hazardous pollutants was about 24,855 tons in 1990; 50,000 inhabitants are living in places where toxic pollution is hazardous to human health); traffic; sub-standard housing. JŪRMALA a suburb of Rīga, on the coast of the Gulf of Rīga. It was famous as a resort with white sand beaches, pine forests, and resort facilities during Latvia’s indepen dence years. Now the Daugava and Lielupe Rivers transport industrial, municipal and agricultural pollution into the Gulf. The Jūrmala Beach is closed to swimming (most beaches in Latvia have met the same fate). The groundwater in Jūrmala is heavily polluted as well. The Sloka cellulose and paper mill also presents a population menace. This is one time famous health resort is now hazardous to people’s health. OLAINE —7 he small town in Rīga County is “famous” for its Chemical factories (Latbio farm, Biolar— which producē medicine and toxic wastes). Heavily polluted wastewater, toxic air pollution, and storage of toxic wastes near inhabited areas are results of this Soviet experiment in building a “Chemical city”. The problems are compounded by fact that living quarters were built around the factories. Anotherfactor is an engineer workforce made up largely of Latvians while over 70% of the work force was brought in from the USSR.

VENTSPILS —the largest Latvian icefree port. It was used as an export port for the USSR. It has facilities for collecting, storing, and transporting different Chemicals, such as oil, ammonia, and potassium chloride. The danger of sea pollution is high with the threat of oil spills ever-present. Ammonia transport from Russia to Ventspils presents a threat of Chemical accidents to a substantial part of Latvia. SOUTHERN LATVIA receives pollution from the Mažeiki oil production facility and the Akmene construction materiāls facility (both in Lithuania). Studies by researchers from the University of Latvia have shown that high degrees of Cr, Va, Mn, Co, Sr, are present in Southern Latvia. If an accident were to occur the Mazeiki plant, the Venta river or prevailing air currents would bring the pollution into Latvia. (Vineta Goba)
