Representative_Docs: ['LATVIA TO JOIN PROTOCOL ON DEATH PENALTY RIGA, 29 Oct, BNS – A Latvian government committee decided on Monday to join a protocol of an international pact foreseeing the abolition of the death penalty, reported the government. Latvia will thus be able to demonstrate in practice its intention of completely dropping the death penalty, also amending the criminal law. For Latvia to join the protocol, amendments to the law must be made excluding the death penalty. The Foreign Ministry has prepared the respective amendments. In 1999 Latvia already joined the 6th protocol of the 1950 European Convention on the Protection of Human Rights and Basic Freedoms, allowing the death penalty only in cases of war crimes and crimes committed under the threat of war. Amendments made to Latvian legislation in 2000 foresee that the death penalty – by shooting – can be applied only for especially heinous murders. For a number of years no death penalties have been carried out in Latvia, as the current and former presidents both used their rights to amnesty those sentenced.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER THE ISSUE OF DEATH PANALTY The president of Latvia has encouraged the members of parliament to consider the practice and recommendations of other European countries when discussing the possibility of keeping the death penalty clause in the Latvian legislation. A discussion about revoking death penalty took place at the conference entitled "Death penalty; safety of an individual and the society" organized by UNO. According to the current legislation a criminal may be sentenced to death for premeditated murder. The same holds true for the new law, which has to pass one final reading. Sociological research has indicated that the majority of society is in favor of the death penalty. However, in most European countries the death penalty has been revoked regardless of the fact that the majority of citizens were in favor of it. The members of parliament who attended the conference, concluded that it is doubtful to count on revocation of the death penalty in Latvia in the near future. NEW REGULATIONS ON EMPLOYMENT OF ALIENS AND FOREIGN CITIZENS The regulations on alien and foreign citizen employment procedure passed by the government of Latvia state that foreign citizens and aliens can be employed only if no qualified specialists in the given field can be found in Latvia. The permits for aliens and foreign citizens to work are issued at the State\'s Employment Office. OBLIGATORY TUITION – TURNED DOWN On February 17th the council of the presidents of the Latvian universities gathered to discuss a proposition about establishing tuition costs for all students. Until now a set number of students have been granted tuition by the state. Since most of the presidents attending the meeting were against the obligatory tuition, the proposition was turned down. The Ministry of Education and Science is against the introduction of the obligatory tuition for students who have been granted tuition by the State. NEWS FROM LATVIA – April 1998 – reported by INTERESTING STATISTICS According to statistics, the economical situation in Latvia has improved. 17,2% of respondents think that the situation has improved slightly, 26% think that there have been no changes, 22,7% think that the situation has slightly worsened, 11.% think that situation has worsened drastically. 21,3% say that it is hard to answer. The largest part of respondents – 82% – agree that this is not the best time for saving money. However 12,7% say that they are able to save a bit (last year the number of respondents who were able to save some means was only 8.1%). A large part of the respondents – 64% say that they have not been able to buy anything cf value. 68% of families assert that they will not be able to buy any large purchases within the next 12 months. Only 5.2% respondents are hoping to save up money for larger purchases. 4.1 % of responding families are planning to buy a car within the next two years or to buy or build a house – 3.7%, to spend larger amount of money in order to improve the living conditions – 7,6%. The majority of the respondents are not planning to make any major purchases. According to statistics 77% of the alcohol sold in Latvia is also made in the country. From the total amount of vodka sold in Latvia 37,13 % is locally produced. Seafood accounts for more than 50% of the Latvian food export. The poll taken by The Baltic Data House last January indicates that 48% of the respondents think Latvia should join the EU, 23% think Latvia shouldn\'t join the EU, and 29% did not have an opinion regarding this matter. Last year\'s polls showed that only 32% of the respondents favored a possible joining of EU by Latvia. Compared to January the ratings of the new political party ("Tautas partija") headed by ex-prime minister Andris Šķēle have dropped by 5%. However, the ratings of a currently leading political party "Latvijas ceļš" have also dropped. Due to this dive taken by "Latvijas ceļš", the party of Šķēle is still the most popular one among the responŠarlote Līduma in Rīga dents -13,8% put it oq top of their list. The second most popular political party is TB/LNNK (13,7% respondents), followed by the Social Democrats (12,5%), the Farmers Union (7,6%), "Latvijas ceļš" (6,7%), and Democratic Party "Saimnieks" (6,3%). If the president of Latvia would be elected directly by all eligible voters, then the next president of Latvia would most likely be poet Jānis Peters. 19,6% of the respondents in a poll taken by the firm SKDS have favored Peters as the first candidate for president. He is followed closely by the present president Guntis Ulmanis (19,1%). Next comes Anatolijs Gorbunovs (17,2%) followed by composer Raimonds Pauls (12,9%), Andris Šķēle (11,5%), Valdis Birkavs (3%) and Ziedonis Čevers (2,4%). LATVIA SUPPORTS UNO ACTION IN IRAQ On February 19th the Parliament of Latvia Saeima decided to support the endeavor of the General Secretary of the UNO, the Security Council of UNO, USA and other member states of the UNO to resolve the conflict between USA and Iraq. However, if the diplomatic talks will fail to resolve the conflict, a unit from the Latvian Armed Forces will participate in the military operations. The unit made up of Latvian volunteers would join the operation as a part of the US troops CASE CLOSED The criminal investigation on the cause of sinking of the ferry "Estonia" has been closed due to lack of evidence. THE WARMEST DAY IN FEBRUARY February 19th was the warmest day of the month since the year 1881. The temperature during the day reached + 7.2 C (about 44 F). In the little coastal village of Mērsrags it got up to +7,7 C (about 45 F). THE PRESIDENT OF LATVIA VISITS ISRAEL President G. Ulmanis and about 30 Latvian businessmen visited Israel. During the official visit with the President of Israel, Mr. E. Weizman, Mr. Ulmanis apologized for Latvian participation in the Holocaust activities during WWII. The Simon Wiesentahl Center asked Mr. Ulmanis to apologize and start legal proceedings against Latvian Nazi war criminals in order to heal the scars of Holocaust and to improve Latvian-Hebrew relations. Prime minister of Israel, Mr. Benjamin Netanyahu, expressed an interest in developing joint activities with Latvia. A joint Latvian-Israel cooperation program in culture, education and science was approved. FLOODS On February 23rd the commission on emergency situations reached a decision to detonate the dam of Sala district, in order to avoid the flood in the city of Jēkabpils. That, however, helped only for a couple of hours and then the flood got worse and worse. The situation was especially critical on Saka island. There are altogether 54 houses on the Saka island and at least 75 persons are living on the location. Only 20 – 30 of them had agreed to evacuate. Till February 27th some 40 hoses were flooded. The Minister of Environment and Regional Development Anatolijs Gorbunovs predicts that the flood related loss will reach Ls 150 000. DISTRICT OF LIEPĀJA – A PART OF EUROREGION The district of Liepāja and other districts from 5 different countries bordering with the Baltic sea have signed an agreement to form a "Euroregion". Other participating regions are the Klaipēda district in Lithuania, the region of Kaliningrad in Russia, the regions of Bornholm in Denmark, three regions of Sweden and four regions of Poland. THE FIRST OFFICIAL REFUGEE In February the first person who has submitted a written request to grant the status of a refugee in Latvia was registered. Altogether 73 illegal immigrants reside in Latvia. Most of them do not want to be granted the status of refugee because by being illegal immigrants they have more chances with the help of international organizations to move to some Western European countries. LATVIAN ARTISTS IN FINLAND Latvian artists Zigmunds Vilnis and Andris Garokalns participated in an ice sculpture contest in Hemenlinn, Finland and took the second place. It is a big success because this is only the second time that Latvian artists participate in such a contests. Last year Latvian artists were given the viewers appreciation award. The first place award was given to a Japanese artists. THE SKRUNDA RADIO-LOCATION ISSUE The Ministry of Foreign Affairs considers that the talks about Russia\'s hopes to lengthen the rental agreement of the Skrunda radio-location unit is impossible. Latvia and Russia have signed the agreement and its conditions need to be carried out. The Ministry of Foreign Affairs hasn\'t received from Russia any official document with a request to renew the rental agreement of the radio-location unit. However, the commander of the Russian strategic missiles units colonel Vladimir Jakovlev has informed journalists in Russia that Moscow wants to extend the agreement about the radiolocation unit. APPRECIATION Larry Napper, the US ambassador to Latvia has expressed appreciation of Bill Clinton, the President of the US about the decision by Latvian Parliament Saeima to participate in implementing the resolution of the UNO Security Council in Iraq. NEW MILITARY COLLEGE IN THE BALTICS Jānis Stars, the chairman of the foreign affairs department at the Latvian Ministry of Defense has announced that the defense ministers of the Nordic countries and the Baltics have reached an agreement to establish The Baltic Military College in the Estonian town of Tartu. The language of instructons at the college is going to be English. Most instructors will come from the Nordic countries, but the students – from the Baltics. The curricullum is going to be very similar to the existing military college curriculums in the Nordic countries. Yet, it will also have some distinctions determined by the unique geographic and political situations in the Baltic countries. AN ALTERNATIVE REPORT SENT TO THE UNO The newspaper "Diena" has informed that during the past 4 years Latvia has not submitted a report to the UNO about the childrens1 rights situation in Latvia. Recently two such reports have been sent to the UNO – one written by the government of Latvia, the other one by the organization "Save the Children." The authors of this second report think that the government of Latvia is not interested in accurately informing the UNO about the situation of children in Latvia. According to this report the birth rate in Latvia has dropped by 63% between the years of 1991 -1996. 85% of the families live under the poverty level. 10-15 thousand children don\'t attend schools, and only 20 % of the children are in excellent health. The juvenile delinquency rates, addictions and prostitution among children is growing at an alarming rate. 15 copies of this report were sent to the UNO on March 4th. RECORDING COMPANY "EMI RECORDS" RECORDS FOKSONGS FROM LATVIA Recording company "EMI Electrola" has recently released another record in the series "New Spirits". This new,age album is entitled "Women of power and grace". In this project women from different cultural backgrounds sing folksongs about mother Earth. Among performers from such countries as Brazil, India, Spain, Korea, USA, and Estonia, leva Akurātere is representing Latvia with two folksongs. LATVIAN ATHLETES – AWARDED! The government has reached a decision to award monetary prizes to the Latvian athletes who participated in Nagano games and have made it into the top 6. The fifth place will receive Ls 10 000. The sixth – Ls 6000 (after the taxes). The coaches of the award-winning athletes will also get monetary awards ranging from ls 500 -18 800. FOR 5 YEARS-5 LAT BILLS! On March sth exactly five years ago the first five Lat bills were issued by the National Bank of Latvia. Back then 5 Lats were equivalent to 1000 Latvian rubles. NOTABLE ACHIEVEMENT The biatholonist from Latvia o|egs Maļuhins took the 4th place in the World Cup tournament. TOURISM DEVELOPEMENT One of the Regions of Latvia Vidzeme and the Southern Regions of Estonia are going to cooperate in developing cross-border tourism. The main sponsor of this project is Germany\'s Ministry of Economy. The Ministry will also provide the wages for employees, their training, consultations, and office arrangement. NEWS FROM "ALDARIS" Experts from auditors\' firm "Lloyd\'s Register Quality Assurance" have awarded the quality certificate ISO 9001 to the Latvian beverage company "Aldaris". This certificate is given to high quality products made by companies whose organizational standards are also very high. Besides "Aldaris" the ISO 9001 certificate is awarded to five other Latvian companies. LATVIA ON INTERNET Thanks to the joint efforts of "Latnet" and "The Soros foundation – Latvia" information about the Latvian literature is now available on the internet. The address of the page is http://vip.latnet.lv/lek, and it contains information about the Latvian literary classics, new releases, and information about the new writers. Besides, a data base has been created on the internet that will contain results from various research projects about the Latvian literature conducted by Latvian students. The same data base will also include writings of new authors.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER LATVIA – A FULL FLEDGED MEMBER OF WTO As of February 10th Latvia is a full fledged member of the World Trade Organization. THE MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE ACCEPTED On February 4th, the Latvian Parliament approved Mr. Pēteris Salkazanovs as the Minister of Agriculture. 66 votes were cast for and 21- against the approval. Thus the forming of the government has been completed. P. Salkazanovs is a deputy from the Union of Social Democrats which is not a part of the governmental coalition. That was basically the main reason it took so long to accept the Minister of Agriculture. LATVIAN PARLIAMENT VOTES ON THE DEATH PENALTY On February 4th, Latvian Parliament conceptually has accepted to call off the death penalty. 73 members of Saeima voted for the proposal, 16 – mostly Social Democrats and members of TBI LNNK – voted against it. After the President of Latvia Guntis Ulmanis announced a moratorium on the death penalty in 1996, no death penalty has been carried out in the country. NEW SURPRISES IN THE RATINGS OF POLITICAL PARTIES The most surprising fact about the political parties, according to statistics, is the increasing popularity of the Union of Social Democrats. In January the Peoples party (Tautas partija) is in the lead with 24.7%, in the second place is the Union of Social Democrats with 18.1%. "Latvijas ceļš" which has steadily been the second, in January is only in the third place with 17.8%, followed by TB/LNNK. ON UNEMPLOYMENT 49, 6% of all unemployed registered in Latvia are ethnic Latvians, 36,5% – Russians. The unemployment rate of other minorities living in Latvia doesn\'t exceed 5%. 4.9% of them are Byelorussians, 3% Ukrainians, 2.9% – Poles, 1.5% – Lithuanians, 0/2% – Jews. March 1999 NEWS FROM LATVIA – reported by Šarlote Līduna in Rīga INTERESTING STATISTICS ON THE SUBJECT OF CORRUPTION According to statistics, the peope of Latvia divide corruption into low, medium, and high-level corruption. Most of the respondents have agreed that low-level corruption means giving the corrupt person under 100 Lats, which is mostly given to police and local governmental officials. The medium-level corruption is considered to be up to 10,000 Lats and given to customs officials and officials of the tax and revenue service. The high-level corruption (above 10,000 Lats), according to the respondents, is connected with the privatization process of the big factories and corporations. The most corrupt workers are considered to be in the police, highway patrol, in courts, and among politicians and high officials of the state. ILLEGAL WORKERS This January in Riga, 6 employers have been fined for employing illegal workers. The estimated number of illegal workers is about 170 in Rīga, about 40 -m Ventspils. INFLATION IS ON THE RISE The inflation rate in January 1999 was already 1.2%. The inflation of the entire year of 1998 was only 2.8%, This means that the inflation is rapidly increasing. This January the price of vegetables has increased by 19.5%, fruits – by 10.9%, gasoline – by 6.7%, the groceries – by 1.2%, alcoholic beverages and tobacco – by 1.4%, clothing and shoes – by 0.6%, prices of utilities such as water and electricity increased by 0.3%, telephone – by 0.7%. STRANGE THINGS WITH \'THE SHOE" One of the most prestigious European film festivals is the Berlin Film Festival. This year the movie "Shoe" by Latvian director Laila Pakalniņa is going to be shown in Berlin under the program \'The New German Cinema". This unusual situation is due to the fact that L. Pakalniņa\'s movie was largely sponsored by Germany. Prior to the Berlin Film Festival, the "Shoe" represented Latvia at the Rotterdam International Film Festival. Regular moviegoers can view the film "Shoe" at movie theaters in Switzerland and France. 8 YEARS SINCE THE BARRICADES 8 years have passed since the tragic events of January 1991, when barricades had to be erected in downtown Rīga in order to protect our Parliament and to fight for our freedom. This year, in January, many special events took place in the city to commemorate "The days of the barricades". ONE YEAR OF "THE CHARTER" Many high-level events took place on January 16th in honor of the first anniversary of signing The Charter between the United States and the three Baltics states. THE FIRST PRESIDENTIAL CANDIDATE NOMINATED The political union "Tēvzemei un Brīvībai\'VLNNK has officially announced its candidate for the presidential elections – Mr. Jānis Priedkalns who currently is the Ambassador of Latvia at the UNO. Two other parties – "Latvijas ceļš" and "Jaunā partija" have also nominated their candidates – Anatolijs Gorbunovs and Raimonds Pauls, respectively. However, both men have turned down the nomination offer. The Social Democrat Union still can\'t agree on the results of their vote. One socialist wing says that they have voted for Atis Lejiņš – a socially active Latvian from abroad (not a member of the Social Democrats), the other wing says that they never voted for him. It makes TB7LNNK the first political force to nominate a presidential candidate. THE ANNUAL UNDP REPORT AVAILABLE ON INTERNET At the end of January, a new survey of United Nations Development Program (UNDP) was released. Latvia is in 92nd place among the 174 countries. According to UNDP, Estonia is in 72nd place and Lithuania – in 79th. The entire survey of UNDP can be accessed on the Internet page http://www.undp.riga.lv/un/hdr/ Latvian.htm. FEBRUARY 15TH – THE DAY OF KĀRLIS ULMANIS The President of Latvia Guntis Ulmanis has received a letter from the Sate of Nebraska, USA, stating that February 15th has been proclaimed the Day of Kārlis Ulmanis. Kārlis Ulmanis (former President of Latvia) is the only leader of a country who has graduated from the University of Nebraska. He received his bachelors degree in Agriculture on February 15th, 1909. GERMANY – THE MOST IMPORTANT PARTNER IN 1998 The most important partner in terms of trade with Latvia in 1998 has been Germany. The export of Latvian goods to Germany was 15.7% of all exports. Great Britain was in the second place with 13,4%, followed by Russia with 12,4%. In terms of imports, Germany again takes the first place. 16,9% of all the imports has come from Germany. Russia is second with 11.5% of the imported goods. During the last year the exports from Latvia to Russia have dropped by 59.3 million Lats which makes 32,5% and imports from Russia have decreased by 22,5 million Lats. The main export item from Latvia is wood, the main import items are electronic devices and machinery. COCAINE SMUGGLING SCANDAL IN LATVIA On January 22, the largest amount of cocaine smuggled into Latvia (1,86 kilos) was discovered at the Rīga airport among the personal belongings of the President of The Latvian Model\'s Association – Daira Silava. Ms. Silava and the models from the association were arriving from a fashion show in Venezuela. Police and airport security guards noticed that Ms. Silava\'s visa to Venezuela was issued for the entire year. Knowing that big fashion shows don\'t take place in that country, they decided to check Ms. Silava more closely, which resulted in finding cocaine worth $US 200,000 – $300,000. The experts are puzzled by the fact that Ms. Silava managed to smuggle the cocaine through two other airport transfer points to connecting flights – New York, USA and Zurich, Switzerland. ACCIDENTS CAUSED BY UNITS OF INTERIOR AFFAIRS According to the information given by Ministry of Interior Affairs, in the first nine months of 1998, 222 car accidents involving cars from the Department of Interior Affairs have been registered. 86 of those accidents were caused by the staff of Ministry of Interior Affairs. In 1998, the total number of car accidents resulting in fatalities or seious injuries has risen by 647 or 16,65% in comparison with 1997. LOW PERCENTAGE OF DETECTED CRIMES According to the chief of the Latvian State Police Mr. Aloizs Blonskis, there are about 100 organized crime groups currently active in Latvia. Therefore the percentage of detected crimes is relatively low – only 52.4%. CRITICAL SITUATION WITH THE NEWBORN Every year the number of children abandoned by their mothers right after the birth is increasing. Last year, 63 mothers in Rīga alone have given up their children, but this year in January already – 6. According to these women, the main reason for giving up the children is poverty or issues related to poverty, such as not being able to provide food for children, not having electricity and running water in the house. The number of newborn babies killed by mothers has also risen. It seems, however, that this number will rise even more, because in Latvia a mother can be sentenced to a maximum of only 5 years in prison for killing her newborn. ESTONIA STILL IN THE BALTIC\'S According to a poll taken in Estonia, most of the residents of Estonia consider Estonia to be one of the Baltic countries. This poll was conducted by "Saar Poll" after some high officials in Estonia had announced that Estonia should be viewed as a Nordic country rather than a Baltic state. INTERESTING STATISTICS According to statistics, 21.6% residents of Latvia consider themselves as "patriots of Latvia". About 44.5% think that they are "moTe patriots than not", 19.2% think that they are "more nonpatriots than patriots" and 4.8% say that they "are not patriots of Latvia". When the respondents are grouped as Latvians and non-Latvians, the situation is as follows: 81.9% Latvians agree that they "are patriots of Latvia", 4.9% don\'t knbw and 13.2% "are not patriots of Latvia". Among non-Latvians, 46.5% agree that they are "patriots of Latvia", 16.3% don\'t know and 37.2% say that they "are not patriots of Latvia. MORE PEOPLE PREFER CELLULAR PHONES The number of the cellular phone users in Latvia is still increasing. In January, 1998 2.1 % of the people living in Latvia were regular cellular phone users. However, in January, 1999 this number has risen to 6.8%. This could be explained by the fact that the prices of regular phone calls are rising almost by the month, but the quality of the service is not improving accordingly. TROUBLE WITH THE TWO LAT COINS The two Lat coin, depicting a cow on the front side, is still a legitimate means of payment, despite the fact the Bank of Latvia doesn\'t mint the particular coins anymore. The reason the Bank of Latvia made this decision is that statistics show that this coin is the easiest to counterfeit. LATVIAN TV STATION LNT NEWS ON THE INTERNET Starting January, 1999 the Latvian Independent TV station LNT (Latvijas Neatkarīgā Televīzija) has opened a web site on the Internet. It\'s address is: www.laiks-lnt.com. By using such programs as "MS Internet Explorer 4.0" or "Netscape Navigator 4.04", or more recent ones, one can watch all the LNT news programs aired during the month. Daily evening news can be viewd on Internet shortly after the broadcast. LATVIAN MUSEUM – ONE OF THE BEST The Museum of Local Lore in Limbaži (Limbažu novadpētniecības muzejs) is the only museum in the Baltic countries recognized among the 10 best museums of Europe. Besides, it is nominated for the European Museum Award which will be announced on May 15th in Lublin, Slovenia. NEW MAGAZINE ABOUT THE BALTICS The Latvian international airlines company "air Baltic" is going to issue a new magazine called "Baltic Outlook", which will be placed in all "air Baltic" airplanes. The first issue is due this coming March. MAP OF RIGA FOR WALKERS AVAILABLE In order to promote the growth of tourism in Latvia, the city government has published a brochure with maps for people who prefer to walk. It is called "For Walkers in Rīga" (Rīgas kājāmgājējiem). The brochure has been mostly financed by the EU program PHARE. There will be two routes described in the brochure. Both will start at the same location – 22 Skārņu street in Old Riga. The city council is also intending to put up some signs to indicate the designated routes. The brochure will be available in Latvian, Russian, English, and German and it can be obtained in tourist agencies, at the railroad station, the harbor, the airport, etc. LATVIAN MODEL – THE MRS. TOURISM WORLD PHOTOGENIC Latvian model Lettcija Pone has won the title of Mrs. Tourism World Photogenic in the pageant, where participants form some 50 countries competed. Prior to this pageant, Letīcija Pone has worked as a model and has won the title of "The Second Vice Mrs. Latvia \'97". THE WINNERS IN SNOW SCULPTURING The XVII International Championship and Festival of the Snow Sculpture took place from January 18th till 23rd in Switzerland. According to the votes of professional jurors, the team of four Latvians took the first place. The viewers had chosen the Latvian team as their second favorite team. EUROPEAN ZOOS EXCHANGING ANIMALS On January 9th, the tiger Artis who was born in the Rīga Zoo, departed for a new home in Hannover, Germany where he will become the head of a tiger family. His sister Astra also may leave Rīga, but before she does the workers at the Rīga Zoo have to make sure that their parents are expecting again. The Rīga Zoo participates in a program for preservations of tigers in Europe. Each tiger our zoo gives away will be replaced by another animal, which Rīga Zoo needs at that time. RUSSIAN – A STATE LANGUAGE OF LATVIA? The Russian language should gain the status of the State language in all three Baltic countries, announced the leader of the Communist Party\'s Union in Russia. Such an announcement was based on his other statement – namely that "Russian language during many centuries has been serving as a means of communication and understanding between many nations". He has also informed the press that on March Ist he will hold a meeting with the representatives of social workers of the CIS countries and the Baltics and will raise this issue again.']

Representative_Docs: ['KNOW AUSTRALIA AUSTRALIA & AUSTRALIANS How Big is Australia? 2,974,581 square miles, of which 1,149,320 square miles are in the tropics. It is the smallest continent and the largest island in the world. The United States of America is 1,028,000 square miles. Japan is 262,000 square miles (about 47,000 square miles less than New South Wales), and Great Britain and Northern Ireland are 94,000 square miles. Inside Australia you could squeeze about 32 United Kingdoms, 24 Norways, 29 New iZealands, 4 Mexicos or 4 Netherlands East Indies. What is the Length of Australia\'s Coastline? Including Tasmania, 12,210 miles — 244 miles more than the seaboard of the United States of America. No one has counted the mileage of good bathing beaches, but hundreds of firstclass surfing resorts attract enormous crowds in the summer months. What Is the Population? The census taken on June 30th, 1947. showed a total of 7.579,358. Of this figure, 3,797,370 were males and 3,781,988 were females. How Many People are there In Employment? In 1939 the total occupied civilians was 2,750,600 (2,107,000 males and 643,600 females), excluding the defence forces. In June, 1947, the corresponding figures were 3,216.900 (2,447,300 males and 769.600 females). The number of these in civil employment, excluding wage earners in rural industry and females in private domestic service, were:" In 1939—1,730,200 (1.293.100 males and 437,100 female; m April, 1948, the re-i cord figure of 2,358,500 (1,717,300 males and 641,200 females). How Many Live in Cities and Big Towns? More than 5,200,000, including 3,845,000 in the six State capital cities. Proportions of population distribution are:—Urban metropolitan 50.72 p.c, urban provincial 17.98 p.c, rural 31.05 p.c, migratory 0.25 p.c. Decentralisation plans include better health facilities, better education and better transport for country areas, in which about 2,354.000 people live. How Many Australians are Between 20 and 45 Years Old? The approximate figure is 2,847,000, which is about 366,000 more than the number under 20 years of age. What is the Australian Birthrate? The rate for the nine months ended September, 1948, was equivalent to an annual rate of 23.00 per thousand of population. The actual number of births was 132.974. The 1947 rate of 24.1 babies for each thousand of the population indicates the record number of more than 182,384 births during the year. In 1931 the rate was 18.2, to which it had fallen from 25 in 1921, 27.2 in 1901 and 34.5 in 1891. How Many Marriages are There a Year? The number during the nine months ended September, 1948, was 54,863, equivalent to an annual rate of 9.5 per thousand of population. In 1947, the total was 76,457, representing a rate of 10.1 Der thousand of the population. The wartime rate reached 12 per thousand, an abnormally high rate. The immediate prewar years showed a far better rate than the 5.% of the 1931 depression year. How Many Deaths Each Year? The Australian death rate is one of the lowest in the world. During the nine months ended September, 1948, there were 58,794 deaths, equivalent to an annual rate of 10.2 per thousand of population. In 1947, it was 9.7 per thousand of population. Until 1932, it has shown steady decrease in spite of the increasing average age of the population, Australia is a healthy country, and medical services are always improving, even though their level is al ready as high as that of any other country. Infant deaths per 1,000 live births were in 1947 only 29, a figure bettered only by New Zealand. For the nine months ended September. 1948, it was 28. Are the Aborigines Dying Out? As in all newly-settled countries, the natives suffered at first from their contact with the white race. It has been estimated that at the time of the first white settlement in 1788 there were about 300,000 aborigines in Australia. Many were badly treated in the early years, but it is estimated that there are still 50,000 full-blood aborigines and 25,000 hall-castes on the mainland. The aborigines are one of the most primitive peoples in existence. They have a highly developed culture, but possess, in their native tribal state, no knowledge of metals or agriculture. They are mostly nomads, scattered chiefly over the Northern Territory, the north of Western Australia and northern Queensland. Many roam vast reservations. from which all white men, except Government protectors, are strictly excluded. They are there encouraged to live their traditional communal life. Others, no longer tribal, live in supervised camps. Others, again, live in normal contact with the white man and are usually in employment, particularly as stockmen on cattle stations. They also show remarkable proficiency as trackers, in which capacity they are employed by the police to aid in bringing criminals to justice. A number of aborigines in central Australia, of whom Albert Namatjira \'and Edwin Pareroultja are the best known, have shown unusual ability as painters. The aborigines are protected in all parts of Australia, and it is an offence to supply them with alcoholic liquor. Many of the detribalised young men enlisted in the armed forces during the war. Plans for the education and training for lull citizenship of detribalised aborigines are in operation. Various church missions co-operate. How Many Australians to the Square Mile? The density of population is only 2.6 persons to the square mile, but about one-third of the continent is too arid to support anyone, while the rainfall in another one-third is too low to permit of close settlement. How Does This Compare? Before the Nazis began the mass murder of millions, the density in Europe was 121 persons to the square mile. In Belgium, however, there were 698.8, in the Netherlands 671.3, and in the United Kingdom 506.4. In Asia, in spite of some crowded areas, the density was only 73. Japan\'s density was high at 389.4 but on its arable land, only 16 per cent, of its area, it was 2,774 persons to the square mile —the highest in the world. The country with the greatest overall density in the world is Java, with 737 to the square mile. In Canada the density is 3.08 and in the United States of America 43. Population, States, Territories, Capitals? 1947 census figures are: New South Wales. 2,984.838 (Sydney, 1.484,004); Victoria, 2,054,701 (Melbourne, 1,226,409; Queensland, 1,106.415 (Brisbane, 402, 030); South Australia, 646,073 (Adelaide, 382,454); Western Australia. 502,480 (Perth, 272.528»; Tasmania, 257,078 (Hobart. 76,534); Australian Capital Territory, 16,905 (Canberra, 15,156); Northern Territory, 10,868 (Darwin, 2,538); Territory of Papua-New Guinea, 9,439 non-indigenous persons (including 5,954 Europeans) and about 1,500,000 Melanes ian s; Nauru Island, 225 Europeans, 1,379 Nauruans, 51 other Pacific islanders, 37 Indians, 1,163 Chinese; Norfolk Island, 938 mainly Europeans. Populations of principal provincial cities and towns in each State are: New South Wales— Newcastle, 127,138; Wollongong-Port Kembla, 62,960; Broken Hill. 27,054; Maitland, 19,151; Goulbum, 15,991. Victoria— Geelong, 44,561; Ballarat, 40,181; Bendigo, 30,779. Queensland— Rockhampton, 34,988; Townsville, 34.109; Toowoomba, 33,290; Ipswich. 26,218. South Australia-Port Pirie, 12,019. Western Australia—KalßOorlie, 22,376. Tasmania—Launceston, 40,449.', "KNOW AUSTRALIA NATURE AND THE EARTH ARE BOUNTIFUL. What about primary production? In spite of its small population, Australia is one of the leading agricultural countries of the world, and its products contribute a great deal to the food and clothing supplies of many oversea countries. For many years after the first settlements, all activities were concentrated on primary production, and, apart from the excitement of the gold period in the 1850s, there was little attempt to establish secondary industries until well into the present century. The range of Australian pastoral and agricultural industries is immense. There are few primary industries not represented in the Commonwealth in greater or lesser degrees, and in several of them Australia is among the world's greatest producers. Wool production easily surpasses that of any other country, and the vast flocks of sheep enabled Australia to do its full share of feeding and clothing the Allied services and the gallant people of Britain during the war. Australian wool is now helping to clothe the war-impoverished peoples of Europe and Asia, as Australian fdod is helping to feed Britain and many Asiatic and Pacific Island peoples during their period of postwar stringency. Australia produces 27 per cent, of the world's wool and 57 per cent, of its merino wool. It provides 41 per cent, of all wool and 70 per cent, of aU merino wool entering into world trade. Lamb, mutton and sheepskins are also important products of the sheep industry. Cattle provide the beef and dairy products and leather which are all outstanding products of Australia. Other livestock raised in vast numbers include pigs, horses and poultry. Australia is one of the principal wheat exporting countries of the world. Other coarse grain crops | are also harvested. Fruit of all varieties is grown in every State, and the production of vegetables is being accelerated. Dried, canned and preserved fruit, as well as jams and canned vegetables, are important Australian products, likewise honey and powdered and condensed milk. How about some figures? HOW MANY SHEEP? About 102,000,000, which is well below the normal average of recent years, because of several bad seasons. WHAT IS THE AVERAGE WOOL CLIP? It usually exceeds 1,000,000,000 lb. This is an average per fleece shorn of about 10 lb. WHAT IS THE ANNUAL VALUE OF ALL WOOL SALES? The total value realised for 1945-46 was f A 58.597.000. In 1946-47 it was £97,737,000. As a result of even higher prices, 1947-48 sales realised £155,547,893. HOW MANY CATTLE ARE THERE IN AUSTRALIA? About 13,800,000, including over 3,000,000 dairy cows. WHAT IS THE BUTTER PRODUCTION? It ranges from 140,000 to 212,000 tons a year. AND CHEESE? About 40,000 tons a year. HOW MANY HORSES? About 1,195,000. WHAT IS THE BEEF PRODUCTION? The 1947-48 production was 561,100 tons, and there are important plans for a substantial increase. HOW MUCH MUTTON AND LAMB? For 1947-48 production was 294,900 tons. AND HOW MUCH PIGMEAT? Pdoruction of pork for 1947-48 was 26,300 tons; production of bacon and ham — 43,900 tons. AND SUGAR CANE? Normal production is about 5,000,000 tons of sugar cane (800,000 tons of cane sugar). In the 1948-49 record season almost 6.500,000 tons of cane was cut, which yielded 944,000 tons of cane sugar. What is the total annual value of primary products? Gross value in 1946-47, the latest available figure, was £370.000,000, compared with £255,000.000 in 1938-39. The 1947-48 figure is estimated at £603,000,000, an increase of 63 per cent, on 1946-47, and the 1948-49 figure is estimated at £600,000,000. How- large are cattle and sheep stations? Some cattle leaseholds cover thousands of square miles in the Northern Territory, but the tendency to break up big estates is evident. The largest cattle station, the Alexandria, in the Northern Territory, covers an area of about 10,000 square miles. The world's largest merino stud, in New South Wales, covers 520,000 acres and grazes a maximum of 120,000 sheep. What is the wheat production? The average used to be 154,000,000 bushels a year from 13,000,000 acres. The 1946-47 season yielded only 117,262,132 bushels from 13,172,438 acres, well below average because of bad seasonal conditions. The 1947-48 harvest bettered the 1932-33 record by some 6,000,000 bushels at approximately 220,000.000 bushels. Acreage for the year totalled 13,874,000. Australia is exporting over 133.000,00 bushels to Britain, India and other countries sorely in need of her wheat and flour. Other large crops for 1947-48 totalled: Oats 40,697,000 bushels, and rice 2,676,000 bushels. Maize, hay and other fodder crops are also largely grown. What area is served by irrigation projects? At present more than 880.000 acres. This will be substantially increased by new projects, particularly in Victoria and New South Wales. Which is the largest reservoir? The Hume Dam, on the Murray River, near Albury (border town between New South Wales and Victoria). It now holds 1,250,000 acre feet of water (326,700,000.000  and is the largest In The Southern Hemisphere. Already almost two and a half times the size of Sydney Harbour, which has an area of 22 square miles, it is being enlarged to hold 2.000,000 acre feet. INDUSTRIES ARE WELL ESTABLISHED How many factories are there in Australia? The 1946-47 figure of 34,767, the latest available, increase of 7,826 since 1939 — does not indicate adequately the size and scope of the expanding manufactures of the Commonwealth. Secondary industry received tremendous impetus during 1924-18 war, and expanded steadily until, at the time of the outbreak of the recent war, it had surpassed primary industry in value, and many thousands fo factories were available for conversion to war production. In 1939 the steel output of Broken Hill Pty. Co. Ltd., made it one of the largest plants in the British Empire. It now has a capacity of over 1,000,000 tons of steel annually, including certain tvrjes which are the best and cheapest in the world. Many other undertakings were also equipped for mass production. Since prewar, expansion has been remarkable. It was found possible to produce different kinds of aircraft and aircraft engines in mass quantities (no aircarft engines were made in Australia before the war), and to make, in private and in Government factories and annexes, an astonishing variety of the needs of the Commonwealth and its allies. Heavy industry's present capacity of over 1,500.000 tons of steel annually is to be substantially increased, and a wide range of new industries involving a capital expenditure of some £98,000.000 has been or soon will be established. How many factory workers are there? The total of 826,300 (618,800 men and 206,500 women) factory employees in January, 1949, gives a better picture of the vast manufacturing expansion. Before the war there were 565,106 factory workers (412,591 men and 152,515 women). What is the value and range of the output of manufaeturlng industries? In 1938-39 it was £500,420,000 gross. For 1943-44, the peak war year, it was £891.739,000. In 1946-47 it was £1,011.026.000. Principal manufactures include iron and steel, textiles, electrical and radio equipment and appliances, drugs and chemicals, paints, optical instruments, agricultural implements and machinery, metal work and machinery of most kinds, clothing, leather, furniture, aeroplanes, motor engines, ships, food processine, confectionery, building materials. Complete automobiles, rayon and other important new manufactures are now in production.", 'KNOW AUSTRALIA Australia —a place to live in nOW MANY PEOPLE COULD LIVE IN AUSTRALIA Scientists have made different estimates, but the figure generally accepted is 20,000,000. Much of the central area of the continent is too deficient in natural water to support a large population, and it lies mostly between the summer and winter rainfall areas. Irrigation and water conservation projest in New South Wales, Victoria. South Australia, Queensland and Western Australia have done much and will do more to bring prosperity and close settlement to what would otherwise be ordinary sheep country very sparsely populated. But the possibilities in this direction are limited. There are in the Commonwealth about 600,000 square miles of "temperate" coun,try, which is about one-third of the temperate area of the United States of America. After allowing for mountainous and inaccessible land, it is considered that this area could support some 20,000,000 people in comfort. CAN EUROPEANS LIVE IN TROPICAL AUSTRALIA? Thousands have done so for many years, especially on the east coast of Queensland. Settlers in the inland tropical areas have reared large and healthy families over several generations. HOW DOES THE AUSTRALIAN CLIMATE COMPARE WITH CLIMATES OF OTHER COUNTRIES? In a country the size of Australia it is possible to experience every kind of climate. The west coast of Tasmania has been compared with the west coast of Ireland with its rain and mists. Much of the southern part of Australia has been compared with the Meditteranean basin with its warm summer and long hours of sunshine. But in the Australian Alps snow sports can be enjoyed for several winter months. Because of its geographical position, island form and the absence of striking physical features, Australia has, on the whole, latitude for latitude, a more temperate climate than most other countries. In the interior and during exceptionally dry summers the temperature occasionally reaches, or exceeds in places, 120 degrees F. in the shade, and during the dry winters the major portion of the country south of the tropics is subject to ground frosts. The hottest area is in the northern part of Western Australia; the coldest, in the south-eastern part of the continent—the region of the Australian Alps. Here, the temperature seldom reaches 100 degrees F., even in the hottest summer, while in winter readings as low as zero F. are occasionally recorded. The "elements" in Australia are ordinarily peaceful, and, while destructive cyclones have occurred in various places, principally coastal areas, such visitations are rare. Australia is not w ithin an earthquake zone. Inland, there is a large area of desert country, much of it resembling the Sahara. The arid area, including the sandy regions, stretches from the far west of New South Wales and south – west Queensland to northern Western Australia. Lakes and rivers marked on the map in this belt are no more than dry water-courses, except for a few days following the rare rains. This belt is surrounded by a semi-fertile belt with low rainfall not suitable for close settlement. The wheat plains and sheep country of the south-east river basin have been compared with the Caspian steppes of Russia, the tropical north of Australia with the monsoon area of central India, and the heavy rainfall area of the north Queensland coast with the Madras coast of India. WHAT ARE THE TEMPER ATIRES IN THE PRINCIPAL CENTRES Centre Mean Mean Summer Winter Sydney 71.0 .. 54.3 Melbourne .... 66.6 .. 50.0 Brisbane .. .. 76.6 .. 59.8 Adelaide .. .. 72.9 .. 53.1 Perth 73.1 .. 56.1 Hobart 61.3 .. 46.9 Canberra .. .. 68.3 .. 43.9 Darwin .. .. 84.1 .. 78.4 WHAT ABOI T WATER AM) TRANSPORT IN THE OUTBACK? In many low rainfall areas the water supply is augmented by artesian bores, which bring water from far below the ground. Possibilities are being examined for inland irrigation schemes. Until recently, the transport facilities in sparsely populated districts have been primitive, but defence roads and aerodromes, which have been built since the outbreak of war, are making possible greatly improved civil transport services. That old standby in arid country, the camel, has practically disappeared before the automobile and the aeroplane. WHAT ARE THE DISTANC ES BETWEEN CAPITALS? Mileage , Air Rail Brisbane-Sydney . .. 454 613 Sydney-Canberra . .. 148 190 Sydney-Melbourne .. 440 590 Canberra-Melbourne .... 292 518 Mcl bourne-Adelaide .. 404 483 Melbourne-Hobart ..383 — Adelaide-Perth .. .. 1419 1617 The distance by air from Adelaide to Darwin is 1700 miles, and from Brisbane to Port Moresby, 1423 miles. WHAT ARE AT STRALIA\'S PRINCIPAL TRANSPORT SERVICES? In addition to comprehensive and modern air and rail services, as well as, normally, good coastal shipping services, most important centres are connected by comfortable motor road services. Cities are served by modern State rail and State or municipal electric tramway systems, supplemented by municipal and private motorbus lines and, notably in Sydney, ferry services. This is also the case in the larger country towns. City transport is usually inexpensive. WHAT IS THE MILEAGE OF VI S I KALIAN RAJDLWAI Sf Railways total about 28,000 miles, and carry a yearly average of 500,000.000 passengers. AM, except about 700 miles, are owned and operated either by the Commonwealth or the States. They include 7350 miles of standard 4 ft. 8J in. gauge; 6130 miles of 5 ft. 3 in.; and 14,200 miles of 3 ft. 6 in. Standardisation of the gauges at 4 ft. 8J in. has been decided on in respect of Commonwealth. New South Wales, Victorian and South Australian lines, most of the mileage to be converted being in Victoria, which now has the sft. 3 in. guage, and in South Australia. The north-south transcontinental line is also to be completed at the standard gauge. It is hoped to include Queensland and Western Australia in the scheme later. While, overall, the railways run at a loss, this is counter-balanced by the fact that fares and freight charges are kept as low as possible, while many sparsely settled areas are served by lines which are unprofitable from a commercial, but hot from a national, point of view. Australian railways were built in the first instance at huge capital cost—£A3ss,ooo,ooo has still to be written off—to open up a continent, and in this they have succeeded notably. AND AIR ROUTES? These total about 39,000 miles within Australia. This figure is being increased by the Australian National Airlines Commission, an Australian Commonwealth corporation which operates Trans-Australia Airlines or T.A.A., and several private companies each of which operates in competition an air network between capital cities and country centres, including many of the outback settlements in the interior. Services are frequent and fares reasonable. Australian internal air services carry well over 1,000,000 passengers a year and have an unsurpassed record for safety. There are, in addition, direct and frequent air services between Australia and its Territories, the United Kingdom, the U.S.A., Canada, and Japan and of course, important points between, such as. for example, Singapore. Included are services to the United Kingdom operated by Qantas Empire Airways, an Australian Commonwealth instrumentality, in co-operation with services by British Overseas Airways Corporation; between Australia and its Territories and other Pacific island points, by Qantas; to Japan by the Royal Australian Air Force in conjunction with Qantas; to New Zealand by Tasman Empire Airways, an Australian and New Zealand instrumentality; and to the U.S.A. and Canada by British Commonwealth Pacific Airlines, of which Australia is joint owner with the United Kingdom and New Zealand. Details of overseas air services to Australia are as follows: Sydney-London: Via Darwin, Singapore, Calcutta, Karachi, Cairo and Rome in five days by Constellation* aircraft—12,059 miles. (Qantas route in parallel operation with 8.0.A.C.). Sydney-8.0.F.U. (Japan): Via Darwin and Manila in 44 hours by Lancastrian—4,765 miles— twice weekly. (R.A.A.F. route). Sydney-Hong Kong: Via Darwin and Minila in 34 hours by Skymaster—4,650 miles (Qantas charter service). Svdney-V.wieoiiver: Via Fiji. Canton island, Honolulu and San Francisco in four days by Skymaster—9,7oB miles—twice weekly from Sydney (B.C.P.A. route). Sydney-Auckland: In eight hours 1,356 miles —daily (T.E.A. route). Sydney-Rabaul: Via Port Moresby, and Lac in three days—2,4BB miles —once weekly (Qantas route). sydney-Lae: In two days—three times weekly. With the following extension services: To Madang once weekly; to Manus via Rabaul and Kavieng once fortnightly. (Qantas route). Sydney-Suva: Via- Noumea in 31 boon -2,168 miles— once fortnightly (Qantas route). Sydney-Norfolk Island: In four hours—93o miles—once fortnightly (Qantas Route». Sydney-Lord Howe Island: In three hours 45 minutes — 436 miles — once weekly (Qantas route). The only non-British overseas airline operating to Australia at present is the U.S. Pan-American Airways line between Sydney and San Francisco. HOW MANY MILES OF ROADS ARE THERE IN AUSTRALIA? There are about half a million miles, including valuable strategic highways built during the war.']

Representative_Docs: ["LATVIAN PENSION LAW CHANGES SUSPENDED At the request of 35 opposition members, Latvian parliamentary chairman Janis Straume has suspended the recently adopted amendments to the pension law, which raise the retirement age and cease payments to working retirees. The Latvian Pensioners' Federation and other groups opposed to those changes have begun gathering signatures for a referendum on the amendments. The government has said that the amendments to Latvia's pension law will stabilise the pension system, which is currently running a deficit of 37 million lats (US$62 million). That figure is predicted to reach 70 million lats by the end of the year.", 'LATVIAN PENSIONS WILL NOT BE INDEXED ON 1 APRIL According to the Director, Nikolajs Baranovskis, the proposal to begin the indexing of pensions on 1 April as put forward by the State Social Insurance Fund, writ not be implemented at this time. Mr. Baranovskis cited three reasons for the delay: the social taxes collected in the first two months of 1995 are Iks than expected (enterprises owe the social budget 56 million lats); almost 3 million lats is due from state irtstftirtions changing over to statute corporations; when the social budget was separated from the state budget, the Ministry of Finance deducted 2.7 million lats. At a press conference, Welfare Minister Andris Berzins and State Minister for Social Affairs Vladimirs Makarovs could not say when pensions would be indexed, but stressed it would commence as soon as the budget has the requisite financial resources. Mr. Makarovs noted that 90 claims are in commercial courts to recover 15 million lats in overdue social tax payments from various enterprises, and that some enterprise bank accounts are being frozen. The law on state pensions says that pensions will be indexed four times annually, if funds in the state budget allow. The last Indexing occurred on 10ctober, 1994, and the average pension is currently 30.04 lats.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER GOVERNMENT: 1. The Šķēle government\'s active role in reducing the several deficits in Latvian State accounts met ar unexpected inimical coalition – opposition parties, the labor forces and pensioners united in that call for a referendum to let the citizens rule on the legality of increasing the age for pensioning, as well as the non-payment of pensions to those working. This appears to be a misuse of a referendum. At the center of the dispute is the provision that anybody, whose pension is above 60 lats, may no longer work. However, there are only about 15,000 of such people in Latvia, and the Federation of Pensioners is not at all keen on the referendum, apparently believing that the government wants not only to save the pension system, but also to increase them regularly. The signatures have been collected since September 6 for a month at 636 stations in Latvia and 33 abroad. The first day showed scant interest. 2. It was only three years ago (in 1996) that a constitutional change was made and the necessary legislation formed in Latvia to found a Constitutional Court. This year the government is making noises of disbanding this court, pointing to its very light caseload. The government argues that the duties of the Constitutional Court could well be handled by the Supreme Court. Opponents, including the Latvian justice to the European Civil-Rights Court, Egils Levits, point out that legislation should be changed so that any Latvian inhabitant can approach the court on constitutional matters. 3. The Agricultural Ministry and the Customs Administration (part of the State Income Service) have concluded an agreement to fight alcohol, cigarette and fuel smuggling. This is the biggest area of income losses to the state. 4. In July, Latvia had a 0.9% deflation. Food products became less expensive (except eggs), and increases were found but in gasoline prices. In August prices dropped by 0.2%. 5. The President, Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga, returned to Montreal in August to settle her affairs in that country. While there, she met with the President of OCTOBER 1999 WHAT\'S HAPPENING IN LATVIA by Laimons Streips in Rīga Canada, Jan Cretien, and the foreign secretary Lloyd Axeworthy to discuss the assistance programs and investments Canada has in Latvia. Mrs. Vīķe-Freibergt was also assured of Canada\'s support for Latvia\'s quest to join the North-Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO). Jānis Straume, the Speaker of the Saeima, was appointed to represent the President in her absence. 6. The new President also started foreign working visits, first to Estonia and Finland (important as the presiding country of the EU) and later to Lithuania. 7. Acting on a request by Turkish authorities, the Latvian government has taken supplies out of the Interior Ministry stockpiles. Human-aid supplies, namely stretchers, medical supplies and blankets were taken by road to Vilnius, and from there by plane to Istambul. POLITICS 1. As should be known to everybody, Soviet Russia, upon concluding the 1920 peace treaty with Latvia, renounced all claims on Latvian land "forever". This "forever" lasted but twenty years. The far-right party "For Fatherland and Freedom" staged a picket in front of the Russian Federation embassy, calling their attention to this violation. 2. As I pointed out in last month\'s summary, there is no "blind trust" in Latvia. Well, the premier minister, Andris Šķēle has invented it. He has appointed an auditor from Price, Waterhause- Cooper to find an independent party, who would assume his 100% ownership of Aye Lat, the food/liquor/sweets producer. It is believed that a Swiss company might be chosen. 3. After four futile years of wrangling, this government has finally put an end to the privatization of Latvian Navigation (LN). 44% of its stock will be sold to an as yet unknown strategic investor, 6% will be sold to LN employees and pensioners, for certificates – 15%, but 24% will be sold at the Latvian Stock Exchange (Birža). The government is slated to get over 50 million lats from this venture. EDUCATION 1. The two large Latvian universities, namely the University of Latvia and Medical Academy will be pooled into one educational organization. This follows after the University of Latvia introduced a department of medicine, while the Medical Academy formed a number of humanitarian and scientific departments. This fact is much discussed by the Latvian media. It appears that other universities will also be combined, since there are several that teach much the same subjects. 2. The Latvian Judicial College, a joint effort of the Swedish and Latvian governments and the Soross Foundation of Latvia, conferred the first Master degrees in this field having been taught not only Latvian law, but also European Union law. All subjects are taught in English. 3. A Military College for all three Baltic states has been opened in Tartu, Estonia. The students and faculty are from several countries, and the teaching language is English. 4. Out of 1100 schools in Latvia there are about 90 that are dangerous to the pupils. The Pļaviņi high school was closed because it is located too close to the river Daugava, and the ground-water is quite high. Some school buildings, in fact, are fire hazards, some have crumbling ceilings and some have roofs that leak whenever it rains. ECONOMY 1. The Rīga Bank of Commerce, which fell foul on major purchases of Russian notes and was closed by the Bank of Latvia, is slated to be reopened in September, since the government authorized 1 million lats from its bank holdings. 2. As the Latvian Railway is being liberalized, some competition in transportation of goods within the Latvian territory is emerging in Liepāja. The Latvian Railway system is not worried by this, since they have habitually lost money delivering goods within Latvia. The same thing is happening in the largest East European harbor – Ventspils. 3. Great Britain was first in importing goods from Latvia, and Germany was the number one exporter of goods to Latvia. 4. The fact that Latvia is going to be in the EU, is going to yield the country some 22.3 million lats this year, which will be earmarked for waste-collection modernization, including installing an incinerator, as well as supplying river-front towns with water purification systems. 5. Against last summer, Latvia has lost much of excise taxes from fuel products. Since there are more cars, something is amiss. Andrejs Sončiks, the director of the State Income Service has told media that the excise taxes were lost to smuggling and that high government officials are covering for the smugglers. DEFENSE 1. The Latvian Sea Forces, together with ships of neighboring countries, celebrated their 80. year of existence. The Latvian fleet has 18 ships, most of them involved in protecting the seacoast from smugglers and intruders, as well as mine-sweeping. 2. Gaidis Zeibolts, the commander of Latvian Sea Forces, was promoted to Admiral and assigned to the Defense Ministry as the NATO Integration Plan Secretary. 3. In the Kurzeme town of Saldus a 3.5 ha (14 acre) plot has been dedicated to the German soldiers fallen during the last battles of World War 11. Right now there have been reburied 12,500 remains. The plan is to bring together some 30,000 former German soldiers. The reburying ceremony was attended by the German ambassador to Latvia, Reinhard Krauss, as well as quite a few relatives of the fallen soldiers from Germany. FOOD 1. It seems that the purchase of grown cereals (rye, wheat, oats and barley) with the new Agricultural Minister\'s Aigars Kalvītis efforts, for the first time will take place in a well-organized manner. Heretofore this has been a major complaint area of farmers. CULTURE 1. The publishing company Preses nams (The Press House) is issuing a major, richly illustrated 3volume encyclopedia of Latvian stage and movie personalities. This is part of the encyclopedic series Latvia and Latvians. 2. On August 14 in Madona, all Latvian opera tenors honored the greatest of them – the late Jānis Zābers, who was born in that part of the country. 3. Elīna Garanča, the pretty 22-year old, fastemerging mezzo-soprano won first place at the Miriam Hellina Helsinki contest, where the jury contained no less than lleana Cotrubs, Joanna Southerland and Regina Renicka. It was strange for a mezzo to beat all the sopranos. 4. The Latvian National Opera, which has seen much funding from overseas, has again fallen on dire straits. The government froze their bank account, permitting only to pay staff salaries. According to the management, this will severely cut new productions, and the Opera, in fact, has stopped selling tickets. Apparently, the Opera management will meet with Finance Ministry officials to set up a schedule to pay 1,7 million is social security taxes, and the Opera might become a non-profit corporation. SPORTS 1. As was pointed out in the last issue, the European Youth Track and Field meet was held at the Rīga Daugava stadium. It was a well-organized and well-attended occasion, both – by spectators and athletes alike. Athletes of 42 European countries took to the field. The largest number of medals – 20, of those 10 gold – was won by Germany, especially the wonder woman of sprint Schielke, who alone won three golds. 2. In a surprise move, a relatively unknown Latvian-Russian athlete Irina Latve, became the European Youth champion in the 800 m dash. Māris Putenis, who had been first in European Youth in the field of distance walking, won the 10,000 m event very handily and also became a gold medal winning European champion. Moreover, Jānis Liepa was fourth in javelin throw, missing third place and a bronze medal by a mere 19 centimeters. Māris Kadaks was fifth in 10,000 m distance walking. 3. On august 11, Skonto absolved the first game in London against Chelsea, the English premier league team duly beat Skonto 3:0 (0:0) in London. In the return match in Rīga, they played to a 0:0 draw; in terms of goal opportunities, corner kicks etc., the game was fairly equal, except that Chelsea did not play soccer, it was more of a ballet performance. 4. In the World Military Games Latvians won four gold medals. Staņislavs Olijers, a Latvian-Russian, whom Russia really wanted in their corner – won the 110 m hurdles, with an excellent 13.32 seconds. Aigars Fadējevs, despite debilitating diarrhea, won the 10,000 meter distance walking, and two other soldiers – athletes, namely Valentīna Gotovska in broad-jump, and Inga Motmiller in laekvon-do also got gold medals. 5. In the beach volleyball European Youth Games Inga Pūliņa and Ingūna Minusā became champions, by winning all games, while the up to 18 years of age group Inga Vētra and Inese Jursone got themselves a silver medal. 6. The Brit, Garry Johnson, has been hired as training manager for the Latvian soccer team. NATURE 1. Because of the unusually hot and dry weather in Latvia, there have been a number of forest fires, surely not on the scale of California, but still something to fight. Especially difficult are peat-bog fires which are very hard to put out. There was a major peat-bog fire in the Rīga nearby-town of Ķekava, which was being fought by firemen, volunteers and a large number of Police Academy cadets, and National guardsmen. By the 10th of August, the Ķekava fire was localized, yet in other parts in Latvia both forest and peat-bog fires were raging. CITIES 1. A German tourist was badly burned by \'lake amber" of phosphorus that is regularly washed out of the Baltic sea, after Soviet military fired phosphorus containing shells into the sea. The phosphorus pieces resemble amber. When the tourist picked it up, it burst into flame, and could not be shaken of the hand. She tried to dislodge it against her shin, and burned that also. She was taken to a Liepāja hospital with 11% of her body badly burnt. The city has made provisions to post the sea-shore in Latvian, Russian and English languages warning of this danger. 2. The trees of Kronvalds\' Park! Under Kronvalds park the city Of Rīga wants to build an underground parking garage, convenient to the Rīga Mayor\'s office and the Congress Building, where much goes on. Oh, yes – the Russian Embassy is also close by. The poor trees of Kronvalds\' Park! They, with the permission of the Rīga Environmental Department, were cut (nineteen of them) at 6:00 AM on a weekday morning. The cutters explained that this was done so as not to interfere with the traffic. The Latvian Greens responded with uncommon anger. They said that the trees were cut so early in the morning as a criminal event. On the fence, which the builders had erected, they put on a sign "Kronvalds\' Park Graveyard". And prophesied that other Rīga\'s parks will also be infested with parking garages. 3. Thirty two sets of twins attended the North Vidzeme twin meet. The youngest of these were 4month old Ritvars and Edgars Kauliņi. CRIME 1. The Tālbergs brother\'s gang (all 28 of them) was finally put on trial. They are accused of murder, attempted murder, torture, armed robbery in several Vidzeme towns, as well as in Rīga, Estonia and Lithuania. They have been accused of banditry and especially of pronounced hardheartedness. Among them is a father and his son, as well as two women. Since death penalty is no longer allowed in Latvia, these people are assured of long prison sentences. 2. This not a joke. There was an attempt of piracy on the river Daugava. It seems that some young people rented a boat, and after a while three men from another boat boarded theirs, beat and robbed them. But – alas! The police were up to their duties. Using a helicopter and a police cutter, they very handily caught the culprits.']

It has been published by the Multicultural History Society of Ontario in Toronto under its Studies in Ethnic and Immigration History. It carries the ISBN 0-919045-28-6 ( bound ) and ISBN 0-919045-30-8 ( pbk ) numbers. The book has three parts, Notes, Biographical Notes and Bibliography sections. This book is the story of some of those Lithuanians who came to Canada from the DP camps ( The same in many cases can be said about Estonians, Latvians, Poles and others. Ed. ). It chronicles their arrival and early years in their new country and follows their achievements and struggles to make a new life for themselves in a strange land. But the book also examines Canadian immigration policy. The author has made extensive use of Immigration and Labour Department documents to uncover the story of Canadian official attitude to these Displaced Persons. Especially illuminating is the method of selection in the refugee camps and the contract system under which the DPs were accepted into this country. Canada wanted only strong, healthy young people to take the unskilled jobs her own people were unwilling to do. It was quickly apparent that no educated people need apply – or at least their education had to be carefully hidden. The Lithuanians, anxious to get out of the purgatory of the DP camps in a Europe laid waste after the war, eagerly accepted the Canadian Labour Department conditions. The men agreed to contract themselves to work for one year in the mines, the lumber camps, in the railroad gangs and on the Pressures and Parallels Canada had to accept at leasta token number of Displaced Persons The country had been an executivemember ofthe Intergovernmental Committee on Refugees sincethecommittee\'s inceptionin !938.Then the refugees in question had been men and women fleeing Hitlers persecutionin Germany and Austria.ln 1943 the Committee on Refugeeswas reorganizedand itsmandate extended todeal withallpersons who had toleave theirhomelands because of threattolifeorproperty arisingfrom race,religionor politicalbelief.Alreadyinmid war the Allieswere anticipatingthe stupendous chaos of postwar Europe. The committee was alsothe salvefora bad conscience Secretagree mcnts made atTeheran, Yaltaand elsewhere were tohand over rights tomany of itsneighbours tothe Soviet Union. They were all gratefull to the country that was giving them a second chance to rebuild their lives. By the end of that contracted year, most of these men and women were not willing to remain in such demeaning, low – paying jobs. They moved to the cities to continue the training they had had in Europe or to endeavour to resume the professional careers they had had before the war. This was not at all what Canadians had in mind. This book movingly describes the obstacles that were put in the way of these immigrants in their quest for a better life and the tenacity with which many of these "new" Lithuanian DPs set out to find a place of dignity in Canadian society. Milda Danys is well qualified to write this history. She was born in Montreal a year after her parents and older sister came to Canada from Augsburg DP camp, in (Germany. She interviewed some two  Lithuanian immigrants in the course of her research forthis volume, and much of the story is told in their own words. Ms. Danys has maintained her contacts with the Lithuanian community, especially in Montreal, where she now lives. She is active in the Baltic human rights movement and Amnesty International, and was chief local organizer of an international conference in Baltic Studies held at the Université de Montreal in June 1984.
