Representative_Docs: ['CRIME RATE IN ESTONIA HIGHEST AMONG THE BALTICS Estonian Police Chief Uuno Ellen told BNS on 18 May that the current crime rate in Estonia per 10,000 residents is 74,2, in Latvia-50.0 and in Lithuania-46.2. The overall number of crimes cwnnmtted in the Baltics from January through April of this year as compared with the figures for the same period in 1993 declined by 14.1% in Estonia, 317% in Latvia, and 20.3% in Lithuania. Ellen said that the rate of violent crimes, fraud, blackmail, and illegal possession of weapons had increased substantially in Estonia during the first four months of this year as against the same period last year. In the town of Narva, at the Russian border, car thefts rose by 112%, whflefor Estonia as a whole the increase was 23%.', 'Crime Down, Unemployment Up In Baltic According to data of the Ministry of Internal Affairs, since March the number of crimes in Latvia has been declining (5470 in March, 4938 in April, 4607 in May and 4572 in June) but violent crimes have been rising and the number of murders has doubled during the first half of this year as compared with the number for the same period in 1992. The RFE/RL Latvian Service reported on 15 Jury that during the first half of 1993 some 28,345 crimes were committed in Latvia, 32,025 in Lithuania, and 18,960in Estonia, but the crime rate was highest in Estonia (124 crimes committed per 10,000 residents — compared with 108 in Latvia and 85 in Lithuania). Diena also reported on 13 July that about 65,900 persons have been officially registered as jobless and that the unemployment figure nationwide has risen to 4.6%. On 1 July the unemployment rate in Lithuania remained at 1.5% with 32,242 people officially registered as unemployed (an increase of 755 from 1 June), Respublika reported on 8 July.', 'THE CHICAGO LATVIAN NEWSLETTER * In December four Latvian high school students took part in an International computer science competition in Capetown, South Africa. Three of them returned home with medals; one gold and two silver. This is the first time when high school students from Latvia take such high places in international contests. * According to the "Business Eastern Europe" rent for office space in Riga is the highest in the Baltics and it is the same as in Helsinki or Stockholm. On the average it is 25 USD per square meter. * According to the official statistics of Ministry of Agriculture half of the meat in the meat processing industry in Latvia has been brought into the country illegally. The representatives of the meat processing industry classify this information as an accusation that is not based on facts. It is possible that the numbers don\'t add up due to the shortcomings in book-keeping. * According to the number of prisoners per capita Latvia takes a lead among European countries. There are 400 prisoners for every 100,000 people. In the countries of Northern Europe this number is 8 times smaller. * The former prime minister Andris Šķēle announced that he will establish his own political party. The first congress of this party may take place around Easter. So far official announcements about who will be in this party have not been made, but many popular names are being linked to it. * The Board of directors of the European Bank of Reconstruction and Development has made a recommendation to its authorized agents to hold the next meeting of European Bank of Reconstruction and Development in Riga. The Minister of Finances of Latvia says that usually the recommendations of the board of directors are the ones which are accepted. It means that some 3000 NEWS FROM LATVIA February 1998 reported by – 4000 leading financiers from around the world may come to Rīga for this meeting. * The Growth Domestic Product (GDP) in the third quarter of 1997 has grown in comparison to 1996 and has reached 7,6%. This is the highest growth of GDP after the regaining of Independence. Among the three Baltic states Latvia takes the middle position. The Minister of Finances Roberts Zīle has announced that the growth of GDP is one of the most important facts that have happened in Latvia in 1997. * The level of unemployment rate in Latvia has constantly been decreasing. On December 1,1997 the rate was 6,7%. The highest level of unemployment is in the Eastern part of the country – in Rēzekne 28,5% Krāslava 22%, in Preiļi 21,8% and Balvi 20,7%. In May the unemployment rate in the country peaked at 7,7%. * Although at the end of December the working hours for the Citizenship and Migration Service (Pilsonības un migrācijas pārvalde) were lengthened to help all Latvian citizens to change the old USSR passports to the passports of the Republic of Latvia, 20 000 citizens still haven\'t done it. Starting January 1998 the old USSR passports are no longer valid. Most of the citizens who haven\'t changed the passport have decided to do it during the last days of the year. A fee of 25 Lats is going to be charged to persons who haven\'t made the change required by the law. * The National Women\'s League of Latvia (Latvijas Sieviešu nacionālā līga) has started to develop a program of equal rights for all genders. The goal for this program is to concentrate on four different themes: legislation; education and employment; health and violence; women in social life and politics. This program will be finalized in Spring 1998. A similar program from Spain has been used as a base for the project. The program is financially supported by the PHARE program of EU. Šarlote Līduma in Rīga * The number of children born In the city of Riga in 1997 was 5505. This number is lower than in 1996 – 5715. The number of deceased in Riga in 1997 is 10 580, which also is lower than in 1996 (10,753). Also, last year 3898 marriages have been (3674 in 1996) registered in the city. 938 (912 in 1996) of these have been registered in churches. * The President of the Bank of Latvia Einārs Repše considers that 1997 has been a year of quiet but consistent development and that these traits will continue into 1998. Mr. Repše predicts many mergers of Latvian banks. He also predicts that the GDP (Growth Domestic Product) this year will be 6% and the inflation will be around 8% Last year the inflation was 7% instead of the predicted 8%. * A complete tourist guide for Latvia will be available in the stores starting April. It will be available in Latvian, English, German and Russian languages. The volume of 200 hundred pages will contain information about the possibilities of vacations in Latvia. So far it will be the only tourist guide which will look separately at Latvian regions. The book will contain descriptions of the most characteristic features of the regions. The information also will be placed on Internet. * According to statistics in 1997, 2994 persons have been naturalized. In the previous two years 1995 and 1996 together that number has been 3999. * In 1997 the number of registered enterprises has been the smallest after regaining of independence. It is considered that most of the people living in Latvia who have wanted to have their own enterprise, have it by now. Right now there are 51 private enterprises for every 1000 people living in Latvia. * According to the chairman of the Latvian Police Aldis Lieljuksis, growth of violent crimes has been observed lately. Altogether in 1997, 36,865 registered criminal acts have been committed. One of the most negative traits is the growth of violent crimes – 259 homicides and 400 crimes which resulted in the victim\'s death. Also, the crimes connected to drugs and alcohol are still on the rise. * 56,6% of people living in Latvia consider that corruption is very common in the country. 40,3% claim to have come into contact with the problem of corruption. 71,1% claim that officials are corrupt, 48,5% that policemen, 47,7% that the judges and 47,6% that the deputies are corrupt. Only 0,9% think that the corruption in Latvia is not common. * 27% of all investments in Latvia are from Denmark, 14% from Russia, 11% from the USA, 7% from UK, 5% from Germany, 5% from Sweden, 4% from Switzerland, 3% from Finland, 3% from Liberia, 2% from Austria, 19% from various other countries. The largest investor in terms of the number of investing companies and persons is Russia. The biggest investors come from USA with 177 million USD investment into Lattelekom. The second place with 40 million USD takes Norwegian company "Statoil" and Norwegian "Varner Gruppen" and "Hakon Gruppen" with the same amount of investment. Until the end of 1997 foreign investors had invested 520 million Lats in the Latvian economy (in 1993 – 50,3 million Lats). * After seven attempts to introduce changes of the legislation of the Republic of Latvia, parliament has lengthened the presidential and parliamental terms from three to four years. New changes will also affect the voters – instead of two days of voting now there will be only one day – the first Saturday of October. These changes will not affect the current parliament and president because they will become effective only after the next elections. * At the EU Summit in Luxembourg a proposal to enroll all 11 countries aspiring to enter the EU was accepted. In April bilateral dialogues are anticipated with the first 6 candidating countries, with the rest which includes Latvia, the talks about accelerating the entering into EU will be held. The remaining 5 countries can join the first 6 candidates as soon as they fulfill the necessary criteria towards joining the EU. In September 1998 new reports will be issued on the progress that the candidating countries have made. * In December a closed meeting was held by the government of Latvia to review the proposed agreement about the border with Russia. There were no objections raised against the newly revised version of this agreement. The talks about the border between Latvia and Russia have lasted for about a year and a half. The progress in these talks was reached when Latvia renounced it\'s demand to include a reference to the Peace treaty of 1920 in the agreement. The Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Russia has not yet agreed to name a concrete date for the signing of it. * According to initiative of the President of the Republic of Latvia a special government meeting was held regarding the country\'s defense. The Minister of Defense Tālavs Jundzis is planning to decrease the army by some 20 – 25% by April 1998. This action was initiated by comparatively small defense budget for the year 1998. It is smaller than in 1997; only 0,67% from Growth Domestic Product (GDP). In comparison Estonia and Lithuania have raised their defense budgets to 1,5% from their GDP. * * International credit rating agency MOODY has granted Latvia a credit rating Baa, which is equivalent to "Standard & Poor\'s" rating 888 and was granted to Latvia last year. Estonia and the Czech Republic have been granted one rating higher, but Lithuania – two lower. * The historical center of Rīga was included in UNESCO\'s World\'s inheritance centers committee\'s list where currently more than 400 different objects from all around the world, are included. So far the historical center of Rīga, the archeological complex of Grobiņa, the Livs village of Kosrags, the valley of Abava, the fortress of Daugavpils and wooden cottages in Jūrmala have been included only in the temporary list. Now the work towards joining all other objects to the list and to international acknowledgment will continue. * Beginning his visit to the United States of America President of the Republic of Latvia Guntis Ulmanis has addressed so far two audiences. First speech was held at a Institute for East – West Studies where President talked about possibilities of business and investments in Latvia The second speech was during meeting with the bank "Credit Suisse first Boston" which was interested in hearing about different projects of investments in the Baltic\'s. * Protesting against the death penalty the Dean of the Police Academy Zenons Indrikovs has refused to lead a group that was assigned to design a law of criminal penalty. According to Z. Indrikovs EU doesn\'t need this kind of barbaric leftover from the Middle Ages. Along with his refusal he has sent. 85 letters from "Amnesty International" which all express a protest towards the death penalty in Latvia. In 1996 President of the Republic of Latvia Guntis Ulmanis announced a moratorium on the execution of the death penalty. By entering European Council in 1995 Latvia agreed to undertake within a year the protocol Nr. 6 from Europe\'s Human Rights convention, which anticipates to abolish the death-penalty during the time of peace. However, in 1996 two people have been sentenced to death and executed.']

Representation: building trust_residential houses_latvian scientists_packing case_general builders_living space_sq metres_june 1951_leading article_state university
Representative_Docs: ['Letters to the Editor SHOW MANY SHORTCOMINGS IN OCCUPIED LATVIA There is much complaint over transit service in Riga, the capital and largest city in Latvia. Factory workers are severely punished for arriving late at work. Unless they walk miles to work, what can they do if street cars and buses do not operate on schedule? (Cina, January 15) . . . Street car service is disrupted by frequent carrier breakdowns which some times amount from 20 to 50 cars. According to one complainant, this is mainly due to defective pig iron brake parts produced by the "Baltijas Rūpnieks" factory. (Sovietskaja Latvija, February 27). The housing inaustry also presents a sad picture. On occasion of a Party Active meeting of the Riga Contsructors Union, it came to light that 7 or 22 Riga building enterprises had not fulfilled their construction plans. Only 84 per cent, of their allotted funds were used, and of 14,200 sq. metres of planned housing, only 55 per cent, was completed. These dry figures are supplemented and illustrated by a number of letters to the Editor. "The Forgotten House" is the caption of a letter signed by Klimov, Zhdanov, Makovski, Gusev and Malkov, all Russians. A 16-unit apartment house was constructed in Riga for railroad workers and their families on Kruzes Street. Apartments Nos. 5 and 15 flood with water from melting snow on the roof, and there is constant complaint of the faulty plumbing in three other units. Electric installations have not yet been connected the main. The house has not yet been assigned a street number, and consequently no mail is delivered. Designated authorities have been repeatedly informed, but no improvements are forthcoming. (Sovietskajaj Latvija, February 2). Housing for building industry workers is no better. A group of Building Administrati on workers of the Molotoff District of Riga complain that since last spring they have requested repair of their communal living quarters, but receive no response from the administration. (Sovietskaja Latvija, January 31). Finally, it appears that even Communist Party functionaries cannot secure good housing. The deputy chairman of the District Committee of Malta tells that even in 1950 a ten-roomed house was required for Committee use. But, because of the Malta Building Office liquidation and transfer to the office in neighboring Rēzekne, the situation has remained as before and the mittee yet awaits its quarters. The Razna Sanatarium is in dire need of repairs. Although no capital investment is required, no action is taken. Three hundred metres of electric cable allotted to Malta have been installed in Rēzekne. The Committee has sent five complaints in succession to comrade Kisis, Minister of Communal Affairs, but has received no reply. (Cina, January 15). It appears that neglect and shortcoming in public property administration is an inevitable disease in the Soviet Socialist economy. Electrification is one of the most advertised slogans in Soviet Russia since Lenin\'s time. Yet, of the actual conditions in Latvia we learn from other letters to the Editor. Two kolkhozes of Ludze district, named after Molotoff and Malenkov, have been working for two years on the construction of a small hydro-electric power station on the Ilize River. Completed in October, 1951, the station could not operate before inspection by a proper commission, which arrived from Ludze only in December. Testing the turbine and generator, the commission found that the station had only a capacity of 9 kwts., whereas it was planned for 41.5 kwts. The final approval was reserved for another commission of more competent experts from Riga, who arrived in January, but found no defects in the station\'s construction. However, it was decided that still other examinations must be made, but as yet no commission has arrived. Thus the community was still without electric light four months after completion of the hydroelectric station. (Sovietskaja Latvija, February 8). Most of the newly organised kolkhoz centres are still without electric light, and a letter to the Editor (Cina, January 16) is devoted to an explanation – of life in the kolkhoze without light during the winter. If the windscreen glass of a lantern breaks, one must buy a new lantern because no glass for replacement is available. If a kerosene lamp chimney is broken, an entire new lamp must be bought because chimneys are not sold separately. The Soviet revolution began with the object of abolishing "capitalist" exploitation of the working people. However, the Soviet sysiem has become a device of merciless oppression and exploitation of the workers and peasants by the ruling Party clique. While the Moscow regime has succeeded in creating a powerful war machine, the needs of the working population are direly neglected. The controlled Soviet press cannot conceal: this fact; it becomes evident as one reads the letters to the Editor. There is, however, one commodity in abundance, namely, propaganda literature, which is intensively distributed despite the chronical paper shortage. According to one letter to the Editor published by Sovietskaja Latvija on March 23rd, the news stands at the Ventspils railroad station are supposed to sell also stamps, envelopes and writing paper. However, the consumer must first buy some pamphlets at a minimum cost of two rubles. This literature is apparently in little demand if the salespeople must resort to compulsive methods.', 'Latvian science and Latvian scientists have made their contributions to the progress of the world. The Riga Polytechnic Institute, founded in 1862 soon excelled In the departments of architecture, mechanics,chemistry, forestry, agriculture marine, engineering and economy Many Latvian scientists and various specialists received their education there, among them R. Kalnings (b.lB73)former Minister of Finance, who placed Latvian currency on the gold standard in 1922; P. Stakle (b. 1881), chief consultant engineer for the Kegums Dam and Power station, built in the nineteenthlrties. Also graduates of the Institute were: E. Laube (b.1880), the architect who built the very modern hotel at the health resort of Kemeri: P. Kundzins (b.1880), builder of several churches and organizer of the open-air Miseum of Architecture; A. Raisters (b.1888), who erected numerous rural club houses and who was active in the reconstruction of devastated areas, after the first World War.The latter three became professors at the State University. Also many other former pupils of the Polytechnic Institute, active in their chosen professional pursuits, contributed to the rebuilding program that so conspicuously marked the entire period which followed the first World War in Latvia. Numerous Latvian scientists and specialists received their training and degrees at the University of Dorpat (Tartu) and in Russian and German Universities. After Latvia gained her independence, there was a general rush among these intellectual leaders to organize the country\'s State University and other higher educational institutions. They prepared textbooks, organized experimental stations, Laboratories, etc. In 1919 the Latvian State University replaced the former Polytechnic Institute, evacuated by the Russian government to Moscow and liquidated after the Bolshevik coup d\'etat.During its last years of existence, the "Riga Poytechnicum" had as its Director the eminent Dr. P. Walden, son od a Latvian farmer (b. 1863), the discoverer of the "Walden reaction" and of numerous processes for the synthetic production of various organic compositions. This distinguished Latvian scientist was elected member of the St.Petersburg Academy of Sciences, and is prominently mentioned in the Encyclopedia Britanica. Although certain German and Russian circles expressed strong doubts at the time of its creation, the Latvian State Unyversity gave the most satisfactory results from its very inception, owing to the presence of an adequate number and variety of Latvian scientists who formed the nucleus of the different faculties. The University opened its courses in ultaneously, a law was promulgated, providing for the auto – nomous self-government of the University. The most active Latvian scientists who have participated in the founding of the Latvian Alma Mater were: the philologist J. Endzelins (b. 1873),formerly of the University of Kharkov and author of an 840-page Scientific Latvian Grammar; the historian J. Krugers – Krodzinieks (1851-1924); the ethnographer P. Schmits (1869-1938), former Director of the Far-Eastern Institute for Oriental Languages and specialist in Chinese philology, as well as *. prominent LATVIAN SCIENTISTS Latvian folklorist; lS.Felsbergk (1866-1928), professor of the history of arts at the Dorpa4" University; The archaeologist Fr. Balodis (b.1882), professor at the Moscow Archaeological Institute discoverer of the home settlement of the Golden Horde at the estuary of the Volga, and prominent in conducting excavations in Latvia; A. Tentelis (1876-1940) professor of history at the St. Petersburg University and the first Director of the Latvian Historical Institute; the economist K.Ba--lod (1864-1931), professor of the Berlin University,organizer of the rationing system in Germany during the first World War, an author of more than fifty works; Prof. L. Kundzins (b, 1855), Director of the Dorpat Veterinary Institute, Dromineņt anatomist and discoverer of the Bos Primigenius in Latvia; Prof. A. Kirchensteins t.fc.1872), disciple of Prof. Roux and himself a renowned bacteriologist; Prof. M. Bimanis (b.1864), President of the Latvian Power Committee, builder of Moscow\'s water supply system, spec"alist in civil engineering and sanitary technology; K. Rezevskis(lB7l-1939), engineer and professor of mechanics at the Technical Institute of St. Petersburg: the mathematician J. Vitols t.lB77;,later Director of the Latvian State Electric Factory; E.Zarlns(lBTC), professor of chemistry and pharmacology at the University of Dorpat; Dr. J. Ruberts (1874-1934), prominent ophthalmolog – Ist and professor at the Kiev University; Dr. J. Dzlrne (b. 1861), surgeon and professor at the Dorpat University; Dr. p Snikers (b.1875), specialist in venereal diseases and professor at the St. Petersburg Academy of Military Medicii.e; Dr.R.Kxlmbergs (b.1874), physiologist and hormone specialist, professor at the Kharkov University; J. Varsbergs (b.1879) expert in meadow culture; P. Ereishmanls (b.1885), mineralogist and geologist, first Director of the State Agricultural Academy. The foregoing, together with numerous, were Instrumental in educating and training the younger generations of Latvian scientists. The concrete results of Latvian research work have been published in the Act*. Universltatis Latviensis, tne Annals of the Latvian State University and in numerous monographs,ttere were more than 9,290 graduates from the State University curing the years 1919-1959. Many fenous foreigen professors taught at the State University, among whom we may mention R. Vipper (b.1659), the n ted Russian historian, and J. packman(b.lBB3) the well known Swedish anthropologist. The overwhelming majority of the faculty,however was composed of Latvians. Beside the University, the other highest scientific establishment In Latvia was the Latvian Historical Institute,opened in 1936 with a purely scientific program. It wss the foundation of the Latvian Academy of Sciences, or rether of one of its divisions, During its short existence the Historical Institute did important research work the results of which were published in the Latvijas Vestures Institūta Žurnāls (a quarterly) and in special monographs Very prominent was the publication Les Sources de 1\' Histoire de Lettonie, which appeared in 2 volumes. Volume I (1936) was devoted to the first Latvian political newspaper Pēterburgas Awihses (1862-1865), the second volume – to sources of Latvian medieval history. In 1937, in Jelgava was opened the State Agricultural Academy to foster the sciences of agriculture and forestry. A more detailed report about achievement of Latvian scientists in different branches of science is published in the Latvian Encyclopaedia, Vol. XI. Riga, 1935, pp. 22397-22527.', 'Achievements of Communism — In Theory and Practice "The constant improvement of the living conditions of the workers is the foremost characteristics of Socialism. Care for the increased welfare of the people is dazzlingly demonstrated by the great extent of building of residential houses." —Leading article of RAHVA HAAL, June 12, 1951. Thus far the Bolshevik theory. We should like to illustrate these dazzling results of building activities with some examples culled from RAHVA HAAL, the organ of the Estonian Communist Party "The fulfilment of the build ing plan for housing has grea faults to contend with. Dur ing the Five Year Plan ther< have been built and rehabili tated (i.e., repaired building damaged by warfare. Ed.) resi dential buildings with a tota area, of 1,135,000 sq. metres ii towns and industrial settle ments" (i.e., about half a sq metre per inhabitant and year Ed.). (April 18, 1951, repor by Ist CP secretary I. Kabin) "Fifty-three per cent, of th< living space of Tallinn hac been damaged or destroyed bj warfare. In the postwar year: over 10,000 sq. metres of living space have besn built and rehabilitated in Tallinn" (i.e. the inhabitants of Tallinn whc lost their flats through warfare have obtained less than one sq metre of living space per inhabitant during seven years. Ed.). (April 22, 1951). "Only during a few years the living space turned over to the Tallinn workers has a deficit of more than 34,000 sq. metres (i.e., as compared to the plan. Ed.). The building of residential houses has not improved during the present year. During four months only 10 per cent. of the living space provided for by the plan have been turned over to the Housinge Fund." (June 3, 1951). "In the postwar years altogether .64 residential houses have been built and rehabilitated in Tartu." (During the war more than 2000 residential houses were desroyed in Tartu. Ed.). (June 10, 1951). "During five years Kohtla-Jarve has got almost 100,000 sq. metres of new living space." (i.e., every new town-dweller has received 4 sq. metres of living space. Ed.), (March 28, 1951). "During the Five Year Plan 2800 residential houses have been built and rehabilitated in the countryside." (After World War I, during Estonia\'s independence, about 40,000 residential houses were built in the countryside during five years. Ed.). (April 18, 1951). "Of the republican plan for 1950 in the feld of house building only 50.9 per cent, are fulfilled." (March 29, 1951, the budget speech of the chairman of the council of ministers, A. Muurisepp). "Most serious is the. backwardness of building of residential houses." (Feb. 9, 1951). "The housing shortage in our towns is also most severe." (Jan. 1, 1951, the new year speech of the chairman of the council of ministers, A. Veimer). "Everybody knows that the quality of work at many of our buildings is far from satisfactory." (Jan. 9, 1951, leading article). "The bad quality of the building work is causing frequent alterations." (June 12, 1951, leading article). "The Tartu Building Trust is one of the few building organisations in the republic which have fulfilled the building plan But "the factory of building details is still often producing crooked steps, ceiling plates are inexact . . . The quality of building works receives little attention. E.g., the house on 4. June 21st Street, was turned over into exploitation two years ago, but the roof is already leaking, windows and doors have centimeter-wide cracks, the ranges have cracks through sinking . . . On the building of the schoolhouse at Paiestee ■ Street, crookedly laid walls : foundations with holes, etc may be observed." (June £ i 1951). As regards the Tallinn Build ing Trust No. 1, "on mos building sites one may observ many rejects, alterations ar frequent . . . E.g., the briga idier of the housepainters, Mer iste when plastering house No 1, at 106, Parnu Street, discov ered that the plaster on th< walls was not even, full o cracks and partly lacking. Ir stead of ordering the respon sible workers to repair th( faults discovered, the mange ment of the trust decided tha the discoverer of the faults should remedy them." (Jan. 9 1951). "The communal house for young building workers of the building trust of the Tallinr . executive commitee (built duri ing the second Soviet occupation. Ed.) is in extremely bad repair. The roofs are leaking." (May 31, v 1951). "The communal boarding houses of the preparatory school i for the leading cadres of kolkhozes (at Kehtna. Ed.) were I turned over into exploitation last year, but this year capital repairs are necessary already. ... In houses which got ready a few months ago capital repairs are under way. The roofs were not rainproof, as a consequence the ceilings were spoilt and the chimneys got cracked, becoming a danger. By a common effort of the pupils canals for the fresh water pipes were dug, but the water was available for a few days only in the communal boarding houses, because the pipes broke." (August 24, 1950). "Many building organisations, especially the Tallinn and Narva building trusts have systematically not fulfilled and are not fulfilling their plan for capital buildings . . . The Tallinn building trust is unable systematically to supply the building sites with materials and the builders with necessary tools." (June 12, 1951, leading article). As regards the Tallinn building trust "there often are long pauses in the work, work is lagging, the workers are earning little . . . Often such works are quickly finished which quickly fulfil the plan. Other jobs frequently remain undone." (June 12, 1951). "Some economic managers have permitted people to move into houses not yet completely ready. It often happens that in the houses where people have moved in work remains unfinished for years, thus causing various inconveniences for the inhabitants as well as dilapidation of the houses." (Oct. 2, (1950, leading article). "The building and assembling trust of the ministry for sovkhozes was to have built a silo barn for the Kostivere sovkhoz by June 15, but on June 10 there was not even a project in existence for this building. Furthermore, on May 1 the trust had to deliver to the sovkhoz one cattle byre and one residential house and on June 1G two residential houses, but the trust could not manage this. The reason is that work is stopping constantly because of lack of building materials. The necessary equipment is also lacking." (June 10, 1950). "At the Party meeting of Kohtla-Jarve there were complaints because of planless building as the town lacks a general plan to date." (Kohtla-Jarve is a place where there has been more building than anywhere else. Ed.) (Jan. 16, 1951). "Already in November of last year the building of a new two-storied house was started in Parnu-Jaagupi. The house was to be ready in February, but is unfinished to date as all stoves were sinking crookedly and had to be rebuilt." (June 22, 1951). "The building work is rendered difficult by the bad work of the Supply Office of Agriculture as regards the supply with necessary materials. The projects and calculations are not organised any better. They arrive late and often contain grave faults."  23, 1951) "The "Sade" kolkhoz in Marjamaa raion has not been able to start the building of a cattle barn because of lack of cement and bricks. The kolkhoz "Punane Koit" has not been able to start the building of a pigsty for lack of cement." (June 23, 1951). "The foundation of the byre at the kolkhoz "Punane Taht" in Tartu raion is unfinished because of lack of lime. The work has stopped. The ground is dug for a pigsty in the kolkhoz "Uhme Soprus," but the work cannot be continued because lime is lacking. In the kolkhoz called for Zhdanov the building of a cattle barn has been discontinued for lack of lime and cement." (Estonia is rich in both. Ed.), (June 26, 1951). (To be continued)']

Name: 112_co operation_latvia russia_border co_cross border
Representation: co operation_latvia russia_border co_cross border_foreign minister_republic latvia_russia intergovernmental_economic co_intergovernmental commission_latvian russian
Representative_Docs: ["Foreign Minister and Rezekne City Council representatives discuss prospects for regional co-operation with neighbouring countries On 17 July, Minister for Foreign Affairs, Maris Riekstiņš, was on a working visit to the City of Rēzekne so as to meet with local government representatives and to discuss prospects for regional co-operation with the neighbouring countries, and in particular in the border regions and towns. The Chairman of the Rēzekne City Council, Juris Guntis Vjakse, informed the Foreign Minister about the city's cultural and economic co-operation with foreign countries and said that local entrepreneurs were interested in developing co-operation with the closest neighbours such as Russia and expanding their activities in other regions as well. In this context, the Minister briefed the city leaders on current developments in Latvia-Russia relations and on the work of the Inter-govemmental Commission, inviting the officials to inform both the Foreign Ministry and the Intergovernmental Commission on those important co-operation issues that require resolution, so that these could be included in the bilateral dialogue with the Russian Federation. The Minister also mentioned the forthcoming visit to Central Asia by the President of Latvia and the opening of a Latvian embassy in Egypt, inviting entrepreneurs to consider their possible interests in these regions and to maintain an active dialogue with the economics diplomats of the Latvian embassies in other countries of interest. The Chairman of the Rēzekne City Council reported the problematic situation in the city regarding energy supply in relation to the operation of the Lithuanian investor E enerģija and their intention to take action against Latvia in an international court. The Foreign Minister promised to acquaint himself with the situation and proposed the initiation of a discussion on this issue at Cabinet level. As part of his working visit, the Foreign Minister opened the press conference, organised by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and the Rēzekne City Council, for the regional journalists of the Latgale region on issues such as what needs to be taken into account when travelling abroad and on the responsibilities of Latvian consular officials and their possibilities in assisting Latvian residents while abroad. During the visit, Minister Riekstiņš visited The State Border Guard College, at which the Director of the College, General Ivars Zalitis, offered an informative insight into the border guard educational system. General Zalitis informed the Minister about the co-operation partners of the College and participation in international missions. The Director mentioned the development co-operation project with Moldova, as an example of successful co-operation, in which Moldovan border guards were trained in aspects of dog handling. While in Rēzekne, the Foreign Minister visited the air conditioning and ventilation systems factory Nedbaltic to acquaint himself with the company's experience in export abroad.", "CROSS-BORDER CO-OPERATION DISCUSSED AT LATVIA-RUSSIA MEETING On 8 April a delegation of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, led by the Under-Secretary of State Mr Martins Virsis visited Pskov where they met Governor of Pskov region Mr Evgeny Mikhailov, representative of tie Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Russia in Pskov Mr Sergey Yefimenko, Mayor of Pskov Mr Mikhail Khorenen and President of the Chamber of Trade and industry of the region Mr Vladimir Zubov. In fie course of the meeting tie parties discussed tie following aspects of Latvia-Russia co-operation; cross-border co-operation, issues pertaining to the improvement of border crossing procedures and border infrastructure and the opportunities to stimulate business contacts between the two countries. The officials noted ttat the frontier regions of Latvia and Pskov region had common intereste in raising the level of welfare in the regions and the field of economy in general, which provided a good foundation for constructive co-operation. On 9 and 10 April the delegation visited St. Petersburg where they met Plenipotentiary Representative of tie Russian Federation President in the North-Western region Mr Victor Cherkesov, representative of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs Mr Victor Lopatnikov, Governor of St Petersburg Mr Vladimir Yakovtev, vice-governor of the Leningrad obtast Mr Vladimir Kfritov and Deputy Chairman of Russia's North-western Customs Authority Mr Pavel Korchazhkin. During tie meetings the parties discussed issues pertaining to the development of the legal basis for Latvia-Russia relations, stimulation of business contacts, improvement of border-crossing procedures and border infrastructure, cross-border co-operation, co-operation in the Baltic Sea region, the Northern Dimension and other topics, The parties were in agreement that Latvia's accession to the EU would stimulate bilateral economic co-operation and serve the intereste of the people of both countries. The Russian side acknowledged the good co-operation and active relations that had developed between that particular region of Russia and Latvia. On 11 and 12 April 2002 delegation visited Moscow where they met Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs of Russia Mr Yevgeny Gusarov, Director of the 2nd Department of European Affairs Mr Alexander Udaftsov, Member of the Russian State Duma (Russian Regions) Mr Gleg Morozov, Vice-president of the Union of Industrialists and Entrepreneurs of Russia Mr Igor Jurgens and Chairman of the Foreign and Defence Policy Council Mr Sergey Karaganov. The Russian side pledged to stimulate the signing of bilateral agreements that are important for both sides. Officials of tie Ministry of Foreign Affaire of Russia expressed readiness to stimulate cross-border co-operation and business contacts. The sides agreed to continue co-operation between the Ministries of Foreign Affairs of both states. The discussions took place in the atmosphere of mutual interest", "Foreign Ministers of Latvia and Russia note historic significance of visit On 18 December, as part of his working visit to Latvia, Sergey Lavrov, Foreign Minister of the Russian Federation, met with Maris Riekstiņš, Foreign Minister of Latvia. During the meeting, the Ministers discussed topical issues of bilateral co-operation as well as co-operation between the EU and Russia, and the most important international issues: the situations in Kosovo and Iran. During the meeting, the Ministers exchanged the ratification documents for the State Border Treaty between the Republic of Latvia and the Russian Federation, and signed an Agreement between the Governments of Latvia and the Russian Federation on the status of Latvia's burial sites in the territory of Russia and Russia's burial sites in the territory of Latvia, and an Agreement between Latvia and Russia on cooperation on social security issues. The Foreign Ministers noted the historical significance of this visit, and agreed that the first bilateral visit to Latvia by Russia's Foreign Minister, as well as the coming into force of the Treaty on the Latvia-Russia State Border Treaty, should be regarded as a positive step within the context of bilateral relations. The Ministers agreed that work on the enhancement of the border infrastructure should be continued. It is also necessary to establish a Latvia-Russia state border demarcation commission in order to begin work on determination of the border. Minister Riekstiņš emphasized that it is important to sign an agreement between Latvia and Russia on border crossing points. The Ministers also acknowledged that the practical advantages of the Latvia-Russia Intergovernmental Commission (IGC) instrument should be actively utilised to resolve issues of practical co-operation in the future. They agreed that the IGC Task Force on Humanitarian Co-operation would convene in February 2008 in Riga, followed by a meeting in Riga of the Task Force on Economic Co-operation in April 2008. The third IGC working group, the Task Force on Co-operation in the Transportation Sector, will meet in Riga on 20 December 2007. Minister Riekstiņš emphasized that solutions should be sought for the normalisation of the situation on the EU-Russia border, and for the increase of the throughput capacity of border crossings. The Ministers concurred that the experts should reach an agreement in the near future on the opening of the Opuli-Mogili border control point. The Ministers confirmed the need for expert consultations to continue in order to achieve definite results in the issue of displaced items of cultural and historical value. The Ministers also agreed on the priority to finalise such agreements as On the Avoidance of Double Taxation and the Prevention of Fiscal Evasion with Respect to Taxes on Income and Capital and On the Protection and Promotion of Foreign Investment. Likewise, it is important to finalise negotiations on the draft agreement On the Procedures of the Issue of Visas and The Crossing of State Borders by Frontier Zone Inhabitants in the near future. Work should continue towards the signing of the agreement On Co-Operation in the Prevention of Emergency Situation Threats and the Resolution of their Consequences and the agreement On Co-Operation Regarding Environmental Protection. Minister Lavrov informed his Latvian counterpart of the invitation by Russia for Latvian State President Valdis Zatlers to visit Russia. Latvia noted that it has forwarded an invitation to the leader of Russia's government to take part in the summit of the Council of the Baltic Sea States (CBSS), to take place in Riga in June 2008. The Ministers agreed that co-operation between historians in the research of various historical issues should be encouraged. Mr Riekstiņš accentuated the need to provide the same level of access to archives as is granted to Russian researchers in Latvia. Responding to the Russian Foreign Minister, Minister Riekstiņš provided a briefing on the integration processes Edited by Pēteris Kļaviņš within Latvia's community. Mr Riekstiņš went on to familiarise Mr Lavrov with the priorities of the Latvian Presidency in the CBSS and on progress in their implementation. The Ministers also discussed potential areas of cooperation between the EU and Russia, including talks on the new Partnership and Co-operation agreement. Latvia expressed support for Russia's accession process to the World Trade Organisation in the near future: highlighting the'need to resolveseveral existing issues, including the imposition of equal tariffs for exports and imports by rail freight within the territory of Russia. With regard to the Treaty on Conventional Armed Forces in Europe (CFE), Minister Riekstiņš noted that Russia's unilateral decision to suspend its participation in the Treaty would not promote mutual trust. However, he commended Russia's intention, as confirmed by Foreign Minister Lavrov, not to increase the concentration of Russian armed forces near Russia's borders. Mr Lavrov noted that Russia is prepared to continue negotiations with the NATO alliance on this issue. During the press conference Minister Riekstiņš expressed Latvia's view that the U.S. negotiations with Poland and the Czech Republic regarding the deployment of an anti-missile defence shield should not have a negative impact on the balance of power in Europe. Mr Riekstiņš also indicated that it is important to ensure the transparency of this negotiation process. As part of his visit, the Russian Foreign Minister also met with President Valdis Zatlers, and Prime Minister Aigars Kalvitis."]

Name: 114_aleksandras lileikis_war criminal_criminal aleksandras_war criminals
Representation: aleksandras lileikis_war criminal_criminal aleksandras_war criminals_simon wiesenthal_wiesenthal center_security police_head vilnius_vilnius security_year old
Representative_Docs: ['LITHUANIAN DOCTORS SAY ALLEGED WAR CRIMINAL FIT TO STAND TRIAL A panel of Lithuanian doctors has found that alleged Nazi war criminal Aleksandras Lileikis is fit to stand trial. "The current state of Lileikis\'s health does allow him to take part and testify in the trial, but any stress could lead to a health condition hazardous to his We," the panel concluded in its report. The 91-year-old Lileikis is accused of handing over Jews to Nazi death squads while head of the Vilnius security police during the German occupation of Lithuania. His trial had to be postponed in September after his legal team said he was too ill to appear in court. Under Lithuanian law, trial in absentia for medical reasons is prohibited.', 'LITHUANIAN PRESIDENT VOWS TO PROSECUTE WAR CRIMINALS Valdas Adamkus has reaffirmed Lithuania\'s commitment to prosecuting war criminals. Commemorating the 55th anniversary of the Vilnius ghetto massacre on 23 September, Adamkus repeated that Lithuania "firmly supports the further prosecution of those who participated in Nazi war crimes". With regard to the suspension of the trial of accused war criminal Aleksandras Lileikis, Adamkus insisted that it "should not be interpreted in any way as a weakening of the Lithuanian governmenf s resolve to bring those who are guilty of such crimes to justice". The suspension has drawn criticism from Israel and the Simon Wiesenthal Center.', "LITHUANIAN PRESIDENT VOWS TO PROSECUTE WAR CRIMINALS Valdas Adamkus has reaffirmed Lithuania's commitment to prosecuting war criminals. Commemorating the 55th anniversary of the Vilnius ghetto massacre on 23 September, Adamkus repeated that Lithuania firmly supports the further prosecution of those who participated in Nazi war crimes. With regard to the suspension of the trial of accused war criminal Aleksandras Lileikis, Adamkus insisted that it should not be interpreted in any way as a weakening of the Lithuanian government's resolve to bring those who are guilty of such crimes to justice. The suspension has drawn criticism from Israel and the Simon Wiesenthal Center."]

Name: 115_membership nato_latvia membership_nato membership_public opinion
Representation: membership nato_latvia membership_nato membership_public opinion_market public_support latvia_opinion research_latvia nato_two thirds_december 2003
Representative_Docs: ["Support for Latvia's membership of NATO continues to grow The support of Latvian society for Latvia's membership of NATO continues to increase and has reached its highest level ever. The most recent survey by market and public opinion research company Latvijas Fakti conducted in june 2005 shows that 77.7% of Latvia's population supports Latvia's membership of NATO, a 3.7% increase from August 2004. 27.8% of Latvian residents expressed their support for NATO, saying that Latvia's membership of NATO increases the safety of the State and its residents; 15.2% considered that membership improved Latvia's international image and influence; and 11.8% thought a common defence was cheaper than an individual one. The majority of Latvian residents (55.1 %) were convinced that involvement of NATO in international peacekeeping operations was required, as it would increase stability not only in the world but also in Europe4? Latvian residents also voiced significant support (30.4%) for the participation of Latvian military personnel in international peacekeeping operations. 20 -29 July 2005", 'VISAS NO LONGER REQUIRED As of 1 January 2003, citizens of Australia, New Zealand and Canada, in possession of their country\'s official citizen travel document are able to enter the territory of the Republic of Latvia without a visa and remain there for up to 90 days within a six-month period. The unilateral establishment of a visa-free regime for the citizens of the said countries is based on Latvia\'s efforts to promote economic and cultural contacts with Australia, New Zealand and Canada, and contribute to efforts to achieve the easing of entry requirements for citizens of Latvia wishing to travel to these countries. TWO THIRDS FAVOUR NATO MEMBERSHIP The most recent survey by the market and public opinion research company Latvijas Fakti, conducted in December 2002, shows that two thirds of Latvia\'s population support NATO membership for Latvia. In comparison to survey results from July 2002, the number of respondents supporting or not disagreeing with Latvia\'s membership of NATO has risen from 66.1% to 68.5%. Of those not disagreeing, 14.1% expressed the wish to receive more information on the subject (in March the figure was 16.7%, in July 14.8%). Results of the survey also reveal that with Latvia having been invited to become a NATO member state, the number of people who have not formed an opinion on Latvia\'s NATO membership has fallen (from 11.5% in March to 7.3% in July and 5.5% in December). Meanwhile 26.0% of respondents (26.6% in July) consider that Latvia\'s membership of NATO is unnecessary. Of those with a negative point of view, in response to the question "How is it possible to guarantee Latvia\'s security without NATO membership?" 26.4% answered that Latvia should preserve its', 'I\'OI I I I X A What You Can Learn from Latvia\'s "Global-Sawy" Marketing to Join NATO The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) was formed in 1949 to act as a Western bulwark against the Soviet Union. Since NATO still does not trust Russia, it is involved in providing security to the Balkans, Afghanistan, and countries elsewhere in the world. The people with whom 1 have spoken say Latvia\'s commitment to democracy and justice convinced NATO that Latvia would be a good member. Even though few Latvians understand the role that marketing played in getting Latvia into NATO, "I get by with a little help from my friends," as the Beatles song attests. What are the rules of marketing that Latvia has followed? Although I played a very small role in the events leading up to Latvia\'s admission to NATO, here are the insights I have garnered. Speak the language of your target market fluently Although companies in the commercial world know that they must market globally but act locally, many countries do not realize the implications of this slogan. Latvia, however, started to change when Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga became President in 1995. She was born in Latvia but fled to the West when the Soviet Union "liberated" Latvia (from itself) during World War EL She grew up in Canada and speaks fluent Latvian, English, and French. Freiberga was a compromise candidate for President when the legislature could not agree on two other candidates. The previous President was a nice, mildmannered guy, whose grandfather was Latvia\'s last president before the Soviet occupation. However, he was an Eastern-European stereotype of bad hair and mismatched clothes. I won\'t even mention the ties he wore. He was always seen carrying a shopping bag after official visits abroad and spoke with a classic, Eastern-European, broken accent, "How for to nice to have seeing you." In contrast, Freiberga is an elegant lady. When she was elected President, Latvia\'s image began to change. Although NATO consists of 26 countries, the United States pays 50% of the budget. Consequently, it is a huge advantage that Freiberga can speak English. She also speaks French, which helped to charm the French President, Jacques Chirac. Although the French do not initiate much action in NATO, they do use their veto power a lot so Freiberga was able to address whatever objections the French might have had. Share the same enemies and fears as your target market At a recent black-tie dinner in Washington, D. C, the Latvian President got a standing ovation for proclaiming "We, the Latvian people, have not been put by the good Lord on this earth to make Russia happy." There has been a long-standing enmity between the United States and Russia as well. Although the two countries have a better relationship than during the cold war, a remnant of mistrust still exists today. President Bush was in Latvia last year. Having a NATO meeting in Latvia "sticks in the craw" of the Russians. Bill Clinton was the first United States President to visit Latvia, which proves that we Latvians know how to market ourselves to both political parties. I love it. We got the two parties to "out Latvia" each other. Get client references The Latvian Diaspora was constantly using marketing communications to their advantage. As the Soviet Union was crumbling, Latvians put pressure on the White House and were able to win the support of influential members of Congress. They, in turn, were able to secure resolutions in support of Latvia at a crucial time. According to a recent publication on how Latvia got into NATO, the idea was to have policymakers constandy informed of the Latvian cause through the use of briefings, meetings, receptions, electronic mail, the Internet, and letter-writing campaigns. When it comes to marketing, you have to be utterly shameless at times. I guess it is poetic justice that the last name of one Diaspora Latvian who did the most to get Latvia into NATO is Kukainis, which translates as "bug." Develop strategic alliances (or the cheeseDurger defense" of Latvia) If you are a small player in any field, you form an alliance with large players in order to seem bigger. Latvians capitalized on every imaginable source of support from labor unions to other ethnic groups. I attended an event for African diplomats in Washington, D. C. They learned about Latvia\'s plight and then wrote letters to the "powers that be" who determine who gets into NATO so that they got a constant stream of messages. I sometimes think we got where we are just by getting people to vote for us so that the Latvians would not annoy them anymore. I got into the action as the U. S. representative of the Latvian Investment and Development Agency (www. liaa.gov.lv). Our goal was to attract U. S. investment to Latvia, which could then become another part of the strategic alliance effort. When McDonald\'s had its eye on Latvia back in 1995, some Latvians protested. I started a media campaign to change people\'s minds and wrote an article entitled "Kādēļ Latvijai vajadzīga McDonald\'s čīzburgera aizsardzība," which translates as "The Cheeseburger Defense of Latvia." Latvians were protesting that this symbol of American capitalism would be located near the sacred national monument. In a commentary which appeared on January 10, 1995,1 suggested that Latvia could better protect itself by having a chain of McDonald\'s restaurants facing the Russian border rather than a line of NATO tanks. McDonald\'s did come to Latvia and hundreds of U.S. firms followed, including Microsoft. All of the U.S. companies in Latvia do their part by lobbying the United States government on Latvia\'s behalf. Be super-American (or client-oriented) Although Latvians are indifferent to, if not against, the war in Iraq, they have a contingent of troops there. They want their own wing of F- 16s (four jets) to guard the skies over Latvia, which is "music to the Pentagon\'s ears." Currently, Latvia, Lithuania, and Estonia share four F-16s based in Lithuania. I sometimes think the purpose of NATO is to sell military hardware. This rule applies in business as well. Be super-nationalistic to your client. You do not want to be seen driving a Toyota when you are trying to sell your services to General Motors. I know a vendor who sent a proposal by way of FedEx to solicit business from UPS! Make a Good Impression Regardless of how compelling your verbal argument is, you have to have a compelling image to go along with it. We have an elegant president, but we needed something more. A Latvian immigrant and a history professor at the University of Wisconsin-Eau Claire came up with the idea of a occupation museum. Patterned after the Holocaust Museum in Tel Aviv which detailed the images of what happened to the lews under the Nazis, this museum would tell the story of Latvia being occupied by both the Soviets and the Nazis. In order to handle all of these marketing efforts, Latvia established the Latvian Institute, which is now run by the first Latvian ambassador to the United States. Who is the person who headed the Latvian Institute previously? She is none other than the person who has made such an impact on the world scene, Latvia s current President. Although it helps to have a good cause or service or product to market, not much will happen unless you go out and shake the marketing tree. John Freivalds Jānis Freivalds was born in 1944 in Skujene, Latvia. He graduated from the School of Foreign service at Georgetown University and received his master s degree in International Affairs from George Washington University, both in Washington, D. C.']

Representative_Docs: ['EUROVISION 2003 IN RIGA TO WELCOME PARTICIPANTS FROM 26 COUNTRIES The European Broadcasting Union (EBU) has decided that next year the Eurovision song contest, with its final concert taking place in the Latvian capital of Riga, will include participants from 26 countries. The number of participating countries will be the highest in Eurovision history, since 24 countries took part in this year\'s competition in Tallinn, Estonia. As in the previous years, the song contest involving viewers\' votes by telephone will be televised live. The countries whose singers ranked among the top 18 this year will take part in the concert. These countries are: Latvia, Malta, Estonia, UK, France, Cyprus, Spain, Sweden, Romania, Russia, Croatia, Israel, Belgium, Bosnia-Herzegovina, Slovenia, Turkey, Greece and Austria. Germany, being one of the "big 4* European countries, will also take part in 2003, despite Its 21 st place this year. Countries missing out this year following a poor performance in 2001 will again join the contest next year; these include Iceland, Ireland, the Netherlands, Norway, Poland and Portugal. Participating for the first time will be Ukraine. Latvia earned the opportunity to host the Eurovis\'ion song contest in May 2003 because of the victory of Latvian singer Maria Naumova in this year\'s event in Tallinn.', 'LATVIAN SINGER WINS EUROVISION SONG CONTEST 2002 Latvian singer Marija Naumova, who participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2002 under the stage name of Marie N„, won the Eurovision finals in the Estonian capita! Tallinn on 25 May 2002, As the contest was won by the Latvian representative. Latvia will get the right to host next year\'s Eurovision finals. When a German reporter asked Marija Naumova what\'s really going on with Eurovision so that small countries like Estonia and Latvia keep winning it, she replied: "Forget what you\'re doing, forget that you\'re Germans, forget the Ist place, just give a little wamrth in your performance" and earned a rapturous round of applause from the audience. The song / Wanna, performed by Naumova, scored 176 points in the Eurovision finals, and the runner-up was Maltese singer fra Losco with 164 points she earned for the song 7th Wonder. Naumova herself wrote music to the winning song / Wanna and created lyrics together with Marats Samauskis.', 'GOVERNMENT APPROVES EUROVISION FINALS IN LATVIA The Cabinet of Ministers at the meeting on 4 June 2002 approved organising the finals of the 48th Eurovision song contest In Latvia, Minister of Environmental Protection and Regional Development, acting Minister of Culture Mr Vladimirs Makarovs told reporters. The Ministry of Culture, in conjunction with the State Chancellery, will sign Latvia\'s letter of confirmation that will state Latvia\'s determination to organise the event, and guarantee that the Latvian State Television (LTV) is allotted no less than CHF 5 million to organise the contest. A task fGrce will be set up by the LTV, National Radio and Television Council, several ministries\' state secretaries and their deputies. The task force will work on organising the Eurovision song contest and report to the government on estimated expenses, the most suitable location and venue and other matters. By the Midsummer Holiday {23 June), LTV could prepare a rough estimate, the composition of the task force and guidelines for implementation of the project, Mr Makarovs said. The contest\'s organisational matters will be reviewed by LTV, while the task force will be a "political guarantee that the event takes place". Latvia earned the opportunity to organise the contest\'s finals in 2003 after pop singer Marija Naumova won the 47th Eurovision song contest in Tallinn this year. Edited by Ēriks Zvirbulis']

Representative_Docs: ["PERSONAL HOUSING LOANS SOAR IN LATVIA THIS YEAR Personal housing loans issued by Latvian banks in the first half of this year increased by 42.8% or LVL 101.612 million to reach a value LVL 339.193 million at the end of June, according to the Latvian Association of CommerciaJ Banks. Hansabanka led Latvia's banks in terms of the value of personal housing loans granted in the period: LVL 79.515 million, up 26.7% or LVL 16.768 million since the end of the last year. Hansabanka's share of the personal housing loans market was 23.4% in late June 2003. Latvijas Unibanka issued LVL 63.773 million in personal housing loans in the first half of this year, an increase of 28.1% or LVL 13.996 million over figures for the end of 2002, and accounted for 18.8% of the market. Latvijas Hipotēku un zemes banka (Latvia's Land and Mortgage Bank) had issued LVL 43.988 million in personal housing loans in the period, a rise of 25.7% or LVL 8.989 million over results at the end of last year, and held a market share of 13%. Personal housing loans issued by Hansabanka grew 5.3%, while growth for Latvijas Unibanka and the Mortgage Bank was 6.8% and 5% respectively.", "MORE FOREIGNERS DEPOSIT MONEY IN LATVIA'S BANKS THAN IN ANY OTHER BALTIC COUNTRY The percentage of non-residents depositing money in Latvia's commercial banks at the end of June reached 49.8% of the total volume of deposits, which is the highest figure in the Baltic States, according to data from tie Association of Latvian Commercial Banks. In Lithuania, the percentage of non-resident depositors is much less, only 5.5%, but in Estonia 13.9%. The total volume of such deposits in the Baltic States has dropped compared to the figure at the end of June last year. Last year, the percentage for Latvia was 50.9%, Lithuania 7.8% and Estonia 16.4%. By the end of June, Baltic commercial banks had drawn in 10.53 billion euro (or 6.28 billion lats). The breakdown among the banks in each country: Latvia 4.46 billion euro (2.68 billion lats), Lithuania 3.09 billion euro (1.84 billion lats), Estonia 2.98 billion euro (1.78 billion lats). The volume of personal deposits in Baltic commercial banks is at 4.44 billion euro (2.65 billion lats). Latvia has 23 banks, Lithuania 14, Estonia 7.", 'LONG-TERM DEPOSIT AND CREDIT RATES DOWN IN 1995\n\nAccording to the State Statisticai Bureau, the annual long-term deposit rāte decreased last year on average by 37.2% when compared to 1994. The average deposit rāte was 19.8% per yearin 1995. Itdropped from 20.15% in Janu ary 1995 to 15.7% in December 1995. Between November and December. the average annual deposit rāte went up by 0.1%. In 1995. the lowest long-term deposit rāte of 15.4% was reported in August, whiie the highest average rāte of 20.5% was calcuiated in October.
