Representative_Docs: ["By Alvis Brigis Marked by record attendance, an exceptional folk dancing presentation, an inquisitive young bear, and the most unlikely appearance by Willy Wonka ever, Valodas Periods 2005 (Mv 31 – Aug. 13) was a memorable two weeks for everyone involved. It started off with a bang as 77 campers, a new record for the English-speaking session, were dropped off by their parents that first Sunday. Instantly friendships were rekindled, new friendship bonds were forged, and the speculation about the theme or Skerslu Gājiens, the traditional camp role playing game, began to run rampant. Katskilu Nometne: Valodas Periods 2005 All was right in Nometne, and everyone could sense that it was going to be a fun session. Everyone was abuzz as they walked over to the opening ceremonies lead by Camp Director leva Alverson. She gladly welcomed the returning campers and new arrivals, and then set out the rules of Katskilu Nometne. First and foremost, she warned, it was imperative to ensure no food was kept in the cabins and to always remember the triple-trill bear signal that counselors would blow on their whisdes if they ever saw such a creature. Unbeknownst to all, this proved to l)e the first foreshadowing of our large mammalian visitor. The campers, counselors and administration quickly settled into a pleasant routine. During the mornings the kids attended classes dedicated to Latvian heritage and culture. Their teachers included Inga Garbarino (Latvian Language Department), Irena Jasuta (Singing and Folk Dancing), and Nicky Van Valkenburg, Dace and Alexandra (Art Department). In the afternoon they were set loose to engage in sports like Tautas Bumba or Ultimate Frisbee and to swim in the surprisingly overflowing Grill Lake. Prior to supper they received religious instruction from Mac. Laris Sal ins, the person who currently claims the longest streak of summers at Nometne. And in the evening their activities consisted of classic Latvian outdoor games (Ara Rotaļas), Capture the Flag, a variety of dances, Casino Night, Movie Night, and a special candlelight church service, and other run events. Starting at row one left to right: Peter Blassman, Jake Addison, Cameron Smith, Petri An sons, Emily Stein, Kaiva Hinkle, Kaija Sorgman, Taylor Addison, Silvija Meixner, Emmi Lovko, Ricky Souza, Natālija Lakis, Jenna Dzelzgalvis, Casey Tichey, Michael and Konrad Dzelzgalvis, Marshall Rudzi tis, Trent Munson, Jillian Stein, Ben Smith, kairina Lakis, Peter Zirnis Capture the Flag proved to be one of the more memorable events when, just as one team was about to seal the victory', the counselors sounded the bear signal and the entire camp was ordered to squeeze into a guest building to wait for the bear to pass. After a few nervous moments and a proper headcount, the campers headed off for their evening snack, not a little bit miffed that the bear had interrupted their good time. But there was still more fun to be had. Not long after, an agitated Willy Wonka made a surprise appearance at camp and was introduced by his shady assistant Chester Chesterton. Apparendy his Oompa-Loopmas were running amok and had halted candy pnxluction worldwide. He asked the kids for their assistance in the matter and, being fans of candy, the campers graciously agreed. Unfortunately, the FBI was also on Mr. Wonka's tail Katskilu Nometne: Valodas Periods 2005 and somewhat complicated things when they rolled into camp. What followed was a most amazing adventure, during which the campers went to great lengths to collect the evidence necessary to exonerate Mr. Wonka. In the end it was this evidence and testimony by team captains Harper and Emily that resulted in the release of Wonka from (..itskill Mountain Court. Following the dramatic culmination of the Wonka case everyone, campers and counselors alike, began to realize that VaUxlas Peri(xTs 2005 was rapidly coming to an end. As if to echo this downcast mood, it finally began to rain the last night of camp just prior to the Last Dance which was set to take place outside on the basketball court. The venue was quickly changed and, to be honest, nobody really noticed. The campers danced into the night, they were allowed to stay up a little later than usual, and began to reminisce about the year. The counselors, too, discussed what a great time they'd had and how they dreaded the return to real life. The following morning I witnessed one of the better Folk Dancing presentations I've ever had the pleasure to see at Katskili. It was wonderful to watch the various groups of boys, girls and counselors as they flawlessly danced six unique numbers which they'd all learned in well under two weeks under the explicit direction of gurus Irena jasuta, Elissa Millers and Andrejs Salins. The show went so well that the parents and grandparents in attendance offered-up a standing ovation, demanding an encore. Kudos Nometne! And so, Valodas Periods 2005 started on a good note, ended on a great note, and now we're all left waiting for another record-breaking session in 2006. Fortunately, when considering the awesome campers due to return, the dedicated counselors, hard-working administration and especially recent trends indicating a boom in the Iksldsu (Latvian toddler) population, it becomes apparent that the best is yet to come at Katskili!", 'There is a new program emerging from the \'Valodas periods" (the "Language period") of the Latvian Lutheran Church of New York this year. Eight young people are being prepared to be confirmed at the Catskill camp this summer. The "Valodas periods" has been a growing enterprise. The progam began 25 years ago to reach out to families where the children are part of the Latvian community but do not speak fluent Latvian. A generation later the program is going strong. In the summer of 2006 there were 75 children in the 2 week long "Valodas periods" and 17 toddlers in "īkšķiši" ("Tom Thumbs and Thumbelinas") in the 1 week long "Mommy and me" program. The "Valodas periods" is largely modeled on the program that has been effective and beloved in the Latvian language periods. Days begin by singing "Rīta gaisma mūžīga" ("The eternal light of morning") at the flag. At the end of the day, as the flag is lowered, "Dievs, svētī Latviju" ("God bless Latvia") is sung. There are campfire skits, dances, folk dancing, folk song singing, sports, even "šķēršļu gājieni". ("Obstacle course" is the literal, but inadequate translation for these dramatic group activities.) In the first years the camp had a greater emphasis on preparing children for eventual Latvian language fluency. After a while it became clear that this was not a realistic or appropriate goal. Now the emphasis is on learning about and experiencing Latvian culture. The morning sessions are, therefore, spent in study "rotations". The equally important emphasis is on fostering friendships. "Valodas periods" is part of the summer camp program of Valodas periods the Latvian Lutheran Church of New York. Religious instruction in the "reliģiskās apceres" ("religion classes") and the worship services on Sunday morinings and Wednesday evenings have always been a central element of camp life. Three years ago a family from the "Valodas periods" asked to be confirmed at camp. Pastor Saliņš agreed to do the preparation which entailed both meeting at camp but also separately during the year. Last year a whole group of families approached Pastor Saliņš about confirmation. The result was a group of 8 young people who Pirmā rindā no kr.: Miša  7 ~«--«.-m.- — f,. —.... . ~ |, Inga O\'Brien. Otrā rindā diakone Eva DzelzgaTve, draudzes priekšniece Māra Buka, māc. Arvīds Ziedonis Vasarsvētkos Filadelfijas Sv. Jāņa draudzē iesvētīja sešus jauniešus. began their studies during camp last year. They met each day during "Klusā stunda" ("Quiet hour"). This is a two year program that will conclude with confirmation during the Sunday morning service on the middle Sunday of "Valodas periods". The materials that were used for study were "Baptized We Live" by Daniel Erlanger and "The Original Jesus" by Tom Wright. The confirmands were given regular assignments via email during the year. They were also required to attend services at their local churches and report on their experience. To be confirmed the families of the confirmands either had to be members of the Latvian Lutheran Church of New York or else receive permission from their local churches to be confirmed in this way. The highlight of the "Valodas periods" confirmation study was the Confirmation retreat that happened on April 14th and 15th this year. Robert Eason, Chris LaMonte, Steven Jasuta, Frik Muir, Laura Van Valkenburg, Kersti Francis, Anda Simmons, Eva Eason un Nikolete Robertella came from as far away as Cleveland and Virginia. We met for a weekend of study, reflection, meditation, prayer and friendly fellowship. After the confirmation this August, the church is planning to begin another 2 year course of study in the summer of 2008. Pastor Laris Saliņš', 'This years iesvētāmie are leva and Roberts Eason, Kersti Francis, Eriks Muir, Christopher La-Fonte, Nikolette Robertella, Anda Simmons and Laura Van Valkenburg. "The iesvētāmie come from many areas, Kersti Francis, as far away as West VA., who travels every year to Catskills are bonded by their summers at nometne. Most of them have known each other since īkšķīši and most recently by an overnight confirmation retreat held on April 14, 2007 in the Catskills where they all stayed in the Ezen Maja. As individuals, they bring their own interests and insights to the group and this confirmation experience. Erik Muir never misses the opportunity to educate his friends about Latvia. He feels being Latvian has afforded him many different experiences, including this confirmation instruction, which has given him many new perspectives. Erik\'s best memory of īkšķīši is the Olympic circuit that was set up for them every year and standing on the podium to receive their ribbons. He always looks forward to War in the Woods, Capture the Flag and any activity associated with the "sporta komandas." For the last 2 summers these children have been studying with Māc. Laris Saliņš. During Nometne and at home by e-mail. Māc Saliņš said: "Many of you really were able to sum up what the study has meant to you. The camp experience is both the most demanding and the most rewarding thing I do every year." "The Children also requested a special mention of loved ones that have past away but we still want to remember on this special day. Thank you for all your work and help to make the lesvētības a beautiful and meaningful experience." Nikolette Robertella, Anda Simmons, Roberts Easton, Ieva Easton Iesvētāmie in the Catskills Psalm 24: Who shall ascend the hill of the LORD? And who shall stand in his holy place? (Kas drīkst kāpt tā Kunga kalnā, un kas stāvēs Viņa svētajā vietā?) Heb. 11: They were strangers and pilgrims on the earth. But now they desire a better country, that is, a heavenly one. (Tie bija svešnieki un svētceļnieki virs zemes. Bet tie nu ilgojas pēc labākas, debešķīgākas piederības.) Last year this camp, the camp of the Latvian Lutheran Church of New York, celebrated a memorable anniversary – what Latvians call "apaļi gadi" – "round years" – the big "5 – 0". That was a good chunk of history to look back on. But let\'s also not forget that "Valodas periods" – the part of our camp program targeted to children who don\'t have fluent Latvian – has been around now for at least half that time – depends on how you count the beginning of Valodas periods. The program began in 1979 when it ran besides other camp programs. In 1982 Valodas periods became a separate period with its own 2-week time slot. If we count 1982 as the beginning of Valodas periods, then this is our 25th anniversary summer. We can thank Ingrida Miemis for her initiative in beginning this period. She started it for her brother-in-law\'s daughter Joanne, but – her personal need resonated with a much broader need. And it is no accident that Ingrida\'s children and grandchildren are with us here now. This extended of family spirit is what has made our camp work all along. At first this Valodas periods faced substantial obstacles. When the post-WWII refugees first conceived the mission of the Latvian church and the church\'s camp, the model was "fortress Latvia". That meant that only Latvian was spoken. Even in the early 1980s any retreat from this consistent stance was seen by many as blasphemy. It is maybe for this reason that this program is somewhat misnamed as the "Language period". If the goal was to turn the campers into Latvian speakers, most everybody could buy into that. Fortunately this goal was quickly seen as largely unrealistic. The new and more appropriate emphasis was and has remained on the Latvian heritage taught in English but supplemented with Latvian folk songs and dances, and Latvian Lutheran church hymns and liturgy. Language classes offer those who have that calling more in depth opportunities to learn the Latvian language. Especially now, when we can thank God that Latvia is secure in its regained freedom, we no longer need a "fortress mentality" and can reconceive our misson to be a welcoming community for everyone who has a connection to the Latvian Lutheran family. Looking back on the time that I have been involved with the Valodas periods, I like to think that we have come a long way. And I believe that this worship service with the confirmation of 8 of our young people is a beautiful expression of how we\'ve been able to develop meaningful traditions that we can pass on to the coming generations. As a point of contrast I want to share what amounts to a horror story from the early years of the Valodas periods. I\'ve been a pastor of the Latvian Lutheran Church of New York since 1984. In 1986 I was the director of the summer camp. Like this year, there was a Latvian Song Festival over the 4th of July weekend. It did not seem feasible to begin the Latvian language camp while the Song Festival was going on. The camp committee, therefore, decided to rearrange the order of the periods. That summer camp began with the Valodas periods. The camp commit tee rightly figured that the Song Festival would not much affect the Valodas periods. But there were other significant factors that the committee had seriously misjudged. Even though the campers were here for the beginning of camp, most of the counselors were still at the Song Festival in Toronto. As I recall, only Baiba Kļaviņa, who was the program director, and less than a handful of counselors were there for the first evening. We attempted to begin camp in what was the traditional way – with a candlelight service in this sanctuary. If memory serves me, there were about 60 kids in camp. Before beginning the winding journey up the hillside with the pastor in the lead, we instructed the campers that they were to walk quietly and in single file. As this was being told to the campers they were already talking among themselves. 1 think the gist of what they were saying was: "Yah, right! No way, Jose!" That night we didn\'t have a silent, single file procession but a loud and unruly mob. Instead of leading the climb, I found myself Laris Saliņš in the role of frazzled sheep dog running up and down the hillside trying to direct and "shush" the campers. My attempts to lead the campers through a liturgy for the service were a shambles. On that evening it seemed that I would have had just as much lucking trying to get the campers to sing in Swahili as in Latvian. Eventually, as the counselors trickled in from the Song Festival – and slept off their hangovers – the Valodas Periods program got in gear and the outcome was a good experience, but the beginning was terribly shaky. This year when leva Alversons asked me to begin this period with a candlelight service, we knew that we would not have to sell the campers on "why the silent walk" or "why sing in Latvian". Even though a good number of the campers were here for the first time, we knew that we had a solid core of campers who have been here from year to year. They know the drill and they know that the whole experience is better, more beautiful and more meaningful when we respect our traditions. I wrote the text to the hymn that we just sang for the camp\'s 50th anniversary in order to evoke our shared experience of worshiping God in this sanctuary. If this period\'s characteristic walk to our hillside sanctuary were still like mob experience 20 years ago, the hymn would not be appropriate for this group. I felt challenged to translate the hymn into English, because this period has so fully grown into our camp\'s traditions, that we all have a snared experience of the power of worship in this sanctuary in the heart of nature\'s beauty. I was confident that we could sing – without irony – about "calming silence (that) guides our course". Today is not the first Valodas periods Confirmation. 3 years ago Brita and Astrida Vallens were confirmed here. But that was not yet the beginning of a Confirmation program. It was just one family asking for a service and me doing what I could to provide it. But once they took that step, a whole group of other families realized that they would like something similar. We began preparing for this day over a year ago and we met at camp every day last year and this year during Klusā stunda, the Quiet Hour in the early afternoon. We studied the Creed, the 10 commandments, and the high points of the biblical story. We did what Confirmation classes usually do. The main difference being that we lived at substantial distances form one another – some of us as far away as Ohio and Virginia. But we live in the era of the Internet and I was able to give and receive assignments via email. And we all even made our way back here in April for a confirmation retreat. Maybe some of you saw our picture in the church newsletter. We were hit by a late season storm on Sunday morning and almost got snowed in. The whole Confirmation process culminates in this service. By reciting the Apostle\'s Creed, the confirmands affirmed the faith that had been affirmed in their stead – by their parents and godparents – when they were baptized. And soon they will assemble before this altar with a cross of birch to receive Christ\'s body and blood above, below and through the blessed bread and wine. That\'s why we sang that the "God of nature shares His nature." In the letter to the Hebrews we hear about "strangers and pilgrims on earth (who) desire a better country, a heavenly one." This period the whole camp is focusing on the themes of thanksgiving and pilgrimage. Today we thank God that we were able to make a small pilgrimage to this special place of worship, where we can more deeply experience the faith that has sustained the Latvian family thro ugh good days and bad – "dienās baltās, nebaltās" as part of Christ\'s all-embracing family. This is the Good news that Christ confesses – "the Cross has been crowned with Eternal Life, Life sublime." When we open our hearts and minds to Christ\'s words, we receive "Seeds of light" that with God\'s blessing "will blossom in their season". We thank God that God has blessed our work in this Valodas periods for 25 years and will bless it for many years to com. The ultimate harvest will be reaped when God\'s Kingdom comes and His will is done – kā debesīs, tā arī virs zemes – on Earth, as it is in Heaven. Āmen.']

Representative_Docs: ['Truman attlee disagree ON COMMUNIST CHINA! After five days of conferences in Washington, the following communique was issued by President Truman and Prime Minister Attlee, stressing the need of building up the defence forces of their respective countries. The troubled circumstances of the world, says the communique, leaves America and Britain no other choice but to devote themselves with all vigor to this immediate task. Mr. Truman and Mr. Attle< said: "We believe that Communis leaders of the Soviet Union an< China could if they chose modif; their conduct in such a way a to make these defence prepara tions unnecessary. "We shall do everything tha we can through whatever chan nels are open to us to impres: this view upon them and to seel a peaceful solution of existinf issues. "The objectives of Britain anc the United States in foreigr policy are the same—to maintair world peace and respect for th( rights and interests of all peoples to promote strength and confi dence among the freedom-loving countries of the world, to eliminate causes of fear, want anc discontent and to advance thf democratic way of life. "Unity of objectives of our twc countries underlay all discussions There is no difference between us as to the nature of the threat which our countries face, or the basic policies which must be pursued to overcome it. "We recognise that many of the problems which we have discussed :an be decided only through the procedures of the United Nations or the North Atlantic Treaty organisation. The peoples of the United States and Britain will act together with resolution and unity to meet the challenge to peace which recent weeks have madt ?lear to all." After stating that the situatior ;n Korea was one of "great gravity and far-reaching consequences" brought about by the intervention of large numbers ol Chinese Communist troops, the communique continued: "The United Nations forces have the advantage of superior air power and naval support, but on the ground they are confronted by heavy numerical superiority. "The United Nations forces were sent into Korea on the authority and at the recommendation of the United Nations. "The United Nations has not changed the mission which it has entrusted to them. The forces of our two countries will continue to discharge their responsibilities. "We were in complete agreement that • there can be no thought of appeasement or of rewarding aggression whether in the Far East or elsewhere. "Lasting peace and the future of the United Nations as an instrument for world peace depend upon strong support for resistance against aggression. "For our part we are ready, as we have always been, to seek an end to hostilities by means of negotiation. "The same principles of international conduct should be applied to this situation as are applied in accordance with our obligations under the Charter of the United Nations to any threat to world peace. "Every effort must be made to achieve the purposes of the United Nations in Korea by peaceful means and to find a solution of the Korean problem on the basis of a free and independent Korea. "We are confident that the great majority of the United Nations takes the same view. "If the Chinese on their side display any evidence of similar attitude we are hopeful that the cause of peace can be upheld. "If they do not, then it will be for the peoples of the world acting through the United Nations to decide how the principles of the charter can best be maintained. "For our part we declare in advance our firm resolve to uphold them. "We considered two questions regarding China which are already before the United Nations. "On the question of the Chinese seat in the United Nations the two Governments differ. Britain has recognised the Central People\'s Government and considers its representatives should occupy China\'s seat in the United Nations. "The United States has opposed and continues to oppose the seating of the Chinese Communist representatives in the United Nations. "We have discussed our difference of view on this point, and are determined to prevent it from interfering with our united effort in support of our common objectives. "On the question of Formosa we have noted that both Chinese claimants have insisted upon the validity of the Cairo declaration and have expressed reluctance to have the matter considered by the United Nations. "We agreed that the issues should be settled by peaceful means and in such a way as to safeguard the interests of the people of Formosa and the maintenance of peace and security in the Pacific and that consideration of this question by the United Nations will contribute to these ends. "The free nations of Asia have given strong support to the United Nations and have worked for world peace. "Communist aggression in Korea increases the danger to the security and independence of these nations. "We reaffirm our intention to continue to help them. "Pressure of Communist expansion existed in Europe and elsewhere long before the aggression against Korea and measures were taken to meet it. forces of collective security has already been recognised. Action for this purpose is under way Clearly decisions regarding the Far East have their repercussions and effects elsewhere. "In considering the necessities of the Far Eastern situation we have kept in mind the urgency of building up the strength of the whole free world. "We are in complete agreement on the need for immediate action by all North Atlantic treaty countries to intensify their efforts to build up their defences and to strengthen the Atlantic community. "In this critical period, it is a source of satisfaction to us that the views of our Governments on basic problems are so similar. "We believe that this identity of aims will enable our Governments to carry out their determination to work together tc strengthen the unity which has already been achieved among tht free nations, and to defend thoļjjļi vaules which are of fundamenta! importance to the people w< represent."', 'Common need of Nations President Eisenhower declared that all nations defending freedom "are one in their common need and their common cause—and none can sanely seek security alone." The The President made this statement in an address to the National Junior Chamber of Commerce, an organisation of younger businessmen from all parts of the United States. "It is no wonder that our national security is so vast a matter," he said, "for the struggle in which freedom tod ay is engaged is quite literally a total and universal struggle, tl engages every aspect of our lives. It is waged in every arena in which a challenged civilisation must fight to live." President Eisenhower said that it is also an economic struggle, a political struggle, a scientific and an intellectual struggle. "It is a spiritual struggle," he continued, "for one of communism\'s basic assumptions about the nature of men is that they rae incapable of ruling themselves, incapable of attaining the spiritual standards and strength to solve national problems when these require voluntary personal sacrifice for the common good. "This is the communist\'s justification for regimentation. All this we deny. And we must seek in our churches, our schools, our homes and our daily lives the clearness of mind and strongness of heart to guard the chance to live in freedom. "For this whole struggle, in the deepest sense, is waged neither for land nor lor good nor for power— but for the soul of man himself." He referred to the so-called "fortress" theory of defence, whose advocates ask why the United States cannot stand alone and make its own decisions, regardless of other nations. The President gave a few answers to this question. "A total struggle — let us never forget — calls for a total defence," he said. "As there is no weapon too small — no arena too remote — to be ignored, so there is no free nation too humble to be forgotten. "All of us have elarned — first fro mthe onslaught of Nazi aggression, then from communist aggression — that all free nations must stand together, or they shall fall separately. . . "There is no such thing as partial unity. That is a contradiction of terms. "We cannot select those areas of the globe in which our policies or wishes may differ from our allies — build political fences around these areas — and say to our allies: VVc shall do what we want here — and where you do what we want, there and only there shall we favor unity. That is not unity. It is dictation. And it is not the way free men associate." The President took note of the "unhappy murmurings" about the United Nations, and the dismay caused by its "seemingly vain and tedious" political discussions. He pointed out that to the communist world the U.N. has seemed to be exploited in spreading disunity and confusion. To the Free World, he said, the U.N. has seemed it should be a constructive forum for free discussion of the world\'s problems, an effective agency for helping to solve those problems peacefully. "Perhaps," he said, "the greatest worth of the U.N. is previcely this: It holds up a mirror in which world can see its true self. Auti what shall we want to see in such a mirror but the whole truth — at such a time of total struggle?" toots two tunes at once. To its captive audience behind the Iron Curtain it plays a constant dirge of hate tor the Free World. To the Free World it often plays a siren-like tune that drips ot peace and goodwill. To understand such contradiction one must realise the critical internal problems in the Soviet Union. There are indications of a grim struggle for power among its new leaders—Premier Georgi Malenkov; Lavrenti Beria, chief of the secret police; Nikolai Bulganin, head of the Red army; Nikita Khushev, communist party secretary; and Viacheslav Molotov, minister of foreign affairs. All are striving for the support of a restive and oppressed people. "Pravda" says the Soviet Empire is being ruled by an "efficient collective party leadership," but history proves that anything but eventual one-man rule is incomprehensible in a dictatorship. It is evident that these men in the Kremlin have a purpose in maintaining the status quo—of following the Statin line of preaching hate at home and peace abroad. They do not want anyone rocking the boat — either from inside or out — until the scuffle in the wheelhouse is over. That was Lenin\'s line, too. He once said: "If it is possible to win time, to get even a short respite for organisational work, we must obtain it." Apparently the new Soviet corulers feel if they can stir up enough hate against the free nations they will be able to maintain unity at home while they iron out their leadership problems. Analysis of recent Red radio stations showss that Malenkov and company are playing the dissonant tunes of hate and peace as loudly as Stalin ever did. On May 29, Radio Budapest said the United Nations forces in Korea "again used grenades filled with poison gas. The grenades were shot from 155 m.m. cannons along the Sing-Sung sector." The false charge of using poison gas has been refuted many times, but here the communists .refute it themselves. tl is impossible to fire grenades from a cannon and there is no Sing-Sung sector in Korea. On June 1, the communist broadcasters for the East German Home Service launched an attack on Cchancellor Konrad Adenauer. The Free German leader, they siad, reminded them of President Syngman Rhee of the Fepublic of Korea. "What Rhee did in Korea," they said — "create an aggressive army and then launch an attackis what Adenauer hopes to achieve." The communists ginore, of course, two basic facts: that the Republic of Korea was invaded on June 25, 1950, by communist armies and that Adenauer hopes to build a denfence that will prevent a similar communist invasion of his country. On April 25, "Pravda" printed, for the outside world to read, a long and, for "Pravda," relatively gentle comment on President Eisenhower\'s April 16 speech, in which Mt. Eisenhower made a plea for "a true and total peace." Four days after the "Pravda" comment, the Russian clandestine radio station in Azerbajan province of northern Iran referred to "the demagogic speech of Eisenhower . . . who wants to cover up American imperialism and war activities." The Azerbajan station is a propaganda outlet for which the So, viet regime does not officially assume responsibility in order to be able ot use invective more freely.', 'Eisenhower\'s Inauguration Speech Here in full is the Inaugural speech made to the people of America and the world by President Eisenhower on January 21, 1953. "My fellow citizens, the world and we have passed the midway point of a century of continuous challenge. We sense with all our faculties that forces of good and evil are massed and armed and opposed .as rarely before in history. This fact defines the meaning of this day. We are summoned by this honored and historic ceremony to witness more than the act of one citizen swearing his oath of service in the presence of his God. We are called asa people to give testimony in the sight of the world to our faith that the future shall belong to the free. Since the century\'s beginning a time of tempest has seemed to come upon the continents of the earth. Masses of Asia have wakened to strike off shackles of the past. Great nations of Europe have waged their bloodiest wars. Thrones have toppled and their vast empires have disappeared. New nations have been born. For our own country it has been a time of recurring trial. We have grown in power and in responsibility. We have passed through the Anxieties of depression and of war to a summit unmatched in man\'s history. Seeking to secure peace in the world we have had to fight through the forests of the Argonne to the shores of Iwo Jima and to the mountain peaks of Korea. In the swift rush of great events we find ourselves groping to nkow the full sense and meaning of the times in which we live. In our quest of understanding we beseech God\'s guidance. As we summon all our knowledge of the past and we scan all signs of the future, we bring all out wit and will to meet the question how far have we gone in man\'s long pilgrimage from darkness toward light Ar* we nearing the light, a day of freedom and of peace for all mankind or are the shadows of another night closing in upon us. Great as are the preoccupations absorbing us at home, concerned as we are with matters that deeply affect our livelihood today and our vision of the future, each ol these domestic problems is developed by and often created by this question that Involves all human kind. This trial comes at a moment when man\'s power to achieve good or to influence evil surpasses the brightest hopes and the sharpest fears of all ages. We can turn rivers in their courses, level mountains to the plains. Ocean and land and sky are avenues for our colossal commerce. Disease diminishes and life lengthens. Yet the promise of this life is imperilled by the very genius that has made it possible Nations amass wealth. Labor sweats to create and turns out devices to level not only Mo>intains but also cities. Science seems ready to comer upon us as its final gift the power to ♦rase human life from the earth. At such a time in history we, who are free, must proclaim anew our faith. This faith is the abiding creed of your fathers. It is our faith »n the deathless dignity of man governed by eternal, morail and natural laws. This faith defines our full view of life. It establishes beyond debate those gifts of the Creator that are man\'s inalienable rights and that make aU men equal in his sight. In the light of this equality we know that the virtues most cherished by free people — love i of truth, pride of work, devotion I to country — all are treasures equally precious in the lives of the most humble and of the most exalted. The men who mine coal, and fire furnaces, and balance ledgers, and turn lathes, and pick cotton, and heal the sick, and plant corn all serve as proudly and as profitably for America as the statesmen who draft treaties, or the legislators who enact laws. This faith rules our whole way of life. It decrees that we, the people, elect leaders not to rule but to serve. It asserts that we have the right to choice of our own work and to the reward of our own toil. It inspires the initiative that makes our productivity the wonder of the world, and it warns that any man who seeks to deny equality in all his brothers betrays the spirit of the free and invites the mockery of the tyrant. The enemies of this faith know no God but force, no devotion but its use. They tutor men in treason. They feed upon the hunger of others. Whatever defies them they torture, especially the truth. Here, then, is joined no pallid argument between slightly differphilosophies. This conflict strikes directly at the faith of our fathers and the lives of our sons. No principle or treasure that we hold, from the spiritual knowledge of our free schools and churches- to the creative magic of free labor and capital, nothing lies safely beyond the reach of the struggle. Freedom is pitted against slavery — light against dark, the faith we hold belongs not to us alone but to the free of all the world. This common bond binds the grower of rice in Burma and the planter of wheat in lowa, the shepherd in southern Italy and the mountaineer in the Andes. It confers a common dignity upon the French soldier who dies in Indochina, the British soldier killed in Malaya, the American life given in Korea, We know, beyond this, that we are linked to all free peoples not merely by a noble idea but by a simple need. No free people can for long cling to any privilege or enjoy any safety in economic solitude. For all our own material might, even we need markets in the world for the surpluses of our farms and of our factories. Equally, we need for these same farms and factories vital materials and products of distant lands. This basic law of interdependence, so manifect in the commerce of peace, applies with thousand-fold intensity in the event of war. So are we persuaded by necessity and by belief that the strength of all free peoples lies in unity, their danger in discord, to produce this unity, to meet the challenge of our time, destiny has laid upon our country the responsibility of the Free World\'s leadership. So it is proper that we assure our friends once again that, in the discharge of this responsibility, we Americans know and observe the difference between world leadership and imperialism — between firmness and truculence — between a thoughtfully calculated goal and spasmodic reaction to the stimulus of emergencies. We wish our friends the world over to know this above all: We feel this moral strength because we know that we are not helpless prisoners of history- We are freemen. We shall remain free, never to be proven guilty of the one capital offense against freedom, a lack of staunch faith. In pleading our just cause before the bar of history and in pressing our labor for world peace, we shall be guided by certain fixed principles. m These principles are: Abhorring war as a chosen way to balk the purposes of those who threaten us, we hold it to be the first task of statesmanship to develop the strength that will deter the forces of aggression and promote the conditions of peace. For, as it must be the supreme purpose of all free man, so it must be the dedication of their leaders, to save humanity from preying upon itself. In the light of this principle, we stand ready to engage with any and all others in joint effort to remove the causes of mutual fear and distrust among nations, and so to make possible drastic reduction of armaments. The sole requisites for undertaking such effort * are that, in their purpose, they be aimed logically and honestly toward secure peace for all and that, in their result, they provide methods by which every participating nation will prove good faith in carrying out its pledge. Realizing that common sense and common decency alike dictate the futility of appeasement, we shall never try to placate an aggressor by the false and wicked bargain of trading honor for security. For in the final choice a soldier\'s pack is not so heavy i burden as a prisoner\'s chains. Knowing that only a United States that is strong and immensely productive can help defend freedom in our world, we view our nation\'s strength and security as a trust upon which rests the hope of free men crywhere. It is the firm duty of each of our free citizens and of every free citizen everywhere to place the cause of his country before the comfort of himself. Honoring the identity and heritage of each nation of the world, we shall never use our strength to try to impress upon another people our own cherished political and economic institutions. Assessing realistically the needs and capacities of proven friends of freedom, we shall strive to help them to achieve their own security and well-being. Likewise, we shall count upon them to assume, within the limits of their resources, their full and just burdens in the common defense of freedom. Recognizing economic health as an indispensable basis of military strength and the Free World\'s peace, we shall strive to foster everywhere, and to practice policies that encourage productivity and profitable trade. For the impoverishment of any single people in the world means danger to Ihe well-being of all other peoples. Appreciating that economic need, military security and political wisdom combine to suggest \' regional groupings of peoples, we I hope, within the framework of the United Nations to help strengthen such special bonds the world over. The nature of these ties must vary with the different problems of different areas in the Western hemisphere. We join with all our neighbors in the work of perfecting a community of fraternal trust and common purpose in Europe. We ask that enlightened and inspired leaders of the Western nations strive with renewed vigor to make the unity of their peoples a reality. Only when a free Europe unitedly marshals its strength can it effectively safeguard, even with our help, its spiritual and cultural treasures. Conceiving the defense of freedom, like freedom itself, to be one and indivisible we hold all continents and peoples in equal regard and honor. We reject any insinuation that one race or another, one people or another is in any sense inferior or expendable. Respecting the United Nations as the living sign of all peoples\' hope for peace, we shall strive to make it not merely an eloquent symbol but an effective force. And in our quest of honorable peace we shall neither compromise, nor tire, nor ever cease. By these rules of conduct we hope to be known to all peoples. By their observations, an earth of peace may become not a vision but a fact. This hope—this supreme aspiration—must rule the way we live, jWe must be ready to dare all for ; our country, for history does not long entrust the care of fjreedom to the weak or the timid. We must acquire proficiency in defense and display stamina in purpose. We must be willing, individually and as a nation, to accept whatever sacrifices may be required of us. A people that values its privileges above its principles soon loses both. These basic precepts are not lofty abstractions far removed from matters of daily living. They are laws of spiritual strength that generate and find our material strength. Patriotism means equipped forces and a prepared citizenry. Moral stamina means more energy and more productivity on the farm and in the fifJlor*. Love of liberty means the guarding of every resource that makes freedom possible — from the unity of our families and the wealth of our soil to the genius of our scientists. So each citizen plays an indispensable role. The productivity ot our heads, our hands and our hearts is the source of all the strength we can command for both the enrichment of our lives and the winning of peace. No person, no home, no community can be beyond the reach of this call. We are summoned to act in wisdom and in conscience, to work with industry, to teach with persuasion, to preach with conviction, to weigh our every deed with care and with compassion. For this truth must be clear before us. Whatever America hopes to bring to pass in the world must first come to pass in the heart of America. The peace we seek— this is nothing less than the practice and the fulfillment of our whole faith, among ourselves and in our dealings with others. It signifies more than stilling the guns, erasing the sorrow of war, more than an escape from death. "It is a way of life. More than a  for the weary, it is a hope lor the brave. This is the hope that beckons us onward in this century of trial. This is the work that awaits us all. To be done with bravery, with charity—and with prayer to Almighty God.']

Representative_Docs: ['SVEŠVALODU NODALA/OTHER LANGUAGES  SECTION In the end it came dovvn to an aging team and lack of depth. The offensive spark that Sandis Ozoliņš had provided m the upset against Slovakia was missing. Latvia went dovvn 1:4 against Germanv. The dream of moving m among the final eight Olvmpic hockey contenders died February 12. latvia had to settle for ninth place afler beating Ukraine 9:2 tvvo days later. But m the preceding days Latvia\'s hockey team captured the hearts of Latvians around the vvorld, mcluding many nevvfound fans m North America. Finally it vvas our turn to get pumped up and vvave the flag m homes and bars around the continent, to experience the hockey fever that m recent years has svvept latvia. Thanks to to the inanities of the National Hockey League\'s deal vvith the International 01ympic Committee that savv only some players released for the preliminary round, Latvia\'s team – particularly star goaltender Artūrs Irbe – vvere front and centrē m sympathetic North American media. NHL Commissioner Gary Bettman, m a tense exchange vvith Irbe, thretened to impose fines and suspensions if he dared play against the Slovaks even though the Carolina Hurricanes had given their permission. Ozoliņš and Kārlis Skrastiņš, Latvia\'s tvvo NHL blueliners, vvere forced to return after the tie vvith Slovakia and ironically, play against each other m a meaningless NHL game betvveen tvvo teams out of the play-off race. That same nighL latvia vvas being shut dovvn by Germanv They vvere sorely missed. Anyone m Canada or the United States vvho follovvs hockey novv knovvs about the Lavian hockey team. Vancouver Canucks NHL goaltender Pēteris Skudra hasn\'t been on speaking terms vvith the latvian Hockey Federation and media since the VVorld\'s m Norway. There is patclīing up to do. Sergejs Naumovs, vvho novv plays m the Svvedish Elite league and has played m Long Beach and San Diego m the minor pros, is better than the shaky start against the Slovaks vvould attesL but he\'s no SKIP THE SONG FESTTVAL AND GO WATCH HOCKEY By Viesturs Zariņš NHLer. But given Carolina\'s play this year, it\'s very likely that Irbe vvon\'t be available m this year\'s VVorld Campionships m Svveden and Naumovs vvill have to start. Even though hockey fever has gripped latvia since 1997 vvhen Latvia made it to the top international tier, many Latvians abroad have been ambivalenL Some complain that there are too many Russian surnames on the team, although most of the players can speak Latvian and some are m fact at least part latvian or descend from pre-War Russian families vvho lived m Latvia. Others say the sport is to rough, or it\'s not part of our heritage, or it\'s not cultural. Why, one vvriter m the Latvian nevvspaper Laiks a year or so ago even claimed hockey m latvia is a Russian plot because only the Russians can afford to buy equipment for their kids. This opinion was available on Latvians Online Opinions on 2/28/02 Viesturs Zariņš vvas born m England and lives m Canada vvith his family near Toronto. He is a former president of the Latvian National Federation m Canada. He also is chair of the Toronto Latvian School "Valodiņa". Professionally he is a director of information technology m a large finacial insritution. Zariņš has been a hockey fan since grovving up m the east end of Montreal and has follovved Latvian hockey since 1996.', 'With just 2 million citizens and just over 1000 hockey players, Latvia is thrilled every time one of its native sons cracks the big time And defenseman Karlis Skrastins of the Colorado Avalanche is one of the best contemporary examples. Of course, there are others. Helmut Balderis no longer had the speed that earned him the nickname of "Electric Train" by the time he played 26 games with the Minnesota North Stars in 1989-90, but the iconoclastic right wing from Riga proved he was still a proud competitor. More recently, goaltender Arturs Irbe and forward Sergei Zholtok prospered versus NHL competition, although Irbe now earns his living in the Austrian League and Zholtok tragically died while playing for Riga 2000 in November 2004. But Skrastins still soldiers on in the NHL today. The hard-hitting 6-1, 196-pound veteran is Skrastins embodies Latvian hockey pride currently the league\'s reigning ironman, with more than 400 consecutive games played. He also captained Latvia\'s 2005 HHP World Championship entry. The former Nashville Predators draft pick realizes that while his professional involvement is a source of pride to his fellow countrymen, what truly electrifies Latvia is the prospect of success at the top level of international competition in the 2006 Winter Games. "We are proud to be in the Olympics," said Skrastins. "It\'s a big, big deal for a small country like Latvia. It\'s going to be quite an experience. We\'re going to have a lot of new guys, and we\'ll be playing against big teams like the USA and Russia, with all NHL guys. It\'s a good thing for our players and fans. Everyone\'s looking forward to seeing how we do against those big guys." Hockey fans around the world remember the shocking upset Belarus achieved versus Sweden in the 2002 Olympic quarterfinals. But not everyone is aware that Latvia pulled off a similar feat in a must-win situation on home ice in Riga last year. On February 13,2005, Skrastins and his Latvian teammates rallied from a 4-2 deficit in Olympic qualification play to triumph 5-4 over (you guessed it) Belarus. The result secured Latvia\'s place in Torino 2006. "It was like a miracle," Skrastins said with a smile. "There was about four minutes left until the end of the game, and we almost all had our heads down. Then we scored a goal. It was a good thing we were playing at home, because the fans started getting louder and louder. Then we scored another goal and another, and everything had changed. Of course, we were very lucky at the time. But we tried to do our best, and we were happy to get into the Olympics. Latvia defeated the mighty Russians at the 2000 and 2003 11HF World Championships. Could similar upsets be in the oiling for the small Baltic nation at the 2006 Olympics? "It would be nice if we would beat some big teams," Skrastins admitted. "It\'s not our goal, but we\'re going to take it one game at a time. It\'s hockey. You never know what is going to happen. Maybe we can beat some big guys and get into the quarterfinals." Regardless of the final Olympic results, 2006 will be remembered as a banner year for Latvian hockey. The country\'s U-20 team participated in its first-ever lIHF World Junior Championship last month in the Canadian province of British Columbia, and despite finishing ninth out of 10 teams, players like goalie Ugis Avotins and captain Martin Karsums became fan favorites. In May, Riga will proudly host the 2006 lIHF World Championship, showcasing a new downtown arena with a capacity of 12,500. "I think people are going to like it," said Skrastins. "We have a very nice city in Riga. The Old Town in Riga is beautiful, full of culture. Everybody\'s welcome. We\'ve got crazy fans, but in a good way. Latvia will do the best it can do for our visitors." Lucas Aykroyd IIHF.com Olympic writer', 'With just 2 million citizens and just over 1000 hockey players, Latvia is thrilled every time one of its native sons cracks the bie time. ['Outcome of Latvian Elections May have Positive effect on Financial Market

The outcome of Latvia’s early general elections have not left any significant impact on the financial marķēt, but further developments might even have positive effects, because none of the possible coalition partners has raised objections to the privatization of state-ovvned companies and their listing on stock exchange, said number of securities experts told the Baltie

News Service. “I think that the result of the elections is beneficial to financial marķēts. We vvill be vvatehing further developments and the creation of the coalition. If it is made by the [center-right] Unity, the Zatlers Reform Party (ZRP) and the [nationalist conservative] National Alliance, it is good that these political parties do not rule out the privatization of those state-ovvned companies that vvould be better off at the hands of private ovvners,” Māris Rambaks, head of Brokerage Latvia at LHV, told BNS. According to the nevvsvvire, the expert also pointed out that Latvia’s outgoing parliament was much more influenced by the so-called oligarehs’ parties that could block many decisions for personai interests.

“If the coalition is set up by the three right-vving parties -- Unity, ZRP, and the National Alliance - the only question is, who vvill be the senior partner in this coalition. If it is ZRP, vvhich can start carrying out the reforms it has announced, it is necessary to bear in mind that any change of direction affects investors. Investors favor the situation vvhere there are eonerete plāns and goals that are attained. The more stable the government’s social and economic policy, the happier the investors,” Andrejs Martinovs, a board member of FinastaAsset Management, told BNS.

Martinovs also noted to BNS that none of the likely coalition partners had spoken against privatization in the run-up to the elections. “Assuming that these gentlemen are thinking about national interests and Latvia, selling off [state-ovvned] companies and listing their shares vvould be a good impulse for the development of the Latvian securities marķēt. A vvell developed securities marķēt, in turn, only tosters economic grovvth. In the long-term perspective, developing the securities marķēt vvould be even more important than patehing the budget deficit,” he vvas guoted

as saying.

According to BNS, the surveyed experts also pointed out that the elections in Latvia cannot leave any particular impact on selling Latvian government bonds on international marķēts. “Already the referendum on the dissolution of parliament shovved that these decisions have no major influence. The focus of international marķēts in on countries like Greece, vvhereas Latvia is just a trifle nobody worries about against this overall backdrop,” said Rambaks. BNS also reported that Martinovs said the financial marķēts could only be affected if the forming of Latvia’s new coalition drags on and uncertainties appear. This might affect both the price of Latvian bonds and the issue of new bonds.', 'Zatlers makes his political mark, but not without controversy Uldis Ozoliņš – August 12, 2011 On July 23, by a 94 percent majority, Latvian voters as expected supported the referendum instigated by ex-President Valdis Zatlers to dismiss the Saeima. They will return to the polls on Sept. 17 to elect a new parliament. This ended a truly unprecedented series of political events, and signalled the way for at least one new political force to take the stage in Latvian politics—Zatlers himself and his now many supporters. On the same day as the referendum, Zatlers officially founded his party. Curiously, by many commentators' estimation, he put his own persona in the party title: the Zatlers Reform Party (Zatlera Reformu partija, or ZRP). Such a person-driven approach seeks to capitalise on his popularity in dismissing the Saeima, which has rocketed Zatlers' personal approval to 79 percent—an unusual rating for any Latvian politician. As the referendum dust settles and Zatlers' political course becomes clearer, two radically different interpretations are put on his motivation to dismiss the Saeima. The first takes Zatlers at his word that he wanted basically to rid the Saeima of the influence of oligarchs who control several political parties and who had instigated a series of votes against the national interest, or who had in other ways resisted necessary policy implementation. The trigger for Zatlers' move was the Saeima's decision not to allow the Corruption Prevention and Combating Bureau (Korupcijas novēršanas un apkarošanas birojs) to search the home of "bulldozer" MP Ainārs Šlesers. The concern of Zatlers had been to restore a measure of democracy to the working of the Saeima. The leading party, Unity (Vienotība), sharing such sentiments, in fact then invited Zatlers to join it, but he declined in favour of founding his own. The second interpretation is far less favourable. When he triggered the referendum on May 28 by recommending the Saeima dismissal, Zatlers was faced with the likelihood that he would not be elected by the parliament to a second term. As it turned out, the Saeima appointed the financier Andris Bērziņš to the presidency. The dismissal of the Saeima thus is viewed more as an act of revenge. And Zatlers further declined to join Unity as it had stood staunchly against any idea of a coalition with the second-largest party in the Saeima, the Moscow-leaning Harmony Centre (Saskaņas centrs). Zatlers was looking for his own political advantage, being willing to go into coalition with anyone to secure his own power. Debate over Zatlers' motives will continue, but meanwhile his popularity with the electorate has not diminished. In the first party ratings since its founding, ZRP ranked first with Harmony Centre, both favoured by 17.5 perent of the electorate. It seems that Zatlers had taken voters away from all parties, even marginally from Harmony Centre. Unity—the largest party in the dismissed Saeima—slumped to 8.7 percent, while the pivotal Union of Greens and Farmers (Zaļo un zemnieku savienību) declined to 8.1 percent. The Union of Greens and Farmers is very much associated with oligarch and Ventspils Mayor Aivars Lembergs. Also in decline, though less sharply, was the National Alliance (Nacionālā apvienība), claiming 6.3 percent support. No other party came near to the 5 percent barrier required to sit in the Saeima. The great loser appears to be the oligarch Šlesers. His First Party of Latvia/Latvian Way (Latvijas Pirmā partija/ Latvijas Ceļš) had managed to get into the Saeima on previous occasions after blitz advertising campaigns, but support for the party has dropped to just 1.7 percent. On these figures, any kind of coalition is a possibility, either including or excluding Harmony Centre. Yet with more than a month to the elections, we may expect volatility as much as stability in predictions, as the heat is turned on Zatlers and his relative lack of political experience, and the very mixed bag of supporters who have flocked to his flag. An uncomfortable feeling comes precisely from the populist nature of his campaign so far, and the relative lack of substance in policy terms he has announced. While we are used to party platforms in Latvia often being very similar—so voters' decisions revolve around personalities—the search for power seems to override careful policy considerations, making ZRP perhaps just one more party devised for an individual leader, rather than being a genuine party representing stable interests. However, a further sense of discomfort comes from Zatlers' own background. Four years ago, he was an apolitical surgeon who was installed as president by the same oligarchs he now berates. Also in the background is his self-admitted taking of "envelopes" (bribes) as a surgeon, as is the common practice in most post-Soviet countries. This makes his claims to be against oligarchy or corruption ring somewhat hollow. Further, Zatlers' battle cry to get rid of oligarchs has taken on a somewhat bizarre life of its own, with a number of popular events including a "festival for buried oligarchs" (Oligarhu kapu svētki) arousing hilarity. Yet the real problems facing Latvia are not restricted to the activities of oligarchs, however odious. The painful road back to economic health in Latvia is hurting many. The country is experiencing depopulation as workers head abroad, and many small and medium enterprises are in trouble. The desire to repay loans to the International Monetary Fund and other lenders has led the government to slash wages and increase taxes. Latvia now enjoys a two-track economy. One part, largely concerned with transit and finance, is experiencing a boom, but the rest of the economy depressed. Yet the parties struggle to devise any policies to address these issues. Unity's insistence on repaying the debts, maintaining the value of the lat and tightening belts is being increasingly criticised as undermining any growth potential. And corruption is still widespread in administration, while the shadow (non-tax paying) economy remains strong. Not to be outdone, Harmony Centre's potential prime minister candidate, Nils Usakovs, has proposed a radical suggestion: Political parties should put aside all issues to do with nationality and ethnicity—citizenship, official state language, language in schools, etc.—for a period of three years so that they can concentrate on solving the nation's serious economic issues. Directed clearly at nationalist attempts to mobilise around issues such as having Latvian be the only language of instruction in schools, Ušakovs' approach may yet strike a chord among voters, though it is unlikely to be accepted by other parties, whose own economic credentials are often weak and for which national issues may sometimes be a useful pretext to mobilise support. Voters will be faced with more choice in these elections, but the substance of this choice will still be an open question as this intriguing battle intensifies.', "President invites Dombrovskis to form Latvia’s next government

Andris Straumanis

-Oct. 19, 2011

Latvian President Andris Bērziņš has nominated current Prime Minister Valdis Dombrovskis to form the country’s next government, but he also suggested that ali five poNtical parties elected to the Saeima be included the coalition.

Bērziņš made the invitation on Oct. 19 after meeting with representatives of ali parties, according to an announcementfrom the president’s press Office. The president’s invitation follovvs weeks of unsuccessful negotiations between the parties. The Zatlers Reform Party (Zatlera Reformu partija, orZRP), Unity (Vienotība) and the National Alliance (Nacionālā apvienība “Visu Latvijai!” - “Tēvzemei un Brīvībai/LNNK”) announced Oct. 14 that they had come to terms on a three-party coalition that would be backed by 56 of the parliament’s 100 members. But two days later six ZRP MPs broke away from the party, throvving the solidity of the coalition in doubt.

Efforts to include the other two parties have run into objections. When the reformist ZRP at first tried to create a government w 'th just the center-left Harmony Centre (Saskaņas Centrs), many voters protested that Harmony Centre’s pro-Russian štance cast doubt on its loyalty to Latvia. Efforts at broadening the coalition to include the centrist Unity and the right-wing National Alliance ran into trouble, too. The National Alliance has said it would not work with

Harmony Centre. Meanvvhile, ZRP leader and ex-president Valdis Zatlers has said his party would not work with the conservative Union of Greens and Farmers (Zaļo un Zemnieku savienība), which is seen as controlled by Latvia’s oligarchs. Even a last-minute effort to strengthen the three-party coalition by inviting Jānis Duklavs of the Greens and Farmers to take the minister of agriculture’s portfolio in the new government—vvhich would have earned the backing of his party’s 13 seats in the Saeima—was dropped after ZRP objected.

While Klāvs Olšteins and the other five MPs who split from Zatlers’ party have said they would backthe Oct. 14 coalition modei, the president has expressed his doubts.

Nontheless, Bērziņš said in his Oct. 19 announcement, the three-party coalition appears to be the only one that realistically could form the new government. At the same time, the president’s invitation to Dombrovskis does not prevent the parties from vvorking on an improved— and broader—modei, according to the press Office announcement. “The first serious tēst of the would-be coalition’s ability to take action vvill be the vote on the State budget for 2012,” the president said, “vvhich cannot become an experiment with the State and threaten the state’s intemational agreements.”

Once a coalition is assembled, Dombrovskis vvill have to present it to the Saeima for confirmation.

The new Saeima, elected Sept. 17, began work Oct. 17. Zatlers, who campaigned on ridding the parliament of the influence of oligarchs and on reforming govemment and politics, suffered his first defeat, losing in his bid to become speaker ofthe Saeima. Unity’s Solvita Āboltiņa, who served as speaker in the previous Saeima, was elected instead."]

Representative_Docs: ['THE SEVENTH SESSION OF THE SUPREME SOVIET OF THE USSR On August 3, the All-Union Supreme Soviet assembled in the Great Palace of the Kremlin for its seventh Session. The functions of this "parliament, " elected on one-slate tickets by 99. 80% of 144 million voters, consisted in rubber stamping decrees and decisions previously adopted by the Central Committee of the Communist Party. The All-Union Supreme Soviet is composed of two houses: the Soviet of Nationalities, represented by 750 delegates, and the Soviet of the Union, represented by 767 delegates. It is self evident that the sessions of these bodies proceeded in the spirit of complete unanimity, endorsing in the swiftest possible way every proposal submitted to them by the leadership of the USSR-CP. In fact, the time for "debates" was limited to two hours for each of the houses, and approximately five hours for their joint session. The Soviet of Nationalities was sitting from 10 A.M. to 12 noon; the Soviet of the Union from 12 to 2 P, M. ; but the All-Union Supreme Soviet permitted itself the luxury of holding two sessions, the first starting at 2 P.M., and the last at 5 P. M. But it was all over before dark, and presumably nobody was starved meanwhile. During this concentrated, though variant, one-day meeting, the Soviet "lawgivers" completed more business than a western parliament can hope to achieve in half a year. If there was anxiety in Party and Government circles about possible dissent of "opposition" candidates to endorsement of the decisions at the 23rd Congress of the USSR-CP or election of officers, there is no evidence of it in the TASS report (as published by Cina of August 4) from which we have derived the information here presented. The Sessions of August 3 allowed even time for an address by Party General Secretary Brezhnev, and a substantial report by Prime Minister Kosygin on questions of interior and foreign policy of the Soviet Union. Apart from castigating the United States for aggression in Viet-Nam, Kosygin was concerned about peace and security in Europe, on which he commented: The main precondition of safeguarding security and peace in Europe is recognition that the present boundaries in Eastern Europe are unalterable, which includes the Oder-Neisse line, and the frontiers between the two German States. Equally important is to bar West Germany from sharing nuclear power in any form. We still are looking for ways and means of peacefully solving the German question by general recognition of the two German States. It is of interest to note from the TASS report that while Brezhnev was applauded "stormily", Kosygin earned only "prolonged" applause, the rest of the speakers having to be content with ordinary applause. The importance of this Supreme Soviet session rests mainly on formal endorsement of changes in personnel, and the creation of permanent commissions on various subjects, numbering nine to each of the two houses. Of interest is the appointment of M. Suslov as chairman of the Foreign Affairs Committee of the Soviet of the Union; and appointment of J, Peive to the chair of the Committee for Education, Science, and Culture of the Soviet of Nationalities. Peive was the only Latvian, or Baltic representative assigned to chairmanship of a Supreme Soviet committee. This distinction, however, is actually a demotion, because he had served since 1958 as chairman of the Soviet of Nationalities and the Supreme Soviet. The TASS report provides sufficient information on the shifts in Baltic communist representation and the reasons for it. It started with the elevation of A. Pelse to the Moscow Politburo in April, 1966 (see LIB, July, 1966, p. 4, and also "More About Pelse and Voss" on page 7 of this issue). With this old Latvian Bolshevik having been returned to Moscow, further changes in the setup of the Baltic communist hierarchy were almost inevitable. Thus Latvian Peive was replaced as chairman of the Soviet of Nationalities by a Lithuanian, J. Paleckis, who also served as chairman of the meeting of the Supreme Soviet, a purely ceremonial assignment. The essential link in the chain of the various bodies composkig the Supreme Soviet is its Presidium, which traditionally centers on a president and 15 vice-presidents representing the federal Republics. On a suggestion of Brezhnev, the Ukrainian N. Podgorny, who is also a member of the Moscow Politburo, was unanimously elected President of the Presidium. He immediately was faced with the dilemma of eliminating Lithuania from the vice-presidency. According to TASS: No vice-president for Lithuania\'s SSR was elected at this session, because Lithuania\'s Supreme Soviet\'s chairman had been elected to serve as chairman of the Soviet of Nationalities of the USSR. Other developments of interest concerning the Baltic region were: The reappointment of J. Kalnberzins as vice-president of the Supreme Soviet for Latvia, and of A. Mirizepp for Estonia. The Supreme Soviet also confirmed V. Klauson, V. Rubenis, and M. Sumauskas as Prime Ministers of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, respectively. Most of these, though of Baltic descent, have spent the time between the two wars in Russia, and are now willing Moscow vehicles of promoting Russianization of their native stock. An exception to this pattern is Lithuanian Paleckis, who during independence of the Baltic States was a well-known newspaperman of liberal nationalistic leaning. Noteworthy oddity of the procedures of the ephemeral "parliament" was Podgorny\'s "suggestion" to elect, among others, Brezhnev and three outworn old Bolshevik hands--Budionny, Voroshilov and Mikoyan--to membership of the Presidium of the Supreme Soviet. Kosygin was confirmed as chairman of the Council of Ministers of the USSR. TASS also revealed that close to 75 per cent of the members of this Supreme Soviet served for the first time and that about 25 per cent of the delegates were women.', 'THE «97% MAJORITY" – AND HOW TO GET IT The Democratic world today is presumably aware that an "election" in the Soviet Union has nothing in common with an election in countries poverned by normal democratic rights and this "election" is neither free nor direct, neither secret nor general, since a large number of the voters are forcibly deprived of their balloting rights (particularly through removal from the Voters Register,as will be seen from this publication ). But very few people this side of the Iron Curtain know what technical means are used to achieve that the electoral participation is constantly about 98.8>a. Eoually little discussed and made clear to the people of the democratic countries is the masquerade that the powers in Moscow put on to conduct these "elections" also in occupied countries, only to lose little time afterwards in innovating the "el ection procedure" used in the Soviet Union. In newly occupied countries the election is initiated hurriedly since it is necessary to demonstrate to the outside world the justification of the "election": "It was the people\'s choice!" (For instance the Baltic States – were occupied June 15 – 17, and the elections were conducted already in July – in the course of less than a month!).lt will be remembered that Moscow sources have often referred to this "election" directed by its own agents. The following description of the election procedure is based on examined and corroborated material from one of the Baltic countries – Latvia, although the ?roceedings  idenical in all the occupied countries. In Latvia the masquerade of the invaders from Moscow is directed by A.Vishinsky.lmmediately following the Red Army\'s entry into Riga a "new Latvian Government" was created, which in its declaration to the people stated that "it is the firm conviction of the government that the unshakeable friendship of the great Soviet Union toward the Latvian Republic will continue to be a genuine and enormous influence which will assure the independence of Latvia". In order to confirm this still further the new Minister of the Interior made a radio speech in which he stressed that "laws will be drafted to safeguard the immunity of man* s person and the property of the citizen". The new election was announced – "on the basis of the State constitution of independent Latvia through general, equal, secret and proportional ballot? wherwby the Central Electoral Commission was to number the voting papers according t"o the order of All non-communist sections of the people and of political groups (except the social- democrats) had bound themselves in unheard-of unity to draw up the "nomination list for the democratic citizens". The nomination lists were faultlessly drawn up. The united non – communist groups were absolutely assured, of victory over the bolshevik bloc, even though the groups no longer had access to newspapers, which were already in the hands of the bolsheviks, as also the radio, rights to assemble,and the constabulary force. The Central Election CommiBsioh was already completely bolshevized. It was clear that something was not quite in order with Vishinsky* s calculations, or the local Moscow agents had painted the election prospects of the bloc too rosily, about which Vishinsky himself could not be quite certain, as reports reached him: "on reactions in the newly occupied land".
