A. ABERS Baltic peoples under the sway of great powers DR. A. BILMANIS Peace treaties and foreign relations of Latvia especially with the Soviet Russia BALTIC PEOPLES UNDER THE SWAY OF GREAT POWERS SIGNIFICANCE OF THE PEOPLES. You can read many books or treatises on the so-called Baltic Problem, while remaining in complete ignorance as to the peoples inhabiting the Baltic coastlands and their place in history. It has been the manner of many a great historian to treat the Baltic countries with regard merely to the interests and struggles of some big powers to gain control over the territories, and to disregard the native population as if irrelevant in the great processes of the past. However, we feel it is a mistake to discuss Baltic history as a mere competition of neighboring countries in a vacuum of peoples, an approach which cannot give a clear picture of the real course of history. After the World War I, three republics—Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania—were established in this territory. This was only made possible because three different peoples had preserved their individuality and self respect. There is still more from a general point of view,—the eastern border line of these three peoples: Estonians, Latvians or Letts, Lithuanians, always remained the frontier of western culture, a boundary which divided the spheres of influence,—the Roman Catholic and Protestant Church on this side, the Greek Orthodox Church on the other side. If we look for some explanation of this fact, it may be found not only in the geopolitical situation but in the tendencies and minds of these peoples. Since the eighteenth century the Latvians and Estonians were subjected to the Russian crown as well as to the German barons, landlords of the great estates, who enforced a severe serfdom of the peasants. Nevertheless they held out both physically and mentally. Prof. A.ABERS If Baltic coastlands have never been Russian settled in spite of Russian rule lasting for two centuries and in spite of being surrounded by territories peopled by Russians, for a thousand years and more, then there should be no doubt as to the vitality of the Baltic peoples. Having a culture very different from that of the Russians they were hard grains which could not be melted in the big kettle of the Russian Empire. It seems that the higher culture of Latvians and Estonians was the decisive factor which caused the ruling German nobility to refrain from importing Russian peasants into their Baltic estates even after the Great Northern War and the plague had decimated the population in Livonia and Estonia and the rural estates felt a tremendous lack of manpower. Since the thirteenth century the Baltic states, except for Lithuania, were dominated by conditions which aimed at the destruction of the vitality and cultural activities of the native peoples. But they stood fast in a successful defence of their own language, folklore, customs and ways of life. An infiltration of German, Polish, Swedish and Russian into the Latvian and Estonian languages took place during a period seven centuries. Nevertheless, the main root of these two languages remained intact, and was preserved in folk-songs, fairy tales and popular speech. Since the middle of the nineteenth century these popular tongues served to build up the modern languages of Latvian, Lithuanian and Estonian for normal use in every-day life, in journalism, in science, as well as in literature. Along with the preservation of their languages, the Baltic peoples maintained the ideal of their right to independence. They kept in memory and legends the encouraging story of their former independence prior to the German invasion of Latvia and Estonia during the thirteenth century and prior to the Lublin Union of Lithuania with Poland in 1569. A national awakening took place during the second half of the nineteenth century, when a liberalisation of the Russian Empire policy provided opportunities for sons of Baltic peasants to receive a higher education and to obtain better positions. Many a Latvian and Estonian, upon entering a university, or after graduation, devoted much of his energies to the development of the language, education and literature of his peoples, and to the improvement of the press and of economic production. The rise in numbers of the indigenous town population was remarkable, and the cities of Riga, Jelgava, Tartu, Tallinn, became cultural centers not only for the ruling German minority but for the Baltic peoples themselves. Thus, out of the Baltic peasantry arose two selfconfident nations, having rural and town population with their different social classes. In Lithuania the development of the modern nation was delayed by certain specific measures of the Russian regime. "For more than a century (1795—1915)," according to T. Norus and J. Zilius ("Lithuania\'s Case for Independence") "the Lithuanians plodded along under Russia\'s heavy yoke. Previously exhausted by the lawlessness, oppression and disorder which accompanied the union with Poland, translated from that regime to one hundred years of attempted denationalization on the part of the Russian bureaucracy, it would be small wonder if the Lithuanian national aspiration were somewhat enfeebled, yet the country seems to have thrived on adversity. The Lithuanian renaissance commenced with the year 1904 to 1905, coincident with the Russo-Japanese War, and the first Russian Revolution, when freedom of the press was once more restored to the people, and the sphere of their liberties was widened. The ecclesiastics, intelligentsia, and the large majority of the Polonized nobility having turned again to the Lithuanian language, the intellectual classes forwarded the work of the education and culture of the people with an energy and enthusiasm astonishing to witness." THE WAR OVER THE BALTIC. During the Great Northern war (1700—1721) when Sweden and Russia were contending for the position of the big power on the Baltic Sea, Russia was not interested in conquering the then Swedish provinces of Estonia and Latvia. Peter the First invaded them merely to weaken the Swedish war potential in that area. This invasion, however, disclosed the Asiatic ferocity and cruelty of the Russian troops, when Russia had no plans for the future utilization of the inhabitants, or the valuables of the conquered country. The Russian Commander-in-Chief, count Sheremetiev, with the forces at his command in 1705 had already completed the task set by czar Peter. He wrote that except for the cities of Riga, Tallin and Parnu, there was nothing more left to destroy in Livonia and Estonia, since everything had been turned into desert. A Livonian chronicle has described the inroad of Russian troops in Latvia in 1702 in the following way: "They committed atrocities of all sorts among the inhabitants and murdered men, women and children alike, or deported them into captivity. One of the greatest disasters was that many hundreds of young children were loaded into carts like goslings and led away to the Tartary" A savage pillaging of Latvia and Estonia was continued until 1708, and in these eight years (1701—1708) the Russian armies attained the same calamitous results in the Baltic as were accomplished in Germany during the entire Thirty Years\' War, which had been characterized as the most horrible and the most destructive of all modern wars. A general description of the latter is equally apt when applied to Latvia and Estonia during 1709/10. After the battle of Poltava (1709) czar Peter of Russia perceived that the opportunity had come ta annex Estonia and Latvia. Thus in 1710, these countries were afflicted by two great misfortunes at the same time: by Russian occupation and by the plague, which swept the country during this period. The Baltic peasants regarded both of them as punishment from a wrathful God for their sins. The census of 1711 showed that about 120.000 Latvian and Estonian peasants in the Russian occupied territory had died of the plague, this number forming the majority of all the peasantry there. Nobody could count the cultural and material damage and losses caused by the great catastrophe of the war and the "Black Death." Should this dreadful beginning of the Russian domination over Baltic countries be regarded as a satisfactory reason for justifying it? There are many facts disclosing that the sympathies of the native Latvian and Estonian population unvariably were on the side of Swedish administration. They supported the Swedish cause but were defeated, together with the King of Sweden. THE VICTORY OF DESPOTISM. Actually Estonia and "Livonia" (including the northern part of Latvia and southern part of Estonia) passed the Russian administration upon the capitulation of Riga, Tallinn and Parnu in 1710. Afterwards the Russian mastery was ratified by the Peace Treaty of Nystadt (1721), a treaty which confirmed by means of official documents that Sweden had lost the war and was compelled to cede its Baltic provinces to Russia. The Estonian and Latvian peoples had to remain silent. They were only the unwilling frames for Peter\'s "window on Europe.\' "For the first time in their history, two Western European nations, the Latvians and the Estonians, were incorporated into the Eeastern barbarian world, which Peter the Great himself wanted to Europeanise in order to increase the war potential of his empire. He tried to force Western civilization on his people by the whip and the gallows." (A. Schwabe, The Story of Latvia and her Neighbours.) The Western nations—Poles, Saxons and Danes—had aided Peter and Russia to defeat Sweden, and so had the Baltic nobility which was of German origin and nationality. The emergence of Russia as a great military power on the Baltic was the final result of the war, and one which was most unpleasant for the Western nations. Only the German nobility of the Baltic coutries was quite satisfied with the latest change of overlordship. Under Peter and his successors the noble landlords—the Baltic barons— recovered all the privileges over the peasants and estates which they had partly lost under the Swedish government. In conformity with the despotic form of Russian government and the vast autonomy of Russian nobility, the Baltic barons could govern in their manors like little despots, and, no doubt, in the great majority of cases they were not enlightened despots. Theoretically the privileges of the nobles in Estonia and Livonia were based on confirmations given by the Russian czar in 1710 and 1712. The main idea of those Russian confirmations was granting privileges in the widest extent enjoyed previously at some time or other by the German landlords in the Baltic countries under other—Teutonic Order\'s, Bishops, Swedish—administration. Peter the Great and his successors fulfilled his promises that all the privileges of landlords, not acknowledged, or taken away by the Swedish King, were restored. The contrast therefore between Swedish and Russian overlordship in Baltic countries was very sharp. While the second part of the Swedish period was called by Latvian and Estonian peasants "the good Swedish time," the opposite was true in relation to the first -century of Russian domination. Actually the privileges of Baltic barons were based on the whole governmental system in the Russian Empire which was a despote monarchy. The behavior of noblemen toward their peasants was generally as tyrannical as was the conduct of the Czar or Emperor toward his subjects at large. The autocracy and oppression was imposed and backed up by ruthless and cruel punishments. Some Asiatic punitive methods were introduced in Latvia and Estonia because of the influence of the Russian\'s semi-barbaric social atmosphere. The opposition between nobility and peasantry was growing with the upward of economic exploitation connected with social tyranny and terror. It reached its height in the eighteenth century when the landlords began calling the peasants their slaves. The German barons were not reluctant to use such a term showing some ambition to see themselves owners of slaves. That happened at the same time that J. J. Rousseau published his works, so greatly admired in Europe, accentuating that all men are free by birth and cannot be legally deprived of their liberty. The writers of the eighteenth century recognized the peasants\' conviction that through servitude he had been robbed of his natural rights. Moreover he was firmly convinced that it was not his duty to serve the landlord who, in actual fact, was to be blamed for the bad conditions. It was by small forces only that the landlords kept the common folk in obedience. It was Russian troops and Russian bayonets which supported the German barons in their despotism over Latvian and Estonian peasants. In the fifteenth century, the status of the peasants was changed from that of rent-payers into that of rural serfs bound to a certain manor, because the landlords were anxious to assure the necessary manpower for cultivation of domain lands. The number of peasants had been badly reduced as a consequence of war and pestilence. After the Great Northern War the conditions of the peasants deteriviated into a kind of servitude. One reason for this was a further reduction of population because of war and plague, in addition to the despotic influences infiltrating from Russia. The most important contact point with Russian nobility was for the German barons the Court of the Russian Emperor in St. Petersburg. The landlords exerted full control over their subjects in returning the fugitives, in selling peasants without their lands and without their families, in flogging and jailing them. And if a serf was whipped and tortured to death, there was actually no possibility for his kin to obtain a decision against the landlord. There existed a close cooperation between the German nobility in the Baltic provinces and the higher Russian administration and judicial institutions of the Empire. The landlords\' attitude to their subjects was censured by an enlightened fellow-lords, in 1818, as follows: "You landlords of Livonia, you are not much better than the illfamed Oriental satraps notwithstanding your imaginary refinement and your illusory education of which you boast in foreign countries. You are masters of slaves whose human rights you have been annihilating..." Indeed all the wealth of landlords, their noble manorhouses, their gorgeous parks and gardens, their splendid feasts and hunting parties were founded upon the extreme misery and overwork of their unhappy peasantry. Under such terrible conditions, the chances for the Latvian and Estonian peoples to make a decent living were practically lost or at least very small. There seemed to be no sense in working, for the more the peasant worked, the more the landlord demanded from him. However, the inborn diligence made the peasants strive and toil upon their farms, not loosing hope that better days would come in some distant future. The inherent sense of order and beauty bid them use every opportunity for improvement on their own houses and gardens. In spite of all the injustice suffered daily for centuries, their innate sense of justice and love of family-life made them save the moral foundations so necessary for a harmonious society. Subject to the severest servitude and despotism, they never lost their love for freedom, the ardent desire to restore their liberty. THE EMANCIPATION OF THE BALTIC PEASANTRY. With the locking of the Baltic countries into the Russian sphere of domination, these peoples were subjugated to the same despotic conservatism as were the Russians all the time before. The Russian domination over Estonia and western Latvia was won, as we have seen already, in 1710, at a heavy price, that is, the previous laying waste of the new province during the Great Northern War. Conquest by Russia of the whole country was completed by the annexation of eastern Latvia (Latgale) in 1772, one part of Lithuania in 1793, the rest of Lithuania and the Duchy of Courland (the southwestern part of Latvia) in the year 1795, with the partitions of Poland. Thus the territory of the Baltic peoples consisted of many different parts so far as religion, language, history and culture in general were concerned. One of the striking consequences of the 210 years under the continuous Polish reign in eastern Latvia (1562 to 1772) has been the domination of the Church of Rome both there and in Lithuania right up to the present day. In reverse, the Swedish administration did all in its power to consolidate Protestantism in Estonia (1561 to 1710) and Latvia (1621 to 1710), and so did the Dukes of Courland (1562 to 1795). Therefore, the Estonians and the great majority of Latvians are still Protestants, but the Lithuanians are Roman Catholics. Since the Russian administration respected the ancient privileges and the territorial autonomy of the nobles in the Baltic countries, this overlordship involved the maintenance of the different laws and customs peculiar to each district of Latvia, Estonia, Lithuania. The partition, which had been established during different political period, continued, due to the fact that certain institutions of the districts and provinces were taboo for the upper classes of the semifeudal society here, that is to say, for the German and Polish nobility. Thus, for example, the northern part of Estonia (previously Swedish Estonia from 1561) was administered as a Russian "government of Estonia," but the southern part of Estonia together with northern Latvia was administered as a "government of Livonia" (previously Swedish Livonia from 1621). This introduction has been necessary to explain the different courses of the peasants\' emancipation in the various parts of the Baltic countries during the nineteenth century. First of all a so-called Bill of Emancipation was enacted for the "government of Estonia" in 1816. However, the personal freedom was not accompanied by any economic advantages for the peasants. "On the contrary ,they were subordinated more than ever to the economic power of the nobility due to the fact that all land belonged to the landowning noblemen, who were in a position to dictate the terms of rent, which was paid mostly in labor. The nobility could only boast of itself as a benefactor and represent itself as the bearer of the liberal and humane ideas of the epoch." (E.Rink) The "emancipation bills" in the governments of Courland and Livonia directly followed those of the province of Estonia, for all these territories together formed the so-called Baltic Provinces of the Russian Empire. All three territories were under the domination of German landlords who pretended to be the most civilized amidst the nobles of the Empire. In Lithuania, however, and in Latgale (Eastern Latvia) the Emancipation was only a part of the general processes in the Empire, accomplished from 1861 to 1863. Although the "emancipation bills" for the Baltic Provinces were enacted during the years 1816 to 1819, yet in reality they did little good and actually did much harm to the interests of the peasantry there. The real emancipation was started only by the legislation of 1849, 1860 and the years immediately following a short time before the period of the great social reforms by which the Lithuanian and the Russian peasantry was set free. The above story clearly shows the lead of Western Europe in the emancipation of the peasants and in the extension of human rights to the common people. For example, in France the entire peasantry secured its freedom through the Great Revolution (1789-1793), in Poland the abolition of serfdom was decreed at firft in 1794, while in Prussia the emancipation of rural serfs was carried through in 1810. Fate thus made it difficult for Estonians, Latvians, and Lithuanians, to whom the opportunity for economic and cultural progress came, at least half a century later than to other peoples of the Western family of nations. That was the unfavorable consequence of Baltic peoples\' incorporation into the Russian Empire, as it was a semibarbarian, despotic, reactionary state. It was not a federation of nations but a "prison for peoples.". In the first half of the nineteenth century the development of Russia and its annexed provinces was particularly impeded by the despotic and reactionary regime of czar Nicholas L In his reign Russia became a "frozen country," sealed from all contacts with the outside world. Under such circumstances therefore, until the second half of the nineteenth cent iry, the landlords of the Baltic Provinces still held three great advantages of an austere serfdom in their hands. Firstly, they had their domain lands cultivated by the peasants; secondly, they practised flogging of peasants; thirdly, they were the real tyrants of all rural life, equipped with judicial powers and aided by police. There existed no free self-governing communities of peasantry, but the same oldfashioned manors and manorial system. Thus we must conclude that emancipation of peasants and other liberal reforms in the Baltic countries were delayed for about half a century only because of their subjugation under the reactionary Russia. BETWEEN TWO MILL-STONES. From the middle of the nineteenth century, improved economic conditions rendered it possible for a certain part of the Latvian and Estonian population to send their sons to schools and to the university. The universities were either German, as in Tartu (Dorpat), or they were Russian, as in Moscow or in St. Petersburg. The educated people then were either Germans, as was found in the Baltic Provinces, or they were Russians, as was found outside the Baltic, in the public administration of the Russian Empire. The languages used in press and in administrative matters were Russian, German, or French, which was used in the Imperial Court and among the more educated nobility. Under such circumstances it was only natural that Latvians and Estonians after receiving a higher education and obtaining good positions, should be assimilated by the Germans or Russians who were their colleagues, and who formed the great majority of the upper classes of society and bureaucracy. In Lithuania it happened similarly with the educated Lithuanians who were absorbed into the polonized upper classes of society. However, there appeared some vigorous, courageous people who asserted that they remained Latvians, Estonians, or Lithuanians regardless of having received a higher education in other languages. This was the more surprising as these courageous men risked their personal future and often failed to get a good position in their native land or even in the whole Russian Empire. They sometimes were subjected to persecution, as the German administration and police was very alert to combat the movement of "New Latvia" and similar tendencies in Estonia. The administration fought it by censorship and all other means possible. The German influence at the Imperial Court was great also and it was used against the national aspirations of the Baltic peoples. Some of the native patriots were exiled, for example, several Latvians in 1863. Nevertheless, their adherence to the movement was growing. What was the cause for the rise of such national heroes, and what were the prospects for the achievement of their goals? Obviously the cause was not a desire to obtain personal profit, since the easiest way for that was to become absorbed into the German or Russian ruling classes. The movement itself, formed by these courageous men defending their natural rights and developing the culture of their own people, was called "national renaissance." But the rise of this movement cannot be explained by mere foreign influences, such as: "New Germany," "New Italy" and the like. The real cause was, on the one hand, the vitality of the people reflecting in their better educated men, the ability of the popular culture to survive in changed circumstances and in sublime forms. On the other hand, the cause for the patriotic obstinacy of prominent Latvians and Estonians was the historical and actual conflict of the native peasantry with the German nobility, which echoed in the minds of the educated members of the population. Of course, the leaders of the national renaissance had to answer many questions as to the prospects and possible achievements of a movement inspired by their activities. The three Baltic peoples were small, they possessed insufficient strength for fighting Russia and thus obtaining their independence. The leaders therefore were realistic enough to choose ends which could be considered obtainable under the existing political circumstances. Firstly, they regarded as their goal furthering the welfare of their people; developing on this base schools and different cultural institutions, with a resultant improvement in the cultural values. Ancient Greece was their guide and model of a small nation with high culture and refinement. Secondly, they regarded as their goal; breaking the despotism of the Baltic barons and generally annihilating the German supremacy in the Baltic Provinces, by the help of Russian liberals or even in alliance with the Slavophiles. Thus fighting the German oligarchy, the national leaders of the Latvians and the Estonians hoped to encourage their people to become sufficiently self-determined so that instead of German elements they might be able to install individuals and bodies of their own people in the Baltic. The greatest leader of Latvian national renaissance was Christian Valdemars (1825-1891) who was very eager to provide for better sources of income for Latvian and Estonian peoples. Therefore, he not only tried to open doors to landownership as a firm basis for welfare and higher education, but encouraged his people to turn to navigation and shippingtrade. He was supported by the Russian administration in founding naval schools near the coast of the Baltic Sea, and there many Latvian and Estonian sailors were trained. Valdemars was instrumental in establishing Latvian merchant shipping and a merchant fleet. On the other side, he succeeded in establishing a Latvian newspaper for spreading the ideas of the national renaissance; the paper was published in St. Petersburg (1862-1865) with the support of some higher Russian functionaries who were not in favor of the excessive German privileges in the Baltic Provinces. Whereas this struggle against the traditional German power in the Baltic produced strong assistance on the part of Russian nationalists, the failure of the attempts at Russianization, was caused partly by the popular renaissance. What was an ally at the beginning, became an enemy at the end, seeking a complete assimilation of the Baltic coastlands, extermination of the native tongues and conversion\'of Protestants or Catholics to the Greek Orthodox Church. These Russianization tendencies frightened one group of Baltic nationals into a reactionary movement seeking close liaison with the Germans as representatives of Western Civilization and Lutheranism. The description by E. Rink of what happened in Estonia, applies equally well to Latvia. ("A Short Review of the History of Estonia, "p. 4): "The Germans, who directed the education of the population, made belated attempts to Germanize the Estonian people. But their measures were rather weak compared with the steps taken by the Russian government to suffocate the national movements in the Baltic countries... In order to realize the Russianization policy, the administration, the schools and the courts of justice were taken over entirely by the Russian bureaucracy. Russian was introduced everywhere as the official language. The instruction in the schools had to be carried out in Russian also, though the children coming to school did not understand a single word of this language." The Russianization policy in Latvia and Estonia was started by the czar Alexander 111 (from 1881). In Lithuania, the Russianization policy was started much earlier( in 1864). When turning to the Russianization policy in Latvia, the following circumstances must be noted. With the reign of czar Alexander 111 a new pressure was exerted on religious life by the Greek Orthodox Church. Nobody was allowed to renounce his or her allegiance to this Church. And if one member of a married couple was Orthodox, their children had to be baptized in the Orthodox Church. Lutheran pastors were punished for neglecting these regulations. In spite of this coercion, the great majority of Latvians obstinately remained Lutheran, disregarding all the advantages they could gain in public service through their adherence to the "Russian Church." The most noticable compulsion directed against the masses of people in order to forward Russianization was the enforcing of the Russian language in all schools as the only tongue allowed in education. From 1887 it was made obligatory, not only in the public schools, supported by the state or communities, and in the private elementary and highschools, since 1889. The pressure against pupils was almost unbearable, for they were punished even if they spoke their mother tongue in school apart from lessons. To avoid failure, the Russian government took care to install Russian teachers and inspectors in the Baltic schools and their administrative bodies. THE GROWTH OF REVOLUTION. After it became clear that it was no use relying on the Russian acuiiinistration in the struggle against German barons, since the Russians in turn demanded renunciation from the native tongue and historical forms of culture; the movement of national renaissance began to loose its popularity and its impetus. Miscalculations and difficulties in maintaining a stable policy also contributed its decreasing popularity. It was also perfectly clear that the Imperial Crown would never solve the agrarian problems of the Baltic peasants since the Russian monarchy was firmly based on the sympathies of the landowning aristocracy which was also oppressing the Latvian and Estonian peoples. Out of this time of indecision a new Western movement was born which appealed to the wider masses of Baltic peoples—socialism as a movement against the ruling classes. As the latter were, in the Baltic countries, mainly Germans or Poles, the national aspirations of Latvians, Estonians, and Lithuanians to liberate themselves from alien rule and despotism, could quite naturally be allied with the claims of socialism to overthrow the monarchy as well as to annihilate the relics of feudalism within the rural society. This socialistic theory was therefore all the more acceptable to the masses since it turned also against Russianization as one form of the despotism exerted by the Czarist government. And the socialist teachings seemed to show the small peoples a tremendous new source of strength—the solidarity of all oppressed and subjugated peoples throughout the world. Under such conditions it is easy to understand why the great Latvian poet J. Rainis (1865-1929), while being a true representative of the best desires of the Latvian people as a whole, was at the same time a partisan of socialist doctrines and international solidarity. He, also, was the leader in editing a new Latvian newspaper in 1894 to 1897, disseminating democratic and socialist teachings. It is true that the number of laborers was increasing in Latvia and with this increase the social basis ready to accept socialist ideas, was also expanded. It would be quite wrong, however, to imagine that the growth of revolutionary movement in Latvia was due only to the increase in the members of the working classes. The movement of the so-called "New Stream" in Latvia though basically adopting socialist teachings from Western Europe, at first put forward a democratic program for the near future. The latter included liquidation of private ownership by Baltic barons of the big estates; a democratically elected self-government of communities; liberation of the Church from the patronage of the German landlords; and use of the Latvian language in schools, courts of justice and administration. The majority of the Latvian people was strongly in support of such a program, and there was no longer any great difference between a farmer and his agricultural laborers, or the farm-hands hired by a German baron on his large estate,— they all stood behind their common leaders fighting their common enemies:—the privileged German classes in the Baltic and the reactionary Russian administration. Latvians as one united people were struggling for their human rights, for their freedom and for democracy, but most of all for the right to their own land and property, and for independence from the "Barons." In 1897, when open activity proved to be dangerous and many popular leaders were imprisoned, Latvians developed underground activities, especially in socialist and marxian circles, which later were organized into a great party, in 1904, —the Latvian Socialist Democratic Workers\' Party. This Party was the leading force during the Revolution of 1905 in Latvia. "Whatever was the aim and character of this Revolution in Russia, in the Baltic it definitely had an agrarian character, and it was the biggest and most impressive of a series of pcs ant risings, of which Baltic history is so fu11... The main grievance that had aroused the spirit of the peasants was the disproportionate distribution of land. The Latvians felt it a sacrilege and great social injustice that side by side with a large landless population, 48 per cent of the land should be held by 1250 landowners who, on top of everything, were the hated German barons. Only 40 per cent of the land was in the hands of Latvian peasant small-holders, who were very heavily burdened by mortgages and various feudal restrictions." (Dr. A. Schwabe, "The Story of Latvia and Her Neighbours," p. 23.) 1905 was significant in Latvian history as the year of Revolution, which broke out at this time because of the international solidarity of masses within the Russian Empire. This was proof that the Latvian Socialist-Democratic Party really stood for unity of the people. As soon as the people of Latvia heard of tragic events in St. Petersburg that hundreds of Russian workers had been shot by troops on January 9, 1905, the Party announced a general strike which in turn led to demonstrations during which some workers participating in processions in Riga, the capital of Latvia, were shot by Russian troops. There were over 40 persons killed and about 200 wounded. This first important action in a long series of revolutionary clashes in Latvia occurred on January 13, 1905. The Revolution was started by the working-class in a big city, but afterwards the majority of clashes occurred in the country, where the Latvian peasantry met together and selected their representatives to organize a new order in agreement with other communities and their representative bodies, and in complete of disregard the authority of the German landed aristocracy. When the landed gentry perceived the dangerous situation, they asked for help from the Russian government. They were allowed to form special police units who could dispose of Russian troops stationed in the country. Under the orders of such "honor police," the troops carried out many arrests and tortured the suspects as to their participation in the revolutionary movement. Such dispositions and conduct were a new stimulus which roused much sharper steps on the part of mutineers, directed against the country-seats of landlords as well as against the public institutions of the Russian administration. The rebels started by burning the noble country-seats and castles of the German barons. The German "honor police" and Russian troops were expelled from the country-side and driven away to towns and cities. The revolutionaries even occupied some towns though this was achieved through the loss of many killed and wounded. If the Russian revolution of 1905 had been more successful the result of the Latvian Revolution also would have been successful. But on the end the Russians failed, and therefore the Government was able to transfer more troops to Latvia and Estonia. There Russian "punitive expeditions" were able to restore the old order and executed hundreds and thousands of revolutionaries from 1905 to 1906. PROSPERITY AND COLLAPSE. The Revolution of 1905 was not a complete failure. True, the monarchy remained, the old order was restored, the revolutionaries were executed, punished, or exiled, or fled. However, a Constitution was issued by the Czar, and there were elections of a State Council, which was empowered to propose bills for enactment or refusal by the Emperor. Besides that, it was empowered to demand answers from the Imperial ministers. Latvians also had the opportunity to send some representatives to the State Council. Even the Russianization policy was a little weakened in the Baltic, at least during the years immediately after the Revolution. But the general prosperity rapidly increasing in the period between the year 1905 and the World War I stood in no causal nexus to the Revolution. It was merely the consequence of the development of capitalistic economy with the aid of foreign investments. Whereas Latvia and Estonia had some very important sea-ports and cities at favorable spots for industrial enterprises and mills, as the cities of Riga, Liepaja (Libau), Ventspils (Windau), Tallinn (Reval), Narva, the foreign investments were located more in the Baltic coastlands than in Russia itself. Besides, the above-mentioned harbors were used by western Russia, as outlets for exportation of agricultural products and lumber. With the development of factories and foreign trade, the town population increased very rapidly. For example, Riga reached the remarkable number of 517,000 in 1913. Likewise the development of agriculture was being fostered by special education, by Latvian agricultural societies, and by the demand by the increased town population for rural produce. There was an upward trend in the welfare of both the town and the rural population. Nonetheless unrest was rife among the working classes on account of the long work hours, and the majority of the people had not given up the goals for which they had fought in the Revolution of 1905. A rather sudden end was put to the prosperity of Latvia by the World War I. It was clear then, that the Russian army which had been strong enough to surpress revolutions within the country, was not strong enough to defend the citizens of the Russian Empire against the German aggressor. German forces had partly occupied Latvian territory since 1915, but the entire country of Latvia was occupied in 1917. The collapse of prosperity hit the population regardless of whether they remained under invading German forces, or sought refuge in Russia. The people remaining within the occupied area were liable to have their property confiscated and were hungry, the people seeking refuge in Russia were striken by the usual poverty of refugees. The number of Latvian refugees during the World War I has been estimated at about 800,000 persons. In spite of Latvian dissatisfaction with the Russian government, they stanchly defended their country against German forces, and when the German army approached, the civilian population of Latvia left their homes and fled. The explanation for this behavior was.to be found in the long experience which Latvians had had with German ruling classes in the Baltic. Now, when the German compatriots came from Germany, what was expected by Latvian peasants, especially after the Revolution of 1905? They expected revenge of the part of German barons, appropriation of peasants estates and settlement by Germans. Representative_Docs: ["Canada gets new Latvian ambassador Latvians Online – July 20, 2011 Latvia's new ambassador to Canada is scheduled to receive his letter of accreditation July 21 during a ceremony with President Andris Bērziņš in Rīga, according to the president's press office. Juris Audariņš, the new ambassador, since 2004 has been the special assignments ambassador to the Latvian diaspora. He will replace Marģers Krams, who has been Latvia's ambassador to Canada since 2007. Audariņš began working in the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in 1992. Before being put in charge of diaspora affairs, from 2001-2004 he was Latvia's consul general in St. Petersburg, Russia. He also has been a counselor in Latvia's embassy in Italy and has served as assistant state secretary in the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Krams will become the special assignments minister for the European Union directorate, according to the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. The Latvian Embassy is located in Ottawa.", 'MINISTER McDOUGALL ANNOUNCES OPENING OF TALLIN OFFICE, $ 1 MILLION IN HUMANITARIAN ASSISTANCE TO BALTIC STATES. The Secretary of State for External Affairs, the Honourable Barbar McDougall, today ( November 21, 1991 ) announced the official opening of the Office of the Canadian Embaesy in Tallin, Estonia. The assistance to Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. "Canada\'s renewed partnership with the Baltic States is flourishing and I hope to announce the opening of Embassy offices in Riga and Vilnius as well," said Mrs. McDougall. "The new offices will play a very important role in our expanding relations with these three countries." The Minister noted that Canada\'s Embassy in Stockholm is accredited to Latvia and Lithuania and the Embassy in Helsinki to Estonia. Embassy officials will provide trade and investment promotion assistance as well as visa and consular services through the new offices. The $ 1 million in humanitarian assistance will be channelled through the Canadian Red Cross for delivery of medical supplies and other assistance to Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia this winter. The Canadian Red Cross welcomes cash donations from Canadians to this program at any of it 3 branch offices. November 21, 1991', 'MCDOUGALL ANNOUNCES OPENING OF RIGA OFFICE, AGRICULTURAL ASSISTANCE FOR BALTIC STATES. The Secretary of State for External Affairs, the Honourable Barbara McDougall, today ( December 13, 1991 ) announced the official opening of the Office of the Canadian Embassy in Riga, Latvia. The Minister also announced a $ 2.7-million technical assistance project in the agricultural sector for the three Baltic States. These funds are part of a $25-million technical assistance fund announced by Prime Minster Brian Mulroney at the London Summit of the Croup Of Seven ( G-7 ) in July. "The opening of our office in Riga is another sign of the healthy and growing relations between Canada and the Baltic States," said Mrs. McDougall. "Canada opened a similar office in Tallin, Estonia, in November, and I look forward to being able to announce the opening of an office in Vilnius, Lithuania, early in the new year." The Riga office is a satellite of Canada\'s Embassy in Stockholm, which is accredited to Latvia. Through the Riga office, Stockholm-based Embassy officials will provide trade and investment promotion assistance, as well as visa and consular services. As part of Canada\'s continuing program of technical assistance to the Baltic States, External Affairs and International Trade Canada\'s Task Force on Central and Eastern Europe has approved a three-year, $2.7-million project to assist dairy farmers and veterinarians in Latvia, Estonia and Lithuania. The need for properly trained dairy specialists and veterinarians in those states is enormous, in light of the rapid privatization of collective and state farms. The new project will allow 100 Baltic dairy farm managers and veterinarians to receive training at the International Livestock Management Schools (ILMS) of Kemptville, Ontario. Top graduates from the Canadian courses will assist in follow-up training in the Baltic States. Through this process, it is expected that up to 1,200 people will receive instruction. This is one of 14 Canadian technical assistance projects for the Baltic States currently completed or approved. Other projects include assistance to the foreign ministries and parliaments of Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia, and technical and practical advice to firms adapting to a market economy. RELEASE No. 285']

RYAN Governor', 'Windy City Latvians Host Emigre Future Symposium February 10, 1995 will mark the beginning of a major initiative "to review and evaluate the survival potential of a strong Latvian community abroad." The Emigre Future Symposium, sponsored by the Chicago Latvian Society (ČLB) and the American Latvian Association (ALA), will be a rare opportunity to meet with and discuss the most fundamental issues of our community\'s future with representatives of our community organizations, including World Federation of Free Latvians (PBLA) Chairman Vaira Paegle and ALA President Ints Rupners, as well as others, both within and outside of the community structure, who are equally committed to the survival of our unique culture in America. The symposium will be held at the Chicago Latvian Community Center (4146 N. Elston Aye., Chicago), and will begin at 10:00 AM. In an effort to make sure that as many people as possible are able to attend, a donation beginning with $5 was set as the admission charge. Lunch will be provided, for a separate fee, by the ČLB Women\'s Auxiliary at the Community Center. Participants are invited to fill out their day of latvian activity by remaining at the Community Center after the symposium, to enjoy an evening of Valentine\'s day romance by the vocal ensemble Idvasa from Indianapolis (Admission: $15/$ 12). PBLA Proposal The World Federation of Free Latvians, the worldwide Latvian emigre umbrella group, proposed in an article in the December 27 issue of the Latvian newspaper Laiks that Latvian organizations abroad organize a series of seminars, meetings and other events designed as a forum for Latvians abroad to consider, and speak out about, our emigre Latvian society – its positive and negative aspects, and where our society goes from here. According to PBLA, the renewal of Latvian independence demands a reorganization of both Latvian organizations and the community at large. We are no longer a community of "exiles", driven by a single unifying mission; the time has come to recognize our new status as a diaspora community lacking a clear mission or future. This lack of clarity threatens the existence of our organized community. Among the challenges faced by our community is a lack of interest in organizational activity among the middle and younger generations of diaspora Latvians; a lack of Speak Out, Chicago! information about the interests and goals of these community members; a shortage of funds to maintain community organizations and institutions, which can be traced to the lack of new community participants; and the resulting drop in community activity and the collapse of many of our organizations. PBLA proposes that one new mission for our society may be to recognize ourselves as a vital part of the Latvian nation, to survive as Latvians living abroad and to remain active participants in Latvia\'s renewal. In order to accomplish this mission, PBLA asserts that the community must find out what, in fact, the needs and desires of the Latvian community abroad are, and how organizational activity can help to satisfy those needs; seek out the means to integrate the middle and younger generations into organized activity, to include those who may not be Latvian speakers or who may be new emigre\'s from Latvia; seek out new methods to raise funds, both within and outside of the community, to support cultural and educational programs as well as cooperation and assistance activities with Latvia; achieve cooperation and understanding with our compatriots in Latvia; and the renewal of our organizations, so that our activities do not overlap and that they reflect the demands of the middle and younger generations of Latvians abroad. The PBLA proposal then provides an outline for directed seminars or discussions, designed to provide our Latvian community abroad an opportunity to immerse itself in these issues and develop responses to the challenges faced by our community. As a final step, PBLA requested that the results of any such discussions, or any other comments or observations about the future of the Latvian diaspora community, be sent to its offices near Washington, DC (400 Hurley Aye, Rockville, MD 20850), where they would be compiled for eventual publication. Chicago\'s \'Town Meeting" The February 10 Emigre Future Symposium is a first response to this proposal by PBLA. Both organizers, Māra Erkmane of the ČLB and Valdis Ronis of the ALA, hope that it will result in an energetic and open debate on the present and future of the Latvian community in America. Three distinguished emigre Latvians have agreed to share their views on a variety of issues affecting the future of our organized community in the U.S.: Dr. Voldemārs Gulēns of Toronto, the chairman of LATS; Garezers Latvian summer high school director Ilze Kreišmane; and Rev. Ēriks Jēkabsons of Chicago\'s St. Paul\'s Latvian Lutheran Church. Their presentations will, it is hoped, prepare symposium attendees for active participation in a "town meeting" style debate with two panels: one made up of community organization representatives, the other of community activists not formally involved in organized activity but serving as representatives of the various target groups: the middle and younger generations of Latvian Americans, new Latvian immigrants, non-speakers. Erkmane, especially, emphasized the importance of the "town meeting" aspect of the seminar. "I feel it is critical, as an organization leader, to learn what members of the community think about our current and future activities: what they like or don\'t like, what they want and need from the Latvian community, and their suggestions for satisfying those needs. Many Chicago Latvians have a lot to say about the current state of our Latvian community – I hope they will take advantage of this opportunity." #']

Representative_Docs: ['War Has Changed In Korea United States Super-fortress bombers have been compelled to adopt drastic changes in strategy and tactics to meet the increasing handicaps, losses and enemy strength confronting them in North Korea. The whole tempo of the war has changed since the fateful day in last October, when a daytime flight of eight Superfortresses was attacked by Red MlG\'s, which shot down three bombers and damaged the others so severely that none reached home. Now the big bombers operate only at night. They fly as high as 27,000 ft., compared with the 8000 ft. altitude which they observed over Japan in 1945. This new high altitude imposes heavy power strains which shorten the life of the engines. Besides, many of the planes now have 2000 or more hours on their records. Bomb releases have had to be improvised for the 40 bombsall 500-pounders—which they now carry. The old German "beetlebug" jets, which caused heavy losses to Allied planes in the closing months of the Second World War, and which are now joining the Red attack on night bombers, do not carry radar. One comes in from the side with all its lights blazing, while others hover in the blackness, waiting for an aiming sight when the bombers open up at the decoy. Superfort gunners now hold their fire. On one recent raid, a solitary enemy propeller-driven plane appeared, and. in the words of one so severely that none reached us around, apparently reporting our speed and course to ack-ack gunners on the ground. He didn\'t attack." There has also been a highly efficient development of skilled co-operaiton between the enemy jets—both MlG\'s and "Beetlebugs"—and the concentration of searchlights, estimated at 75, ringing Sinuiju, the enemy\'s one remaining vulnerable airfield south of the Yalu. In the opinion of veteran United States pilots, Soviet ackack experts who had experience against the Luftwaffe must be in control of the strengthened defences. Despite these handicaps, th Superfortresses have put al North Korean airfields out o commission except Sinuiju, in th extreme north-west. Sinuiju i protected by 75 searchlights, 100 ack-ack guns and 60 jet fighters. Just across the Yalu River 1,200 other enemy planes are based, openly parked on the runways. To the profane chagrin of United Nations fighter pilots and Superfortress crews, they are able to take off in leisurely manoeuvre style as the United Nations warplanes approach. Until this week, the Reds also based jets at nearby Uiju, but this field on the southern bank of the Yalu has now been knocked out. North Korean civilian workers estimated to number 40,000 are herded together in camps near Sinuiju and are rushed out to repair the cratered runways and extinguish the roaring fires at tne sole surviving Red field in South Korea as soon as the bomber flights turn for home. Superfortress gunners have destroyed 26 MIG jets and downed another 16 "probables" in the air war, although computing gunsights on the Superforts are not swift enough to track modern jets properly. According to official figures 17 Superforts have been lost in the war to both fighters and flak.', 'Australia Will Build West\'s Fastest Plane Jet air battles in "MIG alley" in north-west Korea during the past eight months have shown beyond doubt that Russia has forged away to a half-head lead over the United States in the jet fighter race, with Britain temporarily many lengths behind. Korean dogfights have established that the Russian massproduced MIG 15 fighter is superior in many respects to the West\'s best mass-produced fighter—currently the Sabre. At speeds of 670 miles an hour and beyond, the American Sabre pilots have found that only their superior flying training has enabled them to better the probably Chinese-manned MIGs. And the MIG 15 is not Russia\'s best. The Lavochkin LA-17, reportedly a superior jet, has appeared recently in increasing numbers on East German airfields. Britain\'s best jet fighter, currently in mass production — the Meteor — lags at least 50 miles an hour behind the MIG, Lavochkin and Sabre. In September, 1948, an American Sabre with full combat load and equinment lifted the official record to 670.981 miles an hour. It is certain this figure has since been exceeded by both American and Russian standard aircraft, and British prototypes. Russia, since World War 11, has developed a jet air fleet with a vipor and determination rivalling Hitler\'s build-up of the Luftwaffe in the \'30s. Already she numbers j twin and four-engined jet bombers • among her estimated 14.000-plane ļ , force. Immediately after the Second World War, Stalin\'s aircraft designers turned to jets, utilising German and British knowledge. In 1947, the Stalin prize (first class) was awarded to Artem I. Mikoyan and Mikhail A. Gurevich —from whose initials the MIG gains its name—for their joint design of a new fighter. Two thousand MlG\'s were believed to be on strength with the Red Air Force when Russia last November decided to permit the MIG 15 a thorough workout in "MIG Alley" over north-west Korea. From what Mac Arthur described as the "privileged sanctuary" of Antung. just inside the Manchurian border, where American bomber attack was forbidden, they swarmed up to harry Super Fortresses hammering Yalu  bridges. Quickly the MIG 5 showed its superiority over the American Shooting Star, Thunder jet and carrier-based Panther jet fighters. America was forced to draw on her FB6 Sabres, previously reserved for home defence, to find a counter to the MIG. With little between the two machines, the superior training of the American pilots tipped the scales in their favor. It is probably model "E" of the FB6 Sabre which Australia is to build, only the first model "A" of which has been used in Korea. Sabre controls are reported to have been improved to help match MIG manoeuvrability. The Commonwealth Aircraft Corporation is already well ahead with its plans for tooling up for the manufacture of the powerful British Avon jet engine which will almost certainly be fitted in the Australian Sabre. The Avon, in the Sabre frame, may well close the last gap remaining be\'ow the sonic barrier. (Condensed from The Herald.) xxx Meklēju Ludviku Porietis ar meitu Sarmīti. Pēd. adr. Vācijā: IRO Training School, Block B, Neuburg – Donau, USA Zone, 13b. L. pazinot V. Ivanovam, Turner Hostel, Hut D, Room 40, Canberra A. C. T.', 'COULD RUSSIA OVERRUN EUROPE? To answer this question, which occupies the minds of most of the people of the Western nations, "Collier\'s Magazine" sent one of its experts on a six weeks\' tour to Europe. This is what he found:— The democratic world is not yet prepared to halt a savage Russian onslaught, and the Kremlin could have all Europe for the taking. In three to five weeks of beginning such an offensive, Red Army officers would be living in the homes of Frenchmen, just across the Channel from England, and the menace of armed Communism would hang darkly over the rest of the free world. TANK SPEARHEAD In Eastern Germany, along the Elbe River, stands the German Reds\' Bereitschaften police army of 50,000, trained in offensive warfare, and ready at a moment\'s notice to touch off another Korea, says "Collier\'s." Looming massively behind this puppet force is Russia\'s own might, deployed in positions convenient for a westward thrust. First would come 4400 modern tanks, spearheading a ground army of 25 to 80 well-equipped infantry divisions, and topped off by a fleet of some 1500 planes, many of them jets, equal to the world\'s best. In the rear would be 40 more divisions, at least 9000 more tanks, and 6500 more planes. Against this, the Atlantic Pact nations, by scraping hard, could muster 25 divisions, infantry and armored. If the Russians want war, top Allied intelligence officials predict they will not attack for two or three years, while they re-arm and modernise their fighting forces and increase their stockpile of atomic weapons. Military experts of Western Europe and the United States think the Allies can win this arms race if they do not slacken their pace. "Collier\'s" says the man tipped to lead the Russians westward is tall, suave Marshal Konstantin K. Rokossovsky, 54, one of the principal defenders of Moscow in World War 11. A daring, tough field commander, Rokossovsky was released from the Red Army to become marshal of the Polish armies, where he is ideally located to co-ordinate the Soviet divisions poised in Eastern Europe. In November, 19 49, when Rokossovsky left for Poland, Moscow gave a three-point explanation: He was born in Warsaw, and left there 35 years ago; the Polish Government had asked for him; he was taking out Polish citizenship papers. While Russia\'s armed strength is considerable, and her rearmament far ahead of the West in time, her strength dwindles to matchable proportions when the resources of the Atlantic Pact nations—nearly 350,000,000 people —are considered. If all pledges are kept, and expectations filled, the Western European nations should be able to provide nearly 40 divisions for service by 1953-54. Air: For all fronts, including Western Europe, the Red Air Force has 15,000 to 18,000 firstline planes, including transports, of which up to 8000 are in reserve. By contrast, the U.S. Air Force alone had 9000 operational planes before Korea, with 8000 more in reserve squadrons, and 17,000 in "mothballs." The U.S. Navy and Britain had, perhaps, 15,000 more planes in operation, in reserve, and in storage. Ground: The Soviet forces are indisputably massive, but the enormous area they have to defend tends to diminish their strength at any given point. Behind her European army of 25 to 30 divisions, eight of which are armored, Russia has 70 to 85 divisions, including 25 armored, in five separate armies based on Leningrad, Minsk, Odessa, Tiflis, and Soviet Asia. From these forces 40 divisions drawn from the Minsk and Odessa armies are close enough to Germany to start moving within two weeks. In addition, the Russians plan in a crisis to mobilise 100 extra divisions, which could be put into action within three months. An American general in Europe told "Collier\'s" that, to hold back a Russian blitz through Germany or Austria, Western Europe requires at least 35 divisions, of which 15 should be armored or have armored units attached, to act purely as a delaying force. To back up this force, the West must be able to call on at least 25, and, if possible, 40 reserve divisions to go into action within 10 days. France, at present weak, is building up a strong, wellequipped fighting force. She has 659,000 men under arms, but 100,000 are merely overseas security troops. Of her regular army of 300,000, there are 150,000 pinned down in Indo-China. Britain has six active divisions, all committed abroad. Under her expanded defence programme she has promised to increase these to 10 divisions in 1951, and to provide 12 territorial (part-time) divisions. Belgium, third in importance among the Western European nations, has only 80,000 men under arms. She has a small, but flourishing air force, which many U.S. airmen consider the best on the Continent. Allied military leaders are almost unanimous that West Germany\'s potential strength must be enlisted for a real defence of Western Europe. Germany is the only nation this side of the Iron Curtain with a ready reservoir of trained manpower. The Allies have not yet decided what part Spain should play in a war in Western Europe, but they have agreed she must be kept out of enemy hands.']

Of the 65 persons elected as altemates, 25 are not Latvians. 43 persons were elected to the Party’s Review Committee of those 20 are not Latvian.", 'BALTIC WORLD CONFERENCE MEMORANDUM to the Ottawa Human Rights Experts Meeting of the Conference on Security and Cooperation in Europe SOVIET COLONIAL EXPLOITATION AND. DENIAL OF HUMAN RIGHTS IN SOVIET-OCCUPIED REPUBLICS OF ESTONIA, LATVIA, AND LITHUANIA. BACKGROUND On August 1, 1975 at the signing of the Helsinki Final Act United States President Gerald Ford expressed the sentiments of not only the signatory nations, but of all peace loving people throughout the world, when he said: Our peoples will be watching and measuring our progress. They will ask how these noble sentiments are being translated into actions that bring about a more secure and fust order in the daily lives of each of our nations and citizens. However, the signing of the Final Act has facilitated only sporadic, limited improvements in a few general areas. There has been an increase in cooperation in the fields of economics, science and technology, but in the vitally important humanitarian and related fields, the progress has been negligible. There is no evidence, whatsoever, that a major world power and one of the signatories of the Final Act, the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics, has altered its policy of denial of human rights/ Instead of discontinuing the military occupation of the Baltic States — Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania — the Soviet Union has invaded Afghanistan. The pattern of imperialist aggression that characterized the Soviet occupation of the Baltic States forty-five years ago is today being repeated in Afghanistan. We are faced with the tragic irony of Latvian, Lithuanian, and Estonian youth — themselves subjugated — now forced to serve in the Soviet occupational army in Afghanistan, and perhaps to die because of Soviet attempt to emlnve another peoples. ILLEGAL ANNEXATION OF ESTONIA, LATVIA, AND LITHUANIA BY THE U.S.S.R. In 1939, the three sovereign republics of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania were members in good standing of the League of Nations since 1921 and represented on the League Council, and at peace with all their neighbors. They were signatories of their respective Peace Treaties and of separate mutual Nonaggression Treaties with the U.S.S.R. However, on August 23, 1939 and on September 28, 1939, the Soviet Union and Nazi Germany signed in Moscow a ~nonaggression treaty" and a ..Boundary and Friendship Treaty" and in a secret protocol delineated their respective ~spheres of influence." By using threats, the Soviet Union compelled Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania to sign ~mutual assistance treaties'' allowing the U.S.S.R. to establish military bases in the Baltic States. This was followed by military invasion and forced annexation of the Baltic States. The Soviet claim that the Baltic States had voluntarily joined the Soviet Union, as well as the assertion made in Article 70 of the Soviet Constitution that the Soviet Union was constituted ~as a result of a free self-determination of nations and voluntary association of equal Soviet Socialist Republics," constitutes a most flagrant distortion of truth. The events following the Soviet armed invasion and occupation had no constitutional legality. In fact, the resolution passed on July 21, 1940, by a socalled ~People's Diet of Lithuania" and published in that country's Official Gazette stated: Now the people, helped by the mighty Red Army..., established a Soviet Government in their own country... That the people had been able to establish in their own country a just regime — the Soviet regime — is all due to the Soviet Union. (Vyriausybes Zinios, 1940, No. 719) The Select Committee of the U.S. House of Representatives to investigate the Incorporation of the Baltic States into the U.S.S.R. published its findings in 1954 clearly documenting the Soviet aggression. More recently the illegitimacy of the Soviet annexation of the Baltic States was confirmed by: the Joint Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defense of the Australian Parliament in it's report — On Human Rights in the Soviet Union, 1979; the U.S. 97th Congress Public Law 97—196, passed on June . 18, 1982 and the European Parliament's resolution con-, cerning the situation in Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania passed on June 13, 1983. Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian peoples have never acquiesced to the loss of their national independence. They have never accepted U.S.S.R's rule as legitimate or Brīvās pasaules baltiešu centrālās organizācijas iesniegušas šo memorandu Helsinku nolīguma pildīšanas vērtēšanas konferencei, kas patlaban notiek Otavā. lepazīstinait ar to savus angliski runājošos draugus un paziņas. Aicināti to darīt arī jūsu gados jaunākos ģimenes locekļus. Baltieši apsūdz Kremli permanent. From the end of World War II to 1952, Baltic freedom fighters fought against Soviet rule in a protracted guerrilla war. While the number of people killed during those years is not precisely known, it is estimated that about 50,000 fell in the field of battle in Lithuania alone. Although the hopeless armed resistance finally had to be abandoned, spontaneous and organized activities against the religious and national persecution and against the oppression and the denial of human rights continued unabated. As recently as August 23, 1979, on the 40th anniversary of the Nazi-Soviet conspiracy, forty-five Estonians, Latvians, and Lithuanians released in Moscow an appeal to the governments of the U.S.S.R., Federal Republic of Germany, German Democratic Republic, signatories of the Atlantic Charter and of the Helsinki Final Act, and to the United Nations. They asked to invalidate the Russian-German Pacts with the respective secret protocols of August 23 and September 28, 1939, and of January 10, 1941, and that sovereignty be restored to the Baltic States. Their plea was endorsed by Nobel Peace Prize recipient Andrei Sakharov and other Russian human rights defenders. The Soviet annexation of the Baltic States has never been recognized by the United States and other Western democracies. The Soviet imposed and controlled ~governments" in the-occupied Baltic States do not represent the Baltic peoples. The Soviet occupation of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania violates the UN Resolution 2625 regarding the self-determination of peoples, and the letter and spirit of the Helsinki Final Act. SOVIET EXPLOITATION OF THE BALTIC NATIONS The international problem created by the Soviet seizure of the sovereign Baltic States demands a solution. The Soviet Union, signatory of the Final Act, deliberately and constantly violates all these principles and fundamental human rights, particularly in its non-Russian areas: b) mass arrests and deportations c) conscription of local nationals for service in the occupying country's military forces, including the use of conscripts in the invading columns in Czechoslovakia in 1968, and Afghanistan in 1979 d) Moscow's domination of all economic, political, educational and cultural decisions and activities c) foreign Russian domination of managerial positions and preference in housing, education, and employment 0 government subsidies to non-Bait settlers g) distortion of Lithuanian, Latvian, and Estonian histories and culture h) imposition of the Russian language, foreign to the indigenous peoples, as the official language in government offices and media, and degrading the local languages to a minor role, and attempts to impose the use of the Russian language in literature to create a ~Soviet" culture, literature. CONCLUSION One of the more significant and far reaching features of the Final Act is that it provides, unlike many other well meaning documents of this type, for a continuous examination and reassessment of its implementation through periodic meetings of the signatory States. While the Final Act is not binding under international law, it provides the framework, accepted by East and West, as well as the neutral States, for addressing those problems which create tensions and disagreements between nations and previously have led to armed conflicts. Thus, no signatory State can avoid its responsibility to speak out, when unwarranted deprivation of human rights and freedom occurs. If we are to avoid disenchantment and its far reaching calamitous consequences, and if the Final Act is to serve its purpose and not be cast aside for political expediency, a courageous and truthful approach will be required at the Ottawa Conference. A secure and lasting peace in Europe can be achieved only if the remaining injustices of World War II are eliminated and freedom and independence are restored to the Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian people. Oļģerts R. Pavlovskis Chairman, Baltic World Conference President, World Federation of Free Latvians Lembit Savi President, Estonian World Council Dr. C. Kazys Bobelis President, Supreme Committee for the Liberation of Lithuania']
